content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινὸν_1"> (kindred) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτάδελφον_1"> (very sister) form a single emphatic expression (‘my sister, mine own sister’), not a climax (‘kinswoman, and sister’)—<quote xml:lang="grc">κοινόν</quote> strengthening <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτάδελφον</quote> much as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 535"> <title>O. C.</title> 535</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιναί γε πατρὸς ἀδελφεαί</quote> </cit> ('yea, very sisters of their sire'). <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινόν</quote> refers simply to birth from the same parents (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 202">cp. 202</ref>): it will not bear the added moral sense, ‘having common interests and feelings’: that is only implied, in so far as it may be a result of kinship. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτάδελφος</quote> (subst. below, <ref target="cl503">503</ref>, <ref target="cl696">696</ref>) is merely a poetical strengthening of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφός</quote>, and does not necessarily imply (as it might here) what prose expresses by <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφὸς ὁμοπάτριος καὶ ὁμομήτριος</quote> <bibl n="Lys. 32.4">(Lys. or. 32 § 4)</bibl> </cit>: thus Apollo, son of Zeus and Leto, can address Hermes, son of Zeus and Maia, as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτάδελφον αἶμα καὶ κοινοῦ πατρός</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. Eum. 89">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 89</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρα_1">: the periphrasis (as with <quote xml:lang="grc">κεφαλή</quote>) usu. implies respect, affection, or both (cp. Horace's <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">tam cari capitis</quote> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 1.24.2">Odes 1.24.2</bibl> </cit>).—The pathetic emphasis of this first line gives the key-note of the drama. The origin which connects the sisters also isolates them. If Ismene is not with her, Antigone stands alone. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_τελεῖ_2">; For the various interpretations and emendations, see Appendix. The soundness of the text is doubtful, but no proposed correction is probable. I read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅ_τι_2">, pron., not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτι_2">, conjunction, and supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>. In the direct question, <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ὁποῖον οὐ τελεῖ</quote>; we understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τί</quote>. In the indirect form, it is simplest to say <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδ’ ὅ τι οὐ τελεῖ</quote>: and we certainly could not say, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδ’ ὅ τι [ἐστὶν] ὁποῖον οὐ τελεῖ</quote>, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote> came immediately before <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον</quote>. Here, however, the separation of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς τῶν ἀπ’ Οἰδίπου κακῶν</quote> makes a vital difference. The sentence begins as if it were to be, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οἶσθ’ ὅ τι Ζεὺς οὐ τελεῖ</quote>; But when, after an interval, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον</quote> comes in, the Greek hearer would think of the direct form, <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ὁποῖον οὐ τελεῖ</quote>; and so his ear would not be offended. This, too, suggests the answer to the objection that <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote> ought to follow <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον</quote>. Certainly <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 525">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 525,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστ’ Ὀδυσσεὺς ὅ τι σὲ κἀμὲ πημανεῖ</quote> </cit>, would be parallel only if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οἶσθα, Ζεύς</quote> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote>). Nor could we have (e.g.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οἶσθ’ ἥτις Ζεὺς τῶν νόσων ὁποίαν οὐ τελεῖ</quote>; But, since </gloss>

data.quote: οὐκ ἔστ’ Ὀδυσσεὺς ὅ τι σὲ κἀμὲ πημανεῖ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅ_τι_2_2"> might be <emph>acc.</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote> seems to follow it naturally; and when, afterwards, the sentence takes a shape which makes <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote> <emph>nom.</emph>, the ear does not return on <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote> as on a misplaced word, because the whole is felt as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τί Ζεὺς ὁποῖον οὐ τελεῖ</quote>;—The main objection to reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτι_2_2">, and taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον</quote> as substituted for the direct <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖον</quote> (‘that he fulfils—what not?’) is the shortness of the sentence.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_κακῶν_2">, the ills derived from Oed. (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1088"> <title>Ph.</title> 1088</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπας τᾶς ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit>): i.e. the curse upon the Labdacidae <ref n="Soph. Ant. 594">(594)</ref> which he had inherited, and which he bequeathed to his children in a form intensified by his own acts,—the parricide, the incest, the imprecation upon his sons. That imprecation finds a further fulfilment in Creon's edict. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_ζώσαιν_2"> does not mean, ‘living wearily on,’ but simply, ‘still living’ (not yet dead), so that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_2"> is almost pleonastic, as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 750">750</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι ζῶσαν</quote> </cit>, and so <ref n="Soph. Trach. 305"> <title>Tr.</title> 305</ref>. Sometimes, indeed, the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν</quote> is more emphatic, as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 990"> <title>Ai.</title> 990</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι ζῶν...ἐφίετο</quote> </cit> (while <emph>yet</emph> alive), <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 8">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὸς ἔτι ζῶσαν φλόγα</quote> </cit> (<emph>still</emph> smouldering). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_ἄτερ_4">. I translate as if <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἄτην ἄγον</quote> (or the like) stood in the text, since there can be no doubt that such was the general sense; but I leave the traditional words, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἄτης ἄτερ</quote>, thinking no emendation sufficiently probable to be admitted. A discussion will be found in the Appendix. Here, the following points may be noted. (1) This seems to have been the only reading known to Didymus of Alexandria, <foreign xml:lang="lat">circ.</foreign> 30 B.C. (2) It certainly does not yield any tolerable sense. (3) But the phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτης_ἄτερ_4"> is not, in itself, at all suspicious: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 48"> <title>Tr.</title> 48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πημονῆς ἄτερ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 377">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 377</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάβης ἄτερ</quote> </cit>, 703 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτερ πημάτων</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1148"> <title>Ag.</title> 1148</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαυμάτων ἄτερ</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 683"> <title>Th.</title> 683</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνης ἄτερ</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 338"> <title>Ch.</title> 338</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἄτερ κακῶν</quote>; <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 841">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 841</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄτερ πόνων</quote> </cit>. (4) The gentlest remedy would be </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_4"> for the second </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_οὔτ_4">: ‘nothing painful and notfree from calamity’ (=nothing painful and calamitous). The mental pain was accompanied by ruin to their fortunes. I think this possible, but not quite satisfactory. (5) One word, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτης ἄτερ</quote>, might seem desirable: I had thought of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτηφόρον</quote> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">δικηφόρος</quote>). (6) Donaldson's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτην ἄγον</quote> can be supported by fr. 325 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ δ’ ὄλεθρον δεινὸν ἁλήθεῑ ἄγει</quote>, and fr. 856. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κείνῃ τὸ πᾶν, | σπουδαῖον, ἡσυχαῖον, ἐς βίαν ἄγον</quote>. (7) But no emendation has yet been made which, while giving a fit sense, also accounts palaeographically for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτης ἄτερ</quote> being so old. We cannot assume marginal glosses (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτηρ\\</quote>) in MSS. of 30 B.C. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχρόν_5">, shocking the moral sense: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτιμον_5">, attended by outward marks of dishonour,—as Oedipus imagines his daughters exposed to slights at the public festivals (<ref n="Soph. OT 1489"> <title>O. T.</title> 1489</ref> ff.). Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρόν</quote> in a manner balances the subjective <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγεινόν</quote>, as the external <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτιμία</quote> corresponds with the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1283"> <title>O. T.</title> 1283</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁποῖον_ὄπωπα_5">. The repetition of the negative is warranted by the emphasis: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 416"> <title>Ph.</title> 416</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὁ Τυδέως γόνος, | οὐδ’ οὑμπολητὸς Σισύφου Λαερτίῳ, | οὐ μὴ θάνωσι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1014"> <title>Tr.</title> 1014</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πῦρ, οὐκ ἔγχος τις ὀνήσιμον οὐκ ἐπιτρέψει</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1634">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1634</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς οὐκ, ἐπειδὴ τῷδ’ ἐβούλευσας μόρον, | δρᾶσαι τόδ’ ἔργον οὐκ ἔτλης αὐτοκτόνως</quote> </cit>: and so oft. after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μά</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. El. 626"> <title>El.</title> 626</ref>. We need not, then, change <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> (in v. 5) to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὂν</quote>, with Blaydes, nor <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ὄπωπ’</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσόπωπ’</quote> with Todt.</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ πῦρ, οὐκ ἔγχος τις ὀνήσιμον οὐκ
									ἐπιτρέψει

ὃς οὐκ, ἐπειδὴ τῷδ’ ἐβούλευσας μόρον, | δρᾶσαι
									τόδ’ ἔργον οὐκ ἔτλης αὐτοκτόνως

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_κακῶν_5">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote>, possessive (or ‘partitive’) gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄπωπα_5">,—‘which I have not seen as belonging to, being in the number of,’ our woes. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 694"> <title>O. C.</title> 694</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστιν δ’ οἷον ἐγὼ γᾶς Ἀσίας οὐκ ἐπακούω</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔστιν δ’ οἷον ἐγὼ γᾶς Ἀσίας οὐκ
									ἐπακούω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_7"> is oft. thus joined with the interrogative <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς</quote> (‘what <emph>new</emph> thing?’): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1172">cp. 1172</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1281">1281</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 357"> <title>O. C.</title> 357</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ αὖ τίν’ ἥκεις μῦθον... | φέρουσα … ;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1507"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1507</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἐστίν ...νέορτον αὖ;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν δ’ αὖ τίν’ ἥκεις μῦθον... | φέρουσα …
									;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδήμῳ_πόλει_7">, the whole body of the citizens: so 1141, <ref n="Soph. El. 982"> <title>El.</title> 982</ref>: <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανδήμου στρατοῦ</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 844"> <title>Ai.</title> 844</bibl> </cit>. For the adj. compounded with a noun cognate in sense to the subst., cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">βίος μακραίων</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 518), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐήρετμος πλάτα</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 716"> <title>O. C.</title> 716</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῖναι_8">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">θέσθαι.</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθημι νόμον</quote> denotes simply the legislative act as such; hence it is fitting when the lawgiver is supreme or absolute; as Athena says, <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμὸν...θήσω</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. Eum. 484">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 484</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι νόμον</quote> further implies the legislator's personal concern in the law; hence it is said of legislative assemblies (<ref n="Aristot. Pol. 4.1298a">Ar. <title>Pol.</title> 4. 1. 9</ref>): but it can be said also of the despot, if his interest is implied: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 338E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 338E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεται δέ γε τοὺς νόμους ἑκάστη ἡ ἀρχὴ πρὸς τὸ αὑτῇ ξυμφέρον, δημοκρατία μὲν δημοκρατικούς, τυραννὶς δὲ τυραννικούς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τίθεται δέ γε τοὺς νόμους ἑκάστη ἡ ἀρχὴ πρὸς
									τὸ αὑτῇ ξυμφέρον, δημοκρατία μὲν δημοκρατικούς, τυραννὶς δὲ
									τυραννικούς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_στρατηγόν_8">. Creon is already <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλεὺς χώρας</quote> (155), having become so by the fact of Eteocles falling (173). She calls him <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατηγός</quote> because that was the special capacity in which, as king, he had first to act; but the title serves also to suggest rigour. The poets sometimes speak of the <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆμος</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατός</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title>P.</title> 2. 87</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 566">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 566</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_9">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">cognitum habes</foreign>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 318"> <title>Tr.</title> 318</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὄνομα πρός του τῶν ξυνεμπόρων ἔχεις;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδ’ ὄνομα πρός του τῶν ξυνεμπόρων
									ἔχεις;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰσήκουσας_9">, simply ‘heard’ (not, ‘given heed to’), as <ref n="Soph. OC 1645"> <title>O. C.</title> 1645</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 318"> <title>Ai.</title> 318</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 351"> <title>Tr.</title> 351</ref>, 424. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_κακά_10">, ‘that evils belonging to (proper for) our enemies are coming upon our friends’; i.e. that our brother Polyneices is to share the doom of the Argive dead, by being left unburied. As appears from vv. 1081 ff., Soph. supposes that burial was denied to the slain foemen generally, and not to Polyneices alone. No legend was more familiar at Athens than that of Theseus recovering the Argive corpses from Creon ( <title>Suppl.</title>). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1162">Cp. 1162</ref>, where, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρῶν</quote> are the Argives,—the <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμιοι</quote> in their relation to individuals. Ismene, too, seems to understand the Argives; in her reply verses 11—14 refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλους</quote>, and vv. 15—17 to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρῶν</quote>. It is rare that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρῶν</quote> should have the art., while <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote> has none; but <ref n="Soph. Ant. 365">cp. 365</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1530"> <title>O. T.</title> 1530</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέρμα τοῦ βίου</quote> </cit>.—We might take <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὲχθρῶν κακά</quote> as ‘evils planned by our foes’ (i.e. by Creon): cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 422"> <title>Ph.</title> 422</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τά γε | κείνων κάκ’ ἐξήρυκε</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 512"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 512</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κείνων κακόν</quote> </cit>. So schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν ἐχθρῶν μηχανήματα ἐπὶ τοὺς φίλους ἰόντα</quote>. But (<emph>a</emph>) the authorship of the decree having been already named, we now expect a hint of its purport: and (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθροί</quote> being the natural persons to hurt <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλοι</quote>, the antithesis loses point. Some join <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχοντα τῶν ἐχθρῶν</quote>, ‘coming from foes’; which is open to the objections just mentioned, and also to this, that, after such a verb as <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχειν</quote>, the simple gen. ought to denote place (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 152"> <title>O. T.</title> 152</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πυθῶνος ἔβας</quote> </cit>), not agent. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_11"> does not answer to the <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> in 15, but merely gives a slight emphasis to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_11">; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 1.4.12">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1.4.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ μὲν οὐκ οἶδα</quote> </cit> (though others may know).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀντιγόνη_11">, placed as in <ref n="Soph. OC 1"> <title>O. C.</title> 1</ref> n., 1415; while once (<ref n="Soph. OC 507"> <title>O. C.</title> 507</ref>) the anapaest is in the 4th place.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλων_11">, objective gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῦθος_11">, tidings <emph>about</emph> them: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 221"> <title>Ai.</title> 221</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς αἴθονος ἀγγελίαν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 998"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 998</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξεῖα γάρ σου βάξις</quote> </cit>. In <ref n="Soph. OC 1161"> <title>O. C.</title> 1161</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ...μῦθον</quote> (where the gen. is objective) = ‘speech <emph>with</emph> thee.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ὅτου_11">, referring to a definite time, as 1092, <ref n="Soph. OC 345"> <title>O. C.</title> 345</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 326"> <title>Tr.</title> 326</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 493"> <title>Ph.</title> 493</ref>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οὖ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1201"> <title>O. T.</title> 1201</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 38"> <title>Tr.</title> 38</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 661"> <title>Ai.</title> 661</ref>, 1337). It refers to an indefinite time below, 457. The brothers had fallen on the preceding day. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυοῖν_δύο_13">. The addition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύο_13"> would have more point if two pairs were in question, each consisting of one brother and one sister (as, e.g., one might say, ‘the two husbands were taken from the two wives’): yet it is not pointless, since it helps to suggest the isolation of the sisters. As Greek (esp. tragic) idiom loves to mark reciprocity by a repeated word (73 <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη...φίλου</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 267"> <title>Ai.</title> 267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς ἐν κοινοῖσι</quote> </cit>), so it also loves to mark coincidence or contrast of number, whether this is, or is not, especially relevant (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 14">cp. 14</ref>, 55, 141). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντοιν_14"> is clearly required here, though our MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντων</quote>. So in <ref n="Soph. El. 1297"> <title>El.</title> 1297</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">νῷν ἐπελθόντοιν</quote> is certainly right, L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπελθόντων</quote> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">οι</quote> written over <quote xml:lang="grc">ω</quote> as a correction). A plur. noun can stand with <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 12.73"> <title>Od.</title> 12.73</ref>), and in <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.428"> <title>Il.</title> 16.428</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰγυπιοὶ γαμψώνυχες ...κλάζοντε</quote>. But I have been able to find only one proper parallel for such a combination as <quote xml:lang="grc">δυοῖν ἀδελφοῖν θανόντων</quote>, viz., a verse of an unknown poet, fr. adesp. 153 in Nauck <title>Trag. Frag.</title> p. 679 <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴαντε δ’ ὁρμήσαντες ἐκ συνωρίδος</quote>: and this has survived because Herodian cited it as a solecism (<title>Anecd. Boiss.</title> 3. 244): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδει γὰρ ἐπενεχθῆναι δυϊκῷ δυϊκόν</quote>. In that verse, too, the license had an obvious metrical motive, which did not exist here. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 55">Cp. 55 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφὼ δύο...αὐτοκτονοῦντε;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 58">58</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λελειμμένα</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 62">62</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαχουμένα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῇ_χερί_14">, i.e. each by the other's right hand (as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1425"> <title>O. C.</title> 1425</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνατον ἐξ ἀμφοῖν =ἐξ ἀλλήλοιν</quote> </cit>): so 170 <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλῆς μοίρας</quote>, a doom given by each to the other. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 206"> <title>El.</title> 206</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάτους...διδύμαιν χειροῖν</quote> </cit>, a murder done by two right hands (that of Clytaemnestra and that of Aegisthus). Distinguish the use of the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῖ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο</quote>, as 51, 1233. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεί_15">, temporal, ‘since’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οὗ</quote>), as oft. in Her.and the poets. So also <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.13">Thuc. 1.13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτη δ’ ἐστὶ...τριακόσια...ὅτε...ἧλθε</quote> </cit>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_νῦν_15">, last night: so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">νυκτὸς τῆσδε</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 21"> <title>Ai.</title> 21</bibl> </cit> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 209"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 209</ref>): <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">νυκτὶ τῇδε</quote> <bibl n="Soph. El. 644"> <title>El.</title> 644</bibl> </cit>: while ‘tonight’ is <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐπιούσης νυκτός</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Crito 46A">Plat. <title>Crito</title> 46A</ref>). The Argives, having on the preceding day lost all their leaders except Adrastus, fled shortly before dawn (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 100">cp. 100</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέρτερον_15"> here simply = <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον</quote> (cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">nihil supra</foreign>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_εὐτυχοῦσα_15"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτωμένη_15"> are epexegetic of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ὑπέρτερον</quote>, = <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔθ’ ὅτι εὐτυχῶ οὔθ’ ὅτι ἀτῶμαι</quote>. Of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτᾶσθαι</quote> we find only this pres. part. (below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 314">314</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 384"> <title>Ai.</title> 384</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Supp. 182">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 182</ref>) and <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτώμεσθα</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 269"> <title>Ai.</title> 269</bibl> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔδη_18">: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1525"> <title>O. T.</title> 1525</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_18"> has a slightly ironical tone (<ref n="Soph. OT 1008"> <title>O. T.</title> 1008</ref>), glancing at Ismene's apathy.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐλείων_πυλῶν_18">, the outer door (or gate) of the court-yard, the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔλειοι</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλῆς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">θύραι</quote> of the Homeric house (<ref n="Hom. Od. 18.239"> <title>Od.</title> 18.239</ref>), in distinction from the <quote xml:lang="grc">θύραι μεγάρου</quote>, or inner door from the court into the men's hall. This was the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔλειος θύρα</quote>, or front door, of the later Greek house, in distinction from the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέταυλος θύρα</quote> leading from the court to the inner part. The tragedians commonly use the more stately word <quote xml:lang="grc">πύλαι</quote>, rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">θύραι</quote>, for these outer doors of the palace: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 431">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 431</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύλας τε σεμνὰς ἀνδρὸς ὀλβίου τινὸς | προσῆλθον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 438"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 438</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αὐλείοισιν ἑστηκὼς πύλαις.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
πύλας τε σεμνὰς ἀνδρὸς ὀλβίου τινὸς |
									προσῆλθον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέπεμπον_18">, ‘sought to bring thee forth’: the act., since she had herself fetched or called Ismene; the midd. meaning to summon by a messenger, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 951"> <title>O. T.</title> 951</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ ἐξεπέμψω δεῦρο τῶνδε δωμάτων</quote> </cit>; cp. on 161, 165. The imperf., because she speaks of the motive present to her mind while the act was being done: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 770"> <title>O. C.</title> 770</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τότ’ ἐξεώθεις κἀξέβαλλες</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>

data.quote: τί μ’ ἐξεπέμψω δεῦρο τῶνδε δωμάτων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστι_20">; marking surprise (<ref n="Soph. OT 319"> <title>O. T.</title> 319</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_δηλοῖς_20"> is not intransitive, the thing shown being expressed by the partic. in the nomin., just as below, 242 (cp. on 471), <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.21">Thuc. 1.21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πόλεμος οὗτος ...δηλώσει...μείζων γεγενημένος</quote> </cit>. There is a really intransitive use of <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλόω</quote> in <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 4.12">[Andoc. ] or. 4 § 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλώσει δὲ ἡ τῶν συμμάχων ἔχθρα πρῶτον</quote> </cit>, etc., unless <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλώσεται</quote> should be read there; but the speech is a work of the later rhetoric (see <title>Attic Orators</title>, 1. 137). Not one of the few instances adduced from classical Greek requires <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλόω</quote> to be intransitive: <ref n="Hdt. 2.117">Her. 2.117</ref> (subject <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε</quote>): <ref n="Hdt. 5.78">5. 78</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἰσηγορίη</quote>): <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 483D">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 483D</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ φύσις</quote>). In <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.68">Her. 9.68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλοῖ τέ μοι ὅτι πάντα... ἤρτητο..., εἰ καὶ τότε...ἔφευγον</quote> </cit>, the real subject is the clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> (the fact of their flight shows me). </gloss>

data.quote: δηλώσει δὲ ἡ τῶν συμμάχων ἔχθρα πρῶτον

δηλοῖ τέ μοι ὅτι πάντα... ἤρτητο..., εἰ καὶ
									τότε...ἔφευγον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλχαίνουσ_τι_20"> (for the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> placed before its noun, see on <ref n="Soph. OC 280"> <title>O. C.</title> 280 f.</ref>), ‘that thou art troubled by some tidings.’ The verb is intrans., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπος</quote> being the ‘internal,’ or cognate, accus. (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1326"> <title>Ph.</title> 1326</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσεῖς τόδ’ ἄλγος</quote> </cit>): for its sense cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 302"> <title>O. C.</title> 302</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς δ’ ἔσθ’ ὁ κείνῳ τοῦτο τοὔπος ἀγγελῶν;</quote> </cit> From <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλχη</quote>, the purple limpet (perh. connected with <quote xml:lang="grc">κόχλος, κόγχη</quote>), comes <quote xml:lang="grc">καλχαίνω</quote>, to make, or to be, purple: then fig., to be darkly troubled in mind: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 40">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ τοῖσδε καλχαίνων τέκνοις</quote> </cit>. Hence perh. <quote xml:lang="grc">Κάλχας</quote>, the seer who darkly broods on the future. The descent of this metaphor is curious. <quote xml:lang="grc">φυρ</quote>, the root of <quote xml:lang="grc">πορ‐φύρ‐ω</quote>, signified ‘to be agitated,’—like heaving water, for instance (Skt. <foreign xml:lang="sanskrit">bhur</foreign>, Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ferv-ere</foreign>, Curt. § 415). In <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.16"> <title>Il.</title> 14.16</ref> ff. a man's troubled hesitation is likened to the trouble of the sea just <emph>before</emph> a storm, while as yet the waves are not driven either way: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δ’ ὅτε πορφύρῃ πέλαγος μέγα κύματι κωφῷ</quote> (not yet breaking in foam)...<quote xml:lang="grc">ὣς ὁ γέρων ὥρμαινε δαϊζόμενος κατὰ θυμόν</quote>. The Homeric image is thus subtler than that of a storm in the soul (<cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">Volvere curarum tristes in pectore fluctus,</quote> <bibl n="Lucr. 6.34">Lucr. 6. 34</bibl> </cit>). (2) Then <quote xml:lang="grc">πορφύρω</quote> is said of the mind itself: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 21.551"> <title>Il.</title> 21.551</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστη, πολλὰ δέ οἱ κραδίη πόρφυρε μένοντι</quote> </cit>, ‘was troubled.’ (3) From <quote xml:lang="grc">πορφύρω</quote>, as=‘to be turbid,’ came <quote xml:lang="grc">πορφύρα</quote> as=simply ‘<emph>the dark</emph>’ (purple-fish and dye): and then in later Greek the verb took on the specific sense, ‘to be <emph>purple.</emph>’ (4) <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλχη = πορφύρα</quote>: and hence <quote xml:lang="grc">καλχαίνω</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
τίς δ’ ἔσθ’ ὁ κείνῳ τοῦτο τοὔπος
									ἀγγελῶν;

Volvere curarum tristes in pectore
									fluctus,

ἔστη, πολλὰ δέ οἱ κραδίη πόρφυρε
									μένοντι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γάρ_21">, ‘what, has not,’ etc., introducing an indignant question, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1348"> <title>Ai.</title> 1348</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 249"> <title>Ph.</title> 249</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὼ_δέ_21">, partitive apposition (<quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα καθ’ ὅλον καὶ μέρος</quote>), the whole, which should be in the genitive, being put in the same case as the part,—a constr. freq. in nom., but rare in accus.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 561">cp. 561</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.92">Thuc. 2.92</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο ὑποσχέσεις τὴν μὲν βουλόμενος ἀναπρᾶξαι, τὴν δὲ αὐτὸς ἀποδοῦναι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
δύο ὑποσχέσεις τὴν μὲν βουλόμενος ἀναπρᾶξαι,
									τὴν δὲ αὐτὸς ἀποδοῦναι

lemma: τὼ κασιγνήτω … τὸν μὲν … τὸν δέ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφου_21"> before <quote xml:lang="grc">τὼ κασιγνήτω</quote> shows the first thought to have been,— ‘<emph>of a tomb</emph>, he has deemed our two brothers, the one <emph>worthy</emph>, the other <emph>unworthy</emph>’: but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προτίσας_21">, which has taken the place of a word in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιώσας</quote>, substitutes the idea of preferring one brother to the other. Thus </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφου_21_2"> is left belonging, in strict grammar, to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτιμάσας_21"> only; for the genit. with which, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 49"> <title>O. C.</title> 49</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτιμάσας_ἔχει_21">=a perfect, <ref n="Soph. OT 577"> <title>O. T.</title> 577</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_νόμῳ_23">, the reading of our MSS., was a clumsy attempt to mend a corrupt text, in the sense: ‘having treated (him) in accordance with righteous judgment and usage.’ The lateness of the corruption is shown by </gloss>
lemma: σὺν δίκῃ | χρησθεὶς δικαίᾳ καὶ νόμῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρησθεὶς_23"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">χρησάμενος</quote>, since in classical Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">χρησθῆναι</quote> is always pass., <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.144">Her. 7.144</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (the ships) </note>οὐκ ἐχρήσθησαν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 21.16">Dem. or. 21 § 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἂν χρησθῇ</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐσθής</quote>); of oracles being delivered, <ref n="Soph. OT 604"> <title>O. T.</title> 604</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 355"> <title>O. C.</title> 355</ref>: in Polyb. 2. 32 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐβούλοντο συγχρῆσθαι ταῖς...δυνάμεσι, συγχρησθῆναι</quote> is found, indeed, in some MSS., but is manifestly corrupt. Several conjectures are discussed in the Appendix. It is most improbable that vv. 23, 24 have grown out of one verse, either by the interpolation of v. 24, or by the expansion of v. 23. For it is evidently essential to the contrast with vv. 26—30 that the honours paid to Eteocles should be described with emphasis. Were v. 23 immediately followed by v. 25, the effect would be too bald and curt. I read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_νόμου_23">, ‘with righteous observance of justice and usage’ [<quote xml:lang="grc">νόμῳ</quote> could be retained, but would be harsh; and the corruption of <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκης</quote> would have caused that of <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμου</quote>]. </gloss>
lemma: σὺν δίκης | χρήσει δικαίᾳ καὶ νόμου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκης_23">, following </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύν_23">, was changed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκῃ_23">, and then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρήσει_23"> became </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_χρησθείς_23">, in an attempt to mend the sense. <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν χρήσει δικαίᾳ δίκης καὶ νόμου = δίκῃ καὶ νόμῳ δικαίως χρώμενος</quote>. For the latter, cp. Antiphon or. 5 § 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆσθαι τῇ δίκῃ καὶ τῷ νόμῳ</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 316">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 316</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν τῇ δίκῃ χρῆσθαι θέλει</quote> </cit>. The substantival periphrasis (<quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν χρήσει τινός</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">χρώμενός τινι</quote>) is of a common kind; e.g. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.39">Thuc. 2.39</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ...τὴν ἐν τῇ γῇ ἐπὶ πολλὰ ἡμῶν αὐτῶν ἐπίπεμψιν</quote> </cit>: 5. 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ προόψεως...αὐτῶν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ προΐδοιεν αὐτούς</quote>).—Schütz (1876, <title>Jahr. f. kl. Phil.</title> p. 176) proposed </gloss>

data.quote:
διὰ...τὴν ἐν τῇ γῇ ἐπὶ πολλὰ ἡμῶν αὐτῶν
									ἐπίπεμψιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῆσθαι_δικαιῶν_23">, ‘deeming it right to treat (him) in accordance with justice,’ etc.: where, however, to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῷ</quote> is most awkward: I should prefer <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμῳ</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OT 1526"> <title>O. T.</title> 1526</ref> is an instance in which <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> seems to have supplanted an article (<quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς</quote>) after the sense had become obscured. But <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆσθαι δικαιῶν τῷ νόμῳ</quote> appears somewhat too prosaic and cold, and, in so lucid a phrase, would <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαιῶν</quote> have been likely to become <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίᾳ</quote>? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμου_23">, of funeral rites, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 519">cp. 519</ref>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμιμα</quote> (<ref n="Thuc. 3.58">Thuc. 3.58</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νομιζόμενα ποιεῖν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">iusta facere</foreign>): <ref n="Plut. Sull. 38">Plut. <title>Sull.</title> 38</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηδείας τῆς νενομισμένης</quote>, the usual obsequies. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_νεκροῖς_25">, ethic dat., in their sight (<ref n="Soph. OT 8"> <title>O. T.</title> 8</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσι_κλεινός_25">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1446"> <title>O. C.</title> 1446</ref>). The dead repelled the spirit of the unburied from their converse: <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.71"> <title>Il.</title> 23.71</ref> (the shade of the unburied Patroclus to Achilles) <quote xml:lang="grc">θάπτε με ὅττι τάχιστα, πύλας Ἀΐδαο περήσω. | τῆλέ με εἴργουσι ψυχαί, εἴδωλα καμόντων, | οὐδέ μέ πω μίσγεσθαι ὑπὲρ ποταμοῖο ἐῶσιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντα_νέκυν_26">, by enallage for <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντος</quote>, but also with a reminiscence of the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκύων κατατεθνηώτων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 515">cp. 515</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κατθανὼν νέκυς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκεκηρῦχθαι_27"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">προκεκηρῦχθαι</foreign>, as in 203. The compound with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> usu. = ‘to banish by proclamation’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 430"> <title>O. C.</title> 430</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_καλύψαι_27">, instead of the ordinary <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καλύψαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 443">cp. 443</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1739"> <title>O. C.</title> 1739</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέφυγε... | ...τὸ μὴ πίτνειν κακῶς</quote> </cit>. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ καλύψαι</quote> might be viewed as subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκεκηρῦχθαι</quote>, the latter was probably felt as an impersonal pass. The addition of the art. to the infin. is freq. in drama: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78">cp. 78</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 47"> <title>O. C.</title> 47</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐᾶν_δ_29">. Since <quote xml:lang="grc">τινά</quote> can mean <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα τινά</quote>, it is not necessary to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντας</quote> as subject for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐᾶν</quote>, though in <ref n="Soph. OT 238"> <title>O. T.</title> 238 ff. </ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτ’ ἐσδέχεσθαι μήτε προσφωνεῖν τινα,...ὠθεῖν δ’ ἀπ’ οἴκων πάντας</quote>.—L has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄταφον_ἄκλαυτον_29">. For this order it may be said, that a tribrach contained in one word forms the second foot in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1235"> <title>Ph.</title> 1235</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1314"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1314</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατέρα</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιε</quote>. Also, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄταφον</quote> thus gains a certain abrupt force, and the order corresponds with <quote xml:lang="grc">καλύψαι...κωκῦσαι</quote>. But against it we may urge:—(1) The other order was the usual one: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.386"> <title>Il.</title> 22.386</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται πὰρ νήεσσι νέκυς ἄκλαυτος ἄθαπτος</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 30">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 30</ref> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλαυτος, ἄταφος</quote>. (2) On such a question of order no great weight belongs to L, in which wrong transpositions of words certainly occur; e.g. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 156"> <title>Ph.</title> 156</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1332"> Ph. 1332</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title> 1088</ref>. Here some MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλαυστον ἄταφον</quote>. There is no ground for distinguishing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλαυστος</quote>, as ‘not to be wept,’ from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλαυτος</quote>, ‘not wept’ (see <ref n="Soph. OT 361"> <title>O. T.</title> 361</ref> note in Appendix on <quote xml:lang="grc">γνωτός</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">γνωστός</quote>). L gives the form without sigma here, as below, 847, 876, and in <ref n="Soph. OC 1708"> <title>O. C.</title> 1708</ref>; but the sigmatic form in <ref n="Soph. El. 912"> <title>El.</title> 912</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
κεῖται πὰρ νήεσσι νέκυς ἄκλαυτος
									ἄθαπτος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θησαυρόν_30">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρμαιον, εὕρημα</quote>, taking it as merely ‘treasure <emph>trove</emph>’; but here ‘treasure’ evidently implies ‘store’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 37"> <title>Ph.</title> 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θησαύρισμα</quote>); the carrion-birds can return again and again to their feast.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορῶσι_30">, when they look down upon it from the air. There is no ground for saying that <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορᾶν</quote> was specially ‘to eye <emph>with desire</emph>’: in <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 5.1.15">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5.1.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε πυρὸς ἅπτομαι οὔτε τοὺς καλοὺς εἰσορῶ</quote> </cit>, it is simply ‘look at.’ The conjecture </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε πυρὸς ἅπτομαι οὔτε τοὺς καλοὺς
									εἰσορῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορμῶσι_30">, to be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς χάριν βορᾶς</quote>, ‘<emph>swooping to</emph> the joy of the feast,’ is not only needless, but bad. Far finer is the picture of the birds pausing in their flight at the moment when they first <emph>descry</emph> the corpse below.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_βορᾶς_30"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">γλυκὺν θησαυρόν</quote>, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορῶσι</quote>: lit., a <emph>welcome store</emph> to the birds, when they look upon it, <emph>with a view to pleasure in feeding.</emph> For the sensual use of <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 254a"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 254 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς τῶν ἀφροδισίων χάριτος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_χάριν_30"> is used either adverbially or with a genitive. (1) As an adverb, it means literally, ‘with a view to gratification’: hence (<emph>a</emph>) when the <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις</quote> is one's own, ‘<emph>at pleasure</emph>,’ as Philoctetes calls the birds ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1156"> <title>Ph.</title> 1156</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κορέσαι στόμα πρὸς χάριν</quote>, to glut their beaks on him ‘as they will’: (<emph>b</emph>) when the <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις</quote> is another's, ‘so as to <emph>give</emph> pleasure,’ ‘<emph>graciously</emph>,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1152"> <title>O. T.</title> 1152</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ πρὸς χάριν μὲν οὐκ ἐρεῖς</quote> </cit>. (2) A genitive after <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς χάριν</quote> can denote (<emph>a</emph>) that <emph>in which</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις</quote> consists, as <quote xml:lang="grc">βορᾶς</quote> here: or (<emph>b</emph>) the person or thing <emph>whose</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις</quote> is, as below, 908, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος νόμου...πρὸς χάριν</quote>; ‘in deference to what law?’ <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 538">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 538</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοις τε χρῆσθαι μὴ πρὸς ἰσχύος χάριν</quote> </cit>, ‘not at the pleasure of force,’— i.e. not so that force can do what it pleases. Here, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>πρὸς χάριν</emph> βορᾶς</quote> differs from a simple <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν βορᾶς</quote> by implying the same notion as the adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς χάριν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1156"> <title>Ph.</title> 1156</ref> quoted above: ‘to feast on <emph>at their pleasure.</emph>’—Eustathius on <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.379"> <title>Il.</title> 8.379</ref> (p. 719. 9) defines carrion-birds as <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πρὸς χάριν ὁρῶσι βορᾶς τῆς ἀπὸ τῶν σαρκῶν</quote>. It cannot be doubted that he was thinking of our passage, and that his text, like ours, had <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορῶσι</quote>: but, using the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶσι</quote>, he has made a new phrase, ‘<emph>looking to</emph> pleasure in food,’ and his words afford no argument for joining <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς χάριν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορῶσι</quote> here. </gloss>

data.quote: σὺ πρὸς χάριν μὲν οὐκ ἐρεῖς
νόμοις τε χρῆσθαι μὴ πρὸς ἰσχύος χάριν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_31">, like </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_κἀμοί_31">, depends on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηρύξαντ_ἔχειν_31"> (for which see 22). Creon's edict, addressed to all Thebans, touches the sisters first, since, as the nearest relatives of the dead, they were most concerned to see that he received burial. Antigone speaks with burning indignation. She says, in effect:—‘Thus hath Creon forbidden thee and me to render the last offices to our brother.’ The parenthesis <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω γὰρ κἀμέ</quote> is prompted by the intense consciousness of a resolve. To her, who knows her own heart, it seems wonderful that Creon should even have imagined her capable of obeying such an edict. It is a fine psychological touch, and one of the most pathetic in the play.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἀγαθόν_31">, ironical, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 385"> <title>O. T.</title> 385</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέων ὁ πιστός</quote> </cit>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 873"> <title>Ph.</title> 873</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγαθοὶ στρατηλάται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_κἀμέ_31"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀμοί</quote>), a constr. most freq. when the acc. is a proper name, as <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 24.6">Dem. or. 24 § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέκρουσ’ ἀνθρώπῳ πονηρῷ...Ἀνδροτίωνα λέγω</quote> </cit>. So <ref n="Soph. Trach. 9"> <title>Tr.</title> 9</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1261"> <title>Ph.</title> 1261</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 609">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 609</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Her. 642">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 642</ref>. On the other hand cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 8.24">Dem. or. 8 § 24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ὧν ἂν ἕκαστοι δύνωνται, τούτων τῶν τὴν Ἀσίαν ἐνοικούντων λέγω, χρήματα λαμβάνουσιν</quote> </cit>: Aesch. fr. 169 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ Ἀντικλείας ἆσσον ἦλθε Σίσυφος, | τῆς σῆς λέγω τοι μητρός</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 569"> <title>Ai.</title> 569</ref> where L has <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐριβοία</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sic</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote>, most edd. now give the dat.</gloss>

data.quote:
παρ’ ὧν ἂν ἕκαστοι δύνωνται, τούτων τῶν τὴν
									Ἀσίαν ἐνοικούντων λέγω, χρήματα λαμβάνουσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_31"> enclitic. (1) Taking </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_31_2"> as ethic dat. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτα_31">: ‘There is the good Creon's proclamation for you,—aye, and for <emph>me</emph> too, for I count myself also amongst those forbidden’ (Campbell). Thus </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμοί_31"> is not, like </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_31_3">, a mere ethic dat., but rather a dat. of interest. Such a transition seems hardly possible. (2) Taking </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_31_4"> as ethic dat. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγαθόν_31">: ‘your good Creon, aye and mine, for I own I too thought him so’ (Kennedy). But Antigone is too much occupied with the edict itself to dwell with such emphasis at such a moment on the disappointment which she has experienced as to Creon's amiability. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεῖσθαι_33"> pres. (<ref n="Hom. Od. 5.88"> <title>Od.</title> 5.88</ref>): Eur. has <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖσθε</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Alc. 737">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 737</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">νεόμενος</quote> (in lyr. <ref n="Soph. El. 723"> <title>El.</title> 723</ref>): otherwise the word is not tragic.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσι_εἰδόσιν_33">, synizesis, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 263">263</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 535">535</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1155"> <title>O. C.</title> 1155</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μὴ εἰδότ’ αὐτόν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 321"> <title>Tr.</title> 321</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ξυμφορά τοι μὴ εἰδέναι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_οὐδέν_35">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 466">466</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 983"> <title>O. T.</title> 983</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦθ’ ὅτῳ | παρ’ οὐδέν ἐστι</quote> </cit>. The addition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_35"> serves to mark Creon's point of view more strongly: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 732"> <title>O. C.</title> 732</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκω γὰρ οὐχ ὡς δρᾶν τι βουληθείς</quote> </cit>, n.</gloss>

data.quote: ἥκω γὰρ οὐχ ὡς δρᾶν τι βουληθείς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_δρᾷ_35">, the antecedent <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ</quote> being suppressed,=a dat., <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ δρῶντι</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 18.37">Isocr. or. 18 § 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσήκει βοηθεῖν ὑμᾶς οὐχ οἵτινες ἂν δυστυχεστάτους σφᾶς αὑτοὺς ἀποδείξωσιν ἀλλ’ οἵτινες ἂν δικαιότερα λέγοντες φαίνωνται</quote> </cit>. That such a relative clause was felt as practically equivalent to a noun-case may be seen from <ref n="Thuc. 2.62">Thuc. 2.62</ref>, where it is co-ordinated with a dative: <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔχημα μὲν...καὶ δειλῷ τινὶ ἐγγίγνεται, καταφρόνησις δέ, ὃς ἂν καὶ γνώμῃ πιστεύῃ</quote>. Thuc. uses this constr. esp. in definitions, as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.14">6. 14,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καλῶς ἄρξαι τοῦτ’ εἶναι, ὃς ἂν τὴν πατρίδα ὠφελήσῃ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
προσήκει βοηθεῖν ὑμᾶς οὐχ οἵτινες ἂν
									δυστυχεστάτους σφᾶς αὑτοὺς ἀποδείξωσιν ἀλλ’ οἵτινες ἂν
									δικαιότερα λέγοντες φαίνωνται

τὸ καλῶς ἄρξαι τοῦτ’ εἶναι, ὃς ἂν τὴν πατρίδα
									ὠφελήσῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προκεῖσθαι_36">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 481">cp. 481</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 865"> <title>O. T.</title> 865</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοι πρόκεινται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δημόλευστον_36"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">λευσθέντα ὑπὸ τοῦ δήμου</foreign>, the epith. of the victim being transferred to the doom: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 357"> <title>Tr.</title> 357</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ῥιπτὸς Ἰφίτου μόρος</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1616">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1616</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δημορριφεῖς...λευσίμους ἀράς</quote> </cit>. Death by public stoning would mean that the transgressor was execrated as a traitor to the commonweal: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 435"> <title>O. C.</title> 435</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_37"> ethic dat.: so <ref n="Soph. OC 62"> <title>O. C.</title> 62</ref> and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 761"> <title>El.</title> 761</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτά σοι ταῦτ’ ἐστίν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσθλῶν_37">, gen. of origin with <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφυκας</quote>, from a good stock: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1062"> <title>O. T.</title> 1062</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν τρίτης ἐγὼ | μητρὸς φανῶ τρίδουλος</quote> </cit> n. Cp. fr. 601 (race cannot be relied upon, since often) <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλεται | οὔτ’ ἀπ’ εὐγενέων ἐσθλὸς οὔτ’ ἀχρείων | τὸ λίαν κακός</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἐὰν τρίτης ἐγὼ | μητρὸς φανῶ τρίδουλος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ταλαῖφρον_39">, ‘my poor sister’: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνολβος</quote> (1026), <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσποτμος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 888"> <title>O. T.</title> 888</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλεος</quote>, etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_τούτοις_39">: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1443 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα... | ...τῇδε φῦναι</quote>. The plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τούτοις_39"> means either (1) ‘in these circumstances,’ as here, and <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 101C">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 101C</ref>: or (2) ‘meanwhile,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">inter haec</foreign>, as <ref n="Plat. Sym. 220B">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 220B</ref>. The sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τούτῳ</quote> usually=‘meanwhile’; more rarely ‘in this case’ (<ref n="Thuc. 1.37">Thuc. 1.37</ref>), or ‘at this point’ <ref n="Thuc. 2.8">(id. 2. 8).</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύουσ_φάπτουσα_40">, ‘by seeking to loose or to tighten the knot,’—a phrase, perhaps proverbial, for ‘meddling in any way.’ She can do no good by touching the tangled skein. The Greek love of antithesis naturally tended to expressions like our ‘by hook or by crook,’ ‘by fair means or foul,’ ‘for love or money,’ ‘good or bad,’ etc. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1109">Cp. 1109</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ τ’ ὄντες οἵ τ’ ἀπόντες</quote> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 800">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 800</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόρῳ γε τῷδε συμπεπλέγμεθα ξένῳ, | ὃς οὔτε πάσχων οὔτε δρῶν σιγήσεται</quote> </cit>, which is plainly colloquial,—meaning ‘who will not hold his peace on any terms’; for though <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσχων</quote> suits the recent <emph>imprisonment</emph> of Dionysus, <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶν</quote> could not mean definitely, ‘as a free agent.’ Similarly we may suppose that some such phrase as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε λύων οὔτε ἅπτων</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 417E">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 417E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ ἅπτειν καὶ δεῖν ταὐτόν ἐστι</quote> </cit>) was familiar as=‘by no possible means.’ If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφάπτουσα</quote> is sound, the poet has refined a colloquialism by modifying <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπτουσα</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφάπτουσα</quote>, just as <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δρῶν ἢ τί λέγων</quote> (cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 660">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 660</ref>) appears in <ref n="Soph. OT 71"> <title>O. T.</title> 71</ref> as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι | δρῶν ἢ τί φωνῶν</quote>. Some find a reference to weaving;—‘by loosening the web, or fastening a new thread’; but, though the phrase may have been first suggested by the loom, it was probably used without any such conscious allusion. Quite different from our passage is <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1317"> <title>Ai.</title> 1317</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ξυνάψων ἀλλὰ συλλύσων πάρει</quote> </cit>, ‘not to embroil the feud, but to help in solving it’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 670">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 670</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνας νῦν τέχνας ἔχομεν ἢλόγους | σφαλεῖσαι κάθαμμα λύειν λόγου</quote> </cit>; ‘to loose the knot of controversy.’ —Another view makes the phrase refer to Creon's edict: ‘seeking to undo it, or <emph>to tighten it</emph>,’—i.e. to break it, or to make it more stringent than it already is (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">λύουσα τὸν νόμον, ἢ βεβαιοῦσα αὐτόν</quote>). But, though Antigone has not yet revealed her purpose, too great callousness is ascribed to Ismene if she is supposed to doubt whether her sympathy is invited against or <emph>for</emph> such an edict.—The <emph>act.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφάπτειν</quote> is rare: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 933"> <title>Tr.</title> 933</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον ὡς ἐφάψειεν</quote> </cit>, that he had imposed the deed on her (by his fierce reproaches): <ref n="Pind. O. 9">Pind. <title>O.</title> 9. 60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καθέλοι νιν αἰὼν πότμον ἐφάψαις | ὀρφανὸν γενεᾶς</quote>. Was Porson right in conjecturing </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀπόρῳ γε τῷδε συμπεπλέγμεθα ξένῳ, | ὃς οὔτε
									πάσχων οὔτε δρῶν σιγήσεται
τὸ δὲ ἅπτειν καὶ δεῖν ταὐτόν ἐστι
εἰ μὴ ξυνάψων ἀλλὰ συλλύσων πάρει

τίνας νῦν τέχνας ἔχομεν ἢλόγους | σφαλεῖσαι
									κάθαμμα λύειν λόγου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἵθ_ἅπτουσα_40">? For it, we may observe:—(1) An opposition of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">λύειν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπτειν</quote> suits a proverbial phrase: (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> are elsewhere confused, as <ref n="Soph. OC 80"> <title>O. C.</title> 80</ref> (n.): (3) the single <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</quote> is found in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 517"> <title>O. T.</title> 517</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοισιν εἴτ’ ἔργοισιν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 236"> <title>Tr.</title> 236</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴας εἴτε βαρβάρου</quote> </cit>; Against the conjecture is the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθ’ ἅπτουσα</quote> would have been much less likely to generate the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ θάπτουσα</quote> of our MSS., since the intermediate <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εἰ</emph> θάπτουσα</quote>, being obviously unmeaning, would have been likely to cure itself. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσθείμην_40">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 767"> <title>O. C.</title> 767</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσθέσθαι χάριν</quote> </cit>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέον_40">, ‘for thine advantage’: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 37"> <title>O. T.</title> 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἐξειδὼς πλέον</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμπονήσεις_41"> is the more general word,—‘co-operate’; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνεργάσει_41">, the more explicit,—‘help to accomplish the deed.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖόν_κινδύνευμα_41">, cognate acc. to the verbs in 41: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 344"> <title>O. C.</title> 344</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ | ὑπερπονεῖτον</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_41"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_41"> here is one of sense, not of idiom. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ποῖ</emph> γνώμης ...εἶ</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰέναι</quote>) would mean, ‘to what thought will you have recourse?’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 170"> <title>O. C.</title> 170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ τις φροντίδος ἔλθῃ</quote> </cit>; (n.), <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 705"> <title>Tr.</title> 705</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω...ποῖ γνώμης πέσω</quote> </cit>. But the meaning here is, ‘what can you be thinking of?’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_43"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκόπει</quote>.—Join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_χερί_43">: she lifts her hand.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κουφιεῖς_43">, take up for burial: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1410"> <title>Ai.</title> 1410</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρὰς σὺν ἐμοὶ | τάσδ’ ἐπικούφιζ</quote> </cit>' (the dead Ajax): and the common phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναιρεῖσθαι νεκρούς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_γὰρ_43"> marks surprise (<ref n="Soph. OC 64"> <title>O. C.</title> 64</ref>). The absence of caesura in the first three feet allows each of the two important words (<quote xml:lang="grc">νοεῖς θάπτειν</quote>) to fall slowly from the astonished speaker's lips.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφε_43"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</foreign>, as 516, 1226. <quote xml:lang="grc">σφε</quote> can be s. or pl., m. or f.: <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν</quote>, s. or pl., m., f. or n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόρρητον_43">, acc. neut. in appos. with <quote xml:lang="grc">θάπτειν σφε</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 507E">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 507E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιθυμίας...πληροῦν, ἀνήνυτον κακόν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλει_43">, ‘to’ or ‘for’ (dat. of interest), not ‘by’ (dat. of agent), though the latter might be supported by <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1657">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1657</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ σφε θάψω, κἂν ἀπεννέπῃ πόλις</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἐγώ σφε θάψω, κἂν ἀπεννέπῃ πόλις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἐμὸν_45"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> To the question—‘Do you really mean to bury him?’—the simple answer would have been, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν γοῦν ἐμὸν ἀδελφόν</quote>, ‘I certainly mean to bury my own brother.’ But the word </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμόν_45">—reminding her that he is equally <title>Ismene's</title> brother—prompts the insertion of the reproachful clause, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_θέλῃς_45">. Thus the contrast between </gloss>
lemma: καὶ τὸν σόν, ἢν σὺ μὴ θέλῃς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἐμόν_45"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_σόν_45"> anticipates the emphasis on the word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφόν_45">. The whole thought is,—‘I will certainly do <emph>my</emph> duty,—and <emph>thine</emph>, if thou wilt not,—to a <emph>brother.</emph>’ Since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἐμός</quote> is the same person as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ σός</quote>, this thought can be poetically expressed by saying, ‘I will certainly bury <emph>my</emph> brother,—and <emph>thine</emph>, if thou wilt not’: for the tribute <emph>rendered</emph> to him by one sister represents the tribute <emph>due</emph> from both. Remark that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γοῦν_45"> often emphasises a pers. or possessive pron. (as here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμόν</quote>): 565 <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ γοῦν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 527"> <title>Ai.</title> 527</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς γοῦν ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 626"> <title>O. T.</title> 626</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γοῦν ἐμόν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1499"> <title>El.</title> 1499</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ γοῦν ς</quote> </cit>'.— Two other versions are possible, but less good. (1) Taking </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_σόν_45_2"> as=‘him who is my brother and thine,’ and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν</quote> as=‘<emph>even</emph> if.’ But for this we should expect <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν γοῦν ἐμόν τε καὶ σόν</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄν</quote>. (2) Taking </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_45"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤν_45">, ‘I will bury my brother, even if thou wilt not bury thine.’ But (i) the separation of <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤν</quote> is abnormal: (ii) the mode of expression would be scarcely natural unless <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἐμός</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ σός</quote> were different persons. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφὸν_ἁλώσομαι_46">. Didymus (circ. 30 B.C.) said this verse was condemned as spurious ‘by the commentators’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ τῶν ὑπομνηματιστῶν</quote>). I believe it to be undoubtedly genuine. One modern argument against it is that Antigone should here speak only one verse. But these two verses express the resolve on which the action of the play turns: it is an important moment in the dialogue. And, at such a moment, Soph. often allows a stichomuthia to be broken by two or more verses for the same speaker. See the stichomuthia in 401—406, broken by 404 f.: <ref n="Soph. OT 356"> <title>O. T.</title> 356-369</ref>, broken by 366 f.: <ref n="Soph. OT 1000"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1000—1046</ref>, broken by 1002 f. and 1005 f.: <ref n="Soph. OC 579"> <title>O. C.</title> 579-606</ref>, broken by 583 f. and 599 ff. Further, verse 46 is Sophoclean in three traits: (<emph>a</emph>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφόν_46"> emphasised by position as first word, with a pause after it: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 72">cp. 72</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 525">525</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 278"> <title>O. T.</title> 278</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖξαι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1628"> <title>O. C.</title> 1628</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν</quote> </cit>. (<emph>b</emph>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δή_46"> in rejecting an alternative: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 576"> <title>O. T.</title> 576</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκμάνθαν’· οὐ γὰρ δὴ φονεὺς ἁλώσομαι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 110"> <title>O. C.</title> 110</ref> n. (<emph>c</emph>) The phrase with the aor. part.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1267"> <title>Ai.</title> 1267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις διαρρεῖ καὶ προδοῦσ’ ἁλίσκεται</quote> </cit>. Lastly, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 45">v. 45</ref>, if alone, would be too bald and curt. </gloss>

data.quote: ἐκμάνθαν’· οὐ γὰρ δὴ φονεὺς ἁλώσομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_σχετλία_47">, ‘over-bold.’ The word primarily means ‘enduring’ (&\sqrt;<quote xml:lang="grc">σχε, σχεθεῖν</quote>). Hence: (1) Of <emph>persons</emph>, (<emph>a</emph>) ‘unflinching,’ in audacity or cruelty,—the usu. Homeric sense, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.494"> <title>Od.</title> 9.494</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχέτλιε, τίπτ’ ἐθέλεις ἐρεθιζέμεν ἄγριον ἄνδρα</quote> </cit>; So <ref n="Soph. Phil. 369"> <title>Ph.</title> 369</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 930"> Ph. 930</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σχέτλιε</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Alc. 741">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 741</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
σχέτλιε, τίπτ’ ἐθέλεις ἐρεθιζέμεν ἄγριον
									ἄνδρα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_48">, adv.: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_μέτα_48"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέτεστι</foreign>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐμῶν_48"> (masc.) with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴργειν_48"> only: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 536"> <title>El.</title> 536</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ μετῆν αὐτοῖσι τήν γ’ ἐμὴν κτανεῖν</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Plat. Apol. 19c">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 19 C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ τούτων... οὐδὲν μέτεστι</quote>. For the plur. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1448"> <title>O. T.</title> 1448</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶς τῶν γε σῶν τελεῖς ὕπερ</quote> </cit> (‘thou wilt meetly render the last rites to thine own,’—Iocasta). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ οὐ μετῆν αὐτοῖσι τήν γ’ ἐμὴν
									κτανεῖν
ὀρθῶς τῶν γε σῶν τελεῖς ὕπερ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῷν_50"> ethic dat. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπώλετο_50"> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 81"> <title>O. C.</title> 81</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βέβηκεν ἡμίν</quote> </cit>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπεχθής_50">, hateful to mankind for his involuntary crimes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπώλετο_50_2">, ‘died,’ not merely ‘was disgraced’ (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 59">cp. 59</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλούμεθ’</quote>): she is speaking of the <emph>deaths</emph> which had left them alone. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπειτα_50"> in 53 is merely ‘then,’ ‘in the next place,’ and need not mean that Oedipus died before Iocasta. Here Soph. follows the outline of the epic version, acc. to which Oed. died at Thebes: see Introd. to <title>Oed. Tyr.</title> pp. xvi ff. The poet of the <title>Odyssey</title> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 11.275">11. 275</ref>) makes him survive his consort's suicide, and no version appears to have assumed the contrary. The <title>Antigone</title> knows nothing of his exile from Thebes, or of the sacred honour which surrounded the close of his life, as the story is told in the later <title>Oedipus at Colonus.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἀράξας_51">, ‘<emph>impelled by</emph> them to strike his eyes’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1236"> <title>O. T.</title> 1236</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκε</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τίνος ποτ’ αἰτίας;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοφώρων_51"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ αὐτὸς ἐφώρασεν</quote> (cp. fr. 768 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πλεῖστα φωρῶν αἰσχρὰ φωράσεις βροτῶν</quote>), detected by himself, when he insisted on investigating the murder of Laïus (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1065"> <title>O. T.</title> 1065</ref>). Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόφωρος = φωραθεὶς ἐπ’ αὐτῷ τῷ ἔργῳ</quote>, ‘taken in the act’: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.38">Thuc. 6.38</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κολάζων, μὴ μόνον αὐτοφώρους</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπὸν γὰρ ἐπιτυγχάνειν</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ καὶ ὧν βούλονται μέν, δύνανται δ’ οὔ</quote>: and so in the adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτοφώρῳ λαμβάνειν</quote> etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀράξας_51">, with the golden brooches (<quote xml:lang="grc">περόναι</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1276"> <title>O. T.</title> 1276</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρασσ’ ἐπαίρων βλέφαρα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸς_χερί_51">, emphatic, like <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς πρὸς αὑτοῦ</quote> (1177), but not strictly pleonastic, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτός_51">=‘by his own act,’ i.e. ‘of his free will,’ while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτ_χερί_51"> refers to the instrument, ‘with hand turned against himself.’ Cp. on 56. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλοῦν_ἔπος_53">, a two-fold name (for the same person): as conversely <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 209">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 209</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ δὲ μήτηρ...Θέμις | καὶ Γαῖα, πολλῶν ὀνομάτων μορφὴ μία</quote> </cit>. Valckenär on Eur. <title>Phoen.</title>p. 153 cites Heracleitus <title>Alleg. Hom.</title> 21 (of Hera) <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦν ὄνομα φύσεως καὶ συμβιώσεως</quote>, ‘a name signifying at once birth and marriage’ (since she was <foreign xml:lang="lat">coniunx lovis et soror</foreign>). Seneca <title>Oed. Tyr.</title> 389 <quote xml:lang="lat">mixtumque nomen coniugis, nati, patris.</quote> (The feeble <foreign xml:lang="lat">v. l.</foreign> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐμοὶ δὲ μήτηρ...Θέμις | καὶ Γαῖα, πολλῶν
									ὀνομάτων μορφὴ μία

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάθος_53"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπος</quote> meant, ‘a double calamity,’ since both mother and wife perished.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτάναισι_53">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1264"> <title>O. T.</title> 1264</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεκταῖσιν αἰώραισιν ἐμπεπλεγμένην</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύο_μίαν_55">: <ref target="cl13">see on 13</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοκτονοῦντε_55"> is not literally, ‘slaying themselves,’ or ‘slaying each other,’ but, ‘slaying with their own hands’: the context explains that the person whom each so slew was his own brother. So either (1) <emph>suicide</emph>, or (2) <emph>slaying of kinsfolk</emph>, can be expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθέντης, αὐτοκτόνος, αὐτοσφαγής, αὐτοφόνος</quote>, etc. The compound merely expresses that the deed is done with one's own hand, implying that such a use of one's own hand is unnatural. <emph>The</emph> object of the deed may be one's own life, or another's. This ambiguity of the compound is illustrated by 1175 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόχειρ δ’ αἱμάσσεται</quote>.—XO. <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερα πατρῴας ἢ πρὸς οἰκείας χερός</quote>; ‘by his father's hand, or by his own?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατειργάσαντ_57">, plur. verb with dual subject, as oft., even when another verb with the same subject is dual, as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 6.1.47">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 6.1.47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἰδέτην...ἠσπάσαντο</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 294">Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 294</ref>E <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε παιδία ἤστην ...ἠπίστασθε</quote>: see <ref n="Soph. OC 343"> <title>O. C.</title> 343</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαλλήλοιν_χεροῖν_57">, ‘with mutual hands,’—each brother lifting his hands against the other. It is hard to believe that Soph. would here have written </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἀλλήλοιν_57">, ‘against each other,’ when </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χεροῖν_57"> would seem a weak addition, and the double </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οιν_57"> would be brought into harsh relief by the independence of the two words. The verse is in every way better if we can read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαλλήλοιν_57"> as an epithet of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χεροῖν_57_2">. Now we know that the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάλληλος</quote> was in common use at least as early as the 2nd century B.C. In the extant literature it seems always to correspond with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοις</quote> as=‘<emph>one on top of another</emph>’ (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 23.47"> <title>Od.</title> 23.47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κείατ’ ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοισι</quote> </cit>), being used to mean, ‘in close order’ or ‘in rapid sequence’ (as Polyb. 11. 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐπαλλήλοις τάξεσι</quote>, in close ranks: Alciphro <title>Epp.</title> 3. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ἐπαλλήλους πληγάς</quote>, the showers of blows). An exception might, indeed, be supposed in Philo Judaeus <title>De Mose</title> 3, p. 692, where he is saying that the continuity of human record has been broken <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τὰς ἐν ὕδασι καὶ πυρὶ γενομένας συνεχεῖς καὶ ἐπαλλήλους φθοράς</quote>: which Adrian Turnebus rendered, <quote xml:lang="lat">propter illas eluvionum et exustionum continuas et <emph>alternas</emph> (‘mutual’) interneciones.</quote> But Philo was evidently (I think) using <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάλληλος</quote> in its ordinary sense, and meant merely, ‘owing to <emph>the continuous and rapid succession</emph> of calamities by flood and fire.’ It by no means follows, however, that a poet of the 5th cent. B.C. could not have used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάλληλος</quote> in a sense corresponding with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοις</quote> as= ‘<emph>against</emph> each other,’—the more frequent and familiar sense of the words, as in the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοισιν ἰόντες, ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοισι φέρον πολύδακρυν Ἄρηα</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 3.132"> <title>Il.</title> 3.132</ref>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 50">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοισιν αἴρεσθαι δόρυ</quote> </cit>. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάλληλος</quote> here may have been partly prompted by a reminiscence of <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 931">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 931</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτελεύ|τασαν ὑπ’ ἀλλαλοφόνοις χερσὶν ὁμοσπόροισιν</quote> </cit> (cp. <ref n="Xen. Hiero 3. 8">Xen. <title>Hier.</title> 3. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφοὺς... ἀλληλοφόνους</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_αὖ_58">. Though in L </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_58"> has been inserted by a later hand, it is found in A, and its omission by the first hand in L (which has made other such oversights) cannot weigh much against it. Some of the recent edd. omit it: but the effect of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_αὖ_58_2"> without it would here be intolerably abrupt. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_58"> (‘in our turn’) <ref n="Soph. Ant. 7">cp. 7</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνα_δή_58">, ‘<emph>all</emph> alone’: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1063"> <title>Tr.</title> 1063</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνη με δὴ καθεῖλε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 992"> <title>Ai.</title> 992</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁπάντων δή</quote> </cit>: so esp. with superlatives, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 858"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 858</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανύστατον δή</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.50">Thuc. 1.50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγίστη δή</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψῆφον_60">, the pebble used in voting, then, the vote; here (as below, 632) applied to the resolve or decree of an absolute monarch. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 606"> <title>O. T.</title> 606</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μ’ ἁπλῇ κτάνῃς | ψήφῳ, διπλῇ δέ</quote> </cit>, i.e. not by thine own royal voice alone, but by mine also.</gloss>

data.quote: μή μ’ ἁπλῇ κτάνῃς | ψήφῳ, διπλῇ δέ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυράννων_60">, i.e. Creon: allusive plur., as 67: <title>O.T.</title> 366 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτη_60">, the <emph>powers</emph> of the king, as 173 (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 392"> <title>O. C.</title> 392</ref> n.). The disjunctive </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_60"> (for which Axt proposed </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_60">) means: ‘if we offend against this edict, or (in any way) against the royal powers.’ It could not mean: ‘if we infringe his <emph>edict</emph>, or (by persistence after warning) come into conflict with his <emph>power.</emph>’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_μὲν_61"> is not governed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννοεῖν_61">, but is adv., ‘on the one hand,’ answered by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπειτα_δ_61"> (63) instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο δέ</quote>, as elsewhere by <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ αὖθις</quote> (167), <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἄλλο</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 605"> <title>O. T.</title> 605</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1346"> <title>Ph.</title> 1346</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 441"> <title>O. C.</title> 441</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_61">, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μαχουμένα_61">, marks the intention of nature as expressed in sex,—‘showing that we were not meant to strive with men.’ This might be illustrated by Arist. 's phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλεται ἡ φύσις ποιεῖν τι</quote>, in regard to nature's intention or tendency (<title>De Anim. Gen.</title> 4. 10, etc.). </gloss>
