<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕνεκ_63">, ‘that’ (as <ref n="Soph. OT 708"> <title>O. T.</title> 708</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1395"> <title>O. C.</title> 1395</ref>, and oft.): <emph>not</emph>, ‘because,’ as some take it, supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούειν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_63">, as from the head and fount of authority; so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 264"> <title>El.</title> 264</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ τῶνδ’ ἄρχομαι</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 67"> <title>O. C.</title> 67</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούειν_63">, infin. expressing consequence (‘so that we should hearken’), without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>, as 1076 <quote xml:lang="grc">ληφθῆναι</quote>. We find <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούω τινός</quote>, ‘to obey a person,’ but not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούω τι</quote>, as ‘to hear (and obey) a command.’ Here </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_63"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλγίονα_63"> are accusatives of respect, ‘in regard to these things,’ as <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 340"> <title>El.</title> 340</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν κρατούντων ἐστὶ <emph>πάντ</emph>’ ἀκουστέα</quote>, ‘I must obey the rulers in all things.’ If the accusatives were objective, the sense would be, ‘to hear these taunts’ (or, ‘to be called these names’): cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 607"> <title>Ph.</title> 607</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1235"> <title>Ai.</title> 1235</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_οὖν_65">. <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> (with no answering <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>) emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote> (<ref target="cl11">see on 11</ref>), while <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> has its separate force, ‘therefore,’ as in <ref n="Soph. OT 483"> <title>O. T.</title> 483</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 664"> <title>O. C.</title> 664</ref>. The composite <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν οὖν</quote> (‘nay rather’) would be unfitting here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_χθονός_65">, the gods below (451), and also the departed spirit of Polyneices,—which, like the spirit of the unburied Patroclus (<ref n="Hom. Il. 23.65"> <title>Il.</title> 23.65</ref>), can have no rest till sepulture has been given to the corpse. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1775"> <title>O. C.</title> 1775</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ κατὰ γῆς</quote> (Oedipus): for the allusive plur., <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1419"> <title>El.</title> 1419</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσιν οἱ γᾶς ὑπαὶ κείμενοι</quote> </cit> (Agamemnon). In ref. to the nether world, Attic writers regularly join <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> with <emph>gen.</emph>, not dat.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 841"> <title>El.</title> 841</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ γαίας... ἀνάσσει</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1097"> <title>Tr.</title> 1097</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόν θ’ ὑπὸ χθονὸς | Ἅιδου...σκύλακα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 249a"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 249 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὑπὸ γῆς δικαστήρια</quote> </cit>. Indeed <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> with <emph>dat.</emph> is altogether rare in Attic prose, except as meaning (<emph>a</emph>) under an authority, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ νόμοις</quote>, or (<emph>b</emph>) under a class, as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 205B">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 205B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ὑπὸ πάσαις ταῖς τέχναις ἐργασίαι</quote> </cit>. In poetry, Attic and other, it is freq. also in the local sense: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 337">cp. 337</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ οἴδμασιν.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote: αἱ ὑπὸ πάσαις ταῖς τέχναις ἐργασίαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιάζομαι_τάδε_65">, pass. with cogn. acc., as 1073 <quote xml:lang="grc">βιάζονται τάδε</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1366"> <title>Ph.</title> 1366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄμ’ ἀναγκάζεις τάδε</quote>; and below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 219."> 219.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_βεβῶσι_67"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τυράννων</foreign> in 60, i.e. Creon. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βεβῶσι_67">, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1094"> <title>El.</title> 1094</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μοίρᾳ μὲν οὐκ ἐν ἐσθλᾷ | βεβῶσαν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1358"> <title>O. C.</title> 1358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πόνῳ | ταὐτῷ βεβηκώς</quote> </cit>. Elsewhere Soph. has the simple phrase: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1352"> <title>Ai.</title> 1352</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύειν...τῶν ἐν τέλει</quote> </cit>, and so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 385"> <title>Ph.</title> 385</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 925.">925.</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἐν τέλει</quote> are ‘those in authority,’ —<quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος</quote> meaning ‘final or decisive power,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.118">Thuc. 4.118</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος ἔχοντες ἰόντων</quote> </cit>, ‘let the envoys go as plenipotentiaries.’ Pindar's <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος δωδεκάμηνον</quote> (‘an <emph>office</emph> held for a year’), <title>N.</title> 11. 10, is perh. poetical. As synonyms for <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἐν τέλει</quote> we find (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τὰ τέλη ἔχοντες</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 5.47">Thuc. 5.47</ref>, and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τέλη</quote> simply,—‘the authorities,’ sometimes with masc. part., as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.15">Thuc. 4.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξεν αὐτοῖς...τὰ τέλη καταβάντας βουλεύειν</quote> </cit>. <title>Hellen.</title> 6. 5 has <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μέγιστα τέλη</quote>, ‘the highest magistrates’ (like <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.10">Thuc. 1.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω τῶν βασιλέων καὶ τῶν μάλιστα ἐν τέλει</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 2">cp. 2</ref>. 10). </gloss>

data.quote: μοίρᾳ μὲν οὐκ ἐν ἐσθλᾷ | βεβῶσαν

ἔξω τῶν βασιλέων καὶ τῶν μάλιστα ἐν
									τέλει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πράσσειν_67">: for the art. in the 6th place, with its noun in the next v., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78">cp. 78</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 231"> <title>O. T.</title> 231</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 265"> <title>O. C.</title> 265</ref>, 351: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 674."> <title>Ph.</title> 674.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περισσὰ_πρ_67">.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 780">cp. 780</ref>: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 617"> <title>Tr.</title> 617</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περισσὰ δρᾶν, = πολλὰ πράττειν, πολυπραγμονεῖν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_οὐδ_67"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνόητόν ἐστι</foreign>: prop. of persons, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 553"> <title>Tr.</title> 553</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναῖκα νοῦν ἔχουσαν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_69">, ‘<emph>yet</emph>,’—at some future time: so <ref n="Soph. El. 66"> <title>El.</title> 66</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κἄμ’ ἐπαυχῶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστρον ὣς λάμψειν ἔτι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 257"> <title>Tr.</title> 257</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δουλώσειν ἔτι</quote> </cit> (he vowed that he would <emph>yet</emph> enslave him). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_δρῴης_69"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσοις</quote>): for such substitution of a synonym cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 54"> <title>O. T.</title> 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρξεις...κρατεῖς</quote> </cit> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1501"> <title>O. C.</title> 1501</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφὴς ...ἐμφανής</quote> </cit>. With </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῴης_69"> we have a double </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_69">, the first after the negative, the second after the emphatic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ γ’</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 339"> <title>O. T.</title> 339</ref> n.—Objection has been made to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδέως_69"> on the ground that it ought to mean, ‘with pleasure <emph>to yourself.</emph>’ Wecklein, indeed, takes that to be the sense, supposing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_69"> to be misplaced; i.e. the proper order would have been, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδέως_ἐμοῦ_69"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: but the position of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦ_69"> in the verse sufficiently shows that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_69_2"> must go with it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_γ_69"> (Meineke) would leave </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέτα_69"> awkward: and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσμένης_69"> (Lehrs) would not have been displaced by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδέως_69_2">, which the old scholia confirm. All the difficulty has arisen from failing to distinguish between (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν ἡδέως δρῴης</quote>, and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν μετὰ ἐμοῦ ἡδέως δρῴης</quote>. In (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδέως</quote> could mean only, ‘agreeably to yourself.’ But in (2) it is ambiguous; for the statement is equivalent to saying, ‘your co-operation with me would not be agreeable’; i.e. to you, or to me, or to either of us,—as the context may imply. Here, as the emphatic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοῦ_γε_69"> indicates, she means <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδέως ἐμοί</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 426C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 426C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς...ἂν σφᾶς...ἥδιστα θεραπεύῃ</quote> </cit>, i.e. ‘whoever serves them <emph>most acceptably</emph>’ (not, ‘<emph>most gladly</emph>’). <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 79">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 79</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δῆτ’ ἂν ἥδιστ’ αὐτὸν ἐπεγείραιμι</quote> </cit>; (i.e. most pleasantly <emph>for him</emph>). </gloss>

data.quote: πῶς δῆτ’ ἂν ἥδιστ’ αὐτὸν ἐπεγείραιμι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθ_71"> from <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμί</quote>: ‘be such as thou wilt,’—show what character thou wilt. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1049"> <title>Ph.</title> 1049</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ γὰρ τοιούτων δεῖ, τοιοῦτός εἰμ’ ἐγώ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1271"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1271</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτος ἦσθα τοῖς λόγοισι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1024"> <title>El.</title> 1024</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσκει τοιαύτη νοῦν δῑ αἰῶνος μένειν</quote> </cit>: <title>O.T.</title> 435 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς τοιοίδ’ ἔφυμεν.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote: ἄσκει τοιαύτη νοῦν δῑ αἰῶνος μένειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="όποία_δοκεῖ_71">=(<quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαύτη</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποίᾳ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποίαν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι δοκεῖ σοι</quote>, the relative being attracted into the case of the suppressed antecedent. This was the more natural since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποία σοι δοκεῖ</quote>, ‘of any kind you please,’ was felt as almost one word, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποιαδήποτε</quote>; just so <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς βούλει</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">quivis</foreign>), instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος ὃν βούλει</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 517A">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 517A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήποτέ τις τῶν νῦν ἔργα τοιαῦτα ἐργάσηται οἷα τούτων ὃς βούλει εἴργασται</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Plat. Crat. 432a"> <title>Crat.</title> 432 A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ αὐτὰ τὰ δέκα ἢ ὅστις βούλει ἄλλος ἀριθμός</quote>.—Those who read </gloss>

data.quote:
μήποτέ τις τῶν νῦν ἔργα τοιαῦτα ἐργάσηται οἷα
									τούτων ὃς βούλει εἴργασται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθ_71_2"> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁποῖά_δοκεῖ_71"> compare <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1055"> <title>El.</title> 1055</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ σεαυτῇ τυγχάνεις δοκοῦσά τι | φρονεῖν, φρόνει τοιαῦτα</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδέναι</quote> is not <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονεῖν</quote>. In Attic, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσθ’ ὁποῖά σοι δοκεῖ</quote> could mean nothing but ‘know such things as seem good to thee.’ It could not mean (<emph>a</emph>) ‘Have such sentiments as seem good to thee’: nor (<emph>b</emph>) ‘Be wise in thine own wisdom.’ The Homeric phrases, <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπνυμένα εἰδώς</quote> (‘wise of heart’), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθεμίστια εἰδώς, ἤπια οἶδε</quote> (‘he has kindly feelings,’ <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.405"> <title>Od.</title> 13.405</ref>), etc., have no counterpart in the Attic usage of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδέναι</quote>. In 301 <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσσέβειαν εἰδέναι</quote>, and in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 960"> <title>Ph.</title> 960</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκοῦντος οὐδὲν εἰδέναι κακόν</quote>, the verb means simply ‘to know.’ </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ εἰ σεαυτῇ τυγχάνεις δοκοῦσά τι | φρονεῖν,
									φρόνει τοιαῦτα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάψω_72">, emphatic by place and pause: <ref target="cl46">see on 46</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφόν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλη_φίλου_72">, loved by him, as he is loved by me: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 267"> <title>Ai.</title> 267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς ἐν κοινοῖσι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 620"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 620</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφιλα παρ’ ἀφίλοις.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετ_κείσομαι_72">, i.e. in the same world of the dead (76). The repetition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετά_72"> serves to bring out the reciprocity of love more strongly: <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη μετ’ αὐτοῦ κείσομαι, μετὰ φίλου</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κειμένη</quote>),— instead of the simpler <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη μετὰ φίλου κείσομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσια_πανουργήσασ_74">: having broken a human law in a manner which the gods permit,—viz., in order to observe a divine law. Creon uses the word <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργίας</quote> below, 300. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσια_74"> is peculiarly appropriate since the word was familiar where duty to heaven was distinguished from duty to man: cp. Polyb. 23. 10 <quote xml:lang="grc">παραβῆναι καὶ τὰ πρὸς τοὺς ἀνθρώπους δίκαια καὶ τὰ πρὸς τοὺς θεοὺσὅσια</quote>. The phrase is an <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξύμωρον</quote> (a paradox with a point), like ‘splendide mendax’; i.e. the qualification (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσια</quote>) seems contrary to the essence of the thing qualified. Cp. Milton (<title>Tetrachordon</title>), ‘Men of the most renowned virtue have sometimes by transgressing most truly kept the law’; which is not an oxymoron, because the words, ‘most truly,’ suggest an explanation by showing that ‘kept’ is not used in its ordinary sense.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_κτλ_74">: (I will obey gods rather than men), <emph>for</emph> the other world is more to me than this. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐνθάδε_74"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἢ τοῖς ἐνθάδε</foreign>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 567"> <title>O. C.</title> 567</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐς αὔριον | οὐδὲν πλέον μοι ς οῦ</quote> </cit> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ σοὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μέτεστιν ἡμέρας</quote> (n.). </gloss>

data.quote: τῆς ἐς αὔριον | οὐδὲν πλέον μοι ς οῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείσομαι_76">, though we have had the word in 73. For other examples of such repetition, see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title> 554</ref>, and cp. below 163 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθωσαν</quote>, 167 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου</quote>: 207 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκ γ’ ἐμοῦ</quote>, 210 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἐμοῦ</quote>: 613, 618 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρπει</quote>): 614, 625 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_δ_76"> is better than Elmsley's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_δ_76">, since the primary contrast is between their points of view: ‘if it seems right <emph>to thee</emph>, dishonour the dead,’ rather than, ‘do <emph>thou</emph>, if it seems right, dishonour the dead.’ Remark, too, that the <emph>simple</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δοκεῖ_76"> (without dat. of pron.) is usually a polite formula, ‘if it is pleasing to you (as well as to me)’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 526"> <title>Ph.</title> 526</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ δοκεῖ, πλέωμεν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 645"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 645</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ δοκεῖ, χωρῶμεν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1402"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1402</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δοκεῖ, στείχωμεν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἔντιμα_76">, the honoured things of the gods; the laws which are <emph>theirs</emph> (454 <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν νόμιμα</quote>), held in honour (by them and by men). <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τοῖς θεοῖς ἔντιμα</quote> (25, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 239"> <title>El.</title> 239</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔντιμος τούτοις</quote> </cit>) would have presented the gods only as <emph>observers</emph>, not also as <emph>authors</emph>, of the laws.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτιμάσασ_ἔχε_76"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 22">cp. 22</ref>), ‘be guilty of dishonouring’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 588"> <title>Ai.</title> 588</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ προδοὺς ἡμᾶς γένῃ</quote> </cit>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 957"> <title>O. T.</title> 957</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_78"> is slightly emphasised by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_78"> (<ref target="cl11">see on 11</ref>), which goes closely with it, and does not here answer to the following </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_78">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτιμα_ποιοῦμαι_78"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀτιμάζω</foreign>: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 584 <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ οὐδενὸς ποιεῖ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρᾶν_78">: <ref target="cl67">see on 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ | ...πράσσειν</quote>. The inf. with art. is strictly an acc. of respect (‘as for the acting..., I am incapable of it’), but is practically equiv. to the simple inf., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμήχανος δρᾶν</quote>: see n. on <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 47"> <title>O. C.</title> 47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐμοὶ...τοὐξανιστάναι... | ...ἐστὶ θάρσος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_προὔχοιο_80">, ‘thou canst make these excuses’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ βούλοιο</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1394">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1394</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίροιτ’ ἄν, εἰ χαίροιτ’· ἐγὼ δ’ ἐπεύχομαι</quote> </cit>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">προέχεσθαι</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">προφασίζεσθαι</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.140">Thuc. 1.140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπερ μάλιστα προὔχονται</quote> </cit>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχημα = πρόφασις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 525"> <title>El.</title> 525</ref>).</gloss>

data.quote:
χαίροιτ’ ἄν, εἰ χαίροιτ’· ἐγὼ δ’
									ἐπεύχομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὴ_80">, ‘now,’ as the next thing to be done: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 173">cp. 173</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφον_χώσουσ_80">, prop. to raise a mound on the spot where the remains of the dead had been burned: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 7.336"> <title>Il.</title> 7.336</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβον δ’ ἀμφὶ πυρὴν ἕνα χεύομεν</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 24.799"> <title>Il.</title> 24.799</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆμ’ ἔχεαν</quote> </cit>,—after placing the bones in an urn (<quote xml:lang="grc">λάρναξ</quote>), and depositing this in a grave (<quote xml:lang="grc">κάπετος</quote>). She speaks as if she hoped to give him regular sepulture. This is ultimately done by Creon's command (1203 <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβον...χώσαντες</quote>), though the rites which Antigone herself is able to perform are only symbolical (255, 429). </gloss>

data.quote: τύμβον δ’ ἀμφὶ πυρὴν ἕνα χεύομεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_ταλαίνης_82">, ‘alas for thee, unhappy one’: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1399"> <title>O. C.</title> 1399</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι κελεύθου τῆς τ’ ἐμῆς δυσπραξίας, | οἴμοι δ’ ἑταίρων</quote> </cit>: but the <emph>nom.</emph> when the ref. is to the speaker, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1143"> <title>El.</title> 1143</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι τάλαινα τῆς ἐμῆς πάλαι τροφῆς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οἴμοι κελεύθου τῆς τ’ ἐμῆς δυσπραξίας, | οἴμοι
									δ’ ἑταίρων
οἴμοι τάλαινα τῆς ἐμῆς πάλαι τροφῆς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_προτάρβει_82"> (or, as some write it, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐμοῦ</quote>) is clearly right. If we read <quote xml:lang="grc">μή <emph>μου</emph> προτάρβει</quote>, then the emphasis is solely on the verbal notion. ‘I fear for thee.’—‘<title>Fear</title> not so: make thine own fate prosperous.’ But the stress on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_σόν_82"> renders it certain that the poet intended a corresponding stress on the preceding pronoun: ‘Fear not for <emph>me</emph>— make <emph>thine own</emph> fate prosperous.’ And </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_μοῦ_82"> is no more objectionable than <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ’γώ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 472"> <title>El.</title> 472</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προτάρβει_82">, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 89"> <title>Tr.</title> 89</ref> (with gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρός</quote>). Distinguish <quote xml:lang="grc">προδείσας</quote>, ‘afraid beforehand,’ <ref n="Soph. OT 90"> <title>O. T.</title> 90</ref> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξόρθου_82"> here=‘straighten out,’ i.e. guide in a straight or prosperous course: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 167">cp. 167</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου πόλιν</quote>, 675 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθουμένων</quote>. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξορθόω</quote> is usu. ‘to correct, amend’ (<ref n="Plat. Tim. 90D">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 90D</ref>); more rarely, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνορθόω</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 51"> <title>O. T.</title> 51</ref>), ‘to set upright’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πεσόν</quote>, <ref n="Plat. Laws 862C">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 862C</ref>). In the figurative uses of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθός</quote> and its derivatives the context must always guide our choice between the notion of ‘upright’ and that of ‘straight.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γε_84">. In this combination </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_84"> is like our ‘well’; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖν_84">=‘at any rate’ (i.e. if you <emph>must</emph> do it); and <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the word which it follows. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 233"> <title>El.</title> 233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὖν εὐνοίᾳ γ’ αὐδῶ</quote> </cit>, ‘well, at any rate (i.e. though you will not listen to me) it is with <emph>good-will</emph> that I speak.’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1305."> <title>Ph.</title> 1305.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρυφῆ_δέ_84">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ = ἀλλά</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.86">Thuc. 4.86</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐπὶ κακῷ, ἐπ’ ἐλευθερώσει δέ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: οὐκ ἐπὶ κακῷ, ἐπ’ ἐλευθερώσει δέ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_δ_84">, adv., <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεύσω</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1288"> <title>Ai.</title> 1288</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἦν ὁ πράσσων ταῦτα, σὺν δ’ ἐγὼ παρών.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅδ’ ἦν ὁ πράσσων ταῦτα, σὺν δ’ ἐγὼ
									παρών.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔτως_84"> (adv. of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote>, see on <title>O.T.</title> 931), in just that way—‘likewise’: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1040"> <title>Tr.</title> 1040</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’ αὔτως ὥς μ’ ὤλεσε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταύδα_86">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔργον</quote>, ‘<emph>denounce</emph> it.’ The word occurs only here, the pres. used in this sense being <quote xml:lang="grc">καταγορεύω</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.68">Thuc. 4.68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καταγορεύει τις ξυνειδὼς τοῖς ἑτέροις τὸ ἐπιβούλευμα</quote> </cit>): aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατεῖπον.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
καταγορεύει τις ξυνειδὼς τοῖς ἑτέροις τὸ
									ἐπιβούλευμα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλόν_86">: this Ionic form occurs also <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1196"> <title>Tr.</title> 1196</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὸν δ’ ἄρσεν’ ἐκτεμόνθ’ ὁμοῦ | ἄγριον ἔλαιον</quote> </cit>, though in no other places of tragedy. And Soph. also used the epic form <quote xml:lang="grc">πουλύπους</quote> in a trimeter, fr. 286 <quote xml:lang="grc">νόει πρὸς ἀνδρί, σῶμα πουλύπους ὅπως | πέτρᾳ, κ.τ.λ.</quote> Porson on <ref n="Eur. Hec. 618">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 618</ref> wished to read in our verse either <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖον</quote> (which is inadmissible, as Nauck observes, <title>Eur. Stud.</title> 2. 27), or <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον</quote>, which some edd. receive. But </gloss>

data.quote:
πολλὸν δ’ ἄρσεν’ ἐκτεμόνθ’ ὁμοῦ | ἄγριον
									ἔλαιον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὸν_86"> is manifestly better than <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σιγῶσ_86"> is explained by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐὰν_τάδε_86">, while the thought is strengthened by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσι_86">: she is to tell the news <emph>to all.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θερμὴν_ψυχροῖσι_88"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘thou hast a hot heart for chilling deeds’; i.e. in thy rash enthusiasm thou art undertaking deeds which might well chill thy soul with a presage of death. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_88"> with dat. here=‘with a view to’ (<cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.5.18">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.5.18</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τούτοις ἐθύσαντο</quote> </cit>): not ‘in,’ like <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἔργοις πᾶσι</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1268"> <title>O. C.</title> 1268</bibl> </cit> (n.). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 157">Cp. 157</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θερμήν_88"> has suggested <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχρά</quote>, and the thought of the <emph>dead</emph> has helped (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 621"> <title>O. C.</title> 621</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυς ψυχρός</quote> </cit>). In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 693">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 693</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δείματα</quote> </cit> are said <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφάκει κέντρῳ ψύχειν ψυχάν</quote> (to chill,—where Meineke's <quote xml:lang="grc">ψήχειν</quote>, ‘to wear,’ is improbable). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 1191">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 1191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγερὰ τάδε κρυερὰ πάθεα</quote> </cit>: Eur. fr. 908 <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυερὰ Διόθεν | θανάτου πεμφθεῖσα τελευτή</quote>. For the verbal contrast, Schütz cp. <title>Ad Herenn.</title> 4. 15. 21 <quote xml:lang="lat">in re frigidissima cales, in ferventissima friges</quote>, and <title>A. P.</title> 465 <quote xml:lang="lat">ardentem frigidus Aetnam Insiluit.</quote> He thinks that Ismene (hurt by vv. 86 f.) implies, ‘and a cold heart for thy living sister,’ to which Ant. rejoins by v. 89. But <quote xml:lang="grc">θερμήν</quote> is not ‘affectionate,’ and Ant. seems to mean simply, ‘love and piety banish fear.’— Some understand, ‘with a view to <emph>joyless</emph> things’ (cp. on 650): but this would be weak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁδεῖν_89">, an aor. inf. used in <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.173"> <title>Il.</title> 3.173</ref>, as also by Solon (fr. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργμασιν ἐν μεγάλοις πᾶσιν ἁδεῖν χαλεπόν</quote>), Her., and This Pind. is the only place in classical Attic where any part of the aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕαδον</quote> occurs. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_γ_90">, yes, if (besides having the wish to please them) you shall <emph>also</emph> have the power. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_καὶ_90"> goes closely with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_δυνήσει_90">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 283"> <title>O. T.</title> 283</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ τρίτ’ ἐστί</quote> </cit>. Such cases must be carefully distinguished from those in which </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_καί_90"> form a single expression; see <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref> p. 296. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκοῦν_91"> (‘well, then’) precedes, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὴ_91"> is best taken as giving precision to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅταν_91">,—‘so soon as.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπαύσομαι_91">, ‘I will cease <emph>forthwith</emph>’: so <ref n="Soph. Trach. 587"> <title>Tr.</title> 587</ref>. Cp. the perf., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1279"> <title>Ph.</title> 1279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ μή τι πρὸς καιρὸν λέγων | κυρῶ, πέπαυμαι</quote>, ‘I have done.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχήν_92">, adv., ‘to begin with,’ ‘at all,’—in <emph>negative</emph> sentences; often placed, as here, before the negative word; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 439"> <title>El.</title> 439</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχὴν δ’ ἂν...οὐκ ἂν...ἐπέστεφε</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1239"> <title>Ph.</title> 1239</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχὴν κλύειν ἂν οὐδ’ ἅπαξ ἐβουλόμην</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.39">Her. 3.39</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφη χαριεῖσθαι μᾶλλον ἀποδιδοὺς τὰ ἔλαβε ἢ ἀρχὴν μηδὲ λαβών</quote> </cit>. In <emph>affirmative</emph> sentences the art. is usually added: <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 3.20">Andoc. or. 3 § 20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξῆν γὰρ αὐτοῖς καὶ τὴν ἀρχὴν ἐῶσιν Ὀρχομενίους αὐτονόμους εἰρήνην ἄγειν</quote> </cit>: so <ref n="Isoc. 15.272">Isocr. or. 15 § 272.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔφη χαριεῖσθαι μᾶλλον ἀποδιδοὺς τὰ ἔλαβε ἢ
									ἀρχὴν μηδὲ λαβών

ἐξῆν γὰρ αὐτοῖς καὶ τὴν ἀρχὴν ἐῶσιν
									Ὀρχομενίους αὐτονόμους εἰρήνην ἄγειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθαρεῖ_93">, pass.: so, from liquid stems, 230 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγυνεῖ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 272"> <title>O. T.</title> 272</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθερεῖσθαι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1155"> <title>Ai.</title> 1155</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πημανούμενος</quote> </cit>. The ‘midd.’ fut. in <quote xml:lang="grc">ς</quote> affords numerous examples, as below, 210, 637, 726: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 672"> <title>O. T.</title> 672</ref> and <ref n="Soph. OC 1185"> <title>O. C.</title> 1185</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἐμοῦ_93">, ‘on my part’ (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 95">cp. 95</ref>), rather than simply ‘by me’ (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 63">cp. 63</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθρὰτῷ_δίκῃ_94">, ‘thou wilt be subject to the lasting enmity of the dead.’ The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκείσει_94"> means literally, ‘<emph>wilt be attached to</emph>...,’ i.e., ‘<emph>wilt be brought into a lasting relation with</emph>’; and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχθρά_94"> defines the nature of that relation. The sense is thus virtually the same as if we had, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ θανὼν ἐχθρός σοι προσκείσεται</quote>, ‘the enmity of the dead will cleave to thee.’ The convertible use of <quote xml:lang="grc">προσκεῖσεθαι</quote> is illustrated by 1243 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὶ πρόσκειται κακόν</quote>, as compared with <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1040"> <title>El.</title> 1040</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ σὺ πρόσκεισαι κακῷ</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 240"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 240</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τῳ πρόσκειμαι χρηστῷ</quote> </cit>. Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">προσκείσει</quote> expresses merely the establishment of the permanent relation between the two persons. It does not mean, ‘you will be brought, as his foe, <emph>into dependence</emph> on him’ (i.e. under the power of his curse); as in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 185">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 185</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ πρόσκειμαι δούλα τλάμων</quote> </cit>; ‘to whom have I been assigned as a slave?’ (i.e. by the casting of lots:— the answer is, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύς που κεῖσαι κλήρου</quote>). Nor, again, ‘you will <emph>press upon</emph> the dead as his foe,’ i.e. be hostile and grievous to him: for, as <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκῃ</quote> shows, the punishment is to be hers, not his. The idea might have been expressed in a converse form by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν θανόντα ἐχθρὸν προσθήσει</quote> (cf. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 2.4.12">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2.4.12</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔα_95">, one syll. by synizesis, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1451"> <title>O. T.</title> 1451</ref> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_δυσββουλίαν_95">, the folly proceeding from me, the folly <emph>on my part</emph>, for which I bear the sole blame: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 619"> <title>El.</title> 619</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ γὰρ ἐκ σοῦ δυσμένεια καὶ τὰ σὰ | ἔργ’ ἐξαναγκάζει με</quote> </cit>, ‘it is the enmity on thy part,’ etc. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 631"> <title>Tr.</title> 631</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ πρὼ| λέγοις ἂν τὸν πόθον τὸν ἐξ ἐμοῦ, | πρὶν εἰδέναι τἀκεῖθεν εῖ ποθούμεθα</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 453"> <title>O. C.</title> 453</ref> n. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἡ γὰρ ἐκ σοῦ δυσμένεια καὶ τὰ σὰ | ἔργ’
									ἐξαναγκάζει με

μὴ πρὼ| λέγοις ἂν τὸν πόθον τὸν ἐξ ἐμοῦ, |
									πρὶν εἰδέναι τἀκεῖθεν εῖ ποθούμεθα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τοῦτο_95">, ironical: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 376"> <title>El.</title> 376</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(Electra in answer to her sister's warnings) </note> φέρ’ εἰπὲ δὴ τὸ δεινόν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείσομαι_οὐ_95">: for the position of the negative (which belongs to the verb, not to <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτον</quote>), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 223">cp. 223</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 125"> <title>O. C.</title> 125</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέβα γὰρ οὐκ ἄν</quote> </cit>. We still write <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> here, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ</quote>, because the sentence runs on without pause: but 255 <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβήρης μὲν οὔ</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 545"> <title>Ai.</title> 545</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταρβήσει γὰρ οὔ</quote> </cit>, since in each case a comma can follow the negative.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_οὐ_95">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, because the principal verb <quote xml:lang="grc">πείσομαι</quote> is negatived: <title>O.T.</title> 283 n. She means, ‘even if I have to die, at least I shall not suffer the worst of evils; which is not death, but an ignoble death.’ Cp. frag. adesp. 61 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ κατθανεῖν γὰρ δεινόν, ἀλλ’ αἰσχρῶς θανεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_φίλη_99">, ‘but truly dear to thy friends,’—i.e. both to the dead brother and to the living sister. The words are especially a parting assurance (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθι_99">) that <emph>Ismene's</emph> love is undiminished. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὀρθῶς</emph> = ἀληθῶς</quote>, as Diphilus frag. incert. 20 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὀρθῶς εὐγενῆ</quote>. Others make <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη</quote> active,—‘a true friend to thy friends’ (i.e. to Polyneices): which is certainly the fittest sense in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 609">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 609</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀπ’ εὐγενοῦς τινος | ῥίζης πέφυκας, τοῖς φίλοις τ’ ὀρθῶς φίλος</quote> </cit> (Orestes, when he devotes his life to save his friend's). But here the other view is decidedly preferable. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς ἀπ’ εὐγενοῦς τινος | ῥίζης πέφυκας, τοῖς
									φίλοις τ’ ὀρθῶς φίλος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀελίου_100">, Dor. for the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠελίου</quote>, with <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote> as usu. (808, <ref n="Soph. OC 1245"> <title>O. C.</title> 1245</ref>), though it is sometimes used with ă, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 835"> <title>Tr.</title> 835</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Med. 1252">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1252</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φάος_100">. Two constructions are possible; I prefer the first. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβᾳ φανὲν τὸ κάλλιστον φάος</quote>, the art. going with the superlative, which it emphasises,—‘the very fairest,’—a common use, as <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 289"> <title>Hipp.</title> 289</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἡ καλλίστη παρθένος αἰσχρὰ φανεῖται</quote>; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 17.415"> <title>Od.</title> 17.415</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μέν μοι δοκέεις ὁ κάκιστος Ἀχαιῶν</quote> </cit>. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Θήβᾳ φανὲν κάλλιστον φάος</quote>, the art. going with <quote xml:lang="grc">φανέν</quote>. This seems awkward. When a voc. is followed by a noun or partic. with art., this is normally in direct agreement with the voc., as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ἄνδρες...οἱ παρόντες</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Prot. 337C">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 337C</ref>), as if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ φανεῖσα</quote>. The Schol., who prefers this constr., shirks the difficulty by his paraphrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τῆς ἀκτῖνος τοῦ ἡλίου φῶς, τὸ φανὲν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ μέν μοι δοκέεις ὁ κάκιστος Ἀχαιῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑπταπύλῳ_100">: epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβη</quote> in <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.263"> <title>Od.</title> 11.263</ref>, <ref n="Hes. WD 161">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 161</ref>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκατόμπυλοι</quote> in <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.383"> <title>Il.</title> 9.383</ref> of <quote xml:lang="grc">Θῆβαι Αἰγύπτιαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_προτέρων_100">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1212">cp. 1212</ref>f.: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.10">Thuc. 1.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατείαν...μεγίστην...τῶν πρὸ αὐτῆς</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Tac. Hist. 1.50">Tacitus <title>Hist.</title> 1. 50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">solus omnium ante se principum.</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl>Milton <title>P. L.</title> 4. 322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>Adam, the goodliest man of men since born, | His sons, the fairest of her daughters Eve.</l> </quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl>Goethe <title>Hermann und Dorothea</title> 5. 101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="deu">Von ihren Schwestern die beste.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφάνθης_103"> with an echo of <quote xml:lang="grc">φανέν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 794"> <title>O.C.</title> 794</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμα | ...στόμωσιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσέας_103">, with <quote xml:lang="grc">υ^</quote> as <title>O.T.</title> 157, 188. So <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 4.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα ποτὲ χρυ^σέων Διὸς αἰητῶν πάρεδρος</quote> </cit>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">υ^</quote> was admitted by the lyrists, and from them borrowed by the dramatists, though only in lyrics, and even there only occasionally. Homer never shortens the <quote xml:lang="grc">υ</quote>: for, as <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσέῳ ἀνὰ σκήπτρῳ</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 1.15"> <title>Il.</title> 1.15</ref>) shows, the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσέης</quote> (etc.) must be treated as disyll. by synizesis.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἔνθα ποτὲ χρυ^σέων Διὸς αἰητῶν
									πάρεδρος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλέφαρον_103"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</foreign>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 543">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 543</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νυκτός τ’ ἀφεγγὲς βλέφαρον</quote> </cit> (the moon). Cp. Job iii. 9 (Revised Version), ‘neither let it behold the eyelids of the morning.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Διρκαίων_103">. The Dircè was on the w. of Thebes, the Ismenus on the E. : between them flowed the less famous Strophia: <cit> <bibl n="Call. Del. 76">Callim. <title>Hymn Del.</title> 76</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίρκη τε Στροφίη τε μελαμψηφῖδος ἔχουσαι | )Ισμηνοῦ χέρα πατρός</quote> </cit> (alluding to their common source S. of the town). Though the Ismenus, as the eastern stream, would have been more appropriately named here, the Dircè is preferred, as the representative river of Thebes: so Pindar, ‘the Dircaean swan,’ expresses ‘at Thebes and at Sparta’ by <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεέθροισί τε Δίρκας...καὶ παρ’ Εὐρώτᾳ</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. I. 1">Pind. <title>Isthm.</title> 1. 29</ref>). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 844">Cp. 844</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
Δίρκη τε Στροφίη τε μελαμψηφῖδος ἔχουσαι |
									)Ισμηνοῦ χέρα πατρός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_λεύκασπινφῶτα_106">, in a collective sense: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Πέρσης</quote>, the Persian army, <ref n="Hdt. 8.108">Her. 8.108</ref>, etc. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 90">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λεύκασπις λεώς</quote> </cit> (Dind. <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκοπρεπής</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1099">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1099</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύκασπιν εἰσορῶμεν Ἀργείων στρατόν</quote> </cit>. The round shield, painted white, which the Argive soldier carried on his left arm, is the <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκῆς χιόνος πτέρυξ</quote> of 114. The choice of <emph>white</emph> as the Argive colour may have been prompted by a popular association of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄργος</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργός</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀργόθεν_φῶτα_106">, and to suppose that, after the loss of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ, βάντα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">φῶτα</quote> were accidentally transposed. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title> 1088</ref> where <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένει ’πινικείῳ</quote> is certainly the right order, but the MSS. reverse it. (See also above on v. 29.) Dindorf reads <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ φῶτα βάντα</quote>, assuming tmesis: but tmesis of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> in Soph. occurs elsewhere only before <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1053"> <title>Tr.</title> 1053</ref>) or <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>, and there was no motive here for interposing <quote xml:lang="grc">φῶτα</quote>. Hermann reads <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργόθεν ἔκ</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἀργόθεν</quote>: but elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> comes before, not after, such forms (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Αἰσύμηθεν</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.304"> <title>Il.</title> 8.304</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἁλόθεν, ἐξ οὐρανόθεν</quote>, etc.). If <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργόθεν</quote> is not genuine, then it was probably a gloss on some other form in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐θεν</quote>. Had <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶς Πέλοπος</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">Δαναοῦ</quote>) been in the text, a scholiast would have been more apt to paraphrase with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπ’</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἄργους</quote>. This is against such conjectures as <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργέϊον, Ἀργογενῆ, Ἀργολικόν, Ἰναχίδαν, Ἰνάχιον</quote>, as is also the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">βάντα</quote> suggests a mention of ‘the place whence.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπιόθεν</quote> (Ahrens) would mean ‘from <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄπιος</quote>,’ but we require ‘from <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπία</quote>’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ</quote>, the Peloponnesus, <ref n="Soph. OC 1303"> <title>O. C.</title> 1303</ref> n.), i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπία—θεν</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀλυμπίαθεν</quote>. I had thought of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰναχόθεν_106">, which Mekler, too, has suggested, though he has not supported it by argument. The points in its favour are: (<emph>a</emph>) the order <quote xml:lang="grc">φῶτα βάντα</quote> can be kept: (<emph>b</emph>) after ‘Dircè's streams’ in v. 105 a reference to the Argive river would be appropriate: (<emph>c</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργόθεν</quote> might have come in either as a gloss, or a corruption of the letters <quote xml:lang="grc">αχόθεν</quote>, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ιν</quote> had dropped out after <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύκασπιν</quote>. But I hesitate to displace <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργόθεν</quote>, esp. when a direct mention of Argos here so naturally corresponds with the direct mention of Thebes in v. 101. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανσαγίᾳ_107"> (only here)=<quote xml:lang="grc">πανοπλίᾳ</quote>, modal dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">σάγη</quote> (for accent, cp. Chandler § 72)=‘what one carries,’ and so, generally, ‘equipment’ (<cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 560">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένῳ γὰρ εἰκώς, παντελῆ σάγην ἔχων</quote> </cit>), or, specially, <emph>body-armour:</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 240">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 240</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγχη σταδαῖα καὶ φεράσπιδες σάγαι</quote> </cit> (opp. to the <emph>light</emph> equipment of the <quote xml:lang="grc">τοξότης</quote>).</gloss>

data.quote: ξένῳ γὰρ εἰκώς, παντελῆ σάγην ἔχων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγάδα_πρ_107">, proleptic, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κινήσασα_107">, ‘having stirred to flight,’ etc. cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1292"> <title>O. C.</title> 1292</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξελήλαμαι φυγάς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόδρομον_107">, ‘running <emph>forward</emph>,’ i.e. ‘in headlong haste’: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 211">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 211</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ δαιμόνων πρόδρομος ἦλθον ἀρ|χαῖα βρέτη</quote>. In prose, always of <emph>precursors</emph> (as heralds, or an advanced guard).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξυτέρῳχαλινῷ_107">, ‘in swifter career,’ dat. of manner with <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγάδα πρόδρομον</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1067"> <title>O. C.</title> 1067</ref> (where the Attic horsemen are described rushing in pursuit of the Thebans), <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς γὰρ ἀστράπτει χαλινός</quote>, ‘the steel of every bridle flashes,’—as they gallop on with slack reins. So here, the <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλινός</quote>, which glitters as the horse rushes along, is poetically identified with the career itself, and thus is fitly joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξύτερος</quote>. The phrase seems happy in this context. The Argives began their retreat in the darkness (16): when the sun rises, the flashing steel of their bridles shows them in headlong flight.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξυτέρῳ_107"> does not mean (1) ‘in flight swifter than their former approach’; nor (2) that the reins are shaken ever faster on the horses' necks. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυτόρῳ</quote> (L) was a mere blunder: it could only mean ‘piercing’ (the horse's mouth), not, ‘giving a sharp sound,’ when the reins are shaken.—Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 122">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 122</ref>(describing the Argive besiegers) <quote xml:lang="grc">διάδετοι δέ τοι γενῦν ἱππιᾶν | κινύρονται</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μινύρονται</quote> L. Dind.) <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνον χαλινοί</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 152"> <title>Ib.</title> 152</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄτοβον ἁρμάτων ἀμφὶ πόλιν κλύω</quote>. Our passage suggests horsemen rather than drivers of war-chariots: perh. the poet imagined both, as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1062"> <title>O. C.</title> 1062</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πώλοισιν ἢ ῥιμφαρμάτοις | φεύγοντες ἁμίλλαις</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃνΠολυνείκης_110">. If this were sound, it would be necessary to suppose that after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιλόγων</quote> a dipodia has been lost, such as Nauck supplies by <<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤγαγεν· ἐχθρὸς δ’> ὀξέα κλάζων</quote>. For (1) a verb is wanted to govern <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅν</quote>, and (2) the description of the eagle, beginning with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξέα κλάζων</quote>, clearly refers to the Argive host, not to Polyneices only. But if, with Scaliger, we read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_Πολυνείκους_110">, no such loss need be assumed. The correspondence between anapaestic systems is not always strict, and the monometer <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξέα κλάζων</quote> could stand here, though the anti-system has a dimeter in the same place (129). The MS. reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν...Πολυνείκης</quote> probably arose from a misunderstanding of the scholium:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅντινα στρατὸν Ἀργείων, ἐξ ἀμφιλόγων νεικέων ἀρθείς, ἤγαγεν ὁ Πολυνείκης, οἷον ἀμφιλογίᾳ χρησάμενος πρὸς τὸν ἀδελφόν· διὰ βραχέων δὲ εἶπεν αὐτό, ὡς γνωρίμου οὔσης τῆς ὑποθέσεως</quote>. The Schol. wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρθείς</quote>, to agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">Πολυνείκης</quote>, and not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρθέντα</quote>, to agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατόν</quote>, because it suited the form of his paraphrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον ἀμφιλογίᾳ χρησάμενος πρὸς τὸν ἀδελφόν</quote>. By <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ βραχέων δὲ εἷπεν αὐτό</quote>, the Schol. meant not merely the indefiniteness of <quote xml:lang="grc">νεικέων ἐξ ἀμφιλόγων</quote>, but also the compactness of <quote xml:lang="grc">Πολυνείκους | ἀρθεὶς ἐκ νεικέων</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅντινα στρατὸν ἤγαγεν ὁ Πολυνείκης</quote>. But a transcriber, noticing that the Schol. joined <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρθείς</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">Πολυνείκης</quote>, might easily infer that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν...Πολυνείκης</quote> ought to stand in the text, and might take <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ βραχέων</quote> as meaning that the verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤγαγε</quote> could be understood.—L has the Doric <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾷ</quote>, and presently <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶν, ὑπερέπτα</quote>, which I keep: see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πολυνείκους_νεικέων_110">, playing on the name, like Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 577">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 577</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 658">Aesch. Th. 658</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 829">Aesch. Th. 829</ref>): as elsewhere on that of Ajax (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 432"> <title>Ai.</title> 432</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰάζειν</quote> </cit>), and of Odysseus (fr. 877, <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὶ γὰρ ὠδύσαντο δυσμενεῖς ἐμοί</quote>, have been bitter).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρθείς_110">, ‘having set forth’: so <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.165">Her. 1.165</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀερθέντες ἐκ τῶν Οἰνουσσέων ἔπλεον</quote> </cit>: 9. 52 (of a land-force) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀερθέντες ...ἀπαλλάσσοντο</quote>. Attic prose similarly uses the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρας</quote>, either absolutely, or with dat. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς ναυσί, τῷ στρατῷ</quote>), or, more rarely, with acc. (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ναῦς</quote> <ref n="Thuc. 1.52">Thuc. 1.52</ref>). Here the choice of the word suits the image of an eagle soaring. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεικ_ἀμφιλόγων_110">, lit. in consequence of contentious quarrels, i.e. his claims to the Theban throne, against his brother Eteocles. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 636">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 636</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιλόγους ὀργὰς</quote> </cit> (contentious moods) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκόρεστά τε νείκη</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 500"> <title>Ph.</title> 500</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίλεκτος...ἔρις</quote>. The prep. as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 620"> <title>O. C.</title> 620</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σμικροῦ λόγου</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξέα_κλάζων_112">: Homeric, <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.88"> <title>Il.</title> 17.88</ref> (of Hector) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξέα κεκληγώς</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 16.429"> <title>Il.</title> 16.429</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλα κλάζοντε</quote> </cit> (of vultures fighting): so <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 48">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 48</ref> (the Atreidae) <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαν ἐκ θυμοῦ κλάζοντες Ἄρη.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰετὸς_ὑπερέπτα_112"> seems clearly right. If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_112"> is omitted, we have a metaphor instead of a simile, with harsh effect. If we read <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰετὸς ὥς</quote>, and omit <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εἰς</emph>, γᾶν ὑπερέπτα</quote> could mean only, ‘flew over the land,’ not, ‘flew over the border into the land.’ Further, it is better that the flow of these descriptive verses should not be broken by a paroemiac before v. 116. No argument either way can be founded on v. 130 (where see n.), since, even if it were a paroemiac, that would not require a paroemiac here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερέπτα_112">. The act. strong aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπτην</quote> (as if from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπτημι</quote>) occurs simple only in the <title>Batrachomyomachia</title> (210, if sound) and the Anthol.: compounded, only in the tragic lyrics and in late prose. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1307">Cp. 1307</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λευκῆς_πτ_114">, ‘a wing white as snow’ (the white shield, <ref target="cl106">see on 106</ref>); genitive of quality (or material), equiv. to an epithet: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 533"> <title>O. T.</title> 533</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμης πρόσωπον</quote> </cit> (a bold front): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 19"> <title>El.</title> 19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστρων εὐφρόνη</quote> </cit> (starry night): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1491">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1491</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στολίδα...τρυφᾶς</quote> </cit> (a luxurious robe): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1526"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1526</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γάλακτος...μαστοῖς</quote> </cit> (milky breasts). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στεγανός_114">, pass. here, ‘covered’; but act. in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 358">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στεγανὸν δίκτυον</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 7.1.33">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 7.1.33</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ἀσπίδες...στεγάζουσι τὰ σώματα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπλων_κορύθεσσι_114">. The image of the eagle with white wings, which suited the Argive <emph>descent</emph> on Thebes, here passes into direct description of an invader who comes with many <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κόρυθες</quote>,—the shield, spear, and helmet of heavy-armed troops. For the dat. in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐εσσι</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 976">cp. 976</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χείρεσσι. <emph>ἱπποκόμοις</emph> </quote>, ‘with horse-hair crest’ (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.132"> <title>Il.</title> 13.132</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱ. κόρυθες</quote> </cit>). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύν_114"> denoting what one wears or carries, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 207"> <title>O. T.</title> 207</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1258"> <title>O. C.</title> 1258</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 30"> <title>Ai.</title> 30</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδῶντα πεδία ξὺν νεορράντῳ ξίφει</quote> </cit>. There is no real difference here between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετά_114"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύν_114_2">: Donaldson refines too much in suggesting that <quote xml:lang="grc">μετά</quote> means merely ‘by their sides,’ while <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύν</quote> ‘denotes a closer union’ (i.e. ‘on their heads’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στὰς_μελάθρων_117"> there is a momentary return to the image of the flying eagle,—‘having stayed his flight above my dwellings,’—before swooping. The words do not mean that the Argive army was posted on hills around Thebes: the only hills available were to the N. of the town. The <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰσμήνιος λόφος</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 9.10.2">Paus. 9.10.2</ref>), on which Donaldson places the Argives, was merely a low eminence close to one of the <emph>city</emph> gates. Thebes stood on a low spur <emph>of</emph> ground projecting southward, and overlooking the plain. Sophocles has elsewhere described the Argive besiegers, with topographical correctness, as having ‘set their leaguer <emph>round the plain of Thebes</emph>’ (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1312"> <title>O. C.</title> 1312</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Θήβης πεδίον ἀμφεστᾶσι πᾶν</quote> </cit>). Struve's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτάς_117"> (a participle not found elsewhere except in composition with a preposition) seems improbable, and also less forcible.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φονώσαισιν_λόγχαις_117"> once more merge the image of the eagle,—as at v. 115,—in literal description of a besieging army, save in so far as the figurative <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιχανών</quote> suggests a monster opening its jaws. The word was perh. suggested by <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.79"> <title>Il.</title> 23.79</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ μὲν κὴρ | ἀμφέχανε στυγερή</quote> </cit> (hath gaped for me—i.e. ‘devoured me’). These transitions from clear imagery to language in which the figure is blurred by the thought of the object for which it stands, are thoroughly Sophoclean: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 866"> <title>O. T.</title> 866</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φονώσαισιν_117">: the word is not rare in later writers, but in classical Greek occurs only here and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1209"> <title>Ph.</title> 1209</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονᾷ, φονᾷ νόος ἤδη</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τομάω</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 582"> <title>Ai.</title> 582</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑπτάπυλον_στόμα_117">, prop. the access afforded by seven gates: fr. 701 <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβας λέγεις μοι τὰς πύλας ἑπταστόμους</quote> (sevenmouthed as to its gates). Nauck changes <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμα</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλισμ’</quote> to avoid hiatus: but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1202"> <title>O. T.</title> 1202</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλεὺς καλεῖ | ἐμός</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβα_120">, emphatic by place: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 46">cp. 46</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρίν_ποθ_120">, ‘or <emph>ever</emph>,’ as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 17"> <title>Tr.</title> 17</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱμάτων_120">, streams of blood, as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1293">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1293</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱμάτων εὐθνησίμων | ἀπορρυέντων</quote> </cit> (with ref. to one person). Soph. has the plur. only here: Aesch. and Eur. use it several times each, either in this sense, or as=‘deeds of bloodshed’ (once as=‘slain persons,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵματα σύγγονα</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1503">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1503</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γἑνυσιν_120"> might be locative dat., ‘in’; but seems rather instrumental, ‘with.’ After </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλησθῆναι_120"> the missing short syllable is best supplied by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_120"> (Triclinius). The constr. is, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τε πλησθῆναι, καὶ Ἥφαιστον στεφάνωμα πύργων ἑλεῖν</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_120_2"> irregularly placed, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 258"> <title>O. T.</title> 258</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στεφάνωμα_120">: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 910">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 910</ref> (of Troy) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ δὲ στεφάναν κέκαρσαι | πύργων</quote>: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 15"> <title>O. C.</title> 15</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πευκάενθ_Ἥφαιστον_120">, the flame of pine-wood torches (<cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 11.786">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 11. 786</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">pineus ardor</quote> </cit>). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1007">Cp. 1007</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.426"> <title>Il.</title> 2.426</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">σπλάγχνα δ’ ἄρ’ ἀμπείραντες ὑπείρεχον Ἡφαίστοιο</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖος_124">, introducing the reason; <ref n="Soph. OC 947"> <title>O. C.</title> 947</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐτάθη_124">, lit. ‘was made intense,’ here suggesting both loud sound and keen strife. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 12.436"> <title>Il.</title> 12.436</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ἶσα μάχη τέτατο πτόλεμός τε</quote> </cit>: 23. 375 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποισι τάθη δρόμος</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 574">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 574</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεῖνε δὲ δυσβάϋκτον | βοᾶτιν τάλαιναν αὐδάν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ἐπὶ ἶσα μάχη τέτατο πτόλεμός τε

τεῖνε δὲ δυσβάϋκτον | βοᾶτιν τάλαιναν
									αὐδάν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάταγος_124">, <emph>clatter</emph> of arms (a word expressive of the sound), as distinguished from <quote xml:lang="grc">βοή</quote>, a human cry; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.211">Her. 7.211</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ βάρβαροι ὁρέοντες φεύγοντας βοῇ τε καὶ πατάγῳ ἐπήϊσαν</quote> </cit>. The Argives began to retreat in the night: at dawn, the Thebans made a sally in pursuit of them, and turned the retreat into a rout.</gloss>

data.quote:
οἱ δὲ βάρβαροι ὁρέοντες φεύγοντας βοῇ τε καὶ
									πατάγῳ ἐπήϊσαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιπάλῳ_δράκοντος_124">, a thing hard to vanquish for him who was struggling against the (Theban) dragon, —i.e. for the Argive eagle. The two readings between which the MSS. fluctuate, viz., </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_δράκοντι_124"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_δράκοντος_124">, arose, I feel sure, from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιπάλῳ_δράκοντος_124_2"> (V has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπάλω...δράκοντος</quote>). For the gen. after this adj., cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 94</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μένος γήραος ἀντίπαλον</quote>, a spirit that wrestles with old age: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 922">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 922</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεναίων γόος ἀντίπαλος</quote> </cit>, wails contending with marriagesongs.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δράκων_124"> certainly means the Thebans,—the <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαρτοί</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1534"> <title>O. C.</title> 1534</ref>) sprung from the dragon's teeth sown by Cadmus, and thence called <quote xml:lang="grc">δρακοντογενεῖς</quote> (schol.), Ovid's <quote xml:lang="lat">anguigenae</quote> ( <ref n="Ov. Met. 3. 531"> <title>Met.</title> 3. 531</ref>): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1125">cp. 1125</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ σπορᾷ δράκοντος</quote>. Poetry often represented a struggle between an eagle and a dragon or snake (<quote xml:lang="grc">δράκων</quote> could mean either, the ‘dragon’ being conceived as a sort of huge python); as <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.201"> <title>Il.</title> 12.201</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.4"> <title>Od.</title> 4.4</ref>. 11.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυς_124"> in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσχείρωμα_124"> must refer to difficulty experienced by the vanquished Argives, not by the victorious Thebans. The word must mean, then, ‘<emph>a thing hard to overcome</emph>,’ not, ‘<emph>a victory won with difficulty.</emph>’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωτος</quote> is ‘hard to subdue’ (<ref n="Hdt. 7.9">Her. 7.9</ref>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχείρωτος</quote> is ‘unsubdued’ (<ref n="Thuc. 6.10">Thuc. 6.10</ref>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχείρωτος</quote> ‘easy to subdue’ (<ref n="Xen. Hell. 5.3.4">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 5.3.4</ref>). Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπάλαιστος, δυσπάλαμος, δύσμαχος</quote>, etc., used with poetical irony to express the irresistible. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 560"> <title>O. T.</title> 560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάσιμον χείρωμα</quote> </cit> is a deed of deadly violence: in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1022">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 1022</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβοχόα χειρώματα</quote> are works of the hand in mound-making. In itself, <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωμα</quote> might mean ‘a thing achieved with difficulty’; but here the irony is clearly pointed against the routed Argives: the poet does not mean that the Thebans won with difficulty. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωμα</quote> is here the opposite of what Aesch. calls <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐμαρὲς χείρωμα</quote>, a thing easily subdued: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1326">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1326</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δούλης θανούσης, εὐμαροῦς χειρώματος</quote>. The Theban <quote xml:lang="grc">πάταγος Ἄρεος</quote> was a thing which the Argives could not overcome.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιπάλῳ_δράκοντι_124"> explain either (<emph>a</emph>) ‘a hard-won victory for the dragon foe’: but this gives a wrong sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωμα</quote>: or (<emph>b</emph>) join the dat. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὲτάθη_124">: ‘a din was raised by the dragon foe (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.55"> <title>Il.</title> 22.55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχιλῆϊ δαμασθείς</quote> </cit>), a thing hard (<emph>for the Argive</emph>) to subdue.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωμα</quote>, placed as it is, cannot be thus dissociated from the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπ. δράκοντι</quote> and mentally referred to another dat. which is left to be understood.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιπάλου_δράκοντος_124"> understand (<emph>a</emph>) a thing <emph>on the part of</emph> the dragon foe which was hard (for the Argive) to overcome; i.e. ‘an irresistible onset of the dragon foe.’ But such a construction of <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωμα</quote> with the gen. seems impossible, esp. when there is no dat. to help it out. Or (<emph>b</emph>) ‘a hard-won victory of the dragon foe’; which gives a wrong sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωμα</quote>.—The form of the word is in one respect unique. Every similar neuter noun compounded with <quote xml:lang="grc">δυς</quote> is from a verb so compounded: as <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσέργημα, δυσημέρημα, δυσπράγημα, δυσσέβημα, δυστύχημα, δυσφήμημα, δυσχέρασμα, δυσχρήστημα, δυσώπημα</quote>. But there is no such verb as <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχειρόω</quote>, ‘to subdue with difficulty.’ The noun has been boldly coined to express <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχείρωτον πρᾶγμα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεγάλης_127">: 1350 <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλοι... λόγοι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 95B">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 95B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ μέγα λέγε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 10.547">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 10. 547</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Dixerat ille aliquid magnum.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥεύματι_127">: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 88">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλῳ ῥεύματι φωτῶν</quote> </cit> (so <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 412"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 412</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῦμα Περσικοῦ στρατοῦ</quote> </cit>). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1437">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1437</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦσαι διώκων ῥεῦμά τ’ ἐξορμῶν στρατοῦ</quote> </cit>. The transposition </gloss>

data.quote: παῦσαι διώκων ῥεῦμά τ’ ἐξορμῶν στρατοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥεύματι_πολλῷ_127"> is unnecessary. In the same dipodia an anapaest must not precede a dactyl, nor a dactyl an anapaest; but a spondee can be followed by a dactyl, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 146"> <title>O. C.</title> 146</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλῶ δ’. οὐ γὰρ ἂν ᾦδ’ ἀλλοτρίοις</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: δηλῶ δ’. οὐ γὰρ ἂν ᾦδ’ ἀλλοτρίοις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσοῦ_ὑπεροπλίαις_130">, ‘in the haughty pride of clanging gold.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεροπλίαις</quote> seems a certain correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεροπτίας</quote> (see cr. n.), and has justly won its way with recent edd. The word is fitting, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεροπλία</quote> is prop. ‘overweening confidence in arms’; and Soph. has used the epic plur. with the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.205"> <title>Il.</title> 1.205</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗς ὑπεροπλίῃσι</quote> </cit>: so too <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 25">Theocr. 25. 138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένεϊ ᾧ | ἠδ’ ὑπεροπλίῃ Φαέθων</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καναχῆς_130">, of metal, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 16.105"> <title>Il.</title> 16.105</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πήληξ βαλλομένη καναχὴν ἔχε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλτῷ_πυρί_131">, i.e. with the thunderbolt which Zeus brandishes in his hand before hurling it: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 1714">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 1714</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλλων κεραυνόν, πτεροφόρον Διὸς βέλος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαλβίδων_ἄκρων_131">, at his topmost goal, i.e. at his goal on the top of our walls. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκρων</quote> might mean merely ‘uttermost,’ but is rather associated in the poet's mind with the object meant by <quote xml:lang="grc">βαλβίδων</quote>. In <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1180">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1180</ref> Capaneus is struck by Zeus at the moment that he is surmounting the <quote xml:lang="grc">γεῖσα τειχέων</quote>, the coping of the walls. The <quote xml:lang="grc">βαλβῖδες</quote> were the posts, to which a rope was attached, marking the point from which runners in the double foot-race (<quote xml:lang="grc">δίαυλος</quote>) set out, and to which they returned: hence both starting-point and goal.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρμῶντα_131">: for the partic. as subst., without either art. or <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 697"> <title>El.</title> 697</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύναιτ’ ἂν οὐδ’ ἂν ἰσχύων φυγεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 498A">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 498A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΚΑΛ. εἶδον. ΣΩ. τί δέ; νοῦν ἔχοντα λυπούμενον καὶ χαίροντα</quote> </cit>; The name of Capaneus could be left unmentioned, since the story was so famous. No leader of the Argive host, except Polyneices, is named in this play. The attack of Capaneus was said to have been made at the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἤλεκτραι πύλαι</quote> on the s. side of Thebes ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 423">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 423</ref>, <ref n="Paus. 9.9">Paus. 9.9.8</ref>). His fall from the scaling-ladder, as the lightning struck him, was often represented in art.</gloss>

data.quote: δύναιτ’ ἂν οὐδ’ ἂν ἰσχύων φυγεῖν

ΚΑΛ. εἶδον. ΣΩ. τί δέ; νοῦν ἔχοντα λυπούμενον
									καὶ χαίροντα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νίκην_131">, cogn. acc. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαλάξαι_131">, to raise the cry <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαλαί</quote> for victory: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 1763">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 1763</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαλαί, ἰὴ παιήων, | τήνελλα καλλίνικος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιτύπᾳ_134">, restored by Porson (<title>Aav.</title> p. 169) for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίτυπα</quote>, is certainly right. Adjectives in <quote xml:lang="grc">ος</quote>, compounded with a prep., are oft. of three terminations in epic poetry, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιελίσση, ἀμφιρύτη, ἀντιθέη</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 13.378"> <title>Od.</title> 13.378</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιβρότη</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 2.389"> <title>Il.</title> 2.389</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποδεξίη</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 9.73"> <title>Il.</title> 9.73</ref>), etc. The dramatists could admit some such forms, esp. in lyrics; thus they have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐναλία</quote> as well as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐνάλιος, ἡ ἐννυχία</quote> as well as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐννύχιος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τανταλωθείς_134">, ‘<emph>swung</emph>,’ that is, sent flying through the air from the edge of the wall on which he was just setting foot. The word expresses the force with which the thunderbolt struck him, just as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιτύπᾳ</quote> expresses the crash when he struck earth. This form of the verb occurs only here. Arist. uses both <quote xml:lang="grc">ταλαντεύομαι</quote> (pass.) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ταλαντεύω</quote> (act. intr.) as ‘to sway to and fro.’ The Schol., explaining by <quote xml:lang="grc">διασεισθείς</quote> (i.e. ‘with a rude shock,’ which is substantially right) quotes Anacreon 78 [<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν] μελαμφύλλῳ δάφνᾳ χλωρᾷ τ’ ἐλαίᾳ τανταλίζει</quote> (where the subject was perh. a god, or the wind). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυρφόρος_135">, ‘torch in hand’: so of Prometheus (<ref n="Soph. OC 55"> <title>O. C.</title> 55</ref>, where see n.) and Artemis (<ref n="Soph. OT 207"> <title>O. T.</title> 207</ref>). <ref n="Aesch. Seven 433">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 433</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φλέγει δὲ λαμπὰς διὰ χερῶν ὡπλισμένη· | χρυσοῖς δὲ φωνεῖ γράμμασιν, πρήσω πόλιν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βακχεύων_135">: so oft. Eur. as <title>H. F.</title> 898 <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύσσα βακχεύσει</quote>: but this is the only place where Soph. connects <emph>evil</emph> frenzy with the name of a god whom this same Ode invokes (154).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥιπαῖςἀνέμων_135">. Capaneus, breathing fury and slaughter, is likened to a deadly tempest. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιπαί</quote>, ‘blasts,’ <ref n="Soph. Ant. 929">cp. 929</ref> and <ref n="Soph. OC 1248"> <title>O. C.</title> 1248</ref> n. So <ref n="Aesch. Seven 63">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 63</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν καταιγίσαι πνοὰς | Ἄρεως</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶχε_μέν_138">, ‘but those things indeed’ (the threats of Capaneus) ‘went otherwise’ (than he had expected): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα δ’ ἐπ’ ἄλλοις μέγας Ἄρης ἐπενώμα</quote>, ‘while to others great Ares assigned various dooms,’ etc. The poet has described how <title>Zeus</title> smote the most formidable foe. As to the other Argive chiefs, he briefly adds that <title>Ares</title> struck them down by various deaths: i.e. they perished, not by a stroke from heaven, but in the course of battle. In L's reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶχε_ἐπἄλλοις_138">, one cause of corruption has evidently been a confusion between alternative modes of expressing ‘some’ and ‘other,’ viz. (1) by doubled <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος</foreign>, (2) by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μέν, τὰ δέ</quote>. It is in favour of our reading (Erfurdt's) that it helps to account for this, since it supposes that </gloss>
lemma:
εἶχε δ’ ἄλλαι τὰ μὲν ἄλλαι τὰ δ’ ἐπ’
									ἄλλοις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μέν_138"> was answered by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλα_δέ_138">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1671"> <title>O. C.</title> 1671</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τὸ μέν, ἄλλο δὲ μή</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.147"> <title>Il.</title> 6.147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μέν τ’ ἄνεμος χαμάδις χέει, ἄλλα δέ θ’ ὕλη | τηλεθόωσα φύει</quote> </cit>. It is immaterial that, here, </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ τὸ μέν, ἄλλο δὲ μή

τὰ μέν τ’ ἄνεμος χαμάδις χέει, ἄλλα δέ θ’ ὕλη
									| τηλεθόωσα φύει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μέν_138_2"> means, not, ‘<emph>some</emph> things,’ but, ‘<emph>those</emph> things’; since the latter is its first sense also where we render it by ‘some.’ Further, with regard to </gloss>
