<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλᾳ_138">, remark that this form of adverb is used elsewhere also in ref. to the course ordained by <emph>gods or fate: </emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 1443"> O. C. 1443</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα δ’ ἐν τῷ δαίμονι, | καὶ τῇδε φῦναι χἁτέρᾳ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 511">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 511</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ ταῦτα ταύτῃ μοῖρά πω τελεσφόρος | κρᾶναι πέπρωται</quote> </cit>. For other proposed readings, see Appendix.</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ ταῦτα ταύτῃ μοῖρά πω τελεσφόρος | κρᾶναι
									πέπρωται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπενώμα_138">: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 310">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 310</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λάχη τὰ κατ’ ἀνθρώπους | ὡς ἐπινωμᾷ στάσις ἁμά</quote> </cit>, apportions.— </gloss>

data.quote:
λάχη τὰ κατ’ ἀνθρώπους | ὡς ἐπινωμᾷ στάσις
									ἁμά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυφελίζων_138"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">στυ^φελός</quote>, ‘firm,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">στυ—/φω</quote>, to compress), ‘striking heavily’: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.581"> <title>Il.</title> 1.581</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἑδέων στυφελίξαι</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξιόσειρος_138">, ‘right-hand trace-horse,’ here means a vigorous ally, who does more than his own share of the work. Ares has brought the Theban chariot victoriously through the crisis of the race against its Argive rival. In the fourhorse chariot-race the four horses were harnessed abreast: the two in the middle were under the yoke (<quote xml:lang="grc">ζύγιοι</quote>), being called <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μέσος δεξιός</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μέσος ἀριστερός</quote> (schol. <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 122">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 122</ref>): the two outside horses drew in traces (<quote xml:lang="grc">σειραῖοι</quote>). The chariot went down the right-hand side of the course, turned sharply from right to left at the distance-post (<quote xml:lang="grc">καμπτήρ, νύσσα</quote>), and came back down the left side. Hence, at the turning-point, the right-hand tracehorse had most work to do; and the best horse was put in that place. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 721"> <title>El.</title> 721</ref> (at the turning-post) <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιόν τ’ ἀνεὶς | σειραῖον ἵππον εἶργε τὸν προσκείμενον</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Sym. 4. 6">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 4. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρματηλατοῦντα δεῖ ἐγγὺς μὲν τῆς στήλης κάμψαι</quote>, quoting from <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.336"> <title>Il.</title> 23.336</ref> the precept <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δεξιὸν ἵππον | κένσαι ὁμοκλήσαντ’, εἶξαί τέ οἱ ἡνία χερσίν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 842">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 842</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζευχθεὶς ἕτοιμος ἦν ἐμοὶ σειραφόρος</quote> </cit> (said by Agam. of Odysseus): and cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1640"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1640</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξιόχειρος_138">, explained by the schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">γενναῖος καὶ παραδέξιος</quote>, is read by Musgrave, Hartung, and Pallis. Hartung A. renders it ‘der Starke,’—understanding it as ‘the strong and deft striker.’ Neither <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιόχειρος</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιόχειρ</quote> seems to occur, though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριστερόχειρ</quote> (left-handed) is found in late Greek. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑπτὰ_λοχαγοί_141">. In <ref n="Soph. OC 1313"> <title>O. C.</title> 1313</ref> ff. the list agrees with that of Aesch. ,— Amphiaraus, Tydeus, Eteoclus, Hippomedon, Capaneus, Parthenopaeus, Polyneices. (Adrastus, who escaped, is not counted as one of the seven.) Capaneus, though not slain by human hand, is included, since he was vanquished. Amphiaraus, according to the legend which Soph. recognises in <ref n="Soph. El. 837"> <title>El.</title> 837</ref>, was swallowed up by the earth, but seems here to be reckoned among those who fell in fight (cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1313"> <title>O. C.</title> 1313</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσοι_ἴσους_141">, instead of saying simply <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἑπτά</quote>,—a common idiom: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 750">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 750</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσους ἴσοισι πολεμίοισιν ἀντιθείς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.2">Her. 1.2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσα πρὸς ἴσα</quote> </cit>: 9. 48 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσοι πρὸς ἴσους</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 774C">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 774C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσα ἀντὶ ἴσων</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζηνὶ_τροπαίῳ_141">, to Zeus who makes a <quote xml:lang="grc">τροπή</quote>, or rout, of enemies. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 671">Eur. <title>El.</title> 671</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ πατρῷε καὶ τροπαῖ’ ἐχθρῶν ἐμῶν</quote> </cit>. So he is invoked after a victory, <ref n="Eur. Her. 867."> <title>Her.</title> 867.</ref>(In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 303"> <title>Tr.</title> 303</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ τροπαῖε</quote> </cit> is usu. taken as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποτρόπαιε</quote>, ‘averting.’) In his relation to war, Zeus was worshipped also as <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγήτωρ</quote> (esp. at Sparta), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρειος</quote> (at Olympia, etc.), <quote xml:lang="grc">Στράτιος, Χρυσάωρ</quote> (in Caria), <quote xml:lang="grc">Στήσιος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐπιστάσιος</quote> (the Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat">stator</foreign>, stayer of flight).</gloss>

data.quote: ὦ Ζεῦ πατρῷε καὶ τροπαῖ’ ἐχθρῶν ἐμῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγχαλκα_τέλη_141">, ‘tributes of panoplies,’ as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 238"> <title>Tr.</title> 238</ref> Heracles dedicates <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμοὺς τέλη τ’ ἔγκαρπα Κηναίῳ Διΐ</quote>, i.e. ‘dues of fruits,’—alluding to the <quote xml:lang="grc">τέμενος</quote> of which the produce was given to the god (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 754"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 754</ref>). Not, (1) ‘complete suits of armour’: nor (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπλιτικὰ τάγματα</quote>, ‘troops of warriors,’ as Eustath. took it (p. 686. 16), led perh. by <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 47">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίρρυμά τε καὶ τρίρρυμα τέλη</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλὴν_στυγεροῖν_144">, ‘wretched’ (as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 166"> <title>Ph.</title> 166</ref>): not, ‘hateful,’ nor, ‘filled with hate.’ Of the seven Argive leaders, Polyneices was the only one who could not properly be said to have been vanquished, since he was not more vanquished than victorious. But, in excepting him, the poet associates him with the brother who was his victim as well as his conqueror. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑπτὰ...ἔλιπον..., πλὴν τοῖν στυγεροῖν</quote>, is a lax way of saying, ‘defeat befell each of the seven Argive leaders, except <emph>in the case of</emph> the two brothers,’—in which an Argive leader and a Theban leader slew <emph>each other.</emph>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρός_144">, etc., gen. of origin (38).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθ_αὑτοῖν_144"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἀλλήλοιν</foreign>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 40.29">Dem. or. 40 § 29</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἂν ἐν αὑτοῖς διενεχθῶσι γυνὴ καὶ ἀνήρ</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 347D">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 347D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγοντάς τε καὶ ἀκούοντας ἐν μέρει ἑαυτῶν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὧν ἂν ἐν αὑτοῖς διενεχθῶσι γυνὴ καὶ
									ἀνήρ

λέγοντάς τε καὶ ἀκούοντας ἐν μέρει
									ἑαυτῶν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δικρατεῖς_λόγχας_144">, two spears, each of which was victorious over the wielder of the other. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 251"> <title>Ai.</title> 251</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικρατεῖς Ἀτρεῖδαι</quote> </cit>, two Atreidae, each of whom is a king. That is, <quote xml:lang="grc">δικρατεῖς</quote> is equiv. to two distinct epithets (<quote xml:lang="grc">δύο</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατοῦσαι</quote>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1055"> <title>O. C.</title> 1055</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διστόλους...ἀδελφάς</quote> </cit>, two journeying sisters: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 17"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυκνόπτεροι</quote> </cit> = many, and feathered (n.): see <ref n="Soph. OT 846"> <title>O.T.</title> 846</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στήσαντε_144">, having set in position, <emph>levelled</emph>, against each other. The Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">δόρυ</quote> was chiefly a missile; here the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγχη</quote> is used for thrusting. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γάρ_148">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ γάρ</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 988 n.), can be used with or without an ellipse. Here there is no ellipse, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέλθωμεν</quote> follows (153), and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_148">, introducing the reason given by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθε</quote>,= ‘<emph>since.</emph>’ Below, 155, there is an ellipse, —‘But (let us cease), for Creon comes’; where </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_148_2"> might be rendered ‘<emph>indeed.</emph>’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεγαλώνυμος_148">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μεγάλην περιποιοῦσα δόξαν</quote>: the personified Nikè is ‘of great name,’ because victory is glorious.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυαρμάτῳ_148"> implies warlike renown, as well as wealth and splendour (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 845">cp. 845</ref>). Already in <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.391"> <title>Il.</title> 4.391</ref> the Cadmeans are ‘urgers of horses’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">κέντορες ἵππων</quote>): so <title>Scut. Herc.</title> 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">Βοιωτοὶ πλήξιπποι</quote>: Pindar <title>Ol.</title> 6. 85 has <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάξιππον...Θήβαν</quote>, <ref n="Pind. I. 7"> <title>Isthm.</title> 7. 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλαρμάτου πόλιος</quote> (as elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσάρματος, εὐάρματος</quote>). Critias , speaking of the inventions for which various cities were famous, says (fr. 1. 10) <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβη δ’ ἁρματόεντα δίφρον συνεπήξατο πρώτη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιχαρεῖσα_148">, with gladness responsive to that of Thebè. The goddess Nikè has come to meet the victors, and their joy is reflected in her radiant smile. (We can imagine her descending towards them from the sky, like the winged Nikè of Paeonius found at Olympia.) The doubts which have been felt as to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιχαρεῖσα</quote> disappear if it is observed that <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρεῖσα</quote> here refers to the <emph>outward manifestation</emph> of joy, not merely to the feeling in the mind. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντί</quote> expresses the <emph>answer</emph> of smile to smile, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλάμπω</quote> of light to light, or in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιφθέγγομαι</quote> of sound to sound. I do not take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντί</quote> here to mean <emph>merely</emph> ‘over against,’ as when <ref n="Pind. O. 3">Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 3.19</ref> says <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη γὰρ αὐτῷ... | ...ὀφθαλμὸν ἀντέφλεξε Μήνα</quote>, the (mid-month) moon showed the light of her eye over against him. —Not (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον αὐτῇ χαρεῖσα</quote> (schol.), i.e. merely, ‘rejoicing as Thebes does,’ which extenuates <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιχαρεῖσα</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">συγχαρεῖσα</quote>. Nor (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τῶν κακῶν χαρεῖσα</quote>, i.e. rejoicing in requital of past troubles. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπολέμων_νῦν_150">, ‘after the recent wars.’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_150">, cp. <ref> <title>Ph.</title> 271</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ πολλοῦ σάλου | εὕδοντ’</quote>, sleeping, after long tossing on the sea. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_150"> referring to the recent past (=‘just now’), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.13">Dem. or. 18 § 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡλίκα νῦν ἐτραγῴδει καὶ διεξῄει</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 7.1.26">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 7.1.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναμνησθέντας τὰ νῦν ἤδη γεγενημένα</quote> </cit> (i.e. the events of the Peloponnesian war, which had ended four years before).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέσθαι_150"> (L), as infin. for imperative (<ref n="Soph. OC 481"> <title>O. C.</title> 481</ref> n.), has a certain solemnity which seems to make it better here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέσθε_150">, though the latter is not excluded by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέλθωμεν</quote>. The last syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">θἑσθαι</quote> answers to the second of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθίστων</quote> in 137; each is an ‘irrational’ syllable (- for ˘): see <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεῶν</foreign>, monosyll. by synizesis (<ref n="Soph. OC 964"> <title>O. C.</title> 964</ref> n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παννυχίοις_150">, since a <quote xml:lang="grc">παννυχίς</quote> was esp. grateful to the city's tutelar god Dionysus (1147), whose rites are <quote xml:lang="grc">νύκτωρ τὰ πολλά</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Ba. 486">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 486</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_Θήβας_150"> (gen. sing.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλελίχθων_150">, = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁ τὴν Θήβης χθόνα ἐλελίζων</foreign>, shaking the ground of Thebes (with his dances): for the objective gen., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 333"> <title>O. C.</title> 333</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγων αὐτάγγελος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Βάκχιος_150"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">Βάκχος</foreign>, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 225">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 225</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δ’ Ἀφροδίτην πρόσθ’ ἄγειν τοῦ Βακχίου</quote> </cit>, and oft. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν δ’ Ἀφροδίτην πρόσθ’ ἄγειν τοῦ
									Βακχίου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρχοι_150"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς χορείας</quote> (schol.). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1146">Cp. 1146</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γάρ_155">: <ref target="cl148">see on 148</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρέων_155">, monosyll. by synizesis, as <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέων</quote> <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.183"> <title>Od.</title> 1.183</ref>; in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1493">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1493</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπνέων</quote> </cit> is a spondee. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1073"> <title>O. C.</title> 1073</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">*(πέας</quote> </cit>, a monosyll.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μενοικέως_155">,=˘¯¯, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1003"> <title>O. C.</title> 1003</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θησέως</quote> </cit> (¯¯), and so oft.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεοχμὸς_νεαραῖσι_155">. Neither adj. is suspicious; <emph>new</emph> events have made a <emph>new</emph> ruler; and the doubled adj. is quite in the poet's manner. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1266">Cp. 1266</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέος νέῳ ξὺν μόρῳ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 735"> <title>Ai.</title> 735</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέας | βουλὰς νέοισιν ἐγκαταζεύξας τρόποις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 475"> <title>O. C.</title> 475</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰὸς...νεαρᾶς νεοπόκῳ μαλλῷ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1259"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1259</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γέρων γέροντι συγκατῴκηκεν πίνος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 613"> <title>Tr.</title> 613</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καινῷ καινὸν ἐν πεπλώματι</quote> </cit>, etc. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">νεαρός</quote> usu.= ‘young,’ it occurs also in the sense of ‘novel,’ as in Pindar's <quote xml:lang="grc">νεαρὰ ἐξευρεῖν</quote> (<title>N.</title> 8. 20). Three views of the metre have been taken. (1) That v. 156 should be enlarged to a dimeter by supplying one anapaest or its equivalent. (2) That v. 156 should be reduced to a monometer by omitting <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοχμός</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">Μενοικέως</quote>. (3) That both v. 156 and v. 160 should be made dimeters by supplying three anapaests or</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεοχμός_155">. Seyffert's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρείων_155"> is at first sight attractive, as accounting for its own disappearance; but, since it is the same word as <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέων</quote>— which had an epic form <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρείων</quote>, as conversely Pind. and Aesch. use <quote xml:lang="grc">κρέων</quote>—this would be rather a feeble pun than a strong <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote>. Either </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρχων_155"> or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταγός_155"> is possible.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_συντυχίαις_155">, fortunes <emph>sent by</emph> the gods,—the possessive gen. denoting the authors, just as it can denote the parents: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1116"> <title>Ph.</title> 1116</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότμος...δαιμόνων</quote>: <title>Aeol.</title> fr. 37 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς δὲ δαιμόνων τύχας | ὅστις φέρει κάλλιστ’, ἀνὴρ οὗτος σοφός</quote>. (In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 34"> <title>O. T.</title> 34</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαιμόνων συναλλαγαῖς</quote> </cit> is different.) <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐπὶ</emph> συντυχίαις</foreign> means that the fortunes are the <emph>conditions</emph> which have made Creon king: this </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_155"> with dat. of attendant circumstance sometimes=our ‘in,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1268"> <title>O. C.</title> 1268</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἔργοις πᾶσι</quote> </cit> (n.), sometimes ‘for,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 406">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῖν’ ἐπὶ συμφοραῖς</quote> </cit> (i.e. to celebrate them), cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1230"> <title>El.</title> 1230</ref>: here we could say, ‘<emph>under</emph> the new dispensations of the gods.’ (Distinguish 88 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ψυχροῖσι</quote> as =‘for’ in the sense ‘with a view to.’) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῆτιν_ἐρέσσων_158">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">consilium animo volutans</foreign>, ‘turning it over’ busily in the mind. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρέσσειν</quote>, to ply the oar, is fig. said of putting a thing in lively motion, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 139">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 139</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρέσσων σὸν πόδα</quote> </cit>. Then also of activity in speech, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 251"> <title>Ai.</title> 251</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρέσσουσιν ἀπειλάς</quote> </cit>, ‘they <emph>ply</emph> threats’ (utter them repeatedly and loudly): or, as here, in thought. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 231">Cp. 231</ref>.—(<title>Not</title>, ‘speeding his counsel hitherward,’ i.e. coming to disclose it: ‘<foreign xml:lang="lat">advolvens</foreign>, i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">patefacturus</foreign>,’ Ellendt.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύγκλητον_158">, specially convoked;—implying that there were other and regularly appointed seasons at which the king met the <quote xml:lang="grc">γέροντες</quote> in council. At Athens four meetings of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκλησία</quote> were regularly held in each <quote xml:lang="grc">πρυτανεία</quote> (a period of 35 or 36 days): these were <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρίαι</quote> (though the term may once have been restricted to the <emph>first</emph> of them), or <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμιμοι</quote>. An extraordinary meeting was <quote xml:lang="grc">σύγκλητος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάκλητος</quote>. Pollux 8. 116 <quote xml:lang="grc">σύγκλητος ἐκκλησία ἣν ἐξαίφνης ἐποίουν μείζονος χρείας ἐπιλαβούσης· ἐκαλεῖτο δὲ καὶ κατακλησία, ὅτι καὶ τοὺς ἐκ τῶν ἀγρῶν κατεκάλουν</quote> (<emph>down</emph> to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστυ</quote>). <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 3.1275b">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 3.1</ref>. 10 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνίαις γὰρ οὐκ ἔστι δῆμος, οὐδ’ ἐκκλησίαν νομίζουσιν ἀλλὰ συγκλήτους</quote>: ‘in some States there is no popular body, and they have no regular assembly, but only <emph>meetings on special occasions.</emph>’ <quote xml:lang="grc">σύγκλητος</quote> is one of those words which, though a technical term at Athens, could still be used by Attic poets without any prosaic local allusion being felt,— just as they used <quote xml:lang="grc">πρύτανις, ἐπιστάτης, ἄρχων, ψήφισμα</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔθετο_158"> is another example. The presidents of the ecclesia were said <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας προθεῖναι</quote> when they invited a discussion. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.14">Thuc. 6.14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πρύτανι ... γνώμας προτίθει αὖθις Ἀθηναίοις</quote> </cit>, ‘lay the question again before the assembly.’ <ref n="Thuc. 3.42"> <title>Id.</title> 3. 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς προθέντας τὴν διαγνώμην</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 4.2.3">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 4.2.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς πόλεως λόγον περί τινος προτιθείσης</quote> </cit>. Lucian <title>Menipp.</title> 19 has <quote xml:lang="grc">προὔθεσαν οἱ πρυτάνεις ἐκκλησίαν</quote>, ‘gave notice of’: but for this the usual phrase was that of Aeschin. or. 2 § 60 <quote xml:lang="grc">προγράψαι τοὺς πρυτάνεις ἐκκλησίας δύο</quote>. Here, </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ πρύτανι ... γνώμας προτίθει αὖθις
									Ἀθηναίοις

τῆς πόλεως λόγον περί τινος
									προτιθείσης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέσχην_158"> is</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔθετο_158_2"> suggests Creon's personal interest in the question: the active would denote the mere act (<ref target="cl8">see on 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῖναι</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1249">Cp. 1249</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">προτίθεσθαι</quote> more oft. denotes what one proposes <emph>to</emph> oneself.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινῷ_πέμψας_158">, lit. having sent (notice of the meeting) by means of a summons addressed to each of us. The <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρυγμα</quote> is the mandate which <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρυκες</quote> carried to each of the fifteen elders,—not, of course, a public proclamation: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 164">cp. 164</ref>. For the absolute <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμπω</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 5.43">Thuc. 5.43</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμπει εὐθὺς ἐς Ἄργος ἰδίᾳ</quote> </cit>: and so oft. (Not, ‘having sent for us,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταπεμψάμενος</quote>: cp. on 19.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δ_162">. The perils of the war are now over; the affairs of civil government claim my next care; and I have therefore sent for you, the nearest supporters of my throne.</gloss>
lemma: τὰ μὲν δὴ πόλεος . . ὑμᾶς δ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεος_162"> occurs only here in Soph. , but twice in the trimeters of Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 218">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 218</ref>, <title>Suppl.</title> 344), and thrice in those of Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 897">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 897</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 412"> <title>El.</title> 412</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 595"> <title>Ion</title> 595</ref>). Eur. has also in trimeters <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφεος</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1027">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1027</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1331">Eur. Bacch. 1331</ref>, <title>Suppl.</title> 703), and <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνεος</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 641"> <title>Cycl.</title> 641</ref>). In Comedy we find <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρεος</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 465">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 465</ref>, <title>Plut.</title> 1044), and <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσεος</quote> (<title>Vesp.</title> 1282, 1458). Such forms, which metrical convenience recommended to Attic poets, must not be confounded with the Ionic genitives in <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote>, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιος</quote>. The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλευς</quote>, contracted from <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεος</quote>, is used by Theogn. 776 etc.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλῷ_σείσαντες_162">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 22"> <title>O. T.</title> 22</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤρθωσαν_162">, made <emph>upright</emph>, ‘righted’: but below 167, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου</quote>=was keeping <emph>straight:</emph> cp. on 83. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δ_162">. The perils of the war are now over; the affairs of civil government claim my next care; and I have therefore sent for you, the nearest supporters of my throne.</gloss>
lemma: τὰ μὲν δὴ πόλεος . . ὑμᾶς δ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεος_162"> occurs only here in Soph. , but twice in the trimeters of Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 218">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 218</ref>, <title>Suppl.</title> 344), and thrice in those of Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 897">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 897</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 412"> <title>El.</title> 412</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 595"> <title>Ion</title> 595</ref>). Eur. has also in trimeters <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφεος</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1027">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1027</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1331">Eur. Bacch. 1331</ref>, <title>Suppl.</title> 703), and <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνεος</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 641"> <title>Cycl.</title> 641</ref>). In Comedy we find <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρεος</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 465">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 465</ref>, <title>Plut.</title> 1044), and <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσεος</quote> (<title>Vesp.</title> 1282, 1458). Such forms, which metrical convenience recommended to Attic poets, must not be confounded with the Ionic genitives in <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote>, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιος</quote>. The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλευς</quote>, contracted from <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεος</quote>, is used by Theogn. 776 etc.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλῷ_σείσαντες_162">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 22"> <title>O. T.</title> 22</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤρθωσαν_162">, made <emph>upright</emph>, ‘righted’: but below 167, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου</quote>=was keeping <emph>straight:</emph> cp. on 83. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_πάντων_164">, (chosen) out of all, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίχα_164"> <emph>adv.</emph> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέσθαι</quote>) apart from them: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 656">cp. 656</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεως . . ἐκ πάσης μόνην</quote>, 1137 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἐκ πασᾶν τιμᾷς</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1351"> <title>El.</title> 1351</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅν ποτ’ ἐκ πολλῶν ἐγὼ | μόνον προσηῦρον πιστόν</quote> </cit>. In other places, where <quote xml:lang="grc">δίχα</quote> is <emph>prep.</emph> with gen., we find it similarly connected with another expression of like purport, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 749"> <title>Ai.</title> 749</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ...κύκλου | . . μεταστὰς οἷος Ἀτρειδῶν δίχα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅν ποτ’ ἐκ πολλῶν ἐγὼ | μόνον προσηῦρον
									πιστόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστειλ_ἱκέσθαι_164">: lit., by means of messengers I <emph>caused</emph> you <emph>to set forth</emph>, so that you should come (epexeg. inf.): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 60"> <title>Ph.</title> 60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ σ’ ἐν λιταῖς στείλαντες ἐξ οἴκων μολεῖν</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">στέλλεσθαι</quote> (midd.) ‘to summon <emph>to oneself</emph>’ (<ref n="Soph. OT 434"> <title>O. T.</title> 434</ref>): cp. n. <ref n="Soph. OT 860"> <title>O. T.</title> 860</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_μέν_164">, answered by <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ αὖθις</quote>: see 61 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέβοντας_164">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μένοντας</quote> (169), part. of the imperf.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ἐσέβετε</quote>: so 1192: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 835"> <title>O. T.</title> 835</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ παρόντος</quote> </cit> (n.): and cp. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1565"> <title>O. C.</title> 1565</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρόνων_κράτη_164">, powers belonging to the throne: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 60">cp. 60</ref>, 173. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡνίκ_Οἰδίπους_167"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The only obscurity arises from the use of the plur. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνων_167"> in 168. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνων_παῖδας_167"> ought to mean, ‘the descendants of Laïus and Oedipus,’ viz. Eteocles and Polyneices. But, as the sentence stands, it must mean, ‘the offspring of Laïus and of Oedipus <emph>respectively</emph>’; viz. Oedipus, the son of Laïus; Eteocles and Polyneices, the sons of Oedipus. The relative clause, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡνίκ_πόλιν_167">, induced the poet to add immediately the other relative clause to which the same person is subject, viz. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_διώλετο_167">, instead of inserting, after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου πόλιν</quote>, words expressing their loyalty to Oedipus. We might, indeed, suppose that, after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου πόλιν</quote>, we were intended to supply mentally, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὰ ἐκείνου θρόνων κράτη σέβοντας</quote>. But against this is the fact that, after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_αὖθις_167">,—‘in the first place’ . . ‘in the second place,’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_167"> (in <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπεὶ</quote>) would scarcely have been thus used to introduce a distinct third clause. Evidently </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_167_2"> links <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡνίκα ὤρθου</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ διώλετο.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέδοις_φρονήμασιν_167">, with steadfast sentiments (of loyalty), modal dat., as oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">προθυμίᾳ, εὐνοίᾳ, φρονήματι</quote> (<ref n="Thuc. 2.62">Thuc. 2.62</ref>), etc. Hartung, whom some recent editors follow, adopts </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέδους_167"> on the strange ground that Soph. must otherwise have written <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμμένοντας</quote>. But <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>μένοντας</emph> ἀμφὶ τοὺς κείνων παῖδας</foreign>=‘remaining around them,’ and the modal dat. is added no less legitimately than the causal dat. in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 701">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 701</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλίᾳ μένειν</quote> </cit>, ‘to remain through cowardice.’ Soph. could have said <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπέδους φρονήμασιν</quote>, as he has said <quote xml:lang="grc">συντρόφοις | ὀργαῖς ἔμπεδος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 639"> <title>Ai.</title> 639</ref>): but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέδοις_167"> is better here, both (<emph>a</emph>) because a series of accusatives has preceded, and (<emph>b</emph>) because, as <quote xml:lang="grc">μένοντας</quote> has already marked their constancy, we now want an epithet for their <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονήματα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτε_170"> causal, <ref n="Soph. OT 918"> <title>O. T.</title> 918</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_μ_170">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 14">cp. 14</ref> n.: for <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς</quote>, 51 n.: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῆς_μίαν_170">, 13 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παίσαντες_πληγέντες_170">. In Attic prose the verb ‘to strike’ usu. had as pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">τύπτω</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">παίω</quote>), fut. <quote xml:lang="grc">τυπτήσω</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">πατάξω</quote>), aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάταξα</quote>, aor. pass. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπλήγην</quote>. The aor. of <quote xml:lang="grc">παίω</quote> is mainly a poetical word, used in tragedy, more rarely in comedy, and by Xenophon. In Attic prose <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαισα</quote> is usu. the aor. of <quote xml:lang="grc">παίζω</quote>. Meineke proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήξαντες</quote> here, but that aor. (except in comp. with a prep.) is almost unknown to classical <quote xml:lang="grc">αττιξ. παισθέντες</quote>, again, though that aor. pass. occurs twice in Aesch. , is very rare.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόχειρι_μιάσματι_170">, the stain of a kinsman's murder (<ref target="cl51">see on 51</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1176">cp. 1176</ref>): cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 849">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 849</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ | αὐτοφόνα. <emph>σύν</emph> </quote>, as 1266 <quote xml:lang="grc">νέῳ ξὺν μόρῳ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1663"> <title>O. C.</title> 1663</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν νόσοις</quote>: <ref n="Pind. O. 2">Pind. <title>O.</title> 2. 42</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν ἀλλαλοφονίᾳ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_δή_173">, I <emph>now:</emph> where <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> nearly=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 968"> <title>O. T.</title> 968</ref> n. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 3">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 3</ref> (after Gaia came Themis) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ δὴ τὸ μητρὸς δευτέρα τόδ’ ἕζετο | μαντεῖον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτη_173">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 166">cp. 166</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένους_ὀλ_173">, by nearness of kinship to the dead, <quote xml:lang="grc">γένους ἀγχιστεῖα</quote> forming one notion, on which the genit. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὀλ</quote>. depends, as on words meaning ‘near.’ The neut. plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστεῖα</quote> (only here) would most properly mean ‘rights’ or ‘privileges’ of such nearness (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριστεῖα, πρωτεῖα</quote>, etc.), but seems here to be merely a poetical equiv. for the abstract <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστεία</quote>. In Attic law <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστεία</quote> was any degree of relationship on which a claim to an inheritance could be founded in the absence of a will otherwise disposing of it. To claim an inheritance under a will was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφισβητεῖν κατὰ διαθήκην</quote>: to claim on the ground of relationship, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφισβητεῖν κατ’ ἀγχιστείαν. συγγένεια</quote>, consanguinity, might, or might not, constitute <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστεία</quote>: e.g. Isaeus says of the relationship of mother to son that it is <quote xml:lang="grc">συγγενέστατον μὲν τῇ φύσει πάντων, ἐν δὲ ταῖς ἀγχιστείαις ὁμολογουμένως οὐκ ἔστιν</quote> (or. 11 § 17), since a mother could not inherit from her son. (See <title>Selections from the Attic Orators</title>, pp. 331, 344.) Creon succeeds as the nearest male relative. Aesch. , Soph. , and Eur. ignore the Boeotian legend which gave a son, Laodamas, to Eteocles (<ref n="Hdt. 5.61">Her. 5.61</ref>), and a son, Thersander, to Polyneices (id. 4. 147, etc.). The sisters represent the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάτη ῥίζα</quote> (599). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμήχανον_δέ_175">. ‘You were loyal to the kings whose successor I am. Now (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_175">) a man cannot be really known until he has been tried in office. (I do not, therefore, ask you to pledge your loyalty to me until I have been so tested.) I will, however, tell you the principles which I intend to observe.’ Thus </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_175_2"> merely marks the transition to a new topic. It is not directly adversative, as if he meant: ‘You were loyal to my predecessors, <emph>but</emph> I do not yet ask you to be loyal to me.’ On that view, however, the general connection of thoughts would remain the same.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχήν_176">, ‘soul,’ the man's moral nature generally: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρόνημα_176">, the ‘spirit’ of his dealing in public affairs, according as his aims are lofty or mean, his policy bold or timid (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 207">cp. 207</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιόνδ’ ἐμὸν φρόνημα</quote>): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμην_176">, the intellectual aspect of the man, his ability and judgment. In <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.124">Her. 5.124</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὴν οὐκ ἄκρος</quote> </cit>, 3. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">διεπειρᾶτο αὐτοῦ τῆς ψυχῆς</quote>, the word=‘fortitude.’ But the usage of Soph. favours the more general sense here: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 227">cp. 227</ref>, 929, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1361"> <title>Ai.</title> 1361</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληρὰν...ψυχήν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 219"> <title>El.</title> 219</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σᾷ δυσθύμῳ τίκτουσ’ αἰεὶ | ψυχᾷ πολέμους</quote> </cit>. Plato has the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ψυχῆς τὴν γνώμην</quote> for ‘the intellect’ (<title>Legg.</title> 672 B). </gloss>

data.quote:
σᾷ δυσθύμῳ τίκτουσ’ αἰεὶ | ψυχᾷ
									πολέμους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαῖς_177">, duties of administration. It might be explained as a generic plur. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχή</quote>, in the sense of ‘sovereignties,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 3.15">Isocr. or. 3 § 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ μοναρχίαι</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 3.16">§ 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς τυραννίδας</quote> </cit>, etc.: but it seems truer to say that the Athenian poet was thinking of public offices or magistracies. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμοισιν_177"> has a general sense: the king is concerned with <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοι</quote> both as <quote xml:lang="grc">νομοφύλαξ</quote> and as <quote xml:lang="grc">νομοθέτης</quote>: but, as the context suggests, it is of law-<emph>giving</emph> that Creon is more particularly thinking. Tournier has suggested <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχῇ τε καὶ θρόνοισιν</quote>, but we must recollect how largely the language of Attic tragedy is tinged with democratic associations.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντριβής_177">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">exercitatus:</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 769B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 769B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντριβής γε οὐδαμῶς γέγοια τῇ τοιαύτῃ τέχνῃ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐντριβής γε οὐδαμῶς γέγοια τῇ τοιαύτῃ
									τέχνῃ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανῇ_177"> ‘be found,’ without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>, as <ref n="Pind. P. 5">Pind. <title>P.</title> 5. 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφανταί θ’ ἁρματηλάτας σοφός</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.8">Thuc. 1.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κᾶρες ἐφάνησαν</quote> </cit> (were found to be). Not: ‘be <emph>revealed</emph>, by being conversant.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Nic. Eth. 1131a">Arist. <title>Eth. Nic.</title> 5.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὶ γὰρ ἐν μὲν τοῖς οἰκείοις τῇ ἀρετῇ δύνανται χρῆσθαι, ἐν δὲ τοῖς πρὸς ἕτερον ἀδυνατοῦσιν. καὶ διὰ τοῦτο εὖ δοκεῖ ἔχειν τὸ τοῦ Βίαντος, ὅτι ἀρχὴ ἄνδρα δείξει· πρὸς ἕτερον γὰρ καὶ ἐν κοινωνίᾳ ἤδη ὁ ἄρχων</quote> </cit>. Besides Bias of Priene, others of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑπτὰ σοφισταί</quote>,—as Chilon, Pittacus, Solon,—had this saying ascribed to them. <ref n="Plut. Sull. 30">Plut. <title>Sull.</title> 30</ref>(Sulla ) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκότως προσετρίψατο ταῖς μεγάλαις ἐξουσίαις διαβολὴν ὡς τὰ ἤθη μένειν οὐκ ἐώσαις ἐπὶ τῶν ἐξ ἀρχῆς τρόπων</quote> (as not allowing characters to be constant under the influence of habits formed in office), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔμπληκτα καὶ χαῦνα καὶ ἀπάνθρωπα ποιούσαις</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. JC 2.1.12">Shaksp. <title>Jul. Caes.</title> ii. 1. 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">He would be crown'd:— How that might change his nature, there's the question....The abuse of greatness is, when it disjoins | Remorse from power.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
πολλοὶ γὰρ ἐν μὲν τοῖς οἰκείοις τῇ ἀρετῇ
									δύνανται χρῆσθαι, ἐν δὲ τοῖς πρὸς ἕτερον ἀδυνατοῦσιν. καὶ διὰ
									τοῦτο εὖ δοκεῖ ἔχειν τὸ τοῦ Βίαντος, ὅτι ἀρχὴ ἄνδρα δείξει· πρὸς
									ἕτερον γὰρ καὶ ἐν κοινωνίᾳ ἤδη ὁ ἄρχων

He would be crown'd:— How that might change
									his nature, there's the question....The abuse of greatness is,
									when it disjoins | Remorse from power.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὶ_γάρ_178">. A ground for the preceding statement is introduced by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_178">, though the compression of the thought slightly obscures the connection. ‘A man cannot be known until he has been tested in power. <title>For</title> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γὰρ</quote>) a man in power may easily be deterred, by fear of unpopularity, from pursuing the counsels best for the State: and if he is so deterred, <title>I</title> think him worthless.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσαν_πόλιν_178">, the whole city, as 656 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεως ... ἐκ πάσης</quote>, 776 <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσα...πόλις</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 851"> <title>Ai.</title> 851</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πάσῃ πόλει</quote> </cit> (in the hearing of all the city). In prose the art. would have been added (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.29">Thuc. 7.29</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ πόλει πάσῃ</quote> </cit>, 4. 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμπάσῃ τῇ πόλει</quote>, 2. 65 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ξύμπασα πόλις</quote>); but its omission in poetry being so common, it is strange that <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν</quote> should have been suspected here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἅπτεται_178">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>, since the relative clause is general (‘<emph>such</emph> an one <emph>as</emph> does not...,’ Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui</foreign> with subjunct.): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1175"> <title>O. C.</title> 1175</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ μὴ | χρῄζεις</quote> </cit>. Instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_ἅπτεται_178"> we should more often find <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις ἃν μὴ ἅπτηται</quote>: yet the instances of the indic. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> in general statement are not rare even in prose; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.64">Thuc. 2.64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵτινες...ἥκιστα λυποῦνται, ἔργῳ δὲ μάλιστα ἀντέχουσιν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.64"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις λαμβάνει.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οἵτινες...ἥκιστα λυποῦνται, ἔργῳ δὲ μάλιστα
									ἀντέχουσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_του_178">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 111">cp. 111</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεικέων ἐξ ἀμφιλόγων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκλῄσας_ἔχει_178"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 22">cp. 22</ref>),=a perf., in the sense ‘has shut once for all,’ ‘keeps shut.’ Distinguish the prose idiom, <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 9.12">Dem. or. 9 § 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερὰς...ἔχει καταλαβών</quote> </cit>, <emph>has seized, and keeps.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_πάλαι_178">, an emphatic formula (‘seems, and has always seemed’), <ref n="Soph. El. 676"> <title>El.</title> 676</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 966"> <title>Ph.</title> 966</ref>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1049"> <title>El.</title> 1049</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι δέδοκται ταῦτα κοὐ νεωστί μοι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.105"> <title>Il.</title> 9.105</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον ἐγὼ νοέω, ἠμὲν πάλαι ἠδ’ ἔτι καὶ νῦν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: πάλαι δέδοκται ταῦτα κοὐ νεωστί μοι

οἷον ἐγὼ νοέω, ἠμὲν πάλαι ἠδ’ ἔτι καὶ
									νῦν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μείζον_182">: whoever recognises a friend <emph>more important</emph> than his country, —i.e. with stronger claims upon him: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντὶ_τῆςπάτρας_182"> instead of the simple gen., or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤ_182"> with accus., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 576"> <title>Tr.</title> 576</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε μήτιν’ εἰσιδὼν | στέρξει γυναῖκα κεῖνος ἀντὶ σοῦ πλέον</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 638">Cp. 638</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γάμος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μείζων φέρεσθαι</quote>, more important to win: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 772"> <title>O. T.</title> 772</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ γὰρ ἂν καὶ μείζονι | λέξαιμ’ ἂν ἢ σοί</quote> </cit>..., ‘to whom more important,’ i.e. with a better claim on my confidence,— nearer and dearer. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὥστε μήτιν’ εἰσιδὼν | στέρξει γυναῖκα κεῖνος
									ἀντὶ σοῦ πλέον

τῷ γὰρ ἂν καὶ μείζονι | λέξαιμ’ ἂν ἢ
									σοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεῖζον_182"> (which was written by the first hand in L) is specious, —‘a more important thing,’ a greater good: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 784">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 784</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα γὰρ ηὑγένειά σου</quote> </cit>, <ref n="Eur. Andr. 209"> <title>Andr.</title> 209</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Λάκαινα μὲν πόλις | μέγ’ ἐστί</quote>. But Demosthenes, at least, seems to have read <quote xml:lang="grc">μείζον’</quote>: for, in applying the verses to Aeschines, he paraphrases thus (or. 19 § 248): <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων οὐδὲν Αἰσχίνης εἶπε πρὸς αὑτὸν ἐν τῇ πρεσβείᾳ, ἀλλ’ ἀντὶ μὲν τῆς πόλεως τὴν Φιλίππου ξενίαν καὶ φιλίαν πολλῷ μείζονα ἡγήσατο αὑτῷ καὶ λυσιτελεστέραν, ἐρρῶσθαι πολλὰ φράσας τῷ σοφῷ Σοφοκλεῖ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδαμοῦ_λέγω_182">: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 497">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 497</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς δέ τις | τὸ πρὶν νομίζων οὐδαμοῦ, τότ’ ηὔχετο</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 210">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 210</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Σκῦρον οὐδαμοῦ τίθης</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">nullo in numero habes</foreign>). <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 1.2.52">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1.2.52</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε μηδαμοῦ παρ’ αὐτοῖς τοὺς ἄλλους εἶναι πρὸς αὐτόν</quote> </cit>, ‘so that the rest <emph>were nowhere</emph> with them in comparison to him.’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενὸς λόγου</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οὐδενὶ λόγῳ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖσθαι, ἐν οὐδεμιᾷ μοίρᾳ ἄγειν</quote>, etc. </gloss>

data.quote:
θεοὺς δέ τις | τὸ πρὶν νομίζων οὐδαμοῦ, τότ’
									ηὔχετο

ὥστε μηδαμοῦ παρ’ αὐτοῖς τοὺς ἄλλους εἶναι
									πρὸς αὐτόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_γάρ_184">. Here, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ γάρ</quote> above (178), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_184"> introduces a reason; but here, again, the connection is obscured by the form of the sentence. The reason is contained in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_γιγνώσκων_184"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> (188). ‘I have no esteem for a man who prefers popularity or private friendship to the good of the State (178— 183); <emph>for</emph> (184) I well know that all private welfare depends on the welfare of the State; and so I should never commit the faults which I have just condemned in others.’—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴστω_184"> is confirmed against the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴστωρ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵστωρ</quote>) by those passages in which it is joined with an accus., as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 7.411"> <title>Il.</title> 7.411</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκια δὲ Ζεὺς ἴστω</quote> </cit>, 15. 36 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴστω νῦν τοδε Γαῖα</quote>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_σιωπήσαιμι_185">. Applied to the actual case, Creon's words mean, ‘I should never be deterred by fear of popular murmurs (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 692">cp. 692</ref> ff.) from publishing such an edict as this against burying Polyneices, when I clearly saw that otherwise a disastrous precedent would be set. And though Polyneices was my nephew, I should never allow myself to recognise as friend or kinsman a man who had borne arms against the country.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχουσαν_ἀστοῖς_186">. Demosthenes paraphrases this by <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχουσαν ὁμοῦ</quote> (or. 19 § 248); whence Dobree and Shilleto surmised that he read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆσσον</quote> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 312"> <title>O. C.</title> 312</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχουσαν ἡμῶν ἆσσον</quote> </cit>). Now I think that I can explain why Demosthenes so paraphrased. He is applying the verses to Aeschines (see above, n. on 182): <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δὲ ἄτην ὁρῶν στείχουσαν ὁμοῦ, τὴν ἐπὶ Φωκέας στρατείαν, οὐ προεῖπεν οὐδὲ προεξήγγειλεν</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote> which Aeschines saw approaching was the interference of Philip in the Sacred War,—his action against the Phocians. If Demosthenes had said <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχουσαν ἀστοῖς</quote>, this must have seemed to refer to the fellow-citizens of Aeschines, —the Athenians. The orator therefore modified the poet's phrase by substituting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοῦ_186">,—a word vague enough to suggest the concern of other Greek states besides Phocis in the peril.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντὶ_σωτηρίας_186">, added for emphasis; ‘ruin, <emph>and not</emph> welfare, which a king is bound to promote.’ (The art. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_186"> is merely generic, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἄτην</quote>.) So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 267"> <title>Tr.</title> 267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φανεὶς δὲ δοῦλος ἀνδρὸς ἀντ’ ἐλευθέρου</quote> </cit>, a slave, and not a free man (as he ought to be): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1490"> <title>O. T.</title> 1490</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκλαυμέναι | πρὸς οἶκον ἵξεσθ’ ἀντὶ τῆς θεωρίας</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: φανεὶς δὲ δοῦλος ἀνδρὸς ἀντ’ ἐλευθέρου

κεκλαυμέναι | πρὸς οἶκον ἵξεσθ’ ἀντὶ τῆς
									θεωρίας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνδρα_χθονός_187">; cp. <ref n="Plat. Lysis 213b">Plat. <title>Lysis</title> 213 B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φίλον ἂν εἴη φίλον τοῦ φιλουμένου...τὸ μισοῦν ἄρα πάλιν ἐχθρὸν τοῦ μισουμένου</quote>. <ref n="Andoc. 1.96">Andoc. or. 1 § 96</ref> (in a <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμιος ἔστω Ἀθηναίων.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμαυτῷ_187"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλον_187">. Some MSS. of Dem. (<ref n="Dem. 19.247">or. 19 § 247</ref>) give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτοῦ</quote> in the quotation, but here the dat. is clearly better. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θείμην_187"> ‘hold’ (rather than ‘make’): cp. <ref n="Tyrt. 12. 1">Tyrt. 12. 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἂν μνησαίμην οὔτ’ ἐν λόγῳ ἄνδρα τιθείμην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_σῴζουσα_189">, ‘who bears us safe.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζειν</quote> was esp. said of a ship or its captain: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 511D">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 511D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν.. ἐξ Αἰγίνης δεῦρο σώσῃ</quote> </cit>, if she (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ κυβερνητική</quote>) has carried us safely from Aegina to Athens.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτης_189"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> It is only while she remains upright, as we sail on board of her, that we can make real friends. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθῆς_189"> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθωσαν</quote> in 163) refers to the ship maintaining a safe stability, as opposed to capsizing: the contrast is given by <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὑπτίοις</emph>..σέλμασιν..ναυτίλλεται</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 716">716</ref> (where <ref target="cl716">see n.</ref>). So <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Fam. 12.25.5"> <title>Ep. ad Fam.</title> 12. 25. 5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">ut rectam teneamus (navem)</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_ποιούμεθα_189">, we make the friends (whom we really make): since friends made at the cost of endangering or wrecking the ship of the State cannot properly be considered friends at all: they are <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλοι ἄφιλοι</quote>. For the use of the art., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.40">Thuc. 2.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ..πάσχοντες εὖ ἀλλὰ δρῶντες κτώμεθα τοὺς φίλους</quote> </cit>. The thought is like that ascribed to Pericles by <ref n="Thuc. 2.60">Thuc. 2.60</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ γὰρ ἡγοῦμαι πόλιν πλείω ξύμπασαν ὀρθουμένην ὠφελεῖν τοὺς ἰδιώτας ἢ καθ’ ἕκαστον τῶν πολιτῶν εὐπραγοῦσαν ἀθρόαν δὲ σφαλλομένην. καλῶς μὲν γὰρ φερόμενος ἀνὴρ τὸ καθ’ ἑαυτὸν διαφθειρομένης τῆς πατρίδος οὐδὲν ἧσσον ξυναπόλλυται, κακοτυχῶν δὲ ἐν</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ..πάσχοντες εὖ ἀλλὰ δρῶντες κτώμεθα τοὺς
									φίλους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμοισι_191">, here, rules of conduct, principles, as <ref n="Soph. El. 1043"> <title>El.</title> 1043</ref>; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 907"> <title>O. C.</title> 907</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔξω_191">, pres. (used also in Attic prose): the Attic fut. was <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐξήσω</quote>. The pres. here expresses purpose (‘I intend to make Thebes prosperous’). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 731A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 731A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλονεικείτω δὲ ἡμῖν πᾶς πρὸς ἀρετήν· ὁ γὰρ τοιοῦτος αὔξει τὰς πόλεις</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
φιλονεικείτω δὲ ἡμῖν πᾶς πρὸς ἀρετήν· ὁ γὰρ
									τοιοῦτος αὔξει τὰς πόλεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφὰ_τῶνδε_192">, the more usu. constr.: but <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1262"> <title>O. C.</title> 1262</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφὰ τούτοισιν</quote> </cit> (n.). This use of the word is freq. in Attic prose, as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 276d"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 276 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσα τούτων ἀδελφά.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_Οἰδίπου_192">. In regard to origin, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> is properly said of parents, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> of ancestors: <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 12.81">Isocr. or. 12 § 81</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μὲν ἀπὸ θεῶν τοὺς δ’ ἐξ αὐτῶν τῶν θεῶν γεγονότας</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 466">Cp. 466</ref>, 471, 1066; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 260"> <title>Ph.</title> 260</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖ πατρὸς ἐξ Ἀχιλλέως</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 202"> <title>Ai.</title> 202</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονίων ἀπ’ Ἐρεχθειδᾶν</quote> </cit>. But poetry oft. has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> of the parent, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 571"> <title>O. C.</title> 571</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀφ’ ὅτου πατρὸς γεγώς</quote> </cit>: while, again, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> oft. denotes merely the stock (including progenitors above the parent): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1056">cp. 1056</ref>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαθοὶ καὶ ἐξ ἀγαθῶν</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 246a"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 246 A</ref>), etc. The poetical indifference on this point is well seen in fr. 104, where <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μὲν δυσσεβεῖς κακῶν τ’ ἄπο | βλαστόντας</quote> is opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δ’ ὄντας ἐσθλοὺς ἔκ τε γενναίων ἅμα | γεγῶτας</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς μὲν ἀπὸ θεῶν τοὺς δ’ ἐξ αὐτῶν τῶν θεῶν
									γεγονότας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορί_195"> was the ordinary Attic form, occurring in prose (as <ref n="Thuc. 1.128">Thuc. 1.128</ref>, 4. 98), and was prob. used by Soph. as well as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόρει_195">, which metre requires in <ref n="Soph. OC 620"> <title>O. C.</title> 620</ref> (n.), 1314, 1386: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1304"> <title>O. C.</title> 1304</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἐφαγνίσαι_196">, to perform all due rites over the grave; i.e. to make the proper offerings to the dead (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγίσματα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 402"> <title>O. C.</title> 402</ref> n.), esp. libations, <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_196"> in the compound cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 440"> <title>El.</title> 440</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χοὰς | οὐκ ἄν ποθ’, ὅν γ’ ἔκτεινε, τῷδ’ ἐπέστεφε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 484"> <title>O. C.</title> 484</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάσδ’ ἐπεύχεσθαι λιτάς</quote> </cit>, i.e. ‘over’ the rite. </gloss>

data.quote:
χοὰς | οὐκ ἄν ποθ’, ὅν γ’ ἔκτεινε, τῷδ’
									ἐπέστεφε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφαγνίσαι__196"> is the reading of L; the force of the prep. is rightly given in the glosses, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τῷ τάφῳ ἁγνίσαι τὰ πάντα</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τῷ τάφῳ ὁσίως ποιῆσαι</quote>. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαγνίζειν</quote> is not elsewhere extant, there seems no reason to question it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφαγνίσαι_196"> has been preferred by some, merely because that compound is recognised by the old grammarians (Suid. , Hesych., Phrynichus in Bekk. <title>Anecd.</title> 26). But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαγνίζειν</quote> meant esp. to purify from guilt (<foreign xml:lang="lat">expiare</foreign>): <ref n="Paus. 2.31.8">Paus. 2.31.8</ref> (of Orestes at Troezen) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκάθαιρον καὶ εἱστίων, ἐς ὃ ἀφήγνισαν</quote>, ‘until they had purged him.’ Similarly in midd., <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 1145">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 1145</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἂν θεοῖσι τοῖσι νερτέροις | ἀφαγνίσηται</quote> </cit>, until she has made expiatory offerings to them. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαγνεύω</quote> in <ref n="Plut. Mor. 943">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 943</ref>C (the souls of the good are to suffer only so long) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον ἀφαγνεῦσαι καὶ ἀποπνεῦσαι τοὺς ἀπὸ τοῦ σώματος..μιασμούς</quote> (‘to purge away’: perh. we should read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαγνίσαι</quote>). The force of </gloss>

data.quote:
πρὶν ἂν θεοῖσι τοῖσι νερτέροις |
									ἀφαγνίσηται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπό_196"> is thus the same as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφοσιοῦσθαι</quote>, and in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφιερώμεθα</quote> as used by <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 451">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 451</ref> (‘I have been hallowed,’ i.e. purified). The case of <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀφαγιστεύσας</quote> below (247) is different from that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαγνίσαι</quote> here: it is, I think, for <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἀφαγιστεύσας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἀρίστοις_197">, implying that, in his case, the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόχειρ μίασμα</quote> (172) is to make no difference. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1379"> <title>Ai.</title> 1379</ref>, where Odysseus offers to join in funeral honours to Ajax (notwithstanding his offence), <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν ἐλλείπειν, ὅσον | χρὴ τοῖς ἀριστοῖς ἀνδράσιν πονεῖν βροτούς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρχεται_κάτω_197">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> were supposed to pass through the earth, and to be drunk by the spirits of the dead: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 164">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 164</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει μὲν ἤδη γαπότους χοὰς πατήρ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.94"> <title>Od.</title> 10.94</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hec. 535">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 535</ref> ff. The dat., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 711"> <title>O. T.</title> 711</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρησμὸς..ἦλθε Λαΐῳ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πολυνείκην_198"> here, but <quote xml:lang="grc">Πολυνείκη</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 375"> <title>O. C.</title> 375</ref>. Both forms are sound. From about 400 B.C. the Attic tendency of proper names in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ης</quote> was to pass from the 3rd to the 1st declension. Attic inscriptions of <foreign xml:lang="lat">circ.</foreign> 410—350 B.C. give the acc. in -<quote xml:lang="grc">ην</quote> more often than that in -<quote xml:lang="grc">η</quote>. From c. 350 to 30 B.C. the gen. in -<quote xml:lang="grc">ου</quote> is far more frequent than that in -<quote xml:lang="grc">ους</quote>. Even proper names in -<quote xml:lang="grc">κλέης</quote>, which kept the acc. in -<quote xml:lang="grc">κλέα</quote> to c. 300 B.C., afterwards formed it in -<quote xml:lang="grc">κλῆν</quote>. (No Attic inscript. gives -<quote xml:lang="grc">κλῆ</quote>.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_198">: see n. on 32 <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω γὰρ κἀμέ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γτν_πρῆσαι_199">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 582">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 582</ref>(of Polyneices) <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν πατρῴαν καὶ θεοὺς τοὺς ἐγγενεῖς | πορθεῖν, στράτευμ’ ἐπακτὸν ἐμβεβληκότα</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρσαι</quote>, for <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῆσαι</quote>, would be a needless change here. ‘To burn his country’ means ‘to burn his native city’: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1421"> <title>O. C.</title> 1421</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτραν κατασκάψαντι</quote> </cit>, when thou hast laid thy native city in ruins. <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς πρῆσαι</quote> is to burn the gods' temples and the ancient wooden images (<quote xml:lang="grc">βρέτη</quote>) therein: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.109">Her. 8.109</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπιμπράς τε καὶ καταβάλλων τῶν θεῶν τὰ ἀγάλματα</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 809">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 809</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θεῶν βρέτη | ᾐδοῦντο συλᾶν οὐδὲ πιμπράναι νεώς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐμπιμπράς τε καὶ καταβάλλων τῶν θεῶν τὰ
									ἀγάλματα

οὐ θεῶν βρέτη | ᾐδοῦντο συλᾶν οὐδὲ πιμπράναι
									νεώς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_ἐγγενεῖς_199">, of the <emph>race</emph>, here in a large sense, of the Cadmean stock: while <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ πατρῷοι</quote> are usu. rather the gods of one's own <emph>family</emph> (<ref n="Soph. OC 756"> <title>O. C.</title> 756</ref> n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 428"> <title>El.</title> 428</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός νυν θεῶν σε λίσσομαι τῶν ἐγγενῶν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατελθών_199">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">καταχθεὶς ὑπὸ τῆς πόλεως</quote>: on the shield of Polyneices, Dikè was portrayed saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάξω δ’ ἄνδρα τόνδε</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 647">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 647</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γθέλησε_δέ_199">, rhetor. epanaphora (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 610"> <title>O. C.</title> 610</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνει μέν . . φθίνει δέ</quote> </cit>). Since <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσασθαι</quote> cannot govern <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν . . καὶ θεούς</quote>, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠθέλησε_μὲν_199"> should in strictness have preceded <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρῆσαι_199">. Prose would have used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπρῆσαι</quote>, though Thuc. has the pres. part. of the simple form (6. 94 <quote xml:lang="grc">πίμπραντες</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἄκρας_199">, here in its proper sense, of a town being sacked ‘from top to bottom’ (<ref n="Hom. Il. 13.772"> <title>Il.</title> 13.772</ref>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1241"> <title>O. C.</title> 1241</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἵματος_πάσασθαι_199"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πατέομαι</quote>), denoting the extreme of savage hatred; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 4.35"> <title>Il.</title> 4.35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠμὸν βεβρώθοις Πρίαμον</quote> </cit>: 24. 212 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ ἐγὼ μέσον ἧπαρ ἔχοιμι | ἐσθέμεναι</quote>: Theogn. 349 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν εἴη μέλαν αἶμα πιεῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_ἄγειν_199">, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μέν</quote> had preceded <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵματος</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1228"> <title>O. T.</title> 1228</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσα | κεύθει, τὰ δ’ αὐτίκ’ εἰς τὸ φῶς φανεῖ</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 117"> <title>Tr.</title> 117</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφει, τὸ δ’ αὔξει</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.157"> <title>Il.</title> 22.157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παραδραμέτην, φεύγων, ὁ δ’ ὄπισθε διώκων</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅσα | κεύθει, τὰ δ’ αὐτίκ’ εἰς τὸ φῶς
									φανεῖ

παραδραμέτην, φεύγων, ὁ δ’ ὄπισθε
									διώκων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκεκηρῦχθαι_τάφῳ_203"> can be explained only by supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote> or the like. But in 196 <quote xml:lang="grc">κρύψαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαγνίσαι</quote> depended on <quote xml:lang="grc">κηρύξας ἔχω</quote> in 192 (I have proclaimed to the people). It would be intolerably awkward to communicate the second part of the proclamation in an oblique form with the principal verb unexpressed: —‘(<title>I tell you that</title>) it has been proclaimed.’ The choice lies between (1) Musgrave's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκεκήρυκται_τάφῳ_203">, and (2) Nauck's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκεκηρῦχθαι_λέγω_203">. In favour of (1) remark:—(<emph>a</emph>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφῳ_203"> is not, indeed, necessary with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτερίζειν_203">, which can be used absolutely; as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.455"> <title>Il.</title> 11.455</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰρ ἐπεί κε θάνω, κτεριοῦσί με δῖοι Ἀχαιοί</quote> </cit>, ‘will give me funeral honours’: but, as the main point is that a <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφος</quote> is given to one brother and refused to the other, the addition of </gloss>

data.quote:
αὐτὰρ ἐπεί κε θάνω, κτεριοῦσί με δῖοι
									Ἀχαιοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφῳ_203_2"> to the more general term </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτερίζειν_203_2"> is plainly desirable here. (<emph>b</emph>) The misplacement of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτε_203"> is due to the thought of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κωκῦσαι_203"> having come only after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφῳ_203_3"> had been uttered (<quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε κτερίζειν μήτε</quote> having been preferred to <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κτερίζειν μηδέ</quote>), and is not bolder than (e.g.) the misplacement of <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1411"> <title>Ph.</title> 1411</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὴν τὴν Ἡρακλέους | ἀκοῇ τε κλύειν λεύσσειν τ’ ὄψιν</quote>. (<emph>c</emph>) The MS. error may have arisen from a reminiscence of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκεκηρῦχθαι</quote> in 27. The line of Carneades ( L. <ref n="D. L. 4.64">Diog. 4. 64</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον σχολῆς τῆσδ’ ἐκκεκηρῦχθαι λέγω</quote>, is no argument for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_203"> in the text of Sophocles. What could the parodist have made of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφῳ_203_4">? The tragic solemnity of the decree was the point of the parody, which uses </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκεκ_203">. in a different sense from the poet's (‘I proclaim that he is banished from this school’: <ref target="cl27">see on 27</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐᾶν_δ_205">': <ref target="cl29">see on 29</ref>. Construe, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθαπτον_ἰδεῖν_205">, mangled for all to see, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέμας_κυνῶνἐδεστόν_205">, in the body (acc. of respect) which birds and dogs devour. L favours </gloss>
lemma:
δέμας καὶ πρὸς οἰωνῶν καὶ πρὸς κυνῶν
									ἐδεστόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰκισθέντ_205">': but this is a point on which our MSS. have little weight. Reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰκισθέντ_205_2">', it would be also possible to take </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδεστόν_205"> as masc., with a slight pause after it; but this seems less good. With </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰκισθέν_δέμας_205"> is accus. in appos.: leave him unburied, a body eaten (etc.), and mangled. Some recent edd. prefer this.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέμας_205"> of a corpse, as 903, <ref n="Soph. El. 756"> <title>El.</title> 756</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 40">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 40</ref> etc.: in Hom. always of the living, who has <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote> only of the dead: in Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote> is said of either.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδεῖν_205">: the <emph>aor.</emph> inf., as in the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">θαῦμα ἰδέσθαι</quote>, since the aor. suggests the moment at which the startling sight catches the eye, whereas the pres. inf. would suggest continued gazing. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρόνημα_207">: on 176.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκ_ἐμοῦ_207">, by an act of mine (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 63">cp. 63</ref>, 93), while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν γ’ ἐμοί</quote> in a negative sentence=(not) if I can help it (<ref n="Soph. OC 153"> <title>O. C.</title> 153</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμὴν_ἐνδ_208">, shall <emph>have</emph> honour <emph>before</emph> the just, schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕξουσι πρὸ τῶν δικαίων</quote>. Such a constr. of <quote xml:lang="grc">προέχω</quote> occurs nowhere else. But the objection to rendering, ‘shall have the advantage of the just in honour’ (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμήν_208"> as acc. of respect) is that, after <quote xml:lang="grc">προέχειν</quote> in this sense, the <emph>point</emph> of advantage was regularly expressed by the <emph>dat.:</emph> see examples on <ref n="Soph. OC 1007"> <title>O. C.</title> 1007</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμῆ_208"> ( PallisA. ) is most probable, since either TIMHI or <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμηι</quote> might easily have become the accus. before the verb. Hermann read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέξους_208">' (which I do not understand) because of the hiatus (but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 351"> <title>O. T.</title> 351</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προεῖπας</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 107"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοέντας</quote> </cit>); and because the honours claimed for Polyneices are only equal, not superior. But Creon's meaning is explained by <ref n="Soph. Ant. 514">vv. 514 ff.</ref>: the honour is greater for a public foe than for a patriot. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμήσεται_210">: cp. <ref target="cl93">on 93.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀς_211"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_211"> in v. 212, not as thinking it certain, but because, with the least change, it gives a satisfactory construction. Soph. has this crasis in fr. 428 <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλων τε μέμψιν κεἰς θεοὺς ἁμαρτάνειν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 538B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 538B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παράνομόν τι δρᾶσαι ἢ εἰπεῖν εἰς αὐτούς</quote> </cit> (‘with regard to them’). For the place of the prep., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 367">cp. 367</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 734"> <title>O. T.</title> 734</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δελφῶν κἀπὸ Δαυλίας</quote> </cit>. With the MS. reading, the accusatives in v. 212 must be governed by </gloss>

data.quote:
παράνομόν τι δρᾶσαι ἢ εἰπεῖν εἰς
									αὐτούς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_ἀρέσκει_211"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ ταῦτα δρᾶσαι διανοεῖ</quote>. Greek was bold in constructions <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ σύνεσιν</quote>, and might possibly have tolerated this: but it seems improbable. In the apparently similar instances the periphrasis for the transitive verb always contains a noun directly suggestive of that verb: as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 572">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 572</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο κἄμ’ ἔχει πόθος = τοῦτο κἀγὼ ποθῶ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 814">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 814</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθορὰς... ψήφους ἔθεντο</quote> </cit>: <title>Suppl.</title> 533 <quote xml:lang="grc">γένος...νέωσον αἶνον</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 289"> <title>Theb.</title> 289</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζωπυροῦσι τάρβος ...λεών</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.81">Dem. or. 19 § 81</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθνάναι τῷ φόβῳ...ξένους</quote> </cit>. Nor can the accusatives in v. 212 be explained as mere accus. ‘of respect’; nor as if, by a euphemism, <quote xml:lang="grc">παθεῖν</quote> were understood.—There is much in favour of the view that </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῦτο κἄμ’ ἔχει πόθος = τοῦτο κἀγὼ
									ποθῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρέον_211"> in v. 211 has displaced an infin., such as <quote xml:lang="grc">παθεῖν, λαβεῖν, λαχεῖν, ποιεῖν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δρᾶν</quote>. In v. 1098 L has <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐβουλίας δεῖ, παῖ Μενοικέως, λαβεῖν</quote>, where later MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέον</quote> in place of <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>: see n. there. If <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέον</quote> is not genuine in v. 211, then it is much more likely to have been a mere gloss on <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖ Μενοικέως</quote> than a corruption of a similar word. The conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρεῖν</quote>, then, merits no preference; though the acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> could stand with it ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 714">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 714</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρούντων...τὰ πρόσφορα</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 699">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 699</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀκταῖς νιν κυρῶ</quote> </cit>).—Brunck wrote </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταὔτ_211">', understanding <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρέσκει</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτὰ σοί</quote>, ‘my view is the same as yours.’ But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_211"> is right. The Chorus say—‘Such is my lord's pleasure. And, of course, he can do as seems him good.’ Their tone is sufficiently interpreted by vv. 216, 220, 278. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1.119">Her. 1.119</ref> (Harpagus to Astyages) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφη... ἀρεστὸν εἶναι πᾶν τὸ ἂν βασιλεὺς ἔρδῃ</quote>. The Chorus do not oppose Creon; but they feel a secret misgiving; they wish at least to remain passive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντί_γ_213"> the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">που</quote> closely adheres to <quote xml:lang="grc">παντί</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the whole expression; as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1506"> <title>El.</title> 1506,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις πέρα πράσσειν γε τῶν νόμων θέλει</quote> </cit>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the whole phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρα πράσσειν</quote>. The transposition </gloss>

data.quote: ὅστις πέρα πράσσειν γε τῶν νόμων θέλει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_που_213"> is open to the objection that <quote xml:lang="grc">παντί</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote>, claims the chief emphasis. <quote xml:lang="grc">παντί που <emph>πάρεστι</emph> </quote> has also been proposed. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνεστι_213"> is slightly more suitable to this context, because more suggestive of <emph>tacit disapproval.</emph> ‘It is <emph>possible</emph> for you’ (‘but we doubt whether it is expedient’). <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστί σοι</quote> is generally said rather when the speaker means, ‘it is easy for you,’ or ‘it is open to you,’— in seconding a wish of the other person, or in making an offer to him. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 364"> <title>Ph.</title> 364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄλλα μὲν πάρεστί σοι | πατρῷ’ ἑλέσθαι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 766"> <title>O. T.</title> 766</ref>. — <quote xml:lang="grc">παντί που <emph>μέτεστί</emph> σοι</quote>, which some prefer, is still less suitable here. It would imply a right shared by the King with some other man or men (cp. on 48).—Though the antecedent (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῶν</quote>) to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡπόσοι_ζῶμεν_213"> is understood, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρι_213"> can stand at the end of the verse, since such a relative clause was felt almost as a noun-case: <ref target="cl35">see on 35</ref>.
