Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 560">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ θίγω δῆθ’ οἵ μ’ ἔφυσαν</quote> </cit>; (=<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν φυσάντων</quote>). </gloss>

data.quote: ἦ θίγω δῆθ’ οἵ μ’ ἔφυσαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἦτε_215"> can be explained only by an ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμελεῖσθε</quote> or the like. After verbs of ‘taking care,’ the usu. constr. is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> with fut. indic.; but <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἄν</quote> with subj. is sometimes found, as <ref n="Plat. Hipparch. 9. 2"> <title>Hipparch.</title> 9. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμελεῖσθαι ὡς ἂν πραχθῇ...ἢν μή τις ἐπιμελῆται ὡς ἂν ταῦτα περαίνηται</quote>. In elliptical phrases, where a precept or charge is given (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα</quote>, etc., being understood), the regular constr. is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> with fut. ind., as <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 1.21">Lys. or. 1. 21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως τοίνυν ταῦτα μηδεὶς ἀνθρώπων πεύσεται</quote> </cit>. The elliptical <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μή</quote> with subjunct. is different,—‘take care <emph>lest</emph>,’ —a deferential way of hinting an objection (<ref n="Plat. Crat. 430D">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 430D</ref>), like the simple elliptical <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> with subjunct. <ref n="Soph. OC 1180"> <title>O. C.</title> 1180</ref>. Since, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμελεῖσθαι</quote> could be followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἄν</quote> with subjunct., it is conceivable that Soph. should write <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἂν σκοποὶ ἦτε</quote> instead of the usual <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως σκοποὶ ἔσεσθε</quote>. I cannot think, with Wecklein, that the sentence is broken off, as though Creon said,—‘<title>In order that</title> ye may be watchers of my mandate,’—being about to add, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐπιχωρεῖτε</quote>.—Dindorf's </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅπως τοίνυν ταῦτα μηδεὶς ἀνθρώπων
									πεύσεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_εἶτε_215"> is supported by usage, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 765"> <title>O. T.</title> 765</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν μόλοι δῆθ’ ἡμὶν ἐν τάχει πάλιν</quote> </cit>; Nauck (<title>Cur. Eur.</title> 11. 79) refuses <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶμεν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτε</quote> to tragedy, but is not convincing. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1046"> <title>O. T.</title> 1046</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδεῖτ</quote> </cit>' (for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδείητε</quote>) is certain: and <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτε</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴητε</quote> is strictly parallel. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτε</quote> occurs in <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 21.195"> <title>Od.</title> 21.195</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖοί κ’ εἶτ’ Ὀδυσῆι<*> ἀμυνέμεν, εἴ ποθεν ἔλθοι</quote> </cit>; In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 921">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 921</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦμεν</quote> </cit> might replace <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶμεν</quote>: though in <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 349"> <title>Hipp.</title> 349</ref>, at least, the opt. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶμεν</quote> seems required. But <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν εἶτε</quote> is here less fitting than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἂν ἦτε</quote>, because a request is less suitable than an injunction.</gloss>

data.quote: πῶς ἂν μόλοι δῆθ’ ἡμὶν ἐν τάχει πάλιν

ποῖοί κ’ εἶτ’ Ὀδυσῆι<*> ἀμυνέμεν, εἴ
									ποθεν ἔλθοι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκοποί_215">, <foreign xml:lang="grc">φύλακες</foreign>, who watch to see that no one breaks the edict. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_215"> is better than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_215">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_εἰρ_215">., the commands: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1620">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1620</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφρονεῖν εἰρημένον</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.26">Her. 7.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴρητο συλλέγεσθαι...στρατόν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόθες_τοῦτο_216">, set him this as a task (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1249">cp. 1249</ref>), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαστάζειν_216">, for him to take in hand (<foreign xml:lang="lat">suscipiendum</foreign>); the act. inf. as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 231"> <title>O. C.</title> 231</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνον...ἀντιδίδωσιν ἔχειν</quote> </cit>. For the lit. sense of the verb cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 827"> <title>Ai.</title> 827</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς με βαστάσῃ | πεπτῶτα</quote> </cit> (raise me). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_γ_216">: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσκοποι τῶν ἀστῶν</quote> are still needed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἐπεντέλλοις_218"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτ’ ἂν εἴη...ὃ ἐπεντέλλεις</foreign>; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 647"> <title>O. C.</title> 647</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγ’ ἂν λέγοις δώρημα</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 26."> <title>Ph.</title> 26.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλῳ_218"> is a bad one, for the contrast is between commands, not persons; and an awkward ambiguity would arise, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_218"> might then seem to mean the watching of the corpse.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιχωρεῖν_218">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">accedere</foreign>, to join their side: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.107">Thuc. 4.107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξάμενος τοὺς ἐθελήσαντας ἐπιχωρῆσαι... κατὰ τὰς σπονδάς</quote> </cit>. <title>Mirab.</title> 133 <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ τῷ ἐπιγράμματι ἐπεχώρησε καὶ ὁ τόπος ἐκεῖνος</quote> (corroborated it). </gloss>

data.quote:
δεξάμενος τοὺς ἐθελήσαντας ἐπιχωρῆσαι... κατὰ
									τὰς σπονδάς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπιστοῦσιν_218"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπειθοῦσιν</foreign>: 381, 656. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_218"> cogn. acc.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 66">cp. 66</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_220"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρᾷ_220">, a constr. most freq. in negative sentences, usu. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Dem. 1.15">Dem. or. 1 § 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς οὕτως εὐήθης ἐστὶν...ὅστις ἀγνοεῖ</quote> </cit>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἄν</quote> and opt. (<cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 360B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 360B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς ἂν γένοιτο οὕτως ἀδαμάντινος, ὃς ἂν μείνειεν</quote> </cit>). But it occurs also in affirmative sentences, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 170">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοῦτο δ’ ἥκεις ἀμαθίας ...ἣ...τολμᾷς</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 4.52">Her. 4.52</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδεὶς ἂν γένοιτο οὕτως ἀδαμάντινος, ὃς ἂν
									μείνειεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_220"> (lit., ‘and verily’) here confirms the last speaker's remark by adding an assurance that disobedience does indeed mean death; while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_220"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μισθός_220"> emphasises that word. ‘And I can tell you that the</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κέρδος_220">, ‘gain,’ i.e., as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδων</quote> shows, the prospect of gain, with the generic art. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1242">cp. 1242</ref>): so fr. 749 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κέρδος ἡδύ, κἂν ἀπὸ ψευδῶν ἴῃ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διώλεσεν_220">, gnomic aor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶ_οὐχ_223">: cp. on 96.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_223"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅτι</foreign>, as <ref n="Soph. OT 548"> <title>O. T.</title> 548</ref>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 963"> <title>El.</title> 963</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηκέτ’ ἐλπίσῃς ὅπως | τεύξει</quote> </cit>. This use is rare in Attic prose (for after <quote xml:lang="grc">θαυμάζω</quote>, etc., <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> =‘how’), though freq. in Her., as <ref n="Hdt. 2.49">2. 49</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ φήσω ὅκως...ἔλαβον</quote>. Yet cp. <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 296E">Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 296 E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω ὑμῖν πῶς ἀμφισβητοίην...ὅπως οὐ πάντα ἐγὼ ἐπίσταμαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάχους_ὕπο_223"> is the reading of the MSS. Aristotle quotes this verse as an example of a <quote xml:lang="grc">προοίμιον</quote> used by the speaker to avert a danger from himself, and gives it thus:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναξ, ἐρῶ μὲν οὐχ ὅπως σπουδῆς ὔπο</quote> ( <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 3. 14"> <title>Rhet.</title> 3. 14</ref>§ 10). Hence some edd. adopt <quote xml:lang="grc">σπουδῆς</quote>, as coming from a source older than our MSS. But, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάχους_223"> is free from objection, such a change is unwarrantable. Aristotle's quotations seem to have been usually made from memory, and his memory was not infallible. To take only three examples cited by Bellermann, we find: (1) <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 256"> <title>El.</title> 256</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ βία γὰρ ταῦτ’ ἀναγκάζει με δρᾶν</quote> </cit>, quoted <title>Metaphys.</title> 4. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ βία με ταῦτ’ ἀναγκάζει ποιεῖν</quote>: (2) <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 774"> <title>O. T.</title> 774</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ πατὴρ μὲν Πόλυβος ἦν</quote> </cit>, quoted <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 3. 14"> <title>Rhet.</title> 3. 14</ref>§ 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ πατὴρ ἦν Πόλυβος</quote>: (3) <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 911"> <title>Ant.</title> 911</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸς δ’ ἐν Ἅιδου καὶ πατρὸς κεκευθότοιν</quote> </cit>, quoted <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 3. 16"> <title>Rhet.</title> 3. 16</ref>§ 9 with <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβηκότων</quote> as last word. So <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.592"> <title>Il.</title> 9.592</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κήδε’ ὅσ’ ἀνθρώποισι πέλει τῶν ἄστυ ἁλῴη· ἄνδρας μὲν κτείνουσι</quote> </cit>, is quoted <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 1.7"> <title>Rhet.</title> 1. 7</ref>§ 31 with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσα κάκ’</quote> substituted for the first two words, and <quote xml:lang="grc">λαοὶ μὲν φθινύθουσι</quote> for the last three. </gloss>

data.quote: ἀλλ’ ἡ βία γὰρ ταῦτ’ ἀναγκάζει με δρᾶν

μητρὸς δ’ ἐν Ἅιδου καὶ πατρὸς
									κεκευθότοιν

κήδε’ ὅσ’ ἀνθρώποισι πέλει τῶν ἄστυ ἁλῴη·
									ἄνδρας μὲν κτείνουσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξάρας_224"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>), aor. part., not pres., because, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ</quote>- shows, the notion is, ‘having set in nimble movement’ (at starting). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 342">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 342</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κοῦφον αἴρῃ βῆμ’ ἐς Ἀργείων στρατόν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ κοῦφον αἴρῃ βῆμ’ ἐς Ἀργείων
									στρατόν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φροντίδων_224">, possessive gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστάσεις_224">, halts belonging to thoughts, i.e. caused by them. Others understand, ‘halts <emph>for</emph> thought’ (made in order to reflect),— which is less simple. Cp. <title>De Anim.</title> 1. 3 (p. 407a 32) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ νόησις ἔοικεν ἠρεμήσει τινὶ καὶ ἐπιστάσει</quote> (halt) <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἢ κινήσει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁδοῖς_224">, locative dat.; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 553"> <title>O. C.</title> 553</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῖς | ἐν ταῖσδ</quote> </cit>', ‘in this my coming.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυκλῶν_224"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">περιστρέφων</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχὴ_ηὔδα_227"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The naïveté consists in the direct quotation of what his <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχή</quote> said, rather than in the statement that it spoke; thus <ref n="Hor. S. 1.2.68">Hor. <title>Sat.</title> 1. 2. 68</ref> (quoted by Schneid.) is really similar,—<quote xml:lang="lat">Huic si...Diceret haec animus.</quote> Take </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_227"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μυθουμένη_227"> only. I do not think that <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὔδα...μυθουμένη</quote> was meant to mark garrulity; the language is not homely enough: rather it is simply,— ‘found a voice, speaking many things.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφη λέγων</quote> is not similar (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 757"> <title>Ai.</title> 757</ref>).—Cp. Launcelot Gobbo in <cit> <bibl n="Shak. MV 2.2">Shaksp. <title>Merch.</title> 2. 2:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">Certainly my conscience will serve me to run from this few my master. The fiend is at mine elbow and tempts me...My conscience says, ‘Launcelot, budge not.’ ‘Budge,’ says the fiend. ‘Budge not,’ says my conscience.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Certainly my conscience will serve me to run
									from this few my master. The fiend is at mine elbow and tempts
									me...My conscience says, ‘Launcelot, budge not.’ ‘Budge,’ says
									the fiend. ‘Budge not,’ says my conscience.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλας_τλήμων_228">, nom., not voc., because each is rather a comment (‘hapless that thou art!’) than properly an address: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 185"> <title>O. C.</title> 185</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τλάμων</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 753"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 753</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τάλας ἐγώ</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 61">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ μῶρος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μένεις_228"> is better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μενεῖς_228">, since, ‘<emph>are you</emph> tarrying again?’ (his halts having been frequent, 225) is more graphic than, ‘<emph>will you</emph> tarry again?’— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_228"> cannot mean here, ‘on the contrary’ (i.e. instead of going on).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_οὐκ_228">, as <ref n="Soph. OT 937"> <title>O. T.</title> 937</ref>, 976, etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλγυνεῖ_228">, pass.: cp. on 93 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθαρεῖ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλίσσων_231">, turning over and over in the mind: cp. on 158 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρέσσων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνυτον_231"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ὁδόν</quote>), gradually made my way (impf.); whereas <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤνυσα</quote> would have suited a quick journey. Cp. this impf. in <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 127C">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 127C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμῇ ταύτῃ ἤνυτον</quote> </cit>, ‘they could make no progress by that means.’ Soph. has this tense also in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 319"> <title>Tr.</title> 319</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον ἤνυτον</quote>): cp. below, 805. In <ref n="Dem. 21.104">Dem. or. 21 § 104</ref> our MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἤνυε</quote>. For the use of the verb in ref. to journeys, cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2.97">Thuc. 2.97</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδὸς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμερῶν ἀνδρὶ εὐζώνῳ τριῶν καὶ δέκα ἀνύσαι</quote>. The Attic <emph>pres.</emph> seems to have been <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύτω</quote> as=‘to accomplish,’ or ‘to make way,’ but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύειν</quote> as=‘to hasten.’ <title>Plut.</title> 413 <quote xml:lang="grc">μή νυν διάτριβ’ ἀλλ’ ἄνυε</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 606"> <title>Ran.</title> 606</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύετον</quote>: though in <title>Plut.</title> 606 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μέλλειν | χρή σ’, ἀλλ’ ἀνύειν</quote>, some MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύτειν</quote> (see Pors. on <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 463"> <title>Phoen.</title> 463</ref>). This is the distinction meant by the grammarian in Bekk. <title>Anecd.</title> 411. 28 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνύττειν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sic</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Ἀττικοὶ ὅπερ ἡμεῖς, ἁνύειν δὲ τὸ σπεύδειν</quote>. (The aspirated forms lack good evidence.) Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρύω</quote>, Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρύτω</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχολῇ_βραδύς_231">, reluctantly and slowly; the opposite of <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν σπουδῇ ταχύς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1223"> <title>Ph.</title> 1223</ref>), with eagerness and speed. <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ</quote> oft.=‘at a slow pace’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">πορεύεσθαι</quote>, <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4.1.16">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 4.1.16</ref>; <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποχωρεῖν</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 3.78">Thuc. 3.78</ref>). As <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύς</quote> could mean ‘sluggish’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 306"> <title>O. C.</title> 306</ref>), we might here refer <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ</quote> to pace, and <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύς</quote> to reluctance; but, though the common use of <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ</quote> in regard to pace helps to make it suitable here, it is better, in this context, to give <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ</quote> the moral and <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύς</quote> the physical sense. For <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ</quote> combined with another word in such an expression, cp. Polyb. 8. 30 <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ καὶ βάδην ποιεῖσθαι τὴν πορείαν</quote>. There is no lack of point. Such a messenger ought to have come <quote xml:lang="grc">σπουδῇ ταχύς</quote>.—The conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>σπουδῇ</emph> βραδύς</foreign> is (I think) not only wrong but bad. It would mean, ‘slow in my haste’; eager to arrive, yet moving slowly. <quote xml:lang="grc">σπεῦδε βραδέως</quote>, to which it is supposed to allude, meant, ‘never remit your efforts, but advance circumspectly towards your aim’: <quote xml:lang="lat">festina lente</quote> (on which see Erasmus in the <title>Adagia</title>); <quote xml:lang="deu">Eile mit Weile;</quote> Goethe's <quote xml:lang="deu">Ohne Hast, ohne Rast.</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σπεῦδε βραδέως</quote> was a favourite maxim of Augustus, <ref n="Suet. Aug. 25">Suet. <title>Aug.</title> 25</ref>; Gellius 10. 11 § 5, on whom, as often, Macrobius has drawn, <ref n="Macr. 6.8.9"> <title>Sat.</title> 6. 8. 9</ref>.) The frightened and irresolute <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote>, —sent, sorely against his will, on a hateful errand,—had no more <quote xml:lang="grc">σπουδή</quote> than Mr Facing-both-ways. Wecklein, keeping <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ</quote>, supposes the Guard to mean, ‘this was a case of <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ βραδύς</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">σπουδῇ βραδύς</quote>,’—an improbably obscure and feeble jest at such a critical moment. The variant given by the schol., <foreign xml:lang="grc">σχολῇ <emph>ταχύς</emph> </foreign>, would be an oxymoron, designedly comic; ‘I took my time about hurrying,’ ‘'twas but a laggard haste that I made.’ A cheerful epigram of this sort would better suit a mind more at ease. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέλος_μέντοι_233">, <emph>at last</emph>, however; <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasising the word before it: <ref n="Soph. OT 442"> <title>O. T.</title> 442</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνίκησεν_233">, impers., as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.54">Thuc. 2.54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνίκησε δὲ...λοιμὸν εἰρῆσθαι</quote> </cit> (the opinion prevailed that...): <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.101">Her. 6.101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνίκα μὴ</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_234"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολεῖν_234">. In Attic prose a dat. of the person after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχομαι</quote> is freq., and oft. can be rendered (as here) only by ‘<emph>to</emph>,’ though it is properly rather a dat. of interest. Thus <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.13">Thuc. 1.13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀμεινοκλῆς Σαμίοις ἦλθε</quote> </cit>=‘ A. came to the Samians,’ though the primary notion is, ‘the Samians enjoyed the advantage of A. 's coming’ (to build triremes for them). So id. 1. 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς αὐτοῖς...ἦλθον ἄγγελοι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 321C">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 321C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποροῦντι δὲ αὐτῷ ἔρχεται Προμηθεύς</quote> </cit>. In poetry this dat. is freely used after verbs of motion, but the idea of interest is always traceable; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 186">cp. 186</ref> n. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 358">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦλθεν αὐτῷ Ζηνὸς ἄγρυπνον βέλος</quote> </cit>. So here, <quote xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν σοί</quote> is not strictly a mere equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν πρὸς σέ</quote>, but implies Creon's interest in the news. The notion is, ‘to come and place myself at your disposal.’ For the emphatic place of </gloss>

data.quote: ἀλλ’ ἦλθεν αὐτῷ Ζηνὸς ἄγρυπνον βέλος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_234_2">, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 273">cp. 273</ref> (and 46 n.): for the pause after the first syllable of the verse, 250, 464.—<quote xml:lang="grc">κεἰ</quote>, ‘<emph>and</emph> if’: not ‘<emph>even</emph> if.’ If <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> were taken as=‘<emph>even</emph>,’ there would be a very harsh asyndeton, whether the stop were at <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote>, or (as Nauck places it) after <quote xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν</quote>. It is true that <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> could mean ‘<emph>even</emph>,’ without causing an asyndeton, if we adopted Wecklein's tempting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσων_234"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσω_δ_234">': but the latter is confirmed by <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 302"> <title>O. T.</title> 302</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ μὴ βλέπεις, φρονεῖς δ’ ὅμως</quote> </cit> (where see n.),—<quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> introducing the apodosis after a concessive protasis. For </gloss>

data.quote: εἰ καὶ μὴ βλέπεις, φρονεῖς δ’ ὅμως

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_234"> as=‘<emph>and</emph> if,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 447"> <title>Ai.</title> 447</ref>, 1057. The transposition </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἴ_σοι_234"> is improbable, as destroying the significant emphasis and pause on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_234_3"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μηδέν_234">, what is as nought, — a tale of simple discomfiture: since he can only report the deed, without giving any clue to the doer. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1107"> <title>Tr.</title> 1107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν τὸ μηδὲν ὦ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1275"> <title>Ai.</title> 1275</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη τὸ μηδὲν ὄντας ἐν τροπῇ δορός</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἤδη τὸ μηδὲν ὄντας ἐν τροπῇ δορός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ἐλπίδος_235">, not ‘hope,’ but ‘the hope’—defined in next v.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεδραγμένος_235"> is certain. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.393"> <title>Il.</title> 13.393</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνιος δεδραγμένος</quote> </cit> (and 16. 486). <ref n="Diod. 12. 67">Diod. 12. 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δράξασθαι καιροῦ</quote>. (Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. Per 1.1">Shaks. <title>Per.</title>f. 1. 49</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">Gripe not at earthly joys.</quote> </cit>) Here the phrase is meant to be homely. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεφραγμένος_235"> was simply an attempt to mend L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπραγμένος_235">. We should require the dat. with it. The gen. cannot be justified by instances in which poetry uses a gen. of the agent without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote>, after pass. part., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 807"> <title>Ai.</title> 807</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φωτὸς ἠπατημένη</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 497">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 497</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγεὶς θυγατρός</quote> </cit>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἄν_235"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅτι οὐ</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μόρσιμον_235">: i.e. if you do kill me, then it was my destiny to be killed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθ_οὗ_237">, on account of which: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 264"> <title>O. T.</title> 264</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθ’ ὧν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 585"> <title>El.</title> 585</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθ’ ὅτου.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πρᾶγμ_237">: cp. on 67. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_237"> prefaces the statement: <ref n="Soph. OT 277"> <title>O. T.</title> 277</ref> n.: cp. below, 478, 999. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_στοχάζει_241"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘yes, you take your aim well, and seek to fence yourself round against the charge.’ The mark at which the man aims is his own safety; and this is explained by the next phrase. Commentators have made difficulties by assuming that the metaphors of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στοχάζει_241"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποφράγνυσαι_241"> must be harmonised into a single picture,—as of an archer shooting from covert. But in fact there is a rapid transition from one to the other; the second interprets the first; and all that is common to them is their military source. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στοχάζομαι_241"> was familiar in a sense akin to that which it has here: cp. <ref n="Plat. Lach. 178">Plat. <title>Lach.</title> 178</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">στοχαζόμεν οι τοῦ συμβουλευομένου ἄλλα λέγουσι παρὰ τὴν αὑτῶν δόξαν</quote> (trying to hit the thought of the person who consults them): Polyb. 6. 16 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀφείλουσι δὲ ἀεὶ ποιεῖν οἱ δήμαρχοι τὸ δοκοῦν τῷ δήμῳ καὶ μάλιστα στοχάζεσθαι τῆς τούτου βουλήσεως</quote>. So here the verb suggests a designing person, whose elaborate preamble covers a secret aim. Creon is quick to suspect bribery (221). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1033">Cp. 1033</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε τοξόται σκοποῦ | τοξεύετ’ ἀνδρὸς τοῦδε</quote>. Schneidewin thought that <quote xml:lang="grc">στοχάζει</quote> might here be a term of hunting or war, with ref. to the erecting of nets on poles, or of palisades. <quote xml:lang="grc">στοιχίζειν</quote> was so used, of nets in hunting ( <title>Cyneg.</title> 6. 8). But <quote xml:lang="grc">στοῖχος</quote> is from rt <quote xml:lang="grc">στιχ</quote>, while <quote xml:lang="grc">στόχος</quote> is from a probably distinct rt <quote xml:lang="grc">σταχ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">στάχυς</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">στεχ</quote> (perhaps lengthened from <quote xml:lang="grc">στα</quote>). In <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 3.14">Aristot. <title>Rhet.</title> 3. 14</ref>§ 10 the citation of v. 223 is immediately followed by the words <quote xml:lang="grc">τί φροιμιάζῃ</quote>; which Nauck (with Bergk) substitutes for <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γε στοχάζει</quote> here. But, though the schol. there says that Creon spoke them, they evidently belonged to some other passage, which Arist. cites as a second example: perh. to <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1162">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1162</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί φροιμιάζει νεοχμόν; ἐξαύδα σαφῶς</quote> </cit>. A schol. on <foreign xml:lang="lat">Arist. l. c.</foreign> says, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ τί φροιμιάζῃ ἐν τισὶ τῶν ἀντιγράφων οὐ κεῖται</quote> (i.e. in some MSS. of Arist. ); which looks as if the words had been deleted, in such copies, by readers who could not find them in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀποφράγνυσαι_241">. Inscriptions of the 5th cent. B.C. show <quote xml:lang="grc">φάρξαι</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">φράξαι</quote>) to have been the old Attic aor. (Meisterhans p. 89), and so <quote xml:lang="grc">ναύφαρκτος</quote>, etc.: but the analogy of the pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">φράττω</quote> recommends <quote xml:lang="grc">φράγνυμι</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">φάργνυμι</quote>. For the constr., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 8.104">Thuc. 8.104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐβούλοντο ἀποφάρξασθαι αὐτοὺς οἱ ἐναντίοι</quote> </cit> (to shut them off).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πρᾶγμα_241">, so soon after 239: cp. on 76. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηλοῖς_ς_242">: <ref target="cl20">see on 20</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δεινά_243">, dangers,—i.e. the <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ ἔπη</quote> (277) which he brings: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_243"> (yes, I am cautious) for, etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτ_243">', <foreign xml:lang="lat">tandem aliquando</foreign>, <ref n="Soph. OT 335"> <title>O. T.</title> 335</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπαλλαχθείς_243">, ‘having been removed,’ i.e. ‘having taken yourself off’; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Wasps 484">Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 484</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆπ’ ἄν, ὦ πρὸς τῶν θεῶν, ὑμεῖς ἀπαλλαχθεῖτέ μου</quote> </cit>; </gloss>

data.quote:
ἆπ’ ἄν, ὦ πρὸς τῶν θεῶν, ὑμεῖς ἀπαλλαχθεῖτέ
									μου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δή_245">, without more ado: <ref n="Soph. OC 31"> <title>O. C.</title> 31</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάψας_245">, because the essential rite was the throwing of earth on the body: cp. on 80, and below, 256.—The <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπί_245"> is ‘and’ (rather than ‘both,’ answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> in 247); it introduces an explanation of <quote xml:lang="grc">θάψας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διψίαν_245">, as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 495">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 495</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πηλοῦ ξύνουρος διψία κόνις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Lucr. 2.376">Lucr. 2. 376</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">bibula...arena.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀφαγιστεύσας_245"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἀ</quote>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_χρή_245">, i.e. having made the due offerings, perh. flowers (<ref n="Soph. El. 896"> <title>El.</title> 896</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφη</quote> of wool. We may doubt whether the poet thought of any <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> as having been poured by Antigone at this first visit; see n. on 429.—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαγιστεύσας</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαγιστεύσας</quote> are equally possible; but I prefer the former, because here, as v. 256 suggests, the idea is that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφοσιωσάμενος</quote>,—having avoided an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote> by satisfying religion: <ref target="cl196">see on 196</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαγνίσαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρῶν_248">: he does not think of women.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_δικέλλης_248">. For the enclitic <quote xml:lang="grc">του</quote> so placed, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 20">cp. 20</ref> n.: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_οὐ_248">, <ref n="Soph. OC 972"> <title>O. C.</title> 972</ref> n. <quote xml:lang="grc">γενηΐς, γενής</quote> (only here), is prop. an adj., an implement with a <quote xml:lang="grc">γένυς</quote> (jaw), or blade: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 485"> <title>El.</title> 485</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφάκης γένυς</quote> </cit>, the two-edged blade (of a bronze axe). Hesych. <quote xml:lang="grc">γενῇδα· ἀξίνην, πέλεκυν</quote> (referring, as the acc. shows, to some other passage): and here the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενῄς_248"> is prob. the same as the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίνη</quote> below (1109), which was to be used in raising the mound (1203). We may render ‘pickaxe,’ since this properly has a blade as well as a point. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενῄς_248_2"> would break the hard surface. Then the earth would be thrown up (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβολή</quote>) with the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκελλα_248">, which was a sort of heavy two-pronged hoe, used, like the Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat">ligo</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">bidens</foreign>, in hoeing up soil: the <quote xml:lang="grc">μάκελλα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μία, κέλλω</quote> to drive forward) being a like tool with one prong. The <quote xml:lang="grc">σμινύη</quote> was like the <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκελλα</quote>, a two-pronged hoe. ‘Mattock’ is the nearest word for it. ‘Spade’ would better suit <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμη</quote> (or the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">λίστρον</quote>), though this was prop. rather a shovel. For the combination cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. TIT 5.3.11">Shaks. <title>Tit. Andr.</title> 5. 3. 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">'Tis you must dig with mattock and with spade.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
'Tis you must dig with mattock and with
									spade.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δικ_ἐκβολή_248">, throwing up of earth by mattock (possessive gen. denoting the subject, <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ ἣν ἡ δ. ἐκβάλλει</quote>): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκβ_248">, abstract for concrete, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφή</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">θρέμμα</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1"> <title>O. T.</title> 1</ref> n.). In Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">μοδ. ἐκβολάδες</quote> is a mining term, ‘out-put.’— The epithets </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στύφλος_248"> (‘hard,’ <ref n="Soph. Ant. 139">cp. 139</ref>), and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χέρσος_248"> ‘dry,’ tell something which the preceding words, and the following </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρρώξ_248">, would not alone have told; viz. why no foot-prints were traceable.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπημαξευμένη_248">, lit. ‘traversed (i.e. furrowed) by a carriage’ with its (four) wheels, =<quote xml:lang="grc">τροχοῖς ἁμάξης κεχαραγμένη</quote>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_248">, not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_248">, since as Eusth. says (on <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.485"> <title>Il.</title> 18.485</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ...ἅμαξα οἱ μὲν παλαιοὶ ψιλοῦσι, οἱ μέντοι νεώτεροι Ἀττικοὶ ἐδάσυναν</quote>. (Cp. n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπήνη</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 753"> <title>O. T.</title> 753</ref>.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἦν_248">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> added to the predicate, where the subject has the art., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 618"> <title>O. T.</title> 618</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 491">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 491</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ σηματουργὸς δ’ οὔ τις εὐτελὴς ἄπ’ ἦν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 726">Aristoph. <title>Pl.</title> 726</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς φιλόπολίς τίς ἐσθ’ ὁ δαίμων καὶ σοφός</quote> </cit>. Not: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἐργάτης τις</quote> (the doer, whoever he is) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσημος ἦν</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κύριός τις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 288"> <title>O. C.</title> 288</ref> n.). </gloss>

data.quote: ὁ σηματουργὸς δ’ οὔ τις εὐτελὴς ἄπ’ ἦν

ὡς φιλόπολίς τίς ἐσθ’ ὁ δαίμων καὶ
									σοφός

lemma: ἀλλ’ ὁ ἐργάτης ἄσημός τις ἦν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἡμεροσκόπος_253">, the man who took the first watch of this day, was the first who had watched at all. If a sentinel had been near the body, Ant. must have been seen. The other men were somewhere near. (Afterwards, they all watched, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 413.">Ant. 413.</ref>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμεροσκ_253">, in prose <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεροφύλαξ</quote> (<ref n="Xen. Hell. 7.2.6">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 7.2.6</ref>), as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">νυκτοφύλαξ</quote> (id. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 2. 18"> <title>An.</title> 7. 2. 18</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείκνυσι_παρῆν_253">: historic pres. combined with past tense; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 1.6">Lys. or. 1 § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὴ δέ μοι παιδίον γίγνεται, ἐπίστευον ἤδη καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐμαυτοῦ ἐκείνῃ παρέδωκα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπειδὴ δέ μοι παιδίον γίγνεται, ἐπίστευον ἤδη
									καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐμαυτοῦ ἐκείνῃ παρέδωκα.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσχερές_253">, not merely ‘perplexing,’ but ‘distressing’ (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 1395"> <title>Ai.</title> 1395</ref>), since they foreboded punishment. So <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσχέρεια</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">molestia</foreign> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 473"> <title>Ph.</title> 473</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_μὲν_255"> answered by <quote xml:lang="grc">σημεῖα δ’</quote> (257).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυμβήρης_οὔ_255"> (cp. on 96), not entombed: i.e. there was no <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβος</quote>, indicating that the ashes had been buried beneath it (1203): the body itself lay there, though covered over with dust. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυμβ_255">, prop., ‘provided with a mound,’ but below, 946, merely=‘tomb-like’; and so in <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 889">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 889</ref>Euripides says <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβήρεις ἕδρας</quote>, ‘seat on a tomb.’—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λεπτὴ_ἐπῆν_255">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">λέπτῃ δὲ κόνει κεκαλυμμένος</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 545">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 545</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοξάζων μὲν οὔ, | τύχῃ δὲ...ὁρμισθείς</quote>): for this introduction of a new finite verb, where a participial clause was expected, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 813">cp. 813</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγος_ὥς_255"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τινος</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 629"> <title>O. T.</title> 629</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι κακῶς γ’ ἄρχοντος</quote> </cit>, n.): the gen. is not absol., but possessive, denoting the author: ‘as of (i.e. from) one avoiding.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγος_255">, the guilt incurred by one who passed by an unburied corpse without throwing earth on it: <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ γὰρ νεκρὸν ὁρῶντες ἄταφον, καὶ μὴ ἐπαμησάμενοι κόνιν, ἐναγεῖς εἶναι ἐδόκουν</quote>. Aelian <title>Var. Hist.</title> 5. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος καὶ οὗτος Ἀττικός, ὃς ἂν ἀτάφῳ περιτύχῃ σώματι ἀνθρώπου πάντως ἐπιβάλλειν αὐτῷ γῆν· θάπτειν δὲ πρὸς δυσμὰς βλέποντας</quote>. So, too, Aelian says of the hawk, <title>Hist. Anim.</title> 2. 49, <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκρὸν δὲ ἄνθρωπον ἰδὼν ἱέραξ, ὡς λόγος, πάντως ἐπιβάλλει γῆν τῷ ἀτάφῳ· καὶ τοῦτο μὲν αὐτῷ οὐ κελεύει Σόλων, ὡς Ἀθηναίους ἐπαίδευσε δρᾶν</quote> (though our schol. ascribes the precept to a prehistoric <quote xml:lang="grc">Βουζύγης</quote>): and of the elephant, <title>H. A.</title> 5. 49 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἐλέφαντα θεασάμενος ἐλέφας νεκρὸν οὐκ ἂν παρέλθοι, μὴ τῇ προβοσκίδι γῆν ἀρυσάμενος καὶ ἐπιβαλών, ὁσίαν τινὰ ἀπόρρητον ὑπὲρ τῆς κοινῆς φύσεως τελῶν</quote> (‘fulfilling some mysterious law of piety imposed by Nature’), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ φεύγων ἄγος· εἶναι γὰρ τὸ μὴ δρᾶσαι τοῦτο ἐναγές</quote>. It was remembered as a disgrace to Lysander that, having put to death some prisoners of war, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ ἀποθανοῦσιν ἐπήνεγκε γῆν</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 9.32.6">Paus. 9.32.6</ref>). Cp. id. 1. 32. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντως ὅσιον ἀνθρώπου νεκρὸν γῇ κρύψαι</quote>. <ref n="Hor. Carm. 1. 28. 33">Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 1. 28. 33</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">precibus non linquar inultis, Teque piacula nulla resolvent...licebit Iniecto ter pulvere curras.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρός_257">, here a wild beast, as dist. from domesticated animals (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1081">cp. 1081</ref>): more often the term excludes only birds and fishes.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="του_257"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρός_257_2"> also: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 370">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 370</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἐλπίδος γὰρ οὔτε του δόξης ὁρῶ | θάρσος παρ’ ἡμῖν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτ’ ἐλπίδος γὰρ οὔτε του δόξης ὁρῶ | θάρσος
									παρ’ ἡμῖν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_σπάσαντος_257">. The negatives in 257 affect <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθόντος</quote>: and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_257"> stands with <quote xml:lang="grc">σπάσαντος</quote> as if we had simply <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐλθόντος, οὐ σπάσαντος, σημεῖα ἦν. <emph>οὐ</emph> </quote> is not here an irregular substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote>, as in 250: this would be so only if we had <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἐλθόντος</quote>. Either <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ σπ</quote>. or <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε σπ</quote>. would be correct, but the latter would suppose an <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> understood before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθόντος</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπάσαντος_257"> of rending, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1003">cp. 1003</ref>. It could not mean, ‘having cast up earth’ over the body (as Triclinius took it). The point is that the body must have been covered before the beasts had had time to come. The poet has preferred this order to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπάσαντος_ἐλθόντος_257"> (i.e. ‘or even having come’), because, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἦλθον, καὶ ἔσπασαν ἄν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοι_φύλακα_259">. The regular form would be, <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις κακοῖς ἐρροθοῦμεν ἐν ἀλλήλοις, ἐλέγχοντες φύλαξ φύλακα</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος ἄλλον</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> being the part in apposition with the whole (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς</quote>). The irregularity of the form in the text is threefold. (1) For <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις κακοῖς ἐρροθοῦμεν</quote> we have an equiv. in sense, though not in grammar, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοι_ἐρρόθουν_259">. (2) In spite of this, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀλλήλοισιν</quote> is retained, whereas <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἡμῖν</quote> is now needed. (3) As a <emph>plur.</emph> part. would have been awkward after <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι</quote>, we have <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ <emph>ἐλέγχων</emph> φύλακα</quote>, which thus is virtually equiv. to a gen. absol., <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλακος ἐλέγχοντος φύλακα</quote>. Remark that, even in regular examples of partitive apposition, a participle, describing what all do, is sometimes thus made singular; <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 7.3.47">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 7.3.47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οί ἱππεῖς οἴχονταί μοι ἄλλος ἄλλῃ διώκων</quote> </cit> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">διώκοντες</quote>). It is only the first of these three points that this passage has in common with others to which it has been compared. ‘<title>They disputed</title>, some saying this, some that,’ often appears in Greek as ‘<emph>there was a dispute</emph>,’ etc., without causing <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ μὲν...οἱ δέ</quote> to be changed into the gen. absol. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.74">Her. 8.74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ ἐλέγετο,...οἱ μὲν ὡς...χρέον εἴη ἀποπλέειν, Ἀθηναῖοι δέ</quote> </cit> etc. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.23">Thuc. 4.23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ ἀμφοτέρων...ἐπολεμεῖτο, Ἀθηναῖοι μὲν περιπλέοντες..., Πελοποννήσιοι δέ</quote> </cit> etc. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 200">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 200</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στάσις τ’ ἐν ἀλλήλοισιν ὠροθύνετο, | οἱ μὲν θέλοντες...οἱ δέ</quote> </cit> etc. (This illustrates the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀλλήλ</quote>. here, but is less bold, since the noun is sing.) <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1462">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1462</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν δ’ ἔρις στρατηλάταις, | οἱ μὲν πατάξαι...οἱ δέ</quote> </cit> etc. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1131"> <title>Bacch.</title> 1131</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν δὲ πᾶσ’ ὁμοῦ βοή, | ὁ μὲν στενάζων... αἱ δέ</quote> etc. For </gloss>

data.quote:
οί ἱππεῖς οἴχονταί μοι ἄλλος ἄλλῃ
									διώκων

πολλὰ ἐλέγετο,...οἱ μὲν ὡς...χρέον εἴη
									ἀποπλέειν, Ἀθηναῖοι δέ

ὑπ’ ἀμφοτέρων...ἐπολεμεῖτο, Ἀθηναῖοι μὲν
									περιπλέοντες..., Πελοποννήσιοι δέ

στάσις τ’ ἐν ἀλλήλοισιν ὠροθύνετο, | οἱ μὲν
									θέλοντες...οἱ δέ

ἦν δ’ ἔρις στρατηλάταις, | οἱ μὲν πατάξαι...οἱ
									δέ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρρόθουν_259">, of a confused noise of angry tongues, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 290">cp. 290</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥόθος</quote> (onomatop.) is said of a rushing noise of waves, or of oars dashed into them, etc.: then, fig., <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 406">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσίδος</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλέγχων_259">, questioning (434, <ref n="Soph. OT 333"> <title>O. T.</title> 333</ref>, 783: <ref n="Soph. El. 1353"> <title>El.</title> 1353</ref>), here, in the sense of ‘accusing.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἂν_ἐγίγνετο_259">: and blows would have come at last,—had not the matter been settled by the proposal mentioned at 268. (Not, ‘blows <emph>would</emph> come,’ i.e. were <emph>often</emph> exchanged, as Nauck takes it.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελευτῶς_259">', ‘at last,’ the adverbial use, found even with another partic., as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.53">Thuc. 6.53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυραννίδα χαλεπὴν τελευτῶσαν γενομένην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_κωλύσων_259">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 297"> <title>O. T.</title> 297</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_οὑξειργ_262">, was the doer (in the belief of his comrades). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργής_262">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">manifestus facti</foreign>: <cit> <bibl> <title>O. T.</title> 535</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λῃστής τ’ ἐναργής.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_262"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκαστος</quote>), evolved from <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδείς</quote>: fr. 327 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ δ’ οὐδεὶς δοκεῖ | εἷναι, πένης ὤν, ἄνοσος, ἀλλ’ ἀεὶ νοσεῖν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 20.74">Dem. or. 20 § 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδεὶς φθόνῳ τὸ μέλλον ἀκούσῃ, ἀλλ’, ἂν ἀληθὲς ᾖ, σκοπείτω.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μηδεὶς φθόνῳ τὸ μέλλον ἀκούσῃ, ἀλλ’, ἂν ἀληθὲς
									ᾖ, σκοπείτω.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕφευγε_εἰδέναι_262">, ‘pleaded in defence that he knew nothing of it.’ For this pregnant use of <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγειν</quote> cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 390">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 390</ref>, where the Argive king says to the Danaides, whose cousins threaten to seize them under Egyptian law, <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ τοι δὲ φεύγειν κατὰ νόμους τοὺς οἴκοθεν | ὡς οὐκ ἔχουσι κῦρος οὐδὲν ἀμφὶ σοῦ</quote>: you must plead, in accordance with Egyptian law, that they have no right over you. So <quote xml:lang="lat">defendere</quote>, <title>In Pison.</title> 10. 5 <quote xml:lang="lat">si triumphum non cupiebas, cuius tandem rei te cupiditate arsisse defendes?</quote> (‘will you plead?’) Note that this use (like the absol. legal sense, ‘to be a defendant,’ from which it comes) was necessarily restricted to <emph>pres.</emph> and <emph>impf.</emph> Hence we must not cite, with Donaldson, <ref n="Dem. 27.1">Dem. or. 27 § 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος τοὺς υὲν σαφῶς εἰδότας τὰ ἡμέτερα ἔφυγε μηδὲν διαγνῶναι περὶ αὐτῶν, εἰς δ’ ὑμᾶς τοὺς οὐδὲν τῶν ἡμετέρων ἀκριβῶς ἐπισταμένους ἐλήλυθεν</quote> (which means: ‘he <emph>has avoided</emph> any decision on the case being given by those who knew our affairs thoroughly, but has come to you,’ etc.): nor, with Paley, <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 1.3.19">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 1.3.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαγόμενος θανάτου...ἀπέφυγεν, ὅτι οὐ προδοίη τὴν πόλιν ἀλλὰ σώσαι</quote> </cit>, i.e. ‘being put on trial for his life, <emph>he was acquitted</emph>, (on the plea) that he had not betrayed the city,’ etc.</gloss>

data.quote:
ὑπαγόμενος θανάτου...ἀπέφυγεν, ὅτι οὐ προδοίη
									τὴν πόλιν ἀλλὰ σώσαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_262"> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδέναι_262">, as after verbs of denying, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 572">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 572</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρνοῦντο μὴ πεπτωκέναι</quote> </cit>.—Only one other version is tenable, viz., ‘shrunk from knowing it’; but this could hardly be said in the sense, ‘shrunk from confessing that he knew it.’—Others understand <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἄλλους</quote> as subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδέναι</quote>, ‘shrunk from (the others) knowing it’ (‘entzog sich dem Wissen der anderen’), which is impossible. So also is Campb.'s version, ‘always <emph>escaped</emph>, so that we could not know him,’ which would, in the first place, require <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφυγε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μύδρους_264">, lumps of red-hot iron. <quote xml:lang="grc">μύδρος</quote>=<emph>a molten mass</emph>, from rt <quote xml:lang="grc">μυδ</quote>, whence <quote xml:lang="grc">μύδος</quote>, ‘moisture,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">μυδάω</quote>, etc. Cyril <title>Adv. Iulian.</title> 359 quotes this verse, after referring to a Chaldean custom of making an oath more solemn by causing those who took it to pass between the severed portions of a victim (<quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ μέσων... διχοτομημάτων</quote>). It is probably the oldest trace in Greek of ordeals analogous to the medieval ‘judgments of God.’ The word <quote xml:lang="grc">μύδρος</quote> occurs elsewhere in connection with a solemn sanction for an oath. In <ref n="Hdt. 1.165">Her. 1.165</ref> the Phocaeans <quote xml:lang="grc">μύδρον σιδήρεον κατεπόντωσαν</quote>, swearing not to return till it should float. <title>Aristid.</title> 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ Ἀριστείδης ὥρκισε μὲν τοὺς Ἕλληνας καὶ ὤμοσεν ὑπὲρ τῶν Ἀθηναίων</quote> (to observe the defensive league against Persia, 479 B.C., Grote 5. 257), <quote xml:lang="grc">μύδρους ἐμβαλὼν ἐπὶ ταῖς ἀραῖς</quote> (in sanction of the curses on traitors) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὴν θάλατταν</quote>. I conceive that in these passages, as elsewhere, <quote xml:lang="grc">μύδρος</quote> has its proper sense,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῦρ_διέρπειν_265"> must here refer to a definite ordeal, by walking through a fire. The idea, at least, of such an ordeal appears in the familiar Attic phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ πυρὸς ἰέναι</quote> (‘to go through fire and water’); <ref n="Plat. Sym. 4. 16">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 4. 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγωγ’ οὖν μετὰ Κλεινίου κἂν διὰ πυρὸς ἰοίην</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 133">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 133</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τοῦ πυρὸς | ἐθέλω βαδίζειν</quote> </cit>. But it is doubtful whether the actual use of any such ordeal in the historical age can be inferred from <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 54.40">Dem. or. 54 § 40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιοπιστότερος τοῦ κατὰ τῶν παίδων</quote> </cit> (by the lives of one's children, cp. <ref n="Dem. 29.26">or. 29 § 26</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμνύοντος καὶ διὰ τοῦ πυρός</quote>, i. e. swearing that one is ready to undergo the test by fire. It has been suggested that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰόντος</quote> has fallen out after <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρός</quote>, which seems improbable. But the phrase may be rhetorical. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 11.787">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 11. 787</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the Hirpi):</note> medium freti pietate per ignem Cultores multa premimus vestigia pruna.</quote> </cit>—Becker <ref n="Thphr. Char. 183"> <title>Char.</title> 183</ref> notices some other ordeals. There was a temple in Achaia, the priestess of which, before election, was proved by drinking ball's blood; if impure, she died (<ref n="Paus. 7.25.13">Paus. 7.25.13</ref>). Perjury, and some other crimes, were assayed by the accused mounting the steps of an altar for burnt sacrifice (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάρα</quote>): if he was guilty, flames appeared ( <title>Aeth.</title> 10. 8). Incontinence was tried by the test of entering a grotto of Pan at Ephesus (Achilles Tatius 8. 6).</gloss>

data.quote:
medium freti pietate per ignem Cultores
									multa premimus vestigia pruna.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὺς_ὁρκ_265">, to swear by the gods; the acc. is cognate (the god being identified with the oath), like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκον ὀμνύναι</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5.4.31">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5.4.31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα...ὄμνυμί σοι θεούς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρ_265">.: for the art., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 236">cp. 236</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_είργασμένῳ_265">: the conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_265">' is needless, since <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε</quote> can be understood before <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεύσαντι</quote>: see <ref n="Soph. OT 239"> <title>O. T.</title> 239</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλέον_268">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 40">cp. 40</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγει_268">, between two past tenses: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 254">cp. 254</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_εἷς_268">. It is at first sight tempting to write <quote xml:lang="grc">τις, εἷς ὃς πάντας</quote>. But such emphasis on the idea of ‘one against all’ seems hardly appropriate here. And <quote xml:lang="grc">τις εἷς</quote> sometimes = <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς τις</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.61">Thuc. 6.61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί τινα μίαν νύκτα καὶ κατέδαρθον</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Plat. Soph. 235B">Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 235B</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτ’ ἐκφεύξεται</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ οὐ τοῦ γένους εἶναι τοῦ τῶν θαυματοποιῶν τις εἷς</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Parm. 145d"> <title>Parm.</title> 145</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν τινι γὰρ ἑνὶ μὴ ὂν οὐκ ἂν ἔτι που δύναιτο ἔν γε ἅπασιν εἶναι</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: καί τινα μίαν νύκτα καὶ κατέδαρθον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔτρεψεν_270">, impelled, here =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνάγκασε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 358"> <title>O. T.</title> 358</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_εἴχομεν_270"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘for we did not know how to gainsay him, nor how, <emph>if we did the thing</emph> (advised by him), we could prosper.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιφωνεῖν_270"> means <emph>opposing</emph> his suggestion, so </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῶντες_270"> means acting on it. Others join </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_δρῶντες_270">, ‘by what course of action.’ Since, however, a definite proposal was before them—viz., reference to Creon—we must then understand, ‘by what <emph>other</emph> course’ (than the proposed one). But the sense is, ‘We could not refute him, and, on the other hand, we dreaded your anger if we followed his advice.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴχομεν_270"> has the same sense in both clauses. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω</quote>, as=‘<emph>to know how</emph>,’ takes (1) an infin., or (2) a relat. clause with subjunct., as 42, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 705"> <title>Tr.</title> 705</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω...ποῖ γνώμης πέσω</quote> </cit>. This is merely an indirect form of the deliberative subjunct., <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ γνώμης πέσω</quote>; So here, <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξαιμεν</quote> would be <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξωμεν</quote> if the principal verb were in a primary tense. The direct question would be, <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς καλῶς πράξωμεν</quote>; Carefully distinguish <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> with optat. after this <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω</quote>, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 991"> <title>Tr.</title> 991</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἔχω πῶς ἂν | στέρξαιμι</quote> </cit>, I know not how I <emph>could.</emph> The two constructions are combined again in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 428"> <title>Ai.</title> 428</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι σ’ ἀπείργειν οὐδ’ ὅπως ἐῶ λέγειν | ἔχω</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ γὰρ ἔχω πῶς ἂν | στέρξαιμι

οὔτοι σ’ ἀπείργειν οὐδ’ ὅπως ἐῶ λέγειν |
									ἔχω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_273"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 234">cp. 234</ref>), rare for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς σέ</quote>. After <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναφέρω τι</quote> the pers. is usu. expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴς τιvα</quote> (less oft. by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά τιvα</quote>). But Lysias has the dat. in <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.84">or. 12. 84</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς τὰς ἀπολογίας ἀνοίσει</quote> </cit>, ‘to whom he will carry back his defensive pleas’ (i.e. ‘on whom he will lay the blame,’ and so, again, in <ref n="Lys. 7.17">or. 7 § 17</ref>): yet <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.64"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς... ἀπολογίας εἰς ἐκεῖνον ἀναφερομένας</quote> </cit>. In Mod. Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναφορά</quote> is used of an official ‘report.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_ἐνίκα_273">: <ref target="cl233">see on 233</ref>: the impf. differs from the aor. only as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδόκει</quote> (‘seemed good’) from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξε</quote> (‘was resolved’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλος_273">, perh. taken by shaking lots in a helmet (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 396">cp. 396</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1285"> <title>Ai.</title> 1285</ref>): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθαιρεῖ_273">, reduces, i.e. ‘condemns’: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.37">Lys. or. 13 § 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν...ψῆφον...τὴν...καθαιροῦσαν</quote> </cit>, the vote of condemnation.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀγαθόν_273">, iron., whereas his second errand is a true <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρμαιον</quote> (397).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκων_ἑκοῦσιν_273">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 198"> <title>Tr.</title> 198</ref> (the herald detained against his will by a throng of questioners) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ έκὼν ἑκοῦσι δὲ | ξύνεστιν.—οἶδ’ ὅτι</quote>, adverbial (‘doubtless’), like <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆλον ὅτι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 758">cp. 758</ref>: so used even in the middle of a clause, as <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 9.1">Dem. or. 9 § 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων, οἶδ’ ὅτι, φησάντων γ’ ἄν</quote> </cit> (‘when all, I know, would certainly admit,’ etc.). </gloss>

data.quote: πάντων, οἶδ’ ὅτι, φησάντων γ’ ἄν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_278">, ethic dat., ‘for me,’ rather than dat. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεύει_278"> as=‘advises’; the latter dat. is rare (<cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 697">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 697</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστοῖς...βουλεύω σέβειν</quote> </cit>); in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 807"> <title>Tr.</title> 807</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1055"> <title>Ai.</title> 1055</ref> the dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεύω</quote> is a dat. of interest (to plot <emph>against</emph> one). In poetry the act. can mean, not only ‘to form a plan’ (<ref n="Soph. OT 619"> <title>O. T.</title> 619</ref>), or to <emph>give</emph> counsel (<ref n="Soph. OT 1417"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1417</ref>), but also, like the midd., to deliberate.—With </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_278"> supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1253">cp. 1253</ref>; <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 145">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 145</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ παίζων ἔλεγεν</quote>, look <emph>whether he did not</emph> speak (i.e. I suspect that he spoke) in jest. To supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾖ</quote> is also possible (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1180"> <title>O. C.</title> 1180</ref>), but less fitting here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_278">, adv., ‘perchance’: <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεήλατον_278"> sent by gods (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 992"> <title>O. T.</title> 992</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεήλατον μάντευμα</quote> </cit>), <emph>i.e. wrought</emph> by them (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 285">cp. 285</ref>), since there was no trace of human agency (249): not, imposed upon a human agent by a divine commission (as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 255"> <title>O. T.</title> 255</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶγμα θεήλατον</quote> </cit>). So in <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.667"> <title>Il.</title> 16.667</ref> Zeus provides supernaturally for the burial of Sarpedon. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC"> <title>O. C.</title> </ref> p. xxxv.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_ξύννοια_278">, the art. being equiv. to a possessive pron., as 1089 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν γλῶσσαν, τὸν νοῦν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 571E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 571E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς σύννοιαν αὐτὸς αὑτῷ ἀφικόμενος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_278">, i.e. ever since the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> spoke (249). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 289">Cp. 289</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_μεστῶσαι_280">, ‘before thou hast actually filled me with anger’: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_280"> has nothing to do with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρίν_280">, but belongs solely to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεστῶσαι_280">, a strong word, the stress on which makes it easier for the force of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_280_2"> to pass over the enclitic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_280">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 772"> <title>O. T.</title> 772</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ γὰρ ἂν καὶ μείζονι | λέξαιμ’ ἄν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 989"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 989</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίας δὲ καὶ γυναικὸς ἐκφοβεῖσθ’ ὕπερ</quote> </cit>; where in each case <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> goes with the verb. We must distinguish the ordinary combination <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν καί</quote>, ‘before <emph>even</emph>,’ which would be in place here only if Creon meant, ‘Cease, before you have <emph>so much as</emph> angered me’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 396"> <title>Tr.</title> 396</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾄσσεις, πρὶν ἡμᾶς κἀννεώσασθαι λόγους</quote> </cit> (before we have even renewed our talk): <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 1033">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 1033</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμπουσιν ἤδη ’πισκόπους | ἐς τὴν πόλιν, πρὶν καὶ τεθύσθαι τοῖς θεοῖς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 458B">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 458B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι..., πρὶν καὶ ὑμᾶς ἐλθεῖν,... επεδειξάμηv.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: τῷ γὰρ ἂν καὶ μείζονι | λέξαιμ’ ἄν
ποίας δὲ καὶ γυναικὸς ἐκφοβεῖσθ’ ὕπερ

πέμπουσιν ἤδη ’πισκόπους | ἐς τὴν πόλιν, πρὶν
									καὶ τεθύσθαι τοῖς θεοῖς

πάλαι..., πρὶν καὶ ὑμᾶς ἐλθεῖν,...
									επεδειξάμηv.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμέ_280"> would be unmeaning: no one else is angry.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεστῶσαι_280_2">: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 330E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 330E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποψίας...καὶ δείματος μεστός</quote> </cit>, and so often. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότερον_284"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Did they think him <emph>good?</emph> Or, thinking him <emph>bad</emph>, did they yet honour him?—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκρυπτον_284"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῇ</quote>) = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθαπτον</quote>: the word is specially suitable here to the covering with dust (256). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 621"> <title>O. C.</title> 621</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκρυμμένος νέκυς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 838"> <title>El.</title> 838</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυφθέντα</quote> </cit> (of Amphiaraus swallowed up by the earth). Bellermann cites an inscr. from Smyrna (<title>Rhein. Mus.</title> 1872, 27 p. 465) <quote xml:lang="grc">παίδων σε φίλαι χέρες, ὡς θέμις ἐστί, | κρύψαν</quote>. The impf. (‘were for burying’) refers to the motive present to the agent's mind when the act was undertaken: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 19">cp. 19</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέπεμπον</quote> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφικίονας_ναούς_284">, temples surrounded by columns, an epith. marking their stateliness and splendour, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 1099">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 1099</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν περιστύλοις δόμοις</quote> </cit> (of a temple), <title>I. T.</title> 406 <quote xml:lang="grc">περικίονας ναούς</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ναὸς περίστυλος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">περίπτερος</quote> (so called because the ceiling of the colonnade projected like a wing, from the cella) had a colonnade on each of its four sides: the <quote xml:lang="grc">ναὸς ἀμφιπρόστυλος</quote>, only on two (front and back): but, though the latter would satisfy the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφικίονας</quote>, the poet doubtless meant the former.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀναθήματα_284">, votive offerings, such as gold and silver vessels of all kinds; statues; bronze tripods, etc. (Cp.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆν_ἐκείνων_284"> (depending on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυρώσων_284">), i.e. the territory of Thebes (cp. on 199), since the land belongs to the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ ἐγχώριοι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πολιοῦχοι</quote>: not merely the <quote xml:lang="grc">τεμένη</quote> attached to their shrines.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμους_διασκεδῶν_284">, to scatter the laws abroad, i.e. to shatter the fabric of civil order: cp. Tennyson, <title>Red ruin</title>, <emph>and the breaking up of laws.</emph> <ref n="Cic. Agr. 2. 37">Cic. <title>Agr.</title> 2. 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">disturbare... legem.</quote> So <quote xml:lang="grc">διασκεδ</quote>. of breaking up a treaty (<ref n="Soph. OC 620"> <title>O. C.</title> 620</ref>), or a king's power (<ref n="Soph. OC 1341"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1341</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διασκεδῶν_284"> would suit <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναθήματα</quote>, but could not possibly be joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν</quote> (as if the latter meant ‘State’): hence it must be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμους</quote> only. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_θεούς_288"> owing to the natural emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς κακούς</quote>, the ambiguity is only grammatical. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.150">Her. 7.150</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ δέ σφεας παραλαμβάνειν τοὺς Ἕλληνας</quote> </cit>, oblique for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ δέ σφεας παρελάμβανον οἱ Ἕλληνες</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπεὶ δέ σφεας παραλαμβάνειν τοὺς
									Ἕλληνας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_289">, the edict, depending on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλις_φέροντες_289"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">aegre ferentes</foreign>): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_πάλαι_289">, even from the moment when it was proclaimed; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 279">cp. 279</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι</quote> only refers back to 249.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεως_ἄνδρες_289">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς τῆσδέ τις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 236"> <title>O. T.</title> 236</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρρόθουν_289">, muttered: 259n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_289">, against me, dat. of object, as after <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπαίνω, μέμφομαι</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρα_σείοντες_289">, ‘tossing the head,’ in defiant menace (so <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">caput quassans,</quote> <bibl n="Verg. A. 12.894">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 12. 894</bibl> </cit>), instead o going quietly under the yoke. <title>Mor</title> 170 E <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς τυράννους ἀσπάζονται,...ἀλλὰ μισοῦσι σιγῇ κάρα σείοντες</quote> (alluding to this v.). So, acc. to Suidas, <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign>, Soph. used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναχαιτίζειν</quote> (prop. said of a horse <emph>throwing the mane back</emph>, rearing) <emph>as</emph>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπειθεῖν καὶ ἀντιτείνειν</quote>, ‘to be restive.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπὸ_ζυγῷ_289">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1639">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1639</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύξω βαρείαις</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύγλαις</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόφον_289">, the back of the neck, a word used of draughtanimals (of the <emph>human</emph> nape, perh. only once, <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.573"> <title>Il.</title> 10.573</ref>): hence, fig., Eur. fr. 175 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις δὲ πρὸς τὸ πῖπτον εὐλόφως φέρει | τὸν δαίμον’, οὗτος ἧσσόν ἐστ’ ἀνόλβιος</quote>. id. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 302">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 302</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρτα τοι τοὐλεύθερον | ἐν τοῖς τοιούτοις δυσλόφως φέρει κακά</quote>, ‘impatiently.’ (<cit> <bibl n="Shak. 3H6 3.1.16">Shaksp. <title>Henry VI.</title> Pt. III. 3. 1. 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">yield not thy neck To fortune's yoke.</quote> </cit>)—Nauck writes the verse thus, <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶτον δικαίως εἶχον εὐλόφως φέρειν</quote>, because Eustathius, in alluding to it, once represents it by <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶτον εὐλόφως εἶχον</quote> (on <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.285"> <title>Od.</title> 5.285</ref>), and twice by <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶτον εὐλόφως φέρειν</quote> (on <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.573"> <title>Il.</title> 10.573</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.169"> <title>Od.</title> 10.169</ref>). But Eustath. was quoting, or rather paraphrasing, from memory, and confused our verse with Eur. fr. 175 (quoted above); also, perhaps, with Lycophron 776 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐλόφῳ νώτῳ φέρειν</quote>. His references to Sophocles are often loose and inexact. See Appendix.</gloss>

data.quote: yield not thy neck To fortune's yoke.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δικαίως_289">, loyally. Donaldson had a too ingenious view that the word here meant, ‘with</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέργειν_289">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">tolerare</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 486"> <title>Tr.</title> 486</ref>(Lichas advising Deianeira with regard to Iolè) <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργε τὴν γυναῖκα</quote>, <emph>be patient of her.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_293">, masc., the malcontents (for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_293">, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 63">cp. 63</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτους_293"> the watchers of the corpse: the pronouns joined as in 39 (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰργάσθαι_293"> is best taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεπίσταμαι_293">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1092">cp. 1092</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάμεσθα...αὐτὸν...λακεῖν</quote>. The inf., instead of the partic., with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσταμαι</quote>, seems unknown in Attic prose, except, of course, where the sense is ‘to know how.’ <ref n="Soph. Ant. 472">Cp. 472</ref>. The inf. might, indeed, depend on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρηγμένους_293">, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάγω</quote> (to induce) oft. takes an inf.: but (<emph>a</emph>) as a matter of fact, an infin. seems not to occur after <quote xml:lang="grc">παράγω</quote> in this sense: and (<emph>b</emph>) it may be noticed that Attic idiom often prefers the form, ‘induced by them, he did it,’ to, ‘he was induced by them to do it’: e.g. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 4.8.5">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 4.8.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὺς...λόγῳ παραχθέντες ἀπέκτειναν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 5.10">Dem. or. 5 § 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς ἐπαχθέντες ὑμεῖς...προεῖσθε Φωκέας</quote> </cit>. For this participial expression of the leading idea, see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1038"> <title>O. C.</title> 1038</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμισμ_296">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">institutum.</foreign> This primary and general sense of the word was almost confined to poetry ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 269">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 269</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλληνικὸν νόμισμα θυστάδος βοῆς</quote>), the special sense, ‘current coin,’ being the ordinary one. For the other sense, the usual word was <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμιμον</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος</quote>). Hence in <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 247">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 247</ref>, when Socrates says, <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ | ἡμῖν νόμισμ’ οὐκ ἔστι</quote>, Strepsiades rejoins, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ γὰρ ὄμνυτ’; ἢ | σιδαρέοισιν, ὥσπερ ἐν Βυζαντίῳ</quote> (i.e. if gods are not <emph>current</emph> with you, do you swear by iron coin?): where the schol. remarks that <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμισμα</quote> meant <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὲ μὲν τὸ νόμιμον ἔθος, ποτὲ δὲ τὸ κόμμα τοῦ τετυπωμένου χαλκοῦ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβλαστε_296">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 611"> <title>O. C.</title> 611</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστάνει δ’ ἀπιστία.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πορθεῖ_296">, ‘sacks’ (not merely, in a general sense, ‘ruins’): money invites attack, and often purchases betrayal: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 3. 16. 13">Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 3. 16. 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">diffidit urbium Portas uir Macedo et subruit aemulos Reges muneribus.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
diffidit urbium Portas uir Macedo et subruit
									aemulos Reges muneribus.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδ_296"> (after <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 39">cp. 39</ref>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνδρας_296">, individual citizens, as distinguished from <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεις. <emph>ἐξανίστ</emph>. <emph>δόμων</emph> </quote>, drives them from their cities by corrupt intrigue,—for which the <quote xml:lang="grc">στάσεις</quote> of democrat and oligarch in Greek cities gave many openings. The phrase is strikingly illustrated by the verses in which Timocreon of Rhodes, when an exile, assailed Themistocles ( <ref n="Plut. Them. 21">Plut. <title>Them.</title> 21</ref>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μὲν κατάγων ἀδίκως, τοὺς δ’ ἐκδιώκων, τοὺς δὲ καίνων, | ἀργυρίων ὑπόπλεως</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδ_παραλλάσσει_298">, this trains and perverts good minds of men, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἵστασθαι πρὸς αἰσχρ. πράγμ</emph>.</quote>, to address themselves to base deeds. <quote xml:lang="grc">παραλλάσσει</quote>=‘alters sideways’; i.e. causes to turn out of a straight course into an oblique course; hence, like <quote xml:lang="grc">παράγει, παραστρέφει</quote>, perverts. Cp. <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 8.1342a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 8. 7</ref>§ 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ψυχαὶ παρεστραμμέναι τῆς κατὰ φύσιν ἕξεως</quote> (their minds being warped from their natural condition). Since <quote xml:lang="grc">παραλλάσσει</quote> implies a bad <emph>training</emph>, it can be followed, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθίζει</quote> and like words, by an infin.: it is unnecessary, then, to make </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵστασθαι_298">, in its relation to <quote xml:lang="grc">παραλλάσσει</quote>, merely epexegetic (‘so that they set themselves’); though it might, of course, be so. Wecklein takes </gloss>
