<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_παραλλάσσει_298"> as parenthetic = <quote xml:lang="grc">παραλλάσσουσα</quote>: but this, too, is needless, nor is it supported by <ref n="Soph. Ant. 537">537</ref> (where <ref target="cl537">see n.</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵστασθαι_τι_298"> means here, ‘to <emph>set oneself</emph> facing it,’ so ‘to turn to it, address oneself to it,’ just like <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέπεσθαι πρός τι</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 452E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 452E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἄλλον τινὰ σκοπὸν στησάμενος ἢ τὸν τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ</quote> </cit>, ‘having set himself to some other aim,’ etc. Distinguish some other phrases with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵστασθαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> which are not really similar: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.11">Thuc. 3.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ὅ τι χρὴ στῆναι</quote> </cit> (a power to which they could rally): <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.56">4. 56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὴν ἐκείνων γνώμην ἀεὶ ἕστασαν</quote> </cit> (they had always sided with the Athenian policy): <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.34">6. 34</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὰ λεγόμενα καὶ αἱ γνῶμαι ἵστανται</quote> </cit> (men's minds adapt their attitudes to what is said). </gloss>

data.quote:
πρὸς ἄλλον τινὰ σκοπὸν στησάμενος ἢ τὸν τοῦ
									ἀγαθοῦ
πρὸς τὴν ἐκείνων γνώμην ἀεὶ ἕστασαν

πρὸς τὰ λεγόμενα καὶ αἱ γνῶμαι
									ἵστανται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανουργίας_ἔχειν_300">: showed men how to practise villanies. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote> of the moral habit, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.368"> <title>Od.</title> 1.368</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μνηστῆρες ὑπέρβιον ὕβριν ἔχοντες</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.305"> <title>Il.</title> 9.305</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσσαν ἔχων ὀλοήν</quote> </cit>. The inf. might be epexegetic, but really depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδειξεν</quote> as implying ‘taught’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 195">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 195</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς λύπας | ηὕρετο... | ᾠδαῖς παύειν</quote> </cit> (has found out <emph>how to...</emph>). <quote xml:lang="grc">δείκνυμι</quote> of <emph>invention</emph>, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1195"> <title>Ai.</title> 1195</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δς στυγερῶν ἔδειξεν ὅπλων | Ἕλλασι κοινὸν Ἄρη</quote> </cit>: fr. 396. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατοῦ φρυκτωρίαν | ἔδειξε, κἀνέφηνεν οὐ δεδειγμένα</quote> (Palamedes).</gloss>

data.quote:
δς στυγερῶν ἔδειξεν ὅπλων | Ἕλλασι κοινὸν
									Ἄρη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδέναι_300">, ‘to know,’ i.e. to be conversant with (cp. on 71) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντὸς_δυσσέβ_300">, impiety of (shown in) <emph>any</emph> deed,=<quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν δυσσεβὲς <emph>ἔργον</emph> </quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 603">cp. 603</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου τ’ ἄνοια</quote>): for <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 761"> <title>O. C.</title> 761</ref> n. Note </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντὸς_ἔργ_300">. after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανουργίας_300">: the familiar use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργία</quote> extenuates the force to which etymology entitles it, while in <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν ἔργον</quote> that whole force is felt: so <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν ποιεῖν</quote> is stronger than <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργεῖν</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν λέγειν</quote> than <quote xml:lang="grc">παρρησιάζεσθαι</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Apol. 39">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 39</ref>A <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τις τολμᾷ πᾶν ποιεῖν καὶ λέγειν</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 407">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 407</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παντὸς ἂν λόγου κακοῦ | <emph>γλώσσῃ</emph> θιγόντα καὶ πανουργίας</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσης</quote> must be supplied, showing how <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργία</quote> could be used without direct reference to its derivation. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνυσαν_302">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 231">cp. 231</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνῳ_ποτέ_302">, at some time or other: i.e. they will be caught sooner or later. With </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοῦναι_δίκην_302">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1041"> <title>Ph.</title> 1041</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίσασθε, τίσασθ’ ἀλλὰ τῷ χρόνῳ ποτέ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέπραξαν_ὡς_302"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>, 292), as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 723">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 723</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τόδ’ ἐξέπραξεν, ὥστε Βόσπορον κλῇσαι μέγαν</quote> </cit>; The verb is here ironical; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 479A">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 479A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ αὐτὸ διαπεπραγμένοι εἰσὶν ὥσπερ ἂν εἴ τις τοῖς μεγίστοις νοσήμασι συνισχόμενος διαπράξαιτο μὴ διδόναι δίκην .. τοῖς ἰατροῖς, μηδε= ἰατρεύεσθαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ τόδ’ ἐξέπραξεν, ὥστε Βόσπορον κλῇσαι
									μέγαν

τὸ αὐτὸ διαπεπραγμένοι εἰσὶν ὥσπερ ἂν εἴ τις
									τοῖς μεγίστοις νοσήμασι συνισχόμενος διαπράξαιτο μὴ διδόναι
									δίκην .. τοῖς ἰατροῖς, μηδε= ἰατρεύεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεύς_304">, in his quality of <quote xml:lang="grc">Βασιλεύς</quote> (<ref n="Xen. Anab. 3.1.12">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.1.12</ref>), is fitly invoked by a king who vows that he will uphold the royal authority. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 487">Cp. 487</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρκιος_304">, adverbial: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1637"> <title>O. C.</title> 1637</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῄνεσεν τάδ’ ὅρκιος</quote> </cit> (n.). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 823">Cp. 823</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">λυγροτάταν</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_αὐτόχειρα_304">, the very man (248) whose hand strewed the dust, said with an emphasis corresponding to that with which the Guard had insisted on the absence of any clue (249). For <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτο</quote>-, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 56">cp. 56</ref>, 172. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφου_ταφῆς_304">, as 490, 534, <ref n="Soph. OT 1447"> <title>O. T.</title> 1447</ref>: in a symbolical sense like that of <quote xml:lang="grc">θάψας</quote> (246). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνος_308">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 304"> <title>O. T.</title> 304</ref> n.— ‘Death alone shall not suffice for you,’ already implies a threat of torture. To make this threat explicit, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_δηλώσητε_308"> is added, as if merely <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θανεῖσθε</quote> had preceded. ‘(You shall not die,) until you have first been hung up alive, and have revealed (the authorship of) this outrage.’ They are to be suspended by the hands or arms, and flogged. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 106"> <title>Ai.</title> 106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανεῖν γὰρ αὐτὸν οὔ τί πω θέλω . . πρὶν ἂν δεθεὶς πρὸς κίον’ ἑρκείου στέγης . . μάστιγι πρῶτον νῶτα φοινιχθεὶς θάνῃ</quote> </cit>. <title>Phorm.</title> 1. 4. 43 <quote xml:lang="lat">ego plectar pendens.</quote> In <ref n="Plat. Laws 872B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 872B</ref> a slave who has slain a free man is to be flogged, and then (if he does not die under the lash, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάνπερ βιῷ παιόμενος</quote>) put to death by other means. Other views of </gloss>

data.quote:
θανεῖν γὰρ αὐτὸν οὔ τί πω θέλω . . πρὶν ἂν
									δεθεὶς πρὸς κίον’ ἑρκείου στέγης . . μάστιγι πρῶτον νῶτα
									φοινιχθεὶς θάνῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρεμαστοί_308"> refer it to (1) <emph>mere</emph> suspension, as a torture, like that of Melanthius in <ref n="Hom. Od. 22.175"> <title>Od.</title> 22.175</ref>: (2) stretching on a cross-like frame; cp. Alexis <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Athen. 134 A <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδιστ’ ἀναπήξαιμ’ αὐτὸν ἐπὶ ξύλου λαβών</quote>. Impalement (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνασταυρόω, ἀνασκολοπίζω</quote>) is certainly not meant.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντες_κρεμαστοί_308">, ‘suspended alive,’ as <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶν</quote> is oft. joined with another partic.: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 2.6.29">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 2.6.29</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὥσπερ οἱ ἄλλοι . . ἀποτμηθέντες τὰς κεφαλάς, ὅσπερ τάχιστος θάνατος δοκεῖ εἶναι, ἀλλὰ ζῶν αἰκισθεὶς ἐνιαυτόν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐχ ὥσπερ οἱ ἄλλοι . . ἀποτμηθέντες τὰς
									κεφαλάς, ὅσπερ τάχιστος θάνατος δοκεῖ εἶναι, ἀλλὰ ζῶν αἰκισθεὶς
									ἐνιαυτόν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηλώσηθ_308">: as to the belief that torture was sure to wring the truth from slaves, cp. Isae. or. 8. 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπόταν δοῦλοι καὶ ἐλεύθεροι παραγένωνται, . . οὐ χρῆσθε ταῖς τῶν ἐλευθέρων μαρτυρίαις, ἀλλὰ τοὺς δούλους βασανίζοντες οὕτω ζητεῖτε εὑρεῖν τὴν ἀλήθειαν τῶν γενομένων</quote>. (Cp. <title>Selections from the Attic Orators</title>, p. 358 n.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕβριν_308">, in concrete sense (<ref n="Soph. OC 1029"> <title>O. C.</title> 1029</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_ἁρπάζητε_310">, with grim irony, since they are to die before they can apply the lesson. So <ref n="Soph. OC 1377"> <title>O. C.</title> 1377</ref> (Oed. calls down destruction upon his sons) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἀξιῶτον τοὺς φυτεύσαντας σέβειν</quote>, where see n. Cp. below, 716.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κέρδος_310">, accus. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1242">cp. 1242</ref>): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθεν_οἰστέον_310"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθηθ_310">, aor., learn <emph>once for all:</emph> but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρπάζητε</quote>, pres., go on stealing.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἅπαντος_310">, from every source, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κερδαίνειν_310">: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 2.9.4">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 2.9.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἦν οἷος ἀπὸ παντὸς κερδαίνειν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 735">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 735</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ποτίσταται | κἀκ παντὸς ὑμεῖς μηχανώμεναι</quote> </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ γὰρ ἦν οἷος ἀπὸ παντὸς κερδαίνειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_315"> suits a timid appeal: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1414"> <title>O. C.</title> 1414</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκετεύω σε πεισθῆναί τί μοι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_315"> (Boeckh), though favoured by L, could not be justified as an expostulatory ‘now.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δώσεις_315">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1287"> <title>O. C.</title> 1287</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδοὺς ἐμοὶ | λέξαι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_315">, ‘without more ado,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ ἔχω</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1066"> <title>Ph.</title> 1066</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ σοῦ φωνῆς ἔτι | γενήσομαι προσφθεγκτός, ἀλλ’ οὕτως ἄπει</quote>; <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 237c"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 237 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν μὲν οὕτως οὐκ ἔχω εἰπεῖν</quote> </cit> (‘off-hand’).</gloss>

data.quote: νῦν μὲν οὕτως οὐκ ἔχω εἰπεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴω_315">, delib. subjunct., somewhat rare in pres. (<ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ὡς_315"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς καὶ νῦν</foreign>: for the hyperbaton of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 590"> <title>Ai.</title> 590</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 949"> <title>El.</title> 949</ref>, 1243, etc.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνιαρῶς_315">, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_315">: but Eur. , like Ar. , has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνι^αρός</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 230">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 230</ref> etc.): Aesch. does not use the adj., though he has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνι^ος. ἀνία</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote>) is used by Soph. , but not by Aesch. or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_317"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠσίν_317">, through association with such phrases as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 605">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 605</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾷ δ’ ἐν ὠσὶ κέλαδος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_317"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ ψυχῇ</quote> denotes the seat, and, equally with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, here=our ‘in’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.55"> <title>Il.</title> 1.55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ γὰρ ἐπὶ φρεσὶ θῆκε θεά</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: τῷ γὰρ ἐπὶ φρεσὶ θῆκε θεά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δὲ_318"> is right, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δαὶ_318"> (L): see Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥυθμίζεις_318">, bring under <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥυθμός</quote>, i.e. reduce to a clear form, <emph>define</emph>, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_318"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>), <emph>with respect to its seat</emph> (i.e. whether it is in the ears or in the mind). Cp. <title>Metaphys.</title> 11. 10 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν μὴ ῥυθμίσῃ τις</quote>, unless one reduce (the opinions) to a clear form, or method. So he oft. associates <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥυθμός</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα</quote>, as <title>Phys. Ausc.</title> 8. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σχηματιζόμενον καὶ ῥυθμιζόμενον</quote>, what is being reduced to form and system. For the epexegetic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_318_2"> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 103"> <title>Ai.</title> 103</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧ τοὐπίτριπτον κίναδος ἐξήρου μ’ ὅπου</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 890"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 890</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα μὴ λεύσσειν ὅπου</quote> </cit>.—Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. Tro 4.5.244">Shaksp. <title>Troil.</title> 4. 5. 244</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">That I may give the local wound a name, And <emph>make distinct</emph> the very breach whereout Hector's great spirit flew.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ἧ τοὐπίτριπτον κίναδος ἐξήρου μ’ ὅπου

That I may give the local wound a name, And
										  the very breach whereout Hector's
									great spirit flew.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_ὦτ_319">, acc. defining </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_319">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1301"> <title>Ph.</title> 1301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες με χεῖρα</quote>: cp. on <ref n="Soph. OC 113"> <title>O. C.</title> 113</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμ_ὡς_319">, impatient, as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 590">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 590</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμ’ ὡς τεθνήξεις</quote> </cit>: elsewhere in Soph. it expresses pity or grief (1270, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 354"> <title>Ai.</title> 354</ref>, 587).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάλημα_319"> suits Creon's contemptuous impatience. The schol. (see crit. n.) prob. read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλημα_319"> (‘a knave,’ lit., ‘fine meal,’ from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλέω</quote>): cp. <title>Au.</title> 430 <quote xml:lang="grc">παιπάλημ’ ὅλον</quote>. But if Creon used that word, he would seem to give the man credit for real subtlety: he would be taking him too seriously. Thus </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλημα_319_2"> is the word applied by Ajax to his mortal foe, Odysseus (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 381"> <title>Ai.</title> 381</ref>, 389), who is similarly called <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνσοφον κρότημα</quote> in fr. 827: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 927"> <title>Ph.</title> 927</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανουργίας | δεινῆς τέχνημ’ ἔχθιστον</quote> (Neoptolemus). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 756">Cp. 756</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δούλευμα</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 85"> <title>O. T.</title> 85</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κήδευμα</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆλον_319"> like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναργές</quote> (cp. on 263). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰμί_321"> is supplied with <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιήσας</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_γε_321"> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 993">cp. 993</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 907"> <title>Ph.</title> 907</ref>NE. <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρὸς φανοῦμαι . . ΦΙ. οὔκουν ἐν οἷς γε δρᾷς· ἐν οἶς δ’ αὐδᾷς, ὀκνῶ</quote>: and so oft., as <ref n="Soph. OT 565"> <title>O. T.</title> 565</ref>, 1357, <ref n="Soph. OC 848"> <title>O. C.</title> 848</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_322"> implies the contradiction, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποίησας</quote>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ταῦτα_322"> goes with the participle (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προδούς_322">): cp. <title>O.T.</title> 37, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 614"> <title>El.</title> 614</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν τεκοῦσαν ὕβρισεν, | καὶ ταῦτα τηλικοῦτος</quote> </cit>. So Lat., <quote xml:lang="lat">hominem . . studiis optimis deditum, idque a puero</quote> ( <ref n="Cic. Fam. 13. 16">Cic. <title>Fam.</title> 13. 16</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν τεκοῦσαν ὕβρισεν, | καὶ ταῦτα
									τηλικοῦτος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_δεινόν_323">. Creon has pronounced the Guard guilty on mere <quote xml:lang="grc">δόξα</quote>, without proof. The Guard says, ‘It is grievous that, when a man <emph>does</emph> harbour suspicions (<quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ δοκεῖ <emph>γε</emph> </quote>), those suspicions should at the same time (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_323">) be false.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_323"> means that, in such a matter, hasty <quote xml:lang="grc">δόξα</quote> should be avoided altogether. It is always bad to assume a man guilty without proof; it is worse when the rash assumption is also erroneous. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">δόκησις ἀγνώς</quote>, ‘a blind suspicion’ (<ref n="Soph. OT 681"> <title>O. T.</title> 681</ref>), and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 608"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 608</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμῃ δ’ ἀδήλῳ μή με χωρὶς αἰτιῶ</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 311">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 311</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ ἢν δοκῇς μέν</quote> </cit>, (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δὲ δόξα σου νοσεῖ</quote>,) | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονεῖν δόκει τι.</quote>—Nauck supposes a play on two senses of <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖν, ᾧ δοκεῖ</quote> (or, as he reads, <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῇ</quote>) having been suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξε τῷ δήμῳ</quote>, etc.: ‘'Tis monstrous that he who <emph>decides</emph> should have false views.’ But, even if the absolute <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ δοκεῖ</quote> could be thus used, the colloquial frequency of <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μοι ποιεῖν τι</quote>) in Aristophanes suffices to show that <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ δοκεῖ</quote> could not, to an Athenian ear, have suggested ‘the ruler’ or ‘the judge’: it would have seemed to mean merely one who ‘proposes,’ not ‘disposes.’—Schütz makes <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖν</quote> depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ</quote>: ‘'Tis grievous when a man is <emph>resolued</emph> to believe even what is false’ (if only he wishes to believe it). A bold speech for the Guard to Creon; nor does it satisfy either <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: γνώμῃ δ’ ἀδήλῳ μή με χωρὶς αἰτιῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόμψευε_δόξαν_324">, make neat sayings about it,—referring to the rhetorical form of the last verse, with its <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις, δοκεῖ...δοκεῖν</quote>. Not necessarily, ‘<emph>quibble</emph> upon it,’—as if <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖν</quote> had been used in two different senses,—a view of <quote xml:lang="grc">κόμψευε</quote> which has been brought to support the interpretations of v. 323 mentioned above. The verb is usually midd., often with acc., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 333">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 333</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ κεκόμψευσαι πονηρά</quote> </cit> (‘thou hast given subtle form to wicked pleas’): <ref n="Plat. Lach. 197">Plat. <title>Lach.</title> 197</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέπει...σοφιστῇ τὰ τοιαῦτα μᾶλλον κομψεύεσθαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_δόξαν_324">, that <quote xml:lang="grc">δόξα</quote> of yours: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 96">cp. 96</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1110"> <title>El.</title> 1110</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδα τὴν σὴν</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_324"> (‘very well,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>) is better than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_324">, which would mean ‘for the present’ (as opposed to the near future).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_ταῦταδρῶντας_324">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 384">cp. 384</ref>: the pron. gains emphasis by its place. </gloss>
lemma:
ταῦτα … τοὺς δρῶντας = τοὺς ταῦτα
									δρῶντας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δειλά_326">, as involving mean treachery towards king and city. Theognis 835 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀνδρῶν τε βίῃ καὶ κέρδεα δειλὰ καὶ ὕβρις | πολλῶν ἐξ ἀγαθῶν ἐς κακότητ’ ἔβαλεν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινά_326"> (L) is defended by Seyffert as=‘flagitious,’ by Whitelaw as=‘clever’ (iron.), ‘your wondrous winnings.’ In some forms of minuscule writing <quote xml:lang="grc">λ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ν</quote> could easily be confused: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1130"> <title>O. T.</title> 1130</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυναλλάξας</quote> </cit>, where the first <quote xml:lang="grc">λ</quote> has been made from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ν_326">: and <ref n="Soph. OC 1164"> <title>O. C.</title> 1164</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον</quote> has been restored from <quote xml:lang="grc">μολόντ’</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_327"> prefacing the wish (<ref n="Soph. OC 421"> <title>O. C.</title> 421</ref>): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὑρεθείη_327"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δρῶν</quote> (319): <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστ’</quote>,</emph> denoting the best thing that could happen (<ref n="Soph. OT 926"> <title>O. T.</title> 926</ref> n.).—<quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν</quote> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_327"> is needless. We find <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐάν...καὶ ἐάν</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τε...ἐάν τε</quote>: but, as a rule, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τε</quote>)...<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> [not <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐὰν] μή</quote>: e.g. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 751">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 751</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν τύχω κἂν μὴ τύχω</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 660E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 660E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τε μέγας...ἐάν τε σμικρὸς...ἦ, καὶ</quote> </cit> (‘and’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν πλουτῇ καὶ μή</quote>. For the conjunctive form in stating alternatives, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 488"> <title>O. C.</title> 488</ref> n. </gloss>

data.quote: ἐάν τε μέγας...ἐάν τε σμικρὸς...ἦ, καὶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_330"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote>, not with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_330">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμης_330">, of reasonable forecast, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1087"> <title>O. T.</title> 1087</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_κοὐδέν_332">. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σχήματι εἶπεν, ἀντὶ τοῦ, πολλῶν ὄντων τῶν δεινῶν, οὐδέν ἐστιν ἀνθρώπου δεινότερον</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα</quote> is the rhetorical parataxis,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_332"> being equiv. to ‘and <emph>yet</emph>’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1046"> <title>Tr.</title> 1046</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πολλὰ δὴ καὶ θερμὰ κοὐ λόγῳ κακά | … μοχθήσας ἐγώ· | κοὔπω τοιοῦτον οὔτ’ ἄκοιτις ἡ Διὸς | προὔθηκεν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit> It is stronger to say, ‘they are great; and he is greater’: than, ‘though they are great, he is greater.’</gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ πολλὰ δὴ καὶ θερμὰ κοὐ λόγῳ κακά | …
									μοχθήσας ἐγώ· | κοὔπω τοιοῦτον οὔτ’ ἄκοιτις ἡ Διὸς | προὔθηκεν
									κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δεινά_332">, not ‘dread,’ nor ‘able,’ but ‘wonderful.’ There is a certain resemblance to <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 585">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 585</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν γᾶ τρέφει | δεινὰ δειμάτων ἄχη...ἀλλ’ ὑπέρτολμον ἀνδρὸς φρόνημα τίς λέγοι</quote>; but there <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινά</quote>=‘dread,’ and the scope is limited to the violence of human passion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_κοὐδέν_332">. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σχήματι εἶπεν, ἀντὶ τοῦ, πολλῶν ὄντων τῶν δεινῶν, οὐδέν ἐστιν ἀνθρώπου δεινότερον</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα</quote> is the rhetorical parataxis,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_332"> being equiv. to ‘and <emph>yet</emph>’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1046"> <title>Tr.</title> 1046</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πολλὰ δὴ καὶ θερμὰ κοὐ λόγῳ κακά | … μοχθήσας ἐγώ· | κοὔπω τοιοῦτον οὔτ’ ἄκοιτις ἡ Διὸς | προὔθηκεν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit> It is stronger to say, ‘they are great; and he is greater’: than, ‘though they are great, he is greater.’</gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ πολλὰ δὴ καὶ θερμὰ κοὐ λόγῳ κακά | …
									μοχθήσας ἐγώ· | κοὔπω τοιοῦτον οὔτ’ ἄκοιτις ἡ Διὸς | προὔθηκεν
									κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δεινά_332">, not ‘dread,’ nor ‘able,’ but ‘wonderful.’ There is a certain resemblance to <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 585">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 585</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν γᾶ τρέφει | δεινὰ δειμάτων ἄχη...ἀλλ’ ὑπέρτολμον ἀνδρὸς φρόνημα τίς λέγοι</quote>; but there <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινά</quote>=‘dread,’ and the scope is limited to the violence of human passion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_334">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δεινόν</quote>, this wondrous power, man. The schol. quotes <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 15">Theocr. 15. 83</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφόν τοι χρῆμ’ ἄνθρωπος</quote> </cit>. Not adverbial with <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖ</quote>, ‘so’ (in a way corresponding with his <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινότης</quote>), as Bellermann takes it, comparing <ref n="Soph. OT 1005"> <title>O. T.</title> 1005</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἀφικόμην</quote>), where see n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέραν_334">, properly, ‘to the further side of’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">trans</foreign>). The point here is that man dares to cross the sea. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέρα_334"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ultra</foreign>), which Blaydes prefers, would imply wanderings (not merely over, but) beyond sea. A European visitor to New York goes <quote xml:lang="grc">πέραν πόντου</quote>: to San Francisco, <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρα</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 885"> <title>O. C.</title> 885</ref> and p. 279. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειμερίῳ_νότῳ_335"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖ</quote>, goes (driven) by it: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 14.253"> <title>Od.</title> 14.253</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπλέομεν Βορέῃ ἀνέμῳ</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Hom. Od. 14.299"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 299</ref> (of the ship) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ ἔθεεν Βορέῃ ἀνέμῳ</quote>. The dat. might be merely ‘with’ (dat. of circumstance), but is better taken as instrumental. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 588">Cp. 588</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοαῖς</quote>. Some make <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμερίῳ νότῳ</quote> a temporal dat., like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥρᾳ χειμῶνος</quote>, which it can hardly be.— Soph. is thinking of the Aegean, where the prevailing winds were from the N. or N.W. in spring and summer, while stormy south winds were associated with winter: Hesiod warns a man with a voyage before him not to await <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμῶν’ ἐπιόντα νότοιό τε δεινὰς ἀήτας, | ὅς τ’ ὤρινε θάλασσαν ὁμαρτήσας Διὸς ὄμβρῳ | πολλῷ ὀπωρινῷ, χαλεπὸν δέ τε πόντον ἔθηκεν</quote>. The epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμερίῳ</quote> aptly distinguishes this wintry <quote xml:lang="grc">νότος</quote> from that gentle south breeze (now called the ‘embates’) which regularly sets in at sunset in the fair season (cp. Curt. <title>Hist. Gr.</title> 1. 14). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιβρ_οἴδμασιν_336">, passing under swelling waters which open depths around: i.e. he is in the trough of a heavy sea, while on each side the waves rise above his ship, threatening to engulf it. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 1.106">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 1. 106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">his unda dehiscens Terram inter fluctus aperit.</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύχιος</quote>, ‘of the depths’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">βρυχία...ἠχὼ βροντᾶς</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 1082">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 1082</ref>), is formed as if from <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύξ</quote>, of which Oppian uses acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύχα</quote> (‘depth of the sea,’ <ref n="Opp. H. 2.588"> <title>Hal.</title> 2. 588</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποβρύχιος</quote>=‘under water,’ and so neut. pl. adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόβρυχα</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 5.319"> <title>Od.</title> 5.319</ref> etc.). <quote xml:lang="grc">περιβρύχιος</quote> occurs only here. For the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">βρ</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 348">cp. 348</ref>, 1104, 1117. The schol.'s <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠχώδεσι</quote> means that <quote xml:lang="grc">περιβρύχιος</quote> was taken as' ‘roaring around’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">βρυ—χάομαι</quote>), where the <quote xml:lang="grc">υ</quote> would be long, against metre (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 347">cp. 347</ref>). The Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ δὲ κῦμα | βέβρυχεν ῥόθιον</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 5.411"> <title>Od.</title> 5.411</ref>) might suggest this view. </gloss>

data.quote:
his unda dehiscens Terram inter fluctus
									aperit.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερτάταν_338">, highest, as eldest, <quote xml:lang="grc">παμμήτωρ</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. PB 90">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 90</ref>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">παμβῶτις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Phil. 392"> <title>Ph.</title> 392</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 40C">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 40C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν... πρώτην καὶ πρεσβυτάτην θεῶν ὅσοι ἐντὸς οὐρανοῦ γεγόνασι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
γῆν... πρώτην καὶ πρεσβυτάτην θεῶν ὅσοι ἐντὸς
									οὐρανοῦ γεγόνασι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκαμάταν_339">: this form of the fem. occurs only here, unless <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκαμάτῃσι</quote> (and not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκαμάτοισι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">χέρεσσιν</quote> be right in <ref n="Hes. Th. 747">Hes. <title>Th.</title> 747</ref>, but is warranted by similar epic forms in tragedy, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθανάτας</quote> (gen.) <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 619">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 619</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 235">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 235:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδμήταν</quote> </cit> <ref n="Soph. El. 1238"> <title>El.</title> 1238</ref> (and -<quote xml:lang="grc">ης</quote> in dial. <ref n="Soph. OC 1321"> <title>O. C.</title> 1321</ref>). For the initial <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>, cp. the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθάνατος, ἀγοράασθε</quote>, etc. (and see <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, Appendix, note 5, p. 195): but in <ref n="Soph. El. 164"> <title>El.</title> 164</ref> we find '<quote xml:lang="grc">α^κάματα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποτρύεται_339">, prop., wears away for his own purposes (midd.), —<foreign xml:lang="lat">fatigat</foreign>, vexes (with constant ploughing). Earth is ‘immortal,’ and not to be exhausted; but man's patient toil subdues it to his use. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 124"> <title>Tr.</title> 124</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποτρύειν ἐλπίδα</quote> </cit>, to wear it out.—Not, ‘wearies himself by tilling’ the soil. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰλλομένων_ἀρότρων_340"> as the ploughs go backwards and forwards,— turning at the end of one furrow, and going down the next. Cp. <ref n="Nic. Ther. 478">Nicander <title>Ther.</title> 478</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦγε δ’ ἀεὶ σκολιήν τε καὶ οὐ μίαν ἀτραπὸν ἴλλων</quote>, ‘in flying (from the snake), always <emph>make your course wind</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἴλλων</quote>) from side to side, instead of keeping it straight.’ <ref n="Xen. Hunt. 6. 15">Xen. <title>Cyn.</title> 6. 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κύνες ἐξίλλουσαι τὰ ἴχνη</quote>, ‘puzzling out the tracks,’ i.e. going backwards and forwards till they have found a clue. As to the spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴλλω υερσυς εἴλλω</quote>, see Appendix. It is needless to write </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρότων_340">, ‘ploughingseasons’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 825"> <title>Tr.</title> 825</ref>), and to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰλλομένων</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">περιτελλομένων</quote>. The picture of the ploughs at work is more vivid; and, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρότων, ἔτος εἰς ἔτος</quote> would be feebly redundant.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτος_ἔτος_340">, an adverbial phrase, like ‘year in, year out’: for the use of the simple acc. in temporal adverbs (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀωρίαν</quote>) see <ref n="Soph. OT 1138"> <title>O. T.</title> 1138</ref> n.; for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_340">, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.134"> <title>Od.</title> 9.134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλα κεν βαθὺ λήϊον αἰεὶ | εἰς ὥρας ἀμῷεν</quote> </cit> (‘as each year comes round’): <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 18">Theocr. 18. 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἠς ἔτος ἐξ ἔτεος</quote> </cit>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς νέωτα</quote> (next year), and the Gk <quote xml:lang="grc">μοδ. χρόνο σὲ</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνο</quote>, ‘year after year.’ </gloss>

data.quote:
μάλα κεν βαθὺ λήϊον αἰεὶ | εἰς ὥρας
									ἀμῷεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱππείῳ_γένει_341">, the ‘offspring of horses,’ meaning ‘mules’; which are ‘far better than oxen to drag the jointed plough through the deep fallow’ (<ref n="Hom. Il. 10.352"> <title>Il.</title> 10.352</ref>). <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 3.2">Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 3.2</ref> § 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὸ Σιμωνίδης, ὅτε μὲν ἐδίδου μισθὸν ὀλίγον αὐτῷ ὸ νικήσας τοῖς ὀρεῦσιν, οὐκ ἤθελε ποιεῖν ὡς δυσχεραίνων εἰς ἡμιόνους ποιεῖν· ἐπεὶ δ’ ικανὸν ἔδωκεν, ὲποίησε, χαίρετ’, ἀελλοπόδων θύγατρες ἵππων</quote>. As this story suggests, the very fact that the ordinary Attic word for ‘mule’ was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμίονος</quote> (adj. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρικός</quote>) might lead an Attic poet to prefer such a periphrasis as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππειον γένος</quote>. The objections to taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱππείῳ γένει</quote> as simply =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποις</quote>are,that (1) Greek ploughmen used oxen or mules more than horses, and (2) the achievement of taming the horse (350) is thus anticipated. Some understand <emph>both</emph> horses and mules, giving <quote xml:lang="grc">γένει</quote> a double sense—rather awkwardly, I think. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολεύων_341">, <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατὰ σύνεσιν</foreign> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_341">. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.90"> <title>Od.</title> 11.90</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθε δ’ ἐπὶ ψυχὴ Θηβαίου Τειρεσίαο, | χρύσεον σκῆπτρον ἔχων</quote>: 16. 476 <quote xml:lang="grc">μείδησεν δ’ ἱερὴ ἲς Τηλεμάχοιο, | ἐς πατέρ’ ὀφθαλμοῖσιν ἰδών</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.690"> <title>Il.</title> 11.690</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθὼν γὰρ ἐκάκωσε βίη Ἡρακληείη</quote> </cit>. But as Soph. would write <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΟΛΕΥΟΝ</quote>, it was the easier to read <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλευον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κουφονόων_343"> is merely a general epithet, ‘light-hearted,’ ‘blithe and careless’; Theognis 580 <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικρῆς ὄρνιθος κοῦφον ἔχουσα νόον</quote>: cp. the proverbial phraseology of Athens, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνθρωπος ὄρνις, ἀστάθμητος, πετόμενος, | ἀτέκμαρτος, οὺδὲν οὐδέποτ’ ἐν ταὐτῷ μένων</quote> ( <title>Au.</title> 169). The epithet is given to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτες</quote> below (617), and to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐηθία</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. PB 383">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 383</ref>.— Not, ‘quick-witted’ (and therefore harder to catch).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιβαλὼν_343"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">σπείραισι δ</quote>.: it can precede the dat. by so much, because its meaning is already clear, and the dat. is merely a poet. amplification. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόντου_φύσιν_345">, a brood living in the waters of the sea, the tautology being only of the same order as in <quote xml:lang="grc">πόντος ἁλός, ἁλὸς ἐν πελάγεσσιν, πέλαγος θαλάσσης</quote>, etc.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>σπείραισι δικτυοκλ</emph>.</quote>, instr. dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιβαλών</quote>, in the coils of woven nets: for the adj. compounded with a subst. (<quote xml:lang="grc">δίκτυον</quote>) cognate in sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">σπεῖρα</quote>, see n. on <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 716"> <title>O. C.</title> 716</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐήρετμος πλάτα</quote> </cit>.— <title>Au.</title> 528 mentions <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρκη, νεφέλας, δίκτυα, πηκτάς</quote> as nets used by the fowler (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρνιθευτής</quote>). In hunting the lion, bear, boar, deer, hare, etc., various nets were used; the <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκτυον</quote>, to enclose large spaces; the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνόδιον</quote>, to close passages; the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρκυς</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">cassis</foreign>) or tunnel-net. The chief fishing-nets were the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίβληστρον</quote> (castingnet), and the <quote xml:lang="grc">σαγήνη</quote> (drag-net, whence <emph>seine, sean</emph>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιφραδής_348">. Eustath. p. 135, 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">φραδής...ὅθεν σύνθετον ὁ παρὰ Σοφοκλεῖ ἀριφραδὴς ἀνήρ</quote>. This was evidently a mere slip of memory. Neither Soph. nor Eur, uses any compound with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρι</quote>, though Aesch. has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρίδακρυς, ἐρίδματος, ἐρικύμων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηχαναῖς_349">. <foreign xml:lang="grc">μαχαναῖς</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 181"> <title>Ai.</title> 181</ref> and <quote xml:lang="grc">μαχανᾷ</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 133">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 133</ref> are the only instances in which Tragedy gives a Doric form to words from this stem.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγραύλου_349">, having his <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>, or dwelling, in the open country, as opposed to a domesticated animal: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 1187">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1187</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέπει γ’ ὥστε θὴρ ἄγραυλος φόβῃ</quote> </cit>. For the combination with </gloss>

data.quote: πρέπει γ’ ὥστε θὴρ ἄγραυλος φόβῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρεσσιβάτα_349"> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 394E">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 394E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ θηριῶδες τῆς φύσεως καὶ τὸ ἄγριον αὐτοῦ καὶ τὸ ὀρεινόν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ θηριῶδες τῆς φύσεως καὶ τὸ ἄγριον αὐτοῦ καὶ
									τὸ ὀρεινόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λασιαύχενά_θ_350">. The elision at the end of the verse (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισυναλοιφή</quote>) is comparatively rare in lyrics, as in dialogue (1031); but <ref n="Soph. Ant. 595">cp. 595</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πίπτοντ’</quote>, 864 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτογέννητ’</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀχμάζεται_351">, he tames, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφὶ_ζυγῶν_351">, putting the yoke about its neck. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀχμάζω</quote> (prop., ‘to get a firm hold upon’) was esp. used of breaking horses: <ref n="Eur. El. 816">Eur. <title>El.</title> 816</ref> (Thessalians honour a man) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις ταῦρον ἀρταμεἴ</quote> (cuts to pieces) <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς, | ἵππους τ’ ὀχμάζει</quote>. Schol. on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφέζεται_351"> is attractive; for the acc. he cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 409">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 409</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βρέτας...ἐφημένῳ</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 1493">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 1493</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐρώταν ἐφεζόμεναι</quote> </cit>. Add <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 664">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 664</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῦν θέλουσ’ ἐφέζετο</quote> </cit>. The sense would be, ‘seats himself behind the horse’ (in a chariot): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.46"> <title>Il.</title> 5.46</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππων ἐπιβησόμενον</quote> </cit>, etc. But, though oxen were used for draught, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφέζεται</quote> suits <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦρον</quote> less well. The sense, ‘<emph>tames</emph>,’ is clearly that which we require. See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθέγμα_354"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The phrase, ‘man has taught himself speech,’ should not be pressed as if the poet was thinking of a theory on the origin of language. It was the Eleatic view that language came <quote xml:lang="grc">θέσει</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσει</quote>, and Soph, may have known that; but by his <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδιδάξατο</quote> he meant simply, ‘developed for his own benefit, by his own effort.’ So Isocrates (<ref n="Isoc. 3.6">or. 3 § 6</ref>) conceives primitive man as living in a brutal state, and emerging from it by the development of speech and thought,— <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> being one of the human faculties (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐνόντων ἐν τῇ τῶν ἀνθρώπων φύσει</quote>), and the distinctive one:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγενομένου δ’ ἡμῖν τοῦ πείθειν ἀλλήλους καὶ δηλοῦν πρὸς ἡμᾶς αὐτοὺς περὶ ὼ=ν ἂν βουληθῶμεν, οὐ μόνον τοῦ θηριωδῶς ζῆν ἀπηλλάγημεν, ἀλλὰ καὶ συνελθόντες πόλεις ᾠκίσαμεν καὶ νόμους ἐθέμεθα καὶ τέχνας εὕρομεν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hor. S. 1.3.103">Hor. <title>Sat.</title> 1. 3. 103</ref> (men fought,) <quote xml:lang="lat">Donec verba, quibus voces sensusque notarent, Nominaque invenere: dehinc absistere bello, Oppida coeperunt munire et ponere leges.</quote> The Aeschylean Prometheus ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 444"> <title>P. V.</title> 444</ref>) claims to have made men <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔννους...καὶ φρενῶν ἐπηβόλους</quote>, but not (like Shelley's Prometheus) to have also given them language. Cp. Peile's chapter ‘On the Nature of Language’ (<title>Primer of Philology</title>), p. 156: ‘In this way then we may conceive of the</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνεμόεν_φρόνημα_354">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.80"> <title>Il.</title> 15.80</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὡς</emph> δ’ ὅτ’ ἂν ἀΐξῃ νόος ἀνέρος...| ὣς κραιπνῶς μεμαυῖα διέπτατο</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 7.36"> <title>Od.</title> 7.36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν νέες ὠκεῖαι ὡσεὶ πτερὸν ἠὲ νόημα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1081"> <title>O. C.</title> 1081</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀελλαία ταχύρρωστος πελειάς</quote> </cit>: fr. 621 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀελ<emph>λάδες</emph> φωναί</quote>. Not ‘lofty,’ in which sense <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεμόεν</quote> could be said only of a high place. Cp. Shelley, <title>Prometheus:</title> ‘He gave man speech, and speech created thought, Which is the measure of the universe.’ Soph. does not imply that speech created thought; he is rather thinking of them as developed (in their riper forms) together. </gloss>

data.quote:
δ’ ὅτ’ ἂν ἀΐξῃ νόος ἀνέρος...| ὣς κραιπνῶς
									μεμαυῖα διέπτατο
τῶν νέες ὠκεῖαι ὡσεὶ πτερὸν ἠὲ νόημα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστυνόμους_ὀργάς_355">, ‘such dispositions as regulate cities’; i.e. those feelings which lead men to organise social life, and to uphold the social order by their loyalty. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργάς</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 639"> <title>Ai.</title> 639</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συντρόφοις | ὀργαῖς</quote> </cit>, the dispositions that have grown with his growth: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 53">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῄνεσ’ ὀργὰς ἠπίους</quote> </cit>. The relation of <quote xml:lang="grc">φθέγμα</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστυνόμοι ὀργαί</quote> is illustrated by <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 1.1253a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 1.2</ref> § 12, where he is showing that man, more than any other <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγελαῖον ζῷον</quote>, is <quote xml:lang="grc">πολιτικόν</quote>: ‘Speech is intended to explain what is expedient and what is hurtful,—and so also what is just and unjust. It is characteristic of man, as compared with other animals, that he alone has a sense of good and evil, just and unjust; and it is the association of beings with this sense that make a Family and a State.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδιδάξατο_356"> here=simply <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς ἑαυτὸν ἐδίδαξε</quote>. The notion, ‘men taught each other,’ ‘learned by mutual converse,’ cannot be extracted from it. The passive <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάσκομαι</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">μανθάνειν</quote> is freq., but I can find no parallel for the use of the aor. midd. here. For the ordinary use, cp. Plat. <title>Meno</title>p. 93 D <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν υἱὸν ἱππέα...ἐδιδάξατο</quote> (<emph>had</emph> his son <emph>taught</emph> to ride): so <ref n="Plat. Rep. 467E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 467E</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">διδαξαμένους</quote>, ‘when we have had them taught’); [Plat. ] <ref n="Plat. Theag. 122">Plat. <title>Theag.</title> 122</ref>E, <title>De Virt.</title> 377 B; <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 1.6.2">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1.6.2</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Mem. 4.4.5"> <title>Mem.</title> 4. 4. 5</ref>; <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 1338">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1338</ref>. Once or twice <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδιδαξάμην</quote> is merely <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδίδαξα</quote> with the idea of the teacher's interest superadded: <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 59</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ διδάξασθαι | εἰδότι ῥᾴτερον</quote>: so Simonides fr. 145 (of himself) <quote xml:lang="grc">διδαξάμενος χορόν</quote> (unless he meant, ‘caused to be trained’). In <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 783">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 783</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάξαιμ’ ἄν</quote> </cit> (Elmsley) should prob. be read. It is rare for any midd. form, without a reflexive pron., to denote that the subject acts <emph>on</emph> (and not for) himself: thus, ‘he kills himself’ is not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκτείνεται</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκτείνει ἑαυτόν</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Phaedo 61E">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 61E</ref>). The exceptions are chiefly words of the toilet, as <quote xml:lang="grc">λούομαι</quote>. The dative of the reflexive can be more easily understood, as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.40">Thuc. 6.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις...οὐκ...αὐθαίρετον δουλείαν ἐπιβαλεῖται</quote> </cit>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sibi imponet.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγων_358"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Construe: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδιδάξατο</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγειν ἐναίθρεια βέλη δυσαύλων πάγων, καὶ δύσομβρα βέλη</quote>. He learned to build houses, to shelter himself from frost and rain. <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι δύσαυλοι</quote>=frosts which make it unpleasant to bivouack in the open: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 555">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 555</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσαυλίας.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναίθρεια_358">=under a clear (frosty) sky: cp. fr. 154 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν πάγου φανέντος αἰθρίου <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(‘a clear frost') </note> χεροῖν | κρύσταλλον ἁρπά<emph>σωσι</emph> </quote>. Nauck takes <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσαύλων πάγων</quote> as ‘inhospitable <emph>hills</emph>,’ citing Moschion fr. 7. 5 (<title>Frag. Trag.</title> p. 633), who describes primitive man as inhabiting <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρειγενῆ σπήλαια</quote>: but the context is against this. As corrections of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴθρια</quote>, there is little to choose between <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναίθρεια</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαίθρεια</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 335">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 335</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαιθρίων πάγων</quote>: but after <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγων</quote> the loss of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> would be easier than that of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθρία</quote> could be long (as Solon 13. 22): but <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote> is not elsewhere found in the derivatives; for in Cratinus <quote xml:lang="grc">Δηλιάδες</quote> fr. 5 we must read <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑπερβορείους αἴθρια τιμῶντας στέφη</quote> (not, as Blaydes gives it, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑπερβορέους αἴθρι—α</quote>). The spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναίθρεια</quote> is conjectural, but in <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title> 1088</ref> L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινικείῳ</quote>, as metre requires, for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινικίῳ</quote>. Below, v. 814, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινύμφειος</quote> has been conjectured: see n. there.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βέλη_358">, the ‘shafts’ of piercing cold, or of lashing rain. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 873E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 873E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεραυνός, ἤ τι παρὰ θεοῦ τοιοῦτον βέλος ἰόν</quote> </cit> (some such swift and sudden visitation): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 371">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 371</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βέλεσι πυρπνόου ζάλης</quote> </cit> (sent forth from Aetna): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.53"> <title>Il.</title> 1.53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆλα θεοῖο</quote> </cit> (of the pestilence). </gloss>

data.quote:
κεραυνός, ἤ τι παρὰ θεοῦ τοιοῦτον βέλος
									ἰόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντοπόρος_360"> is at once a comment on the achievements already enumerated (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">περιφραδής</quote> in 348), and a general expression absolving the poet from further detail: ‘yes, there is nothing that he cannot provide.’ Isocr. may have had this passage in mind in <ref n="Isoc. 3.6">or. 3 § 6</ref> (quoted <ref target="cl354">on 354</ref>), where an enumeration parallel with that of Soph. is closed by a phrase answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοπόρος,—καὶ σχεδὸν ἅπαντα τὰ δῑ ἡμῶν μεμηχανημένα λόγος ἡμῖν ἐστιν ὁ συγκατασκευάσας</quote>. We must not point thus: <quote xml:lang="grc">βέλη· παντοπόρος, ἄπορος κ.τ.λ.</quote>, when the sense would be weakened, and the construction perplexed (‘all-providing, and in no case without resource, he meets the future’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_361"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνου_361"> are alike admissible; <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον</quote> means, ‘the only thing that he will not achieve is to escape death’; <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον</quote>, ‘the only thing that he will not escape is death.’ In this general view of human achievement, <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον</quote> seems a little the better. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦξιν_ἐπάξεται_361">, <emph>procure</emph> means of escape from death. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάγεσθαι</quote>, prop. ‘to bring into one's own country’; usu. said of calling in allies to help one; or of importing foreign products: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.64">Thuc. 4.64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμμάχους δὲ οὐδέποτε ...ἐπαξόμεθα</quote> </cit>: id. 1. 81 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ θαλάσσης ὧν δέονται ἐπάξονται</quote>. Then often fig., of calling in anything to one's aid: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 823A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 823A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ δὴ παρὸν ἡμῖν τὰ νῦν οἷον μάρτυρα ἐπαγόμεθα· δηλοῖ μὲν ἂν ὃ βουλόμεθα</quote> </cit>: ‘we call to our help, as a witness, the example which is actually present with us; it will show what we mean.’ <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 492"> <title>Gorg.</title> 492</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ ἑαυτοῖς δεσπότην ἐπαγάγοιντο τὸν τῶν πολλῶν ἀνθρώπων νόμον τε καὶ λόγον καὶ ψόγον</quote> (‘<emph>call in</emph> to rule them’). Menander <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑδρία</quote> fr. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">γέροντα δυστυχοῦντα, τῶν θ’ αὑτοῦ κακῶν | ἐπαγόμενον λήθην, ἀνέμνησας πάλιν</quote> (‘seeking to procure forgetfulness of his troubles’). The word is admirably suitable and vivid here: man looks to every side for succour against the foe that is ever in the land,—Death; but from no quarter can he find help. It is surprising that so many recent critics should have confidently condemned <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάξεται</quote>, and sought to replace it by conjectures (see cr. n.). </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ δὲ δὴ παρὸν ἡμῖν τὰ νῦν οἷον μάρτυρα
									ἐπαγόμεθα· δηλοῖ μὲν ἂν ὃ βουλόμεθα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμηχάνων_363">, such as seem to baffle all treatment: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 140"> <title>El.</title> 140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμήχανον | ἅλγος</quote> </cit>: Simonides <emph>ap.</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 344C">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 344C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμήχανος συμφορά.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγάς_363">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦξιν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 799">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 799</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέκτρων...φυγάς</quote> </cit>, means of escape from the union; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 759">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 759</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ τῶν ἀμηχάνων πόρους εὐμηχάνους πορίζων.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
κἀκ τῶν ἀμηχάνων πόρους εὐμηχάνους
									πορίζων.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμπέφρασται_363">, here midd., as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 438">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 438</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δὴ πέφρασμαι</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1016"> <title>O. C.</title> 1016</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφόν_ἔχων_365">: lit., possessing, in his resourceful skill, a thing subtle beyond belief: <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφόν τι</quote> is predicate, and in apposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μηχανόεν τέχνας</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.89">Thuc. 2.89</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα τι τῆς διανοίας τὸ βέβαιον ἔχοντες ἀντιτολμῶσιν</quote> </cit>: ‘they are strong in the confidence of their spirit when they confront the foe.’ There is no ground for altering <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφόν</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν, τοῖον</quote>, or the like.</gloss>

data.quote:
μέγα τι τῆς διανοίας τὸ βέβαιον ἔχοντες
									ἀντιτολμῶσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τ_365">., the inventive quality in his skill: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τέχνας</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 380"> <title>O. T.</title> 380</ref> n.; for the absence of <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶς</quote>, cp. above, 10 (<quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.90">Thuc. 1.90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ...βουλόμενον καὶ ϋποπτον τῆς γνώμης</quote> </cit>: 2. 61 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ ὑμετέρψ ἀσθενεῖ τῆς γνώμης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοτὲ_ἄλλοτ_367">. L has <quote xml:lang="grc">τοτὲ</quote> here: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 739"> <title>El.</title> 739</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τότ’</quote> </cit> (i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">τοτὲ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅλλος, ἅλλοθ’ α+τερος</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 237e"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 237 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοτὲ μὲν ἡ ἑτέρα, ἅλλοτε δὲ ἡ ἑτέρα κρατεῖ</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1745"> <title>O. C.</title> 1745</ref> n. There is no reason, then, for reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὲ μέν</quote> here with inferior MSS., though it is equally good (Plato has <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὲ μὲν</quote> answered by <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὲ δέ</quote>, by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνίοτε δέ</quote>, or by <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις δέ</quote>). </gloss>

data.quote:
τοτὲ μὲν ἡ ἑτέρα, ἅλλοτε δὲ ἡ ἑτέρα
									κρατεῖ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_367"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">κακόν</quote> as well as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθλόν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 212">cp. 212</ref> n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεραίρων_368">, ‘honouring,’ is in my belief a certain correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">παρείρων</quote>. The latter=‘weaving in,’ as a thread into a texture, or a flower into a wreath: for the genuine fig. use of it, see <ref n="Plat. Sym. 6. 2">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 6. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταξὺ τοῦ ὑμᾶς λέγειν οὐδ’ ἂν τρίχα, μὴ ὅτι λόγον, ἄν τις παρείρειε</quote>: ‘while you are speaking, one could not put in a hair, much less a speech’ (so close and continuous is the texture of your speaking). Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">παρείρων</quote> has been explained as, ‘weaving the laws (etc.) into the texture of his life’; but, even if we grant that so strange a phrase would be possible with words added to express ‘the texture of his life,’ it is certainly impossible without them. Dindorf proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">παραιρῶν</quote> as=‘wresting,’ ‘violating’ (a strange sense), and pointed at <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαν·</quote> ‘he comes to evil (though at other times to good) when he violates the laws,’ etc. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΓΕΠΑΙΠΟΝ</quote> could easily generate <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΑΠΕΙΠΟΝ. γεραίρειν</quote>, prop. to distinguish by gifts of honour, is also a general poet. synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμᾶν</quote>: cp. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_δίκαν_369">, Justice, which men swear to observe, taking oaths by the gods (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκοι θεῶν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 647"> <title>O. T.</title> 647</ref> n.): =<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκοις θεῶν κεκυρωμένην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑψίπολις_370"> seems best taken as =<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψηλὴν πόλιν ἔχων</quote>: cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 8">Pind. <title>P.</title> 8. 22</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ δικαιόπολις...νᾶσος</quote> (Aegina). In <ref n="Pind. O. 2">Pind. <title>O.</title> 2. 8</ref>Theron, tyrant of Acragas, is called <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθόπολις</quote> in an active sense, as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶν τὴν πόλιν</quote>. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 510"> <title>O. T.</title> 510</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁδύπολις = ἁνδάνων τῇ πόλει</quote> </cit>: but it is harder to suppose that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψίπολις</quote> could have been intended to mean, ‘standing high in his city.’ Nor would that be the fittest sense. The loyal citizen makes the prosperous city; and her prosperity is his. <ref target="cl189">See on 189</ref>. In this clause the Chorus thinks especially of Creon (191 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῖσδ’ ἐγὼ νόμοισι τήνδ’ αὔξω πόλιν</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπολις_370">. Where the typical citizen is a law-breaker, the city is ruined, and the evil-doer is left citiless. So Creon had described law-breaking as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτην...ἀστοῖς</quote> (185). The contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψίπολις</quote> shows that the sense is not merely, ‘when a man breaks the law he becomes an exile’ (<cit> <bibl n="Lys. 21.35">Lys. or. 21 § 35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ ...πολιτῶν ἀπόλιδας</quote> </cit>). The central thought is the power of human wit to make or mar the <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote>, according as the man is moral or immoral.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_καλόν_370">, the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, such a mood as is not good.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμας_χάριν_370">, by reason of (‘thanks to’) his audacity, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύνεστι</quote>, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπολίς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote>). In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 888"> <title>O. T.</title> 888</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπότμου χάριν χλιδᾶς</quote> </cit> is not precisely similar, since it goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά νιν ἕλοιτο μοῖρα</quote>. Others point at <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύνεστι</quote>, taking <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμας χάριν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτ’ ἐμοί κ.τ.λ.</quote>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτ’</quote> should clearly be the first word in that sentence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέστιος_372">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 249"> <title>O. T.</title> 249</ref> n., where Oed. invokes a curse on himself, should the murderer become <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνέστιος</quote> with him: also <ref n="Soph. OT 240"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 240</ref> n. <ref n="Hor. Carm. 3. 2. 26">Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 3. 2. 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">vetabo, qui Cereris sacrum Vulgarit arcanae, sub isdem Sit trabibus, fragilemve mecum Solvat phaselon.</quote> Schneidewin cp. also Eur. fr. 848 (the dishonourer of parents) <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοι γένοιτο μήτε συνθύ\της ποτέ, κ.τ.λ.</quote>, and <cit> <bibl n="Call. Cer. 117">Callim. <title>Hymn.</title> 6. 117 (to Demeter)</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τῆνος ἐμὶν φίλος, ὅς τοι ἀπεχθής, | εἴη, μήθ’ ὁμότοιχος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ τῆνος ἐμὶν φίλος, ὅς τοι ἀπεχθής, | εἴη,
									μήθ’ ὁμότοιχος.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσον_φρονῶν_372">, ‘may he not become like-minded with me,’ is another way of saying, ‘may I never come to share his sentiments.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.50"> <title>Il.</title> 15.50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἶσον ἐμοὶ φρονέουσα</quote> </cit>, ‘likeminded with me’: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 634">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 634</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ φρονῶν ξυνῳδά</quote> </cit>. In a narrower sense (not intended here) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ αὐτὰ φρονεῖν</quote> was said of agreement in politics, <foreign xml:lang="lat">idem sentire de republica:</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.60">Her. 1.60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὠυτὸ φρονήσαντες</quote> </cit>, ‘having made common cause’ (the <quote xml:lang="grc">στασιῶται</quote> of Megacles and Lycurgus). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρδει_375">, L's reading, should be kept. The indic. is, of course, compatible with generality: for such an indic. after an optative, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 4.51">Dem. or. 4. 51</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νικῴη δ’ ὅ τι πᾶσιν ὑμῖν μέλλει συνοίσειν</quote> </cit>. It is also rather in favour of the indic. that the speaker is here thinking of an actual case. The optat. </gloss>

data.quote:
νικῴη δ’ ὅ τι πᾶσιν ὑμῖν μέλλει
									συνοίσειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρδοι_375"> would be abstract, ‘any one who should conceivably do these things,’ and would be equally correct: cp. on 666. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_376">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 248">cp. 248</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἀνδρῶν</quote>, 319 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δρῶν</quote>). At this moment Antigone is led in by the Guard.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφινοῶ_376">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 316"> <title>O. C.</title> 316</ref> (where Antigone can scarcely believe her eyes, on seeing Ismene,) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔστιν; ἆρ’ οὐκ ἔστιν; ἢ γνώμη πλανᾷ</quote>; For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_376">, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">φοβεῖσθαι εἴς τι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 980"> <title>O. T.</title> 980</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαιμόνιον_τέρας_376">, a portent sent by gods,—so astounding as to require a supernatural cause. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 1.3.5">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1.3.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μή τι δαιμόνιον εἴη</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_376">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 248">cp. 248</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἀνδρῶν</quote>, 319 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δρῶν</quote>). At this moment Antigone is led in by the Guard.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφινοῶ_376">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 316"> <title>O. C.</title> 316</ref> (where Antigone can scarcely believe her eyes, on seeing Ismene,) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔστιν; ἆρ’ οὐκ ἔστιν; ἢ γνώμη πλανᾷ</quote>; For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_376">, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">φοβεῖσθαι εἴς τι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 980"> <title>O. T.</title> 980</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαιμόνιον_τέρας_376">, a portent sent by gods,—so astounding as to require a supernatural cause. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 1.3.5">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1.3.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μή τι δαιμόνιον εἴη</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_εἰδὼς_377"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘How, when I know (that she <emph>is</emph> Antigone, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ἐστί</quote>), shall I maintain that she is <emph>not?</emph>’ (<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὐκ εἶναι</emph> = ὅτι οὐκ ἐστί</quote>). A simple verb of ‘saying’ regularly takes <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> with inf. in oratio obliqua: <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω οὐκ εἶναι = ὅτι οὐκ ἐστί</quote>. If this verb of saying is negatived, the negative with the inf. is still <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς λέξω οὐκ εἶναι</quote>; Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλογέω</quote> has the construction of a simple verb of saying: <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἀντιλογήσω οὐκ εἶναι</quote>; Hermann conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐ τήνδ’ εἶναι</quote> (which is palaeographically very improbable). <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐκ εἶναι</quote> would be the normal constr. after <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἀντιλογήσω</quote>, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλογέω</quote> were viewed in its special quality as a verb of ‘denying.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνοῦμαι μὴ εἶναι</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἀρνήσομαι μὴ οὐκ</quote> (or simply <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1388"> <title>O. T.</title> 1388</ref> n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>; Cp. below, 443 n. It may be noted that, when <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλέγω</quote> means to ‘deny,’ it is more often followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> with the finite verb; when followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> and inf., it more often means ‘to protest against’ a measure; <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.41">Thuc. 3.41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντέλεγε...μὴ ἀποκτεῖναι Μυτιληναίους</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 2.2.20">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2.2.20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρὸν ὂν</quote> </cit> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δεῖ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλέγειν τὸ μὴ οὐχὶ τὸν πλεῖστα πονοῦντα...μεγίστων ἀξιοῦσθαι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιλογήσω_377"> might be deliberative aor., but is rather fut. ind. (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1419"> <title>O. T.</title> 1419</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 310"> <title>O. C.</title> 310</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_δύστηνος_379">: nom. for voc., <ref n="Soph. OC 185"> <title>O. C.</title> 185</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_πατρός_379">, gen. of origin: <ref n="Soph. OC 214"> <title>O. C.</title> 214</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Οἰδιπόδα_379"> occurs as gen. in <ref n="Soph. OT 495"> <title>O. T.</title> 495</ref>, but as voc. <ref n="Soph. OT 1194"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1194.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ποτ_381">; Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1210."> <title>Ph.</title> 1210.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_που_381">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1472"> <title>O. T.</title> 1472</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δὴ κλύω που...;</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπιστοῦσαν_381"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπειθοῦσαν</foreign>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 219">cp. 219</ref>, 656.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγουσι_381">. It is far more probable that the final <quote xml:lang="grc">ιν</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλείοισιν</quote> should have dropped out in L (see cr. n.) than that Soph. should have written <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγουσι</quote>. At Athens <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαγωγή</quote> were technical terms for a process of summary arrest by which any citizen could bring before the magistrates a person taken in a criminal act (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτοφώρῳ</quote>). We have seen (on v. 158) that a word with a technical Attic sense was not necessarily excluded from Attic poetry. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγω</quote> would surely jar here. Allowing for the difference between a technicality and a</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_381"> connects </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπιστοῦσαν_381_2"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθελόντες_381"> (not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀφροσύνῃ</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 737"> <title>O. C.</title> 737</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκείνη_384">, she whom we sought: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 138"> <title>O. C.</title> 138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἐκεῖνος ἐγώ</quote> </cit>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὔργον_ξειργ_384">.: for the order, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 324">cp. 324</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_δέον_386">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1416"> <title>O. T.</title> 1416</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς δέον πάρεσθ’ ὅδε | Κρέων</quote> </cit>: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 78"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 78</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς καλόν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1168"> <title>Ai.</title> 1168</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς αὐτὸν καιρόν</quote> </cit>. L has <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς μέσον</quote>, i.e. ‘he comes forth in public’ (so that you, and all, can speak with him). The phrase occurs elsewhere in Soph., but never with ref. to entrance on the stage: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 609">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 609</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδειξ’ Ἀχαιοῖς ἐς μέσον</quote> (showed him publicly); <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1285"> <title>Ai.</title> 1285</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν κλῆρον ἐς μέσον καθείς</quote> </cit> (i.e. among the others): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 514"> <title>Tr.</title> 514</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσαν ἐς μέσον</quote> </cit> (into the arena). Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς δέον</quote> is not only far the better phrase, but is confirmed by Creon's question in the next v., <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίᾳ ξύμμετρος... τύχῃ</quote>; A, and almost all the later MSS., have <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς δέον</quote>, which may, doubtless, have been a conjecture suggested by <ref n="Soph. OT 1416"> <title>O. T.</title> 1416</ref>, —as is held by those who believe all our MSS. to have come from L; but it looks more like a true reading which L has somehow missed. Cp. on 831. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύμμετρος_387">, commensurate with, i.e. here, opportune for. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 84"> <title>O. T.</title> 84</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύμμετρος γὰρ ὡς κλύειν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1113"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνᾴδει τῷδε τἀνδρὶ σύμμετρος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπώμοτον_388">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">abiurandum:</foreign> there is nothing, the possibility of which men ought to deny on oath: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ὅ τι ἀπομνύναι χρὴ βροτοὺς μὴ οὐκ ἂν γενέσθαι</quote>. Archilochus fr. 76 <quote xml:lang="grc">χρημάτων ἄελπτον οὐδέν ἐστιν οὐδ’ ἀπώμοτον | οὐδὲ θαυμάσιον</quote>. Eupolis <quote xml:lang="grc">ΙΙόλεις</quote> fr. 25 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστ’ Ἀθηναίοισι πρᾶγμ’ ἀπώμοτον;</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψεύδει_389">=falsifies (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδοποιεῖν</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.66">Thuc. 3.66</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν...ψευσθεῖσαν υπόσχεσιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_πίνοια_389">, here, the <emph>after</emph>-thought, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ δεύτεραι φροντίδες</quote>. But usually the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινοέω, ἐπίνοια</quote>, denotes <emph>advance</emph>,— ‘forming a design,’ or ‘inventing’: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 90">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶνον σὺ τολμᾷς εἰς ἐπίνοιαν λοιδορεῖν</quote> </cit>; Cp. Lucian <quote xml:lang="grc">Προμηθεὺς εἷ ἐν λόγοις</quote> 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γε μεταβουλεύεσθαι Ἐπιμηθέως ἔργον, οὐ Προμηθέως ἐστίν</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: οἶνον σὺ τολμᾷς εἰς ἐπίνοιαν λοιδορεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξηύχουν_ἄν_390">, I could have vowed, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχολῇ_δεῦρο_390">, that it would be long before I ever came hither. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 1619">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 1619</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄν ποτ’ ηὔχουν οὔτε σ’ οὔθ’ ἡμᾶς λαθεῖν | Μενέλαον, ὦναξ, ὡς ἐλάνθανεν πάρων</quote> </cit>: ‘I should never have expected that he would escape us’; where (as the order of words shows) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὔχουν</quote>,—the suppressed protasis being, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἠρώτα τις</quote>, ‘if any one had asked me.’ So <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.22">Lys. or. 12 § 22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δ’ ἐβουλόμην ἂν αὐτοὺς ἀληθῆ λέγειν</quote> </cit>, ‘I could wish’ (the ref. there being to present time), <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δυνατὸν ἦν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 869">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 869</ref> n. The needless emendations of this verse have aimed at disjoining <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηύχουν</quote> and attaching it to the infin., or else at removing it altogether. But, though the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> had actually said (in the ‘aside’ at 329) that he did not mean to come back, he was not therefore debarred from using this turn of phrase; ‘I <emph>could have</emph> vowed that I would not come back.’</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ ἄν ποτ’ ηὔχουν οὔτε σ’ οὔθ’ ἡμᾶς λαθεῖν |
									Μενέλαον, ὦναξ, ὡς ἐλάνθανεν πάρων

ἐγὼ δ’ ἐβουλόμην ἂν αὐτοὺς ἀληθῆ
									λέγειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχολῇ_390"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 231">cp. 231</ref>), here iron., ‘not in a hurry’ (<ref n="Soph. OT 434"> <title>O. T.</title> 434</ref> n.); <cit> <bibl n="Shak. TIT 1.2.301">Shaksp. <title>Tit. Andron.</title> 1. 2. 301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘I'll trust by leisure him that mocks me once.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
‘I'll trust by leisure him that mocks me
									once.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῖς_ἀπειλαῖς_391">, ‘by reason of thy threats’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 335">cp. 335</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">νότῳ</quote>) n, 588 (<quote xml:lang="grc">πνοαῖς</quote>), 956 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργαῖς</quote>). Here, the causal dat. seems also to suggest occasion (‘at the time of your threats’): <ref target="cl691">see on 691</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχειμάσθην_391">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1460"> <title>Ph.</title> 1460</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμαζομένῳ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote>), in my sore trouble. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡἐκτὸς_392"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐλπίδων</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ παρὰ ἐλπίδας</quote>. I cannot parallel this zeugma of preps. with a case suited only to the second (in <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 17.760"> <title>Il.</title> 17.760</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περί τ’ ἀμφί τε τάφρον</quote> </cit> both preps. take acc.): and yet it seems to be genuine, the phrase being so energetic and compact. </gloss>
