<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτός_392"> is certainly supported by 330, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτοπος, ἄλογος</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκός</quote> are all very improbable substitutes. Nor can I think, with Schütz, that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτός</quote> is here adverb: ‘the outside joy’ (i.e. outside of one's calculations). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_μῆκος_393">, is not at all (adv.) like in greatness, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αλλῃ_ἡδονῇ_393">, to any other pleasure; i.e. is vastly greater than any other. For the adv. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_393">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lysis 220C"> <title>Lys.</title> 220 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φίλον...οὐδὲν τούτοις ἔοικε</quote> </cit>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῆκος_393">=<foreign xml:lang="lat">amplitudo</foreign>, Empedocles 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οἵης τιμῆς τε καὶ ὅσσου μήκεος ὄλβου</quote>: so Pindar speaks of <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὸς ὄλβος</quote> (<title>P.</title> 2. 26), <quote xml:lang="grc">μακροτέρα ἀρετά</quote> (<title>I.</title> 4. 21), Aristotle of <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὰ τιμήματα</quote> ( <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 3.1279b"> <title>Pol.</title> 3. 5. 6</ref>, opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχέα</quote> <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 4.1291b"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 4. 4. 24</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὰ οὐσία</quote> (<ref n="Aristot. Pol. 4.1290b"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 4. 4. 5</ref>), and Soph. himself of <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὸς πλοῦτος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 130"> <title>Ai.</title> 130</ref>). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔοικε_393"> Wolff-Bellermann cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.71">Thuc. 7.71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...φόβος ἦν... οὐδενὶ ἐοικώς</quote> </cit> (‘great beyond example’), <title>De Vect.</title> 4. 31 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενὶ τῶν παρεληλυθότων ἐοικός</quote> (‘far greater than any of the past’); and for the sentiment, Eur. fr. 554 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τῶν ἀέλπτων ἡ χάρις μείζων βροτοῖς | φανεῖσα μᾶλλον ἢ τὸ προσδοκώμενον</quote>.— Nauck's treatment of this verse and the next has no justification: see cr. n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῑ_ἀπώμοτος_394">, though I had sworn with oaths (sworn solemnly) not to come. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1113"> <title>Ai.</title> 1113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκων οἷσιν ἦν ἐνώμοτος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 150">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 150</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίορκον ὅρκον ὤμοσεν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθῃρέθη_395"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 383">cp. 383</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθελόντες</quote>) is clearly better here than L's <quote xml:lang="grc">καθευρέθη</quote>: and the compound <quote xml:lang="grc">καθευρίσκω</quote> is nowhere found in classical </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάφον_κοσμοῦσα_395">, paying the due rites of burial (<quote xml:lang="grc">τάφον = ταφήν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 490">cp. 490</ref>), by sprinkling the dust and pouring the libations on the corpse. <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμέω</quote> was specially said of obsequies: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 901">cp. 901</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1139"> <title>El.</title> 1139</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτροῖς ἐκόσμησ’.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπάλλετο_395">, as when lots were shaken in a helmet (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 1285"> <title>Ai.</title> 1285</ref>; cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 710"> <title>El.</title> 710</ref>). His </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρμαιον_395"> is the luck of being the first to bring the glad tidings,—as his former mission was ironically called <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο τἀγαθόν</quote> (275). In the discovery and seizure of Antigone he had no greater share than his comrades (432). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρῖνε_399"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνάκρινε</foreign>, question: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 586"> <title>Ai.</title> 586</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κρῖνε, μὴ ’ξέταζε.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαιός_εἰμι_399">: the only certain instance in Tragedy of the personal constr. (<cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.40">Thuc. 1.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαιοί γ’ ἐστε...ἰέναι</quote> </cit>); for in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 1080">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1080</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῷ γε μᾶλλον σαυτὸν ἤσκησας σέβειν | ἢ τοὺς τεκόντας ὅσια δρᾶν, δίκαιος ὤν</quote> </cit>, the latter words are more pointed if ironical (‘just man though thou art’): and <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 55">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ κόσμος οὔτε πρὸς θεῶν ἀγάλματα | φέρειν δίκαιος</quote> </cit> is different (‘proper to bring’). But Ar. has this use, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1283"> <title>Nub.</title> 1283</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολαβεῖν...δίκαιος εἶ</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 1434"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1434</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
πολλῷ γε μᾶλλον σαυτὸν ἤσκησας σέβειν | ἢ τοὺς
									τεκόντας ὅσια δρᾶν, δίκαιος ὤν

καὶ κόσμος οὔτε πρὸς θεῶν ἀγάλματα | φέρειν
									δίκαιος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπηλλάχθαι_399">, perf., for good and all: <ref n="Soph. OT 1050"> <title>O. T.</title> 1050</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_ἐπίστασαι_401">: one of the formulas which often close a messenger's speech, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 484"> <title>Tr.</title> 484</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐπίστασαι λόγον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 480"> <title>Ai.</title> 480</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἀκήκοας λόγον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 876"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 876</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἀκήκοας</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 241"> <title>Ph.</title> 241</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσθα δὴ τὸ πᾶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_καὶ_403">, <ref n="Soph. OT 368"> <title>O. T.</title> 368</ref> n. The first <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> here is not ‘both.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθῶς_403">, ‘rightly’: i.e. do your words express what you really</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_νεκρόν_404">: antecedent with art. drawn into relative clause: <ref n="Soph. OC 907"> <title>O. C.</title> 907</ref> n. <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ οὕσπερ αὐτὸς τοὺς νόμους εἰσῆλθ’ ἔχων, | τούτοισιν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_ἔνδηλα_405">: said triumphantly: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 268">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 268</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. πῶς φής...; ΚΛ. Τροίαν Ἀχαιῶν οὖσαν· ἦ τορῶς λέγω</quote> </cit>; Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1158"> <title>Ai.</title> 1158</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδείς ποτ’ ἄλλος ἢ σύ. μῶν ᾐνιξάμην</quote> </cit>; </gloss>

data.quote:
ΧΟ. πῶς φής...; ΚΛ. Τροίαν Ἀχαιῶν οὖσαν· ἦ
									τορῶς λέγω
οὐδείς ποτ’ ἄλλος ἢ σύ. μῶν ᾐνιξάμην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶταιᾑρέθη_406">: historic pres. combined with aor., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 31"> <title>Ai.</title> 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φράζει τε κἀδήλωσεν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 419">cp. 419</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 359"> <title>Tr.</title> 359</ref> ff., <ref n="Soph. OT 118"> <title>O. T.</title> 118</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίληπτος_406">, seized in the act=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτοφώρῳ</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.69">Her. 3.69</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίλαμπτος... ἀφάσσουσα</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὦτα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσται</quote>, ‘will be caught feeling the ears.’ <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 25.80">[Dem. ] or. 25 § 80</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐπιλήπτους <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the epileptic)</note> φησὶν ἰᾶσθαι, αὐτὸς ὢν ἐπίληπτος πάσῃ πονηρίᾳ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς ἐπιλήπτους   φησὶν ἰᾶσθαι, αὐτὸς ὢν ἐπίληπτος πάσῃ
									πονηρίᾳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκομεν_407">. The occupation of the guards was temporarily gone when they perceived that the burial, which they had been set to prevent, had been effected (255). Creon, speaking to their deputy, sent them back to their post, with orders to discover the culprit (306). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκομεν</quote> (imperf.) simply refers to their taking up their station again near the body. It need not imply that they had escorted their comrade on his way to Creon. We could not take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκομεν...ἐπηπειλημένοι</quote> as merely plur. for sing., and then refer <quote xml:lang="grc">σήραντες</quote>, etc., to the guards collectively. Nor is the conject. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκόμην</quote> needed. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἐκεῖν_408">. Creon's threats were addressed to all the guards: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 305">cp. 305</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_νέκυν_408">: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 351"> <title>O. C.</title> 351</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῆς | οἴκοι διαίτης</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 263"> <title>Ph.</title> 263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν οἱ | δισσοὶ στρατηγοί</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 879"> <title>El.</title> 879</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπὶ τοῖς | σαυτῆς κακοῖσι</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Eccl. 452">Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 452</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ τὸν | δῆμον</quote> </cit>. Aesch. places the art. thus only when it is a <emph>pronoun</emph> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 7">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 385">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 385</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 137">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ</quote>). In Eur. no instance seems to occur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθήμεθ_411"> is better taken as plpf. than pf., for, though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκομεν</quote> is ambiguous, we have a series of historical tenses in 415—421. Ar. sometimes uses and sometimes omits the augment, as <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 638">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 638</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκάθησθε</quote>, <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 304">Ar. <title>Eccl.</title> 304</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθῆντο</quote> (both proved by metre); and if our MSS. can be trusted, classical prose, too, admitted both forms, as Aeschin. or. 2 § 89 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκαθήμην</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.169">Dem. or. 18 § 169</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθῆτο</quote> </cit>. In the five pluperfects furnished by Attic inscriptions of 428— 325 B.C. the syllabic augment is always added, but there is no epigraphic evidence in the particular case of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκαθήμην</quote> (see Meisterhans, p. 77).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκρων_πάγων_411">, with <quote xml:lang="grc">καθήμεθα</quote>, ‘on the hill-top.’ The corpse lay on the highest part of the Theban plain (1110, 1197), with rising ground (<quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι</quote>) behind or around it. The guards post themselves on this rising ground, facing the corpse, and with their backs to the wind. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>), with a verb denoting <emph>position</emph>, occurs only in a few places of poetry; but it is certainly genuine, and deserves attention, for its true force has not (I think) been observed. (1) <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 14.153"> <title>Il.</title> 14.153</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥρη δ’ εἰσεῖδε χρυσόθρονος ὀφθαλμοῖσιν | στᾶς</quote> </cit>'</gloss>

data.quote:
Ἥρη δ’ εἰσεῖδε χρυσόθρονος ὀφθαλμοῖσιν |
									στᾶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπήνεμοι_411">, under the wind, i.e., so that it blew from behind them, not in their faces, as the next v. explains. (At v. 421 the dust is blown in their faces, but that is by the sudden, gusty <quote xml:lang="grc">σκηπτός</quote>.) The idea of ‘sheltered,’ which <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπήνεμος</quote> usu. implies, is less prominent here, yet quite admissible, if we suppose them to sit just below the summits of the <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Ec. 18. 6">Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 18. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦ προσηνέμου μέρους</quote>, on the side <emph>towards which</emph> the wind blows, opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦ ὑπηνέμου</quote>, to windward. Theophr. <title>Causs. Plantt.</title> 3.6.9 opposes <quote xml:lang="grc">πνευματώδης καὶ προσήνεμος τόπος</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὑπήνεμα</quote>: and <title>Hist. An.</title> 9. 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν προσηνέμῳ</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐπισκεπεῖ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάλῃ_412">, the ‘vivid’ subjunct. (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">βάλοι</quote>), after a secondary tense; while in v. 414 we have the normal optat. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκηδήσοι</quote>). For this combination cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.5.17">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.5.17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήγγειλαν, ἐπειδὴ δειπνήσειαν,...ἀναπαύεσθαι, καὶ ἕπεσθαι ἡνίκ’ ἄν τις παραγγείλῃ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
παρήγγειλαν, ἐπειδὴ
									δειπνήσειαν,...ἀναπαύεσθαι, καὶ ἕπεσθαι ἡνίκ’ ἄν τις
									παραγγείλῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγερτί_413">: see on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστακτί</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1251"> <title>O. C.</title> 1251</ref>. Each man was careful to keep wide awake, and also to see that his comrades did so.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κινῶν_413">, urging to vigilance. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 329D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 329D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλόμενος ἔτι λέγειν αὐτὸν ἐκίνουν καὶ εἶπον, ὦ Κέφαλε, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit> The conject. <quote xml:lang="grc">νεικῶν</quote> is needless. For the sing. instead of the plur. (<quote xml:lang="grc">κινοῦντες</quote>) in partitive apposition, see on <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ ἐλέγχων φύλακα</quote>, 260. </gloss>

data.quote:
βουλόμενος ἔτι λέγειν αὐτὸν ἐκίνουν καὶ εἶπον,
									ὦ Κέφαλε, κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιρρόθοις_κακοῖσιν_413">, lit., with obstreperous taunts,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκηδήσοι_414">: fut. opt. in orat. obliqua; the direct form of the threat would be (<quote xml:lang="grc">κλαύσει</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἀκηδήσεις</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν ἀκηδήσῃς</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 374"> <title>Ph.</title> 374</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρασσον κακοῖς | τοῖς πᾶσιν... | εἰ τἀμὰ κεῖνος ὅπλ’ ἀφαιρήσοιτό με</quote>: he said (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιο</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἀφαιρήσει</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 312"> <title>Ai.</title> 312</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δείν’ ἐπηπείλησ’ ἔπη, | εἰ μὴ φανοίην</quote> </cit> (he said, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ φανεῖς</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκηδέω</quote> had been used by <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.427">Hom. <title>Il.</title> 14.427</ref>, 23. 70, <ref n="Aesch. PB 508">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 508</ref>, and recurs in later poetry. The MS. </gloss>

data.quote: δείν’ ἐπηπείλησ’ ἔπη, | εἰ μὴ φανοίην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφειδήσοι_414"> cannot be defended as=‘play the prodigal with,’ i.e. ‘be careless of.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφειδεῖν πόνου</quote> could mean only to be unsparing of labour. In <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.26">Thuc. 4.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφειδὴς ὁ κατάπλους καθειστήκει</quote> </cit> is explained by the next words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπώκελλον γὰρ τὰ πλοῖα τετιμημένα χρημάτων</quote>: they were ‘unsparing’ of their boats, since a value had been set on the latter. Bonitz, to whom <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκηδήσοι</quote> is due, refers to Apoll. Rhod. 2. 98 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἄρα Βέβρυκες ἄνδρες ἀφείδησαν βασιλῆος</quote>, where Choeroboscus has preserved the true <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκήδησαν</quote>. In the schol. on <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 204"> <title>Ai.</title> 204</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φειδόμενοι</quote> </cit> occurs by error for <quote xml:lang="grc">κηδόμενοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέστη_416">, prop., had taken its place. There is a Homeric echo here: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 8.66"> <title>Il.</title> 8.66</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφρα μὲν ἠὼς ἦν καὶ ἀέξετο ἱερὸν ἦμαρ, | τόφρα μάλ’ ἀμφοτέρων βέλε’ ἥπτετο, πῖπτε δὲ λαός· | ἦμος δ’ ἠέλιος μέσον οὐρανὸν ἀμφιβεβήκει, | καὶ τότε δὴ χρύσεια πατὴρ ἐτίταινε τάλαντα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄφρα μὲν ἠὼς ἦν καὶ ἀέξετο ἱερὸν ἦμαρ, | τόφρα
									μάλ’ ἀμφοτέρων βέλε’ ἥπτετο, πῖπτε δὲ λαός· | ἦμος δ’ ἠέλιος
									μέσον οὐρανὸν ἀμφιβεβήκει, | καὶ τότε δὴ χρύσεια πατὴρ ἐτίταινε
									τάλαντα.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθονὸς_416"> (<emph>from</emph> the ground) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀείρας</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 142"> <title>O. T.</title> 142</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βάθρων | ἵστασθε</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυφώς_418">, the whirlwind: the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκηπτός_418"> is the storm of dust (<quote xml:lang="grc">κονιορτός</quote>) which it lifts from the ground. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">σκηπτός</quote> usu.=‘a thunderbolt,’ and by its deriv. ought at least to mean a storm swooping on the earth from the sky; but the schol. attests its use in a larger sense; <quote xml:lang="grc">σκηπτὸς δὲ λέγεται πᾶν πνεῦμα θυελλῶδες, ὅταν συνερείδῃ τῇ γῇ, καὶ πάλιν ἄνω αἴρη·| τὸ δὲ τοιοῦτο καὶ στρόβι—λόν τινες καλοῦσι, παρὰ τὸ στροβεῖν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐράνιον_ἄχος_418">, a trouble in the sky (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1466"> <title>O. C.</title> 1466</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανία...ἀστραπή</quote> </cit>), since the cloud of dust darkened the sky: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λυποῦν τὸν αἰθέρα, καθὸ ταράσσει αὐτόν</quote>: only <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχος</quote> is rather what annoys <emph>us</emph> than what annoys the (personified) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανός</quote>. In these lines the poet describes the actual physical effects produced by the storm. He mentions the destruction of foliage; and we need some reference also to the main point of all—the obscuring of the air. Therefore I should not take <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐράνιον ἄχος</quote> as=‘a <emph>heaven-sent</emph> plague’; that is presently said by <quote xml:lang="grc">θείαν νόσον</quote> (421). A third version—‘a trouble rising <emph>high as</emph> heaven’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐράνιον πήδημα</quote>, etc.)—is also possible, but less suitable here than either of the others. In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 809">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 809</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴυζε δ’ ὀμφὰν οὐρανίαν</quote> </cit>, the adj. clearly=<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανομήκη</quote>, and so perh. in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 572"> <title>Pers.</title> 572</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμβόασον οὐράνῑ ἄχη</quote>, though there (as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 196"> <title>Ai.</title> 196</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄταν οὐρανίαν φλέγων</quote> </cit>) ‘heaven-sent’ is at least equally fitting.—For the tribrach in the 5th place, see <ref n="Soph. OT 719"> <title>O. T.</title> 719</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίμπλησι_419"> (histor. pres., between <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθαλπε</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνεμεστώθη</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 406">cp. 406</ref>), viz., with dust and scattered leaves.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰκίζων_419">, maltreating, <quote xml:lang="grc">λυμαινόμενος</quote>, by breaking the branches and tearing off the leaves.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἐμεστ_419">., tmesis: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1274">cp. 1274</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 713"> <title>El.</title> 713</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δὲ πᾶς ἐμεστώθη δρόμος</quote> </cit>. Distinguish the adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δέ</quote> (and withal), <ref n="Soph. OT 27"> <title>O. T.</title> 27</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μύσαντες_421">. <foreign xml:lang="grc">μυ—/ω</foreign> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">μῦ</quote>, a sound made with closed lips)=‘to be shut,’ said of the eyes, the lips, or any opening (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 24.637"> <title>Il.</title> 24.637</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ πω μύσαν ὄσσε</quote> </cit>): but the aor. part. regularly meant, ‘with eyes shut’: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 480C">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 480C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέχειν μύσαντα καὶ ἀνδρείως ὥσπερ τέμνειν καὶ κάειν ἰατρῷ</quote> </cit>. There was a proverb, <quote xml:lang="grc">μύσαντα φέρειν</quote> (‘to grin and bear it’), Meineke <title>Com.</title> 3, p. 4. </gloss>

data.quote:
παρέχειν μύσαντα καὶ ἀνδρείως ὥσπερ τέμνειν
									καὶ κάειν ἰατρῷ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόσον_421">, the scourge of the storm: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1141">cp. 1141</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_μακρ_421">.: for the prep. see <ref n="Soph. OC 88"> <title>O. C.</title> 88</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρᾶς_423">, in its bitterness. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρός</quote>, said of persons, means ‘embittered,’ and so ‘hostile,’ etc. (as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1359"> <title>Ai.</title> 1359</ref>). But there is no reason why <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρός</quote> should not also mean ‘embittered’ in the sense, ‘with a bitter feeling of anguish.’ There is a pathos in this which is lost by reading <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρῶς</quote>, ‘shrilly.’ Nor could <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρᾶς</quote> mean merely ‘piercing,’ as if the epithet of the cry were given to the bird itself. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1610"> <title>O. C.</title> 1610</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόγγος πικρός</quote> </cit>, and in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 190"> <title>Ph.</title> 190</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρὰ οἰμωγή</quote>, mean not merely a ‘shrill,’ but a ‘bitter,’ cry; and so conversely here, the epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρά</quote>, while primarily denoting anguish, also suggests the shrill sound. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνῆςλέχος_425"> would be a weak pleonasm for ‘nest’; it is better to take <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνῆς</quote> as the nest, and <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχος</quote> as the restingplace of the young birds within it. The phrases <quote xml:lang="grc">λέκτρων εὐναί, λέκτρων κοῖται, κοίτης λέκτρον</quote>, etc., said of the <emph>marriage</emph>bed, are not properly similar, meaning rather, ‘the bed on which they slept,’ etc.: i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐναί</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">κοῖται</quote> refer to the act of sleeping. <quote xml:lang="grc">κενῆς</quote> is certainly pleonastic with <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοσσῶν ὀρφανόν</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 31"> <title>Ph.</title> 31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κενὴν οἴκησιν ἀνθρώπων δίχα</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. OT 57"> <title>O. T.</title> 57</ref>n.), yet hardly anticipates it; the bird, approaching its nest, feels that it is <quote xml:lang="grc">κενή</quote>, then peeps in, and, sure enough, the <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχος</quote> is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρφανόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτω_δέ_426">, ‘so, I say, she’: for <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> introducing the apodosis in a simile cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 25"> <title>El.</title> 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ γὰρ ἵππος εὐγενής...θυμὸν οὐκ ἀπώλεσεν | ..., ὡσαύτως δὲ σὺ | ἡμᾶς τ’ ὀτρύνεις κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>: so <ref n="Soph. Trach. 112"> <title>Tr.</title> 112</ref> ff., <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ γὰρ ὥστ’...οὕτω δὲ τὸν Καδμογενῆ.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὥσπερ γὰρ ἵππος εὐγενής...θυμὸν οὐκ ἀπώλεσεν |
									..., ὡσαύτως δὲ σὺ | ἡμᾶς τ’ ὀτρύνεις κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψιλόν_426">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνιος</quote>, stripped of the dust which she had sprinkled on it (409). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC"> <title>O. C.</title> </ref> p. 279. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόοισιν_ἐξῴμωξεν_427">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 65"> <title>O. T.</title> 65</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνῳ γ’ εὕδοντα</quote> </cit>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ἠρᾶτο_427">, tmesis: cp. n. on 106.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διψίαν_427">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 246">cp. 246</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_κόνιν_429">. A difficulty presents itself. The essence of the symbolical rite was the sprinkling of dust. She had done that (245). Was it not, then, done once for all? In Horace (<title>C.</title> 1. 28. 35) the passer-by is free when the dust has been thrown; he can go his way. I have never seen this question put or answered. The only answer which I can suggest is that, at her first visit, she had not brought the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote>. (Cp. on 245 ff.) Perhaps the rite was considered complete only if the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> were poured while the dust still covered the corpse. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόχους_430">, or ‘out-pourer,’ was a jug, especially a water-jug, with a handle, and had, of course, various forms; some of the types given by Guhl and Koner (p. 147, fig. 198, 26—31) resemble modern water-jugs for washing.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐκρότητος_430">, ‘well-hammered,’ and so ‘wellwrought,’ is the epith. of a knife (<quote xml:lang="grc">δωρίς</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοπίς</quote>) in <ref n="Eur. El. 819">Eur. <title>El.</title> 819</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρδην_430"> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀέρδην</quote>, from <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρω</quote>) is found with <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδᾶν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 1279"> <title>Ai.</title> 1279</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Alc. 608">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 608</ref>), but is usu. fig., as with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολλύναι</quote>. Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρδην ἐκ πρόχου στέφει = ἄρασα τὸν πρόχουν στέφει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοαῖσι_431">. In <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.519"> <title>Od.</title> 10.519</ref> the three <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> to the dead are of (1) hydromel, (2) wine, (3) water: see <ref n="Soph. OC 479"> <title>O. C.</title> 479</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρισπόνδ_431">, instead of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">τρισίν</quote>: cp. on 346 <quote xml:lang="grc">σπ. δικτυοκλώστοις. χοαί</quote> were to the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρτεροι</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">σπονδαί</quote> to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατοι</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">λοιβαί</quote> could mean either (<ref n="Soph. OC 477"> <title>O. C.</title> 477</ref>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέφει_431">: <ref n="Soph. El. 51"> <title>El.</title> 51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβον... | λοιβαῖσι πρῶτον καὶ καρατόμοις χλιδαῖς | στέψαντες</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱέμεσθα_432">, pres.: for the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1279"> <title>O. C.</title> 1279</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_θηρώμεθ_432">, tmesis, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 746"> <title>El.</title> 746</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δ’ ἑλίσσεται | τμητοῖς ἱμᾶσι</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote> is adv. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 299"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 299</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δ’ ἐποτρύνει</quote> </cit>: cp. above, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85">85.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠλέγχομεν_434">, proceeded to prove against her, i.e. taxed her with, her past and present deeds. We should not supply <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτήν</quote>: the verb governs <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς πράξεις</quote> only: cp. <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 161">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 161</ref>E <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλέγχειν τὰς ἀλλήλων φαντασίας τε καὶ δόξας</quote> (examine into). It would be natural to say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα ἐλέγχω αὐτήν</quote>, but hardly <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς πράξεις ἐλέγχω αὐτήν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπαρνος_οὐδενός_434"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἀπαρνουμένη</foreign>, the gen. with the adj. corresponding to the acc. with the verb; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">κωλυτικός, ποιητικός τινος</quote> etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθίστατο_434">, she did not take up the position of denying anything. Her <emph>attitude</emph> towards the charge was one of simple confession. <quote xml:lang="grc">καθίστασθαι</quote> with a predicative adj. expresses definite assumption of a character, or complete attainment of a state; <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.102">Thuc. 3.102</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύμμαχον καθεστήξει</quote> </cit> (will have definitely allied itself): 6. 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέμιοι καθέστασαν</quote>: 4. 78 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πᾶσι...ὕποπτον καθεστήκει</quote>: 2. 59 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄποροι καθεστῶτες</quote>: 4. 26 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφειδὴς...καθεστήκει</quote>: 6. 59 <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπωτέρα...ἡ τυραννὶς κατέστη</quote>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 306"> <title>Ai.</title> 306</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμφρων...καθίσταται</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 703"> <title>O. T.</title> 703</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονέα...καθεστάναι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅμ_436"> for the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΜ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΛΛ</quote>) is certain; <quote xml:lang="grc">καθίστ</quote>. would be unmeaning with the adverbs, and we cannot supply a new verb. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.77.6">Thuc. 7.77 § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σιτία ἅμα κομίζειν</quote> </cit>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμα</quote> (for the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα</quote>) is due to Reiske. Besides <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμα μὲν ...ἅμα δέ</quote> (‘partly’...‘partly’), we also find double <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμα</quote>, (<emph>a</emph>) wherethe clauses are linked by <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, as here; <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 496B">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 496B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἅμα τε ἀπαλλάττεται ἄνθρωπος καὶ ἅμα ἔχει</quote> </cit> (cp. <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 497a"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 497 A</ref>): (<emph>b</emph>) with partic. and finite verb: id. <ref n="Plat. Tim. 38b"> <title>Tim.</title> 38 B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα ἅμα γεννηθέντες ἅμα καὶ λυθωσιν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. G. 3.201">Verg. <title>G.</title> 3. 201</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">simul arva fuga, simul aequora verrens.</quote> </cit> Cp. <ref n="Hor. S. 1.7">Hor. <title>S.</title> 1. 7. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">Inter | Hectora Priamiden animosum atque inter Achillen.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὧν ἅμα τε ἀπαλλάττεται ἄνθρωπος καὶ ἅμα
									ἔχει

simul arva fuga, simul aequora
									verrens.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλους_438"> <quote xml:lang="grc">φησί, διὰ τὸ εἷναι τὴν Ἀντιγόνην τοῦ βασιλικοῦ γένους</quote> (schol.): he is a <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦλος</quote> of the family. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 54">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρηστοῖσι δούλοις συμφορὰ τὰ δεσποτῶν | κακῶς πίτνοντα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
χρηστοῖσι δούλοις συμφορὰ τὰ δεσποτῶν | κακῶς
									πίτνοντα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_ταῦθ_439"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς κακὸν δὲ...ἀλγεινόν</quote>: ‘all these things’=‘all such objects as the safety of friends.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_439">, ‘to obtain,’ epexeg. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσσω</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 638">cp. 638</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 81"> <title>Ph.</title> 81</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1015"> <title>El.</title> 1015</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προνοίας οὐδὲν ἀνθρώποις ἔφυ | κέρδος λαβεῖν ἄμεινον</quote> </cit>: where, as here, we have a gen. depending on the comparat., instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote> with nom.— Semitelos takes <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπολαβεῖν</quote>: ‘all these considerations are naturally lower in my estimate than my own safety.’ Similarly Campbell; ‘It is in my nature’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ πέφυκε</quote>—a questionable sense) ‘to take less account of all this than of my own safety.’ But such a use of <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμβάνειν</quote> does not seem warranted by <ref n="Thuc. 2.42">Thuc. 2.42</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν τιμωρίαν...ποθεινοτέραν λαβόντες</quote>, or by such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμβάνειν τι ἐν πόθῳ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1679"> <title>O. C.</title> 1679</ref>).—Blaydes's <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄλλ’</quote> … <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</quote> </emph> is attractive, but unnecessary; and palaeographically it is not probable. </gloss>

data.quote:
προνοίας οὐδὲν ἀνθρώποις ἔφυ | κέρδος λαβεῖν
									ἄμεινον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὲ_δή_441"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶ</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 546">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 546</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ τὴν ὄρεγμα δεινὸν ἡμιλλωμένην | τύμβου ’πὶ κρηπῖδ’ ἐμπύρους τ’ ὀρθοστάτας, | μεῖνον</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 274">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 274</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΥ. οὗτος, ὦ σέ τοι. ΠΕ. τί βωστρεῖς</quote> </cit>; The abrupt acc. calls the person's attention in a rough and harsh way. A governing verb is sometimes added, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1445"> <title>El.</title> 1445</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ τοι, σὲ κρίνω, ναὶ σέ, τὴν ἐν τῷ πάρος | χρόνῳ θρασεῖαν</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1226"> <title>Ai.</title> 1226</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ δὴ τὰ δεινὰ ῥήματ’ ἀγγέλλουσί μοι | τλῆναι... | σέ τοι, τὸν ἐκ τῆς αἰχμαλωτίδος λέγω</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 271">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 271</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ τὴν σκυθρωπὸν καὶ πόσει θυμουμένην, | Μήδειαν, εἶπον</quote> </cit>, etc. Antigone has her eyes bent on the ground: she is neither afraid nor sullen, but feels that Creon and she can never come to terms. There is nothing in common between their thoughts. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 499">Cp. 499</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
σὲ τὴν ὄρεγμα δεινὸν ἡμιλλωμένην | τύμβου ’πὶ
									κρηπῖδ’ ἐμπύρους τ’ ὀρθοστάτας, | μεῖνον
ΕΥ. οὗτος, ὦ σέ τοι. ΠΕ. τί βωστρεῖς

σέ τοι, σὲ κρίνω, ναὶ σέ, τὴν ἐν τῷ πάρος |
									χρόνῳ θρασεῖαν

σὲ δὴ τὰ δεινὰ ῥήματ’ ἀγγέλλουσί μοι |
									τλῆναι... | σέ τοι, τὸν ἐκ τῆς αἰχμαλωτίδος λέγω

σὲ τὴν σκυθρωπὸν καὶ πόσει θυμουμένην, |
									Μήδειαν, εἶπον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φὴς_δεδρ_442">., a zeugma. </gloss>
lemma: φὴς δεδρακέναι, ἢ καταρνεῖ μὴ δεδρ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταρνεῖ_442">. In this compound (found only here) <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote> gives the notion of ‘downright,’ ‘explicit’: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">καταφάναι</quote> (to affirm), <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάδηλος. <emph>μή</emph> </quote> regularly precedes the inf. when <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνεῖσθαι</quote> means ‘to deny,’ but not when it means ‘to refuse’: <title>Phaedr.</title> 256 A <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρνηθῆναι τὸ αὑτοῦ μέρος χαρίσασθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶκοὐκ_443">, corresponding with the alternatives in Creon's question : for the conjunctive form, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1192">cp. 1192</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 164">[Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 164</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναί, καὶ δίκαια ταῦτα κοὐκ ἄλλως λέγω.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ναί, καὶ δίκαια ταῦτα κοὐκ ἄλλως λέγω.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μή_443">: for the art., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78">cp. 78</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ οὐκ</quote> is unnecessary, though it would be normal: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1387"> <title>O. T.</title> 1387</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν ἐσχόμην | τὸ μὴ ’ποκλῇσαι</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_μέν_444">. If she had denied the charge, the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> must have been detained; now, he can go. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κομίζοις_ς_444">. gives a contemptuous permission. So in gentle command, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 674"> <title>Ph.</title> 674</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωροῖς ἂν εἴσω</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 624"> <title>Tr.</title> 624</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχοις ἂν ἤδη</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1636">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1636</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόμιζε σαυτὴν...δόμων ἔσω.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξω_444"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="β_αἰτίας_444">, after which </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλεύθερον_444"> is pleonastic: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 464"> <title>Ai.</title> 464</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμνὸν φανέντα τῶν ἀριστείων ἄτερ</quote> </cit>: and see n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">κενῆς</quote> above, v. 424. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῆκος_446">, adv., ‘at great length,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">μακράν, τέλος</quote>, etc. If we read <quote xml:lang="grc">σύντομον</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">σύντομα, μῆκος</quote> might be obj. acc. to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπέ</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">συντόμως</quote> seems right. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 698">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 698</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή τι μακιστῆρα μῦθον ἀλλὰ σύντομον λέγων | εἰπὲ καὶ πέραινε πάντα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
μή τι μακιστῆρα μῦθον ἀλλὰ σύντομον λέγων |
									εἰπὲ καὶ πέραινε πάντα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔδησθα_447">, not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔδης_τὰ_447">, is certainly right. This 2nd pers. occurs in seven places of drama, two of which require it (<ref n="Eur. El. 926">Eur. <title>El.</title> 926</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 108"> <title>Cycl.</title> 108</ref>), while the other five admit it (this v., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 988"> <title>Tr.</title> 988</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 329">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 329</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 554"> <title>Th.</title> 554</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 551"> <title>Eccl.</title> 551</ref>). Similarly <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦσθα</quote> is either necessary or admissible whenever it occurs in Attic drama. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 132">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 132</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφησθα</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφης</quote> nowhere: but the case for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφησθα</quote> as the <emph>sole</emph> classical form seems less strong than for <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔδησθα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦσθα. ἔφησθα</quote> is required in four Homeric passages (<ref n="Hom. Il. 1.397"> <title>Il.</title> 1.397</ref>, 16. 830: <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.357"> <title>Od.</title> 3.357</ref>, 23. 71), but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφης</quote> in one, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.331"> <title>Il.</title> 22.331</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἕκτορ, ἀτάρ που ἔφης Πατροκλῆ’ ἐξεναρίζων</quote> </cit>, and in another it is traditional, 22. 280 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦτοι ἔφης γε</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφησθα</quote> is unlikely). The ending is -</gloss>

data.quote:
Ἕκτορ, ἀτάρ που ἔφης Πατροκλῆ’
									ἐξεναρίζων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σθα_447">, not -<quote xml:lang="grc">θα, ς</quote> being an integral part of it: Curtius compares Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">-sti</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">dedi-sti</foreign>), and Gothic <foreign xml:lang="gothic">-st</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="gothic">saisô-st</foreign>, thou sowedst), <title>Gk Verb</title> pp. 34 ff. Besides <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσθα, ᾔδησθα, ἦσθα</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφησθα</quote>, the forms which take <quote xml:lang="grc">σθα</quote> are the <quote xml:lang="grc">ηομ. εἶσθα</quote> (‘thou wilt go’); the presents indic. <quote xml:lang="grc">διδοῖσθα</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 19.270"> <title>Il.</title> 19.270</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθησθα</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 9.404"> <title>Od.</title> 9.404</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.476">24. 476</ref>); and a few subjunctives and optatives (as <quote xml:lang="grc">βάλῃσθα, βάλοισθα</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηρυχθέντα_447">, the plur. partic. impersonal, as 570 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡρμοσμένα, 576 δεδογμένα</quote>: a use more freq. with adjectives, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδύνατα, δίκαια</quote> (cp. <title>O.C.</title>485n.), esp. verbals (below, 677). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔδη_448">: on the form cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1525"> <title>O. T.</title> 1525</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔμελλον_448">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδέναι</quote>: ‘why was I not likely to know it?’=‘of course I knew it.’ <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 605C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 605C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνδεινόν που</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ οὐ μέλλει</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ γε δρᾷ αὐτό</quote>; <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 4.1.6">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 4.1.6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’ υἱὸν... ἑώρακας αὐτοῦ ὡς καλός ἐστι;—τί δ’ οὐ μέλλω</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑωρακέναι</quote>);</gloss>

data.quote:
τὸν δ’ υἱὸν... ἑώρακας αὐτοῦ ὡς καλός ἐστι;—τί
									δ’ οὐ μέλλω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμφανῆ_448">. I prefer this to L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκφανῆ_448">, not because Soph. does not elsewhere use <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφανής</quote>, but because, in the two places where Aesch. has used it, it has the sense of <emph>emerging into view</emph> ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 398">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 398</ref>, the Greeks going into action at Salamis, or of <emph>standing out</emph> among other objects which are less distinct (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς ἐκφανὲς τέκμαρ</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 244">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 244</ref>). The sense required here is simply, ‘public.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δῆτ_449">, ‘And you <emph>indeed</emph> dared ...?’ Not, ‘And <emph>then</emph>’ (i.e. with that knowledge), which would be <quote xml:lang="grc">κᾆτα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεύς_450"> is opposed to Creon's edicts, not only as supreme god and therefore guardian of all religious duty, but also in each of his two special qualities,—as <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1606"> <title>O. C.</title> 1606</ref> n.),—and as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐράνιος</quote>, since the denial of burial pollutes the realm of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἄνω θεοί</quote> (1072). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_θεῶν_451">. For this rare gen. (instead of the regular dat.) with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύνοικος_451">, cp. Lycurgus <title>In Leocr.</title> § 145 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος ἐν ταύτῃ τῇ χώρᾳ σύνοικος ὑμῶν γενήσεται</quote>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1382"> <title>O. C.</title> 1382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη ξύνεδρος Ζηνός</quote> </cit>. ‘The Justice that dwells with the gods below’ is their personified right to claim from the living those religious observances which devote the dead to them. A person who omits such observances is defrauding Hades of his own: see 1070. This Justice, then, ‘has not ordained such laws’ as Creon's; it has not <emph>forbidden</emph> kinsfolk to bury their dead; on the contrary, it has bound them to do so. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιούσδ_ὥρισεν_451"> is a certainly true correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἳ_ὥρισαν_451">. With the latter, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἳ</quote> are either Zeus and <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote>,—which would be the natural sense,—or <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ κάτω θεοί</quote>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">τούσδε νόμους</quote> are the laws of sepulture. But, after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_451"> in 450, referring to Creon's edicts, the demonstrative pronoun here also should refer to them. Creon has just called his own laws <quote xml:lang="grc">τούσδε νόμους</quote> (449). If Antigone, immediately afterwards, used <quote xml:lang="grc">τούσδε νόμους</quote> to describe the divine laws, the stress on <quote xml:lang="grc">τούσδε</quote> would be extremely awkward. Further, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιούσδ’ ὥρισεν</quote> has a pathetic force which renders it incomparably finer here than the somewhat tame statement of fact, ‘who have appointed the laws of burial among men.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγραπτα_νόμιμα_454">. <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 1.13">Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 1.13</ref> § 2 distinguishes (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδιος νόμος</quote>, the particular law which each community defines for itself, which is partly written, partly (so far as consisting in custom) unwritten: (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς νόμος</quote>, the universal, unwritten law of nature (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κατὰ φύσιν</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι γάρ, ὃ μαντεύονταί τι πάντες, φύσει κοινὸν δίκαιον καὶ ἄδικον, κἂν μηδεμία κοινωνία πρὸς ἀλλήλους ᾖ μηδὲ συνθήκη, οἷον καὶ ἡ Σοφοκλέους Ἀντιγόνη φαίνεται λέγουσα, ὅτι δίκαιον, ἀπειρημένον</quote> (=in spite of the edict), <quote xml:lang="grc">θάψαι τὸν Πολυνείκη, ὡς φύσει ὂν τοῦτο δίκαιον</quote>. (Here he</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμιμα_454">, observances sanctioned by usage, is the more correct word: so <ref n="Plat. Laws 793A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 793A</ref> observes that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ καλούμενα ὑπὸ τῶν πολλῶν ἄγραφα νόμιμα</quote> cannot properly be called <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοι</quote>, but still must be taken into account: <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσμοὶ γὰρ οὗτοι πάσης εἰσὶ πολιτείας, μεταξὺ πάντων ὄντες τῶν ἐν γράμμασι τεθέντων τε καὶ κειμένων καὶ τῶν ἔτι τεθησομένων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσφαλῆ_454">, they stand fast for ever, like the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν ἕδος ἀσφαλὲς αἰεί</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 6.42"> <title>Od.</title> 6.42</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνητὸν_ὄντ_454">, ‘one who is a mortal,’—i.e. Creon; but it is needless to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ σά</quote>: the expression is the more forcible for being general. Cp. Eur. fr. 653 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θαῦμ’ ἔλεξας, θνητὸν ὄντα δυστυχεῖν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 799">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 799</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντας δὲ θνητοὺς θνητὰ καὶ φρονεῖν χρεών</quote>. Bothe's <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητὰ φύνθ’</quote>, rashly adopted by Nauck, is a wanton change, which the ambiguity of the neut. pl. makes still worse.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερδραμεῖν_454">, out-run, and so fig., prevail over: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 578">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 578</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν δ’ αὖ κρατηθῇς καὶ τὰ τοῦδ’ ὑπερδράμῃ</quote> </cit>, and his cause prevail (Canter's certain corr. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεκδράμῃ</quote>): <ref n="Eur. Ion 973"> <title>Ion</title> 973</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πῶς τὰ κρείσσω θνητὸς οὖσ’ ὑπερδράμω</quote>; (prevail against Apollo). Ithas been proposed to refer <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητὸν ὄνθ’</quote> to Antigone: but if she said, ‘I did not think your edicts so strong that I, a mortal, could prevail over divine law,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">δύνασθαι</quote> would rather imply that, if she had been able, she would have been willing to do so. Besides, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερδραμεῖν</quote> is more naturally said of the law-giver who sets his law above the other law. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἢν δ’ αὖ κρατηθῇς καὶ τὰ τοῦδ’
									ὑπερδράμῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_κἀχθές_456">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.53">Her. 2.53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέχρι οὗ πρώην τε καὶ χθές</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 677D">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 677D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔπος εἰπεῖν, χθὲς καὶ πρώην γεγονότα</quote> </cit> (where the phrase is presently strengthened into <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀτεχνῶς χθὲς γενόμενον</quote>). The usu. Attic form was <quote xml:lang="grc">χθὲς καὶ πρώην</quote>, though <quote xml:lang="grc">πρώην καὶ χθές</quote> also occurs. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Catul. 61">Catullus 61. 137</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">hodie atque heri.</quote> </cit> So <quote xml:lang="lat">heri et nudius tertius.</quote> Tryphon, an Alexandrian grammarian of the Augustan age, is quoted by Apollonius <title>De Adverb.</title> p. 556, 32 as saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">χθὲς ἀττικώτερον τοῦ ἐχθές</quote>: but the reverse seems to be the case. Attic Comedy supports <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθές</quote> against <quote xml:lang="grc">χθές</quote> in a majority of cases; though <quote xml:lang="grc">χθές</quote> may have been preferred, even in prose, after a vowel.</gloss>

data.quote: μέχρι οὗ πρώην τε καὶ χθές

ὡς ἔπος εἰπεῖν, χθὲς καὶ πρώην
									γεγονότα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῇ_456">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 482"> <title>O. T.</title> 482</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντα</quote> </cit> (of the oracles which are operative, effectual), and <ref n="Soph. OT 45"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 45</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάνη_456">, with prodelision of the temporal augment in the 6th place, as <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐγὼ ’φάνην</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 974"> <title>O. C.</title> 974</bibl> </cit> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_458"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν νομίμων</quote>)... </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_δίκην_458">, the penalty belonging to these laws: i.e. the penalty of breaking them. The emphasis on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_458_2"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_θεοῖσι_458">, the forensic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, denoting the tribunal: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 916B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 916B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διαδικαζέσθω δὲ ἔν τισι τῶν ἰατρῶν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 464"> <title>Gorg.</title> 464</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέοι ἐν παισὶ διαγωνίζεσθαι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.35">Lys. or. 13 § 35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δὲ δῆμος ἐν τῷ δικαστηρίῳ ἐν δισχιλίοις ἐψηφίσατο</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν τὴν κρίσιν</quote>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 677"> <title>O. T.</title> 677</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν...τοῖσδ</quote> </cit>', n. </gloss>

data.quote: διαδικαζέσθω δὲ ἔν τισι τῶν ἰατρῶν

ὁ δὲ δῆμος ἐν τῷ δικαστηρίῳ ἐν δισχιλίοις
									ἐψηφίσατο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δώσειν_460">. The fut. inf. and the pres. inf. are equally common after <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλω</quote> in Soph. (<ref n="Soph. OT 967"> <title>O. T.</title> 967</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανουμένη_γάρ_460"> introduces the reason for her conduct. ‘It was not likely that I should obey your edicts, and thereby incur punishment after death, for the sake of avoiding immediate death. <title>For</title>, as to death, I knew already that I must die some time or other; and if it is to be a little sooner, so much the better.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_οὔ_460">; <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμελλον ἐξειδέναι</quote> (448). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_προὐκήρυξας_461">: Even if thou hadst not proclaimed death as the penalty of infringing the edict. The apodosis might be either (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξῄδη ἄν</quote>, implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ οὔ</quote>; or (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδει ἄν με θανεῖν</quote>, implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">θανουμένη</quote>. But (<emph>a</emph>) is best: ‘I should have known it, even if you had not brought it publicly to my knowledge.’ For <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ εἰ</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 305"> <title>O. T.</title> 305</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_χρόνου_461">, the natural term of life (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 963"> <title>O. T.</title> 963</ref>): expressed below by <quote xml:lang="grc">πρίν μοι μοῖραν ἐξήκειν βίου</quote> (896). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔτ_461">, i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτό</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1267"> <title>El.</title> 1267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ σε θεὸς ἐπόρισεν | ἁμέτερα πρὸς μέλαθρα, δαιμόνιον | αὐτὸ τίθημ’ ἐγώ</quote> </cit>: fr. 154 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοιμ’ ἂν αὐτὸ μὴ κακῶς ἀπεικάσαι. <emph>αὖτε</emph> </quote> (L) would mean, ‘again,’ ‘on the other hand’ (so far from thinking it a loss). The epic <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖτε</quote> is used by Soph. in one lyric passage (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1010"> <title>Tr.</title> 1010</ref>); by Aesch. both in lyrics and in trimeters; never by The Eur. simpler <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτό</quote> is more probable here. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴ σε θεὸς ἐπόρισεν | ἁμέτερα πρὸς μέλαθρα,
									δαιμόνιον | αὐτὸ τίθημ’ ἐγώ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_464"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">φέρεται</foreign> (<ref n="Soph. OC 6"> <title>O. C.</title> 6</ref> n.).—The woman uses the masc. gender in putting the general case. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1017">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1017</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι μόνη σὺ σῶν ἀπεζύγης τέκνων· | κούφως φέρειν χρὴ θνητὸν ὄντα συμφοράς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτοι μόνη σὺ σῶν ἀπεζύγης τέκνων· | κούφως
									φέρειν χρὴ θνητὸν ὄντα συμφοράς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσδε_ἀλγύνομαι_465"> in 468, returning upon the thought <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ οὐδὲν ἄλγος</quote> in 466. This series of three clauses, in which the second is opposed to the first, and the third re-iterates the sense of the first, is peculiarly Sophoclean: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1111"> <title>Ai.</title> 1111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ τι τῆς σῆς οὕνεκ’ ἐστρατεύσατο | γυναικός,... | ἀλλ’ οὕνεχ’ ὅρκων οἷσιν ἦν ἐνώμοτος, | σοῦ δ’ οὐδέν</quote> </cit>: similar instances are <ref n="Soph. OT 337"> <title>O. T.</title> 337</ref> f., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 431"> <title>Tr.</title> 431</ref> ff. This touch would hardly have come from an interpolator. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ γάρ τι τῆς σῆς οὕνεκ’ ἐστρατεύσατο |
									γυναικός,... | ἀλλ’ οὕνεχ’ ὅρκων οἷσιν ἦν ἐνώμοτος, | σοῦ δ’
									οὐδέν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλγος_466">, nom., <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_οὐδέν_466">,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἄν_466">. For the position of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> (to which objection has been taken) cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 333"> <title>El.</title> 333</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγῶ ’πὶ τοῖς παροῦσιν· ὥστ’ ἄν, εἰ σθένος | λάβοιμι, δηλώσαιμ’ ἂν οἷ’ αὐτοῖς φρονῶ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 439"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 439</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχὴν δ’ ἄν, εἰ μὴ τλημονεστάτη γυνὴ | πασῶν ἔβλαστε, τάσδε δυσμενεῖς χοὰς | οὐκ ἄν ποθ’, ὅν γ’ ἔκτεινε, τῷδ’ ἐπέστεφε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλγῶ ’πὶ τοῖς παροῦσιν· ὥστ’ ἄν, εἰ σθένος |
									λάβοιμι, δηλώσαιμ’ ἂν οἷ’ αὐτοῖς φρονῶ

ἀρχὴν δ’ ἄν, εἰ μὴ τλημονεστάτη γυνὴ | πασῶν
									ἔβλαστε, τάσδε δυσμενεῖς χοὰς | οὐκ ἄν ποθ’, ὅν γ’ ἔκτεινε, τῷδ’
									ἐπέστεφε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθαπτον_νέκυν_467">, had allowed him to be an unburied corpse. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθαπτον</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>, cp. <title>Hist. An.</title> 8. 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">δύναται δ’ ἄποτος ἀνέχεσθαι</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>): and <ref n="Soph. OT 412"> <title>O. T.</title> 412</ref> n. L has </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ηἰσχόμην_467">, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠνσχόμην_467"> appears only as one of several readings in the later MSS.,—the other readings being manifestly impossible. The first question is, Could an Attic poet have used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνσχόμην</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνεσχόμην</quote>? We can only say that we find nothing really like it, and that no support for it can be drawn from the Homeric forms in which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά</quote> suffers apocope, viz., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνσχεο = ἀνασχοῦ</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 23.587"> <title>Il.</title> 23.587</ref> etc.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνσχήσεσθαι</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 5.104"> <title>Il.</title> 5.104</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνσχετά</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 2.63"> <title>Od.</title> 2.63</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνσχεθέειν</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Od. 5.320"> <title>Od.</title> 5.320</ref>). Still, there is force in Prof. Tyrrell's remark (<title>Classical Review</title>, vol. 11. p. 140) that ‘<quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνσχόμην</quote> is just the form in which an Attic poet would have applied apocope of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά</quote>, inasmuch as he would have felt that he was only sacrificing the redundant augment.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾔσχυναν_κύνες_467">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.74"> <title>Il.</title> 22.74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὅτε δὴ πολιόν τε κάρη πολιόν τε γένειον | αἰδῶ τ’ αἰσχύνωσι κύνες κταμένοιο γέροντος</quote> </cit>. If the <quote xml:lang="grc">ες</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">κύνες</quote> had been obliterated, <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυν</quote> would easily have arisen (esp. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ν</quote>); and a change of <quote xml:lang="grc">υ</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">ο</quote> would have taken <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔσχυναν</quote> far towards <quote xml:lang="grc">ηἰσχόμην</quote>. But, while I still hold that this brilliant conjecture has no small degree of probability, I also recognise the justice of the criticism that the context here decidedly favours a verb in the first person.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ὅτε δὴ πολιόν τε κάρη πολιόν τε γένειον |
									αἰδῶ τ’ αἰσχύνωσι κύνες κταμένοιο γέροντος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_μητρός_467">. This is like saying, ‘the son of the same womb.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 497">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 497</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερον ἀδελφὼ μητρός ἐστον ἐκ μιᾶς</quote> </cit>; Yet it has been seriously urged by many critics, as a ground for change, that a mention of the <emph>father</emph> was indispensable. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῆς</quote> need not be altered to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμῆς</quote> (Seyffert)</gloss>

data.quote: πότερον ἀδελφὼ μητρός ἐστον ἐκ μιᾶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_θανόντ_467">. It is quite true that, when written, these words have an awkward ambiguity; but they would have had none when spoken, since a slight pause after <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρός</quote> would have been required to bring out <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντ’</quote>. This is the right test to apply in the case of a play written to be acted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχεδόν_τι_470">, ‘almost,’ iron., ‘it might perhaps be said that...’: so <ref n="Soph. El. 608"> <title>El.</title> 608</ref> (also at the close of a defiant speech), <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γὰρ πέφυκα τῶνδε τῶν ἔργων ἴδρις, | σχεδόν τι τὴν σὴν οὐ καταισχύνω φύσιν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 550"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 550</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ σοὶ δοκῶ φρονεῖν κακῶς | γνώμην δικαίαν σχοῦσα, τοὺς πέλας ψέγε</quote> </cit> (end of a speech): also <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1038"> <title>Ai.</title> 1038</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1665"> <title>O. C.</title> 1665</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ δὲ σοὶ δοκῶ φρονεῖν κακῶς | γνώμην δικαίαν
									σχοῦσα, τοὺς πέλας ψέγε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μώρῳ_μωρίαν_470">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 754">cp. 754</ref>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote> gives bitterness (<title>O.T.</title> 371).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀφλισκάνω_470"> with dat., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 854">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 854</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γέλωτα θηβαίοις ὀφλεῖν</quote> </cit>, etc.; but in this use it can also take <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός τινα</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά τινι</quote> (Plato). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 511"> <title>O. T.</title> 511</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_παιδός_471"> (the offspring consisting in the maiden) the maiden his offspring, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηλοῖ_471"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὂν</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠμόν_471">, shows herself fierce, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_πατρός_471">, from a fierce sire (i.e. by the disposition inherited from him). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 20">Cp. 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλοῖς...καλχαίνουσα</quote> (n.): the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote> is somewhat bold, but possible for poetry; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 709">cp. 709</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤφθησαν κενοί</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 896B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 896B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδεικται ψυχὴ τῶν πάντων πρεσβυτάτη</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέννημα_471"> occurs below, 628, <ref n="Soph. OT 1167"> <title>O. T.</title> 1167</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 315"> <title>Tr.</title> 315</ref>, meaning always ‘that which is begotten,’ the offspring. So in Plato the word always means the thing produced; for in <title>Sophist.</title> 266 D, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ὁμοιωμάτων τινῶν γέννημα</quote>, where Ast takes it as=‘<foreign xml:lang="lat">confectio</foreign>,’ the sense is, ‘the other a product (consisting in) certain images.’ In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 850">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 850</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπώνυμον δὲ τῶν Διὸς γεννημάτων | τέξεις κελαινὸν Ἔπαφον</quote> </cit>, the word, if genuine, would certainly mean ‘begetting’; but Wieseler's correction, <quote xml:lang="grc">γέννημ’ ἁφῶν</quote> (‘an offspring called after the touch of Zeus’), is highly probable. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γέννημα τῆς παιδός</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γεννηθεῖσα παῖς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1164">cp. 1164</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων σπορᾷ</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1233"> <title>El.</title> 1233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γοναὶ σωμάτων ἐμοὶ φιλτάτων</quote> </cit> (her brother), <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1098">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1098</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων...βλάστημα</quote> </cit>. Here, the thought would have been complete without <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς παιδός</quote> (‘the offspring shows the father's fierceness’), which is added, as if by an after-thought, for the further definition of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γέννημα</quote>. I cannot believe that Soph. intended <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γέννημα τῆς παιδός</quote> to mean, ‘the inborn disposition of the maiden,’—an unexampled sense for <quote xml:lang="grc">γέννημα</quote>. On the other hand, all the emendations are unsatisfactory and improbable. The language, though somewhat peculiar, appears to be sound. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπώνυμον δὲ τῶν Διὸς γεννημάτων | τέξεις
									κελαινὸν Ἔπαφον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴκειν_κακοῖς_472">, not ‘to succumb’ to them, but to bend before them (as trees before a storm, 713), with a prudent view to self-preservation. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 320">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 320</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ οὐδέπω ταπεινός, οὐδ’ εἴκεις κακοῖς, | πρὸς τοῖς παροῦσι δ’ ἄλλα προσλ βεῖν θέλεις</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
σὺ δ’ οὐδέπω ταπεινός, οὐδ’ εἴκεις κακοῖς, |
									πρὸς τοῖς παροῦσι δ’ ἄλλα προσλ βεῖν θέλεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τοι_473">: so oft. in threat enings: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1064">cp. 1064</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1107"> <title>Tr.</title> 1107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὖ<*> τοι τόδ’ ἴστε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 298"> <title>El.</title> 298</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἴσθι τοι τίσου<*> γ’ ἀξίαν δίκην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἴσθι τοι τίσου<*> γ’ ἀξίαν
									δίκην.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίπτειν_473">, instead of the regular <quote xml:lang="grc">πίπτοντα</quote>: this inf. after <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα</quote> (as= ‘I know that...’, not, ‘I know how to...’) is not rare in poetry; cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 691"> <title>O. T.</title> 691</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1329"> <title>Ph.</title> 1329</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 173">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 173</ref>, 431, 435: so after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσταμαι</quote> above, 293, and 1092. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀπτὸν_περισκελῆ_475">, tempered to hardness: for the proleptic adj., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.75">Thuc. 2.75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔρετο τὸ ὕψος...μέγα</quote> </cit>, <ref n="Eur. El. 376">Eur. <title>El.</title> 376</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πενία</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάσκει δ’ ἄνδρα τῇ χρείᾳ κακόν</quote> (to be bad). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περισκελής_475">, dried or parched all round, from <quote xml:lang="grc">σκέλλω</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">torrere:</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.190"> <title>Il.</title> 23.190</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ πρὶν μένος ἠελίοιο | σκήλεῑ ἀμφὶ περὶ χρόα ἴνεσιν</quote> </cit>: hence, fig., <quote xml:lang="grc">αί περισκελεῖς φρένες</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 649"> <title>Ai.</title> 649</ref>): cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">retorridus.</foreign> From the same rt come <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληρός, σκελετός</quote> (skeleton), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσκελής</quote> (dried),—this last having a fig. sense in the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσκελὲς αἰεί</quote> (‘stubbornly,’ <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.68"> <title>Od.</title> 1.68</ref>).</gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ πρὶν μένος ἠελίοιο | σκήλεῑ ἀμφὶ περὶ χρόα
									ἴνεσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_πυρός_475">, <emph>by means of</emph> fire; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 990">cp. 990</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 710"> <title>Ph.</title> 710</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὠκυβόλων τόξων...ἀνύσειε...φορβάν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θραυσθέντα_ῥαγέντα_476">, ‘broken and shivered.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαγέντα</quote> is here the stronger word, in so far as it pictures the fragments of the ruptured iron flying asunder, while <quote xml:lang="grc">θραυσθέντα</quote> merely says that the iron is broken into pieces. As Heinrich Schmidt observes, the foremost idea in <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥηγνύναι</quote> is that of the separation of the parts,—the <emph>rent</emph> or <emph>rift</emph> being brought before us; in <quote xml:lang="grc">θραύειν</quote>, that of a whole being broken into small pieces (<title>Synonymik der Gr. Sprache</title>, vol. 111. pp. 304 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλινῷ_δ_477">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 714"> <title>O. C.</title> 714</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποισιν τὸν ἀκεστῆρα χαλινόν</quote> </cit>, n. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικρῷ_477"> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1253"> <title>Ai.</title> 1253</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγας δὲ πλευρὰ βοῦς ὑπὸ σμικρᾶς ὅμως | μάστιγος ὀρθὸς εἰς ὁδὸν πορεύεται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μέγας δὲ πλευρὰ βοῦς ὑπὸ σμικρᾶς ὅμως |
									μάστιγος ὀρθὸς εἰς ὁδὸν πορεύεται.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταρτυθέντας_477">, brought under discipline, made docile. <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρτύω</quote> =to equip, or prepare (<ref n="Soph. OC 71"> <title>O. C.</title> 71</ref>): then, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμόζω</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 908"> <title>O. C.</title> 908</ref>), in a fig. sense, to bring into order, regulate, by a course of training: cp. <ref n="Plat. Laws 808D">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 808D</ref> (a child is the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑβριστότατον θηρίων</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσῳ μάλιστα ἔχει πηγὴν τοῦ φρονεῖν μήπω κατηρτυμένην</quote> (not yet brought under discipline): <ref n="Plut. Mor. 38">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 38</ref>C (the sensuous impulses, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ἐφ’ ἡδονὴν ὁρμαί</quote>, are disastrous) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν ἐᾷ τις ἀφέτους, ᾗ πεφύκασι, χωρεῖν, καὶ μὴ...καταρτύῃ τὴν φύσιν</quote> (discipline the character). <ref n="Plut. Them. 2">Plut. <title>Them.</title> 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς τραχυτάτους πώλους ἀρίστους ἵππους γίνεσθαι φάσκων, ὅταν ἧς προσἡκει τύχωσι παιδείας καὶ καταρτύσεως</quote> (education and discipline). In <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 473">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 473</ref> the act. perf. part. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατηρτυκὼς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέτης</quote>) is said to be a term applied to a horse whose mouth was ‘fully furnished’ with teeth (i.e. which had shed its foal's teeth), and hence, ‘broken in,’ ‘tamed’: at any rate, it must be kept distinct from the passive <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρτυθείς</quote> as used here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπέλει_478">, which occurs only here: Hesych. explains it by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεστι</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέπελεν</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεγένετο</quote>. He would hardly have invented the imperf., if he had not met with it in literature: and the metrical convenience of such a synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεστι</quote> is a further reason for believing that it was current. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_478">: for the omission of the anteced. <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ</quote>, <ref target="cl35">see on 35</ref>: for the gender, on 464.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_πέλας_478">: cp. fr. 83 ('tis better to conquer by any means) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ δοῦλον αὐτὸν ὄντα τῶν πέλας κλύειν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὕτη_δ_480">. Creon began by addressing Antigone (473). He now denounces her to the Chorus. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1078"> <title>O. T.</title> 1078</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη δ’ ἴσως, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit> (of Iocasta).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑβρίζειν_δ_480">: epanaphora (<ref n="Soph. OT 25"> <title>O. T.</title> 25</ref> n.). The sense is, ‘Her <emph>disobedience</emph> was an act of consummate insolence; and her <emph>defiance</emph> now makes it worse.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξηπίστατο_480">, ‘knew thoroughly,’ with bitterness; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 686">cp. 686</ref>; Eur. fr. 796 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις σωφρονεῖν ἐπίσταται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότ_480"> is explained by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑ<emph>περβαίνουσα.—τοὺς προκ</emph> </quote>., which had been set forth: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 865"> <title>O. T.</title> 865</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1189">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1189</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν νόμον...τὸν προκείμενον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτοις_480">, neut., these deeds: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 468">cp. 468</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνοις ...τοῖσδε.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεδρακυῖαν_γελᾶν_480">=to exult <emph>in</emph> having done it. For the partic., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Wasps 1007">Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 1007</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐκ ἐγχανεῖταί σ’ ἐξαπατῶν Ὑπέρβολος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_484">, ‘now,’ i.e. ‘under these circumstances,’ is better than <quote xml:lang="grc">νυν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄρ’</quote> here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_κράτη_484">, if this victory shall remain on record for her, without bringing her any punishment. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτη_484">, deeds of might, and so prevalence, victory, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 476"> <title>El.</title> 476</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκα, δίκαια φερομένα χεροῖν κράτη</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 689"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 689</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδα τοιοῦδ’ ἀνδρὸς ἔργα καὶ κράτη</quote> </cit>. For </gloss>

data.quote: οὐκ οἶδα τοιοῦδ’ ἀνδρὸς ἔργα καὶ κράτη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείσεται_484">, cp. <ref n="Pind. I. 4">Pind. <title>I.</title> 4. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὶν δ’ ἐν Ἰσθμῷ διπλόα θάλλοισ’ ἀρετά, | Φυλακίδα, κεῖται</quote>, ‘for thee, Phylacidas, a double glory of valour is <emph>laid up</emph> at the Isthmus.’ So, here, <quote xml:lang="grc">κείσεται</quote> means, ‘placed to her credit,’ ‘permanently secured to her’; cp. the colloquialism, ‘to <emph>score</emph> a success.’ Other interpretations are:—(1) ‘If this royal power of mine shall have been instituted without penalty for her.’ For the word <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτη</quote>, this sense is tenable (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 60">cp. 60</ref>, 166, 173, <ref n="Soph. OT 237"> <title>O. T.</title> 237</ref>): it is the whole phrase that appears strained. And <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</quote> </emph> (said with bitter emphasis) evidently refers to Antigone's acts; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 483">cp. 483</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις</quote>. Semitelos reads </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείσεται_484">: ‘If this sovereignty of mine’ (here Creon lifts his sceptre) ‘shall yield to her without punishing her.’ The verb would, however, be strange, and somewhat weak. (2) ‘If these edicts shall have been set forth without penalty for her.’ This last gives an impossible sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτη</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1126">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 1126</ref> ff. illustrates the poetical ambiguity of <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτη</quote>, the debate there being whether, by <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷα κράτη</quote>, Aesch. meant, ‘a victory over a father,’ or ‘power derived from a father.’—For the form of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνατί_484">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1251"> <title>O. C.</title> 1251</ref> n.: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_484"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">τά</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 471"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 471.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφῆς_486">, (child of) a sister, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφιδῆ</quote>: for the gen., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 380">cp. 380</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 825">825.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴθ_ὁμαιμονεστέρα_486">, ‘or nearer in blood to me than any member of my family.’ The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμαιμονεστέρας</quote> (see cr. n.) would mean, ‘or (child of) one nearer in blood to me,’ etc. She could be the child of no one nearer than a sister, unless it were of a mother or of a daughter; and it is far-fetched to suppose that Creon means, ‘my niece,—aye, my sister, or my granddaughter.’ All that he means is, ‘my niece,—aye, or the nearest relation possible.’ This is more simply and clearly said by the nom. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμαιμονεστέρα</quote>. If the comparative were here restricted to the regular Sophoclean sense of the positive <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμαιμος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμαίμων</quote>, as meaning brother or sister (see on <ref n="Soph. OC 330"> <title>O. C.</title> 330</ref>), then the gen. could be explained in another way, viz., as a rhetorical hyperbole: ‘sister's child, or child of one who was thrice my sister,’ —like Plato <ref n="Plat. Lysis 210"> <title>Lysis</title> 210</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πατὴρ καὶ ἡ μήτηρ καὶ εἴ τι τούτων οἰκειότερόν ἐστι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυρεῖ_486"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσα</quote>), =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 362"> <title>O. T.</title> 362</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονέα...κυρεῖν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_ἑρκείου_487"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">πάντων τῶν οἰκείων</foreign> (schol.): so Eustath. 1930, 30 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκεῖον Δία ἐκεῖνος</quote> (Sophocles) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐν οἴκῳ πάντας δηλοῖ</quote>.
