The altar of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς ἑρκεῖος</quote> stood in the court-yard (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>) in front of the Greek house; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρκος</quote> denoting the buildings which enclose the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>, or, sometimes, the space so enclosed, the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote> itself. In <ref n="Hom. Od. 22.334"> <title>Od.</title> 22.334</ref> Phemius thinks of passing from the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαρον</quote> into the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή, Διὸς μεγάλου ποτὶ βωμὸν | Ἑρκείου</quote>. (Cp. my <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 58.) This is the altar at which Peleus was sacrificing, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλῆς ἐν χόρτῳ</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 11.774"> <title>Il.</title> 11.774</ref> : cp. Athen. 5, p. 189 F): as in <ref n="Plat. Rep. 328C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 328C</ref> there is sacrifice in the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>. So in <ref n="Hdt. 6.68">Her. 6.68</ref> Demaratus supplicates his mother especially by <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ Ἑρκείου Διὸς τοῦδε</quote> (whose altar or image he is touching, <quote xml:lang="grc">καταπτόμενος</quote>). Priamisslain <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς...κρηπίδων βάθροις...Ζηνὸς ἑρκείου</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Tro. 16">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 16</ref>),—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τῇ ἐσχάρᾳ, τοῦ Ἑρκείου</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 4.17.4">Paus. 4.17.4</ref>): cp. Ovid <title>Ibis</title> 286, <title>Cui nihil Hercei profuit ara Iovis.</title> In Cratinus jun., <quote xml:lang="grc">Χείρων</quote> 1 ff. (c. 350 B.C.), a returned exile says, <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυγγενεῖς | καὶ φράτορας καὶ δημότας εὑρὼν μόλις | εἰς τὸ κυλικεῖον ἐνεγράφην</quote> (put on the feasting-list—<quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ προσδοκίαν</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὸ γραμματεῖον</quote>): <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς ἔστιμοι | ἑρκεῖος, ἔστι φράτριος</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκεῖος</quote> corresponds with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυγγενεῖς</quote>. Dionysius 1. 67 expresses the attributes of the Roman Penates by the words <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷοι, γενέθλιοι, κτήσιοι, μύχιοι, ἐρκεῖοι</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκίους</quote> in his text should be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκείους</quote>: so L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκίου</quote> here).—In relation to the family, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote> is also <quote xml:lang="grc">γενέθλιος</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 16</ref>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύναιμος</quote>, <ref n="Pind. O. 659">Pind. O. 659</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμόγνιος</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφέστιος</quote> (as presiding over household life: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 492"> <title>Ai.</title> 492</ref>, <ref n="Hdt. 1.44">Her. 1.44</ref>).—For the god's name used to denote that which he protects, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 345">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 345</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφευγας τὸν ἐμὸν ἱκέσιον Δία</quote> </cit>, = my supplication, with its consequences. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλύξετον_488">. The pres. ind. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύσκω</quote> occurs in Apollon. Rhod., and the pres. part. in Hom. <title>Od.</title>; otherwise we find only the fut. (usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύξω</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύξομαι</quote>, <ref n="Hes. WD 363">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 363</ref>) and aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤλυξα</quote> (once midd., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαλύξωμαι</quote> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 656"> <title>Ai.</title> 656</ref>). The gen. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόρου_488"> follows the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολύεσθαι, ἀπαλλάττεσθαι</quote>, etc. The regular constr. of the verb is with the acc. (as in the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρας ἀλύξας</quote>), and so Eur. always has an acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξήλυξα</quote>. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 627"> <title>El.</title> 627</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θράσους | τοῦδ’ οὐκ ἀλύξεις</quote> </cit>, the gen. is not like this, but causal (‘<emph>for</emph> this boldness’). Oppian's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξήλυξε μόροιο</quote> ( <ref n="Opp. H. 3. 104"> <title>Hal.</title> 3. 104</ref>) was obviously suggested by our verse. So in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1044"> <title>Ph.</title> 1044</ref> Soph. has ventured on <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς νόσου πεφευγέναι</quote>, thinking, doubtless, of the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">πεφυγμένος ἦεν ἀέθλων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_οὖν_489">, for <emph>indeed</emph>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 771">cp. 771</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ γὰρ οὖν</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 980"> <title>O. C.</title> 980</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ οὖν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 985"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 985</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἓν γὰρ οὖν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαιτιῶμαι_κείνην_489">, I accuse her, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεῦσαι_τάφου_489">, of having had an equal share in plotting this burial. For the inf. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαιτιᾶσθαι</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 603"> <title>El.</title> 603</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν πολλὰ δή με σοὶ τρέφειν μιάστορα | ἐπῃτιάσω</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Wasps 1446">Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 1446</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴσωπον οἱ Δελφοί ποτε...φιάλην ἐπῃτιῶντο κλέψαι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Criti. 120C">Plat. <title>Critias</title> 120C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τίς τι παραβαίνειν αὐτῶν αἰτιῷτό τινα</quote> </cit>. For the substantival </gloss>

data.quote:
ὃν πολλὰ δή με σοὶ τρέφειν μιάστορα |
									ἐπῃτιάσω

Αἴσωπον οἱ Δελφοί ποτε...φιάλην ἐπῃτιῶντο
									κλέψαι

εἴ τίς τι παραβαίνειν αὐτῶν αἰτιῷτό
									τινα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσον_489"> with gen., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 818">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 818</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῖος εἶναι τῆς τύχης τ’ ἴσον φέρειν</quote> </cit>: id. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 547"> <title>Ph.</title> 547</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δωμάτων ἔχων ἴσον</quote>.—Another construction is possible: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον ἐπαιτιῶμαι κείνην τοῦδε τοῦ τάφου, βουλεῦσαι</quote>: ‘I charge her equally with this burial,—i.e. with plotting it.’ The constr. with the gen. is frequent (<cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 974">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 974</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὴ= κἀμὲ γάρ τι ξυμφορᾶς ἐπαιτιᾷ</quote> </cit>;): the objection here is that the epexegetic infin. <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεῦσαι</quote>, requiring a case different from that governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαιτιῶμαι</quote>, ought to come last: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 52B">Plat. <title>Crito</title> 52B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὲπιθυμία σε ἄλλης πόλεως οὐδ’ ἄλλων νόμων ἔλαβεν εἰδέναι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1399">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1399</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῄζω στόματος | παίδων ὁ τάλας προσπτύξασθαι</quote> </cit>. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 644"> <title>O. T.</title> 644</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ σέ τι | δέδρακ’, ὀλοίμην, ὧν ἐπαιτιᾷ με δρᾶν, ὧν</quote> </cit> might represent either (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ὧν, δρᾶν</quote> being epexegetic, or (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἅ</quote> (acc. governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν</quote>); the latter is simplest.</gloss>

data.quote: ὁμοῖος εἶναι τῆς τύχης τ’ ἴσον φέρειν
ὴ= κἀμὲ γάρ τι ξυμφορᾶς ἐπαιτιᾷ

οὐδ’ ὲπιθυμία σε ἄλλης πόλεως οὐδ’ ἄλλων νόμων
									ἔλαβεν εἰδέναι

χρῄζω στόματος | παίδων ὁ τάλας
									προσπτύξασθαι

εἴ σέ τι | δέδρακ’, ὀλοίμην, ὧν ἐπαιτιᾷ με
									δρᾶν, ὧν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεῦσαι_489">, of plotting mischief, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 807"> <title>Tr.</title> 807</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτα...πατρὶ βουλεύσασ’ ἐμῷ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1055"> <title>Ai.</title> 1055</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατῷ...βουλεύσας φόνον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_καλεῖτ_491">, ‘And now call her.’ This is the <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> so frequent in the orators, when the speaker turns to call for witnesses, documents, etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 16.13">Lys. or. 16 § 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί μοι ἀνάβηθι</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.35">or. 13 § 35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί μοι ἀνάγνωθι τὸ ψήφισμα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυσσῶσαν_ἐπ_492">.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 58"> <title>O. T.</title> 58</ref> n.: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.55">Her. 9.55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαινόμενον καὶ οὐ φρενήρεα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπήβολον_492">, ‘in possession of’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">compos</foreign>), a word used by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 444">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρενῶν</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 542">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 542</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσου</quote>), but not by Eur. Itbelonged to the diction of Ionian epos, appearing first in <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.319"> <title>Od.</title> 2.319</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ νηὸς ἐπήβολος οὐδ’ ἐρετάων</quote>), and is used by Herod. ; Plato, too, admits it (as <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 289"> <title>Euthyd.</title> 289</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμης ἐπηβόλους</quote>). Düntzer would derive it from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄβολος</quote> (‘meeting with’), assumed from the late verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβολέω</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιβολέω</quote> ( Rhod. <ref n="Plat. Apol. 3. 1148"> Ap. 3. 1148</ref>); but it is more likely that it was simply an epic metrical license for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίβολος</quote>. The sense seems to come from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβάλλομαι</quote> as=‘to throw oneself on’ a thing, and so take possession of it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_τεχνωμένων_493">, the mind of those who are planning nothing aright (planning utter mischief) in the dark, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φιλεῖ_ᾑρῆσθαι_493">, is apt to stand convicted of its treason, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσθεν_493">, beforehand,—i.e. before the treasonable deed has been done. For the order of words (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ θυμός</quote> being divided from the attributive gen. by the predicate), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.8">Thuc. 2.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δὲ εὔνοια παρὰ πολὺ ἐποίει τῶν ἀνθρώπων μᾶλλον ἐς τοὺς Λακεδαιμονίους</quote> </cit>. <title>Ismene</title> has not yet been caught in a disloyal act; but her guilty conscience has already shown itself. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡ δὲ εὔνοια παρὰ πολὺ ἐποίει τῶν ἀνθρώπων
									μᾶλλον ἐς τοὺς Λακεδαιμονίους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλοπεύς_493"> here answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτειν</quote> as=‘to do by stealth or fraud,’ <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1137"> <title>Ai.</title> 1137</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ ἂν κακῶς λάθρᾳ σὺ κλέψειας κακά</quote> </cit>. It denotes the plotter's treachery towards the State, not the betrayal of the plotter by his own conscience (as some have taken it). </gloss>

data.quote: πόλλ’ ἂν κακῶς λάθρᾳ σὺ κλέψειας κακά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾑρῆσθαι_κλοπεύς_493"> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤν</quote>), as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 576"> <title>O. T.</title> 576</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονεὺς ἁλώσομαι</quote> </cit>: the perf. (expressing that the exposure is already decisive), like <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὑρῆσθαι</quote> <ref n="Soph. OT 1050"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1050</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεχνωμένων_493"> midd., as usual; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 80"> <title>Ph.</title> 80</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχνᾶσθαι κακά</quote>. This is better than to make it pass., as a gen. absol., (‘when utter mischief is being contrived,’) a constr. which seems to require the change of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶς</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶν</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μηδὲν ὀρθῶς</quote> could hardly mean, ‘things which (are) in no wise well,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντα. οἱ μηδὲν ὀρθῶς τεχνώμενοι</quote>=those who plan nothing in such a way as to be right (the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 407"> <title>Ph.</title> 407</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παν<*>ς ἂν λόγου κακοῦ | γλώσσῃ θιγόντα καὶ πανουργίας, ἀφ’ ἧς | μηδὲν δίκαιον ἐς τέλος μέλλοι ποιεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_μέντοι_495">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 442"> <title>O. T.</title> 442</ref> n. ‘(I hate such plotting in the dark:) however, I certainly hate this also,—when a detected traitor seeks to glorify the treason.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μισῶ_ὅταν_495">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμνησαι ὅτε...ἐποίησας</quote> ( <ref n="Xen. Ec. 2. 11">Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 2. 11</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὶ=δ’ ἡνίκ’ Αἴας εἷλκε</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Tro. 70">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 70</ref>) etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπειτα_495">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτα</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 760"> <title>Ai.</title> 760</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις ἀνθρώπου φύσιν | βλαστὼν ἔπειτα μὴ κατ’ ἄνθρωπον φρονῇ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλλύνειν_495">, to make <quote xml:lang="grc">καλός</quote> (fr. 786 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσωπα καλλύνουσα</quote>, of the waxing moon), here, to make specious: so <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 944B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 944B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐδιάβολον κακὸν καλλύνων</quote> </cit> (a soldier excusing himself for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλων ἀποβολή</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλεις_μεῖζον_497">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν</quote>,— not that <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλω</quote> could not take a simple acc., but a Greek would mentally supply a general inf. to balance <quote xml:lang="grc">κατακτεῖναι</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.85">Thuc. 3.85</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόγνοια...τοῦ ἄλλο τι</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ κρατεῖν τῆς γῆς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑλών_497">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 641"> <title>O. T.</title> 641</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτεῖναι λαβών.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_οὐδέν_497">. He desires nothing more,—and will take nothing less.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπαντ_ἔχω_497">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 749">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 749</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο πάντ’ ἔχω μαθών</quote> </cit> (‘tell me that, and I am satisfied’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρεστὸν_οὐδέν_500">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεστὸς οὐδείς</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1195"> <title>O. T.</title> 1195</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν | οὐδέν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_ἀρεσθείη_500">. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 686">Cp. 686</ref>. If sound (as it seems to be), this is a solitary example of the aor. pass. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρέσθην</quote> as=‘became pleasing,’ and must be defended by the pass. (or midd.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρέσκομαι</quote> as used by , <ref n="Hdt. 6.128">Herod. 6. 128</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρέσκοντο</quote>, ‘they were approved’ (or, ‘they pleased’): 9. 79 <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε Αἰγινήτῃσι ἅδοιμι μήτε τοῖσι ταῦτα ἀρέσκεται</quote> (those who approve this course). Considering the Ionic affinities of Attic Tragedy, this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρέσκομαι</quote> in Ionic prose seems a sufficient warrant for a corresponding use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρέσθην</quote>, whether we take it as properly passive (‘was approved’), or as a pass. form used to supplement the middle (‘pleased’). I do not add Eur. fr. 942 <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς ἀρέσκου</quote>, because there I should read <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς ἀρέσκου</quote>, ‘propitiate the gods,’ the Attic use of the midd.; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 4.3.16">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 4.3.16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος δὲ δήπου πανταχοῦ ἐστι κατὰ δύναμιν ἱεροῖς θεοὺς ἀρέσκεσθαι</quote> </cit>, ‘to propitiate the gods with sacrifice. The Attic passive meant ‘I am pleased,’ <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.129">Thuc. 1.129</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς λόγοις τοῖς ἀπὸ σοῦ ἀρέσκομαι</quote> </cit>, 5. 37 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ βοιωτάρχαι ἠρέσκοντο</quote>, but occurs only in pres. and impf.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρέσθην</quote>, as the corresponding aor., appears only in later Greek, as <cit> <bibl n="Paus. 2.13.8">Paus. 2.13.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀρεσθεὶς τῷ δοθέντι πώματι</quote> </cit>. The traditional <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεσθείη</quote>, then, is at least not less probable than Hermann's </gloss>

data.quote:
νόμος δὲ δήπου πανταχοῦ ἐστι κατὰ δύναμιν
									ἱεροῖς θεοὺς ἀρέσκεσθαι
τοῖς λόγοις τοῖς ἀπὸ σοῦ ἀρέσκομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρεσθείην_500">, when the whole question is viewed in the light of attested usage. As to Elmsley's neat </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρέστ_εἴη_500">, a fatal objection to it is the change to the impers. plur.; as if one said, ‘not one of your words pleases me; and never may I feel pleasure:’ (without, ‘in them’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀμά_501">, a general phrase, ‘my views.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1050"> <title>El.</title> 1050</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε γὰρ σὺ τἄμ’ ἔπη | τολμᾷς ἐπαινεῖν οὔτ’ ἐγὼ τοὺς σοὺς τρόπους.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε γὰρ σὺ τἄμ’ ἔπη | τολμᾷς ἐπαινεῖν οὔτ’
									ἐγὼ τοὺς σοὺς τρόπους.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔφυ_501"> with partic. (‘are naturally ...’), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 9"> <title>O. T.</title> 9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέπων ἔφυς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_502"> marks the transition to another and higher point of view than Creon's. ‘<emph>Thou</emph> wilt never approve my deed. <emph>And yet</emph> how could I have won a better claim to the approval of all who judge rightly?’ In <quote xml:lang="grc">καίτοι πόθεν κλέος γ’</quote> the absence of caesura gives a slower movement, just as in v. 44: she communes with her own thought. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλέος_εὐκλεέστερον_502">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσπνοοι πνοαί</quote> (587), <quote xml:lang="grc">φρένες δύσφρονες</quote> (1261), <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνοι δύσπονοι</quote> (1277): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 894"> <title>Ph.</title> 894</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύνηθες...ἔθος</quote>. Distinguish the case of the adj. compounded with a noun merely cognate in sense to the subst.; above, <ref target="cl7">v. 7 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τιθεῖσα_503">, i.e. symbolically, by sprinkling dust and pouring <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 80">cp. 80</ref>, 395, and <ref n="Soph. OC 1410"> <title>O. C.</title> 1410</ref> n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτοις_ἄν_503">, by all these it would be said, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_ἁνδάνειν_503"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖς</quote>), that this seems good to them: for dat. with pres. pass. (a constr. usually restricted to the pf. pass.) cp. Menander <title>Sentent.</title> 511 <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀληθὲς ἀνθρώποισιν οὐχ εὑρίσκεται</quote>. If the datives were taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνδάνειν, λέγοιτο ἄν</quote> would be too indefinite. At the same time the proximity of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁνδάνειν</quote> has influenced the construction. <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτὸ</quote> has been suggested, but there is nothing suspicious in <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοις τοῦτο</quote>, which Nauck groundlessly condemns.—The pres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκλῄοι_503"> is required by the sense, since the act is a continuing one; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκλῄσαι</quote> would refer to some given moment or particular occasion. The MSS. favour the aor., but most of the recent edd. rightly give the pres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τυραννίς_506">. ‘(If these men dared to say what they think, they would applaud me.) <title>But</title> royalty has the advantage of being able to do and say what it pleases, without being opposed in word or deed’:—and so these men are silent. These are two excellent and vigorous lines,—not only free from the slightest internal mark of spuriousness, but admirably suited to their place, both by thought and by expression. It was an extraordinary freak of arbitrary criticism to reject them. The reasons assigned for doing so deserve mention only for their curious weakness; as (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡ</quote> ought to be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γάρ</quote>—Dindorf: (<emph>b</emph>) Antigone should not mention the <emph>advantages</emph> of the <quote xml:lang="grc">τυραννίς</quote>— JacobA. : (<emph>c</emph>) Creon could not be reproached with <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν λέγειν θ’ ἂ βούλεται</quote>—Nauck: of which last objection Bellermann, in his simple and triumphant vindication of these verses, justly says that it is ‘wholly unintelligible.’ (<emph>d</emph>) Wecklein, too, has effectively defended them. We may add that Creon's reply in v. 508, which refers primarily to vv. 504 f., does not therefore ignore vv. 506 f., since these two vv. cohere closely with the former: vv. 504—507 express a single thought. For similar references in tragedy to the <quote xml:lang="grc">τυραννίς</quote>, as it was viewed by Greeks in the historical age, cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 224">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 224</ref> f., <ref n="Eur. Ion 621">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 621</ref>—632. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μούνη_508">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδε</quote> refers to the Chorus: for the Ionic form, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 308">cp. 308</ref>, 705.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_ὁρᾷς_508">, seest this,=‘takest this view’; viz., that thy deed is right, and that only my power prevents its being publicly approved. A very rare use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾶν</quote>: indeed, I know no strictly similar instance, for we cannot compare <ref n="Soph. OT 284"> <title>O. T.</title> 284</ref>, where see n.: still, it is natural enough. Cp. <title>Phaedr.</title> 276 A <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλον ὁρῶμεν λόγον</quote>. So we say, ‘I do not see it,’=‘I cannot take that view.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">μούνη <emph>τῶνδε</emph> </quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 101">cp. 101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλιστον...τῶν προτέρων</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπίλλουσιν_508"> (for the spelling cp. n. in Appendix on 340), lit., ‘turn’ (or ‘roll’) ‘under,’ said of an animal putting its tail between its legs; cp. Eur. fr. 544 (from his <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰδίπους</quote>,—referring to the Sphinx) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρὰν δ’ ὑπίλασ’ ὑπὸ λεοντόπουν βάσιν | καθέζετ’</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 11.812">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 11. 812</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (a terrified wolf) </note>caudamque remulcens Subiecit pavitantem utero.</quote> </cit> Here, ‘keep down’ the utterance of their thoughts; ‘make their lips subservient’ to thee. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">σαίνειν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">adulari.</foreign>—Euphony commends, though metre does not require, the <quote xml:lang="grc">ν ἐφελκυστικόν</quote>, which L gives. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 571">Cp. 571</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_ἐπαιδεῖ_510">; And art thou not ashamed of it (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαισχύνομαι</quote>), if thou thinkest otherwise than they do?— thinkest, namely, that thou art free to act on thy own views, regardless of thy king. For the sake of argument, he concedes their possible <emph>sympathy</emph> with her, but insists on their loyal <emph>behaviour.</emph> She answers, ‘No, I am not ashamed, for I am doing nothing shameful.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅμαιμος_512"> always of brother and sister in Soph. : <title>O.C.</title> 330 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τε_512">. The soundness of the text is thoroughly vindicated by <ref n="Plat. Laws 627C">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 627C</ref> (quoted by Schneidewin), <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὶ ἀδελφοί που γένοιντ’ ἂν ἑνὸς ἀνδρός τε καὶ μιᾶς υἱεῖς</quote>, which also confirms the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_512"> against the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_δῆτ_514">: why, then, dost thou render (to Polyneices) a tribute impious in the sight of Eteocles?—i.e., which places the latter on the same level with the former.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκείνῳ_514">, ethic dat., ‘in his judgment’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 904">cp. 904</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1446"> <title>O. C.</title> 1446</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάξιαι... πᾶσιν</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 810"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 810</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. OT 40"> <title>O. T.</title> 40</ref>. We cannot well render, ‘impious <emph>towards</emph> him,’ which would be <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἐκεῖνον</quote>: nor can the dat. be one of ‘interest,’ as though <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσσεβῆ</quote> were equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαβεράν</quote>. The next verse agrees well with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνῳ</quote> being ethic dat.: ‘he will not so testify,’ =‘he will not say that he thinks my act impious.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάριν_514"> is usu. explained as acc. of the inner object, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμᾷς τιμήν</quote>. But it would evidently be awkward to have an objective acc. added, as <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμῶ χάριν αὐτόν</quote>. Rather </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμᾷς_514"> is here slightly deflected from the sense, ‘to honour by observance,’ ‘to observe duly,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 1210">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1210</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὓς Φρύγες νόμους | τιμῶσιν</quote> </cit>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 1045"> <title>Ion</title> 1045</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν εὐσέβειαν...τιμᾶν</quote>, and means, ‘to render duly,’ as religious observance requires. So I should take it also in the parallel phrase, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 828">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 828</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴαν | τιμῶν χάριν</quote> </cit>, duly rendering grace to thy sire.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_νέκυς_514">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 26">cp. 26</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_τοι_516">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">siquidem</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. OT 549."> <title>O. T.</title> 549.</ref>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφε_516">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 44">cp. 44</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἴσου_516">, <emph>only</emph> on a level with, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1019"> <title>O. T.</title> 1019</ref>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον</quote>=‘equally <emph>little</emph>’ (<ref n="Hdt. 2.3">Her. 2.3</ref>), or ‘equally <emph>vain</emph>’ (id. 8. 79): and id. 8. 109 <quote xml:lang="grc">τά τε ἱρὰ καὶ τὰ ἴδια ἐν ὁμοίῳ ἐποιέετο</quote>, i.e. made sacred things of (<emph>only</emph>) the same account as things profane. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 393">Cp. 393</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δοῦλος_517">. No, Eteocles</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πορθῶν_δέ_518">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> introducing an objection, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 379"> <title>O. T.</title> 379</ref>: for the partic., <ref n="Soph. OT 1001"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1001</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1011">1011.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_δ_518">, but the other (perished) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιστὰς ὑπὲρ τῆσδε γῆς</quote>, as this land's champion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπερ_518"> is paroxytone as virtually following its case, since the gen. is supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε γῆν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1231"> <title>Ai.</title> 1231</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μηδὲν ἀντέστης ὕπερ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτους_519">, the reading of the MSS., has been rejected by nearly all modern editors in favour of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσους_519">, which the Scholiast mentions as a variant. But the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους</quote> is perfectly suitable, —‘these laws,’ the laws of sepulture (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ θάπτειν</quote>, as a schol. paraphrases); and everything that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσους_519_2"> would convey is already expressed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅμως_519">. ‘One was the country's foe, the other its champion —granted. <title>Nevertheless</title> Hades desires these laws,’—i.e. even in the case of the foe. A corruption of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσους_519_3"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτους_519_2"> is very improbable. Rather </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσους_519_4"> was merely one of those conjectures which so often appear in the margin of the MSS., having for their object the supposed improvement of a point. The MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσος</quote> in 520 does not strengthen the case for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσους</quote> here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαχεῖν_ἴσος_520"> is usu. explained, ‘equal in respect to obtaining (rites),’ i.e. with an equal claim to rites. The phrase is not only without any parallel, but seems impossible. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσος λαχεῖν νόμιμα</quote> would be very strange; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσος λαχεῖν</quote>, absolutely, is stranger still. The train of thought strongly favours </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσους_520">, as Nauck suggests and Semitelos reads. ‘Hades may desire these rites; but the good man does not (desire) to receive only the same rites as the wicked’: i.e. Eteocles will not be satisfied with the equality merely because Polyneices was his brother (517): he will think of the contrast between that brother's merits and his own. The dead can be said <quote xml:lang="grc">λαγχάνειν νόμους</quote> (of burial), in the sense of obtaining that which the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοι</quote> give. Therefore we need not write <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτωθεν_521">, simply ‘below’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1070">cp. 1070</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 424">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 424</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ κάτωθεν ἀσπόνδῳ θεῷ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 23.28">Dem. or. 23 § 28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κάτωθεν νόμος</quote> </cit>, the law below (=the continuation of a law already cited). We need not understand here, ‘if these things are approved <emph>from</emph> below.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτω_στὶν_521"> has the MS. authority: but it is most improbable that Soph. would have given such a needlessly unpleasing verse, and the change is sufficiently explained by a later belief that the sense required <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐαγῆ_521">, right in respect to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote>, i.e. free from it, pure (<ref n="Soph. OT 921"> <title>O. T.</title> 921</ref>). She means: ‘who can tell if Eteocles, in the world below, will not think it consonant with piety that Polyneices should be honoured?’ Perhaps earthly feuds are made up there. Creon answers, ‘No,—foe once, foe always,— even in death: Eteocles <emph>will</emph> resent it.’ Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.543"> <title>Od.</title> 11.543</ref> where the spirit of Ajax in Hades will not speak to Odysseus— <quote xml:lang="grc">κεχολωμένη εἵνεκα νίκης | τήν μιν ἐγὼ νίκησα</quote>.—There would be far less point in Creon's words if we took them to mean, ‘<emph>my</emph> dead foe is still my foe’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1348"> <title>Ai.</title> 1348</ref>, 1372). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτοι_συνέχθειν_523">. ‘Even if my brothers hate each other still, my nature prompts me, not to join Eteocles in hating Polyneices, but to love each brother as he loves me’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 73">cp. 73</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλη...φίλου μέτα</quote>. Cp. Polybius 1. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλόφιλον δεῖ εὶ=ναι τὸν ἀγαθὸν ἄνδρα καὶ φιλόπατριν, καὶ συμμισεῖν τοῖς φίλοις τοὺς ἐχθροὺς καὶ συναγαπᾶν τοὺς φίλους</quote>. Eur. imitates our verse, <title>I. A.</title> 407 (Agam. to Menelaus) <quote xml:lang="grc">συσσωφρονεῖν γάρ, οὐχὶ συννοσεῖν ἔφυν</quote>, ‘nay, my sympathies are with prudence, not with frenzy.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_524">, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 87"> <title>Ai.</title> 87</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 92"> <title>Tr.</title> 92</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1196"> <title>Ph.</title> 1196</ref>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">νυ—ν</quote> <title>O.T.</title> 658, and oft.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>κείνους</emph> = τοὺς ἐκεῖ</quote>, the dead. Nauck proposes <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκρούς</quote>, which would be a deplorable change. For the pause after the emphatic word, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 46">cp. 46</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_526"> introducing the new person: <ref n="Soph. OC 549"> <title>O. C.</title> 549</ref> n. At Creon's command (491), two <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολοι</quote> had gone to bring Ismene. The door from which she now enters is that by which she had left the stage (99). It is supposed to lead to the <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικωνῖτις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 578">cp. 578</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰβομένη_526">, the correction of Triclinius for the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">λειβομένη</quote>, enables us to keep <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυα</quote>, instead of changing it to <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυ</quote>, when <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλάδελφα</quote> must be taken as adv. (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</ref> n.). The Schol. so took it (he paraphrases by <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλαδέλφως</quote>), and it would seem, therefore, that he read <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυ λειβομένη</quote>. But, though this constr. is quite admissible, it would be far more natural that <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλάδελφα</quote> should agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυα</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OC 1251"> <title>O. C.</title> 1251</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">λείβων δάκρυον</quote>: neither <quote xml:lang="grc">λείβω</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴβω</quote> occurs elsewhere in Soph. ; and the only other place in Tragedy where <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴβω</quote> has good support is <ref n="Aesch. PB 400">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 400</ref>, where Hermann, by reading <quote xml:lang="grc">δακρυσίστακτον ἀπ’ ὄσσων π(αδινῶν δ’ εἰβομένα π(έος</quote>, for the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">δακρυσίστακτον δ’...λειβομένα</quote>, restores the metre. But <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ δάκρυον εἴβειν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυα λείβειν</quote> were equally familiar as Homeric phrases; and if an Attic poet could use the latter, there was certainly no reason why he should not use the former. I may remark, too, that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτω_526"> points to a reminiscence of the phrase</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτω_526_2">, adv., ‘downwards’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 716">cp. 716</ref>, fr. 620 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦτα κυλλαίνων κάτω</quote>. Nauck's objection, that <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτω εἰβομένη</quote> could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">κατειβομένη</quote>, would have force only if <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτω</quote> were necessarily a prep., substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote>. He proposes <quote xml:lang="grc">καταὶ</quote> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">καταιβάτης</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεφέλη_ὕπερ_528">, a cloud of grief (resting) on her brow,—as dark clouds rest on a mountain-summit: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 173">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 173</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγνὸν δ’ ὀφρύων νέφος αὐξάνεται</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 228">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 228</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἀμήχανον | κἀκ χαλεπᾶς δύας ὕπερθ’ ὀμμάτων | κρημναμέναν νεφέλαν</quote> </cit>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">συννεφής = συνωφρυωμένος</quote>. Cp. <title>Deme supercilio nubem</title> ( <ref n="Hor. Ep. 1. 18. 94">Hor. <title>Ep.</title> 1. 18. 94</ref>). The cloud of sorrow is associated with the rain of tears: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. ANT 3.2">Shaksp. <title>Ant.</title> 3. 2. 51</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">Will Caesar weep?—He has a cloud in 's face.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰν ἀμήχανον | κἀκ χαλεπᾶς δύας ὕπερθ’ ὀμμάτων
									| κρημναμέναν νεφέλαν

Will Caesar weep?—He has a cloud in 's
									face.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱματόεν_528">, here, ‘<emph>suffused with</emph> blood,’ darkly flushed. This application of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱματόεις</quote> to the human face seems unparalleled, though in <title>Anthol. P.</title> 6. 154 Leonidas of Tarentum (c. 280 B.C.) has <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλλα τε πεπταμένων αἱματόεντα ῥόδων</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1487">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1487</ref> was less daring when he called a dark blush <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὑπὸ βλεφάροις | φοίνικ’</quote> (‘crimson’), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρύθημα προσώπου</quote>. It recalls the well-known fragment of Ionin Athen. 603 E, where the schoolmaster objects to Phrynichus's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ πορφυρέαις παρῇσι</quote>, on the principle, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ κάρτα δεῖ τὸ καλὸν τῷ μὴ καλῷ φαινομένῳ εἰκάζειν</quote>,—and Sophocles makes a lively defence of it. Shaksp. uses ‘bloody’ for ‘blood-red’: <cit> <bibl n="Shak. H5 1.2"> <title>Hen. V.</title> 1. 2. 101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">unwind your bloody flag.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥέθος_528"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρόσωπον</foreign>, as <title>H. F.</title> 1203 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρες ἀπ’ ὀμμάτων | πέπλον, ἀπόδικε, ῥέθος ἀελίῳ δεῖξον</quote>. Cp. Eustathius 1090, 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰστέον ὅτι ῥέθεα οἱ μὲν ἄλλοι τὰ μέλη φασίν, Αἰολεῖς δὲ μόνοι, κατὰ τοὺς παλαιούς, τὸ πρόσωπον ῥέθος καλοῦσιν</quote>. This suggests that the Attic dramatists had lyric precedent for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥέθος</quote>: as Lycophron (173) may also have had for using it as =<quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote>. The Homeric use is confined to the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ ῥεθέων</quote> (thrice in <title>Il.</title>, never in <title>Od.</title>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχύνει_528">, i.e. overcasts its sunny beauty: cp. Thomson, <title>Spring</title> 21, <title>Winter ...b</title><<title>*> his driving sleets</title> Deform <emph>the day delightless.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_δ_531"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρ’, εἰπέ</quote> (534).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφειμένη_531">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">submissa</foreign>, ‘lurking,’ as a viper lurks under stones: <title>H. A.</title> 8. 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ δ’ ἔχιδναι ὑπὸ τὰς πέτρας ἀποκρύπτουσιν ἑαυτάς</quote>. <title>H. F.</title> 72 <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζω νεοσσοὺς ὄρνις ὣς ὑφειμένη</quote>, like a cowering hen (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφειμένους</quote> Kirchhoff). The word may also suggest a contrast between Antigone's bolder nature and the submissive demeanour of Ismene (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 335"> <title>El.</title> 335</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ἐν κακοῖς μοι πλεῖν ὑφειμένῃ δοκεῖ</quote> </cit>, ‘with shortened sail’). But we should not render it by ‘submissive’; its primary reference is to the image of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχιδνα</quote>. Others render, ‘having crept in,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">clam immissa.</foreign> The act. can mean to ‘send in secretly’ (see on <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφείς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 387"> <title>O. T.</title> 387</ref>), but the pass. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφίεσθαι</quote> does not seem to occur in a corresponding sense.</gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν δ’ ἐν κακοῖς μοι πλεῖν ὑφειμένῃ
									δοκεῖ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχιδνα_531">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 770"> <title>Tr.</title> 770</ref> (the poison works) <quote xml:lang="grc">φοινίας | ἐχθρᾶς ἐχίδνης ἰὸς ὥς</quote>. So of Clytaemnestra (<ref n="Aesch. Lib. 249">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 249</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 271">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 271</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχίδνης καὶ πυρὸς περαιτέρω</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Ion 1262."> <title>Ion</title> 1262.</ref>This image for domestic treachery is quaintly illustrated by the popular notions mentioned in <title>Mirab.</title> 165 (p. 846 <emph>b</emph> 18 Berl. ed.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ περκνοῦ ἔχεως τῇ ἐχίδνἡ συγγινομένου</quote>,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λήθουσά_ἐξέπινες_532">. It seems unnecessary to suppose a confusion of images. The venom from the echidna's bite is here described as working insidiously, and, at first, almost insensibly. So Heracles says of the poison, which he has already (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 770"> <title>Tr.</title> 770</ref>) compared to the echidna's, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δὲ χλωρὸν αἷμά μου | πέπωκεν ἤδη</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1055"> <title>Tr.</title> 1055</ref>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 784"> <title>El.</title> 784</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε γὰρ μείζων βλάβη | ξύνοικος ἦν μοι, τοὐμὸν ἐκπίνουσ’ ἀεὶ | ψυχῆς ἄκρατον αἷμα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἥδε γὰρ μείζων βλάβη | ξύνοικος ἦν μοι, τοὐμὸν
									ἐκπίνουσ’ ἀεὶ | ψυχῆς ἄκρατον αἷμα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτα_κἀπαναστάσεις_533">. The dual is commended, as against <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτας</quote>, by a certain scornful vigour; just as at 58 the dual has an emphasis of its own. And the combination with a plural is no harsher than (e.g.) <title>Laches</title> p. 187 A <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ εὑρεταὶ γεγονότε</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 530"> <title>O. C.</title> 530</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὗται δὲ δύ’ ἐξ ἐμοῦ...παῖδε, δύο δ’ ἄτα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
αὗται δὲ δύ’ ἐξ ἐμοῦ...παῖδε, δύο δ’
									ἄτα.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαναστάσεις_533">, abstract (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτα</quote>) for concrete: so 646 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνους</quote> (bad sons): <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνος ἡ πᾶσα βλάβη</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 622"> <title>Ph.</title> 622</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλεθρος</quote>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρόνων_533">, object. gen. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπανίστασθαι θρόνοις</quote>). Creon suspects the sisters of being in league with malcontent citizens (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 289">cp. 289</ref>), who wish to overthrow his rule. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μὴ_535">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 443">cp. 443</ref>. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 57.59">[Dem. ] or. 57 § 59</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν ἐξομόσαιτο μὴ οὐκ εἰδέναι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 949A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 949A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαρνηθέντι καὶ ἐξομοσαμένῳ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐκ ἂν ἐξομόσαιτο μὴ οὐκ εἰδέναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμορροθεῖ_536">, concurs, consents: fr. 446 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμορροθῶ, συνθέλω, | συμπαραινέσας ἔχω</quote>. Schol. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 851">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 851</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμορροθεῖν δὲ κυρίως τὸ ἅμα καὶ συμφώνως ἐρέσσειν</quote> </cit>. So <title>Orphic Argonaut.</title> 254 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμορροθέοντες</quote>, ‘rowing all together’ (cp. above on 259). The image thus agrees with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύμπλουν</quote> in 541. Ismene remembers her sister's words: ‘even if you should change your mind, I could never welcome you now as my fellow-worker’ (69). She says, then, ‘I consider myself as having shared in the deed—if my sister will allow me.’ Nauck sadly defaces the passage by his rash change, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ ἥδ’· ὁμορροθῶ</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὁμορροθεῖν δὲ κυρίως τὸ ἅμα καὶ συμφώνως
									ἐρέσσειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_αἰτίας_537"> depends on both verbs. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμμετίσχω_537"> having prepared the ear for a partitive gen., no harshness is felt in the reference of that gen. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρω_537"> also. We cannot take the gen. with the first verb only, and regard <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ φέρω</quote> as parenthetic. Some real instances of such a parenthetic construction are given in the n. on 1279 f.; but the supposed examples often break down on scrutiny. Thus in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 274"> <title>Ai.</title> 274</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔληξε κἀνέπνευσε τῆς νόσου</quote>, the gen. goes with both verbs (cp. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1113"> <title>O. C.</title> 1113</ref>): for <ref n="Soph. OC 1330"> <title>O. C.</title> 1330</ref>, see n. there: in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 331">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 331</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων μετασχὼν καὶ τετολμηκὼς ἐμοί</quote> </cit>, the pron. might be dat. of interest with the second partic.; but we ought perhaps to read <quote xml:lang="grc">συντετολμηκώς τ’ ἐμοί</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_σ_538">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 407"> <title>O. C.</title> 407</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐᾷ τοὔμφυλον αἷμά σ’, ὦ πάτερ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐᾷ τοὔμφυλον αἷμά σ’, ὦ
									πάτερ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινωσάμην_538">: prodelision of the augment, as 457 (n.): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1602"> <title>O. C.</title> 1602</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖ ’πόρευσαν σὺν χρόνῳ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύμπλουν_541">: cp. <title>H. F.</title> 1225 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῶν καλῶν μὲν ὅστις ἀπολαύειν θέλει, | συμπλεῖν δὲ τοῖς φίλοισι δυστυχοῦσιν οὔ</quote>. <title>I. T.</title> 599 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ναυστολῶν γάρ εἰμ’ ἐγὼ τὰς συμφοράς, | οὗτος δὲ συμπλεῖ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_542">, and not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργοις</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 307"> <title>Ph.</title> 307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις | ἐλεοῦσι</quote>, they show compassion in word (only). Theognis 979 <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοι ἀνὴρ εἴη γλώσσῃ φίλος ἀλλὰ καὶ ἔργῳ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ἀτιμάσῃς_544">, do not reject me (the word used by the suppliant Oed., <ref n="Soph. OC 49"> <title>O. C.</title> 49</ref>, 286), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_οὐ_544"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 443">cp. 443</ref> n.), so as to hinder me from dying with thee, and paying due honour to the dead. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγνίσαι_θ_544">. is to make him <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνός</quote>, i.e. to give him the rites which religion requires; as, conversely, a corpse which is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμοιρος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτέριστος</quote> is also <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνόσιος</quote> (1071). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 1211">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 1211</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ αὐτῶν σώμαθ’ ἡγνίσθη πυρί</quote> </cit>, where their corpses <emph>received the rites</emph> of fire, i.e. were burned. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 196">Cp. 196</ref> n. If Ismene shares in the penalty of the deed, she will share in the merit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_μοι_546">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">μή’ μοί</quote>, since the main emphasis is on the verbal notion (‘<emph>share</emph> not my death,’ rather than, ‘share not <emph>my</emph> death’): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 83">cp. 83</ref> n. The combination <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοι...σὺ</quote> has a scornful, repellent tone (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1441"> <title>O. C.</title> 1441</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινά_546">, adv.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 577"> <title>Ai.</title> 577</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέροπτα</quote> </cit> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_θιγες_546">. If this were an instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνω</quote> with acc., it would be a solitary instance in Soph. , who has <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνω</quote> with genitive in nine passages; in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 667">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 667</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέσται ταῦτά σοι καὶ θιγγάνειν, ταῦτα</quote> is nom. Nor is there any authentic instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνω</quote> with acc. in classical Greek. In <title>H. F.</title> 963, <quote xml:lang="grc">πατὴρ δέ νιν | θιγὼν κραταιᾶς χειρὸς ἐννέπει τάδε, νιν</quote> depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννέπει</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 764"> <title>Ai.</title> 764</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὲν γὰρ αὐτὸν ἐννέπει· τέκνον</quote> </cit>, etc. In <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 1">Theocr. 1. 59</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ τί πα ποτὶ χεῖλος ἐμὸν θίγεν</quote> </cit>, the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ</quote> is understood with <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέθιγεν</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> is adv., ‘at all.’ Nor does <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύω</quote> govern an acc. below in 859, 961 (where see notes). Krüger (11. § 47. 12. 2) treats </gloss>

data.quote: ὁ μὲν γὰρ αὐτὸν ἐννέπει· τέκνον
οὐδέ τί πα ποτὶ χεῖλος ἐμὸν θίγεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅ_546"> here as a sort of adverb (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 11. § 46. 6. 9), i.e., <emph>in a case where</emph> you did not put your hand (to the deed, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ ἔργου</quote>); but this is very awkward. Rather, I think, there is an unusual kind of attraction, due to the special form of the sentence. We could not say (e.g.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ μὴ ἐρᾷ τις, οὐ θηρᾶται</quote>, (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἅ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα ὧν</quote>). But here <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ ὧν μὴ ’θιγες ποιοῦ σεαυτῆς</quote> would have been intolerable, on account of the second gen. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιοῦ</quote>. For the sake of compactness, and of clearly marking the object to <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιοῦ</quote>, the poet has here allowed </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅ_546_2"> to stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα ὧν</quote>. I do not compare <ref n="Soph. OC 1106"> <title>O. C.</title> 1106</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτεῖς ἃ τεύξει</quote>, holding that <quote xml:lang="grc">ά</quote> there=<quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα ἅ</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote>): see n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποιοῦ_σεαυτῆς_547">, a somewhat rare phrase. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.129">Her. 1.129</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑωυτοῦ ποιέεται τὸ Κύρου ἔργον</quote> </cit>. [Plat. ] <ref n="Plat. Hipp. Min. 372">Plat. <title>Hipp. min.</title> 372</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτοῦ ποιούμενος τὸ μάθημα εἶναι ὡς εὕρημα</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.36">Dem. or. 19 § 36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς αὑτὸν ποιούμενος <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(taking on himself)</note> τὰ τούτων ἁμαρτήματα</quote> </cit>. In <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 8.9">Thuc. 8.9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑαυτοῦ...τὸν στόλον ἴδιον ποιήσασθαι</quote> </cit>, the gen. goes with the adj.</gloss>

data.quote: εἰς αὑτὸν ποιούμενος   τὰ τούτων ἁμαρτήματα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρκέσω_547"> in the pers. constr., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 76"> <title>Ai.</title> 76</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνδον ἀρκείτω μένων</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Soph. OT 1061"> <title>O. T.</title> 1061</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_λελειμμένῃ_548">, bereft of thee. <quote xml:lang="grc">λείπομαί τινος</quote>, to lag behind, then, fig., to be deprived of, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 474"> <title>El.</title> 474</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας λειπομένα</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 406">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέος ἐγώ, πάτερ, λείπομαι φίλας | ...ματρός.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλος_548"> has been groundlessly suspected, for no other reason, seemingly, than because it is masc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηδεμών_549">, alluding esp. to v. 47. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.1.17">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.1.17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς δέ, οἷς κηδεμὼν...οὐδεὶς πάρεστιν</quote> </cit>, who would have no one <emph>to plead our cause</emph> (no ‘friend at court,’ such as the younger Cyrus had in his mother Parysatis). In <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.163"> <title>Il.</title> 23.163</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηδεμόνες</quote> </cit> are the chief mourners for the dead. In Attic, though sometimes poet. for <quote xml:lang="grc">κηδεστής</quote>, the word did not necessarily imply kinship. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλγοῦσα_δῆτ_551">, yes, indeed, it is to my own pain that I mock <emph>thee</emph>,— if I do mock. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτα_551"> assents (<ref n="Soph. OT 445"> <title>O. T.</title> 445</ref> n.) to <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ὠφελουμένη</quote>: there is, indeed, no <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφελος</quote> in it, but only <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_σοί_551">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1092"> <title>Ai.</title> 1092</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν θανοῦσιν ὑβριστής</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1315"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1315</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐμοὶ θρασύς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_γ_551">, for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γέλωτ’</quote>, is supported by the accent <quote xml:lang="grc">γελῶτ’</quote> in L, and seems right. It smooths the construction; and <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γελῶ γ’</quote> better expresses that the taunt sprang from anguish, not from a wish to pain. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">γέλωτα γελῶ</quote>, without an epithet for the subst., is unusual.—Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 79"> <title>Ai.</title> 79</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν γέλως ἥδιστος εἰς ἐχθροὺς γελᾶν;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔκουν γέλως ἥδιστος εἰς ἐχθροὺς
									γελᾶν;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_νῦν_552">, <emph>now</emph>, at least: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1276"> <title>O. C.</title> 1276</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὑμεῖς γε</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμπλάκω_554">, and am I to miss the</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἵλου_555">, alluding to v. 78 f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῆν_555">: for the emphatic pause, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 907"> <title>Ph.</title> 907</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν ἐν οἷς γε δρᾷς· ἐν οἷς δ’ αὐδᾷς, ὀκνῶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_λ_556"> (but you did not choose death) without my words (my arguments against that course) having been spoken,—referring to vv. 49—68. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> with the negative verbal (=<quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν τοὺς ἐμοὺς λόγους ῥηθῆναι</quote>) cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 228">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 228</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ δ’ ἀσφάκτοις | μήλοισι δόμων μὴ πάριτ’ ἐς μυχόν</quote> </cit> (‘before sheep have been slain, pass not,’ etc.). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπὶ δ’ ἀσφάκτοις | μήλοισι δόμων μὴ πάριτ’ ἐς
									μυχόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_τοῖς_557">: ‘you seemed wise to the one side (Creon); I, to the other’ (to Hades and the dead). Nauck pronounces the text unsound, objecting to the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς</quote>: but that it was good Attic is sufficiently shown by <ref n="Plat. Laws 701E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 701E</ref> (cited by Wolff) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ συνήνεγκεν οὔτε τοῖς οὔτε τοῖς</quote> (it profited neither party). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 742"> <title>O. C.</title> 742</ref>, n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δὲ τῶν.—σὺ μὲν <emph>σοί</emph> </quote>, the schol.'s reading, is very inferior.—For the rhetorical <quote xml:lang="grc">χιασμός</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 538"> <title>O. T.</title> 538</ref> n. (and <ref n="Soph. OT 320"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 320</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μήν_558">, and yet,—though I <emph>did</emph> shrink from breaking Creon's law,—I am now, morally, as great an offender as you, since I sympathise with your act. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάρσει_559"> is not said with bitterness (that could hardly be, after 551): rather it means, ‘Take heart to live,’ as Whitelaw renders it. These two verses quietly express her feeling that their lots are irrevocably sundered, and exhort Ismene to accept the severance.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_ψυχή_559">, my life, a periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote>, like <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 998"> <title>O. C.</title> 998</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πατρὸς | ψυχὴν...ζῶσαν</quote> </cit> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_559">, i.e. ever since she resolved to break the edict. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1161"> <title>O. T.</title> 1161</ref>.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_ὠφελεῖν_559">, so as to (with a view to) serving the dead. The dat., as with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαρκεῖν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 871"> <title>Ph.</title> 871</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνωφελοῦντά μοι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 842">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 842</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τοῖς θανοῦσι πλοῦτος οὐδὲν ὠφελεῖ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 665">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 665</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς φίλοισιν ὠφελεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 419">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 419</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλοισιν ὠφελεῖν ἔχειν</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπωφελεῖν</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 441"> <title>O. C.</title> 441</bibl> </cit>.—Dobree proposed to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote> as subj. to the inf., ‘so that (you) are helping the dead,’ —i.e., your offer of help (552) is made to one who is already as good as dead. But <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ</quote> could not be thus understood; and this sense (which it has been sought to obtain by emendations, see cr. n.) would be frigid. </gloss>

data.quote: ὡς τοῖς θανοῦσι πλοῦτος οὐδὲν ὠφελεῖ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὼ_φημὶ_561"> as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 317"> <title>O. C.</title> 317</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ φημὶ</quote> </cit>, and this may probably be taken as the traditional accentuation, though some modern edd. write <quote xml:lang="grc">τὼ παῖδέ φημι, καί φημι</quote>. The justification of the oxytone <quote xml:lang="grc">φημὶ</quote> is in the emphasis which falls on it. Similarly it has the accent when parenthetic, as Lucian <ref n="Lucian Deor.Conc. 2"> <title>Deor. Conc.</title> 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὶ γάρ, φημί, οὐκ ἀγαπῶντες κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὼ_δέ_561">, partitive apposition: <ref target="cl21">see on 21</ref>.</gloss>
lemma: τὼ παῖδε … τὴν μὲν … τὴν δέ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτίως_561">, because Creon had hitherto regarded Ismene as being of a docile and submissive nature: cp. on 531 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφειμένη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλάστῃ_563">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 238"> <title>El.</title> 238</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τίνι τοῦτ’ ἔβλαστ’ ἀνθρώπων</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 804"> <title>O. C.</title> 804</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσας...φρένας</quote> </cit> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_πράσσουσιν_563">, dat. of interest: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 132"> <title>Tr.</title> 132</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μένει γὰρ οὔτ’ αἰόλα νὺξ | βροτοῖσιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: μένει γὰρ οὔτ’ αἰόλα νὺξ | βροτοῖσιν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξίσταται_563">, stands aside, gives place (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 672"> <title>Ai.</title> 672</ref>); and so, leaves its proper place, becomes deranged: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 928">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 928</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐξ ἕδρας σοι πλόκαμος ἐξέστηχ’ ὅδε</quote> </cit>. The converse phrase is commoner, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξίσταμαι τῶν φρενῶν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1105">cp. 1105</ref>.—Schneidewin cp. <title>Antigone</title> fr. 165 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκουσον· οὐ γὰρ οἱ κακῶς πεπραγότες | σὺν ταῖς τύχαισι τοὺς λόγους ἀπώλεσαν</quote>,—which plainly glances at our passage. For similar allusions cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1116"> <title>O. C.</title> 1116</ref> n. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἐξ ἕδρας σοι πλόκαμος ἐξέστηχ’
									ὅδε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_γοῦν_565"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 45">cp. 45</ref> n.) <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέστη.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῖς_565">, i.e. Antigone: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 10">cp. 10</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδ_ἄτερ_566"> explains </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνῃ_566">: cp. on 445.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιώσιμον_566"> is Ionic and poet.; the Attic word was <quote xml:lang="grc">βιωτός</quote>. It is needless to change </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_566"> (subst.) into <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς</quote>. The more usual phrase was, indeed, impers., as <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.45">Her. 1.45</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ οἱ εἴη βιώσιμον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 47D">Plat. <title>Crito</title> 47D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα βιωτὸν ἡμῖν ἐστί</quote> </cit>; But, just as we can have <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ βίος οὐ βιωτός ἐστι</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1691"> <title>O. C.</title> 1691</ref>), so also <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν βιωτόν ἐστι</quote>, no form of life is tolerable. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1337"> <title>O. T.</title> 1337</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτ’ ἐμοὶ βλεπτόν, ἢ | στερκτόν, ἢ προσήγορον | ἔτ’ ἔστ’</quote> </cit>, etc., where the only difference is that the subst. <quote xml:lang="grc">τί</quote> corresponds to an object. accus., and not as here to a cognate (<quote xml:lang="grc">βίον βιῶναι</quote>). </gloss>

data.quote:
τί δῆτ’ ἐμοὶ βλεπτόν, ἢ | στερκτόν, ἢ
									προσήγορον | ἔτ’ ἔστ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_μέντοι_567">, ‘nay, but...’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524"> <title>Ph.</title> 524</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ αἰσχρὰ μέντοι σοῦ γέ μ’ ἐνδεέστερον | ξένῳ φανῆναι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδε_λέγε_567">, say not ‘<quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote>,’ speak not of her as still with thee, for she is already numbered with the dead. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵδε</quote> are <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἐνθάδε</quote>, the living (75), as <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνοι</quote> (525) are <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἐκεῖ</quote>, the dead (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 76">cp. 76</ref>). The peculiarity is that we should have expected either (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε</quote>, acc. to <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγε</quote>, or (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε</quote>, as a direct quotation from the last verse: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.88">Dem. or. 18 § 88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἦν...; ὑμεῖς, ὦ ἄνδρες Ἀθηναῖοι. τὸ δ’ ὑμεῖς ὅταν λέγω, λέγω τὴν πόλιν</quote> </cit>. If (e.g.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμῶν</quote> had preceded <quote xml:lang="grc">ω ἄνδρες</quote>, Dem. would doubtless have said <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ὑμῶν</quote>, or else <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμᾶς δ’</quote>. Here, however, no fair objection would remain if we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τὸ ἥδε μὴ λέγε</quote>, i. e. ‘never use the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote> about her,’—which makes the sense more general than if he said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τῆσδε μὴ λέγε</quote>, i. e. ‘do not say (that you cannot live without) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote>.’ The question, then, seems to resolve itself into this:—Wishing to give the more general sense just indicated, could the poet say <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἥδε</quote>? To show that the art. was not always required in such quotation, it is enough to cite <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 21">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγε δὴ μόλωμεν</quote> </cit>, by the side of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μόλωμεν</quote> <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 26"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 26</ref>. While, then, I cannot produce any exact parallel for this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote>, I think it reasonable to suppose that colloquial idiom would have allowed it. Those who deny this have two resources. (1) To point thus: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἥδε μέντοι—μὴ λέγ’·</quote> i.e. instead of adding <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ἔτι</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">μέντοι</quote>, he breaks off his sentence—‘do not speak of her.’ So Bellermann. (2) Semitelos reads <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἥδε μέν σοι μὴ λέγ’ ὡς ἄρ’</quote> [for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ] ἔστ’ ἔτι</quote>, ‘do not say that you have <emph>her</emph> any longer.’ As to <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι</quote>, see cr. n. above. Neither of these readings gives such a forcible sense as the vulgate. </gloss>

data.quote:
τίς ἦν...; ὑμεῖς, ὦ ἄνδρες Ἀθηναῖοι. τὸ δ’
									ὑμεῖς ὅταν λέγω, λέγω τὴν πόλιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυμφεῖα_568">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερά</quote>, ‘nuptials,’ as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 7"> <title>Tr.</title> 7</ref> (but <ref n="Soph. Trach. 920"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 920</ref> ‘bridal-chamber,’ as in sing. below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1205">1205</ref>); here=<quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφην</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 481">Eur. <title>El.</title> 481</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὰ λέχεα</quote> </cit>, thy spouse: and so <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνή</quote>, etc.—Having failed to win Creon's pity for herself, Ismene now appeals to his feeling for his son. Haemon's coming part in the play is thus prepared. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρώσιμοι_569">, a poet. form (only here), analogous to the epic forms of adjectives in which a short vowel is lengthened for metre's sake (cp. on 492). Though the verb was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρόω</quote>, the adj. with the suffix <quote xml:lang="grc">σιμο</quote> would properly be formed from the subst. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄροσις</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 27"> <title>O. C.</title> 27</ref> n.). Suidas gives <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρόσιμον κλίμα· τὸ ἀροτριούμενον</quote>. For the metaphor cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1256"> <title>O. T.</title> 1256</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρῴαν ...ἄρουραν</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Soph. OT 1485"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1485</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1497">1497</ref>: <ref n="Lucr. 4.1107.">Lucr. 4. 1107.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_γ_570"> (‘Another marriage is possible for him’). ‘No, not in the sense of the troth plighted between him and her,’—not such a union of hearts as had been prepared there. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡρμοσμένα_ἦν_570">, impers., lit., ‘as things had been adjusted’; cp. on 447: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.112">Her. 1.112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν κακῶς βεβουλευμένα ἔσται</quote> </cit>: id. 6. 83 <quote xml:lang="grc">τέως μὲν δή σφι ἦν ἄρθμια ἐς ἀλλήλους</quote>. The choice of the word has been influenced by the Ionic and poet. use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμόζειν</quote> as=to betroth (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἑγγυᾶν</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.137">Her. 3.137</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅρμοσται</quote> </cit> (perf. pass. as midd.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν Μίλωνος θυγατέρα...γυναῖκα</quote>, he has become engaged to her: <ref n="Pind. P. 9">Pind. <title>P.</title> 9. 127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμόζων κόρᾳ | νυμφίον ἄνδρα</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 2">Cp. 2</ref> Epist. <ref n="Plut. Cor. 11. 2"> Cor. 11. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡρμοσάμην γὰρ ὑμᾶς ἑνὶ ἀνδρὶ παρθένον ἁγνήν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υἱέσι_571">, the regular Attic dat. plur. (as <ref n="Plat. Rep. 362E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 362E</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 1001">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1001</ref>, etc.), from the stem <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱυ</quote>-, which furnished also the Attic nom. and gen. plur., <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱεῖς, υἱέων</quote>; and the dual <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱῆ</quote> (or rather <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱεῖ</quote>, Meisterhans p. 63), <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱέοιν</quote>. The Attic forms of the sing. and the acc. plur. were taken from <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱο</quote>-, except that <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱέος, υἱεῖ</quote> were alternative forms for the gen. and dat. sing. Here L has the epic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υἱάσιν_571">, from a third stem, <quote xml:lang="grc">υἱ</quote>-, whence the Homeric forms <quote xml:lang="grc">υἷος, υἷι</quote>,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὰς_γυναῖκας_571">, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγῶ_571">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1161">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1161</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόβολος ἐμός, σωτὴρ δόμοις, ἐχθροῖς βλάβη</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
πρόβολος ἐμός, σωτὴρ δόμοις, ἐχθροῖς
									βλάβη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἷμον_572">. L has <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵμων</quote>. Soph. would have written AIMON: hence the tradition is subject to the same ambiguity as in KPEON. The analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">δαῖμον</quote> would probably have recommended the form in <quote xml:lang="grc">ο</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγαν_λυπεῖς_573">, ‘Nay, thou art too troublesome,’—the impatient phrase of one who would silence another, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 589"> <title>Ai.</title> 589</ref> (Ajax to Tecmessa) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγαν γε λυπεῖς· οὐ κάτοισθ’</quote>, etc.: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 592"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 592</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ ἄγαν ἤδη θροεῖς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_575">, L's reading, is right. Creon has been asked,—‘Can you indeed mean to deprive your son of his bride?’ He grimly replies, ‘I look to the Death-god to break off this match.’ The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔφυ_575"> in the later MSS. was obviously a mere conjecture,—and a weak one. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεδογμένα_576"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>), = <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοκται</quote>: cp. on 570 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡρμοσμένα</quote>, 447 <quote xml:lang="grc">κηρυχθέντα</quote>. Cp. Menander <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρρηφόρος</quote> 1. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδογμένον τὸ πρᾶγμ’· ἀνερρίφθω κύβος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κἀμοί_577">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδογμένα</quote>. It is settled, for both of us: i.e., I shall not change my mind, and it is vain for thee to plead. The datives are ethic. We might also understand, ‘settled <emph>by</emph> thee, as by me,’—alluding to the words of the Chorus in v. 211 and in v. 220. But I now feel, with Mr T. Page, that this would be somewhat forced.—We must not point thus: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σοί γε. καί μοι μὴ τριβάς</quote>, etc. (so Semitelos). This would be more defensible if, in 576, <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote> had stood with <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδογμένα</quote>: but, as it is, the vagueness of the latter confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σοί γε κἀμοί</quote>. Bellermann, giving 576 to Ismene, adopts Kern's <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σοί γε κοινῇ</quote> (‘yes, and she shall die with you’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τριβάς_577">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖσθε</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 345">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 345</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μή μοι πρόφασιν, ἀλλὰ κατάθου τὸ βέλος</quote> </cit>: <title>Vesp.</title> 1179 <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοί γε μύθους.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλὰ μή μοι πρόφασιν, ἀλλὰ κατάθου τὸ
									βέλος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_577">, plur., as <ref n="Soph. OT 868"> <title>O. T.</title> 868</ref> (masc.), <ref n="Soph. OC 43"> <title>O. C.</title> 43</ref> (fem.), <ref n="Soph. El. 436"> <title>El.</title> 436</ref> (neut.), etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τοῦδε_578">, <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Compare 484 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ νῦν ἐγὼ μὲν οὐκ ἀνήρ, αὕτη δ’ ἀνήρ</quote>: 525 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ δὲ ζῶντος οὐκ ἄρξει γυνή</quote>: also 678 ff. This much-vexed passage is sound as it has come down to us. Creon means: ‘henceforth they must be <emph>women</emph>, and must not roam unrestrained.’ The fact that a woman has successfully defied him rankles in his mind. Hence the bitterness of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γυναικας_578"> here. The Attic notions of feminine propriety forbade such freedom as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνειμένας_578"> denotes. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 516"> <title>El.</title> 516</ref> (Clytaemnestra finding Electra outside the house) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνειμένη μέν, ὡς ἔοικας, αὖ στρέφει</quote>: Electra should be restrained, <quote xml:lang="grc">μή τοι θυραίαν γ’ οὖσαν αἰσχύνειν φίλους</quote>. So pseudoPhocyl. 216 (keep a maiden in-doors), <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ μιν ἄχρι γάμων πρὸ δόμων ὀφθῆμεν ἐάσῃς</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 16">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπή τοι γυναικῶν ἔξοδος</quote> </cit>. The emphasis of <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναῖκας</quote> here is parallel with the frequent emphasis of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</quote> (as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 693">Eur. <title>El.</title> 693</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα γίγνεσθαί σε χρή</quote> </cit>, a <emph>man</emph>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1368"> <title>O. C.</title> 1368</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵδ’ ἄνδρες, οὐ γυναῖκες, εἰς τὸ συμπονεῖν</quote> </cit>.—All the emendations are weak or improbable. See Appendix. </gloss>

data.quote:
αἵδ’ ἄνδρες, οὐ γυναῖκες, εἰς τὸ
									συμπονεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοἱ_θρασεῖς_580">. Remark how well the use of the masc. here suits the taunt conveyed in the last verse. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλας_580"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>)... </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορῶσι_580">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 29"> <title>O. C.</title> 29</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλας γὰρ ἄνδρα τόνδε νῷν ὁρῶ</quote> </cit>: and see <ref n="Soph. OC 586"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 586</ref> n. </gloss>

data.quote: πέλας γὰρ ἄνδρα τόνδε νῷν ὁρῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶν_ἄγευστος_582">, act., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψαυστος ἔγχους</quote> </cit> n. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 1069">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 1069</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἄρτι πένθους τοῦδε γεύομαι πικροῦ</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.46">Her. 7.46</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...θεὸς γλυκὺν γεύσας τὸν αἰῶνα</quote> </cit> (having allowed men to taste the sweetness of life). </gloss>

data.quote: ὡς ἄρτι πένθους τοῦδε γεύομαι πικροῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεισθῇ_θεόθεν_583">, i.e. by an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρά</quote> (likened to a storm, or earthquake, that shakes a building): when a sin has once been committed, and the shock of divine punishment has once been felt. In the case of the Labdacidae the calamities were traced to the curse called down on Laïus by Pelops, when robbed by him of his son Chrysippus (<ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref> p. xix.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτας_ἐλλείπει_583">, (for these men, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς = τούτοις οἷς</quote>) no sort of calamity is wanting. Some join </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλλείπει_583"> with <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρπον</quote>,</emph> on the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">παύεσθαι</quote> with part., ‘never fails to go’; but this constr. is at least very rare.
