In a probably spurious <quote xml:lang="grc">ψήφισμα</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> <ref n="Dem. 18.92">Dem. or. 18 § 92</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐλλείψει εὐχαριστῶν</quote>: but <ref n="Xen. Mem. 2. 6">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 2. 6</ref>§ 5 (adduced by Wecklein) is not an example, for there <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ὲλλείπεσθαι εὖ ποιῶν</quote>=‘not to be outdone in generosity.’ Then in <title>Phaedr.</title> 272 B <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι ἂν αὐτῶν τις ἐλλείπῃ λέγων</quote> =simply ‘omit <emph>in</emph> speaking.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενεᾶς_πλῆθος_583">. The phrase is bold, and somewhat strange; but I do not think that it is corrupt. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενεᾶς_583"> here is the whole race, not (as in 596) a generation of the race. The words mean literally, ‘<emph>over a multitude of the race</emph>’; i.e., the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote> does not cease with the person who first brought it into the family, or with his generation, but continues to afflict succeeding genera tions. The collective noun <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεᾶς</quote> justifies the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πλῆθος</quote>: as he might have said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπογόνων πλῆθος</quote>. It is needless, then, to write <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεᾶν</quote>. We cannot understand, ‘to the fulness of the race,’ i.e. till the race has been exhausted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅμοιον_586">, adv.: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 628D">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 628D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμοιον ὡς εἰ...ἡγοῖτό τις.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντίαις_586"> (see cr. n.) is far the most probable reading. The loss of the second <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote>, leaving <quote xml:lang="grc">ποντίας</quote>, would easily have brought in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλός</quote>, which the metre shows to be superfluous. In Greek poetry there is no objection to the three epithets with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πνοαῖς_586">: the whole phrase would be felt as meaning, ‘stormy seawinds from Thrace.’ Construe: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅταν_οἶδμα_586">, when a surge, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντίαις_πνοαῖς_586">, driven by stormy sea-winds from Thrace (instr. dat., cp. on 335 <quote xml:lang="grc">νότῳ</quote>), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιδράμῃ_ὕφαλον_586">, rushes over the dark depths of the sea (lit., the darkness under the surface of the sea). For <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπν. πνοαῖς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 502">cp. 502</ref> n.: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θρῄσσαισιν_586">, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 192">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 192</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοαὶ δ’ ἀπὸ Στρυμόνος μολοῦσαι</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 654"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 654</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θρῄκιαι πνοαί</quote> </cit> (and <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1418">1418</ref>): <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.5"> <title>Il.</title> 9.5</ref> (where the tumult in the breasts of the Greeks is likened to a storm) <quote xml:lang="grc">Βορέης καὶ Ζέφυρος, τώ τε Θρῄκηθεν ἄητον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελαινὰν_θῖνα_590">, the dark-coloured mud or sand that the storm stirs up from the bottom of the sea. <quote xml:lang="grc">θίς</quote> is masc. in Homer, Ar. , and Arist. , and that was prob. its usual gender. Soph. has it fem. again in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1124">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1124</ref>, and so it is in later writers. In the <quote xml:lang="grc"> <title>ιλ.</title>θίς</quote> is always the sea-shore; in <title>Od.</title>that is its regular sense, but once (12. 45) it means ‘heap.’ It is used as here by <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Wasps 696">Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 696</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς μου τὸν θῖνα ταράττεις</quote> </cit> (my very depths). <cit> <bibl n="Verg. G. 3.240">Verg. <title>G.</title> 3. 240</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">at ima exaestuat unda Vorticibus, nigramque alte subiectat arenam.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
at ima exaestuat unda Vorticibus, nigramque
									alte subiectat arenam.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσάνεμοι_590"> should be read. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσάνεμον_590"> could not here be adv. with <quote xml:lang="grc">βρέμουσιν</quote>, and must therefore be epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">θῖνα</quote>, when it could mean only <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ὑπὸ ἀνέμων ταραχθεῖσαν</quote> (schol.), i.e. ‘stirred up by the storm,’—a strained sense for it. Cp. Apoll. Rhod. 1. 593 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτήν τ’ αἰγιαλόν τε δυσήνεμον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόνῳ_βρ_590"> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 427">cp. 427</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιπλῆγες_590"> (only here) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκταί_590">, headlands which are struck in front, struck <emph>full</emph>, by the waves; in contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">παραπλῆγες</quote>, ‘struck obliquely’: see <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.417"> <title>Od.</title> 5.417</ref> (Odysseus seeking a place to land) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤν που ἐφεύρω | ἠτονάς τε παραπλῆγας λιμένας τε θαλάσσης</quote> (‘shores where the waves strike <emph>aslant</emph>’). Soph. was doubtless thinking of the Homeric phrase.—Not (1) ‘beating back the waves,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίτυποι</quote>: nor (2) ‘beaten again,’—i.e. by the everreturning waves. This last is impossible. —Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1240"> <title>O. C.</title> 1240</ref> where Oed. is likened to a <quote xml:lang="grc">βόρειος...ἀκτὰ κυματοπλήξ</quote>. Oppian <ref n="Arr. Cyn. 2.142"> <title>Cyn.</title> 2. 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρημνοῖσι καὶ ὑδατοπλήγεσιν ἄκραις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαῖα_593">, predicate: I see that, from olden time, the house-troubles (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων πήματα</quote>) of the (living) Labdacidae are heaped upon the troubles of the dead. The dead are now Laïus, Oedipus, and his two sons. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαῖα</quote> carries us back to the starting-point of the troubles, —the curse pronounced on Laïus by Pelops (cp. on 583).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶμαι_593">, midd., as in Homer and Attic Comedy, but not in Attic prose; which, however, used the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">περιορᾶσθαι</quote> (<ref n="Thuc. 6.103">Thuc. 6.103</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">προορᾶσθαι</quote> (<ref n="Dem. 18.281">Dem. or. 18 § 281</ref>, etc.). Soph. has <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρωμένη</quote>, midd., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 306"> <title>Tr.</title> 306</ref> (dial.); and so, too, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδόμην</quote> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 351"> <title>Ph.</title> 351</ref>(dial.), etc. Though </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκων_593"> answers metrically to the first two syllables of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγευστος</quote> in 582, it is not suspicious, because the second syllable of the trochee can be irrational (a long for a short): see Conversely Anal., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθιτῶν_593"> is metrically admissible, though its first syllable answers to the first of <quote xml:lang="grc">σεισθῇ</quote> in 583. This correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">φθιμένων</quote> is strongly confirmed by the similar error of the MSS. in <ref n="Eur. Alc. 100">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 100</ref> (see cr. n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπαλλάσσει_596">, releases (by exhausting the malignity of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρά</quote>): so'oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαλλάσσω τινὰ κακῶν, φόβου</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενεὰν_γένος_596">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1067">cp. 1067</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυν νεκρῶν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 475"> <title>Ai.</title> 475</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ἦμαρ ἡμέρα</quote> </cit>.—The subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_596"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐρείπει θεῶν τις</quote> being parenthetical) is ‘the Labdacid house,’ i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεά</quote> in the larger sense (585), supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεάν</quote> just before. This is simpler than to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">πήματα</quote> as subject.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύσιν_596">, deliverance from trouble, as <ref n="Soph. OT 921"> <title>O. T.</title> 921</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1171"> <title>Tr.</title> 1171</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_ἐσχάτας_599"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> (1) The first question is,—are we to read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_τέτατο_599"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐτέτατο_599">? If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτέτατο</quote>, then the sentence is complete at <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοις</quote>. A new sentence beginning with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_αὖ_599"> would be intolerably abrupt: yet neither <quote xml:lang="grc">κᾆτ’</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταύταν</quote> appears probable. This difficulty would be avoided by changing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέρ_599"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπερ_599">: but then <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίζας φάος</quote> must mean, ‘the comfort (or hope) afforded by the <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίζα</quote>,’—a strange phrase. And <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτατο</quote> confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρ</quote> as well as <quote xml:lang="grc">φάος</quote>. I therefore prefer </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_τέτατο_599_2">. (2) The next point concerns </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_599">. Reading <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ τέτατο</quote>, Wecklein still refers <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίζας</quote>, not to <quote xml:lang="grc">φάος</quote>, saying that the constr. is as though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἇς ὑπὲρ ἐσχάτας ῥίζας</quote> had preceded. This is a grammatical impossibility. With <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ τέτατο, <emph>νιν</emph> </quote> can refer only to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάος_599">. Can this be justified? Thus, I think. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσχάτη_ῥίζα_599"> of the family is the last remaining means of propagating it. A light of hope (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάος_599_2">) was ‘spread above’ this ‘last root,’—as sunshine above a plant,—because it was hoped that the sisters would continue the race. The sisters themselves are, properly speaking, the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάτη ῥίζα</quote>. But as the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίζα</quote> can also have an abstract sense, denoting the chance of propagation, the sisters can here be identified with the <emph>hope</emph>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">φάος</quote>, which shines above the <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίζα</quote>. In Greek this is the easier since <quote xml:lang="grc">φάος</quote> was often said of <emph>persons</emph>, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 18.102"> <title>Il.</title> 18.102</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ τι Πατρόκλῳ γενόμην φάος</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 841">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 841</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δέσποτ’, ὦ μέγιστον Ἕλλησιν φάος</quote> </cit>. To say <quote xml:lang="grc">καταμᾶν φάος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">δόμων</quote>) is like saying, ‘to mow down the hope of the race,’—in this case, the two young lives. A further reason against referring </gloss>

data.quote: ὦ δέσποτ’, ὦ μέγιστον Ἕλλησιν φάος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_599_2"> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥίζα</quote> is that the verb should then be, not <quote xml:lang="grc">καταμᾷ</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαμᾷ</quote>, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1178"> <title>Ai.</title> 1178</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γένους ἅπαντος ῥίζαν ἐξημημένος</quote> </cit>: a root is not ‘mowed down,’ in such a case, but cut out of the ground. The proposed change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάος_599_3"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλος_599">, though not difficult in a palaeographical sense, is condemned by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέτατο_599">, which does not suit <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλος</quote>, but exactly suits <quote xml:lang="grc">φάος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 831"> <title>Ph.</title> 831</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάνδ’ αἴγλαν ἃ τέταται τανῦν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 11.19"> <title>Od.</title> 11.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ νὺξ ὀλοὴ τέταται δειλοῖσι βροτοῖσι</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Hes. WD 547">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 547</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀὴρ πυροφόρος τέταται μακάρων ἐπὶ ἔργοις</quote> (rich men's fields): Theogn. 1077 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρφνη γὰρ τέταται</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 616B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 616B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ παντὸς τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς τεταμένον φῶς</quote> </cit>. As to the proposed substitution of <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλος</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρ</quote>, (with <quote xml:lang="grc">φάος</quote> retained,) it would be as violent as needless.—For </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ νὺξ ὀλοὴ τέταται δειλοῖσι
									βροτοῖσι

διὰ παντὸς τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς τεταμένον
									φῶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥίζας_599"> cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 2">Pind. <title>O.</title> 2. 46</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθεν σπέρματος ἔχοντα ῥίζαν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 765"> <title>El.</title> 765</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόρριζον...ἔφθαρται γένος</quote> </cit>: Lucian <ref n="Lucian Tyr. 13"> <title>Tyr.</title> 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανωλεθρία παντὸς τοῦ γένους καὶ ῥιζόθεν τὸ δεινὸν ἅπαν ἐκκεκομμένον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἀμᾷ_601"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">καταμᾷ αὖ</foreign>, ‘mows down in its turn’ (not, ‘otherwise than we hoped’). In my first edition I adopted the conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοπίς_601">. Prof. Tyrrell's able defence of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόνις_601"> (<title>Classical Review</title>, vol. 11. p. 139), though it has not removed all my difficulties, has led me to feel that more can be said for that reading than I had recognised. I now prefer, therefore, to leave </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόνις_601_2"> in the text, and to re-state here the arguments for and against it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόνις_601_3"> be right, <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνις θεῶν τῶν νερτέρων</quote> is the dust, belonging (due) to the gods infernal, which Antigone strewed on her brother's corpse; it is <quote xml:lang="grc">φοινία</quote>, because the corpse was gory. The strongest point in favour of <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνις</quote> is that it is in harmony with the following words, <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου τ’ ἄνοια καὶ φρενῶν ἐρινύς</quote>. The whole sense then is: ‘She, too—the last hope of the race—is now to die,—for a handful of blood-stained dust (i.e., for a slight, yet obligatory, act of piety towards her slain brother)—and for those rash words to Creon,—the expression of her frenzied resolve.’ On the other hand, the objection to <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνις</quote> is the verb </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταμᾷ_601">, which implies the metaphor of reaping. (See Appendix.) The proposed version, ‘covers,’ is impossible, and, if possible, would be unsuitable. What we want is a verb meaning simply ‘destroys,’ or ‘dooms to death.’ Now it is true that Greek lyric poetry often tolerates some confusion of metaphor (see on v. 117, and cp. <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref> p. lviii): the question is whether this example of it be tolerable. Prof. Tyrrell holds that it is excused by the tumult of feeling in the mind of the Chorus. That is, the metaphor of a young life ‘mowed down’ is not completed by a mention of the <emph>agent</emph>, the Destroyer: it is swiftly succeeded in the speaker's thought by a dramatic image of the <emph>cause</emph>, Antigone sprinkling the dust, and defying Creon. This is conceivable; but it is at least extremely bold.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοπίς_601_2">, then <quote xml:lang="grc">καταμᾷ</quote> is appropriate, and <quote xml:lang="grc">φοινία</quote> also has a more evident fitness. The great objection is the want of unison with <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου τ’ ἄνοια καὶ φρενῶν ἐρινύς</quote>. If the <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου</quote> means ‘both,’ the <quote xml:lang="grc">κοπὶς νερτέρων</quote> is the deadly agency as seen in the girl's rash speech and resolve: if the <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote> means ‘and,’ it is an agency to which these things are superadded. On either view the language is awkward. This must be set against the gain in unity of metaphor.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγου_ἄνοια_603">, folly shown in speech (defining gen.),—Antigone's answer to Creon (450 ff.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 562">cp. 562</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνουν</quote>), 383 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφροσύνῃ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενῶν_ἐρινύς_603">, an erinys of (or in) the mind: i.e. the infatuated <emph>impulse</emph> which urged Antigone to the deed is conceived as a Fury that drove her to her doom. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι οἰστρηθεῖσα ὑπὸ τῶν ἐρινύων...τοῦτο τετόλμηκεν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεάν_604">, epic and Ion. (Hom. , Hes. , Her., Pind. , etc.); admitted by Aesch. and Eur. in lyrics.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύνασιν_604">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 951">cp. 951</ref>. A poetical form used by Pind. , Eur. (in dial. as well as in lyr.), etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατάσχοι_604">. Epic usage admits the optat. (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>) where an abstract possibility is to be stated, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 19.321"> <title>Il.</title> 19.321</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὲν γάρ τι κακώτερον ἄλλο πάθοιμι</quote> </cit>, ‘for I could not (conceivably) suffer anything worse.’ The Homeric instances are chiefly in negative sentences (<ref n="Hom. Od. 3.231"> <title>Od.</title> 3.231</ref> being a rare exception, <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῖα θεός γ’ ἐθέλων καὶ τηλόθεν ἄνδρα σαώσαι</quote>). Attic verse affords some certain examples,—all in negative sentences, or in questions when (as here) a negative answer is expected. So <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 291">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 291</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ὅτῳ | μείζονα μοῖραν νείμαιμ’ ἢ σοί</quote> </cit>. Other instances are <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 172">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 172</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 595">Aesch. Ch. 595</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 620">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 620</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 52">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 52</ref>. Our passage is undoubtedly another genuine instance, and the attempts to alter it (see cr. n.) are mistaken. Attic prose, on the other hand, supplies no trustworthy example: in most of those which are alleged <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> should be supplied. I have discussed this question in <ref n="Soph. OC"> <title>O. C.</title> </ref>, Appendix on v. 170, p. 273.—Men may overstep their due limits: but no such <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερβασία</quote> can restrict the power of Zeus. He punishes the encroachment. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ μὲν γάρ τι κακώτερον ἄλλο πάθοιμι

οὐκ ἔστιν ὅτῳ | μείζονα μοῖραν νείμαιμ’ ἢ
									σοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντογήρως_606"> is unquestionably corrupt. Sleep, the renewer of vigour, could not be described as ‘bringing old age to all.’ Nor can the epithet be explained as ‘enfeebling all,’ in the sense of ‘subduing them’; nor, again, as ‘attending on all, even to old age.’ The neighbourhood of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγήρως</quote> is not in favour of <quote xml:lang="grc">παντογήρως</quote>, but against it; in the case of <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοπόρος—ἄπορος</quote> (360), and of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψίπολις—ἄπολις</quote> (370), there is a direct contrast between the two words. Either <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἀγρῶν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">πανταγρεὺς</quote> (see cr. n.) would be good, if <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’</quote> could be taken from the next verse, and added to this. But <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’</quote> clearly belongs, I think, to the next verse,—as will be seen presently. Bamberger proposed </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντοθήρας_606">, or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντόθηρος_606">. The former would be a subst. like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰχθυοθήρας</quote>, ‘fisherman,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρνιθοθήρας</quote>, ‘fowler’: the latter (which I should prefer), an adj. like <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύθηρος</quote>, ‘catching much’ (Heliodorus 5. 18), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔθηρος</quote>, ‘having good sport.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">παντόθηρος</quote> would suit the sense well. But its probability depends on the way in which we conceive the corrupt <quote xml:lang="grc">παντογήρως</quote> to have arisen. It is evident that the genuine <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγήρως</quote> in the next line had something to do with it. It seems most likely that the eye of the transcriber who first wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">παντογήρως</quote> had wandered to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγήρως</quote>, and that by a mere inadvertence he gave a like ending to the earlier word. Now this might most easily have happened if the sixth letter of the earlier series had been <quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote>, but would obviously have been less likely if that letter had been <quote xml:lang="grc">Θ</quote>. I therefore think it more probable that <quote xml:lang="grc">παντογήρως</quote> arose from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_ἀγρεύων_606"> than from <quote xml:lang="grc">παντόθηρος</quote>. It is immaterial that the last four letters of the latter are nearer to the MS., since, on the view just stated, the transcriber's error arose from the fact that the consecutive letters <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγ</quote> were common to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρεύων</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγήρως</quote>, and that, from these letters onwards, he accidentally copied <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγήρως</quote>. It may be added that such an error would have been easier with a separate word like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρεύων</quote> than with the second part of a compound like <quote xml:lang="grc">παντόθηρος</quote>.—The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρεύω</quote>, ‘to catch’ (common both in verse and in prose) is used by Soph. in fr. 507.— Soph. was thinking of <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.244"> <title>Il.</title> 14.244</ref> ff. (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ὕπνος</quote> speaking to Hera), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλον μέν κεν ἔγωγε θεῶν αἰειγενετάων | ῥεῖα κατευνήσαιμι... | Ζηνὸς δ’ οὐκ ἂν ἔγωγε Κρονίονος ἆσσον ἱκοίμην, | οὐδὲ κατευνήσαιμ’, ὅτε μὴ αὐτός γε κελεύοι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_θεῶν_607"> should answer metrically to 618 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδότι δ’</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_ἄκματοι_607"> (Hermann <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκμητοι</quote>). This supposes merely a transposition of two words, of which L affords undoubted instances (cp. on 107), and the very natural development of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκάματοι</quote> out of the rarer form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκματοι</quote>. For the latter cp. <title>Hom. hymn. Apoll.</title> 520 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκμήτοις δὲ λόφον προσέβαν ποσίν</quote>. The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_607"> seems to me clearly genuine. Many recent editors have condemned it, because <title>Zeus</title> is the marshaller of the seasons (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.134"> <title>Il.</title> 2.134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς μεγάλου ἐνιαυτοί</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.344"> <title>Od.</title> 24.344</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς ὧραι</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 321A">Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 321A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ἐκ Διὸς ὥρας</quote> </cit>). How, then, could the poet say that <title>Zeus</title> is not subdued by ‘the months <emph>of the gods</emph>’<emph>?</emph> The simple answer is that the term <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν</quote> is not opposed to Zeus, but includes him. Though Zeus (the Sky Father) was more especially the <quote xml:lang="grc">ταμίας ὡρῶν</quote>, that function can also be ascribed to the gods collectively: see e.g. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 886A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 886A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκοῦν, ὦ ξένε, δοκεῖ ῥᾴδιον εἷναι ἀληθεύοντας λέγειν ὡς εἰσὶ θεοί;— πῶς;—πρῶτον μὲν γῆ καὶ ἥλιος ἄστρα τε τὰ ξύμπαντα καὶ τὰ τῶν ὡρῶν διακεκοσμημένα καλῶς οὕτως, ἐνιαυτοῖς τε καὶ μησὶ διειλημμένα</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Laws 809d"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 809 D</ref>, as illustrating another reason which made the phrase </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκοῦν, ὦ ξένε, δοκεῖ ῥᾴδιον εἷναι ἀληθεύοντας
									λέγειν ὡς εἰσὶ θεοί;— πῶς;—πρῶτον μὲν γῆ καὶ ἥλιος ἄστρα τε τὰ
									ξύμπαντα καὶ τὰ τῶν ὡρῶν διακεκοσμημένα καλῶς οὕτως, ἐνιαυτοῖς
									τε καὶ μησὶ διειλημμένα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_μῆνες_607"> so natural—the fact, namely, that the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑορταί</quote> were the land-marks of the Calendar: <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνων δὴ πέρι λέγομεν; ἡμερῶν τάξεως εἰς μηνῶν περιόδους καὶ μηνῶν εἰς ἕκαστον τὸν ἐνιαυτόν, ἵνα ὧραι καὶ θυσίαι καὶ ἑορταὶ τὰ προσήκοντα ἀπολαμβάνουσαι ἑαυταῖς ἕκασται... θεοῖς μὲν τὰς τιμὰς ἀποδιδῶσι κ.τ.λ.</quote> And, if </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_607_2"> be genuine, then </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_607"> belongs to this verse, and we gain a fresh argument against those emendations which would append <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’</quote> to v. 606: for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ | ἀκάματοι θεῶν <νιν</quote>> is certainly not probable. See Appendix.—All the immortals have a <emph>life</emph> which is not worn out by those months which they themselves control. The distinction of Zeus is that his <emph>supremacy</emph> over gods and men is unalterable.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκματοι_607">, untiring in their course: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 18.239"> <title>Il.</title> 18.239</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠέλιον δ’ ἀκάμαντα</quote> </cit>: Eur. fr. 597 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκάμας τε χρόνος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνῳ_608"> could be instrumental or causal here (‘not made old <emph>by</emph> time’). It rather seems to be an adverbial dat. of circumstance, ‘not growing old with time’ (as time goes on). <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ</quote> oft.=‘at length’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 437"> <title>O. C.</title> 437</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαρμαρόεσσαν_608"> (only here) =<quote xml:lang="grc">μαρμαρέαν. μαρμαίρω</quote> and its cognate adj. are applied to any <emph>sparkling</emph> or <emph>flashing</emph> light (as of sun or stars, bright eyes, gleaming metal). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.532"> <title>Il.</title> 1.532</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπ’ αἰγλήεντος Ὀλύμπου</quote> </cit>. Blackwall A. compares the language of St Paul in <cit> <bibl n="1 Timothy 6.15">1 Tim. 6. 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μακάριος καὶ μόνος δυνάστης...φῶς οἰκῶν ἀπρόσιτον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὁ μακάριος καὶ μόνος δυνάστης...φῶς οἰκῶν
									ἀπρόσιτον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_ἔπειτα_611"> (acc. of duration) is what will immediately follow the <emph>present</emph> moment (cp. <ref n="Plat. Parm. 152c">Plat. <title>Parm.</title> 152</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ τε νῦν καὶ τοῦ ἔπειτα</quote>), and is here distinguished from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μέλλον_611">, the <emph>more distant</emph> future; <cit> <bibl n="Pl. Per. 5.2">Plaut. <title>Pers.</title> 778</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" xml:lang="eng">(quoted by Schneid.)</note> qui sunt, quique erunt <note anchored="true" place="inline">(<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔπειτα</quote>)</note>, quique fuerunt, quique futuri sunt posthac <note anchored="true" place="inline">(<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μέλλον</quote>)</note> </quote> </cit>. It is much as if we said, ‘to-morrow, and for all time.’ Many have compared <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1263">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1263</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τά τε πρῶτα | τά τ’ ἔπειθ’ ἅ τ’ ἔμελλε τυχεῖν</quote> </cit>: but even if Seidler's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅ τ’</quote>, rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσ’</quote>, be there the true correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσα τ’</quote>, the parallelism is not strict, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἔπειτα</quote> would then mean ‘what followed <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πρῶτα</quote>,’ not, ‘what is to follow <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν</quote>.’</gloss>

data.quote:
qui sunt, quique erunt  , quique fuerunt, quique futuri
									sunt posthac

τά τε πρῶτα | τά τ’ ἔπειθ’ ἅ τ’ ἔμελλε
									τυχεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_πρίν_611"> is usu. explained as a compressed form of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ καὶ τὸ πρὶν ἐπήρκεσε</quote>: but this is at least much bolder than the examples which are brought to support it, as <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.31">Dem. or. 18 § 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τότε καὶ νῦν καὶ ἀεὶ ὁμολογῶ</quote> </cit>, which would be parallel only if it were <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν καὶ ἀεὶ καὶ τότε ὁμολογῶ</quote>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν τε καὶ πάλαι δοκεῖ</quote> (181) is irrelevant, since <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλαι</quote> can take the pres. (279). Rather, perh., </gloss>

data.quote: καὶ τότε καὶ νῦν καὶ ἀεὶ ὁμολογῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαρκέσει_611">, ‘will hold good,’ means, ‘will be found true,’—both in the future, and if we scan the past.—For <quote xml:lang="grc">το—</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">πρίν</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 180"> <title>O. C.</title> 180</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι—; προβίβαζε. <emph>ἐπαρκέσει</emph> </quote> </cit>, will hold out, hold good,=<quote xml:lang="grc">διαρκέσει</quote>: so only here, perhaps, for in Solon fr. 5. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">δήμῳ μὲν γὰρ ἔδωκα τόσον κράτος ὅσσον ἐπαρκεῖ</quote>, we must surely read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρκεῖ</quote>, with Coraës. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάμπολύ_γ_613"> (Heath), for <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολις</quote>, is not only the best emendation, but (in my belief) a certain one. I do not know whether it has been noticed that <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολϋν</quote> in one of the late MSS. (see cr. n.), —a mere blunder for <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολις</quote>,—forcibly illustrates the ease with which the opposite change of <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολύ γ’</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολις</quote> could have occurred. The <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος</quote>, then, is:— ‘Nothing <emph>vast</emph> comes to (enters into) the life of mortals, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote>, free from a curse (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω...αἰτίας</quote>, 445)’—without bringing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 531D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 531D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολυ ἔργον</quote> </cit>, <title>Legg.</title> 823 B <quote xml:lang="grc">πάμπολύ τι πρᾶγμα</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 677e"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 677 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς δ’ ἀφθόνου πλῆθος πάμπολυ</quote> </cit>. Too much power, or wealth, or prosperity —anything so great as to be <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κατ’ ἄνθρωπον</quote>—excites the divine <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόνος</quote>: the man shows <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote>, and this brings <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.10">Her. 7.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς τὰ ὑπερέχοντα ζῷα ὡς κεραυνοῖ ὁ θεὸς οὐδὲ ἐᾷ φαντάζεσθαι, τὰ δὲ σμικρὰ οὐδέν μιν κνίζει; ὁρᾷς δὲ ὡς ἐς οἰκήματα τὰ μέγιστα αἰεὶ καὶ δένδρεα τὰ τοιαῦτα ἀποσκήπτει τὰ βέλεα; φιλέει γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τὰ ὑπερέχοντα πάντα κολούειν</quote> </cit>. L. <ref n="D. L. 1.3.2">Diog. 1. 3. 2</ref>(Zeus) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν ὑψηλὰ ταπεινῶν, τὰ δὲ ταπεινὰ ὑψῶν</quote>. Soph. fr. 320 <quote xml:lang="grc">καλὸν φρονεῖν τὸν θνητὸν ἀνθρώποις ἴσα.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὁρᾷς τὰ ὑπερέχοντα ζῷα ὡς κεραυνοῖ ὁ θεὸς οὐδὲ
									ἐᾷ φαντάζεσθαι, τὰ δὲ σμικρὰ οὐδέν μιν κνίζει; ὁρᾷς δὲ ὡς ἐς
									οἰκήματα τὰ μέγιστα αἰεὶ καὶ δένδρεα τὰ τοιαῦτα ἀποσκήπτει τὰ
									βέλεα; φιλέει γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τὰ ὑπερέχοντα πάντα κολούειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρπει_613">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1087"> <title>Ai.</title> 1087</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρπει παραλλὰξ ταῦτα</quote> </cit> (come to men): for the dat., cp. above, 186. The inf. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρπειν_613"> would be admissible after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote>, since this is not a precept (like <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ πλουτεῖν ἀδίκως</quote>), but a statement of fact. In 706 L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote> by mistake for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote>, and such errors are frequent. And <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖν</quote> in 622 might seem to recommend <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρπειν</quote> here. Yet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρπει_613_2"> seems right. For this is not what the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος</quote> <emph>says</emph>,—as <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖν</quote> in 622 depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπος πέφανται</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσαντι παθεῖν</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 313">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 313</ref> on <quote xml:lang="grc">μῦθος...φωνεῖ</quote>. The constant fact, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἕρπει</quote>, <emph>is</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 435"> <title>Ph.</title> 435</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγῳ δέ σ’ ἐν βραχεῖ | τοῦτ’ ἐκδιδάξω· πόλεμος οὐδέν’ ἄνδρ’ ἑκὼν | αἱρεῖ πονηρόν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάμπολις_613"> is impossible. For the attempts to explain it, and for other conjectures, see Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_δή_615"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_615"> introduces an explanation of the law just stated. ‘No inordinate desire comes to men without bringing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote>. <title>For</title> hope, which can be a blessing, can also be a curse, by luring a man to pursue forbidden things; and then he sins blindly, till the gods strike him. The gods cause him to mistake evil for good; and his impunity is of short duration.’ Creon is destined to exemplify this. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύπλαγκτος_615">, roaming widely—as a mariner over unknown seas—in dreams of the future. Soph. was perh. thinking of <ref n="Pind. O. 12">Pind. <title>O.</title> 12. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵ γε μὲν ἀνδρῶν | πόλλ’ ἄνω, τὰ δ’ αὖ κάτω ψεύδη μεταμώνια τάμνοισαι κυλίνδοντ’ ἐλπίδες</quote>, ‘at least, the hopes of men are</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλοῖς_ὄνασις_616">, by cheering them, and inciting to worthy effort. This clause is inserted merely for the sake of contrast with the next. When Greek idiom thus co-ordinates two clauses, the clause which we should subordinate to the other is that which has <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>; as here, ‘<emph>though</emph> a blessing to many.’ So <ref n="Soph. OC 1536"> <title>O. C.</title> 1536</ref> (n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ μὲν ὀψέ δ’</quote>, ‘late, though surely.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάτα_ἐρώτων_617">. The gen. is best taken as subjective, a cheating (of men) by desires; i.e., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτες ἀπατῶσι</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίς</quote> <emph>is</emph> such an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάτη</quote>, because it ends in that. If the gen. were objective, the sense would be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπὶς ἀπατᾷ ἔρωτας</quote>. This is equally possible, but hardly so natural. In 630, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάτας λεχέων</quote>, the gen. is neither of these, but one of relation (a deceiving of him about his marriage). Cp. <ref n="Hes. WD 460">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 460</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεωμένη οὔ σ’ ἀπατήσει</quote>, when ploughed again, the soil will not disappoint thee.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κουφονόων_617">: <ref target="cl343">see on 343</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρπει_618">, creeps on him, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_εἰδότι_618">, knowing nothing. Others construe, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἕρπει εἰδότι</quote>, nothing comes to him aware of it; i.e. he understands the true meaning of nothing that happens to him. This is somewhat forced; and that <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote> is object to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδότι</quote> is confirmed by Antiphon or. 1 § 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ ἐπιβουλευόμενοι οὐδὲν ἴσασι πρὶν ἐν αὐτῷ ὦσι τῷ κακῷ γ’ ἤδη</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 964"> <title>Ai.</title> 964</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀγαθὸν χεροῖν | ἔχοντες οὐκ ἴσασι, πρίν τις ἐκβάλῃ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τἀγαθὸν χεροῖν | ἔχοντες οὐκ ἴσασι, πρίν τις
									ἐκβάλῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_προσαύσῃ_619">. Attic, like epic, poetry can use simple <quote xml:lang="grc">πρίν</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἄν</quote> (308), with subjunct.: so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 965"> <title>Ai.</title> 965</ref> (see last n.), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 917"> <title>Ph.</title> 917</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 608"> <title>Tr.</title> 608</ref>, 946, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσαύσῃ_619"> (only here), ‘burn against.’ The simple verb occurs <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 5.490"> <title>Od.</title> 5.490</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα μή ποθεν ἄλλοθεν αὔοι</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῦρ</quote>), ‘kindle.’ Attic had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναύω</quote>, ‘kindle,’ and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαύω</quote>, ‘parch.’ The image here seems to be that of a man who walks, in fancied security, over ashes under which fire still smoulders (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lucr. 4.927">Lucr. 4. 927</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">cinere ut multo latet obrutus ignis</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 2.1.7">Hor. c. 2. 1. 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">incedis per ignes Suppositos cineri doloso</quote> </cit>). There was a prov., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πυρὶ βέβηκας</quote> (Suidas, etc.).</gloss>

data.quote: cinere ut multo latet obrutus ignis

incedis per ignes Suppositos cineri
									doloso

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόδα_619">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 697">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 697</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω κομίζων ὀλεθρίου πηλοῦ πόδα</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 263">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πημάτων ἔξω πόδα | ἔχει</quote> (and so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1260">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1260</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς κλαυμάτων</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 109">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 109</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω πραγμάτων</quote> </cit>).—Some render </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσαύσῃ_619_2"> ‘bring to,’ assuming an <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔω</quote> equiv. in sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρω</quote>: but the evidence for this is doubtful: see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοφίᾳ_620">, modal dat.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">σοφῶς</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 233"> <title>El.</title> 233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνοίᾳ γ’ αὐδῶ</quote> </cit>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 405"> <title>O. T.</title> 405</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμῷ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 659"> <title>O. C.</title> 659</ref>), etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκ_του_620">, i.e. by some wise man of olden time:—not like the <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔφα τις</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 369">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 369</ref> (alluding to Diagoras). Cp. frag. adesp. 383 (schol. on <ref n="Soph. Trach. 296"> <title>Tr.</title> 296</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τοῦτο τοὔπος ἐστὶν ἀνδρὸς ἔμφρονος, | ὅταν καλῶς πράσσῃ τις, ἐλπίζειν κακά</quote>. For similar <quote xml:lang="grc">γνῶμαι</quote> in tragic lyrics, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 750">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 750</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 313."> <title>Ch.</title> 313.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεφάνται_620">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1"> <title>Tr.</title> 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος μὲν ἔστ’ ἀρχαῖος ἀνθρώπων φανείς</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. OT 525"> <title>O. T.</title> 525</ref>, 848. </gloss>

data.quote: λόγος μὲν ἔστ’ ἀρχαῖος ἀνθρώπων φανείς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποτ_622"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The sense of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_622"> here is not ‘sometimes,’ but ‘at one time or another,’ ‘at length,’ as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1041"> <title>Ph.</title> 1041</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τῷ χρόνῳ ποτέ</quote>. A moment arrives when he makes the fatal error. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀάω</quote>), as the heaven-sent influence that leads men to sin, is properly ‘hurt done to the mind.’ Milton, <title>Samson</title> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμμεν_622"> (used also by Pind. and Sappho) occurs nowhere else in tragedy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλίγιστον_625">, a superl. used not only in epic poetry but also by Attic writers (as Ar. and Plat. ), is right here. The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλιγοστόν_625"> cannot be defended by <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Peace 559">Aristoph. <title>Pax</title> 559</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοστῷ χρόνῳ</quote> </cit>, which is merely another form of <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοστῷ ἔτει</quote> (Cratinus jun. <quote xml:lang="grc">Χείρ</quote>. 1); i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοστός</quote> has its proper sense, ‘one of many’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">multesimus</foreign>), and the <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνος</quote>, like the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτος</quote>, is conceived as the last of a series. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλιγοστὸς χρόνος</quote> would mean, not, ‘a fraction of time,’ but, ‘one in a small number of <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνοι</quote>’ or periods. In <ref n="Aristot. Met. 9.1045b.25">Arist. <title>Metaph.</title> 9. 1. 14</ref> most MSS., and the best, have <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλίγιστον...χρόνον</quote>: while Ab (cod. Laur. 87. 12) is the only MS. cited in the Berlin ed. (p. 1053 <emph>a</emph> 9) for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλιγοστόν</quote>. And otherwise <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλιγοστός</quote> occurs only in later Greek, as <title>Anton.</title> 51 <quote xml:lang="grc">καταβὰς ὀλιγοστός</quote>, ‘having gone to the coast <emph>with a small retinue</emph>’; <ref n="Plut. Caes. 49">Caes. 49</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλιγοστῷ τοσαύτην ἀμυνομένῳ πόλιν</quote> ‘fighting so great a State <emph>with a small force.</emph>’— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσει_ἄτας_625">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσει καλῶς</quote>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν κατὰ νοῦν</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Rep. 366B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 366B</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 549">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 549</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτας_625"> is here ‘calamity’ (as in 584, 614), while in the last verse <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄταν</quote> is rather ‘infatuation.’—Donaldson changed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτας</quote> here to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγους</quote>, because the strophe (614) also ends with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote>. On the other hand Dindorf ejects <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote> from 614 (leaving a lacuna). But I believe <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote> to be genuine in both places, as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἕρπει</quote> also is both in 613 and in 618. We have to remember, first, that Soph. (like other ancient poets) easily tolerated repetition of words (see on <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title> 554</ref>); secondly, that tragic lyrics could admit refrains, and might, by a kindred instinct, permit such verbal echoes as these. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήν_626"> instead of the usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μήν</quote> (526).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέατον_626">, ‘youngest and last,’ Megareus being dead (1303): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 807">cp. 807</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν νεάταν ὁδόν</quote>; so 808, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1185"> <title>Ai.</title> 1185</ref>. As applied</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀχνύμενος_626"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόρον_626"> as internal acc.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.361"> <title>Il.</title> 5.361</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχθομαι ἔλκος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλιδος_628">: but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_μελλογάμου_628"> should be retained. Except in the lexicons, <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶλις</quote> occurs only here and in a verse of Callimachus, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτίκα τὴν τᾶλιν παιδὶ σὺν ἀμφιθαλεῖ</quote>, quoted by the Schol., who says, <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶλις λέγεται παρ’ Αἰολεῦσιν ἡ ὀνομασθεῖσά τινι νύμφη</quote>. Hesychius has, <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶλις· ἡ μελλόγαμος παρθένος καὶ κατωνομασμένη τινί· οἱ δὲ γυναῖκα γαμετήν· οἱ δὲ νύμφην</quote>. This shows that <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶλις</quote> could mean, not only an affianced bride, but also a bride after marriage: just as <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφη</quote> can mean either. The epithet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_μελλογάμου_628_2"> is not, then, superfluous; and <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς μελλονύμφου</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 633">633</ref> is no argument against it. On the other hand </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλιδος_628_2">, without the epithet, would have a crude effect. A passage in Pollux (3. 45) has been taken to prove that he had <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς μελλογάμου</quote> in his text. It does not prove this,—nor the reverse. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς μελλογάμου</quote> in Pollux should be (as Semitelos saw) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μελλόγαμον</quote>, and we should refer his words solely to <ref n="Soph. Ant. 633">v. 633</ref>. His point is simply that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μελλόνυμφος</quote> is more correct than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μελλονύμφη</quote>.—Curtius connects <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶλις</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τέρ‐ην</quote>, tender; <quote xml:lang="grc">θρόνα</quote>, flowers: Sanskrit <foreign xml:lang="sanskrit">ta/r-una-s</foreign>, youthful, tender, <foreign xml:lang="sanskrit">ta/l-unI</foreign>, girl, young woman. He supposes the first idea to be that of a plant sprouting or blossoming (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλος</quote>). This at least agrees well with what we know as to the usage of <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶλις</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάτας_630"> (gen. sing.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λεχέων_630">, a deceit practised on <*> disappointment, in regard to his <*>. The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">λεχέων</quote> is one of relation <*>ed, perhaps, by the idea of privation <*> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάτη</quote> were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστέρησις</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάντεων_ὑπέρτερον_631"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">βέλτιον ἢ μάντεις ἴσασιν</foreign> (better, therefore, than they could tell <*> Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος παροιμιακῶς, ὁπότε μὴ <*>σμῷ χρώμεθα, ἀλλ’ αὐτόπται τῶν πρ<*> γινόμεθα</quote>. <title>H. F.</title> 911 <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΓ. <*> δόμοισι.—ΧΟ. μάντιν οὐχ ἕτερον <*></quote>. ‘I will not bring a seer, other than <*>self’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 6"> <title>O. T.</title> 6</ref>), i.e. ‘I need no <*> tell me that’:— imitated by the <*> of the <title>Rhesus</title> 949 <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφιστὴν δ’ ἄλλον <*>τάξομαι</quote>, who also has 952 <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾔδη <*>δὲν μάντεως ἔδει φράσαι</quote>. Cp. <title>O. </title><<title>*></title> <*>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελείαν_632"> He announces that he will not yield.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψῆφο_632"> 60.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρα_μή_632">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μῶν</quote>, ‘can it be <*>’ <ref n="Soph. El. 446"> <title>El.</title> 446</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_μελλονύμφου_632">: for <*> gen., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.140">Thuc. 1.140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Μεγαρέων <*>ισυα</quote> </cit>, and n. on 11.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυσσαίνων_632">, the <*>ing of the MSS., is a word not extant e<*>where, but as correctly formed as <quote xml:lang="grc"><*> χαλεπαίνω</quote>, etc. At first sight it <*> strong: <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσσα</quote> is ‘raving.’ But <*>ain vehemence of language characte<*> Creon (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 280">cp. 280</ref> ff.). Instead of saying <*>, ‘have you come here in displeas<*> he says, ‘have you come here to sto<*> me?’ As </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_μέν_632"> shows, there is a <*> contrast with the sisters: he had des<*> Ismene as <quote xml:lang="grc">λυσσῶσαν</quote> (492). I therefore think <quote xml:lang="grc">λυσσαίνων</quote> genuine, and a finer reading than the variant noted in L, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμαίνων_632">. The latter word is used by Hesiod, and in Attic comedy. Some recent edd. place it in the text. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_μέν_634">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 498">cp. 498</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανταχῇ_δρῶντες_634">, ‘however I may act.’ <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1369"> <title>Ai.</title> 1369</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἂν ποιήσῃς, πανταχῇ χρηστός γ’ ἔσει</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.27">Her. 9.27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντῃ γὰρ τεταγμένοι</quote> </cit> (wherever we may be posted) <quote xml:lang="grc">πειρησόμεθα εἶναι χρηστοί</quote>: id. 8. 110 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντως ἕτοιμοι ἦσαν λέγοντι πείθεσθαι</quote> (‘ready in every case’). </gloss>

data.quote: ὡς ἂν ποιήσῃς, πανταχῇ χρηστός γ’ ἔσει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σός_635">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1323"> <title>O. C.</title> 1323</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμας_χρηστάς_635">, having good counsels, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπορθοῖς_635"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτάς</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_635">, thou settest them before me as rules. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορθόω</quote> (a rare word) means, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπευθύνω</quote>, (1) ‘to straighten out.’ and then (2) ‘to guide in a straight course.’ <ref n="Plat. Laws 757E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 757E</ref> (praying the gods) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορθοῦν τὸν κλῆρον πρὸς τὸ δικαιότατον</quote>, ‘to direct the lot (for magistracies) in the best interests of justice.’ Here the <quote xml:lang="grc">γνῶμαι</quote> are the <quote xml:lang="grc">κανόνες</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">regulae</foreign>, which are to guide the youth's course: cp. fr. 430 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε τέκτονος | παρὰ στάθμην ἰόντος ὀρθοῦται κανών</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 52">Eur. <title>El.</title> 52</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμης πονηροῖς κανόσιν ἀναμετρούμενος | τὸ σῶφρον</quote> </cit>.—Others understand: (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορ<*> γνώμας μοι</quote>, thou guidest <emph>my</emph> views, <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>στὰς ἔχων</quote>, having good views (of thine own). Or (2) ‘Having good views, thou guidest me,’ supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπορθοῖς</quote> (like <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 104"> <title>O. T.</title> 104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπευθύνειν πόλιν</quote> </cit>). But <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> would then be awkward. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pl. Trin. 2.2">Plaut. <title>Trin.</title> 304</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" xml:lang="eng">(a son to his father)</note> sarta tecta tua praecepta usque habui mea modestia.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
γνώμης πονηροῖς κανόσιν ἀναμετρούμενος | τὸ
									σῶφρον

sarta tecta tua praecepta usque habui mea
									modestia.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξιώσεται_637">, pass.; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 210">cp. 210</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 581"> <title>O. C.</title> 581</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλώσεται</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 672"> <title>O. T.</title> 672</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγήσεται</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξ_φέρεσθαι_637">, will be <emph>esteemed</emph> more important to win (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 439">cp. 439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσσω λαβεῖν</quote>): so <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 161">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 161</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε καὶ ἄλλων διδάσκαλος ἀξιοῦσθαι δικαίως</quote>, ‘to be justly ranked as a teacher.’ The same use is implied in <title>Legg.</title> 917 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπόσης ἂν τιμῆς ἀξιώσῃ τὸ πωλούμενον</quote> (at whatever price he may value...).—L's reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξίως_ἔσται_637">, though tenable, seems slightly less probable, when we observe that this adv. is regularly used either (<emph>a</emph>) with gen., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίως ἑαυτῶν</quote>, etc., or (<emph>b</emph>) absol., in such phrases as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.40">Thuc. 3.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κολάσατε...ἀξίως τούτους</quote> </cit>, ‘according to their deserts.’ (So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 133"> <title>O. T.</title> 133</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίως</quote> </cit>=‘as the case required.’) Thus we could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος ὁ γάμος ἀξίως ἔσται μείζων φ</quote>., ‘will <emph>deservedly</emph> (=on its merits) be a greater prize.’ But it is less natural to say, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς γάμος ἀξίως ἔσται μ. φ.</quote>, ‘no marriage will <emph>rightly</emph> be preferred,’ etc., where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίως</quote> becomes a mere equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίως</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">προσηκόντως</quote>. The change of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιώσεται</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίως ἔσται</quote> would have been the easier, since the ordinary fut. was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιωθήσομαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_ἡγουμ_637">, (with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μείζων_637">), than thy good guiding: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.34">Her. 1.34</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετὰ δὲ Σόλωνα οἰχόμενον</quote> </cit>. —It is a mistake (I think) to detect a mental reserve in the participle (‘than thy guiding, <emph>if</emph>, or <emph>when</emph>, it is good’). Haemon knows that his one chance of saving Antigone is first to mollify his father, and then to urge the argument from public opinion (688 ff.). His deference is unqualified. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάρ_639"> in assent (<ref n="Soph. OT 1117"> <title>O. T.</title> 1117</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_ἔχειν_639">, lit., ‘to be disposed in one's breast,’=<quote xml:lang="grc">φρονεῖν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">διακεῖσθαι</quote>. The phrase differs in two points from others which seem like it. (1) The gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διά_639"> in such phrases regularly denotes a state or act of the mind, whereas <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρνων</quote> represents the mind itself. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχειν_639"> in such phrases is always trans., the intrans. verb being <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω τινά</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ αἰσχύνης, αἰτίας, ὀργῆς, φυλακῆς</quote>, etc. But <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμὶ δῑ ἡσυχίης</quote> (<ref n="Hdt. 1.206">Her. 1.206</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ φόβου</quote> (<ref n="Thuc. 6.59">Thuc. 6.59</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ ὄχλου</quote> (<ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 888">Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 888</ref>). Here </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτω_639">, going with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχειν_639_2">, shows that the verb is intrans.,—not trans., with <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> understood. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄπισθεν_γνώμης_640">, one ought to place oneself under the guidance of a father's counsel, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_640">, in all things (adv. neut. pl., <ref n="Soph. OT 1197"> <title>O. T.</title> 1197</ref> etc.). Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστάναι</quote> depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote>,—the indefinite subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τινά</quote>) being continued with it; and the whole clause explains <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω</quote>. The image from a soldier posted behind his leader suits the military tone in which Creon presently enforces the value of discipline (670). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 471D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 471D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε καὶ ἐν τῇ αὐτῇ τάξει εἴτε καὶ ὄπισθεν ἐπιτεταγμένον</quote> </cit>. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄπισθεν ἑστάναι γνώμης</quote> is a poetical equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκολουθεῖν τῇ γνώμῃ</quote> (<ref n="Thuc. 3.38">Thuc. 3.38</ref>).—We could also render,—‘that all things rank second to a father's will’: when </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴτε καὶ ἐν τῇ αὐτῇ τάξει εἴτε καὶ ὄπισθεν
									ἐπιτεταγμένον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστάναι_640"> would depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ στέρνων ἔχειν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">νομίζειν</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστάναι</quote> applies to the <quote xml:lang="grc">τάξις</quote> of persons more naturally than to the estimation of things: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.27">Her. 9.27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα δοκέει ἐπιτηδεότατον ἡμέας εἶναι ἑστάναι</quote> </cit> (in battle): and the constr. is also less simple. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱστάναι</quote> (which Musgrave proposed) would suit that view better. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἵνα δοκέει ἐπιτηδεότατον ἡμέας εἶναι
									ἑστάναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_643"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, as 760, <ref n="Soph. OT 359"> <title>O. T.</title> 359</ref>, and oft.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνταμύνωνται_643">, a neutral word: thus <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.67">Thuc. 2.67</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς αὐτοῖς ἀμύνεσθαι</quote> </cit>, to retaliate; but 1. 42 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ὁμοίοις ἡμᾶς ἀμύνεσθαι</quote>, to reward: here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῖς_643"> defines it.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐξ ἴσου πατρί</emph> = ὥσπερ ὁ πατήρ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 516">cp. 516</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 171"> <title>O. C.</title> 171</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστοῖς ἴσα χρὴ μελετᾶν</quote> </cit>.—The son's part is <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς αὐτοὺς ἐχθροὺς καὶ φίλους νομίζειν</quote>,—the definition of a <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμμαχία</quote> as dist. from a merely defensive <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμαχία</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 1.44">Thuc. 1.44</ref>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 523">Cp. 523</ref> n.: <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title>P.</title> 2. 83</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλον εἴη φιλεῖν· | ποτὶ δ’ ἐχθρὸν ἅτ’ ἐχθρὸς ἐὼν λύκοιο δίκαν ὑποθεύσομαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλο_646"> is most simply taken as governed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φῦσαι_646">, though, if we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote> instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλήν_646">, Greek idiom would rather lead us to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιῆσαι</quote>: <ref target="cl497">see on 497</ref>, and cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 125"> <title>Ai.</title> 125</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ὄντας ἄλλο πλὴν | εἵδωλ</quote> </cit>'. This is better than to make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλο_646_2"> object to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴποις_646"> (‘what could one say of him,’ etc.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνους_646">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 533">cp. 533</ref>.—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέλων_646">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1153"> <title>El.</title> 1153</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γελῶσι δ’ ἔχθροι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 79"> <title>Ai.</title> 79</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν γέλως ἥδιστος εἰς ἐχθροὺς γελᾶν</quote> </cit>; 961 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ οὖν γελώντων κἀπιχαιρόντων κακοῖς</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὔκουν γέλως ἥδιστος εἰς ἐχθροὺς γελᾶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_γ_648">. Recent edd. have usually scorned the simple insertion of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_648">, by which Triclinius healed the metre. But it should be noticed that <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> may emphasise <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς φρένας ἐκβάλης</quote>, and not merely <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς φρένας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 747">cp. 747</ref>: <title>O.C.</title> 1278 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θεοῦ γε προστάτην</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> emphasises the whole phrase, not merely the word <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῦ</quote>. The deprecatory force of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>, as seen in <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σύ γε</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1441"> <title>O. C.</title> 1441</ref> n.), also recommends it, even when we have not <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 503">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 503</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μή γε πρὸς θεῶν, εὖ λέγεις γάρ, αἰσχρὰ δέ, | πέρα προβῇς τῶνδ’</quote> </cit>. Without, then, thinking <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρένας γ’</quote> </emph> certain, I think it far more probable than the next best remedy, </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μή γε πρὸς θεῶν, εὖ λέγεις γάρ, αἰσχρὰ δέ,
									| πέρα προβῇς τῶνδ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρένας_ἡδονῇ_648">. As to a third conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ γ’ ἡδονῆς</quote>, the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕνεκα ἡδονῆς γυναικός</quote> (pleasure in her) would be very awkward. Some strange emendations have been proposed: see Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρένας_ἐκβάλῃς_648">, cast off the restraint of reason, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 611"> <title>O. T.</title> 611</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλον... ἐσθλὸν ἐκβαλεῖν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 631"> <title>O. C.</title> 631</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐμένειαν ἐκβάλοι</quote> </cit> (reject friendship). The first idea is that of <emph>casting out</emph> of house or land, banishing. Somewhat similar is <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 46B">Plat. <title>Crito</title> 46B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δὲ λόγους, οὓς ἐν τῷ ἔμπροσθεν ἔλεγον, οὐ δύναμαι νῦν ἐκβαλεῖν</quote> </cit> (reject). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 683">Cp. 683</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς δὲ λόγους, οὓς ἐν τῷ ἔμπροσθεν ἔλεγον, οὐ
									δύναμαι νῦν ἐκβαλεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφ_ἡδονῆς_648">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 382"> <title>Ai.</title> 382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ που πολὺν γέλωθ’ ὑφ’ ἡδονῆς ἄγεις</quote> </cit>. Here the word denotes sensuous impulse: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 21">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονῇ δούς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.38">Thuc. 3.38</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκοῆς ἡδονῇ ἡσσώμενοι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἦ που πολὺν γέλωθ’ ὑφ’ ἡδονῆς ἄγεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχρόν_650">, frigid, joyless: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 353">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 353</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχρὰν μέν, οἶμαι, τέρψιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραγκάλισμα_650">: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 540"> <title>Tr.</title> 540</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαγκάλισμα</quote> </cit> (of a wife); and so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγκάλισμα, ἐναγκάλισμα</quote>. The neuter gives a contemptuous tone. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 320">Cp. 320</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λάλημα</quote>, 756 <quote xml:lang="grc">δούλευμα</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 928">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 928</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄνδον οἰκουρήματα</quote> </cit> (of women), <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1439">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1439</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Χρυσηΐδων μείλιγμα</quote> </cit> (Agamemnon). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_651">. For the full stop after the 5th foot cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 800"> <title>O. T.</title> 800</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕλκος_651">, esp. an ulcer; said in <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.723"> <title>Il.</title> 2.723</ref> of a serpent's venomous bite; hence fitting here in ref. to the false friend, the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχιδνα</quote> in the house (531). So civil strife (<quote xml:lang="grc">στάσις ἔμφυλος</quote>) is described by Solon as <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσῃ πόλει...ἕλκος ἄφυκτον</quote> (4. 17).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλος_651"> is any one near and dear to us; the masc. is used, though the reference is to a wife, because the thought of domestic treason is put in the most general way: so (though with ref. to a woman) 464 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατθανών</quote>, 496 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλούς</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 355">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 355</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδὺ γὰρ φίλους | κἀν νυκτὶ λεύσσειν, ὅντιν’ ἂν παρῇ χρόνον</quote> </cit> (Admetus speaking of his wife: we might read <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος</quote>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡδὺ γὰρ φίλους | κἀν νυκτὶ λεύσσειν, ὅντιν’ ἂν
									παρῇ χρόνον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτύσας_653">, with loathing: <ref n="Aesch. PB 1069">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 1069</ref> (speaking of treason) <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐκ ἔστι vόσος | τῆσδ’ ἥντιν’ ἀπέπτυσα μᾶλλον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠσεί_δυσμενῆ_653"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσαν</quote>), <emph>and</emph> as if she were a foe. For <quote xml:lang="grc">πτύσας</quote> connected by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_653"> with an adj. in a different case, see n. on 381 <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ γ’ ἀπιστοῦσαν...ἄγουσι...καὶ...καθελόντες</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. El. 234"> <title>El.</title> 234</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτηρ ὡσεί τις πιστά</quote>: but nowhere in Attic poetry do we find the epic and lyric use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡσεί τε</quote> as merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡσεί</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 2.780"> <title>Il.</title> 2.780</ref>, <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. 1. 44</ref>, etc.). And, as we have seen, it is needless to assume it here. Yet supposed difficulties about <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡσεί τε</quote> have led Nauck to propose that vv. 652—654 should be made into two, thus: <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτ’ ἂν ἕλκος μεῖζον; ἀλλ’ ἀποπτύσας | τὴν παῖδ’ ἐν Ἅιδου τήνδε νυμφεύειν μέθες</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυμφεύειν_654"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">γαμεῖσθαι</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">nubere</foreign>, as 816. But it also=<quote xml:lang="grc">γαμεῖν</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">uxorem ducere:</foreign> <ref n="Eur. IA 461">Eur. <title>I.A.</title> 461</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδης νιν, ὡς ἔοικε, νυμφεύσει τάχα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπιστήσασαν_656">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 219">cp. 219</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψευδῆ_656">: referring to his solemn and public declaration, 184—210. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτενῶ_658">. For the emphatic pause, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 72">cp. 72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θάψω</quote>, and n. on 46.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ταῦτ_658">', after an announcement of resolve, and before a defiant imperative, as <ref n="Soph. OT 426"> <title>O. T.</title> 426</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 455"> <title>O. C.</title> 455</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 820"> <title>El.</title> 820</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 992">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 992</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 959">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 959</ref> etc. Similarly <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς οὖν τάδε</quote>, <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 1030">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1030</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφυμνείτω_658">, repeatedly invoke (a scornful word): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1305">cp. 1305</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 1275"> <title>O. T.</title> 1275</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δία_ξύναιμον_658">: <ref target="cl487">see on 487</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_φύσει_659">, those who, by birth, are relatives: for the place of the adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσει</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 792"> <title>El.</title> 792</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θανόντος ἀρτίως</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 216">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 216</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν παρεστώτων τότε</quote> </cit>. For the neut., instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐγγενεῖς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 448"> <title>Ph.</title> 448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν πανοῦργα καὶ παλιντριβῆ...τὰ δὲ | δίκαια καὶ τὰ χρήστ’.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκοσμα_659">, unruly: so of Thersites, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.213"> <title>Il.</title> 2.213</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς ῥ’ ἔπεα φρεσὶν ᾗσιν ἄκοσμά τε πολλά τε ᾔδη, | μάψ, ἀτὰρ οὐ κατὰ κόσμον, ἐριζέμεναι βασιλεῦσιν</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 730">Cp. 730</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
ὅς ῥ’ ἔπεα φρεσὶν ᾗσιν ἄκοσμά τε πολλά τε ᾔδη,
									| μάψ, ἀτὰρ οὐ κατὰ κόσμον, ἐριζέμεναι βασιλεῦσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρέψω_659"> with predicative adj., as 1080, <ref n="Soph. OT 98"> <title>O. T.</title> 98</ref>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρτα_γ_659">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκόσμους θρέψω</quote>. It is needless to supply a more general verb, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιήσω</quote>: the ruler's relation to his people justifies <quote xml:lang="grc">θρέψω</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1"> <title>O. T.</title> 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τέκνα</quote> </cit>. ‘If I allow my own kindred to be unruly, I shall be obliged to tolerate unruliness in the citizens at large. For my authority as a ruler will be gone.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερβάς_663">, absol., having transgressed: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.501"> <title>Il.</title> 9.501</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε κέν τις ὑπερβήῃ καὶ ἁμάρτῃ</quote> </cit>: so <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 366A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 366A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερβαίνοντες καὶ ἁμαρτάνοντες.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ὅτε κέν τις ὑπερβήῃ καὶ ἁμάρτῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπιτάσσειν_663">, prop. said of a master giving orders to slaves (<ref n="Soph. OC 839"> <title>O. C.</title> 839</ref>): so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιτάγματα</quote> are a despot's commands (<ref n="Aristot. Pol. 4.1292a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 4.4</ref>. 28). For the art., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78">cp. 78</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοεῖ_663">, as 44.—Antigone ‘did violence to the laws’ by her deed: she seemed ‘to dictate to her rulers’ when she proclaimed a law superior to theirs (450 ff.). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 482">Cp. 482</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στήσειε_666">: the optat. (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν ἂν στήσῃ</quote>) puts the case in the most general way: any one whom she might conceivably appoint. Hence this optat. suits <quote xml:lang="grc">γνῶμαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1032">cp. 1032</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 92"> <title>Tr.</title> 92</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γ’ εὖ | πράσσειν, ἐπεὶπύθόιτο, κέρδοσἐμπολᾷ</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. OT 315"> <title>O.T.</title> 315</ref> (n.), <ref n="Soph. OT 979"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 979.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
τό γ’ εὖ | πράσσειν, ἐπεὶπύθόιτο,
									κέρδοσἐμπολᾷ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τἀναντία_666">, i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μεγάλα καὶ ἄδικα</quote>. So oft. in euphemisms, <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.62">Thuc. 4.62</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαθὸν ἢ...τὰ ἐναντία</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 472C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 472C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐδαιμονίας τε πέρι καὶ τοῦ ἐναντίου</quote> </cit>. Cp. Leutsch <title>Paroem.</title> <ref>App. 1. 100</ref> <!--looks like Eleg. Alex. Adesp. 437.1--> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρεισσόνων γὰρ καὶ δίκαια κἄδικ’ ἔστ’ ἀκούειν</quote>: and the verse cited by schol. on <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 75">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦλε, δεσποτῶν ἄκουε καὶ δίκαια κἄδικα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: εὐδαιμονίας τε πέρι καὶ τοῦ ἐναντίου

δοῦλε, δεσποτῶν ἄκουε καὶ δίκαια
									κἄδικα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτον_ἄνδρα_668"> refers to the indefinite subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύειν</quote> in 666:—the man who thus obeys. The looseness of grammatical connection would hardly be felt when the sense was so clear. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1035">Cp. 1035</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’</quote>). So in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 942"> <title>O. C.</title> 942</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτούς</quote> </cit> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πόλιν</quote> in 939.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶςεὖ_668">: for the change of word in the epanaphora cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1501"> <title>O. C.</title> 1501</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφὴς μὲν ἀστῶν ἐμφανὴς δὲ τοῦ ξένου</quote> </cit> (n.).</gloss>

data.quote: σαφὴς μὲν ἀστῶν ἐμφανὴς δὲ τοῦ ξένου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_668"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρχειν_668"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ἄρχοι ἄν</quote>) as well as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλειν_668">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορὸς_χειμῶνι_670">. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 474">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 474</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺς κλύδων | ἡμῖν τε καὶ σοὶ ξυμμάχοις τ’ ἔσται δορός.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
πολὺς κλύδων | ἡμῖν τε καὶ σοὶ ξυμμάχοις τ’
									ἔσται δορός.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστεταγμένον_670">, the regular term for placing soldiers at their posts: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.87">Thuc. 2.87</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕπεσθε, χώραν μὴ προλείποντες ᾗ ἄν τις προσταχθῇ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἕπεσθε, χώραν μὴ προλείποντες ᾗ ἄν τις
									προσταχθῇ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραστάτην_670">, one who stands beside one in the ranks (as <quote xml:lang="grc">προστάτης</quote> in front and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάτης</quote> behind): <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 3.3.21">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3.3.21</ref> (the gods are invoked as) <quote xml:lang="grc">παραστάτας ἀγαθοὺς καὶ συμμάχους</quote>. The Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφηβος</quote>, on beginning, at eighteen, his term of service as a <quote xml:lang="grc">περίπολος</quote>, took an oath, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ καταισχυνῶ ὅπλα τὰ ἱερὰ</quote> [the arms given to him by the State], <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐγκαταλείψω τὸν παραστάτην ὅτῳ ἂν στοιχήσω</quote> (by whose side he should be placed): <title>Serm.</title> 43. 48. Thus for an Athenian audience this verse would be effective, and would seem peculiarly appropriate when addressed to the youthful Haemon. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεις_ἥδ_673"> is far better and more spirited than <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεις τ’ ὄλλυσιν ἠδ’</quote> </emph>: it is also strongly confirmed by the similar passage, 296 ff., where we have <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο—τόδ’—τόδ’</quote>, just as here <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη...ἥδ’... ἥδ’</quote>. When <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεις</quote> had become, as in L, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote>,—a corruption found also in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 489">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 489</ref>,—<quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote> may have been added for metre's sake. To <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠδέ</quote> itself there is no objection: it was certainly used in iambics by Soph. (fr. 253, fr. 503), no less than by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch.
