Lib. 1025">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 1025</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 414">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 414</ref>), and by Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Hec. 323">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 323</ref>, <title>H. F.</title> 30).— Campb. reads </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεις_ἥδ_673_2">, and regards the anacoluthon as making the lines ‘more expressive.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμμάχου_674"> is a certain correction of L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμμάχηι_674">. The meaning is, ‘Disobedience causes allied forces (<quote xml:lang="grc">σύμμαχον δόρυ</quote>) to break up in flight.’ It turns union into disunion,—the hope of victory into defeat. With </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_μάχῃ_674"> the sense would be, ‘Disobedience, aiding the spear (of the foe), causes rout.’ But this would represent disobedience as merely one cause of defeat,—an incident that turns the scale. It is evidently more forcible to represent it as breaking up an army which might otherwise have stood united and firm.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροπὰς_καταρρήγνυσι_674">, lit., <emph>causes</emph> rout <emph>to break forth</emph>, i.e. breaks up the army in rout. Cp. Athen. 130 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ γελωτοποιὸς εἰσῆλθε...καὶ πολλοὺς κατέρρηξεν ἡμῶν γέλωτας</quote>, ‘and <emph>caused</emph> shouts of laughter <emph>to break forth</emph> among us’: (not, ‘wreaked many witticisms upon us,’—as Casaubon took it.) The only peculiarity in the use of the verb is that it is here equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖ καταρρήγνυσθαι</quote>. We cannot compare <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 22">Theocr. 22. 172</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος ἀναρρήξαντας</quote> </cit>, ‘having broken <emph>into</emph> strife’ (said of the parties to it), which is merely like <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥῆξαι φωνήν</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ὀρθουμένων_674"> (masc.), of those who have a prosperous course: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.60">Thuc. 2.60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν...ὀρθουμένην</quote> </cit>, opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">σφαλλομένην</quote>: 8. 64 <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνέβη...τὴν πόλιν ἀκινδύνως ὀρθοῦσθαι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 163">Cp. 163</ref>, 167.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_σώματα_674">, ‘the greater number of lives,’ differing from <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς πολλούς</quote> only by bringing out the notion of personal safety more vividly. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 758"> <title>Ai.</title> 758</ref> where the masc. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ...σώματα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_πειθαρχία_674">: called <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς εὐπραξίας | μήτηρ</quote> by <ref n="Aesch. Seven 225.">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 225.</ref>The schol. quotes <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.531"> <title>Il.</title> 5.531</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰδομένων δ’ ἀνδρῶν πλέονες σόοι ἠὲ πέφανται</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
αἰδομένων δ’ ἀνδρῶν πλέονες σόοι ἠὲ
									πέφανται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμυντέ_677">, the impers. neut. plur., as <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.58">Her. 9.58</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνοισι ταῦτα ποιεῦσι οὐκ ἐπιτρεπτέα ἐστί, ἀλλὰ διωκτέοι εἰσί</quote> </cit>: I. <ref n="Thuc. 86">Thuc. 86</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρητέα</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 88">Thuc. 88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολεμητέα</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 118">Thuc. 118</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιχειρητέα</quote>, etc.: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 495"> <title>O. C.</title> 495</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδωτά</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 447">Cp. 447</ref>, 576. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 523">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 523</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμυνῶ δ’, ὅσονπερ δυνατός εἰμι, τῷ νόμῳ</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.140">Thuc. 1.140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς κοινῇ δόξασι βοηθεῖν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐκείνοισι ταῦτα ποιεῦσι οὐκ ἐπιτρεπτέα ἐστί,
									ἀλλὰ διωκτέοι εἰσί

ἀμυνῶ δ’, ὅσονπερ δυνατός εἰμι, τῷ
									νόμῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_κοσμουμένοις_677"> (neut.), the regulations made by <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ κοσμοῦντες</quote>, the rulers: meaning here, his own edicts. For the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμεῖν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1.59">Her. 1.59</ref> (Peisistratus) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνεμε τὴν πόλιν κοσμέων καλῶς τε καὶ εὖ</quote>. And for the pass. thus used, <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.100"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 100</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν κατὰ τὰς δίκας ἐποίεε <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(deïoces)</note>, τάδε δὲ ἄλλα ἐκεκοσμέατό οἱ</quote> </cit>: ‘and the following <emph>regulations</emph> had also <emph>been made</emph> by him.’— Another view (also noticed by the Schol.) makes </gloss>

data.quote: ταῦτα μὲν κατὰ τὰς δίκας ἐποίεε  , τάδε δὲ ἄλλα ἐκεκοσμέατό οἱ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_κ_677"> dat. of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ κοσμούμενοι</quote>, ‘the rulers.’ But (<emph>a</emph>) the only place which might seem to favour this use of the midd. is <ref n="Thuc. 8.24">Thuc. 8.24</ref> (the Chians, the more they prospered) <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσῳ καὶ ἐκοσμοῦντο ἐχυρώτερον</quote>: but there the verb may well be pass., ‘the more securely was their government organised.’ (<emph>b</emph>) As Creon is himself at once <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἀμύνων</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κοσμῶν</quote>, it is more natural that he should speak of his own edicts than of ‘the rulers.’—<quote xml:lang="grc">κόσμος</quote> was said of a constitution, esp. oligarchical (<cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.76">Thuc. 4.76</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταστῆσαι τὸν κόσμον καὶ ἐς δημοκρατίαν...τρέψαι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 8.72">8. 72</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μένειν ἐν τῷ ὀλιγαρχικῷ κόσμῳ</quote> </cit>). The Cretan <quote xml:lang="grc">κόσμοι</quote> were oligarchical magistrates, with military as well as civil powers (<ref n="Aristot. Pol. 2.1272a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 2.10</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
μεταστῆσαι τὸν κόσμον καὶ ἐς
									δημοκρατίαν...τρέψαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὔτοι_ἡσσητέα_678">: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 450">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 450</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτὰρ οὐ γυναικῶν οὐδέποτ’ ἔσθ’ ἡττητέα | ἡμῖν</quote> </cit>. (Cp. Milton, <title>Samson</title> 562 ‘Effeminately vanquished.’) Since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσσᾶσθαι</quote> is only pass., its verbal in <quote xml:lang="grc">τέος</quote> can be only pass.: as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλωτέον</quote> could mean only, ‘one must be taken.’ But even in other cases the verbal in <quote xml:lang="grc">τέος</quote> sometimes answers to the pass., not to the act., sense of the verb: as <ref n="Xen. Ec. 7">Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 7</ref>§ 38 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν ἐκείνη</quote> (the queen-bee) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλίπῃ, οὐδεμία οἴεται τῶν μελιττῶν ἀπολειπτέον εἶναι, ἀλλ’ ἕπονται πᾶσαι</quote>: i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι δεῖ ἀπολείπεσθαι</quote> (pass.), ‘to be left behind’: (for the stationary bees could not be said <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολείπειν</quote> the emigrant.) </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀτὰρ οὐ γυναικῶν οὐδέποτ’ ἔσθ’ ἡττητέα |
									ἡμῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖ_679">: for the pause, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 555">cp. 555</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπεσεῖν_679">: here absol., <emph>to be displaced</emph>,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκ_ἄν_679">: the doubled <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, as oft. in emphatic or excited utterances (<ref n="Soph. OT 339"> <title>O. T.</title> 339</ref> n.). —These two verses (like so many others) have been suspected merely because they are not indispensable. A defence is perhaps hardly needed. It is enough to remark that Creon's irritation under a woman's defiance (484, 525, 579) naturally prompts this further comment on the word <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικός</quote> in 678. And the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικῶν ἥσσονες</quote> (680) has a peculiar force as spoken to Haemon,— whom Creon afterwards taunts as <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικὸς ὕστερον</quote> (746). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_681">: 498, 634.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_χρόνῳ_681">, by our age: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 729">cp. 729</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν χρόνον</quote>, ‘my years’: <ref n="Soph. OT 963"> <title>O. T.</title> 963</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκλέμμεθα_681">, are deceived: so 1218: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 243"> <title>Tr.</title> 243</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ξυμφοραὶ κλέπτουσί με</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖς_περὶ_682"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων περὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν λέγεις</quote>. At first sight it is natural to wish, with Herm. , for <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖς... ὧν λέγεις λέγειν πέρι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1057">Cp. 1057</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃν λέγῃς λέγων</quote>. But here it is fitting that <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</quote> should have the prominence of the first place. And the undoubted harshness of the order may be partly excused by observing that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν λέγεις</quote> is practically equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοί_683">. Creon had urged that filial piety demands the submission of the son's judgment to the <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη πατρῴα</quote> (640); and had warned Haemon against disregarding the voice of reason (648). Haemon replies: ‘Reason is the gift of the gods. I dare not suggest that your reasonings are wrong; but other men, too, may sometimes reason soundly. Now, I know what the Thebans are saying of your action; and, as a son devoted to your welfare, I ought to tell you.’—The tact and deference which mark this speech place Creon's <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθάδεια</quote> in a stronger light.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτημάτων_683">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1050">cp. 1050</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 549"> <title>O. T.</title> 549</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.24">Her. 5.24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτημάτων πάντων ἐστὶ τιμιώτατον ἀνὴρ φίλος ξυνετός τε καὶ εὔνοος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
κτημάτων πάντων ἐστὶ τιμιώτατον ἀνὴρ φίλος
									ξυνετός τε καὶ εὔνοος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_ὅπως_685">: lit., ‘I should not be able to say (and may I never be capable of saying!) in what respect (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>) thou dost not say these things rightly.’ He could not, if he would—and would not, if he could—impugn his father's reasonings. He only suggests that the case may have also another aspect, which Creon has not considered.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_685"> after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_685"> is generic, as after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς, ὅστις</quote> (691, 696): I could not say what point in thy argument is <emph>such as not</emph> to be true:—just as we could have, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδα ὃ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀληθεύεις</quote>. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_685"> might be taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθῶς_685"> (‘how thou sayest <emph>otherwise than</emph> rightly’), but the order of words is against this. [It cannot be explained as substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> through the influence of the optatives.]</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_ἐπισταίμην_685">. For this verb as=‘to be capable of,’ <ref n="Soph. Ant. 472">cp. 472</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 543"> <title>Tr.</title> 543</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δὲ θυμοῦσθαι μὲν οὐκ ἐπίσταμαι | νοσοῦντι κείνῳ</quote> </cit>. For the wish co-ordinated with the statement of fact, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 500">cp. 500</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 582"> <title>Tr.</title> 582</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰς δὲ τόλμας μήτ’ ἐπισταίμην ἐγὼ | μήτ’ ἐκμάθοιμι, τάς τε τολμώσας στυγῶ</quote> </cit>: and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 143"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 143.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐγὼ δὲ θυμοῦσθαι μὲν οὐκ ἐπίσταμαι | νοσοῦντι
									κείνῳ

κακὰς δὲ τόλμας μήτ’ ἐπισταίμην ἐγὼ | μήτ’
									ἐκμάθοιμι, τάς τε τολμώσας στυγῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_ἔχον_687"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 517"> <title>O. T.</title> 517</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς βλάβην φέρον</quote> </cit>), something good, some true thought, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένοιτο_ἑτέρῳ_687">, might come to (accrue to) another also. For <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτο</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 211D">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 211D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τῳ γένοιτο αὐτὸ τὸ καλὸν ἰδεῖν</quote> </cit>. (The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτο μέντἂν</quote> occurs also <ref n="Soph. Aj. 86"> <title>Ai.</title> 86</ref>.)—Not: ‘Yet it might be found well for another’ (to say that you were wrong). Haemon seeks to propitiate his father; but that purpose would scarcely be served by such a speech as this—‘Being your son, I do not contradict you myself, <emph>though I think that other people</emph> might very reasonably do so.’ </gloss>

data.quote: εἴ τῳ γένοιτο αὐτὸ τὸ καλὸν ἰδεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_οὖν_688">: ‘but in any case (i.e., whatever may be the worth of opinions different from yours) it is my natural part to watch on your behalf,’ etc. For <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’ οὖν</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 722">cp. 722</ref>, 769: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1205"> <title>O. C.</title> 1205</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστω δ’ οὖν ὅπως ὑμῖν φίλον</quote> </cit>.—The gen. </gloss>

data.quote: ἔστω δ’ οὖν ὅπως ὑμῖν φίλον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_688"> is supported by the use of the gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">προκήδομαι</quote> (741), <quote xml:lang="grc">προταρβῶ</quote> (83), <quote xml:lang="grc">προνοῶ</quote>, etc., and expresses the idea, ‘in thy defence,’ better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_688"> would do. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 459">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 459</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν...προσκοπούμενος</quote> </cit>.— Herm. adopted the <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> of the schol. in L, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_πέφυκας_688">, which Ellendt approves: but (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφυκας</quote> is then less fitting, and (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’ οὖν</quote> commends the vulgate as genuine.—For the repeated </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_688">, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 889">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 889</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῆνίν_τιν_688">'. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.62">Thuc. 4.62</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τῴ τι ἔστιν ἀγαθὸν ἢ εἴ τῳ τὰ ἐναντία</quote> </cit>. (Distinguish <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1218">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1218</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤν τις,... | ἢ σύμμαχός τις ἢ κασίγνητος</quote> </cit>,— anyone,—be he ally or brother: and <ref n="Eur. Andr. 733"> <title>Andr.</title> 733</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι γάρ τις οὐ πρόσω | Σπάρτης πόλις τις</quote>, which, if sound, is a mere pleonasm.) </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴ τῴ τι ἔστιν ἀγαθὸν ἢ εἴ τῳ τὰ
									ἐναντία

ἤν τις,... | ἢ σύμμαχός τις ἢ
									κασίγνητος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_σόν_690">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν γάρ</quote>: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 671"> <title>O. T.</title> 671</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ σόν, οὐ τὸ τοῦδ’</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1024"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1024</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γὰρ πρὶν...ἀπαιδία</quote> </cit>. In the case of <quote xml:lang="grc">σόν</quote>, at least, this order seems to strengthen, rather than diminish, the emphasis. </gloss>

data.quote: τὸ γὰρ σόν, οὐ τὸ τοῦδ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_690">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 447"> <title>O. T.</title> 447</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τὸ σὸν | δείσας πρόσωπον</quote> </cit>. Jeremiah i. 8 ‘Be not afraid of their faces.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δημότῃ_690">, the ordinary Theban citizen: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 78"> <title>O. C.</title> 78</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_τοιούτοις_691">, causal dat.: thy face is terrible to the citizen <emph>on account of</emph> such words as shall displease thee: i.e. the citizen imagines the stern king's face growing darker at the sound of frank speech, and restrains his lips. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 509">Cp. 509</ref>.) Doubts as to the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις τ</quote>. led Dindorf to suppose the loss of one verse (or more) after 690. Herwerden has suggested something like <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐδείς ποτ’ ἀστῶν ἐμφανῶς χρῆται, πάτερ, | λόγοις τοιούτοις κ.τ.λ.</quote> Nauck thinks that either v. 691 is wholly spurious, or that the words <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις τοιούτοις</quote> are corrupt. But, while the dat. is certainly bold—esp. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὶ δ.</quote> preceding it—it is (I think) quite within the possibilities of classical idiom. We should remember that Athenians were accustomed to use a simple dat. (of ‘time’ or ‘occasion’) in speaking of festivals,—as <quote xml:lang="grc">τραγῳδοῖς καινοῖς</quote>: cp. (e.g.) <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 174A">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 174A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθὲς γὰρ αὐτὸν διέφυγον τοῖς ἐπινικίοις</quote> </cit>, ‘I eluded him yesterday <emph>when he was holding</emph> his sacrifice for victory.’ So, here, the dat. </gloss>

data.quote:
χθὲς γὰρ αὐτὸν διέφυγον τοῖς
									ἐπινικίοις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_τοιούτοις_691_2">, though properly causal, might sound to a Greek ear like, ‘<emph>at</emph> such words,’ i.e. ‘when such words are spoken.’ The causal dat. in 391, <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς σαῖς ἀπειλαῖς</quote>, is similar. Cp. also <ref n="Thuc. 1.84">Thuc. I. 84</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπραγίαις...οὐκ ἐξυβρίζομεν</quote>, where the notion, ‘by reason of successes,’ is similarly blended with the notion, ‘in seasons of success.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_691"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέρψει_691"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1140"> <title>O. C.</title> 1140</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 460"> <title>Ph.</title> 460</ref>), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύων_691"> epexegetic. If, however, the order had been <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύων τέρψει</quote>, then <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς</quote> might have been for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕς</quote>, by attraction. The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_691"> is generic (‘such that not...’), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 696">cp. 696</ref>. For the fut. midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">τέρψομαι</quote> (with pass. sense) cp. fr. 612 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου γε μὴ δίκαια τέρψεται</quote>, and <ref n="Eur. Rh. 194">[Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 194</ref>. For the fut. ind. after a relative with <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1412"> <title>O. T.</title> 1412</ref> n. —Nauck reads </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέρψῃ_691"> (aor. midd.). This rare aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτερψ)άμην</quote> is epic, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 12.188"> <title>Od.</title> 12.188</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τερψάμενος</quote> </cit> (‘having had delight’). It is not Attic, the Attic aor. in that sense being <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτέρφθην</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1140"> <title>O. C.</title> 1140</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπὸ_σκότον_692"> goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούειν_692"> more naturally than, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀδύρεται_692">, and the sense is the same: i.e., he is in the <quote xml:lang="grc">σκότος</quote> where the things are said: for the gen., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65">cp. 65</ref> n., and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 539"> <title>Tr.</title> 539</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μίμνομεν μιᾶς ὑπὸ | χλαίνης</quote> </cit>, which shows that we need not here conceive the sounds as ‘coming from under’ the darkness. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 4.6.4">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 4.6.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέσχεν ὑπὸ σκότου τὸν φθόνον</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1457">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1457</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ σκότου | ξίφη σπάσαντες</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ σκότῳ</quote> also occurs (<ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1030">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1030</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1214">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1214</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάκιστ_εὐκλεεστάτων_695">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1433"> <title>O. T.</title> 1433</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄριστος ἐλθὼν πρὸς κάκιστον</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Plat. Apol. 30">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 30</ref>A <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πλείστου ἄξια περὶ ἐλαχίστου ποιεῖται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_ἔργων_695">, as their result: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1078"> <title>Ai.</title> 1078</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεσεῖν ἂν κἂν ἀπὸ σμικροῦ κακοῦ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: πεσεῖν ἂν κἂν ἀπὸ σμικροῦ κακοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_696"> with causal force (<ref n="Soph. OC 962"> <title>O. C.</title> 962</ref>); hence, too, the generic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήθ_μήθ_696"> which belong to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴασε_696"> (understood with the second <quote xml:lang="grc">μήθ’</quote>), not to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλέσθαι_696">: ‘being one who did not allow’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">quae non permiserit</foreign>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτάδελφον_696">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1">cp. 1</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνφοναῖς_696">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1314">cp. 1314</ref>. The phrases <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν φονῇσιν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ φονῇσιν</quote> are Homeric, and Her.uses the former (with art., <ref n="Eur. Her. 9.76"> Her. 9. 76</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῇσι φονῇσι ἐόντας</quote>). The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν φοναῖς</quote> is used by Pindar, Aesch. , Eur. , and (in parody) by But <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1003">Ar. v. 1003</ref> of this play— the only play of Soph. which contains the word—seems a solitary Attic instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">φοναῖς</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσῆς_699">, a general epithet for what is brilliant or precious: thus <ref n="Pind. P. 3">Pind. <title>P.</title> 3. 73</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑγίειαν...χρυσέαν</quote>, and even ( <ref n="Pind. O. 10">Pind. <title>O.</title> 10. 13</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">στεφάνῳ χρυσέας ἐλαίας</quote> (the wreath of natural olive), as Olympia is <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτηρ χρυσοστεφάνων ἀέθλων</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 1</ref>) in a like sense. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 157"> <title>O. T.</title> 157</ref> (‘golden’ hope), <ref n="Soph. OC 1052"> <title>O. C.</title> 1052</ref> (the ‘golden’ bliss of initiation).—There is no allusion to a <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσοῦς στέφανος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαχεῖν_699"> can take either gen. or acc., the latter being more freq. (<ref n="Soph. OC 450"> <title>O. C.</title> 450</ref> n.). But here the inf. is rather epexegetic (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1098">cp. 1098</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>), the gen. depending on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξία_699">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέρχεται_700">, spreads <emph>over</emph> (the town). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.299"> <title>Od.</title> 1.299</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον κλέος ἔλλαβε... | πάντας ἐπ’ ἀνθρώπους</quote> </cit>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφέρπειν</quote>, of secret rumour, <ref n="Soph. OT 786"> <title>O. T.</title> 786</ref> n. </gloss>

data.quote:
οἷον κλέος ἔλλαβε... | πάντας ἐπ’
									ἀνθρώπους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλλοντος_703">, prospering, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 419"> <title>Ph.</title> 419</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα | θάλλοντές εἰσι νῦν ἐν Ἀργείων στρατῷ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεῖζον_εὐκλείας_703"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">μεῖζον ἢ εὔκλεια.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_παίδων_703">, on their part, from their side: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 738"> <title>Tr.</title> 738</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν, ὦ παῖ, πρός γ’ ἐμοῦ στυγούμενον</quote> </cit>; We understand <quote xml:lang="grc">μεῖζον ἄγαλμά ἐστι τῆς ἐκείνων εὐκλείας</quote>. —The conjecture </gloss>

data.quote:
τί δ’ ἔστιν, ὦ παῖ, πρός γ’ ἐμοῦ
									στυγούμενον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐκλεία_703"> is attractive, (<emph>a</emph>) because <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλλω</quote> so oft. takes a dat. of respect, as <ref n="Hes. WD 234">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 234</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαθοῖσι</quote>), <ref n="Pind. O. 9">Pind. <title>O.</title> 9. 16</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεταῖσιν</quote>), etc.: (<emph>b</emph>) because the strong sigmatism of the verse is thus modified. But the words <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς παίδων</quote> confirm <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐκλείας</quote>, since with <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐκλείᾳ</quote> we should have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">παίδων</quote> alone. It is true that <quote xml:lang="grc">πατὴρ θάλλων εὐκλείᾳ</quote> could mean ‘a father's fame’ (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 638">cp. 638</ref>); but one could not havé, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς παίδων τί μεῖζον ἄγαλμα παίδων εὐκλείᾳ θαλλόντων</quote>;— Triclinius wrongly joined <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐκλείας ἄγαλμα</quote>, thinking of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐκλείας γέρας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 478"> <title>Ph.</title> 478</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφανον εὐκλείας μέγαν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 465"> <title>Ai.</title> 465</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυν_705">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 524">cp. 524</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόρει_705">: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 757">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 757</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λῆμα θούριον φορεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 118">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 118</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπλάγχνον ἔντονον φέρων</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Shak. Cym 3.4">Shaksp. <title>Cymb.</title> 3. 4. 146</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘if you could <emph>wear</emph> a mind | Dark as your fortune is’</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Shak. JC 5.1.113"> <title>Caes.</title> 5. 1. 113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘He <emph>bears</emph> too great a mind.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
‘if you could   a mind | Dark
									as your fortune is’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦθος_705">=a way of <emph>thinking:</emph> the inf. depends on it, as on ‘do not <emph>think.</emph>’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_σύ_705">, your way of speaking,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ σὺ φής</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1124"> <title>O. C.</title> 1124</ref> (n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σοι θεοὶ πόροιεν ὡς ἐγὼ θέλω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐδέν_705">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μηδέν</quote>: it is merely oratio obliqua for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι τοῦτο καὶ οὐδὲν ἄλλο ὀρθῶς ἔχει</quote>. The imperative <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...φόρει</quote> does not affect this: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1085"> <title>Ai.</title> 1085</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ δοκῶμεν δρῶντες ἃν ἡδώμεθα | οὐκ ἀντιτίσειν αὖθις ἃν λυπώμεθα</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μηδέν</quote> could also have stood here, since v. 705 could be regarded as equiv. to, ‘do not feel confident that...’: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1455"> <title>O. T.</title> 1455</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μὴ δοκῶμεν δρῶντες ἃν ἡδώμεθα | οὐκ
									ἀντιτίσειν αὖθις ἃν λυπώμεθα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_705">, antecedent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς φης</quote>, emphatically placed: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 385"> <title>O. T.</title> 385</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνος_707"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονεῖν_707"> only.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχήν_707">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 176">cp. 176</ref>. Theognis 221 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις τοι δοκέει τὸν πλησίον ἴδμεναι οὐδέν, | ἀλλ’</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτοι_709"> after the collective <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Ec. 7. 37">Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 7. 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν κάμνῃ τῶν οἰκετῶν, τούτων σοι ἐπιμελητέον πάντων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διαπτυχθέντες_709">, when laid open. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 984">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 984</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μέντοι πρᾶγμ’, ἔχον καλοὺς λόγους, | εἴ τις διαπτύξειεν, οὐ καλὸν τόδε</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 330"> <title>Andr.</title> 330</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξωθέν εἰσιν οἱ δοκοῦντες εὖ φρονεῖν | λαμπροί, τὰ δ’ ἔνδον πᾶσιν ἀνθρώποις ἴσοι</quote>. Also <quote xml:lang="grc">σκόλιον</quote> no. 7 in Bergk <title>Poet. Lyr.</title> (from Athen. 694 C, etc.) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθ’ ἐξῆν ὁποῖός τις ἦν ἕκαστος | τὸ στῆθος διελόντ’, ἔπειτα τὸν νοῦν | ἐσιδόντα, κλῄσαντα πάλιν, | ἄνδρα φίλον νομίζειν ἀδόλῳ φρενί</quote>. The image might be suggested by various objects,— a casket, tables, fruit, or the like.—Cp. <ref n="Shak. Rom 3.2.83">Shaks. <title>Rom.</title> 3. 2. 83</ref>(of Romeo) ‘Was ever book containing such vile matter So fairly bound?’</gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ μέντοι πρᾶγμ’, ἔχον καλοὺς λόγους, | εἴ τις
									διαπτύξειεν, οὐ καλὸν τόδε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κενοί_709">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντες</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 471">cp. 471</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνδρα_710">, subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μανθάνειν_710">, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 314"> <title>O. T.</title> 314</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα δ’ ὠφελεῖν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>: for the place of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_710">, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 723">cp. 723</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 65"> <title>Tr.</title> 65</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ... | τὸ μὴ πυθέσθαι</quote> </cit> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὲ μὴ πυθέσθαι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_ᾖ_710">: see <ref n="Soph. OT 198"> <title>O. T.</title> 198</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τείνειν_710">, absol., here, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τείνειν τόξον</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τείνειν πόδα</quote>, ‘to strain the cord too tight,’—to be <emph>overrigid</emph> in maintaining one's own views. This poet. use should be distinguished from the ordinary intrans. use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τείνω</quote>, like <foreign xml:lang="lat">tendere</foreign>, ‘to have a direction,’ or ‘take one's way’ (<cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 4.3.21">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 4.3.21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτεινον ἄνω πρὸς τὸ ὄρος</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὰ_ῥείθρ_710">: for ā before initial <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 847"> <title>O. T.</title> 847</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ ῥέπον</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OC 900"> <title>O. C.</title> 900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ ῥυτῆρος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειμάρροις_710">, here a neut. adj., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 449">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 449</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕδατι χειμάρρῳ</quote> </cit>: usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ χειμάρρους</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποταμός</quote>). Tozer, <title>Geo. Gr.</title> p. 84: ‘The numerous torrents (<quote xml:lang="grc">χειμάῤῥοι</quote>) are the natural result of the configuration of the country, for the steep limestone mountains have but little of a spongy surface to act as a reservoir for the rain... It is especially at the time of the autumn rains that the greatest floods take place, and the sudden swelling and violent rush of the stream has furnished Homer with some of his finest similes.’ (<ref n="Hom. Il. 4.452"> <title>Il.</title> 4.452</ref> ff., 16, 384 ff.: imitated by <ref n="Verg. A. 2.305">2. 305 ff.</ref>, <ref n="Verg. A. 12.523">12. 523.</ref>)—Antiphanes (c. 380 B.C.) parodies these verses (fr. incert. 10: Athen. 22 F). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπείκει_713">. Cp. Babrius <foreign xml:lang="lat">fab.</foreign> 36: an oak, torn up by the roots, is being swept down by a boiling torrent, and asks the reeds how they have managed to escape; when a reed (<quote xml:lang="grc">κάλαμος</quote>) answers;—<quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ μὲν μαχομένη ταῖς πνοαῖς ἐνικήθης, | ἡμεῖς δὲ καμπτόμεσθα μαλθακῇ γνώμῃ, | κἂν βαιὸν ἡμῶν ἄνεμος ἄκρα κινήσῃ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόπρεμνα_713"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτόρριζα, πρόρριζα</foreign>; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.541"> <title>Il.</title> 9.541</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαμαὶ βάλε δένδρεα μακρὰ | αὐτῇσιν ῥίζῃσι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
χαμαὶ βάλε δένδρεα μακρὰ | αὐτῇσιν
									ῥίζῃσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔτως_715">, adv. from <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote> (with ‘Aeolic’ acc.); see on <ref n="Soph. OT 931"> <title>O. T.</title> 931</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναός_715">, Doric for <quote xml:lang="grc">νεώς</quote>, allowed by tragedy even in iambics, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 872"> <title>Ai.</title> 872</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 62">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 62</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Med. 523">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 523</ref>: though <quote xml:lang="grc">νᾶες</quote> (953) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ναΐ</quote> occur only in lyrics. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ναός</quote>, temple (286), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθάνα, κυναγός, ὁδαγός, ποδαγός</quote> (1196): and even in Att.prose <quote xml:lang="grc">λοχαγός, οὐραγός, ξεναγός.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκρατῆ_715">, proleptic: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 475">cp. 475</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περισκελῆ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόδα_715">, the sheet: the <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδες</quote> were ropes attached to the two lower corners of the sail, whence their name. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 706">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 706</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ναῦς γάρ, ἐνταθεῖσα πρὸς βίαν ποδί, | ἔβαψεν, ἔστη δ’ αὖθις, ἢν χαλᾷ πόδα</quote> </cit>: a ship dips when strained too hard by the sheet (i.e. when the sheet is hauled too taut), but rights again, if one slackens. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ ναῦς γάρ, ἐνταθεῖσα πρὸς βίαν ποδί, |
									ἔβαψεν, ἔστη δ’ αὖθις, ἢν χαλᾷ πόδα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδέν_716">, generic (such an one as does not...).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτω_στρέψας_716">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῦν</quote>, easily supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">ναός</quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτω</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 527">cp. 527</ref>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφω = ἀναστρέφω</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1453"> <title>O. C.</title> 1453</ref>. Hermann's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_πλοῖον_716"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_λοιπόν_716"> is not only needless, but spoils the force of the phrase: ‘<emph>thenceforth</emph> voyages,’ is an ironical way of saying that the voyage comes to an abrupt end: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 311">cp. 311</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέλμασιν_716">, the rowers' benches: thus </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπτίοις_716"> vividly suggests the moment of capsizing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶκε_θυμοῦ_718">, ‘cease from wrath,’ lit., recede from it. The <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμός</quote> is conceived as ground from which he retires; so <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμοῦ περᾶν</quote>=‘to <emph>go far in</emph> wrath,’ and is contrasted with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκειν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 673"> <title>O. T.</title> 673</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγνὸς μὲν εἴκων δῆλος εἶ, βαρὺς δ’, ὅταν | θυμοῦ περάσῃς</quote> </cit>. For the gen., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 4.509"> <title>Il.</title> 4.509</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ εἴκετε χάρμης | Ἀργείοις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.348"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 5. 348</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶκε, Διὸς θύγατερ, πολέμου καὶ δηϊοτῆτος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.80">Her. 2.80</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴκουσι τῆς ὁδοῦ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.160">id. 7. 160</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπείξομεν τοῦ ἀρχαίου λόγου</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 790">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 790</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεχώρησεν αὐτῷ τοῦ θρόνου</quote> </cit>. Eur. has a somewhat similar phrase, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 900">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῆς δ’ ἐξανεὶς κακῆς, ἄναξ | Θησεῦ, τὸ λῷστον σοῖσι βούλευσαι δόμοις</quote>, where the sense is, ‘having remitted thy wrath,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξανεὶς [σεαυτὸν] ὀργῆς.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
στυγνὸς μὲν εἴκων δῆλος εἶ, βαρὺς δ’, ὅταν |
									θυμοῦ περάσῃς

εἶκε, Διὸς θύγατερ, πολέμου καὶ
									δηϊοτῆτος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δίδου_718">, ‘and concede a change’: allow our pleading to change your mood. A change in Creon's mood implies a change in the whole situation. For the notions thus blended in <quote xml:lang="grc">μετάστασιν</quote> here, cp. Alexis fr. incert. 46 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μετρίων αἱ μείζονες | λῦπαι ποιοῦσι τῶν φρενῶν μετάστασιν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 2.18">Andoc. or. 2 § 18</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴσειν ἔμελλεν...τοῦ τότε παρόντος κακοῦ μετάστασιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οἴσειν ἔμελλεν...τοῦ τότε παρόντος κακοῦ
									μετάστασιν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίδου_718">: a verb oft. used of <emph>concession</emph> to the remonstrance of friends: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 483"> <title>Ai.</title> 483</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦσαί γε μέντοι καὶ δὸς ἀνδράσιν φίλοις | γνώμης κρατῆσαι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1117"> <title>Tr.</title> 1117</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δός μοι σεαυτόν, μὴ τοσοῦτον ὡς δάκνει | θυμῷ δύσοργος</quote> </cit>.—Others place a comma or point at </gloss>

data.quote:
παῦσαί γε μέντοι καὶ δὸς ἀνδράσιν φίλοις |
									γνώμης κρατῆσαι

δός μοι σεαυτόν, μὴ τοσοῦτον ὡς δάκνει | θυμῷ
									δύσοργος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶκε_718">, taking </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_718"> as= ‘also’; ‘yield, <emph>also</emph> permitting thy wrath to change’ (with <quote xml:lang="grc">διδούς</quote>); or ‘yield: also permit,’ etc. (an asyndeton, with <quote xml:lang="grc">δίδου</quote>). On this view, either <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμῷ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμοῦ</quote> is possible. But the fatal objection to it is the weakness of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_718_2">, whether the ‘also’ is explained (<emph>a</emph>) as by Campbell (with <quote xml:lang="grc">δίδου</quote>) —‘if you are angry, be <emph>also</emph> placable’; or (<emph>b</emph>) as by Wecklein (with <quote xml:lang="grc">διδούς</quote>)—‘it is possible not only to moderate one's passion, but <emph>also</emph> to desist from it,’ which implies that he might yield while still angry.—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ἐμοῦν_719"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντος</quote>), i.e., if I also, younger though I am, can contribute a sound opinion. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 292"> <title>O. C.</title> 292</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθυμήματα | ...τἀπὸ σοῦ</quote> </cit>, the thoughts urged on thy part. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1464"> <title>El.</title> 1464</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελεῖται τἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit>. For the modest </gloss>
lemma:
εἴ τις γνώμη πρόσεστι καὶ ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ
									ν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_719">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1100"> <title>O. T.</title> 1100</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χρή τι κἀμὲ...σταθμᾶσθαι</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 192"> <title>Ph.</title> 192</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ κἀγώ τι φρονῶ</quote>.—If <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπ’</quote> </emph> were taken as <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐπί</quote>, it must mean, ‘in my case also.’ <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 475A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 475A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἐμοῦ λέγειν</quote> </cit> (to take me as an instance). In <ref n="Soph. El. 1469"> <title>El.</title> 1469</ref> I formerly thus took <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπ’ ἐμοῦ θρήνων τύχῃ</quote>, but now think that there, too, it is <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἀπό.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρεσβεύειν_719"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρεσβύτατον εἶναι</foreign>, to <emph>be</emph> the <emph>best</emph> thing: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 45">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 45</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσι πρεσβεύει γένος</quote> </cit>, whose birth has precedence (=the eldest): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1365"> <title>O. T.</title> 1365</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρεσβύτερον</quote>) n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φῦναι_721">, should be by nature: <ref n="Pind. O. 9">Pind. <title>O.</title> 9. 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ φυᾷ κράτιστον ἅπαν</quote> (opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">διδακταὶ ἀρεταί</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_721">, adv.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 338"> <title>Tr.</title> 338</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἔχω γὰρ πάντ’ ἐπιστήμην ἐγώ</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. OT 475"> <title>O. T.</title> 475</ref> n.—The merit of listening to good advice is often thus extolled: <ref n="Hes. WD 291">Hes. <title>Op.</title> 291</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος μὲν πανάριστος, ὃς αὐτὸς πάντα νοήσῃ· | ἐσθλὸς δ’ αὖ κἀκεῖνος, ὃς εὖ εἰπόντι πίθηται</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.16">Her. 7.16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον ἐκεῖνο, ὦ βασιλεῦ, παρ’ ἐμοὶ κέκριται, φρονέειν τε εὖ καὶ τῷ λέγοντι χρηστὰ ἐθέλειν πείθεσθαι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Cic. Clu. 31"> <title>pro Cluentio</title> 31</ref>: <ref n="Liv. 22.29">Livy 22. 29.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote: τούτων ἔχω γὰρ πάντ’ ἐπιστήμην ἐγώ

ἴσον ἐκεῖνο, ὦ βασιλεῦ, παρ’ ἐμοὶ κέκριται,
									φρονέειν τε εὖ καὶ τῷ λέγοντι χρηστὰ ἐθέλειν πείθεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_οὖν_722">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἔφυ τοιοῦτος</quote>. This is better than to suppose that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φιλεῖ_γάρ_722"> has changed the form of the sentence (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ οὖν τοῦτο μὴ ταύτῃ ῥέπει</quote>), since this elliptical <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ οὖν</quote> was a familiar Attic idiom: see <ref n="Plat. Apol. 34">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 34</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δή τις ὑμῶν οὕτως ἔχει—οὐκ ἀξιῶ μὲν γὰρ ἔγωγε, εἰ δ’ οὖν</quote> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως ἔχει]—ἐπιεικῆ ἄν μοι δοκῶ πρὸς τοῦτον λέγειν</quote>: ‘If any one of you is so disposed—I do not think that he ought to be so, but <emph>suppose that he is</emph>—I think that I might fairly say to him,’ etc. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 507">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 507</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τοι δοκεῖ σοι, χρῆν μὲν οὔ σ’ ἁμαρτάνειν· | εἰ δ’ οὖν</quote> </cit> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥμαρτες], πιθοῦ μοι</quote> (‘you ought not to have erred,—but if you <emph>have</emph>’). So, without ellipse, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1042">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1042</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ οὖν ἀνάγκη τῆσδ’ ἐπιρρέποι τύχης</quote> </cit>, ‘but if one <emph>should</emph> be doomed to slavery’ (then worthy masters are best). Eur. fr. 463 <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπη μὲν ἄτῃ περιπεσεῖν...| εἰ δ’ οὖν γένοιτο, κ.τ.λ.</quote> Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’ οὖν</quote> in 688 (n.).</gloss>

data.quote:
εἴ τοι δοκεῖ σοι, χρῆν μὲν οὔ σ’ ἁμαρτάνειν· |
									εἰ δ’ οὖν
εἰ δ’ οὖν ἀνάγκη τῆσδ’ ἐπιρρέποι τύχης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_ταύτῃ_722">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 950"> <title>Ai.</title> 950</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ἔστη τῇδε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 511">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 511</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ ταῦτα ταύτῃ. <emph>μή</emph> </quote> </cit> is generic, going with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτῃ_722">: in a way other than this.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥέπειν_722"> to incline (as the scale of a balance does): so <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 862D">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 862D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε ῥέπειν</quote> </cit>, <ref n="Plat. Tim. 79e"> <title>Tim.</title> 79 E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνῃ ῥέπον</quote> (to incline, or tend, in that direction). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μανθάνεινκαλόν_723"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote>): for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 659">cp. 659</ref>: for that of <quote xml:lang="grc">τό</quote>, 710. The simple gen., as <ref n="Soph. OT 545"> <title>O. T.</title> 545</ref>, etc. </gloss>
lemma:
καὶ τὸ τῶν εὖ λεγόντων μανθάνειν
									καλόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_τ_724"> doubled: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1340">cp. 1340</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 637"> <title>O. T.</title> 637</ref>.—L's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλᾶι_724"> really favours </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλᾶ_724"> rather than Hermann's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῇ_724">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> subscript is oft. wrongly added or omitted (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 726">cp. 726</ref> cr. n.); whereas <quote xml:lang="grc">ῆι</quote> was not likely to become <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾶι</quote> here. Either word is admissible; but I slightly prefer <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλᾶ</quote>, for this reason. It is true that the plur. of <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦς</quote> in poetry usu.=simply ‘two’ (51, 1232, 1320, <ref n="Soph. OT 20"> <title>O. T.</title> 20</ref>, 1135). But Soph. has at least one instance of the distributive sense (‘two sets’), viz., <ref n="Soph. OT 1249"> <title>O. T.</title> 1249</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦς</quote>=a twofold brood, i.e. Oed., and his children. (I do not add <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 288"> <title>O. T.</title> 288</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦς | πομπούς</quote> </cit>, taking it to mean merely ‘two,’ not ‘two sets.’) And in Attic prose the distributive use is not rare: thus in <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 722E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 722E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῖ... νόμοι</quote> </cit> are not ‘two laws,’ but ‘two sets of laws.’ We have, then, good warrant for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλᾶ_724_2"> here as=‘two sets of arguments.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῇ_724_2"> is strange (though possible) as=‘in two ways,’ i.e. ‘on both sides.’ It usu. means, ‘doubly’ (<cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 760">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 760</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεἰ θανεῖν μέλλω διπλῇ</quote> </cit>); or ‘twice as much’ (<cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 330C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 330C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλῇ ἢ οἱ ἄλλοι</quote> </cit>). So, here, it would more naturally mean, ‘twice over.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_726"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδαξ_726">, shall we <emph>indeed</emph> be taught: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 385"> <title>El.</title> 385</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ ταῦτα δή με καὶ βεβούλευνται ποιεῖν</quote> </cit>; <ref n="Soph. OT 772"> <title>O. T.</title> 772</ref> n. For <quote xml:lang="grc">διδαξ</quote>. as pass., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 637">cp. 637</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote: ἦ ταῦτα δή με καὶ βεβούλευνται ποιεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_726">, an indignant ‘then’: the word ends a verse also in 923, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 460"> <title>Tr.</title> 460</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1065"> <title>Ph.</title> 1065</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Supp. 521">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 521</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1093"> <title>Hipp.</title> 1093.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_φύσιν_726">, birth, and so age; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1295"> <title>O. C.</title> 1295</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὢν φύσει νεώτερος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδέν_728">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάσκου</quote>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δίκ_728"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὃ μὴ δίκαιόν ἐστι</foreign>: <ref target="cl360">see on 360</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν...τὸ μέλλον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_χρόνον_728">, my years: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 681">cp. 681</ref>.— The change of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄργα_728"> into <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον</quote> (adopted by Nauck) is no gain. The sing. is taken as ‘the cause’ (which he defends). But he means, ‘you should consider, not my age, but my conduct,—my merits’: and this is expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄργα</quote>, just as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 265"> <title>O. C.</title> 265</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα μόνον δείσαντες· οὐ γὰρ δὴ τό γε | σῶμ’ οὐδὲ τἄργα τἀμά</quote> </cit>. Cp. Menander fr. incert. 91 <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τοῦτο βλέψῃς, εἰ νεώτερος λέγω, | ἀλλ’ εἰ φρονούντων τοὺς λόγους ἀνδρῶν λέγω</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονοῦντος...ἀνδρός</quote>: Bentley, <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρω</quote>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄνομα μόνον δείσαντες· οὐ γὰρ δὴ τό γε | σῶμ’
									οὐδὲ τἄργα τἀμά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργον_730">. Haemon has asked that his <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</quote> may be considered. Creon asks scornfully, ‘Do you consider it an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote>— something which you can urge in your favour—to be the champion of a rebel?’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote> would not have been thus used alone, but for the desire to give <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄργα</quote> a derisive echo. The Attic associations of the word help, however, to explain this use. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote> meant (<emph>a</emph>) a thing <emph>worth doing</emph>, as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 424">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 424</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἔργον ἑστάναι</quote> </cit>, it is <emph>no use</emph>...(cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 852"> <title>Ai.</title> 852</ref>); so <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν προὔργου ἐστί</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">non operae pretium est:</foreign> or (<emph>b</emph>), one's allotted task, as <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 862">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 862</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερεῦ, σὸν ἔργον, θῦε</quote>. So here, without meaning so much as ‘achievement’ (<ref n="Soph. El. 689"> <title>El.</title> 689</ref>), it could mean, ‘useful act,’ ‘worthy task.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_ἀκοσμοῦντας_730">: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 387"> <title>Ph.</title> 387</ref>: cp. above 660, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 677."> Ph. 677.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέβειν_730">, as 511. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_731">, not even: <ref n="Soph. OC 1429"> <title>O. C.</title> 1429</ref> (n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἀγγελοῦμεν φλαῦρα</quote>. So far am I from showing honour to evil-doers, that I would not even wish others to do so. Without directly denying that Antigone can be described as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκοσμοῦσα</quote>, he denies that she is <quote xml:lang="grc">κακή</quote>. This involves the whole question between the divine and the human law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιᾷδε_νοσῳ_732">, that of being <quote xml:lang="grc">κακή</quote>. Others understand, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ εἰς τοὺς κακοὺς εὐσεβεῖν</quote>. But the sense of the dialogue runs thus:—‘<title>C.</title> Do you approve of honouring law-breakers?—<title>H.</title> I should not dream of honouring wrong-doers.—<title>C.</title> Is not she<*> then, a wrong-doer?’ Doubtless, Creon could also say,—‘Does not she, then, honour wrong-doers (Polyneices)?’ Here, however, his point is that <emph>she</emph> is a rebel,— not, that her brother was a traitor.—For the fig. use of <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1052">cp. 1052</ref>, and n. on 653.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπείληπται_732">, attacked, as by a disease: so the act., <ref n="Thuc. 2.51">Thuc. 2.51</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ νόσος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δὶς...τὸν αὐτὸν...οὐκ ἐπελάμβανε</quote>. (Distinguish the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίληπτος</quote> in 406.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θήβης_733">, possessive gen., not gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμόπτολις_733">, which=‘of the same city’: the sense is, ‘the united folk of Thebes,’=the whole city, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνδημος πόλις</quote> (7). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 693">Cp. 693</ref>. The epic <quote xml:lang="grc">πτόλις</quote> is used both in lyr. and in dial. by Aesch. and Eur. , but in neither by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_734">, plur. (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote>), combined with the sing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμέ_734">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1194">cp. 1194</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1400"> <title>Ai.</title> 1400</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ μή ’στι σοὶ φίλον | πράσσειν τάδ’ ἡμᾶς, εἶμ’, ἐπαινέσας τὸ σόν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1394"> <title>Ph.</title> 1394</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ σέ γ’ ἐν λόγοις | πείσειν δυνησόμεσθα μηδὲν ὦν λέγω</quote> (and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1219"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1219 ff.</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 858"> <title>H. F.</title> 858</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥλιον μαρτυρόμεσθα δρῶσ’ ἃ δρἆν οὐ βούλομαι</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 391"> <title>Ion</title> 391</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κωλυόμεσθα μὴ μαθεῖν ἃ βούλομαι</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ δὲ μή ’στι σοὶ φίλον | πράσσειν τάδ’ ἡμᾶς,
									εἶμ’, ἐπαινέσας τὸ σόν

ἥλιον μαρτυρόμεσθα δρῶσ’ ἃ δρἆν οὐ
									βούλομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_νέος_735">—despite the difference between your age and mine (726). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_736"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_736"> is clearly right; <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> would throw a false emphasis on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρή_736"> (‘Now, <emph>ought</emph> I to rule...?’): the sense requires the stress to fall on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλῳ_μοί_736">. This dat. ‘of interest’ does not mean, ‘for my own advantage’ (or gain), but, ‘to my own satisfaction,’ i.e. ‘according to my own views.’ Haemon has made light of Creon's protest against dictation from Thebes. Creon rejoins, ‘What, am I to rule Thebes in dependence on any other judgment than my own?’ In <ref n="Eur. Supp. 410">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 410</ref> Creon's herald says, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις γὰρ ἧς ἐγὼ πάρειμ’ ἄπο | ἑνὸς πρὸς ἀνδρός, οὐκ ὄχλῳ, κρατύνεται</quote>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_736"> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτῷ</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 474B">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 474B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ γὰρ δὴ οἶμαι καὶ ἐμὲ καὶ σὲ...ἡγεῖσθαι</quote> </cit>.—Though </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐγὼ γὰρ δὴ οἶμαι καὶ ἐμὲ καὶ
									σὲ...ἡγεῖσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρή_γε_736"> is untenable, the dat. is no argument against it: <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote> could be absolute, the dat. being still a dat. of interest. There is no certain Attic instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote> with dat. In <title>Ión</title> 1317 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσι δ’ ἐνδίκοις | ἱερὰ καθίζειν, ὅστις ἠδικεῖτ’, ἐχρῆν</quote>, Dobree's <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δέ γ’ ἐνδίκους</quote> is needless: the sense is, ‘in the interest of the just, it was right,’ etc. In <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 28.10">Lys. or. 28 § 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἄρχουσι τοῖς ὑμετέροις ἐπιδείξετε πότερον χρὴ δικαίοις εἶναι</quote> </cit>, we should read <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίους</quote>, and just afterwards <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφελομένους</quote>. Xenophon has <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote> with dat. and infin., if the text is sound in <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 4. 35.">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 4. 35.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῖς ἄρχουσι τοῖς ὑμετέροις ἐπιδείξετε πότερον
									χρὴ δικαίοις εἶναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλις_ἔσθ_737">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Pol. 3.1287a">Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 3.16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ δὲ τῆς παμβασιλείας καλουμένης,—αὔτη δ’ ἐστὶ καθ’ ἣν ἄρχει πάντων κατὰ την ἑαυτοῦ βούλησιν ὁ βασιλεύς,— δοκεῖ δέ τισιν οὐδὲ κατὰ φύσιν εἶναι τὸ κύριον ἕνα πάντων εἶναι τῶν πολιτῶν, ὅπου συνέστηκεν ἐξ ὁμοίων ἡ πόλις</quote> </cit>. For Plato, the</gloss>

data.quote:
περὶ δὲ τῆς παμβασιλείας καλουμένης,—αὔτη δ’
									ἐστὶ καθ’ ἣν ἄρχει πάντων κατὰ την ἑαυτοῦ βούλησιν ὁ βασιλεύς,—
									δοκεῖ δέ τισιν οὐδὲ κατὰ φύσιν εἶναι τὸ κύριον ἕνα πάντων εἶναι
									τῶν πολιτῶν, ὅπου συνέστηκεν ἐξ ὁμοίων ἡ πόλις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νομίζεται_738"> with gen., as <ref n="Soph. OC 38"> <title>O. C.</title> 38</ref> (n.).—In a different sense (and rather with an allusion to demagogues) it is said in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 386"> <title>Ph.</title> 386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις γὰρ ἔστι πᾶσα τῶν ἡγουμένων</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι τοῦ λέγοντος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 917"> <title>O. T.</title> 917</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_γ_739"> (L) is much better than <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς γ’ ἐρήμης</quote> (Blaydes and Nauck): Soph. often thus adds <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> to the emphatic adj., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 365"> <title>El.</title> 365</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἂν σύ, σώφρων γ’ οὖσα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 518"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 518</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θυραίαν γ’ οὖσαν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 811"> <title>Ph.</title> 811</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μήν σ’ ἔνορκόν γ’ ἀξιῶ θέσθαι</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐδ’ ἂν σύ, σώφρων γ’ οὖσα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖν_741">, indeed, in fact: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 489">cp. 489</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προκήδομαι_741">: cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc">προσκοπεῖν</quote>, 688. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_παγκάκιστε_742">: so Heracles to his son Hyllus, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1124"> <title>Tr.</title> 1124</ref>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 743"> <title>O. C.</title> 743</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖστον...κάκιστος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_πατρί_742">, engaging in controversy with him, bandying arguments with him. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.60">Thuc. 6.60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρνηθέντι διὰ δίκης ἐλθεῖν</quote> </cit>, to deny the charge, and stand a trial. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.2.8">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.2.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν αὐτοῖς διὰ φιλίας ἰέναι...διὰ παντὸς πολέμου αὐτοῖς ἰέναι</quote> </cit>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ μάχης</quote> (<ref n="Hdt. 6.9">Her. 6.9</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ ἔχθρας</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Phoen. 479">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 479</ref>).— Cp. <ref n="Plut. Mor. 483">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 483</ref>C (a brother, in a brother's defence, ought to brave the displeasure of parents): <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ δὲ ὑπὲρ ἀδελφοῦ παρ’ ἀξίαν κακῶς ἀκούοντος ἢ πάσχοντος ἀντιδικίαι καὶ δικαιολογίαι πρὸς αὐτοὺς</quote> (the parents) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμεμπτοι καὶ καλαί· καὶ οὐ φοβητέον ἀκοῦσαι</quote> (to have said to one) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Σοφόκλειον· ὦ παῖ κάκιστε</quote> (quoting this v.)...<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ αὐτοῖς ἡ τοιαύτη δίκη</quote> (controversy) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἐλεγχομένοις ποιεῖ τὴν ἧτταν ἡδίω τῆς νίκης</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
πάλιν αὐτοῖς διὰ φιλίας ἰέναι...διὰ παντὸς
									πολέμου αὐτοῖς ἰέναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχάς_744">, the king's powers or prerogatives, like <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτη</quote> (60, 166, 173): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 177">cp. 177</ref>, 797. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 864">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 864</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχάς τε πολισσονόμους | πατέρων θ’ ἕξει μέγαν ὄλβον</quote> (Orestes). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 343">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 343</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ κατέσχες ἀρχάς</quote> </cit> (Agamemnon). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_σέβεις_745">: ‘(that plea is void), for,’ etc.: i.e. ‘<emph>nay</emph>, <emph>but</emph> thou dost not...’ Creon has asked, ‘Do I wrong, when I reverence my royal office?’ Haemon answers, ‘Nay, there can be no such reverence, when you dishonour the gods.’ A king rules by the divine grace. He sins against his own office when he uses his power to infringe the majesty of the gods.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμάς_745">, esp. sacrifices (as in this case the offerings to the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρτεροι</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 909"> <title>O. T.</title> 909</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_μιαρόν_746">. In Haemon's last words Creon hears an echo of Antigone's doctrine—that the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν νόμιμα</quote> rank above the human king's edict (453). Hence </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γυναικὸς_ὕστερον_746">, ‘inferior to her,’ ranking after her; so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1366"> <title>Ai.</title> 1366</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 181."> <title>Ph.</title> 181.</ref>Not, ‘unable to resist her influence’ (through love), as though it were <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναικὸς ἧσσον</quote>: a meaning which <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕστερος</quote> could not have. The general sense is, however, the same, —viz., that he ranks behind a woman, who leads him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_τἄν_747"> is a certain correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄν</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1445"> <title>O. T.</title> 1445</ref>, 1469: <ref n="Soph. OC 1351"> <title>O. C.</title> 1351</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 279"> <title>Tr.</title> 279</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 456"> <title>Ai.</title> 456</ref>, 534, etc.). Against the weak conjecture <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄν γ’</quote> </emph> is the repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote>: cp. on <ref n="Soph. OC 387"> <title>O. C.</title> 387</ref>. Where <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄν</quote> has been corrupted in our MSS., it has most often become <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’ ἄν</quote>, sometimes <quote xml:lang="grc">γ’ ἄν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’ ἄν</quote>. But a change of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τἄν</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄν</quote> would also be easy in writing where, as in that of L, the <quote xml:lang="grc">κ</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ</quote> was often attached to the next word (see cr. n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_747"> emphasises the whole phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσσω τῶν αἰσχρῶν</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσσω</quote> alone: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 648">cp. 648</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γοῦν_748">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 24"> <title>O. C.</title> 24</ref> n. To plead <emph>her</emph> cause <emph>is</emph> to be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥσσων τῶν αἰσχρῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_γε_749">. Creon is concerned, not merely as a king whose city will be punished by the gods, but as a man who is to be saved from incurring guilt. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_750"> for the more usual <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 196"> <title>Ph.</title> 196</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔσθ’ ὡς οὐ</quote>.—The strange place of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_750"> is explained by the strong emphasis on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτην_750"> (‘<emph>her</emph>, at <emph>any</emph> time, it is impossible that thou shouldest wed’). Soph. often admits bold arrangements of words (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1245"> <title>O. T.</title> 1245</ref>, 1251: <ref n="Soph. OC 1428"> <title>O. C.</title> 1428</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδ_751"> referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 296">cp. 296</ref> f.). At first sight <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’</quote> </emph> is attractive; but that phrase is properly used with the imperat., and has a defiant or scornful tone (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 669"> <title>O. T.</title> 669</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ οὖν ἴτω</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 961"> <title>Ai.</title> 961</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ οὖν γελώντων</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 186">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 186</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ οὖν βοώντων</quote> </cit>). The quiet <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδ’</quote> is more impressive here.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεῖ_τινά_751">, i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote>: Creon understands him to mean <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ</quote>. As vv. 763 f. show, Haemon is resolved not to survive Antigone. But he has no thought of threatening his father's life: his frantic action at v. 1231 was a sudden impulse, instantly followed by remorse (1245). For the sinister <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1138"> <title>Ai.</title> 1138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ εἰς ἀνίαν τοὔπος ἔρχεταί τινι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 552">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 552 ff.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν ἥκει τινί...δώσει τις δίκην</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.68">Thuc. 4.68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ...μὴ πείσεταί τις, αὐτοῦ τὴν μάχην ἔσεσθαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: τοῦτ’ εἰς ἀνίαν τοὔπος ἔρχεταί τινι

εἰ...μὴ πείσεταί τις, αὐτοῦ τὴν μάχην
									ἔσεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_ὧδεθρασύς_752"> Dost thou go the length of e'en threatening so boldly? The participial clause defines the manner of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεξέρχει</quote>, and so is practically equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε καὶ ἐπαπειλεῖν</quote> etc. The </gloss>
lemma:
ἦ ἐπεξέρχει και ἐπαπειλῶν ὧδε
									θρασύς;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_752"> here belongs to the partic. (distinguish the composite <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καί</quote> in question, <ref n="Soph. OT 368"> <title>O. T.</title> 368</ref>). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 1346">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1346</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐπεξέρχει λίαν</quote> </cit>, (we have erred,) but thou <emph>goest too far</emph> (in vengeance). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 438"> <title>O. C.</title> 438</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν θυμὸν ἐκδραμόντα μοι | μείζω κολαστήν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸν θυμὸν ἐκδραμόντα μοι | μείζω
									κολαστήν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλαίων_754">, as <ref n="Soph. OT 401"> <title>O. T.</title> 401</ref>, 1152. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενώσεις_754">, a poet. word, used by <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 4.1.5">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 4.1.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐπὶ πλούτῳ μέγα φρονοῦντας...ἐφρένου λέγων</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς ἐπὶ πλούτῳ μέγα φρονοῦντας...ἐφρένου
									λέγων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_φρονεῖν_755">, as angrily refusing (754) to hear reason. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δούλευμα_756">: cp. on 650.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_με_756">, ‘do not seek to cajole me,’— referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ πατὴρ ἦσθ’</quote>, as expressive of filial respect. Creon means, ‘do not pretend that you have any of the feelings with which a son ought to regard a father.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_κλύειν_757"> do you wish to speak, and yet not to hear? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_τι_757"> has a euphemistic tone (‘to say something strong, or harsh’), like <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν τι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 336"> <title>El.</title> 336</ref>), but the <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> could hardly be represented in translation without exaggerating it. <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν καὶ ἀκούειν</quote> was a familiar phrase for fair discussion (<cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.22">Thuc. 4.22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγοντες καὶ ἀκούοντες περὶ ἑκάστου ξυμβήσονται</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 189"> <title>O. C.</title> 189</ref>). <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 628"> <title>El.</title> 628</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ὀργὴν ἐκφέρει, μεθεῖσά μοι | λέγειν ἃ χρῄζοιμ’, οὐδ’ ἐπίστασαι κλύειν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 990"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 990</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ προμηθία | καὶ τῷ λέγοντι καὶ κλύοντι σύμμαχος</quote> </cit>. The words imply a claim of equality, and are also full of scorn: hence Creon's outburst. —Not: ‘do you wish to taunt and not to be taunted in return?’—as if <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύειν</quote>=‘to have things said to one’ (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1322"> <title>Ai.</title> 1322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύοντι φλαῦρα συμβαλεῖν ἔπη κακά</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 523"> <title>El.</title> 523</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς δέ σε | λέγω κακῶς κλύουσα πρὸς σέθεν θαμά</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
λέγοντες καὶ ἀκούοντες περὶ ἑκάστου
									ξυμβήσονται

πρὸς ὀργὴν ἐκφέρει, μεθεῖσά μοι | λέγειν ἃ
									χρῄζοιμ’, οὐδ’ ἐπίστασαι κλύειν

ἡ προμηθία | καὶ τῷ λέγοντι καὶ κλύοντι
									σύμμαχος

κακῶς δέ σε | λέγω κακῶς κλύουσα πρὸς σέθεν
									θαμά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄληθες_758"> the word which marks that Teiresias can no longer restrain his wrath against Oedipus (<ref n="Soph. OT 350"> <title>O. T.</title> 350</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_Ὄλ_758">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">μά</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 660"> <title>O. T.</title> 660</ref>, 1088. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1389"> <title>Ai.</title> 1389</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀλύμπου τοῦδ’ ὁ πρεσβεύων πατήρ</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1655"> <title>O. C.</title> 1655</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθ_ὅτι_758">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 276">cp. 276</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαίρων_759">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">impune</foreign>, as <ref n="Soph. OT 363"> <title>O. T.</title> 363</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1299."> <title>Ph.</title> 1299.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_δεννάσεις_759">, lit., <emph>revile</emph> me with (continual) <emph>censures:</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψόγος</quote> is merely censure, fault-finding, not necessarily implying offensive speech (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 689">cp. 689</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc">δεννάζω</quote>, to reproach or revile: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 243"> <title>Ai.</title> 243</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ δεννάζων ῥήμαθ’</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Rh. 925">[Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 925</ref> (the Muse speaking of Thamyris) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἡμῶν πόλλ’ ἐδέννασεν τέχνην</quote>. So Theogn. 1211 (if the verse be his, and not Anacreon's) <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μ’ ἀφελῶς [ἀφίλως?] παίζουσα φίλους δένναζε τοκῆας</quote>, alluding to her saying that they had been slaves. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.107">Her. 9.107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ δὲ τοῖσι Πέρσῃσι γυναικὸς κακίω ἀκοῦσαι δέννος μέγιστός ἐστι</quote> </cit>. This </gloss>

data.quote:
παρὰ δὲ τοῖσι Πέρσῃσι γυναικὸς κακίω ἀκοῦσαι
									δέννος μέγιστός ἐστι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_759"> with dat. is not merely ‘with,’ but implies a <emph>continuing strain</emph> of utterance: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 108"> <title>El.</title> 108</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ κωκυτῷ τῶνδε πατρῴων | πρὸ θυρῶν ἠχὼ πᾶσι προφωνεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 315">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 315</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ δάκρυσι καὶ | γόοισι τὸν θανόντα πατέρα... καταστένουσ’ ἔχεις</quote> </cit> (thou art <emph>ever</emph> lamenting).—Others explain <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> as (<emph>a</emph>) ‘in addition to,’ which implies too sharp a contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">δεννάσεις</quote>, esp. without <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>: (<emph>b</emph>) ‘with a view to,’ i.e. ‘in order to blame me.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1555">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1555</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐπ’ ὀνείδεσιν οὐδ’ ἐπὶ χάρμασιν | ἀλλ’ ὀδύναισι λέγω</quote> </cit> (‘not <emph>for</emph> insult or spiteful joy, but <emph>in</emph> pain’). Here, however, that sense would be weak. —For Dobree's </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπὶ κωκυτῷ τῶνδε πατρῴων | πρὸ θυρῶν ἠχὼ πᾶσι
									προφωνεῖν

ἐπὶ δάκρυσι καὶ | γόοισι τὸν θανόντα πατέρα...
									καταστένουσ’ ἔχεις

οὐκ ἐπ’ ὀνείδεσιν οὐδ’ ἐπὶ χάρμασιν | ἀλλ’
									ὀδύναισι λέγω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_759">, cp. <title>Plut.</title> 64 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι μὰ τὴν Δήμητρα χαιρήσεις ἔτι</quote>. It is plausible, and may be right. But I prefer <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐπὶ</emph> ψόγοισι</quote>, because (in the sense explained above) it is so fitting when an impatient man breaks off a dialogue which has irritated him throughout. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγετε_760">. The plur. is addressed to the two <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολοι</quote> who had ushered the sisters into the house (578, <quote xml:lang="grc">κομίζετ’ εἴσω, δμῶες</quote>). So at 491 the plur. is used, <quote xml:lang="grc">καλεῖτ’</quote>. And, in general, such orders are usu. given in the plur., or by <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> with 3rd pers. (as <ref n="Soph. OT 1069"> <title>O. T.</title> 1069</ref>). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 931">Cp. 931</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσιν ἄγουσιν</quote>. This is against Wecklein's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγ_ἄγε_760">. The objection to L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγαγε_760"> is not only the sing. number, but also the fact that the 2nd aor. imperat. act. (and midd.) of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγω</quote> does not seem to have been used in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μῖσος_760">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 991"> <title>Ph.</title> 991</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ μῖσος</quote> (Odysseus): so <quote xml:lang="grc">μίσημα, στύγος, στύγημα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ὄμματ_760">: <ref n="Xen. Hiero 1. 14">Xen. <title>Hier.</title> 1. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς... ἐθέλει τυράννου κατ’ ὀφθαλμοὺς κατηγορεῖν</quote> (‘to his face’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρόντι_πλησία_760">. The accumulation of words for ‘presence’ marks his vehement anger: cp. Haemon's pleonasm in 764, and <ref n="Soph. OT 430"> <title>O. T.</title> 430</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμοιγε_762"> is placed as if it were to be common to both the clauses (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε... τε</quote>), but the constr. changes: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 913"> <title>El.</title> 913</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδὲ μὲν δὴ μητρὸς οὔθ’ ὁ νοῦς φιλεῖ | τοιαῦτα πράσσειν οὔτε δρῶσ’ ἐλάνθανεν</quote> </cit>.— For </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ οὐδὲ μὲν δὴ μητρὸς οὔθ’ ὁ νοῦς φιλεῖ |
									τοιαῦτα πράσσειν οὔτε δρῶσ’ ἐλάνθανεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_762"> followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_762"> cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1397"> <title>O. C.</title> 1397</ref> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδαμά_762">, neut. plur. adv.: this form is required by metre in 830, as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμᾷ</quote> (Doric) in 874: L always gives <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμᾶι</quote>: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1104"> <title>O. C.</title> 1104</ref>.</gloss>
