<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ὀφθαλμοῖς_762">, <emph>with</emph> them (the instrumental <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, 962, 1003, 1201): an epic phrase, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.587"> <title>Il.</title> 1.587</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὀφθαλμοῖσιν ἴδωμαι</quote> </cit>, etc.: so oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὄμμασιν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 241"> <title>Tr.</title> 241</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_765">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 643">cp. 643</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_θέλουσι_765">, i.e. any who can endure it. Cp. the words of Teiresias, 1087.—Haemon now finally quits the scene. The deuteragonist is thus set free for the parts of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄγγελος</quote> and the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐξάγγελος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ταχύς_766">, in haste <emph>caused by</emph> wrath: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 7.111"> <title>Il.</title> 7.111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ ἔθελ’ ἐξ ἔριδος σεῦ ἀμείμονι φωτὶ/ μάχεσθαι</quote> </cit>, out of mere rivalry.</gloss>

data.quote:
μηδ’ ἔθελ’ ἐξ ἔριδος σεῦ ἀμείμονι φωτὶ/
									μάχεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρύς_766">, resentful: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 673"> <title>O. T.</title> 673</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρὺς δ’, ὅταν | θυμοῦ περάσῃς</quote> </cit>; so as epith. of <quote xml:lang="grc">μῆνις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1328"> <title>O. C.</title> 1328</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργή</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 368"> <title>Ph.</title> 368</ref>). The sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρύ</quote> in 1251 is different. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεῖζον_ἄνδρα_768"> is said in answer to their hint of fear:—let his passion touch the human limit, aye, or overpass it. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 598"> <title>O. C.</title> 598</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί γὰρ τὸ μεῖζον ἢ κατ’ ἄνθρωπον νοσεῖς</quote> </cit>; For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ = ἄνθρωπος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 567"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 567</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
τί γὰρ τὸ μεῖζον ἢ κατ’ ἄνθρωπον
									νοσεῖς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰών_768">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 351"> <title>Ph.</title> 351</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ τἀπὶ Τροίᾳ πέργαμ’</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_οὖν_769"> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 688">cp. 688</ref>, 722.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὼ_τώδε_769">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 561">cp. 561</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τώ</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OT 1472"> <title>O. T.</title> 1472</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖν</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OC 1600"> <title>O. C.</title> 1600</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τώ</quote>), <ref n="Soph. El. 977"> <title>El.</title> 977</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τώδε τώ</quote>)—all fem.—Attic inscriptions of c. 450— 320 B.C. present numerous instances of fem. dual <quote xml:lang="grc">τώ, τοῖν, τούτοιν, οἶν</quote>, but no instance of fem. dual <quote xml:lang="grc">τά, ταῖν, ταύταιν</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷν</quote>. (Meisterhans p. 50.) Hitherto the gen. and dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖν, ταῖνδε, ταύταιν</quote> have been retained even by those edd. who give <quote xml:lang="grc">τώ, τώδε</quote>, etc. (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1462"> <title>O. T.</title> 1462</ref>, 1504: <ref n="Soph. OC 445"> <title>O. C.</title> 445</ref>, 859, 1149, 1290, etc.). But, so far as epigraphic evidence goes, the distinction is arbitrary.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_769"> with the whole phrase </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατακτ_νοεῖς_769"> rather than with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατακτ_769">. alone (for no minor penalty is in view): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 726">cp. 726</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θιγοῦσαν_771">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 546">cp. 546</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_οὖν_771">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 489">cp. 489</ref>, 741. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_772"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεύει_772">; (her doom having been fixed,) by what fate <emph>do</emph> you purpose to slay her? For <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> thus following the interrog., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1314">cp. 1314</ref>. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 278">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 278</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίου χρόνου δὲ καὶ πεπόρθηται πόλις</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 515">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 515</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς καί νιν ἐξεπράξατ’;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ποίου χρόνου δὲ καὶ πεπόρθηται πόλις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφε_772"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτήν</foreign>, Antigone: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 44">cp. 44</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθα_773"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε ἔνθα</foreign>, as <ref n="Soph. OT 796"> <title>O. T.</title> 796</ref>: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 188"> <title>O. C.</title> 188</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγε...με... | ἵν’ ἄν</quote> </cit> etc. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 486"> <title>Ph.</title> 486</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μ’ ἀφῇς | ἔρημον οὕτω χωρὶς ἀνθρώπων στίβου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πετρώδει_κατώρυχι_774">, ‘in a rocky cavern’; schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὑπογείῳ σπηλαίῳ</quote>. Verse 773 shows that Creon is not yet thinking of any particular spot. And <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῶρυξ</quote> shows that he is not thinking of some merely natural grotto or cavern. This word, usu. an adj., here a subst., means a cavern, or chamber, excavated by man's hand: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 1002">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1002</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσοῦ παλαιαὶ Πριαμιδῶν κατώρυχες</quote> </cit>. So the place is described by <quote xml:lang="grc">κατασκαφής</quote> (891). The <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῶρυξ</quote> actually used was near the furthest and highest part of the plain, where Polyneices lay (1197). What, then, was the poet's conception? He seems to suppose the existence of tombs artificially constructed in the rocky <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι</quote> (411) which bordered on the Theban plain. In one of these tombs— chosen for the remoteness of its situation (773)—Antigone is to be immured. The general type of sepulchral chamber supposed here can be illustrated from actual remains which have been discovered in Greece: see below on vv. 1216 ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_μόνον_775">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>, so much as to be barely an expiation; only just enough to avoid the <quote xml:lang="grc">μίασμα</quote>. The conjectural change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_775"> into </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσον_775"> (adopted by several edd.) would be necessary if the indic. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> had to be supplied, since we could not say <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτον ὡς</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος ἐστί</quote>. That change is unnecessary, because it is the inf. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> that is understood. Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 3">Xen. <title>An.</title> 7. 3</ref>§ 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον μόνον γεύσασθαι</quote>, and see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 790"> <title>O. C.</title> 790</ref> for other instances where the inf. is expressed. The inf. is understood, as here, in <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 8">Xen. <title>An.</title> 7. 8</ref>§ 19 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντες πρόβατα ὅσον θύματα</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>): so <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 8.3.20"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 7. 3 § 20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων...ὅσον ἐφόδιον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγος_775"> was used by Soph. in his lost <title>Phaedra</title> to denote <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅγνισμα θυσίας</quote> (Hesych. 1. 63), i.e. ‘an expiatory sacrifice’ (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 325">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 325</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅγνισμα φόνου</quote> </cit>). In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 154">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 154</ref> also <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote> has been taken as=‘expiation,’ but there it seems rather to be ‘pollution.’ Cp. the schol. here: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθος παλαιόν, ὥστε τὸν βουλόμενον καθειργνύναι τινὰ ἀφοσιοῦσθαι βραχὺ τιθέντα τροφῆς, καὶ ὑπενόουν κάθαρσιν τὸ τοιοῦτο, ἵνα μὴ δοκῶσι λιμῷ ἀναιρεῖν· τοῦτο γὰρ ἀσεβές</quote>.—Curtius, <title>Etym.</title> 5th ed., § 118, would write <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅγος</quote> here. He distinguishes two roots. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγ‐, ἄγος</quote>, ‘guilt,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγής</quote>, ‘accursed’; Sanskr. <foreign xml:lang="sanskrit">Aa/g-as</foreign>, ‘vexation,’ etc. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγ‐, ἅγος</quote>, ‘consecration, sacrifice,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅγιος</quote>, etc.: Sanskr. <foreign xml:lang="sanskrit">jag-.</foreign> On the other hand the analogy of <foreign xml:lang="lat">piaculum</foreign> suggests that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote> might combine the sense of ‘expiation’ with that of ‘pollution.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶσ_776">: cp. on 178. The sense is, ‘in order that the whole city may not be defiled’ (as it otherwise would be): <quote xml:lang="grc">μίασμ’ ὑπεκφύγῃ = μὴ μιανθῇ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_σέβει_777">. Polyneices had come to destroy the shrines of the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_θανεῖν_778"> in acc. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεύξεται_778">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 711">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 711</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνειν τὰ πρόσφορα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1106"> <title>O. C.</title> 1106</ref> (n.): fr. 824 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὰ καὶ τὰ τυγχάνων</quote>. This comparatively rare constr. has here been influenced by <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτουμένη</quote>: though it is unnecessary to refer the acc. to the partic. only, or to understand, ‘will successfully ask.’ See, however, <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.23">Her. 5.23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν παρὰ Δαρείου αἰτήσας ἔτυχε...δωρεήν</quote> </cit>: 9. 109 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα γὰρ τεύξεσθαι αἰτήσασαν</quote>: where, in both instances, the acc. depends on the partic. only.—We could not well take <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ θανεῖν</quote> here as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε μὴ θανεῖν</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">κωλύω τὸ μὴ ποιεῖν τι</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_τηνικαῦτα_779">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 552">cp. 552</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀν_779">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐν, Ἅιδου</quote>: 659. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνίκατε_μάχαν_781">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 441"> <title>Tr.</title> 441</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρωτι μέν νυν ὅστις ἀντανίσταται, | πύκτης ὅπως ἐς χεῖρας, οὐ καλῶς φρονεῖ</quote> </cit>. Eur. fr. 433 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρωτα, πάντων δυσμαχώτατον θεόν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 196D">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 196D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴν εἴς γε ἀνδρίαν Ἔρωτι οὐδὲ Ἄρης ἀνθίσταται· οὐ γὰρ ἔχει Ἔρωτα Ἄρης, ἀλλ’ Ἔρως Ἄρη</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἔρωτι μέν νυν ὅστις ἀντανίσταται, | πύκτης
									ὅπως ἐς χεῖρας, οὐ καλῶς φρονεῖ

καὶ μὴν εἴς γε ἀνδρίαν Ἔρωτι οὐδὲ Ἄρης
									ἀνθίσταται· οὐ γὰρ ἔχει Ἔρωτα Ἄρης, ἀλλ’ Ἔρως Ἄρη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πίπτεις_782"> who <emph>fallest upon</emph> men's possessions; who makest havoc of their wealth and fortunes. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.526"> <title>Od.</title> 24.526</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’ ἔπεσον προμάχοις</quote> </cit>, ‘they fell on the fore-fighters’: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπίπτειν</quote> is oft. said of the attacks of disease or passion. Love makes men reckless of possessions: it can bring ruin on great houses and proud cities. Sophocles himself has given us the best commentary: see <ref n="Soph. Trach. 431"> <title>Tr.</title> 431</ref>, referring to the capture of Oechalia by Heracles, who loved Iolè, the daughter of its king, Eurytus: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ταύτης πόθῳ | πόλις δαμείη πᾶσα, κοὐχ ἡ Λυδία | πέρσειεν αὐτήν, ἀλλ’ ὁ τῆσδ’ ἔρως φανείς</quote>. The same thought is finely expressed by Eur. , in a choral ode to <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως</quote>, which this passage has certainly helped to inspire ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 525">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 525</ref> ff.): <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρωτα δέ, τὸν τύραννον ἀνδρῶν, | ...οὐ σεβίζομεν, | πέρθοντα καὶ διὰ πάσας | ἰόντα συμφορᾶς | θνατοῖς, ὅταν ἔλθῃ</quote>. Troy was sacked for the sake of Helen,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλέναυς, ἕλανδρος, ἑλέπτολις</quote>. Medea betrayed her father's treasure to Jason (cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 480">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 480</ref>). The <emph>resistless power</emph> of Love is the central thought of this ode. All that men prize most becomes his prey.—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_παρειαῖς_783">. Ionof Chios (<emph>ap.</emph> Athen. 603 E) describes Soph. as saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς καλῶς Φρύνιχος</quote> (the tragic poet, <foreign xml:lang="lat">flor. c.</foreign> 490) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποίησεν εἴπας· λάμπει δ’ ἐπὶ πορφυρέαις παρῇσι φῶς ἔρωτος</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Mor. 760">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 760</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">σκόπει τοίνυν...τοῖς ἀρηΐοις ἔργοις ὅσον Ἔρως περίεστιν, οὐκ ἀργὸς ὤν, ὡς Εὐριπίδης ἔλεγεν, οὐδὲ ἀστράτευτος, οὐδ’ ἐν μαλακαῖσιν [ἐννυχεύ]ων παρειαῖς νεανίδων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννυχεύεις_783">, keepest thy vigil: perh. here an image suggested by a soldier's night-watch (like Horace's <quote xml:lang="lat">pulcris excubat in genis</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> Cupido, <title>C.</title> 4. 13. 8); cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 6.4.27">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 6.4.27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δὲ τοῖς ὅπλοις ἐνυκτέρευον</quote> </cit>, and so <quote xml:lang="grc">νυκτοφυλακεῖν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. Rom 5.3.94">Shaksp. <title>Rom.</title> 5. 3. 94</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘beauty's ensign yet Is crimson in thy lips and in thy cheeks.’</quote> </cit> Gray, <title>Progress of Poesy</title> 1. 3. 16 ‘O'er her warm cheek and rising bosom move The bloom of young desire and purple light of love.’ </gloss>

data.quote:
‘beauty's ensign yet Is crimson in thy lips
									and in thy cheeks.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερπόντιος_785">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1301">cp. 1301</ref>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτόπιος</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 1340), <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλάσσιος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1411"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1411</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">θυραῖος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 313"> <title>El.</title> 313</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">παράκτιος</quote> (<ref n="Eur. IT 1424">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1424</ref>), etc. So Eur. fr. 434 (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπὶ πόντον ἔρχεται</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Mor. 760">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 760</ref>D quotes an unknown poet, on <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως</quote>:—<quote xml:lang="grc">πῦρ καὶ θάλασσαν καὶ πνοὰς τὰς αἰθέρος | περᾶν ἕτοιμος</quote>. <ref n="Lucr. 1.18">Lucr. 1. 18</ref>(Venus moves) <quote xml:lang="lat">per maria ac montes fluviosque rapaces Frondiferasque domos avium camposque virentes.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_αὐλαῖς_785">. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρόνομοι αὐλαί</quote> =dwellings in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρὸς νεμόμενος</quote>, pastoral wilds: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 349">cp. 349</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγραύλου</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1103"> <title>O. T.</title> 1103</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάκες ἀγρόνομοι</quote> </cit>, upland pastures. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 181"> <title>El.</title> 181</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτὴ βούνομος</quote> </cit>, a shore on which oxen are pastured (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 26"> <title>O. T.</title> 26</ref>).—Some take the sense to be, ‘Love conquers not man only, but fishes and wild beasts’; cp. fr. 856.9 (<quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσέρχεται μὲν ἰχθύων πλωτῷ γένει, | ἔνεστι δ’ ἐν χέρσου τετρασκελεῖ γονῇ</quote>. (How could <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερπόντιος</quote> imply a visit to the fish?) Others find a reference to Paris carrying Helen over the Aegean, Aphrodite visiting Anchises in the pastures of Ida, etc. Rather the poet is merely saying, quite generally, how boundless is the range of Love. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_ἀθανάτων_787">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 443"> <title>Tr.</title> 443</ref> (of Love) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος γὰρ ἄρχει καὶ θεῶν ὅπως θέλει</quote>: fr. 856. 13 (<quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τίν’ οὐ παλαίουσ’ ἐς τρὶς ἐκβάλλει θεῶν</quote>; Eur. fr. 434 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως γὰρ ἄνδρας οὐ μόνους ἐπέρχεται, | οὐδ’ αὖ γυναῖκας, ἀλλὰ καὶ θεῶν ἄνω | ψυχὰς χαράσσει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὲ_ἐστι_787">, = <foreign xml:lang="grc">σὲ δύναται φεύγειν</foreign>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 904">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 904</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πόλεμος ἄπορα πόριμος</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1090"> <title>Ag.</title> 1090</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">στέγην</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ ξυνίστορα | ...κακά</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 3.3.9">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3.3.9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμονες δ’ ἦσαν τὰ προσήκοντα</quote> </cit>: Isae. or. 5 § 26 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξαρνοί εἰσι τὰ ὡμολογημένα</quote>: [Plat. ] <title>Alcib. II.</title> 141 D <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι δέ σε οὐκ ἀνήκοον εἶναι ἔνιά γε...γεγενημένα</quote>. Similarly with a subst., <ref n="Plat. Apol. 18">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 18</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μετέωρα φροντιστής</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_γ_789">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> with the repeated <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1101"> <title>O. T.</title> 1101</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1116."> <title>Ph.</title> 1116.</ref>—The MS. <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’</quote> </emph> could mean only, ‘in the case of’ (and so, ‘among’): a use which is not adequately supported by <ref>Aristeid. <title>Pan.</title> 1. 96</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνῃ τῇ πόλει ἐπὶ τῶν Ἑλληνικῶν</quote>, where he means, ‘in the case of’ (i.e., ‘so far as they are concerned’). Nor could <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’</quote> be an adverb (‘moreover,’ <ref n="Soph. OT 181"> <title>O. T.</title> 181</ref>), as some take it.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἔχων_789">: <title>Phaear.</title> 239 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὴρ ἔχων ἔρωτα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδίκους_791"> proleptic: cp. on 475: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 106"> <title>Tr.</title> 106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνάζειν ἀδακρύτων βλεφάρων πόθον</quote> </cit> (so that they shall not weep).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρασπᾷς_791"> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 298">cp. 298</ref>), a metaphor from a driver jerking his horses aside out of their course: <ref n="Soph. El. 732"> <title>El.</title> 732</ref> (the charioteer) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω παρασπᾷ</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἵππους</quote>), pulls them aside, out of the crowd of chariots. The word is fig. again in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1185"> <title>O. C.</title> 1185</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ σε... παρασπάσει | γνώμης</quote> </cit>, pluck thee from thy resolve. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύναιμον_794">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυναίμων</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος‐ἀνδρῶν</quote> forms one notion: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 862">cp. 862</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1390"> <title>El.</title> 1390</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν φρενῶν ὄνειρον</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 952"> <title>Ph.</title> 952</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα πέτρας δίπυλον</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 325">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 325</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ματρῷον ἅγνισμα...φόνου.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_794"> with aor. part.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 22">cp. 22</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταράξας_794">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">excitasti.</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.153">Dem. or. 18 § 153</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ εἰδῆτε ἡλίκα πράγματα ἡ μιαρὰ κεφαλὴ ταράξασα αὕτη δίκην οὐκ ἔδωκεν</quote> </cit>. In this sense later prose has also <quote xml:lang="grc">συνταράττω</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἵν’ εἰδῆτε ἡλίκα πράγματα ἡ μιαρὰ κεφαλὴ
									ταράξασα αὕτη δίκην οὐκ ἔδωκεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργής_795">, ‘clearly seen,’ ‘present to the lover's sight,’ marks the vivid appeal to the senses, in contrast with the invisible and spiritual majesty of the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμοί</quote> which Love overrides. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναργής</quote> as =‘before our eyes,’ ‘in bodily presence,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 910"> <title>O. C.</title> 910</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 11"> <title>Tr.</title> 11</ref>, 224.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλεφάρωνἵμερος_795">, love-influence from the eyes, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐλέκτρου_νύμφας_795">, of the fair bride. Both genitives are possessive, but <quote xml:lang="grc">βλεφάρων</quote> goes more closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵμερος</quote>, denoting the latter's source. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 929">Cp. 929</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 729"> <title>O. C.</title> 729</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμμάτων... | φόβον...τῆς ἐμῆς ἐπεισόδου</quote> </cit>, fear, shown in your eyes (possess. gen.), of my entrance (objective gen.). In <title>Phaedr.</title> 251 B Plato describes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵμερος</quote> as the desire infused into the soul by an emanation of beauty (<quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλους ἀπορροή</quote>) proceeding from the beloved, and received through the eyes of the lover (<quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τῶν ὀμμάτων</quote>). So the soul is spoken of (<ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 251e"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> E</ref>) as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποχετευσαμένη ἵμερον</quote>, ‘having refreshed herself with the love-shower’ or ‘effluence of beauty.’ And <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵμερος</quote> itself receives fanciful derivations, as <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 251c"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 251 C</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖθεν μέρη ἐπιόντα καὶ ῥέοντα, ἃ δὴ διὰ ταῦτα ἵμερος καλεῖται</quote> (i.e. from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰέναι μέρη</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῖν</quote>): while in <ref n="Plat. Crat. 419"> <title>Crat.</title> 419</ref>E it is explained by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱέμενος ῥεῖ</quote>. The real origin of the word is prob. from rt. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰς</quote>, ‘wish,’ whence <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰότητι</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰς‐μήνη</quote>, <title>Desiderata.</title> Curt. § 617. So Soph. fr. 161 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμμάτων ἄπο | λόγχας ἵησιν</quote>: 733 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμμάτειος πόθος</quote>: 430 (Hippodameia speaking of Pelops), <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιάνδ’ ἐν ὄψει λίγγα θηρατηρίαν | ἔρωτος, ἀστραπήν τιν’ ὀμμάτων, ἔχει</quote> (‘such a subduing arrow of love, a lightning from the eyes’): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 742">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 742</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαλθακὸν ὀμμάτων βέλος, | δηξίθυμον ἔρωτος ἄνθος</quote> </cit>: <title>Suppl.</title> 1004 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμματος θελκτήριον | τόξευμ’ ἔπεμψεν, ἱμέρου νικώμενος</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 525">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 525</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως, Ἔρως ὃ κατ’ ὀμμάτων | στάζεις πόθον, εἰσάγων γλυκεῖαν | ψυχαῖς χάριν οὓς ἐπιστρατεύσῃ</quote> </cit> [i.e. ‘on the eyes’ of mortals: better <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...στάζων</quote>, or else <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἀπ’</quote>—.] </gloss>

data.quote:
μαλθακὸν ὀμμάτων βέλος, | δηξίθυμον ἔρωτος
									ἄνθος

Ἔρως, Ἔρως ὃ κατ’ ὀμμάτων | στάζεις πόθον,
									εἰσάγων γλυκεῖαν | ψυχαῖς χάριν οὓς ἐπιστρατεύσῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐλέκτρου_795">, epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 515"> <title>Tr.</title> 515</ref>. Cp. <title>Anthol. P.</title> 7. 649 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐλεγχέος θαλάμου</quote> (happy nuptials). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεδρος_ἀρχαῖς_797">. I leave these words in the text, without marking them as corrupt, because the case against them is not decisive, while no emendation is certain. But I strongly suspect them. If sound, they mean that the love inspired by the maiden's eyes is a power ‘enthroned in sway by the side of the great laws.’ The great laws are those ‘unwritten’ moral laws which most men feel and acknowledge (cp. on 454 f.); here, especially, the law of loyalty to country, the law of obedience to parents. In Haemon's case, love has shown that it is at least of equal force with these <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμοί</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεδρος_797">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1267"> <title>O. C.</title> 1267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὶ σύνθακος θρόνων | Αἰδώς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1382"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη ξύνεδρος Ζηνὸς ἀρχαίοις νόμοις</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title> 8. 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς ξενίου πάρεδρος | ...Θέμις</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἀρχαῖς_797">, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 699">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 699</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμνοὶ δ’ ἐν ἀρχαῖς ἥμενοι κατὰ πτόλιν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 897"> <title>Or.</title> 897</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν δύνηται πόλεος ἔν τ’ ἀρχαῖσιν ᾖ</quote>. Cp. also on 744.</gloss>

data.quote: σεμνοὶ δ’ ἐν ἀρχαῖς ἥμενοι κατὰ πτόλιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_δρᾶν_797">. He supposes that <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρα δρᾶν</quote> became, first, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεδρον</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΕΡΑΔΡΑΝ— ΠΑΡΕΔΡΟΝ</quote>). Then, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεδρον θεσμῶν</quote> seeming obscure, a marginal gloss <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀρχαῖς</quote> was added. This gloss came into the text, dislodging <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεδρον</quote> became <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεδρος</quote>, to agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵμερος</quote>. The original sense, then, was: ‘the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵμερος</quote> prevails, so that one transgresses the great <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμοί</quote>.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1506"> <title>El.</title> 1506</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις πέρα πράσσειν γε τῶν νόμων θέλει</quote> </cit>. This suits, too, the following lines, where the Chorus says, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὐτὸς</quote> (i.e., like Haemon) <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμῶν | ἔξω φέρομαι</quote>.—See Appendix. </gloss>

data.quote: ὅστις πέρα πράσσειν γε τῶν νόμων θέλει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπαίζει_800">, ‘wreaks her will’ <emph>in</emph> that contest which </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νικᾷ_800"> implies. We find <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπαίζω</quote> with a dat. (1) of the object, as <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 4.134">Her. 4.134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπαίζοντας ἡμῖν</quote> </cit>, ‘mocking us’: (2) of the sphere, as <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 975">Ar. <title>Th.</title> 975</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χοροῖσιν ἐμπαίζει</quote>, ‘sports in dances.’ The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπαίζει</quote> here might also be explained as (<emph>a</emph>) in the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴμερος</quote>, or the <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέφαρα</quote>, i.e. by their agency: or (<emph>b</emph>) ‘on her victim.’ But the interpretation first given appears simpler. (Cp. Vergil's absol. use of <foreign xml:lang="lat">illudere,</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Verg. G. 1.181"> <title>G.</title> 1. 181,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Tum variae illudant pestes.</quote> </cit>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὐτὸς_ἔξω_801">: i.e. like Haemon, I also am moved to rebel against Creon's sentence, and to take Antigone's part.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρομαι_801">, a proverbial image from the race-course: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 993">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 993</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνον ὅπως | μή σ’ ὁ θυμὸς ἁρπάσας | ἐκτὸς οἴσει τῶν ἐλαῶν</quote> </cit>, because some olives marked the limits of the course at the end of the race-course (schol. <emph>ad loc.</emph>), where the chariots turned, and where the horses were most likely to swerve or bolt. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 414B">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 414B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἐπισκοπεῖς με ὥσπερ ἐκτὸς δρόμου φερόμενον, ἐπειδὰν λείου ἐπιλάβωμαι</quote> </cit> (when I get on smooth ground). <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 883">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω δὲ δρόμου φέρομαι λύσσης | πνεύματι μάργῳ</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 853">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 853</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω δ’ ἐλαύνων τοῦ φρονεῖν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μόνον ὅπως | μή σ’ ὁ θυμὸς ἁρπάσας | ἐκτὸς
									οἴσει τῶν ἐλαῶν

οὐ γὰρ ἐπισκοπεῖς με ὥσπερ ἐκτὸς δρόμου
									φερόμενον, ἐπειδὰν λείου ἐπιλάβωμαι

ἔξω δὲ δρόμου φέρομαι λύσσης | πνεύματι
									μάργῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσχειν_δ_801">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 817">cp. 817</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχουσ’</quote>: 820 <quote xml:lang="grc">λαχοῦσ’</quote>, by the rule of continuity (<quote xml:lang="grc">συνάφεια</quote>) in anapaestic systems. In lyrics such elision is rarer (<ref target="cl350">see on 350</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πηγάς_803">, not, the sources or springs, but the streams, of tears: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 852"> <title>Tr.</title> 852</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρωγεν παγὰ δακρύων</quote> </cit>, and oft.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 479"> <title>O. C.</title> 479</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πηγάς</quote>=the water gushing from the bowl. On the other hand in fr. 658 <quote xml:lang="grc">νυκτός...πηγάς</quote>=‘the sources of night’ (the west). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκοίτην_804">. The question between Doric and Attic forms in tragic anapaests cannot be decided by a rigid rule. It depends on the presence or absence of a lyric character. Thus in 110 f. the Doricisms are justified by the purely lyric stamp of the anapaests. Here, Attic forms are preferable. The lyric strains</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλαμον_804"> here has ref. to its special sense, ‘bridal-chamber’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 891">cp. 891</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφεῖον</quote>, 1207 <quote xml:lang="grc">παστάδα</quote>. So oft. in epitaphs on the unmarried; <title>Anthol. P.</title> 7. 489 (by Sappho) <quote xml:lang="grc">Τιμάδος ἅδε κόνις, τὰν δὴ πρὸ γάμοιο θανοῦσαν | δέξατο Περσεφόνας κυάνεος θάλαμος</quote>. Kaibel <title>Epigrammata</title> 241 (on two young brothers) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δισσοὶ συνόμαιμοι, ἰὼ ξένε, τῷδ’ ὑπὸ τύμβῳ | ἄψαυστοι τέκνων κείμεθα κουριδίων· | Ἱκέσιος κἀγὼ νεαρὰν πληρούμενος ἥβαν | Ἕρμιππος κρυερὸν τόνδ’ ἔχομεν θάλαμον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνύτουσαν_804"> with acc. of place, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 607"> <title>Ai.</title> 607</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύσειν</quote>), <ref n="Soph. OC 1562"> <title>O. C.</title> 1562</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξανύσαι</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 231">cp. 231</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεάταν_δέ_806">. In such an epanaphora <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> regularly precedes <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> (as <ref n="Soph. OT 25"> <title>O. T.</title> 25</ref>, 259; <ref n="Soph. OC 5"> <title>O. C.</title> 5</ref>, 610, etc.); but there are numerous exceptions in Soph. , as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1342"> <title>O. C.</title> 1342</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στήσω σ’ ἄγων, | στήσω δ’ ἐμαυτόν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 633"> <title>Ph.</title> 633</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα λεκτά, πάντα δὲ | τολμητά</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 517"> <title>Tr.</title> 517</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τότ’ ἦν χερός, ἦν δὲ τόξων πάταγος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1147"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλει τὸ πᾶν μοι σπέρμα σῶν ὁμαιμόνων, | κάλει δὲ τὴν τάλαιναν Ἀλκμήνην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: στήσω σ’ ἄγων, | στήσω δ’ ἐμαυτόν
τότ’ ἦν χερός, ἦν δὲ τόξων πάταγος

κάλει τὸ πᾶν μοι σπέρμα σῶν ὁμαιμόνων, | κάλει
									δὲ τὴν τάλαιναν Ἀλκμήνην.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέατον_806">, in contrast with <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις</quote>, is best taken as adv.: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 201">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 201</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέατον τεκέων σώματα λεύσσω</quote> </cit>: cp. the adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">τελευταῖον</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1183"> <title>O. T.</title> 1183</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχατον</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1550"> <title>O. C.</title> 1550</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">πανύστατον</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὔποτ_αὖθις_806">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀψομένην</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 857"> <title>Ai.</title> 857</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥλιον προσεννέπω | πανύστατον δὴ κοὔποτ’ αὖθις ὕστερον</quote> </cit>.—Cp. the passage in Swinburne's <title>Erechtheus</title> where the maiden Chthonia, being about to die, speaks with the Chorus of Athenian Elders:—‘People, old men of my city, lordly wise and hoar of head, | I, a spouseless bride and crownless, but with garlands of the dead, | From the fruitful light turn silent to my dark unchilded bed.’ </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἥλιον προσεννέπω | πανύστατον δὴ κοὔποτ’ αὖθις
									ὕστερον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκοίτας_810"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁ πάντας κοιμίζων</foreign>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 831"> <title>Ai.</title> 831</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶ θ’ ἅμα | πομπαῖον Ἑρμῆν χθόνιον εὖ με κοιμίσαι</quote> </cit>. Blaydes conjectures </gloss>

data.quote:
καλῶ θ’ ἅμα | πομπαῖον Ἑρμῆν χθόνιον εὖ με
									κοιμίσαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγκοινος_810">, very plausibly. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 138"> <title>El.</title> 138</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόν γ’ ἐξ Ἀΐδα | παγκοίνου λίμνας</quote>. But these points may be noted. (1) Though we have had <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίτην</quote> so lately as in v. 804, such a repetition is no safe argument for spuriousness: <ref target="cl76">see on 76</ref>. (2) The 2nd and 3rd syllables of <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίτας = καί νιν</quote> in 828, and <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγκοινος</quote> therefore gives a more exact correspondence; but this proves nothing, since a spondee and a trochee are equally admissible. (See Analysis. Metr.) (3) <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίτας</quote> is <emph>here</emph> a more expressive epithet than <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγκοινος</quote> (‘receiving all’): eyes still bright with life and youth are to suffer the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτέρμονα νήγρετον ὕπνον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγει_ἀκτάν_811">, a rare poet. constr. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγω</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1175"> <title>Ph.</title> 1175</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρῳάδα γᾶν μ’ ἤλπισας ἄξειν</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 861">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 861</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόστοι... | εὖ πράσσοντας ἆγον οἴκους</quote> </cit> (so Porson for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς οἴκους</quote>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 178"> <title>O. T.</title> 178</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτὰν πρὸς ἑσπέρου θεοῦ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔθ_ὕμνος_813">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμέναιος</quote> has not been sung by friends escorting bride and bridegroom to their home; nor has the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιθαλάμιον</quote> been sung in the evening at the door of the bridal chamber. (1) For the procession-song, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 18.492"> <title>Il.</title> 18.492</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφας δ’ ἐκ θαλάμων, δαΐδων ὕπο λαμπομενάων, | ἠγίνεον ἀνὰ ἄστυ· πολὺς δ’ ὑμέναιος ὀρώρει</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 1332">Aristoph. <title>Pax</title> 1332</ref> ff. gives a specimen, with the refrain <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑμήν, Ὑμέναῑ ὦ</quote>. Cp. also <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1736."> <title>Av.</title> 1736.</ref>This was specially called the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμάτειον μέλος</quote> (from the carriage conveying the newly-married couple), <title>Etym. M.</title> p. 145. (2) As to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιθαλάμιος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνος</quote>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιθαλάμιον</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μέλος</quote>), sung in the evening, see Phot. <title>Bibl.</title>p. 321 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὰ ἐπιθαλάμια δὲ τοῖς ἄρτι θαλαμευομένοις ἅμα οἱ ἠΐθεοι καὶ αἱ παρθένοι ἐπὶ τῶν θαλάμων ᾖδον</quote>. Extant specimens are Theocritus <title>Idyll.</title> 18 (for Helen and Menelaus), Catullus <ref n="Catul. 61">61</ref> and <ref n="Catul. 62">62</ref>: for a burlesque, see Lucian <ref n="Plat. Sym. 41."> <title>Symp.</title> 41.</ref>The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμέναιος</quote>, though more specially denoting the procession-song, was a general term for a <quote xml:lang="grc">γαμήλιον ᾆσμα</quote>, and could denote the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιθαλάμιος</quote>, in which <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑμὴν ὦ Ὑμέναιε</quote> was the usual refrain (<ref n="Theoc. 18">Theocr. 18. 58</ref>, <ref n="Catul. 61">Catull. 61. 4</ref> etc.): so <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 3">Pindar <title>P.</title> 3. 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <l>οὐδὲ παμφώνων ἰαχὰν ὑμεναίων, ἅλικες </l> <l>οἷα παρθένοι φιλέοισιν ἑταῖραι </l> <l>ἑσπερίαις ὑποκουρίζεσθ’ ἀοιδαῖς</l> </quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Apollon. 4.1160">Apollon. Rhod. 4. 1160</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφιδίαις ὑμέναιον ἐπὶ προμολῇσιν <note anchored="true" place="inline" xml:lang="eng">(threshold)</note> ἄειδον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
νύμφας δ’ ἐκ θαλάμων, δαΐδων ὕπο λαμπομενάων,
									| ἠγίνεον ἀνὰ ἄστυ· πολὺς δ’ ὑμέναιος ὀρώρει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_ὕμνησεν_813">: we expected <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε ὑμνηθεῖσαν</quote>: a finite verb is substituted for the second participial clause: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 255">cp. 255</ref> f.: <title>O.C.</title> 348 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν...ἀλωμένη, | πολλοῖσι δ’ ἡγεῖται</quote>, with n. there on 351.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπινύμφειος_813">, Dindorf's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπινυμφίδιος_813">, is strongly supported by these facts. (1) In <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title> 1088</ref> Soph. certainly used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινικείῳ</quote> instead of the usual <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινικίῳ</quote>. Cp. above, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 358">Soph. 358</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναίθρεια</quote>. (2) In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 334">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 334</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιτύμβιος</quote> </cit> (restored with certainty by Herm. ) had been corrupted into <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιτυμβίδιος</quote>. Bergk's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_νυμφείοις_813"> (‘for crown of nuptials’) is quite possible (cp. n. on 568); but an epithet for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνος</quote> is decidedly preferable here. Bergk relies on the schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">λείπει θύραις ἢ κοίταις</quote>, which suggests that the Schol. read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ νυμφιδίοις</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφείοις</quote>); but, if this were so, the fact would have little weight. The corruption would have been easy.— SchHerm. ütz defends </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπινυμφίδιος_813_2"> as metrically possible. But, though it is possible that a logaoedic dactyl might replace a spondee here, the latter is at least better suited to the grave and mournful rhythm. The antistrophic verse (831) ends with <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκλαύτοις</quote>. So v. 816 ends with <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφεύσω</quote>, and 833 with <quote xml:lang="grc">κατευνάζει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀχέροντι_νυμφεύσω_816">: cp. on 654. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξιφέων_820"> (possessive gen.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίχειρα_820">, ‘the wages of swords,’ i.e. the reward of strife with the sword,—viz. a violent death. The gen. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίχειρα</quote> always denotes that <emph>for which</emph> the reward is given, as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 318">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 318</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψηγόρου | γλώσσης</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Wasps 581">Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 581</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτης</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς δίκης</quote>), <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 608C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 608C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετῆς</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Dem. L. 3.38">[Dem. ] <title>Epist.</title> p. 1484. 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν...πεπονημένων</quote> </cit>. Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ξιφέων</quote> can hardly be a subjective gen., ‘the reward which the sword <emph>gives</emph>’; though the meaning is the same. The ironical sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίχειρα</quote> occurs in Attic prose as well as verse; Antiphon or. 1 § 20 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μὲν διακονήσασα</quote> (in the murder) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει τὰ ἐπίχειρα ὦν ἀξία ἦν</quote> (torture and death). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1382"> <title>El.</title> 1382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀπιτίμια | τῆς δυσσεβείας. <emph>ξιφέων</emph> </quote> </cit> might be poet. plur. for sing., as in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 812">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 812</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκ τε δεξιᾶς | ξίφη καθαρπάζουσιν</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1398"> <title>Or.</title> 1398</ref>(cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρα</quote>, etc.); but it is rather an ordinary plural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόνομος_821">, i.e. of your own free will. No one constrained her to do the act for which she suffers. She knew that death would be the consequence, and she chose it. The word is fitting, since she has set her laws (the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν νόμιμα</quote>) above Creon's. The implied contrast is with the helpless victims of disease or of war.—The word could not mean, ‘by an ordinance <emph>peculiar to your case</emph>,’ i.e. ‘by the unique doom of a living death.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_821"> strengthens </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνη_821">, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1063"> <title>Tr.</title> 1063</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀΐδην_821"> seems preferable to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἅιδην_821"> in the paroemiac. Cp. on 804.— Acc. of motion, like <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμους στείχειν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 643"> <title>O. C.</title> 643</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤκουσα_δή_823">. The Chorus has said, ‘No mortal's fate was ever like thine.’ She continues: ‘I have heard before now (<quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote>) how Niobe perished,—by a doom like mine.’ To which the Chorus reply that Niobe was not a mere mortal (834).—The Theban princess remembers the fate of the Theban queen. Niobe, daughter of Tantalus, married Amphion, king of Thebes. She vaunted that she had borne many children, while Leto had borne only two. Wherefore those two, Apollo and Artemis, slew all her sons and daughters,—at Thebes, as said the Theban story; but Niobe returned to her old home at Mount Sipylus, and was there turned to stone. (Ovid, <ref n="Ov. Met. 6. 310"> <title>Met.</title> 6. 310</ref>, represents her as carried to Sipylus after the change.) <quote xml:lang="grc">Νιόβη</quote> was the title of lost plays by Aesch. and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυγροτάταν_823">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 305">cp. 305</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκιος</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 966"> <title>Ai.</title> 966</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ πικρὸς τέθνηκεν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξέναν_823">, in relation to Thebes; the foreign wife of the Theban king. Pindar wrote a <quote xml:lang="grc">παιάν</quote> on Niobe's marriage, and said that the Lydian <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμονία</quote> was first used at Thebes on that occasion. ( <title>de Mus.</title> 15.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ταντάλου_825">, gen. of parentage: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 486">cp. 486</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 172"> <title>Ai.</title> 172</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς Ἄρτεμις</quote> </cit>: 952 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς ἡ δεινὴ θεός</quote>. Tantalus, son of Zeus, had his royal seat on Mount Sipylus, which belonged to Phrygia in the older and larger sense of that term. In <quote xml:lang="grc">αεσξη. Νιόβη</quote> (fr. 153) he describes his realm as extending ‘twelve days journey’ from Sipylus westward to Ida.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σιπύλῳ_825">. Mount Sipylus is in the country once called Maeonia, and afterwards Lydia. It is a branch of the Tmolus range (N. of which stood Sardis), and extends in a N.W. direction to the Hermus. Magnesia ‘ad Sipylum’ was on that river's left bank. From a remote age volcanic forces were active in this region, known to the Greeks as the <quote xml:lang="grc">κατακεκαυμένη</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Meteo. 2.8">Arist. <title>Meteor.</title> 2.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενομένου σεισμοῦ τὰ περὶ Σίπυλον ἀνετράπη</quote> </cit>. A city called Tantalis, once situated at Sipylus, was said to have perished by an earthquake, which made a lake. Tantalus, like Niobe, is a type of prosperity plunged by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote> into misery. Here, as in the case of Sodom and Gomorrah, some physical catastrophe was at the root of the tradition.—<ref target="cl831">See on 831</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
γενομένου σεισμοῦ τὰ περὶ Σίπυλον
									ἀνετράπη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτενής_826">, prop. ‘strained,’ ‘intent’ (e.g. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτενὴς ὄψις</quote>), or ‘intense’ (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτενεῖς ὀργαί</quote>): here it denotes the close embrace of the ivy. Cp. <quote xml:lang="lat">hederae sequaces</quote> (Persius <foreign xml:lang="lat">prol.</foreign>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πετραία_δάμασεν_826">, the ‘growth of stone’ (the process of petrifaction) ‘subdued her,’ i.e. passed gradually over her whole form: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Met. 6. 301"> Ovid, <title>Met.</title> 6. 301:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Orba resedit | Exanimes inter natos, natasque, virumque, | Diriguitque malis. Nullos movet aura capillos. | In vultu color est sine sanguine: lumina maestis | Stant immota genis: nihil est in imagine vivi. | Ipsa quoque interius cum duro lingua palato | Congelat, et venae desistunt posse moveri. | Nec flecti cervix, nec bracchia reddere gestus, | Nec pes ire potest: intra quoque viscera saxumst.</quote> </cit> For </gloss>

data.quote:
Orba resedit | Exanimes inter natos, natasque,
									virumque, | Diriguitque malis. Nullos movet aura capillos. | In
									vultu color est sine sanguine: lumina maestis | Stant immota
									genis: nihil est in imagine vivi. | Ipsa quoque interius cum
									duro lingua palato | Congelat, et venae desistunt posse moveri.
									| Nec flecti cervix, nec bracchia reddere gestus, | Nec pes ire
									potest: intra quoque viscera saxumst.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_826"> in <quote xml:lang="grc">πετραία</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310">cp. 1310</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_ὄμρροι_828">, <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The poet is thinking of Niobe's petrified form among the lonely mountain-crags (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 24.614"> <title>Il.</title> 24.614</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δέ που ἐν πέτρῃσιν, ἐν οὔρεσιν οἰοπόλοισιν, | ἐν Σιπύλῳ</quote> </cit>). ‘The rain and the snow never leave her, as she pines with grief’: i.e. she is amid the storms that visit snow-crowned Sipylus throughout the year. By these words the poet wishes to call up a general image of bleak and stormbeaten solitude. Niobe's own weeping is then described by </gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν δέ που ἐν πέτρῃσιν, ἐν οὔρεσιν
									οἰοπόλοισιν, | ἐν Σιπύλῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέγγει_δ_828">, etc. Now, if we kept the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμβρῳ_828">, that dat. would go closely with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τακομέναν_828">: ‘as she melts, flows down, with rain’ (or ‘with water’), ‘the snow never leaves her.’ Thus </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέγγει_δειράδας_828"> would be anticipated, and in a prosaic manner; viz., by words suggesting that the appearance of weeping is due to water trickling down the rock. This is the true reason for preferring </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμβροι_828"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμβρῳ_828_2">. It is no argument against <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμβρῳ</quote> that <quote xml:lang="grc">χιών τ’</quote> would answer to <quote xml:lang="grc">τέγγει θ’</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> irregularly placed, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 258 n.). With <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμβροι, θ’</quote> could still follow <quote xml:lang="grc">τέγγει</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’</quote> is better.—For the constr. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμβροι...χιών τ’ οὐ <emph>λείπει</emph> </quote> (verb agreeing in number with nearest subject) <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1132">cp. 1132</ref> f.: <title>O.C.</title> 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργειν γὰρ αἱ πάθαι με χὡ χρόνος ξυνὼν | μακρὸς διδάσκει</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χιών_828"> is taken by Wecklein as=‘snowwater’ (Eiswasser). The only passage which seems to favour that sense is <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 1066">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1066</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰδαῖα...νάπη | χιόνι κατάρυτα ποταμίᾳ</quote> </cit>, but there the adj. makes the difference: ‘snow carried down streams’ can be only ‘snow-water.’ In <ref n="Eur. Andr. 215"> <title>Andr.</title> 215</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θρῄκην χιόνι τὴν κατάρρυτον</quote> means merely, ‘on which snow falls thickly.’ Cp. Quintus Smyrnaeus 1. 293 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαὶ Σιπύλῳ νιφόεντι.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδαμά_828">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 763">cp. 763</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέγγει_δειράδας_831">. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀφρύς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δειράς</quote> could be said of a mountain, Soph. is here thinking simply of the human form. <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκλαύτους</quote> (L) might be proleptic (cp. n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδίκους</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 791">Soph. 791</ref>), but <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκλαύτοις</quote> is better, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀφρύσι</quote> seems to need an epithet.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοιοτάταν_833">, because the stone into which Niobe was changed may be likened to Antigone's rocky tomb: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 150"> <title>El.</title> 150</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ παντλάμων Νιόβα, σὲ δ’ ἔγωγε νέμω θεόν, | ἅτ’ ἐν τάφῳ πετραίῳ | αἰαῖ δακρύεις</quote> </cit>.—The Niobe in the Uffizi Gallery at Florence will occur to many as offering an ideal type of majestic sorrow and beauty not unworthy to be associated with Antigone, and yet suggesting a contrast no less than a resemblance; the contrast between the desolate mother, and the maiden who is going to join those whom she loves (897); between pride steadfast under divine anger, and the piety that has dared to offend man. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἰὼ παντλάμων Νιόβα, σὲ δ’ ἔγωγε νέμω θεόν, |
									ἅτ’ ἐν τάφῳ πετραίῳ | αἰαῖ δακρύεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_θανοῦσαν_834">. The Chorus desire to console Antigone. There is no element of reproof in their words here. She has likened herself to Niobe. ‘And yet Niobe’—the Chorus say—‘was a goddess, while thou art a mortal. But (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_834">) it will be a great glory for thy memory that thy fate was as the fate of a goddess, in life and in death.’ ‘In life’ (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶσαν_834">), and not only in death (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανοῦσαν_834">), because Niobe, like Antigone, was in the fulness of her vitality when she met her doom. The moments of life through which Antigone is now passing are like the moments through which Niobe passed as she felt the beginning of the change into stone.—Why does Antigone rejoin, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_γελῶμαι_834">? Because her thought had been, ‘my doom is terrible and miserable as Niobe's’; but the Chorus had answered, ‘It is indeed glorious for thee to be as Niobe.’ She had looked for present pity. They had comforted her with the hope of posthumous fame. —See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεός_834">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνη μέν ἐστι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 948">cp. 948</ref>). The absence of a pron., to balance <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς</quote>, is unusual, but it is easy to carry on the subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">τέγγει</quote> in 831. Niobe is of divine race, since her father was the son of Zeus, and her mother the Pleiad Taygetè (or Dionè, one of the Hyades). So in <quote xml:lang="grc">αεσξη. Νιόβη</quote> (fr. 157) her family is described as <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ θεῶν ἀγχίσποροι</quote> (near kin), ...<quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔπω νιν ἐξίτηλον αἷμα δαιμόνων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεογεννής_834">, god-begotten. The peculiarity is that the word is formed directly from <quote xml:lang="grc">γέννα</quote>, and not from the stem of the pass. aor. in use: i.e., we should expect <quote xml:lang="grc">θεογέννητος</quote>. But Pindar could coin <quote xml:lang="grc">θεότιμος</quote> (<title>I.</title> 5. 13) as=<quote xml:lang="grc">θεοτίμητος</quote>. Why, then, should not a poet coin <quote xml:lang="grc">θεογεννής</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">θεογέννητος</quote>? It is of little moment that the extant classical literature happens to present no strictly parallel compound with <quote xml:lang="grc">γέννα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ποντογεννής</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πρωτογεννής</quote> being late Byzantine). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θειογενής_834"> occurs in <title>Orac. Sibyll.</title> (5. 261), but is not classical. The Schol.'s paraphrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">θειοτέρου γένους τυγχάνουσα</quote>, is no token (as some fancy) that he read a gen., such as <quote xml:lang="grc">θείου τε γένους</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίτοι_836"> has an illative force, introducing the next step in the reasoning: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 949">cp. 949</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 855"> <title>O. T.</title> 855</ref>.—L's <quote xml:lang="grc">φθιμένα</quote> should prob. be </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθιμένῃ_836"> (see on <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίτην</quote> 804). The variant <quote xml:lang="grc">φθιμένῳ</quote> (noted in L) is warrantable as the masc. of general statement (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 463">cp. 463</ref>): but it would be extremely harsh, when <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσαν...θανοῦσαν</quote> refers to the same person.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκοῦσαι_836">, ‘e'en to have it said of her’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> meaning, ‘even if there is no other comfort’). This seems a little more expressive than <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀκοῦσαι</quote> (Wecklein), and also slightly more probable palaeographically (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 172"> <title>O. C.</title> 172</ref> cr. n.). The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγ_ἀκοῦσαι_836"> is certainly wrong, since a paroemiac could not begin a new sentence. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούω</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">λέγομαι</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">audio</foreign>) with inf., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.131">Her. 3.131</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργεῖοι ἤκουον μουσικὴν εἶναι Ἑλλήνων πρῶτοι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἀργεῖοι ἤκουον μουσικὴν εἶναι Ἑλλήνων
									πρῶτοι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_σύγκληρα_837">, a lot shared by demigods. <ref n="Plut. Mor. 103">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 103</ref>F <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ γὰρ οὐ μόνον ἑαυτὸν εἰδέναι θνητὸν ὄντα τὴν φύσιν, ἀλλὰ καὶ ὅτι θνητῷ σύγκληρός ἐστι βίῳ καὶ πράγμασι ῥᾳδίως μεθισταμένοις πρὸς τοὐναντίον</quote>: i.e. that one <emph>shares the lot</emph> of humanity at large.—The decisive objection to the MS. reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγκληρα_837"> is the sense of the adj.; for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγκληρος</quote> always means either (1) act., having a share in, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 682">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 682</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγκληρον... κασιγνήτην</quote> </cit>, ‘heiress,’=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίκληρον</quote>, and so</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἰσοθέοις_837"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσι_θεοῖσιν_837">, though easy, is needless. The epic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_837"> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόθεος</quote> might well be allowed in tragic lyrics or anapaests, like the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_837"> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθάνατος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 339">cp. 339</ref> n.): and Aesch. once uses it, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 80">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 80</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόθεος φώς</quote>. [But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόθεος</quote> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote> in <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1169">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1169</ref>, <title>I. A.</title> 626 (dial.), as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόνειρον</quote> has in <ref n="Aesch. PB 547">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 547</ref>.] Note that the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἔγκληρα_837"> would have arisen more easily from <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΟΙΣΙΣΟΘΕΟΙΣΣΥΓΚΛΗΡΑ</quote> than from <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΟΙΣΙΘΕΟΙΣΙΝΣΥΓΚΛΗΡΑ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶσαν_θανοῦσαν_838">. The constr., <quote xml:lang="grc">φθιμένῃ μέγα ἐστίν, ἀκοῦσαι ζῶσαν σύγκληρα λαχεῖν</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώσῃ</quote>), is not rare: cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1. 2">Xen. <title>An.</title> 1. 2</ref>§ 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ξενίᾳ τῷ Ἀρκάδι ἥκειν παραγγέλλει λαβόντα τοὺς ἄνδρας</quote> [though shortly before, <quote xml:lang="grc">παραγγέλλει τῷ Κλεάρχῳ λαβόντι ἥκειν</quote>]: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.1.5"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 3. 1. 5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συμβουλεύει τῷ Ξενοφῶντι ἐλθόντα εἰς Δελφοὺς ἀνακοινῶσαι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.2.1"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 3. 2. 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδοξεν αὐτοῖς προφυλακὰς καταστήσαντας συγκαλεῖν τοὺς στρατιώτας</quote> </cit>. The dat. with the inf. is, in such cases, equally right, but the acc. sometimes excludes an ambiguity. (Cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 913"> <title>O. T.</title> 913</ref>.) <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 479"> <title>El.</title> 479</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπεστί μοι...κλύουσαν</quote> </cit> is not similar.—I can see no reason to suppose the loss of a verse. The fact that six anapaestic verses (817—822) follow the strophe proves nothing. Cp. <ref target="cl110">on 110 f.</ref>, and Append. on 155 ff. </gloss>

data.quote:
συμβουλεύει τῷ Ξενοφῶντι ἐλθόντα εἰς Δελφοὺς
									ἀνακοινῶσαι

ἔδοξεν αὐτοῖς προφυλακὰς καταστήσαντας
									συγκαλεῖν τοὺς στρατιώτας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γελῶμαι_839">: <ref target="cl834">see on 834</ref> ff. Cp. <ref n="Job 17:2">Job xvii. 2</ref>: ‘Are there not mockers with me?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυκτήμονες_843">, an epith. which also implies <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐγενεῖς</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1070"> <title>O. T.</title> 1070</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλουσίῳ χαίρειν γένει</quote> </cit>. So these Theban elders are called <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιρανίδαι</quote> (940), and in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1223"> <title>O. T.</title> 1223</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ γῆς μέγιστα τῆσδ’ ἀεὶ τιμώμενοι</quote> </cit>: as Thebes itself, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγλααί</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 153"> <title>O. T.</title> 153</ref>) and <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλαι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1203"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1203</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote: ὦ γῆς μέγιστα τῆσδ’ ἀεὶ τιμώμενοι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρῆναι_844">: so Polyneices appeals to Oed., <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός νύν σε κρηνῶν καὶ θεῶν ὁμογνίων</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1333"> <title>O. C.</title> 1333</ref>, where see n.): and the dying Ajax invokes the <quote xml:lang="grc">κρῆναι</quote> of Troy (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 862"> <title>Ai.</title> 862</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐαρμάτου_844">: see n. on 148 f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλσος_844">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 5"> <title>El.</title> 5</ref> (Argos) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς οἰστροπλῆγος ἄλσος Ἰνάχου κόρης</quote>, as the scene of Io's visitation by Hera. So Thebes is the ‘sacred precinct’ of Dionysus (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 154">cp. 154</ref>, 1137) and the other <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ ἐγχώριοι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπας_844">, even if human sympathy fails me. Soph. has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπας</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμως</quote>) also in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 122"> <title>Ai.</title> 122</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1338">1338</ref>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπα^</quote> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 563"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 563</ref> (dial.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὔμμ_846"> (Aeolic and epic), as <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 620">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 620</ref>: so dat. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὔμμι</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 247"> <title>O. C.</title> 247</bibl> </cit> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπικτῶμαι_846">, acquire, win: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 671">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 671</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τόνδ’ ἐπικτήσαιο σύμμαχον, θεά</quote>. In this general sense the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_846"> is not inconsistent with what <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπας</quote> implies, viz. that she fails to win human sympathy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵα_847">: cp. on 823 (<quote xml:lang="grc">λυγροτάταν</quote>).—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλων_ἄκλαυτος_847">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1034">cp. 1034</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 910"> <title>Ai.</title> 910</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφαρκτος φίλων</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 1722"> <title>O. C.</title> 1722</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕργμα_τυμβόχωστον_848">, an enclosure (prison) with a sepulchral <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶμα</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕργμα</quote> is the chamber in the rock, <quote xml:lang="grc">πετρώδης κατῶρυξ</quote> (774), in which she is to be immured: the <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶμα</quote> consists of the stones heaped up at the entrance, so as to close it: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1216">cp. 1216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμὸν χώματος λιθοσπαδῆ</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕργμα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἵργω</quote>, to shut in) cp. <title>Part. Anim.</title> 2. 15. 1, where it is said that the eyelashes (<quote xml:lang="grc">βλεφαρίδες</quote>) protect the eyes, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον τὰ χαρακώματα ποιοῦσί τινες πρὸ τῶν ἑργμάτων</quote>, like the palings sometimes placed in front of fences (or hedges). The Berlin ed. (p. 958 b 18) there gives <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐργμάτων</quote> from the MSS. just as here L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργμα</quote>, and as, conversely, MSS. of Pindar (<title>I.</title> 1. 27 etc.) give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕργμα</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργμα = ἔργον</quote>. The old edd. of Arist. give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρυμάτων</quote>.—The reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρμα_848"> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρείδω</quote>)=‘mound’: <title>C. I.</title> 4599 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρισθενὲς ἕρμα θανοῦσιν</quote>: <ref>Kaibel <title>Epigr.</title> 1063. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγήραον ἕρμα</quote>. But this seems less fitting here than the notion of ‘prison’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 886">cp. 886</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περιπτύξαντες, 892 ἀείφρουρος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποταινίου_849">, usu., ‘recent,’ ‘fresh’: here, ‘of a new kind’: cp. fr. 154. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονὰς ποταινίους</quote>. A tomb destined for the dead is to receive the living (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 821">cp. 821</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτοῖς_κυροῦσα_851">. This gives an exact correspondence with 870, <quote xml:lang="grc">κασίγνητε γάμων κυρήσας</quote>, and there is every reason to think that 870 is sound. Further, the origin of L's reading is elucidated. The first </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_851"> is omitted (<cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 532">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 532</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πάρις γὰρ οὔτε συντελὴς πόλις</quote> </cit>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 239"> <title>O. T.</title> 239</ref>); and this poetical license might easily have led a corrector to suppose that the first <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> had been lost. Again, the loss of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυροῦσα_851"> would have been easy after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεκροῖς_851">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέτοικος_852">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 868">cp. 868</ref>, 890: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 934"> <title>O. C.</title> 934</ref>. It is her doom, <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσα τυμβεύειν</quote> (888). She is not a dweller with the living, because her abode is the grave; nor with the dead, because she lives.— The similar phrase—perhaps imitated from this—in <ref n="Eur. Supp. 968">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 968</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἐν τοῖς φθιμένοις | οὔτ’ ἐν ζῶσιν ἀριθμουμένα</quote> (Musgrave <quote xml:lang="grc">κρινομένα</quote>) has no such special point: the Argive widows merely mean that their life is a living death. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προβᾶσ_πολύ_853">: having advanced to the furthest limit of rashness, thou hast struck heavily (<quote xml:lang="grc">πολύ</quote>) against the lofty pedestal of Justice. We are to imagine the daring offender as going forward to a boundary where Justice sits enthroned, forbidding all further advance. Instead of pausing there, the rebel still rushes on, to cross the boundary—and, in doing so, dashes herself against the throne of the goddess. For <quote xml:lang="grc">βάθρον</quote> cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1.183">Her. 1.183</ref> (a sitting statue of Zeus) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὸ βάθρον</quote> (pedestal) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ καὶ ὁ θρόνος χρύσεός ἐστι</quote>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέπεσες_853">, Polyb. 1. 39 <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπεσόντες εἴς τινα βραχέα</quote> (having <emph>struck on</emph> some shallows—in sailing). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 409">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 409</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλ’ εὐγενῆ τε καὶ τὸν Αἰσχύνης θρόνον | τιμῶντα καὶ στυγοῦνθ’ ὑπέρφρονας λόγους</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 383"> <title>Ag.</title> 383</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λακτίσαντι μέγαν Δίκας βωμόν</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 539"> <title>Eum.</title> 539</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμὸν αἴδεσαι Δίκας, | μηδέ νιν κέρδος ἰδὼν ἀθέῳ ποδὶ λὰξ ἀτίσῃς</quote>. Solon fr. 4. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ φυλάσσονται σεμνὰ θέμεθλα Δίκης.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
μάλ’ εὐγενῆ τε καὶ τὸν Αἰσχύνης θρόνον |
									τιμῶντα καὶ στυγοῦνθ’ ὑπέρφρονας λόγους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύ_853"> (adv.) =<quote xml:lang="grc">σφόδρα</quote>, violently.—Bellermann, adopting Kvíčala's general view, and also Kern's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσχάτου_853">, renders: ‘having advanced, with extreme rashness (<cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.17">Dem. or. 18 § 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε δικαίως οὔτ’ ἐπ’ ἀληθείας οὐδεμιᾶς εἰρημένα</quote> </cit>), to the lofty threshold (<quote xml:lang="grc">βάθρον</quote>) of Justice, thou hast fallen heavily <emph>down</emph>’ <emph>: i.e.</emph>, ‘ while, with the utmost boldness, thou wast obeying the command of Justice, thou hast been hurled to destruction.’ But (1) </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε δικαίως οὔτ’ ἐπ’ ἀληθείας οὐδεμιᾶς
									εἰρημένα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέπεσες_853_2"> could not mean this: we must at least have <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέπεσες</quote>. (2) The Chorus feel pity, indeed, for Ant. , and recognise the praise of piety, which she has won (817, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 872">Ant. 872</ref>). But they also regard her collision with the city's law as an act of frantic folly (<quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου τ’ ἄνοια καὶ φρενῶν ἐρινύς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 603">Ant. 603</ref>); and they presently tell her that she has left Creon no choice but to punish her (874). By <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote> they understand the law of the State,—not those <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν νόμιμα</quote> which Ant. preferred to obey. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷόν_ἆθλον_856">, ‘some ordeal inherited from thy sire’ (v. 2); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τινά_856"> softens a statement into a conjecture; it is perhaps the work of the fate which he bequeathed. This is better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">τινά</quote> closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷον</quote> as=‘of the paternal kind.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτίνεις_856">, art paying (to the fates which exact it), like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτίνω δίκην, τίσιν, ἄποινα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μερίμνας_857">, gen. sing.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶκτον_857">, acc. depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔψαυσας...μερίμνας</quote> as =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποίησάς με μεριμνᾶν</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 122"> <title>El.</title> 122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνα | τάκεις ὧδ’ ἀκόρεστον οἰμωγὰν | τὸν πάλαι ἐκ δολερᾶς ἀθεώτατα | ματρὸς ἁλόντ’ ἀπάταις Ἀγαμέμνονα</quote> </cit>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν...Ἀγαμέμνονα</quote> is governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνα τάκεις...οἰμωγάν</quote> as =<quote xml:lang="grc">τί οἰμώζεις</quote>; see other examples in n. on 211 f. </gloss>

data.quote:
τίνα | τάκεις ὧδ’ ἀκόρεστον οἰμωγὰν | τὸν
									πάλαι ἐκ δολερᾶς ἀθεώτατα | ματρὸς ἁλόντ’ ἀπάταις
									Ἀγαμέμνονα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρός_857"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότμου_857"> are objective genitives with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶκτον_857_2">.—The objection</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλγ_μερίμνας_857"> is certainly not acc. plur. <ref target="cl546">See on 546</ref> and 961. No Greek hearer of these lyrics could take <quote xml:lang="grc">μερίμνας</quote> for anything but the usual gen. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύω</quote>. If Soph. had intended the acc., he would at least have written <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγεινοτάταν...μέριμναν</quote>.—The interpretation given above would admit the reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶτον_857">, ‘doom,’ which has, however, less authority (cr. n.). <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 166"> <title>El.</title> 166</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀνήνυτον | οἶτον ἔχουσα κακῶν</quote> </cit>. But, as <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶτον πότμου</quote> is an impossible pleonasm, it would then be necessary to take <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ τε ...πότμου</quote> as depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔψαυσας</quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τριπόλιστον οἶτον</quote> could not be a parenthetic acc. in apposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔψαυσας... μερίμνας πατρός</quote>. This would be very awkward. Further, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶκτον</quote> is clearly the right word to introduce the lament <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰώ</quote>, etc., which actually follows.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τριπόλιστον_857"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἶκτον</quote>), a lament which has often been renewed; a thrice-told tale of sorrow. <quote xml:lang="grc">πολέω</quote>=to turn up the soil with the plough: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπολέω</quote>, to plough anew: then fig., to ‘go over the same ground’ again. <ref n="Pind. N. 7">Pind. <title>N.</title> 7. 104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτὰ...τρὶς τετράκι τ’ ἀμπολεῖν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1238">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1238</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δὶς ταὐτὰ βούλει καὶ τρὶς ἀναπολεῖν μ’ ἔπη</quote>; No <quote xml:lang="grc">πολίζω</quote> as =<quote xml:lang="grc">πολέω</quote> occurs, but Pind. once has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπολίζω</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπολέω</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. P. 6">Pind. <title>P.</title> 6. 3</ref>). Similarly a poet might well use <quote xml:lang="grc">τριπόλιστος</quote> as if <quote xml:lang="grc">πολίζω</quote> existed. It is needless to write <quote xml:lang="grc">τριπόλητον</quote>. The epic adj. is <quote xml:lang="grc">τρίπολος.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλεινοῖς_Λαβδακίδαισιν_857">, dat. of interest after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότμου_857_2">, all the fate <emph>for</emph> (i.e. appointed for) us, the Labdacidae, instead of the possessive gen., <quote xml:lang="grc">κλεινῶν Λαβδακιδᾶν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 17">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Θήβαισιν εὐίπποις ἄναξ</quote> </cit>. It is needless to explain the dat. by pressing the deriv. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πότμος</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">πίπτω</quote> (‘what falls <emph>to</emph> one’). Cp. on 571, and on 865 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῷ πατρί</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁμετέρου_857"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν</quote>) is here joined with the dat. of interest, as the possessive pron. is oft. joined with the gen. of the pers. pron. (<quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ δυστήνου κακά</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 344"> <title>O. C.</title> 344</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρῷαι_ἆται_863"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ματρῴων λ. ἆται</foreign> (see n. on 793), calamities of the mother's bed, i.e. springing from marriage with a mother. (So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 526"> <title>O. C.</title> 526</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμων . . ἄτᾳ</quote> </cit>.) For the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆται</quote> in this sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1244"> <title>O. C.</title> 1244</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 215"> <title>El.</title> 215</ref>.—L's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷαι_863"> (‘calamities of my father's marriage’) is possible, but less good, since (<emph>a</emph>) the epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρῷα</quote> denotes the bed <emph>to which</emph> he came: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 976"> <title>O. T.</title> 976</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πῶς τὸ μητρὸς</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοιμήματα_ματρός_864">, the mother's union with her own offspring; the adj.=<quote xml:lang="grc">μετὰ τοῦ αὑτῆς γεννήματος</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1463"> <title>O. C.</title> 1463</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπος . . διόβολος</quote> </cit> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">κτύπος τοῦ ἐκ Διὸς βέλους</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 212">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 212</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμαιμος</quote> </cit> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">συγγενοῦς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθέντης φόνος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμῷ_πατρί_864">, dat. of interest, ‘<emph>for</emph> my sire’ (i.e. to his misery); the whole phrase being equiv. to, ‘a mother's incestuous union with our father.’ The dat. goes with the whole preceding phrase, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτογέννητα</quote> only (‘incestuous in relation to him’), nor with <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιμήματα</quote> only (‘sleep with him’). The latter, which Wecklein assumes, implies <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιμᾶσθαί τινι</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">συγκ. τινί</quote>, an unproved constr.; for <ref n="Hes.
