Th. 213">Hes. <title>Th.</title> 213</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτινι κοιμη·θεῖσα θεὰ τέκε Νὺξ ἐρεβεννή</quote> is of doubtful genuineness, and even there <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτινι</quote> could go with <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσμόρου_864"> is far better than <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσμόρῳ</quote>: without it, the words could imply that only the father's sin merited pity. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵων_866"> (masc.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_866"> is exclamatory: ‘from what manner of parents’ (for the gen., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 38">cp. 38</ref>). The exclamatory <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷος</quote> is freq. in Soph. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1228">cp. 1228</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 997"> <title>Tr.</title> 997</ref>, etc.), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτέ</quote> strengthens it just as it strengthens the interrogative <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 754"> <title>O. T.</title> 754</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 222"> <title>Ph.</title> 222</ref>, etc.). So the exclamatory <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶος</quote> is strengthened by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρα</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 367"> <title>Ai.</title> 367</ref>, 910).—Most commentators make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵων_866_2"> neut. relative to <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιμήματα</quote>: ‘such (wedlock) as that from which I sprang.’ But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_866_2"> is fatal to this; for (<emph>a</emph>) it cannot here mean, ‘in former days’; that would be too weak: (<emph>b</emph>) nor can <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵων ποτέ</quote> mean, ‘of whatever kind they may have been’— like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις ποτέ</quote>. Besides, the masc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕς_866"> most naturally refers to persons denoted by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵων_866_3">. I have therefore pointed at </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρόςἀραῖος_866">, fem.: elsewhere this adj. is always of three terminations. So Attic tragedy uses <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινός, ποθεινός, πτωχός, φανερός</quote>, etc., as fem.: see <ref n="Soph. OC 751"> <title>O. C.</title> 751</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέτοικος_866">: <ref target="cl852">see on 852</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κασίγνητε_870">. Polyneices. His marriage with Argeia, daughter of the Argive king Adrastus, was the seal of the armed alliance against Thebes, and thus the prime cause of Antigone's death (<ref n="Soph. OC 378"> <title>O. C.</title> 378</ref>).—Not Oedipus. Such an allusion would be too repulsive here. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 535"> <title>O. C.</title> 535</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφεαί</quote> </cit> marks the climax of horror, and the word is wrung from unwilling lips. Further, as the doom of the whole race is in question (859), the brother is fitly mentioned. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανὼν_οὖσαν_871">: so Electra says of her brother Orestes, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς μ’ ἀπώλεσας θανών</quote>. Cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1453"> <title>O. T.</title> 1453</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατήναρες_871">. This act. aor. occurs in later poetry. The epic form of the compound is <quote xml:lang="grc">κατεναίρομαι</quote>, aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατενηράμην</quote>. Eur. uses the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναίρω, ἤναρον</quote>, but only in lyrics. Soph. has <quote xml:lang="grc">κατηναρισμένας</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κατεναρίζω</quote>) in dial., <ref n="Soph. Aj. 26"> <title>Ai.</title> 26</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέβειν_872">. ‘Reverent action’ (meaning her loyalty to Polyneices) ‘is, in a</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέβειν_872_2">. The speaker avoids a direct reference to the peculiarly sacred and tender duty which Ant. had fulfilled. The proposed emendations (see cr. n.) would obliterate this touch. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτος_δὲ_873"> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμᾷ παραβατὸν πέλει ( τούτῳ ), ὅτῳ κράτος μέλει</quote>: but an offence against authority cannot be permitted by him who has authority in his keeping. The antecedent to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτῳ_873">, viz. <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ</quote>, is an ethic dat., ‘in his sight’; he must not look on and see the law broken. For this dat., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 904">cp. 904</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδαμᾷ_873"> (Dor.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 763">cp. 763</ref>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλει_873"> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1335">cp. 1335</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.25"> <title>Il.</title> 2.25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧ λαοί τ’ ἐπιτετράφαται καὶ τόσσα μέμηλεν</quote> </cit>. The Chorus echoes Creon's saying (677). </gloss>

data.quote:
ᾧ λαοί τ’ ἐπιτετράφαται καὶ τόσσα
									μέμηλεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόγνωτος_875">, act., deciding for oneself, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὴ_γιγνώσκουσα_875">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμπτός</quote>, ‘blaming,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕποπτος</quote>, ‘suspecting,’ etc. (<ref n="Soph. OC 1031"> <title>O. C.</title> 1031</ref> n.). Not pass., ‘resolved upon’ (i.e. here, ‘adopted’) ‘by one's own choice,’ as Ellendt takes it (‘ultro susceptus’), and as the Schol. perh. did, who gives, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθαίρετος καὶ ἰδιογνώμων τρόπος.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργά_875">, disposition: see n. on 354 ff. (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργάς</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑτοίμαν_878">, imminent, i.e. for which everything is prepared; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 18.96"> <title>Il.</title> 18.96</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτίκα γάρ τοι ἔπειτα μεθ’ Ἕκτορα πότμος ἑτοῖμος</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Plut. Mor. 706">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 706</ref>C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτοιμον τὸ διαφθαρῆναι τοῖς μὴ βοηθοῦντα·. . .τὸν λογισμὸν ἔχουσι</quote> (corruption is imminent for those who have not reason to aid them). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 936">Cp. 936</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
αὐτίκα γάρ τοι ἔπειτα μεθ’ Ἕκτορα πότμος
									ἑτοῖμος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδε_ὄμμα_879"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε ἱερᾶς λαμπάδος ὄμμα</foreign> (793 n.). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 194">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 194</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλάξας... | ἱερὸν...ὄμμ’ αὐγᾶς | ἅλιος</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Eur. Ion 1467"> <title>Ion</title> 1467</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀελίου δ’ ἀναβλέπει λαμπάσιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδάκρυτον_881">, predicate, with proleptic force: no friend mourns my fate,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_ἴστε_883">, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς, εἰ χρείη λέγειν ἀοιδὰς καὶ γόους πρὸ τοῦ θανεῖν, οὐδ’ εἶς ἂν παύσαιτο (λέγων);</quote> The constr. would have been clearer with <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγων</quote> instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_883">, but the latter has naturally been conformed to <quote xml:lang="grc">χρείη</quote>. The conject. <quote xml:lang="grc">παύσειεν</quote> is admissible (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1751"> <title>O. C.</title> 1751</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παύετε θρῆνον, παῖδες</quote> </cit>), but unnecessary, and scarcely probable. Prof. Postgate's suggestion, that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀοιδὰς_γόους_883"> are objects to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴστε_883">, is tenable; but against it is the fact that in such cases the object of the principal verb is almost invariably the anticipated subject of the dependent clause (as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1197"> <title>O. C.</title> 1197</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώσει κακοῦ | θυμοῦ τελευτὴν ὡς κακὴ προσγίγνεται</quote> </cit>); as if here we had, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἴστ’ ἀοιδὰς ὡς μακραί εἰσι</quote>; An object is very rarely so anticipated, as in Isae. or. 10 § 18, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσως οὖν ἄν τις...τὸν χρόνον ὑμῶν θαυμάσειε, πῶς ποτε πολὺν οὕτως εἰάσαμεν.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
γνώσει κακοῦ | θυμοῦ τελευτὴν ὡς κακὴ
									προσγίγνεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_εἷς_883">, more emphatic than <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς ἄν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 281"> <title>O. T.</title> 281</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἄξεθ_885">. When the first of two or more commands is given by <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> with fut. indic., that constr. is usually continued, either (<emph>a</emph>) with <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 1066">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 1066</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὅσον τάχος | χωρήσεταί τις...καὶ... λέξει...</quote> </cit>; or (<emph>b</emph>) with repeated <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>, as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 459">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 459</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἕλξετ’, οὐ παιήσετ’, οὐκ ἀράξετε</quote> </cit>; Here a direct imperat. follows (887), and a note of interrogation must therefore be placed after </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐχ ὅσον τάχος | χωρήσεταί τις...καὶ...
									λέξει...
οὐχ ἕλξετ’, οὐ παιήσετ’, οὐκ ἀράξετε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάχιστακατηρεφεῖ_885">, prop. ‘roofed over,’ oft. an epithet of a natural cave (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 13.349"> <title>Od.</title> 13.349</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπέος</quote> </cit>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 272">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 272</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κ. πέτρᾳ</quote>), and in <ref n="Soph. El. 381"> <title>El.</title> 381</ref> of a chamber (<quote xml:lang="grc">στέγη</quote>) like this <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῶρυξ</quote>. The fact that Nauck wishes to change it into <quote xml:lang="grc">κατώρυχι</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">κατωρυχεῖ</quote>) is a strong instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταβολὴ πάντων γλυκύ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιπτύξαντες_886">: <ref target="cl848">see on 848</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕργμα</quote>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1357">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1357</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τειχέων περιπτυχαί</quote> </cit>. <ref>Kaibel <title>Epigr.</title> 468</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαΐνεος στήλη με πέριξ ἔχει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῇ_887">, wishes: 3rd pers. pres. ind. of <quote xml:lang="grc">χράω</quote>, with contraction into <quote xml:lang="grc">ῆ</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾶ</quote>, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">διψῆν, ζῆν, κνῆν, πεινῆν, σμῆν, χρῆν</quote> (to give an oracle), <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆσθαι, ψῆν</quote>. Schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χρῄζει καὶ θέλει</quote>. If the nom. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶσα_887"> is sound, as it seems to be, in the next v., then </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῇ_887_2"> is certain: for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χρῄζει</quote> has no probability. Only one other passage, however, gives unambiguous support to the forms <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῇς, χρῇ</quote>,—viz. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 778">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 778</ref>, (the Megarian) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ χρῆσθα; σιγῇς, ὦ κάκιστ’ ἀπολουμένα</quote>; ‘will you not make a sound?' (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ θέλεις</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνεῖν</quote>;). There, indeed, Blaydes writes <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ χρή τυ</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶσα_τυμβεύειν_888">, to live entombed. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβεύω</quote>=to entomb (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1063"> <title>Ai.</title> 1063</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα τυμβεῦσαι τάφῳ</quote> </cit>); or to bring as a funeral offering, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 406"> <title>El.</title> 406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὶ τυμβεῦσαι χοάς</quote> </cit>. Here it is intrans.,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τύμβῳ εἶναι</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">σαλεύω</quote>, which means either (1) to put others on a <quote xml:lang="grc">σάλος</quote>,—to toss them: or (2) intrans., to be on a <quote xml:lang="grc">σάλος</quote>. So the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλασσεύω</quote>=to be on the sea. <quote xml:lang="grc">παρθενεύω</quote>, which is trans. in classical Gk. (‘to bring up a maiden’), is intrans. in <ref n="Heliod. 7. 8">Heliod. 7. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ...παρθενεῦον τοῦ ἄστεος</quote>. —The conject. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυμφεύειν_888"> is not right. That taunt would be quite out of place here. Creon says simply, ‘I immure her,—I do not kill her; she can either die, or live,—but in the tomb.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέγῃ_888">, iron.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 381"> <title>El.</title> 381</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσα δ’ ἐν κατηρεφεῖ | στέγῃ χθονὸς τῆσδ’ ἐκτὸς ὑμνήσεις κακά</quote> </cit>, i.e. in a cave, or subterranean cell. </gloss>

data.quote:
ζῶσα δ’ ἐν κατηρεφεῖ | στέγῃ χθονὸς τῆσδ’
									ἐκτὸς ὑμνήσεις κακά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγνοί_889">: <ref target="cl775">see on 775</ref>. His thought is: (1) she had warning: (2) no blood has been shed.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπὶ_τήνδε_889">, in what concerns her. In this phrase with the <emph>acc.</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> more often means, ‘so far as depends on one’: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 514">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 514</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς δ’ ἄτεκνοι τοὐπὶ ς</quote> </cit>': <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1345"> <title>Or.</title> 1345</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σώθηθ’ ὅσον γε τοὐπ’ ἔμ’</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 4.28">Thuc. 4.28</ref> (he told Cleon to try) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἐπὶ σφᾶς εἶναι</quote> (so far as the generals were concerned): <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 1.4.12">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1.4.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλου τινὸς τὸ ἐπὶ σὲ ἀνάγκη ἔσται δεῖσθαι ἡμᾶς</quote> </cit>. In all these places <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> with <emph>dat.</emph> would equally suit the sense; but not so in this verse. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1348">Cp. 1348</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τά γ’ εἰς θεούς</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄλλου τινὸς τὸ ἐπὶ σὲ ἀνάγκη ἔσται δεῖσθαι
									ἡμᾶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετοικίας_890">, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 852">cp. 852</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_οὖν_890">, 688.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στερήσεται_890">, 637. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τύμβος_891">, nom for voc.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 379">cp. 379</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυμφεῖον_891">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1205">cp. 1205</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασκαφής_891">: <ref target="cl774">see on 774</ref>....<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀείφρουρος</emph> = ἣ ἀεὶ φρουρήσει με</quote>, not, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν ἐγὼ ἀεὶ φρουρήσω</quote> (like <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 31">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε φρουρήσεις πέτραν</quote> </cit>): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 886">cp. 886</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀριθμὸν_πλεϊστον_893">: she thinks of Laïus, her father and mother, and her two brothers.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_νεκροῖς_893"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδεκται_893">: the queen of the nether world has greeted them as they passed through the <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύξενοι</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φερσέφασσα_893"> has L's support here. That form occurs also in <ref n="Eur. Hel. 175">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 175</ref>, but <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσέφασσα</quote> in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 490">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 490</ref>. The <title>Il.</title>and <title>Od.</title>have only <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσεφόνεια. Φερσεφόνεια</quote> occurs in <ref n="HH 13"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 13.2</ref>. Pindar uses <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερσεφόνα</quote>. Plato attests that, in his day, the popular form was <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερρέφαττα</quote>, which he explains as the goddess of wisdom, who enables men <quote xml:lang="grc">φερομένων ἐφάπτεσθαι</quote>, to grasp changing phenomena. People were afraid to utter the name <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερσεφόνη</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Crat. 404"> <title>Crat.</title> 404</ref>C). Attic inscrr. of the 4th cent. B.C. give <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσεφόνη, Φερσεφόνη, Φερρέφαττα</quote> (Meisterhans pp. 36 ff.). MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερσέφαττα</quote> (which should perh. be <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερρέφαττα</quote>) in <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 671">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 671</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 287."> <title>Th.</title> 287.</ref>A vase ascribed to c. 435 B.C. gives <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΕΡΣΩΦΑΤΑ</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sic</foreign>, Baumeister <title>Denkm.</title> p. 424). Welcker cites <quote xml:lang="grc">ΦΕΡΕΦΑΣΑ</quote> from an Agrigentine vase (<title>Götterl.</title> 1. 393). We may infer that Soph. , c. 440 B.C., might have used either <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσέφασσα</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">Φερσέφασσα</quote>. The testimony of our oldest and best MS., L, may therefore be allowed to turn the scale.— In <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσεφόνη</quote>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">φον</quote> is certainly <quote xml:lang="grc">φαν</quote>, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργειφόντης</quote>, and this comes out more clearly in <quote xml:lang="grc">Περσέφασσα</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Τηλεφᾶσσα = Τηλεφάεσσα</quote>, <ref n="Apollod. 3.1.1">Apollod. 3. 1. 1.</ref>The first part of the word is prob. <quote xml:lang="grc">φερ, φέρω</quote>; and the name meant originally, ‘she who brings (vegetation) to the light.’ The initial II would then have been due to the following <quote xml:lang="grc">φ</quote> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">πέ‐φυκα</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">φέ‐φυκα</quote>). The replacement of the initial </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φ_893"> may have been prompted by a wish to mitigate the <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσφημία</quote> of the name by avoiding an association with <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρθω</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάκιστα_895">, as a law-breaker (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 59">cp. 59</ref>): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_895"> with superl., see 58 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῖραν_βίου_895">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 461">cp. 461</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξήκειν_895"> of time, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 199."> <title>Ph.</title> 199.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τρέφω_897"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίζω. ἐν ἐλπίδι</foreign> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίσιν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμί</quote> was a common phrase. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πόθῳ λαβεῖν τι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1678"> <title>O. C.</title> 1678</ref>).—</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλη_δέ_897">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 669">cp. 669</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς μὲν...εὖ δέ</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κασίγνητον_κάρα_899">. Eteocles. Whatever view may be taken of vv. 904—920, few would question the genuineness of 900—903: and if the latter are genuine, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δέ_899"> shows that Polyneices is not meant here. She speaks first of those kinsfolk to whom she had rendered pious offices in the usual manner. Then she comes to him who is uppermost in her thoughts,—the brother whose case was different from that of the others. In v. 23 she spoke of the rumour that Eteocles had been duly buried. But nothing here implies her presence at his <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφορά. ἔλουσα κἀκόσμησα</quote> were acts preparatory to the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόθεσις</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> could be rendered afterwards. She loved both brothers (cp. on 523). If father and mother were named here, without any mention of Eteocles, the omission would suggest that from <emph>him</emph> she could expect no welcome,—a contradiction of her real feeling (515). Further, the brevity of this reference to Eteocles heightens the effect of what follows. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμᾶς_900">. This play supposes Oedipus to have died at Thebes: <ref target="cl50">see on 50</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλουσα_κἀκόσμησα_900">: see nn. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1602"> <title>O. C.</title> 1602</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτροῖς τέ νιν | ἐσθῆτί τ’ ἐξήσκησαν ᾗ νομίζεται</quote>. Cp. below, 1201.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοάς_900">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 431">cp. 431</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιστέλλουσα_900">: here, of sprinkling the dust and pouring the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote>: cp. the general sense of the word in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1170"> <title>Ai.</title> 1170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφον περιστελοῦντε</quote> </cit>. More oft. of laying out the dead (like <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμεῖν</quote> here, and <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκαθαρμόζειν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 922"> <title>Ai.</title> 922</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.292"> <title>Od.</title> 24.292</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ ἑ μήτηρ | κλαῦσε περιστείλασα</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 9.485">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 9. 485</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">canibus date praeda Latinis | Alitibusque iaces: nec te tua funera mater | Produxi, pressive oculos, aut vulnera lavi, | Veste tegens.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
canibus date praeda Latinis | Alitibusque
									iaces: nec te tua funera mater | Produxi, pressive oculos, aut
									vulnera lavi, | Veste tegens.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέμας_900">: 205. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίμησα_904"> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">’φάνη</quote>, 457 n.), with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_904"> (last word of v., as <ref n="Soph. OC 642"> <title>O. C.</title> 642</ref>), I honoured thee rightly, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_φρονοῦσιν_904">, in the judgment of the wise; ethic dat. (25 n.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 514">cp. 514</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐτήκετο_906">, lay mouldering: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 82E">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 82E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν...τηκομένη σὰρξ ἀνάπαλιν εἰς τὰς φλέβας τὴν τηκεδόνα ἐξιῇ</quote> </cit>. So <quote xml:lang="lat">tabum</quote> of corpses, <ref n="Verg. A. 8.487">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 8. 487.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅταν...τηκομένη σὰρξ ἀνάπαλιν εἰς τὰς φλέβας
									τὴν τηκεδόνα ἐξιῇ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίᾳ_πολιτῶν_907">. This was Ismene's phrase (79). Antigone had believed hat the city was on her side (509). This has been noted as a mark of spuriousness in the verse. But it cannot (I think) be fairly claimed as such; for, since the Chorus had seemed to fail in sympathy (838), she had regarded herself as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφιλος</quote> (876) in Thebes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_χάριν_908">: <ref target="cl30">see on 30</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατθανόντος_909">, for which a gen. has to be evolved from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόσις_909">. The gen. of that word was not in Attic use (‘mihi non succurrit exemplum ubi <quote xml:lang="grc">πόσεος</quote> aut <quote xml:lang="grc">πόσεως</quote> legatur,’ Pors. <ref n="Eur. Med. 906"> <title>Med.</title> 906</ref>). Why was not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρός</quote> used? Itlooks as if the composer who made up these verses from <ref n="Hdt. 3.119">Her. 3.119</ref> (see above) had sought to impart a touch of tragic dignity by substituting <quote xml:lang="grc">πόσις</quote> for the historian's word, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</quote>. The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατθανόντος</quote> cannot be taken (as some wish) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος</quote>, ‘different from the dead’ (!). (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_φωτός_909">. Why is it assumed that the first husband died before, or with, his child? The two hypotheses of loss should have been kept separate. We wanted something like <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ παῖς ἂν ἄλλος, παιδὸς ἐστερημένῃ</quote>. (3) <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’</quote> </emph> means the first husband's child, but is most awkward.—As to <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστ’ ἀδελφός κ.τ.λ.</quote>, it may be somewhat inelegant; but it is not (as some urge) incorrect, since <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ὅστις = οὐδείς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπροτιμήσασ_913">, ‘having singled thee out for honour,’ with ref. to the supposed cases in which she would <emph>not</emph> have paid the burial rites. The double compound occurs only here,—as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαφοράω</quote> only in <ref n="Soph. OC 1648"> <title>O. C.</title> 1648</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_λαβών_916">, i.e., by forcible arrest, as though I were a criminal. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 470"> <title>O. C.</title> 470</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ ὁσίων χειρῶν θιγών</quote> </cit> (=‘with’), <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 193">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 193</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκτηρίας</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχουσαι διὰ χερῶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνυμέναιον_917">: <ref target="cl813">see on 813</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_919">: cp. on 810.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_φίλων_919"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρημος_919"> (not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχομαι</quote>), forsaken on the part of my friends. The sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1070"> <title>Ph.</title> 1070</ref> is similar, though there it goes with the verb; <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καὶ πρὸς ὑμῶν ὧδ’ ἔρημος, ὦ ξένοι, | λειφθήσομαι δή</quote>...; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαιμόνων_δίκην_921">, that which the gods recognise (451), as distinguished from the human <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκη</quote> (854) which she has offended. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλέπειν_922"> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴς τινα</quote> (for help), as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 398"> <title>Ai.</title> 398</ref>, 514, <ref n="Soph. El. 959"> <title>El.</title> 959</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνα_ξυμμάχων_922">, what ally (of all conceivable allies)?</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεί_δή_922">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 484"> <title>Tr.</title> 484</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί γε μὲν δή</quote> </cit>. For the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 726">cp. 726</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_δυσσέβειαν_922">, the repute of it; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 968"> <title>El.</title> 968</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσέβειαν ἐκ πατρὸς | θανόντος οἴσει</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 676">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 676</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δειλίαν γὰρ καὶ κάκην κεκτήσομαι</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 218"> <title>Med.</title> 218</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσκλειαν ἐκτήσαντο καὶ ῥᾳθυμίαν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 600"> <title>Ion</title> 600</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γέλωτ’ ἐν αὐτοῖς μωρίαν τε λήψομαι</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: καὶ δειλίαν γὰρ καὶ κάκην κεκτήσομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_οὖν_925"> (‘well then’). (1) If the gods approve of my doom, then, after suffering it, I shall become conscious (in the other world) that I have sinned. (2) But if they disapprove of it, and regard Creon as the sinner, then they will punish him at last. And I could wish him no sorer doom than mine. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_θεοῖς_925">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 459">cp. 459</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυγγνοῖμεν_925"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">συνειδείημεν</foreign>. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 9.11">Lys. or. 9 § 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συνέγνωσαν δὲ καὶ αὐτοὶ σφίσιν ὡς ἠδικηκότες</quote> </cit>, ‘became conscious that they had done wrong.’ The word could also mean, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμολογήσαιμεν</quote>, ‘confess’: but in that sense it regularly takes either an inf., as <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.91">Her. 1.91</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συνέγνω ἑωυτοῦ εἶναι τὴν ἁμαρτάδα</quote> </cit>: or a dependent clause, as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 717D">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 717D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυγγιγνώσκοντα ὡς εἰκότως...θυμοῖτ’ ἄν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
συνέγνωσαν δὲ καὶ αὐτοὶ σφίσιν ὡς
									ἠδικηκότες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμαρτηκότες_925"> belongs more closely to the verb than does </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθόντες_925">: cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 70A">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 70A</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ψυχὴ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">διασκεδασθεῖσα οἴχηται διαπτομένη</quote>. For the tragic masc. plur., when a woman speaks of herself, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 399"> <title>El.</title> 399</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵδε_927">, Creon: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 10">cp. 10</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_πλείω_927">, i.e. she will be content if they suffer <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσα</quote>. She can imagine no worse fate. The tame conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μείω_927"> would not express this bitter feeling.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>καὶ</emph> δρῶσιν</quote>, do on their part: <ref n="Soph. OC 53"> <title>O. C.</title> 53</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέμωνῥιπαὶ_ψυχῆς_929">, stormgusts of the soul: both genitives are possessive: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 795">cp. 795</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλεφάρων‐ἵμερος...νύμφας</quote> —Dindorf's expulsion of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑταὶ | ψυχῆς</quote> (cr. n.) is unwarranted. <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχῆς</quote> interprets the figurative sense; for <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑταί</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν αὐτῶν</quote> cp. n. on 155 ff. (<quote xml:lang="grc">νέοχμος νεαραῖσι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_931">, neut., causal gen. (<ref n="Soph. OT 48"> <title>O. T.</title> 48</ref>). After <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε γ’</quote>, this is better than <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην</quote> would be: <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιγάρτοι</quote> (whether with <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> or with <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν</quote>) would be unpleasing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλαύμαθ_931">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 754">cp. 754</ref>: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1260"> <title>Ph.</title> 1260</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς κλαυμάτων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπερ_931"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἕνεκα</foreign> (<ref n="Soph. OT 165"> <title>O. T.</title> 165</ref> n.). <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι</quote> follows, but the change of person excuses the breach of synapheia: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 139"> <title>O. C.</title> 139</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φατιζόμενον</quote> </cit> is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰώ</quote>, and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 143"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 143</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέσβυς</quote> </cit> by <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέν_935"> adv.) to hope that</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_οὐ_935">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, on account of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote> (443 n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατακυροῦσθαι_935">, pres. denoting what is to be; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 113"> <title>Ph.</title> 113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱρεῖ τὰ τόξα ταῦτα τὴν Τροίαν μόνα</quote>. Attic prose used <quote xml:lang="grc">κυροῦν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπικυροῦν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμην, νόμον, ψήφισμα</quote>, etc.). Cp. Creon's peremptory word <quote xml:lang="grc">τελείαν</quote> in 632.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτῃ_935"> (722) combined with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_935">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 39">cp. 39</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὶ_προγενεῖς_938">, ancestral, not merely as protectors of the race, but also as progenitors. She thinks esp. of Ares and Aphrodite, the parents of Harmonia, wife of Cadmus: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 135">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 135</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ τ’, Αρης, πόλιν Κάδμου ἐπώνυμον | φύλαξαι κήδεσαί τ’ ἐναργῶς. | καὶ Κύπρις, ἅτ’ εἶ γένους προμάτωρ, | ἄλευσον</quote> </cit>. Dionysus, the son of ‘Cadmean’ Semele (1115), is another of the deities meant. <quote xml:lang="grc">προγενής</quote>, born before one, a poet. word, unfamiliar to good Attic prose, but used by Aristotle and later writers, usu. in comparat. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ προγενέστεροι</quote>, ‘those who have gone before us’): so <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ μεταγενέστεροι</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
σύ τ’, Αρης, πόλιν Κάδμου ἐπώνυμον | φύλαξαι
									κήδεσαί τ’ ἐναργῶς. | καὶ Κύπρις, ἅτ’ εἶ γένους προμάτωρ, |
									ἄλευσον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_939"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</foreign> (<ref n="Soph. OT 968"> <title>O. T.</title> 968</ref>), as in <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δή.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκέτι_μέλλω_939">, and am no longer (merely) about to be led away: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1074"> <title>O. C.</title> 1074</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρδουσ’ ἢ μέλλουσιν;</quote> </cit> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1255"> <title>Ph.</title> 1255</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀμέ τοι | ταὐτὸν τόδ’ ὄψει δρῶντα κοὐ μέλλοντ’ ἔτι</quote>. Meineke's conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">μελλώ</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλησις</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1356">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1356</ref>) was needless.— Seyffert understands, ‘I make no more delay,’—said firmly and proudly: she scorns to bring punishment on her guards by detaining them longer. But this does not suit <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγομαι δή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_κοιρανίδαι_940">, the Theban <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπατρίδαι</quote> of the Chorus: <ref target="cl843">see on 843</ref>. The nom. with art., instead of voc.,=‘<emph>ye who are</emph> princes of Thebes,’ and so brings out the implied reproach to their apathy. This constr. usu. has a somewhat peremptory tone, as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 218B">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 218B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ οἰκέται...πύλας...τοῖς ὠσὶν ἐπίθεσθε</quote> </cit>. It is different when a voc. precedes the art. and partic., as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 30">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 30</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦνδρες οἱ παρόντες ἐν λόγῳ</quote> </cit>: cp. on 100 f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βασιλειδᾶν_941">, i.e., of the race of the Labdacidae. This correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βασιλίδα_941"> is certain. In <ref n="Plat. Criti. 116C">Plat. <title>Critias</title> 116C</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τῶν δέκα βασιλειδῶν γένος</quote>, the same corruption, <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλίδων</quote>, occurs in the MSS. Suidas has <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλείδης· ὁ τοῦ βασιλέως</quote> (where Küster wrongly proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ τοῦ Βασιλείου</quote>). In adding the patronymic suffix <quote xml:lang="grc">ιδα—</quote> to a stem in <quote xml:lang="grc">ευ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλευ</quote>), the <quote xml:lang="grc">υ</quote> is dropped, as in the gen. plur. (<quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλέ‐ων</quote>): hence <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλε‐ίδης, βασιλείδης</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Πηλείδης.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μούνην_941">, ignoring Ismene; not in bitterness (cp. on 559), but because she feels that, in spirit at least, she herself is indeed the last of the race. It is otherwise when Oed. speaks of his daughters as his only children (<ref n="Soph. OC 895"> <title>O. C.</title> 895</ref>); and when Electra says that she is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ τοκέων</quote> (187),— since Clytaemnestra is a <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ ἀμήτωρ</quote> (1154). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσεβίαν_943">. Epic and lyric poetry could substitute -</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ια_943"> for -</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εια_943"> in fem. nouns from adjectives in -<quote xml:lang="grc">ης</quote>: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1043"> <title>O. C.</title> 1043</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προμηθίας</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Pind. P. 12">Pind. <title>P.</title> 12. 4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐμενίᾳ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 534">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 534</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσσεβίας</quote> </cit>: <title>H. F.</title> 696</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεβίσασα_943">, of respecting a law or custom: so <ref n="Soph. OC 636"> <title>O. C.</title> 636</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 713"> <title>Ai.</title> 713</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">θέσμια...σέβων</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_οὐράνιονφῶς_944">: note the bold order of words, and cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 598"> <title>Ph.</title> 598</ref> f. (<quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος...πράγματος</quote>). </gloss>
lemma:
καὶ Δανάας δέμας ἔτλα ἀλλάξαι οὐράνιον
									φῶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτλα_καί_944"> is a Homeric echo, from <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.382"> <title>Il.</title> 5.382</ref> ff. Aphrodite has been wounded by Diomede: her mother Dionè comforts her by saying that Ares, Hera, and Hades have also suffered wounds: <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτλαθι, τέκνον ἐμόν... | τλῆ μὲν Ἄρης... | τλῆ δ’ Ἥρη... | τλῆ δ’ Ἀΐδης</quote>. So here we have <emph>three</emph> examples—Danaë, Lycurgus, Cleopatra.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέμας_944"> in periphrasis (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 908"> <title>Tr.</title> 908</ref>) here suggests her youthful beauty.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀλλάξαι</emph> οὐρ. φῶς ἐν χαλκοδ. αὐλαῖς</quote>, ‘to give up light, (so as to be) in a prison,’ i.e. to <emph>exchange</emph> the light <emph>for</emph> the darkness of a prison. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλάσσω τί τινος</quote> can mean either to give, or to take, one thing in exchange for another. When <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλάσσω</quote> is used absolutely, with ref. to place, it more naturally means ‘to go to’ (<cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 483">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 483</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλάξασ’ Ἅιδα θαλάμους</quote> </cit>), not, as here, ‘to leave’: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείβω</quote> is freq. in both senses. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1262"> <title>Ph.</title> 1262</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείψας...στέγας</quote> (having quitted them).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκοδέτοις_αὐλαῖς_944">, ‘a brass-bound dwelling’: poet. pl. for sing., like <quote xml:lang="grc">δώματα</quote>, etc.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 785">cp. 785</ref>. Pherecydes (<emph>ap.</emph> schol. Apoll. Rhod. 4. 1091) describes it as ‘a brazen chamber (<quote xml:lang="grc">θάλαμον...χαλκοῦν</quote>) made under ground, in the court-yard (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>) of his house.’ Paus. (2. 23. 7) says that he saw at Argos <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάγεων οἰκοδόμημα, ἐπ’ αὐτῷ δὲ ἦν ὁ χαλκοῦς θάλαμος</quote> (made by Acrisius): i.e. the <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλαμος</quote> itself was above ground;—as Horace calls it <quote xml:lang="lat">turris aenea</quote> (<title>C.</title> 3. 16. 1). By the epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκοῦς</quote> the legend evidently meant to denote the strength and security of the prison,—as though the doors were of bronze. But it is very probable that this epithet originally came into the story through a reminiscence of a tomb (like the ‘treasury of Atreus’ at Mycenae), to the walls of which bronze plates had been nailed. (Cp. <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, ch. 11. § 25.) In Simonides fr. 37. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκεογόμφῳ</quote> is said of the chest in which Danaë was sent adrift,—not of the <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλαμος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυμβήρει_946">: cp. on 255.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατεζεύχθη_946">: was brought under the yoke, i.e., was strictly confined. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.22">Her. 8.22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ... ὑπ’ ἀναγκαίης μέζονος κατέζευχθε ἢ ὥστε ἀπίστασθαι</quote> </cit>, if ye are in the bondage of a control too severe, etc. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύχθη</quote>, 955. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ... ὑπ’ ἀναγκαίης μέζονος κατέζευχθε ἢ ὥστε
									ἀπίστασθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίμιος_948">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 834">cp. 834</ref>. Hermann's <quote xml:lang="grc">καίτοι καί</quote> is preferable to Wieseler's <quote xml:lang="grc">καίτοι γ’ ἦν</quote>, because the doubled <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> is forcible, while <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ Ζηνός</quote> (without a previous <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>) would be somewhat weak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταμιεύεσκε_950">, as a precious charge. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 660">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 660</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίκτει δ’ ὁ θρώσκων</quote> </cit>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’</quote> (the mother) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπερ ξένῳ ξένη | ἔσωσεν ἔρνος</quote>. The iterative form occurs in only three other places of trag.: 963: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 656">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 656</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσκεν</quote> </cit>, Aesch. fr. 305 <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαίεσκον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσορύτους_950">, for metre's sake, like <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 134"> <title>Ai.</title> 134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιρύτου</quote> </cit>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ρρ</quote> regularly follows a simple vowel, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ρ</quote> a diphthong (<ref n="Soph. OC 469"> <title>O. C.</title> 469</ref>).—A bowl (<quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ</quote>) from Caere, of the 5th cent. C.B. , shows the golden rain descending on Danaë; she is sitting on the bed in her chamber, and preparing to retire to rest. A second scene, on the same bowl, represents the moment when she is about to be placed, where Simonides imagines her, <quote xml:lang="grc">λαρνακι ἐν δαιδαλέᾳ</quote>. Both paintings are reproduced by Baumeister (<title>Denkm.</title> p. 407). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_τις_951"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The peculiar place of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_951"> makes it really equiv. to a parenthetic thought: ‘the power of fate (whatever it may be) is a dread power.’ So, while the general sense is what would be ordinarily given by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ μοιριδία δύνασις δεινά τις</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">δύνασίς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστιν</quote>, the actual order of the words is more expressive. This is not merely an instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> preceding the adj. (as though <quote xml:lang="grc">τις δεινά</quote> stood for <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινά τις</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 519"> <title>Ph.</title> 519</ref>). Nor, again, is it strictly parallel with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν δ’ ὁ κύριος | παρῇ τις</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 288), where art. and subst. precede; though it is similar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_ὄλβος_952">: wealth cannot buy off fate; arms cannot vanquish it; walls cannot keep it out; flight beyond sea cannot elude it.—Bacchylides fr. 36</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πύργος_952">, ◌̣ citywalls, with their towers (<ref n="Soph. OT 56"> <title>O. T.</title> 56</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_οὔτ_952">, followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_οὐχ_952">: so even when only one <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> has been used, 249 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζεύχθη_955">, was brought under the yoke. As <quote xml:lang="grc">κατεζεύχθη</quote> in 946 better suited the sense, ‘was strictly confined,’ so here the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύχθη</quote> better suggests the idea, ‘was tamed by imprisonment.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξύχολος_955"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁ Δρύαντος ὀξύχολος παῖς</foreign>: the adj. is epithet, not predicate. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 3.13">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 3. 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Terra procul vastis colitur Mavortia campis, | Thraces arant, <emph>acri</emph> quondam regnata Lycurgo.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Terra procul vastis colitur Mavortia campis, |
									Thraces arant,   quondam regnata
									Lycurgo.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κερτομίοις_ὀργαῖς_956">, causal dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύχθη</quote>: was subjugated by reason of the bursts of fury in which he reviled Dionysus. For the dat., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 391">cp. 391</ref>, 691: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 838"> <title>El.</title> 838</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσοδέτοις ἕρκεσι κρυφθέντα γυναικῶν</quote> </cit>, (Amphiaraus) buried alive, by reason of a woman's golden snares. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργαῖς_956"> might be general, ‘moods’ (355), but here has its special sense, though plur.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 848">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 848</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργὰς ξυνοίσω σοι</quote> </cit>. The dat. could be taken as a modal dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξύχολος</quote> (‘in,’ or ‘with,’ his <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργαί</quote>), but this is less good.—We could not take <quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύχθη...ὀργαῖς</quote> as=‘was enslaved to (his own) fierce moods.’—The recurrence of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κερτομίοις_956"> in 961 is noteworthy, but not a ground for suspicion (76 n.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 613">cp. 613</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἕρπει</quote> with 618: and 614 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote> with 625. The idea of <quote xml:lang="grc">κερτόμιος</quote> is mockery, or bitter jest: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1235"> <title>Ph.</title> 1235</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κερτομῶν λέγεις τάδε</quote>; with the answer, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ κερτόμησίς ἐστι τἀληθῆ λέγειν</quote>. The word is illustrated by the whole scene in which Pentheus mocks and taunts Dionysus (<ref n="Eur. Ba. 451">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 451</ref>—514). So Aesch. fr. 59 made Lycurgus ask, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδαπὸς ὁ γύννις</quote>; (‘whence comes this womanish youth?’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_Διονύσου_957">, by his command (<ref n="Soph. OC 67"> <title>O. C.</title> 67</ref> n.). Lycurgus, having been driven mad by Dionysus, did many violent deeds, until at last the Edonians were commanded by an oracle to imprison him in a cave on Mount Pangaeus ( <ref n="Apollod. 3.5.1">Apollod. 3. 5. 1</ref>). He was afterwards torn asunder by wild horses (<foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign>), or devoured by panthers (<ref n="Hyg. Fab. 132">Hyginus <title>Fab.</title> 132</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πετρώδει_δεσμῷ_957">, the cave. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 864E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 864E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δημοσίῳ δεσμῷ δεθείς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατάφαρκτος_957">: for the spelling, cp. <ref target="cl241">on 241.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτω_959">, i.e., under the discipline of the rocky prison.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθηρόν_959">, bursting into flower, hence, fig., exuberant, or at its height: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Stat. 310D">Plat. <title>Polit.</title> 310D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφυκεν ἀνδρία...κατὰ μὲν αρχὰς ἀκμάζειν ῥώμῃ, τελευτῶσα δὲ ἐξανθεῖν παντάπασι μανίαις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 821">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 821</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις...ἐξανθοῦς</quote> </cit>'. So oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνθος = ἀκμή</quote>, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 998"> <title>Tr.</title> 998</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’ ἀκήλητον | μανίας ἄνθος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
πέφυκεν ἀνδρία...κατὰ μὲν αρχὰς ἀκμάζειν ῥώμῃ,
									τελευτῶσα δὲ ἐξανθεῖν παντάπασι μανίαις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποστάζει_959"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπορρεῖ</foreign>, ‘trickles away,’ so ‘gradually passes off.’ The fig. use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθεῖν</quote> being so familiar, the change of metaphor in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστάζει</quote> would hardly be felt. Wecklein, indeed, conceives that the poet is thinking of a tumour, which bursts when it has attained its full size. Unity of metaphor can be bought too dearly.—Others understand: ‘so dread and exuberant is the rage that <emph>flows from madness</emph>’: i.e., ‘so dreadful was the excess of impiety into which L. had been led by his madness.’ But here we look rather for some direct comment on his punishment. His abasement (<quote xml:lang="grc">ζεύχθη</quote>) is the theme of these verses. The reference to his crime comes later (962). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέγνω_ψαύων_960"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κ. γλώσσαις</quote>: he came to know the god, when in madness he assailed him with taunts. He had mistaken Dionysus for an effeminate mortal (<ref target="cl955">see on 955</ref> f.). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.216"> <title>Od.</title> 24.216</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς πειρήσομαι... | αἴ κέ μ’ ἐπιγνώῃ</quote> </cit> (recognise). <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύω</quote> nowhere else takes an acc. in class. Greek (<ref target="cl546">see on 546</ref>, 859), and it is unnecessary to regard this passage as a solitary example. Poetry, esp. lyric, allowed occasional boldness, and even harshness, in the arrangement of words (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 944">cp. 944</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1251"> <title>O. T.</title> 1251</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. OC 1428"> <title>O. C.</title> 1428</ref>). Even in prose we could have (e.g.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνίκησαν ἄφνω ἐπιπεσόντες τοὺς πολεμίους</quote>, though the partic. could not govern an acc. Here the order is only so far bolder, that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν θεόν</quote> divides <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύων</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κ. γλώσσαις</quote>: as if, in our example, a second qualification of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιπεσόντες</quote> (such as <quote xml:lang="grc">νύκτωρ</quote>) followed <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς πολεμίους</quote>. But, since the meaning of <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύων</quote> is already indicated by <quote xml:lang="grc">μανίαις</quote>, we are not mentally straining forward for a clue to be given by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κερτ. γλώσσαις</quote>. That is, we are not forced to bind the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ψαύων τὸν θεόν</quote>, closely together in our thought, but can easily take the sentence as though it were pointed thus:— <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπέγνω, μανίαις | ψαύων, τὸν θεόν, έν κερτ. γλώσσαις.</foreign> </gloss>

data.quote:
πατρὸς πειρήσομαι... | αἴ κέ μ’
									ἐπιγνώῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μανίαις_960">, modal (rather than causal) dat.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψαύων_960"> like <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαπτόμενος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιβίοις ἐπέεσσι</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 18.415"> <title>Od.</title> 18.415</ref>), but also suggesting profanation,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθίκτου θιγγάνων.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_960">=‘with’ (764 n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλώσσαις_960">, a bold use of the plur., due to the fact that the sing., with an adj., could so easily be fig., e.g. <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὴ γλῶσσα</quote>=‘slander’: so <quote xml:lang="grc">κερτόμιοι γλῶσσαι</quote>=‘taunts.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παύεσκε_963">: <ref target="cl950">see on 950</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθέους_γυναῖκας_963">: the Maenads attendant on Dionysus, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1128">cp. 1128</ref>, 1150, <ref n="Soph. OT 212"> <title>O. T.</title> 212</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 680"> <title>O. C.</title> 680</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔιον_πῦρ_963">, the torches which the Bacchanals swing while they raise the cry <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐοῖ</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OT 211"> <title>O. T.</title> 211</ref> the god himself is <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔιος</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 307">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 307</ref> (Dionysus by night on Parnassus) <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδῶντα σὺν πεύκαισι δικόρυφον πλάκα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μούσας_965">. An interesting illustration is afforded by a sarcophagus in Baumeister's <title>Denkmaeler</title>, p.837. In the centre of the group is the raging Lycurgus, with uplifted axe, about to slay the Dionysiac nymph Ambrosia, who cowers at his feet. A Fury is on each side of him, urging him on. To the right is Dionysus.—about to</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὰ_ἁλός_966">. For the double possessive gen., cp.795,929. <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάγει...ἁλός</quote>, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 88">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλαγος Αἰγαίας ἁλός</quote> </cit>, the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλὸς ἐν πελάγεσσιν</quote>, etc. The rocky islets on the N. side of the entrance from the Euxine to the Bosporus were regularly called <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυάνεαι</quote> simply (without <quote xml:lang="grc">νῆσοι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτραι</quote>, <ref n="Hdt. 4.85">Her. 4.85</ref>). L's </gloss>
lemma: παρὰ δὲ Κυανεᾶν πελάγει δ. ἁλός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πετρῶν_966"> has long been recognised as a gloss. But Wieseler's change</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελάγεων_966">, points to the truth,—as similar small hints in that MS. have been found to do elsewhere also (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1113"> <title>O. C.</title> 1113</ref> n.). The correction, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελάγει_966">, is so easy that it may well have occurred to others; but I have not met with it. It removes the difficulty (insuperable, to my mind) of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρά_966"> with the genitive here. Those who read <quote xml:lang="grc">κυανεᾶν σπιλάδων</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">κυανέων πελαγέων</quote>, are forced to take <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote> as=‘<emph>extending from</emph> the dark rocks (etc.) are the coasts.’ But such a use is wholly unparalleled. As to 1123, see n. there. In <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρέομαι | πὰρ μὲν Σαλαμῖνος Ἀθαναίων χάριν</quote>=‘<emph>from</emph> Salamis’ (i.e., by celebrating it). In <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 68">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 68</ref> the Ravenna has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτρυχόμεσθα παρὰ Καΰστρίων | πεδίων ὁδοιπλανοῦντες</quote>, while other MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ</quote> (also with gen. plur.); but there <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ Καΰστριον | πεδίον</quote> (Dindorf) is certain. In <ref n="Pind. P. 3">Pind. <title>P.</title> 3. 60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνόντα τὸ πὰρ ποδός</quote>, ‘having learned one's nearest business’ (cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 10">Pind. <title>P.</title> 10. 63</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote> has its normal sense,—‘that which begins <emph>from</emph> one's foot,’=which is directly before one in one's path. The corruption of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελάγει_966_2"> into <quote xml:lang="grc">πελαγέων</quote> naturally followed that of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κυανεᾶν_966"> into <quote xml:lang="grc">κυανέων</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκταὶ_Βοσπόριαι_967">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσί</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 948">cp. 948</ref> n.). The <quote xml:lang="grc">Κυάνεαι</quote> are at the point where the coast of the Bosporus joins the western coast of the Euxine. The city Salmydessus stood just S. of the promontory of Thynias, about 60 miles N.W. of the entrance of the Bosporus, near the modern Midjeh. The name Salmydessus was given also to the tract of coast extending S. of the town.—After </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θρῃκῶν_967"> a cretic has been lost (=-<quote xml:lang="grc">τον γονάν</quote> in 980). Boeckh supplies it with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξενος_967">, which is at least simple and fitting. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 726">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 726</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τραχεῖα πόντου Σαλμυδησσία γνάθος | ἐχθρόξενος ναύταισι, μητρυιὰ νεῶν</quote> </cit>. Schütz, referring to the schol. on 969, <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλαγος δέ ἐστι δυσχείμερον περὶ Θρᾴκην</quote>, proposes <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσχιμος</quote> (‘dangerous’), a word used by Aesch. and Eur. , though not by Soph. But the want of a verb is somewhat awkward. Can the missing word be </gloss>

data.quote:
τραχεῖα πόντου Σαλμυδησσία γνάθος | ἐχθρόξενος
									ναύταισι, μητρυιὰ νεῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλῄζεται_967">? (Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1451"> <title>O. T.</title> 1451</ref> n.: and for the sing., below, 1133.) Ships often grounded on the shallows (<quote xml:lang="grc">τέναγος</quote>) which stretched from Salmydessus into the Euxine. The Thracians had set up slabs (<quote xml:lang="grc">στῆλαι</quote>), marking off the coast into allotments for wrecking purposes. Before this was done, there had been much bloodshed between rival wreckers (<ref n="Xen. Anab. 7.5.13">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 7.5.13</ref>).—The MS. †<emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠδ’</quote> </emph> cannot be right. A short syll. is required (=the last syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντες</quote> in 980). In my first ed. I adopted <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδ’</quote> </emph>, the conjecture of Triclinius. Prof. Tyrrell remarks (<title>Class. Review</title> vol. 11. p. 141) that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδέ</quote> is not elsewhere elided in classical poetry. As <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠδέ</quote> could be elided, that may be accidental. Still, it should be noted along with the other facts,—that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδέ</quote> occurs nowhere else in tragedy, and that the hiatus after <quote xml:lang="grc">Βοσπόριαι</quote> must be excused, as in epic verse, by the ictus before caesura (<title>Introd. to Homer</title> p. 194): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 14.175"> <title>Il.</title> 14.175</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλειψαμένη, ἰδὲ χαίτας | πεξαμένη</quote> </cit>. On the whole, I now prefer to leave </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠδ_967">, with an obelus. Either <quote xml:lang="grc">Βοσπόριαι καὶ ὁ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">Βοσπόριαί θ’ ὅ τε</quote> would be possible. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγχίπολις_Ἄρης_970">. This reading (L's) agrees metrically with the antistrophe (981 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαιογόνων</quote>), if we suppose the 2nd and 3rd syllables of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχίπολις</quote> to represent a resolved long syllable. Such a resolution is rare, but not unexampled: <ref target="cl797">see on 797 f.</ref>. We could avoid it by reading, with Gleditsch, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκταὶ Βοσπόριαι, <emph>ἵν’</emph> ὁ Θρῃκῶν ἄξενος | Σαλμυδησσὸς <emph>Ἄρης τ’</emph> ἀγχίπολις</quote>. But (<emph>a</emph>) this does not explain how <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠδ’</quote> came into the MSS.: and (<emph>b</emph>) it is evidently better to say, ‘where Ares saw,’ than, ‘where Salmydessus and Ares saw.’ The reference to the god's cruel joy would thus lose much of its force. If, on the other hand, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγχίπτολις_970"> is read, then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρης_970"> has <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>, and in 981 we must suppose the loss of a syllable after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαιογόνων_970">. But such a loss is very improbable: that verse appears sound. Neither <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγχουρος</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχέπολις</quote> has any likelihood.—Ares is ‘neighbour to the city’ of Salmydessus because his home is in Thrace (<ref n="Hom. Il. 13.301"> <title>Il.</title> 13.301</ref>, etc.). There may also be a special reference to some local shrine. ‘He saw the wound dealt’: i.e., it was a deed such as he loves to see. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φινεΐδαις_971">, dat. of interest, with <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλωθέν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρατόν_971">, accursed, bringing a curse on the authors of the wound. In his dramatic treatment of the story, Soph. had connected this blinding of the sons with the punishment of blindness which the gods afterwards inflicted on Phineus himself (schol. Apoll. Rhod. 2. 178). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕλκος_τυφλωθέν_971">. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τυφλοῦν ἕλκος</foreign>=to <emph>inflict</emph> a <emph>blinding</emph> wound. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 55"> <title>Ai.</title> 55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκειρε... φόνον</quote> </cit>, he <emph>dealt</emph> death <emph>by hewing down.</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 1205">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 1205</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρώσῃς φόνον</quote> </cit>, (wherever) thou <emph>dealest</emph> the <emph>death</emph>-wound. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 11.82">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 11. 82</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">caeso <emph>sparsuros</emph> sanguine <emph>flammam</emph> </quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">caedere sanguinem</foreign> = to <emph>shed</emph> blood <emph>by cutting</emph>). In such pregnant idioms the special verb=a general verb <emph>plus</emph> the partic. of the special verb used instrumentally: e.g., <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλῶ ἕλκος = ποιῶ ἕλκος τυφλῶν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_971"> here=<foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</foreign> of the direct agent: for, as <quote xml:lang="grc">κερκίδων</quote> indicates, she did it with her own hand. Distinguish <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ Διονύσου</quote> in 957 (by his order). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαὸν_κύκλοις_974">, sightless <emph>for</emph> the orbs, i.e., making them sightless. Cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαροῦ λέβητος</quote>, the purifying cauldron.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαστόροισιν_974">. The form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστορος</quote> was used by Aesch. as =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ</quote> (fr. 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρευμενὴσἀ.</quote>, fr. 286 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγαν ἀ.</quote>). The form may have been generally current, since Pherecydes used <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Ἀλάστορος</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζ. Ἀλάστωρ</quote> (Cramer <title>Anecd.</title> 1. 62). The blind orbs are <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστοροι</quote>, ‘avenging spirits,’ in the sense that they mutely appeal to the gods for vengeance.—For the <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 371"> <title>O. T.</title> 371</ref>) Wolff cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.201"> <title>Il.</title> 6.201</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κὰπ πεδίον τὸ Ἀλήϊον οἶος ἀλᾶτο</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: κὰπ πεδίον τὸ Ἀλήϊον οἶος ἀλᾶτο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀραχθέντων_975">. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀράσσω</quote> is used of Oed. striking his eyes with the <quote xml:lang="grc">περόναι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1276"> <title>O. T.</title> 1276</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπό_975"> with dat. of the instrument, as in the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ χερσὶ δαμῆναι</quote>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 202"> <title>O. T.</title> 202</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ σῷ φθίσον κεραυνῷ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κερκίδων_976">, poet. plur. for sing., like <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμοί, σκῆπτρα</quote>, etc.—The <quote xml:lang="grc">κερκίς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κρέκω</quote>, to strike the web in weaving) was ‘like a large netting needle’ (Rich <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v. radius</foreign>), ‘rather longer than the breadth of the web.’ It was used for two purposes. (1) As a rod with which to strike the threads of the woof, in order to condense them. The flat blade called <quote xml:lang="grc">σπάθη</quote> was a later substitute. In the modern loom this is done by the moveable bar called the ‘batten.’ (2) As a shuttle, i.e., an instrument for shooting the threads of the woof (<quote xml:lang="grc">κρόκη</quote>) from one side of the loom to the other, between the threads of the warp (<quote xml:lang="grc">στήμων</quote>). In the East weavers sometimes use a long reed for both these purposes. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 198">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 198</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ Ἰδαίοις ἱστοῖς κερκίδα | δινεύουσ’ ἐξαλλάξω</quote> </cit> (‘no more,</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ Ἰδαίοις ἱστοῖς κερκίδα | δινεύουσ’
									ἐξαλλάξω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατά_977"> in tmesis, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1198"> <title>O. T.</title> 1198</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1689"> <title>O. C.</title> 1689</ref>, etc.—<quote xml:lang="grc">κατατακόμενοι</quote> alludes to their imprisonment; cp. schol. on 980 <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλώσασα τοὺς Κλεοπάτρας παῖδας ἐν τάφῳ καθεῖρξεν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλεοι_μελέαν_977">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 156">cp. 156</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 479"> <title>O. T.</title> 479</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρὸς_γονάν_980">, having their origin from an unhappily-married mother. The epithet is made to agree with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γονάν_980">, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρός_980">, as in 793, <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος‐ἀνδρῶν ξύναιμον</quote>: i.e., <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸσγονή</quote>, mother-source, forms one notion. For <quote xml:lang="grc">γονὰς ἔχειν</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 972"> <title>O. C.</title> 972</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς οὔτε βλάστας πω γενεθλίους πατρός, | οὐ μητρὸς εἶχον</quote> </cit>. For </gloss>

data.quote:
ὃς οὔτε βλάστας πω γενεθλίους πατρός, | οὐ
									μητρὸς εἶχον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνύμφ_980">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 144"> <title>Tr.</title> 144</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλοχοι μέλεαι...καὶ δύσνυμφοι</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 757"> <title>Hipp.</title> 757</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακονυμφοτάταν ὄνασιν</quote> (‘to bless her with a marriage most unblest’). <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1214"> <title>O. T.</title> 1214</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγαμον γάμον</quote> </cit>.—The comma should not be placed after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρός_980_2">, which is inseparable from the following phrase. Without <quote xml:lang="grc">ματρός</quote>, the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντες ἀνύμφευτον γονάν</quote> could still mean, ‘born from one who was unhappily married,’ but would be harsh and obscure. The word </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάθαν_980"> refers to their own fate. Then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρὸς_γονάν_980_2"> supplements this by indicating that they mourn for their mother's fate also. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπέρμα_981">, acc. of respect; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 15.267"> <title>Od.</title> 15.267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἰθάκης γένος εἰμί.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαιογ_981">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 202"> <title>Ai.</title> 202</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεᾶς χθονίων ἀπ’ Ἐρεχθειδῶν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄντασ_Ἐρ_981">, attained unto them, could trace her lineage to them,—her mother Oreithyia being the daughter of Erechtheus. Remark that the acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">σπέρμα</quote> mitigates the boldness of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντασε</quote>, and also suggests its primary meaning—viz., that the genealogy is carried back to a point at which it <emph>meets</emph> the Erechtheid line. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2.143">Her. 2.143</ref> (Hecataeus) <quote xml:lang="grc">γενεηλογν/σαντί τε ἑωυτὸν καὶ ἀναδήσαντι τὴν πατριὴν ἐς ἑκκαιδέκατον θεόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλεπόροις_983">, merely poet. for ‘distant’; lit., to which it is a far journey. Not (I think), ‘spacious’ (i.e. ‘<emph>in</emph> which one can go far’): nor, ‘extending far into the mountains.’ So in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 564"> <title>Ai.</title> 564</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλωπὸς οἰχνεῖ</quote>, the adj. is merely ‘distant’; it has not its full sense, ‘seen afar.’ Boreas carried Oreithyia to a region of Thrace which the poets called ‘Sarpêdon’ (we see the association with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρπάζω</quote>)—not, seemingly, the promontory called ‘Sarpedonion,’ on the S. coast, but in the wilds of Haemus. It is of this that Soph. is thinking here: cp. fr. 575 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς δ’ ἐν ἄντροις, ἔνθα Σαρπηδὼν πέτρα</quote>. That verse is from the <quote xml:lang="grc">Τυμπανισταί</quote>, in which the story of Cleopatra was noticed (cp. on 966); and she was probably the speaker. Oreithyia bore two sons to Boreas, Calais and Zetes; and, besides Cleopatra, another daughter, Chionè. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅμιππος_985">, swift as horses. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 466"> <title>O. T.</title> 466</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀελλάδων | ἵππων</quote> </cit>, ‘storm-swift steeds.’ In prose <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμιπποι</quote>=foot-soldiers who, in the Boeotian army, were sometimes told off to run alongside the cavalry (<ref n="Thuc. 5.57">Thuc. 5.57</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Hell. 7.5.23">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 7.5.23</ref>). Cp. Theogn. 715 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠκύτερος δ’ εἴησθα πόδας ταχεῶν Ἁρπυιῶν | καὶ παίδων Βορέω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθόποδος_985">, steep. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθόπους</quote>, ‘erect upon one's feet,’ seems to be here merely a poet. equiv. (suggested by metrical convenience) for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρθιος</quote>. This was the more natural, since <quote xml:lang="grc">πούς, κνήμη</quote>, etc., were so oft. said of mountains. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 866"> <title>O. T.</title> 866</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψίποδες</quote> </cit>, said of the eternal <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμοι</quote>, differs from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθόπους</quote> here by implying movement (‘of sublime range’). We need not, then, explain <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθόπους</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρθιος τοῖς τοῦ ἀναβαίνοντος ποσί</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπ_ἔσχον_986"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐκείνῃ ἔπεσχον</foreign>, from the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπέχω</quote> as=‘to direct (one's course) against a person,’ ‘to attack him’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 19.71"> <title>Od.</title> 19.71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μοι <emph>ὦδ’</emph> ἐπέχεις κεκοτηότι θυμῷ</quote> </cit>; (‘assail me’): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 22.75"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 22. 75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτῷ πάντες ἔχωμεν</quote> </cit> (‘let us all have at him’).—Others understand, ‘extended even to her,’ ‘reached her,’ which mars the personification.—</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακραίωνες_986">: <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 172">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 172</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιγενεῖς... Μοίρας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνακτες_988">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 843">cp. 843</ref>, 940.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>δύ’ ἐξ ἑνὸς βλ</emph>.</quote>, two seeing by the agency of one (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> as in 973): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 33"> <title>O. C.</title> 33</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ὑπέρ τ’ ἐμοῦ | αὑτῆς θ’ ὁρώσης</quote> </cit>. The words would usu. mean, ‘two seeing, where only one saw formerly.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1764"> <title>O. C.</title> 1764</ref>, where the regular sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν καλῶς</quote>, ‘to fare well,’ has not hindered the poet from using it as=‘to do rightly.’ </gloss>

data.quote: τῆς ὑπέρ τ’ ἐμοῦ | αὑτῆς θ’ ὁρώσης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνακτες_988">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 843">cp. 843</ref>, 940.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>δύ’ ἐξ ἑνὸς βλ</emph>.</quote>, two seeing by the agency of one (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> as in 973): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 33"> <title>O. C.</title> 33</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ὑπέρ τ’ ἐμοῦ | αὑτῆς θ’ ὁρώσης</quote> </cit>. The words would usu. mean, ‘two seeing, where only one saw formerly.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1764"> <title>O. C.</title> 1764</ref>, where the regular sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν καλῶς</quote>, ‘to fare well,’ has not hindered the poet from using it as=‘to do rightly.’ </gloss>

data.quote: τῆς ὑπέρ τ’ ἐμοῦ | αὑτῆς θ’ ὁρώσης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὕτη_κ_990">, the blind have this kind of walking appointed for them,— viz., walking with the help of a guide. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη κ. = αὕτη ἡ κ.</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 471"> <title>O. C.</title> 471</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλευθος</quote> is not predicate (like <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦλαν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 88"> <title>O. C.</title> 88</ref>), as if the sense were, ‘this (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote>) is walking for the blind,—viz. to walk with a guide.’ We do not need the art. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>, because </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλει_990">=not simply ‘is,’ but, ‘is possible.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 848"> <title>O. C.</title> 848</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν ποτ’ ἐκ</quote> (by means of) <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοιν γε μὴ σκήπτροιν ἔτι | ὁδοιπορήσῃς</quote> (the blind Oed.'s daughters). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστιν_991">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 20">cp. 20</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_γε_993">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 321">cp. 321</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῑ_ὀρθῆς_993">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῦ</quote>. A rare instance of the fem. adj. in such a phrase with <quote xml:lang="grc">διά</quote>, which regularly takes a subst. (742 n.); but it follows the analogy of the freq. phrases with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ εὐθείας</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 395"> <title>Tr.</title> 395</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ ταχείας</quote> </cit>, 727 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἑκουσίας</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.92">Thuc. 3.92</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ καινῆς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.116">Her. 5.116</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ νέης</quote> </cit>, 6. 85 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὑστέρης</quote>, 8. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τῆς ἀντίης</quote>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναυκλήρεις_993"> is right. The seer hopes, indeed, that the mischief can still be repaired (1025 ff.), but he thinks that Creon has made a disastrous mistake (1015). He could hardly say, then, <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ ὀρθῆς...<emph>ναυκληρεῖς</emph> </quote>. Creon has only just become king; but he had formerly been regent for some years (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1418"> <title>O. T.</title> 1418</ref>). Aesch. has the verb in this fig. sense ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 652">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 652</ref>). <ref n="Soph. Ant. 167">Cp. 167</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤρθου</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 104"> <title>O. T.</title> 104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπευθύνειν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 923"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 923</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυβερνήτην</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπονθὼς_μαρτυρεῖν_995"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πεπονθέναι</quote>). We could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">μαρτυρῶ σοι εὐεργετήσαντι</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">σύνοιδα</quote>): but less well, <quote xml:lang="grc">μαρτυρῶ εὖ πεπονθώς</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1128"> <title>O.
