C.</title> 1128</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδὼς δ’ ἀμύνω τοῖσδε τοῖς λόγοις τάδε</quote> </cit>, with like emphasis on the partic., ‘I have felt these benefits which I thus requite.’ </gloss>

data.quote: εἰδὼς δ’ ἀμύνω τοῖσδε τοῖς λόγοις τάδε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρόνει_βεβώς_996">, bethink thee that thou art placed. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1358"> <title>O. C.</title> 1358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πόνῳ | ... βεβηκώς</quote> </cit>, n. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 10.173"> <title>Il.</title> 10.173</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν γὰρ δὴ πάντεσσιν ἐπὶ ξυροῦ ἵσταται ἀκμῆς, | ἢ μάλα λυγρὸς ὄλεθρος Ἀχαιοῖς, ἠὲ βιῶναι</quote> </cit>. <title>H.F.</title> 630 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦδ’ ἔβητ’ ἐπὶ ξυροῦ</quote>; <title>Helen.</title> 897 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀκμῆς εἰμὶ κατθανόντ’ ἰδεῖν.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν γὰρ δὴ πάντεσσιν ἐπὶ ξυροῦ ἵσταται ἀκμῆς,
									| ἢ μάλα λυγρὸς ὄλεθρος Ἀχαιοῖς, ἠὲ βιῶναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύχης_996">, interpreting <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυροῦ</quote>, adds dignity and solemnity to the phrase. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_997">, exclamatory. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1112"> <title>El.</title> 1112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν, ὦ ξέν’; ὥς μ’ ὑπέρχεται φόβος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τί δ’ ἔστιν, ὦ ξέν’; ὥς μ’ ὑπέρχεται
									φόβος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θᾶκον_999">. Paus. (9. 16. 1) saw at Thebes, near the temple of Zeus Ammon, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰωνοσκοπεῖον...Τειρεσίου καλούμενον</quote>. Near it was a shrine of <quote xml:lang="grc">Τύχη.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιμήν_999">, a place to which the birds came: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρμος καὶ ἕδρα, ὅπου πάντα τὰ ὄρνεα προσέρχονται</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1077">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1077</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δῶμα πατρὸς καὶ μέγας πλούτου λιμήν</quote> </cit> (‘receptacle’): Aesch. applied the same phrase to Persia ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 250">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 250</ref>). Omens were taken, not only from the flight of birds, but also from the positions in which they settled,—from their sounds, —and from their mode of feeding. The <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμήν</quote> means a place to which they were lured by food, so that their <quote xml:lang="grc">συνεδρίαι</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. PB 492">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 492</ref>), and the other signs, could be noted. Cp. <title>H. A.</title> 9. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθεν καὶ τὰς διεδρίας καὶ τὰς συνεδρίας οἱ μάντεις λαμβάνουσι, δίεδρα μὲν τὰ πολέμια τιθέντες, σύνεδρα δὲ τὰ εἰρηνοῦντα πρὸς ἄλληλα</quote>.—Herwerden conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανοῦ λιμήν</quote>, understanding a space of sky chosen as a field of augural observation (<foreign xml:lang="lat">templum</foreign>). </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ δῶμα πατρὸς καὶ μέγας πλούτου
									λιμήν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῷ_1001">, ill-omened (<title>O.C.</title> 1433). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴστρῳ_1001">, ‘gad-fly,’ then fig., ‘rage,’ a word which often suggests divine stimulation: as Heracles asks, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ δ’ οἶστρος ἡμᾶς ἔλαβε</quote>; ( <title>H. F.</title> 1144).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλάζοντας_1001">, since <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόγγον ὀρνίθων</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρνιθας φθεγγομένους</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 17.755"> <title>Il.</title> 17.755</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’ ὥστε ψαρῶν νέφος ἔρχεται ἠὲ κολοιῶν | οὖλον κεκλήγοντες</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 12.181"> <title>Od.</title> 12.181</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὅτε τόσσον ἀπῆν</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ νηῦς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσον τε γέγωνε βοήσας, | ῥίμφα διώκοντες.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
τῶν δ’ ὥστε ψαρῶν νέφος ἔρχεται ἠὲ κολοιῶν |
									οὖλον κεκλήγοντες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βεβαρβαρωμένῳ_1001">. To the seer, the voices of birds were usually <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔσημοι</quote> (1021). Conversely the sound of a strange language is likened to the twittering of birds: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.57">Her. 2.57</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως δὲ ἐβαρβάριζε</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γυνή</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρνιθος τρόπον ἐδόκεέ σφι φθέγγεσθαι</quote>. Aesch. used <quote xml:lang="grc">χελιδονίζειν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">βαρβαρίζειν</quote> (fr. 440, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1050">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1050</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_χηλαῖσιν_1003">, ‘with’ them: 764 n. —</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοναῖς_1003">, an adverbial dat. of manner, murderously.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1318"> <title>O. C.</title> 1318</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχεται κατασκαφῇ | Καπανεὺς τὸ Θήβης ἄστυ δῃώσειν πυρί</quote>, where the first dat. is one of manner, like <quote xml:lang="grc">φοναῖς</quote> here, and the second (instrumental) answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν χηλαῖσιν</quote>. Elsewhere the Attic use of the subst. is limited to the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν φοναῖς</quote> (696 n.). The Schol. has <quote xml:lang="grc">φοναῖς· ταῖς αἱμακτικαῖς</quote>: as though it were from an adj. <quote xml:lang="grc">φονός</quote>. So some recent edd. take it. Such an adj. could have come from the rt. <quote xml:lang="grc">φεν</quote>, but there is no trace of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπύρων_1005">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερῶν</quote>, burnt-sacrifice; where the omen was given by the manner in which the fire dealt with the offering. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 155">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 155</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντεις δ’ ἐπῆλθες, ἐμπύρων τ’ εἶδες φλόγα</quote> </cit>; <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 954"> <title>Phoen.</title> 954</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπύρῳ χρῆται τέχνῃ</quote>. <title>I. T.</title> 16 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἔμπυρ’ ἦλθε</quote> (had recourse to). This was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δῑ ἐμπύρων μαντεία</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ignispicium</foreign>, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροσκοπία</quote>=<foreign xml:lang="lat">haruspicina</foreign>, divination by inspecting entrails. In , <ref n="Aesch. PB 488">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 488</ref>—499, <ref n="Aesch. PB 488">vv. 488—492</ref> concern <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρνιθομαντεία</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 493">vv. 493—5</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροσκοπία</quote>: and <ref n="Aesch. PB 496">vv. 496—9</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
μάντεις δ’ ἐπῆλθες, ἐμπύρων τ’ εἶδες
									φλόγα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγευόμην_1005">, proceeded to make trial of: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1101"> <title>Tr.</title> 1101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόχθων μυρίων ἐγευσάμην</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 475C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 475C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παντὸς μαθήματος γεύεσθαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παμφλέκτοισι_1006">, fully kindled. Fuel was placed around the offerings on the altar, and ignited at several points. The epithet marks that the failure of the rite was not due to any negligence.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμάτων_1006">. The offering consisted of thighbones cut from a sheep (or ox), with some of the flesh adhering to them, and wrapped round with a double covering of fat. On the top of these thigh-bones were laid parts of the victim's intestines (<quote xml:lang="grc">σπλάγχνα</quote>), including the gall-bladder (<quote xml:lang="grc">χολή</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἥφαιστος_1007"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">πῦρ</foreign> (n. on 120 ff.). It was a good sign if the fire at once seized on the offering, and blazed up in clear flames (Apoll. Rhod. 1. 436 <quote xml:lang="grc">σέλας... | πάντοσε λαμπόμενον θυέων ἄπο</quote>). It was</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μυδῶσα_1008">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 410">cp. 410</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1278"> <title>O. T.</title> 1278</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνου μυδώσας σταγόνας.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηκὶς_μηρίων_1008">, a moisture exuding from them. For <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote> <ref target="cl1011">see on 1011</ref>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 268">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 268</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κηκῖδι πισσήρει φλογός</quote> </cit>, pitchy ooze of flame, i.e., the funeral-fire of pine-wood from which pitch oozes. We might perh. join <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρίων ἐτήκετο</quote>, ‘was distilled from them’: but the other constr. is simpler, and <quote xml:lang="grc">τήκεσθαί τινος</quote> is not found elsewhere.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐτήκετο_1008"> here=exuded: it goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_σποδῷ_1008"> (the embers of the fuel placed around the offering).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέπτυε_1008">, as particles of the fat crackled and were tossed upward on contact with the smouldering fire. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χολαί_1010">. Arist. always uses the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">χολή</quote> for the gall-bladder. In <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 82E">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 82E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χολάς</quote> </cit>=‘kinds of bile,’ the <quote xml:lang="grc">χολῆς εἴδη</quote> of 83 C. Here there was a metrical motive (<quote xml:lang="grc">διεσπείροντο</quote>) for the plur., which denotes not merely the gallbladder, but also the gall dispersed from it. The gall-bladder, and the lobe of the liver, afforded omens, by colour and form, in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροσκοπία</quote> (1005 n.): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 495">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 495</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χολῆς λοβοῦ τε ποικίλην εὐμορφίαν</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Eur. El. 827">Eur. <title>El.</title> 827</ref> ff. But here, in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπυρα</quote>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">χολή</quote> was simply a part of the burnt-offering,—added to the <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote>, because otherwise associated with divination. Cp. the unknown poet in Clemens <title>Strom.</title>p. 851 (it is vain to think that the gods rejoice) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀστῶν ἀσάρκων καὶ χολῆς πυρουμένης</quote>. So, too, Menander <emph>ap.</emph> Athen. 146 E <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ τὴν ὀσφὺν ἄκραν | καὶ τὴν χολὴν ὀστᾶ τ’ ἄβρωτα τοῖς θεοῖς | ἐπιθέντες αὐτοὶ τἄλλα καταπίνουσ’ ἀεί</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταρρυεῖς_1010">, running down, dripping, with the fat which was melting off them: Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρρεόμενοι, καθυγραινόμενοι</quote>. This use of the adj. is parallel with a frequent use of the verb, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 15">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν ἀνάκτορα | φόνῳ καταρρεῖ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 8.65"> <title>Il.</title> 8.65</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥέε δ’ αἴματι γαῖα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 142">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 142</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῖ δὲ γάλακτι πέδον</quote> </cit>, etc.—<quote xml:lang="grc">καταρρυεῖς</quote> could also mean, ‘slipping down’; but it does not appear that the <quote xml:lang="grc">μηροί</quote> were displaced; they were merely bared. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηροί_μηρίων_1011"> in 1008,—thighbones, with some flesh on them. <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρός</quote> is the ordinary word for ‘thigh.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote> was the sacrificial word, denoting thighbones, with so much flesh as the sacrificer chose to leave upon them. The tendency to give the gods more bone than meat is noticed by the poets quoted on v. 1010 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀστῶν ἀσάρκων—ὀστᾶ ἄβρωτα</quote>), and by <ref n="Hes. Th. 556">Hes. <title>Th.</title> 556</ref>(where men offer <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀστέα λευκά</quote> to the gods),— as it is implied in the story there told, of Prometheus giving the worst parts of the ox to Zeus, and keeping the best for men. Since the bone was an essential part of the offering, <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote> cannot be merely, ‘slices cut from the thighs.’ In the Homeric phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ πίονα μηρία καίειν</quote>, the word means, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μηροί</quote> here, thigh-bones wrapped in fat, the <quote xml:lang="grc">κνίσῃ... κῶλα συγκαλυπτά</quote> of <ref n="Aesch. PB 496">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 496</ref>. In <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 3.456"> <title>Od.</title> 3.456</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ μηρία τάμνον | πάντα κατὰ μοῖραν</quote> </cit>, the phrase is equiv. to the <quote xml:lang="grc">μηροὺς ἐξέταμον</quote> of the <title>Il.</title>(1. 460 etc.); i.e., <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote> includes the bones. Only one ox is there in question, but <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα</quote>=‘completely.’ The <quote xml:lang="grc">ηομ. μῆρα = μηρία</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 1.464"> <title>Il.</title> 1.464</ref>).</gloss>

data.quote: ἐκ μηρία τάμνον | πάντα κατὰ μοῖραν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλυπτῆς_1011">=‘which had been wrapped round them’; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 21.321"> <title>Il.</title> 21.321</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσσην οἱ ἄσιν καθύπερθε καλύψω</quote> </cit>, ‘so thick a covering of silt will I lay on him.’ This is better than to make the adj. active, ‘covering,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμπτός</quote>, ‘blaming’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 446"> <title>Tr.</title> 446</ref>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιμελῆς_1011"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πίων</quote>), prop., soft fat (<foreign xml:lang="lat">adeps</foreign>), as dist. from <quote xml:lang="grc">στέαρ</quote>, stiff fat, tallow (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sebum</foreign>). The fat was laid in a double layer round the <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.460"> <title>Il.</title> 1.460</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρούς τ’ ἐξέταμον κατά τε κνίσῃ ἐκάλυψαν, | δίπτυχα ποιήσαντες</quote> </cit>. So human bones are wrapped <quote xml:lang="grc">δίπλακι δημῷ</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.243"> <title>Il.</title> 23.243</ref>.— </gloss>

data.quote:
μηρούς τ’ ἐξέταμον κατά τε κνίσῃ ἐκάλυψαν, |
									δίπτυχα ποιήσαντες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέκειντο_1011">, lay outside of, i.e., had been bared of, the fat. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτα_1012">, adverbially with <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνοντα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 848">cp. 848</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵα</quote> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργίων_μαντεύματα_1012">, ‘oracles derived from rites,’ —the predictions which he could have made if the rites had given him a sign. They gave none; and so his hopes of reading the future came to nought (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνοντα_1012">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 906"> <title>O. T.</title> 906</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνοντα... | θέσφατα</quote> </cit>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 765"> <title>Tr.</title> 765</ref> (where Heracles offers burnt sacrifice) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως δὲ σεμνῶν ὀργίων ἐδαίετο | φλὸξ αἱματηρά.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσήμων_1012">, not giving the <quote xml:lang="grc">φλογωπὰ σήματα</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. PB 498">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 498</ref>) which burnt offerings can yield. Such signs might be good or evil, according to the aspects of the fire (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1007">cp. 1007</ref> n.). But here the fire had refused to burn at all. Like the birds, these rites also had left him without any definite sign—though with a strengthened presentiment of evil. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡγεμών_1014">. Cp. Statius <ref n="Stat. Theb. 10.603"> <title>Theb.</title> 10. 603</ref>: the daughter of Teiresias describes the omens to him, <quote xml:lang="lat">patriasque illuminat umbras.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1015">, of cause, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 620"> <title>O. C.</title> 620</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σμικροῦ λόγου</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 957">Cp. 957</ref>, 973.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενός_1015">, counsel, as 993.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσεῖ_1015">, i.e., has incurred a <quote xml:lang="grc">μίασμα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1141">cp. 1141</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμοί_1016">, the public altars of the gods, usu. raised on a base (<quote xml:lang="grc">κρηπίς</quote>) with steps (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 854">cp. 854</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 182"> <title>O. T.</title> 182</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσχάραι_1016">, portable braziers, used in private houses either for sacrifice to household deities (esp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑστία</quote>), or for purposes of cooking. Harpocration <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign> quotes Ammonius of Lamprae (an Attic writer of the 1st cent. A.D. , who left a treatise <quote xml:lang="grc">Περὶ βωμῶν καὶ θυσιῶν</quote>):—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάραν φησὶ καλεῖσθαι τὴν μὴ ἔχουσαν ὕψος,...ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ γῆς ίδρυμένην</quote>. It stood on four legs, instead of having a pedestal like the <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμός</quote> (Ross <title>Inscrr.</title> 3. 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάραν τετράποδον</quote>). It was used in sacrifice to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥρωες</quote>, who, not being <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοί</quote>, had no claim to <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμοί</quote>: Pollux 1. 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάρα δ’ ἰδικῶς δοκεῖ ὠνομάσθαι, ἐφ’ ἧς τοῖς ἥρωσιν ἀποθύομεν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντελεῖς_1016">, in their full tale, ‘one and all.’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁλόκληροι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁλοσχερεῖς</quote> could be used, where the notion was that of a total to which no unit was lacking.—Not, ‘receiving <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερὰ τέλεια</quote>’; nor, ‘serving for all rites’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τέλη</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλήρεις_1017"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσίν</quote>) are defiled, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπ_κυνῶν_1017">, by birds and dogs, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βορᾶς_γόνου_1017">, with their food, (torn) from the son of Oed. This sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήρης</quote> belongs also to <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέως</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">μεστός</quote>, but esp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάπλεως</quote>, as to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπίμπλημι</quote>. The fig. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήρεις</quote> might here allow us to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">βορᾶς</quote>, but it goes more naturally with the agents. For the gen. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόνου_1017">, describing the source or material of the <quote xml:lang="grc">βορά</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1220">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1220</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρεῶν...οἰκείας βορᾶς</quote> </cit>, food supplied by their own flesh (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκείας</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκείων</quote>: cp. above, 793). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσμόρου_1017">, adverbially with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπτῶτος_1017">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσμόρως</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 823">cp. 823</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λυγροτάταν ὀλέσθαι</quote>, n. —Two other constructions are possible. (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_γόνου_1017"> in appos. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βορᾶς_1017">: ‘their food,—viz., the son’: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1040">cp. 1040</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βορὰν | φέρειν νιν</quote>. But this seems forced, when the reference is to dispersed morsels of his flesh. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_γόνου_1017_2"> as gen. absol., ‘as,’ or ‘since,’ he has fallen. Such a gen, absol., however, ought here to express, not, ‘as he has fallen,’ but, ‘as he has been left unburied.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κᾆτ_1019">, ‘and then,’ here=‘and so.’ It usually means, ‘and after that,’ i.e., ‘and nevertheless’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 418"> <title>O. C.</title> 418</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυστάδας_1019">, accompanying sacrifice: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 269">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 269</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλληνικὸν νόμισμα θυστάδος βοῆς</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.499"> <title>Il.</title> 9.499</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὲν τοὺς</quote> </cit> (the gods) <quote xml:lang="grc">θυέεσσι καὶ εὐχωλῇς ἀγανῇσιν | λοιβῇ τε κνίσῃ τε παρατρωπῶσ’ ἄνθρωποι | λισσόμενοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρνις_1021">, as <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.219"> <title>Il.</title> 24.219</ref>; <ref n="Soph. El. 149"> <title>El.</title> 149</ref>; <title>H. F.</title> 72, fr. 637: <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 168">Ar. <title>Av.</title> 168</ref> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ὄρνις οὗτος</quote>, a quotation from tragedy: <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς οὗτος ὄρνις</quote>;). But <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρνι—ς</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Ba. 1364">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1364</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 833">Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 833</ref>, etc.) is said to have been normal in Attic. —The ruggedness of the rhythm gives a certain impressive slowness, perhaps purposed. When an iambic verse has no caesura in the 3rd or in the 4th foot, it almost always has the ‘quasi-caesura’ (elision) after the 3rd foot (as if <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσήμους</quote> were <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφημοῦσ’</quote>). For other exceptions, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1091"> <title>Ai.</title> 1091</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Μενέλαε, μὴ γνώμας ὑποστήσας σοφάς</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101"> <title>Ph.</title> 101</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1064"> Ph. 1064</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1369"> Ph. 1369</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 509">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 509</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θρῄκην περάσαντες μόγις πολλῷ πόνῳ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσήμους_1021">: cp. on 1002. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βεβρῶτες_1021">, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντες ὄρνιθες σιγῶσι</quote> had preceded. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.87">Her. 1.87</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὥρα πάντα μὲν ἄνδρα σβεννύντα τὸ πῦρ, δυναμένους δὲ οὐκέτι καταλαβεῖν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς ὥρα πάντα μὲν ἄνδρα σβεννύντα τὸ πῦρ,
									δυναμένους δὲ οὐκέτι καταλαβεῖν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδροφθόρου_1021">. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρόφθορον αἷμα = ἀνδρὸς ἐφθαρμένου αἷμα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 208"> <title>Ph.</title> 208</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδὰ τρυσάνωρ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 711"> <title>O. C.</title> 711</ref>, n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔχημα εὔιππον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεί_1025">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάν</quote>, with subjunct.: <ref n="Soph. OC 1225"> <title>O. C.</title> 1225</ref>. The subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμάρτῃ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>) is quickly supplied by the next clause.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνολβος_1025">, of folly, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1156"> <title>Ai.</title> 1156</ref>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσποτμος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 888"> <title>O. T.</title> 888</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκεῖται_1027">. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.115"> <title>Il.</title> 13.115</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀκεώμεθα θᾶσσον· ἀκεσταί τοι φρένες ἐσθλῶν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἀκεώμεθα θᾶσσον· ἀκεσταί τοι φρένες
									ἐσθλῶν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκίνητος_1027">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 336"> <title>O. T.</title> 336</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτεγκτος</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 51E">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 51E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὲν ἀεὶ μετὰ ἀληθοῦς λόγου, τὸ δὲ ἄλογον· καὶ τὸ μὲν ἀκίνητον πειθοῖ, τὸ δὲ μεταπειστόν</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.203"> <title>Il.</title> 15.203</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ τι μεταστρέψεις; στρεπταὶ μέν τε φρένες ἐσθλῶν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ μὲν ἀεὶ μετὰ ἀληθοῦς λόγου, τὸ δὲ ἄλογον·
									καὶ τὸ μὲν ἀκίνητον πειθοῖ, τὸ δὲ μεταπειστόν

ἤ τι μεταστρέψεις; στρεπταὶ μέν τε φρένες
									ἐσθλῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐθαδία_1028"> (poet. for <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθάδεια</quote>), self-will, incurs the reproach of <quote xml:lang="grc">σκαιότης</quote> (for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀφλισκάνει_1028"> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 470">cp. 470</ref>). As <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιός</quote> is a quick-witted man, of flexible and receptive mind, so <quote xml:lang="grc">σκαιός</quote> is one whose mental clumsiness makes him unapt to learn. <quote xml:lang="grc">σκαιότης</quote>, ‘ineptitude,’ is often associated with ignorance and with inaccessibility to new ideas. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 411E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 411E</ref>; one who omits to cultivate his mind acts <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ...καὶ ἀγριότητι, ὥσπερ θηρίον..., καὶ ἐν ἀμαθίᾳ καὶ σκαιότητι μετὰ ἀρρυθμίας τε καὶ ἀχαριστίας ζῇ</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 10.15">Lys. or. 10 § 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγοῦμαι...τοῦτον...οὕτω σκαιὸν εἷναι ὥστε οὐ δύνασθαι μαθεῖν τὰ λεγόμενα</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Wasps 1183">Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 1183</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σκαιὲ κἀπαίδευτε</quote> </cit>. So here <quote xml:lang="grc">σκαιότης</quote> expresses a stupidity that is deaf to remonstrance. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡγοῦμαι...τοῦτον...οὕτω σκαιὸν εἷναι ὥστε οὐ
									δύνασθαι μαθεῖν τὰ λεγόμενα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶκε_θανόντι_1029">, ‘make a concession to the dead,’ i.e., give him the burial rites which are his due. It is not as if he were a living foe, and prowess (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκή</quote>) could be shown by resisting his claim. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ θανόντι</quote> have been groundlessly suspected (see cr. n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέντει_1029">, stab. Cp. the scene in the <title>Iliad</title> where the Greeks prick Hector's corpse with their swords; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.371"> <title>Il.</title> 22.371</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἄρα οἵ τις ἀνουτητί γε παρέστη</quote> </cit>: and <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.421"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 24. 421</ref>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">κεντεῖν</quote> of cowardly or treacherous wounding, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1244"> <title>Ai.</title> 1244</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς ἢ κακοῖς βαλεῖτέ που | ἢ σὺν δόλῳ κεντήσεθ’ οἱ λελειμμένοι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: οὐδ’ ἄρα οἵ τις ἀνουτητί γε παρέστη

ἡμᾶς ἢ κακοῖς βαλεῖτέ που | ἢ σὺν δόλῳ
									κεντήσεθ’ οἱ λελειμμένοι.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπικτανεῖν_1030">, ‘slay <emph>anew.</emph>’ In comp. with verbs of killing, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> usu.= either ‘in addition’ (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1733"> <title>O. C.</title> 1733</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπενά<*>οιξον</quote> </cit>, n.), or ‘over’ a grave, etc., as usu. <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>πισφάττειν</quote>: but <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1288">cp. 1288</ref>: Laert. <ref n="D. L. 2.17">Diog. 2. 17</ref>§ 135 (Menedemus) <quote xml:lang="grc">Βίωνος...ἐπιμελῶς κατατρέχοντος τῶν μάντεων νεκροὺς αὐτὸς ἐπισφάττειν ἔλεγε</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 946">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 946</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναίρων νεκρόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_φρονήσας_1031">, having conceived kindly thoughts; a very rare use of the aor. part. in this sense, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ φρονῶν</quote>. The aor. part. of <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονέω</quote> usu. means, (1) ‘having come to a sound mind,’ <ref n="Soph. OT 649"> <title>O. T.</title> 649</ref>, and so <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 8.141">Isocr. or. 8. § 141,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ φρονήσαντας</quote> </cit>: (2) ‘having formed a project,’ as <ref n="Hdt. 7.145">Her. 7.145</ref>: (3) in the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τὠυτὸ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ αὐτὰ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονήσαντες</quote>, ‘having come to an agreement,’ <ref n="Hdt. 1.60">Her. 1.60</ref>, 5. 72.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μανθάνειν_δ_1031">': for the elision (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισυναλοιφή</quote>) see <ref n="Soph. OT 29"> <title>O. T.</title> 29</ref> n.: and cp. above, 350.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_λέγοι_1031">: for the optative in the <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote>, see 666 n. With </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδιστον_1031"> we supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 315"> <title>O. T.</title> 315</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_1033"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign>: <title>O.C.</title> 343.—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκοποῦ_1033">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοξεύουσι</quote>: the gen. as with <quote xml:lang="grc">στοχάζομαι</quote>: so <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 4.100"> <title>Il.</title> 4.100</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀΐστευσον Μενελάου</quote> </cit>: 14. 402 <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴαντος δὲ πρῶτος ἀκόντισε</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 241">Cp. 241</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐδὲ_μαντικῆς_1034"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: not even by seer-craft do ye leave me unattempted: in your plots against me ye resort even to seer-craft. Two points in this phrase are notable. (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπρακτος_1034"> =‘not worked,’ in the sense of, ‘not plotted against.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν</quote> oft.=‘to intrigue’; and ‘to intrigue <emph>against</emph> one’ might be expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειππερί τινος</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί τινι</quote>, though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβουλεύο τινί</quote> is the usu. phrase. But, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβουλεύομαι</quote> had a personal pass. use (to be plotted against’), we could not say <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσονται</quote>, ‘they are the objects of an intrigue.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπρακτος</quote> is therefore bolder than its prose equivalent, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεπιβούλευτος</quote>. Still, for poetry, it seems possible. (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντικῆς_1034">. Such a gen., joined to a verbal adj. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_1034"> privative, more often denotes the agent, answering to a gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> after a pass. verb, or to the subject of an act. verb: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 847">cp. 847</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 685"> <title>Tr.</title> 685</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτῖνος...ἄθικτον</quote> </cit> (untouched <emph>by</emph> the ray). Here, the instrument, <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντική</quote>, is, in fact, personified as the agent: i.e., <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντικῆς</quote> does not correspond to the instrum. dat. in <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μαντικῇ πράσσετε περὶ ἐμοῦ</quote>, but to the nom. in <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μαντικὴ πράσσει περὶ ἐμοῦ ὑμῖν</quote> (ye have even seer-craft practising on me). An easier reading would be </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντικῇ_1034">. The instrumental dat. is often retained with the negative verbal; as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 219E">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 219E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρήμασι...μᾶλλον ἄτρωτος ἢ σιδήρῳ</quote> </cit>: fr. com. anon. 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεπιβουλεύτου φθόνῳ</quote>. But poetical usage seems to warrant <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντικῆς</quote>.—The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπρατος_1034"> (see Appendix) would forestall the taunt which now forms the climax, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξημπόλημαι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_γένους_1034">, ‘by the tribe of those men.’—the <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντεις</quote> implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντικῆς</quote>. Creon, though he addresses Teiresias, is speaking as much to the Chorus as to him. If we read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_1034"> (without <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’</quote> </emph>), as relative, it would naturally</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῖν_1034">: it could hardly refer to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντικῆς_1034_2">. The conjecture of Semitelos, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντικοῖς_1034">, would then be attractive. But such a substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντεσι</quote> would be very strange. And, if we keep L's <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’</quote> </emph>, the scornful demonstrative sufficiently interprets the reference to <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντεις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπαί_1034"> in trimeters, as <ref n="Soph. El. 711"> <title>El.</title> 711</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 892">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 892</ref>, 944, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 417."> <title>Eum.</title> 417.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένους_1034">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1055">cp. 1055</ref>.—For other views of the passage, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξημπόλημαι_1036">. Creon means: ‘The Thebans have bribed Teiresias to frighten me. He has taken their money. In return, he is to deliver me into their hands. I am like a piece of merchandise which has been sold for export, and put on board the buyer's ship.’ <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1063">Cp. 1063</ref>. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.1">Her. 1.1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεμπολημένων</quote> </cit> (Ion.) <quote xml:lang="grc">σφι σχεδὸν πάντων</quote>, when they had sold off almost everything.—Neither <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφορτίζομαι</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφορτίζομαι</quote> occurs elsewhere, except that an old glossary (cited by Dind.) gives <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεφορτίσατο</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">exoneravit</foreign> (‘unladed’). In later Greek we find <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφορτοῦσθαι ναῦν, ἔμφορτος</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφορτοῦν</quote> (both act. and midd.). Here, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπεφόρτισμαι_1036">, the reading of the first hand in L, marks the completion of the sale by the delivery of the goods. The Schol. quotes Callimachus (fr. 529), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποιήσαντό με φόρτον</quote>.—The correction in L, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπεφόρτισμαι_1036">, is far inferior. It would mean, ‘unladed (as a cargo) from a ship’: not, ‘made into a cargo,’ nor, ‘exported as a cargo.’—In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 537"> <title>Tr.</title> 537</ref> there is a like association of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπόλημα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">φόρτος</quote> (though the passage is not otherwise similar). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. ERR 3.1">Shaks. <title>Com. Err.</title> 3. 1. 72</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘It would make a man mad as a buck, to be so bought and sold.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
‘It would make a man mad as a buck, to be so
									bought and sold.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὸ_ἤλεκτρον_1037">: electron, or silver-gold, from the goldmines of Tmolus in Lydia, the range s. of Sardis. Croesus dedicated at Delphi a lion of refined gold (<quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσὸς ἄπεφθος</quote>), standing on a pedestal formed by 117 half-plinths, or ingots, of gold,—four being of refined gold, and the rest of this electron, or ‘white gold’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">λευκὸς χρυσός</quote>); <ref n="Hdt. 1.50">Her. 1.50</ref>. The celebrity of this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάθημα</quote> in Greece helps to explain the poet's phrase. Stein on <foreign xml:lang="lat"> Her.l.c.</foreign> shows that the ratio of silver to gold in electron was about 3 to 7. Pliny, who makes the ratio only 1 to 4, describes electron both as a natural blend of metals, and as an artificial product (<quote xml:lang="lat">fit et cura, … addito argento</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Her. 33.80"> Her. 33.80</ref>).— <ref n="Paus. 5. 12">Paus. 5. 12</ref>§ 7 distinguishes the two senses of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤλεκτρον</quote>, (1) silver-gold, (2) amber. The latter is the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤλεκτρον</quote> of Herodotus (3. 115), and of <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.460"> <title>Od.</title> 15.460</ref>, where a Phoenician brings a golden <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρμος, —μετὰ δ’ ἠλέκτροισιν ἔερτο</quote> (‘strung with amber beads’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὸ_1037"> is a certain correction of L's <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ προ</quote> (cr. n.): in class. Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤλεκτρον</quote> is always neut., as it is in Paus. also.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰνδικὸν_χρυσόν_1037">: <ref n="Hdt. 3.94">Her. 3.94</ref> speaks of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰνδοί</quote> as sending Dareius an annual tribute of 360 talents in gold dust (<quote xml:lang="grc">ψῆγμα</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_αἰετοί_1040">: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 24.310"> <title>Il.</title> 24.310</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς τε σοὶ αὐτῷ | φίλτατος οἰωνῶν, καί εὑ κράτος ἐστὶ μέγιστον</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 4</ref>(the Delphian priestess) <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσέων Διὸς αἰητῶν πάρεδρος</quote> (the golden eagles on the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμφαλός</quote>). <ref n="Hor. Carm. 4. 4. 1">Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 4. 4. 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">ministrum fulminis alitem.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅς τε σοὶ αὐτῷ | φίλτατος οἰωνῶν, καί εὑ
									κράτος ἐστὶ μέγιστον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ὥς_1042">, not even (I say) in that case,—repeating the supposition, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ εἰ θέλουσ’</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.379"> <title>Il.</title> 9.379 ff.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ εἴ μοι δεκάκις τε καὶ εἰκοσάκις τόσα δοίη, | ... οὐδέ κεν ὣς ἔτι θυμὸν ἐμὸν πείσεῑ Ἀγαμέμνων</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Hom. Od. 22.61"> <title>Od.</title> 22.61</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ εἴ μοι πατρώϊα πάντ’ ἀποδοῖτε | ...οὐδέ κεν ὣς ἔτι χεῖρας ἐμὰς λήξαιμι φόνοιο</quote>.—Attic prose, too, used <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ὥς</quote>, ‘even in that case’ (<ref n="Thuc. 1.44">Thuc. 1.44</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὥς</quote> (id. 1. 132), etc.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>παρήσω.</emph> οὐ μή</quote>, with the 2nd pers. fut. ind., prohibits; but with the 1st or 3rd pers. it can be used in emphatic denial, though the aor. subjunct. is more usual: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1052"> <title>El.</title> 1052</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ σοι μὴ μεθέψομαί ποτε</quote> </cit>: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 177"> <title>O. C.</title> 177</ref>. There is no reason, then, for suspecting the text (see cr. n.). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδ’ εἴ μοι δεκάκις τε καὶ εἰκοσάκις τόσα
									δοίη, | ... οὐδέ κεν ὣς ἔτι θυμὸν ἐμὸν πείσεῑ Ἀγαμέμνων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὺς_μιαίνειν_1044">. Teiresias had said that the altars were defiled (1016). Creon replies that he will not yield, even if birds fly with the carrion up to the very throne of Zeus;—‘<emph>for no mortal can pollute the gods.</emph>’ Campbell takes this to be an utterance of scepticism, like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔφα τις | θεοὺς βροτῶν ἀξιοῦσθαι μέλειν</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. Ag. 369">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 369</ref>),—anticipating the Epicurean conception of gods who are neither pleased nor angered by men.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλά_1046">, adv.,=‘very,’ with adj.: <ref n="Soph. OC 1514"> <title>O. C.</title> 1514</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_1047">, = <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὐπρεπῶς</foreign>, in a bad sense: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 505">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 505</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾀσχρὰ δ’ ἢν λέγῃς καλῶς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 5.89">Thuc. 5.89</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετ’ ὀνομάτων καλῶν</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 1191">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄδικ’ εὖ λέγειν</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 807"> <title>O. C.</title> 807</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_οἶδεν_1048"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Instead of being angered by Creon's bitter words, Teiresias is communing with the mournful thought which they suggest—the thought of human folly. His sorrowful exclamation here is like his <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντες γὰρ οὐ φρονεῖτ’</quote> in the scene with Oedipus (<ref n="Soph. OT 328"> <title>O. T.</title> 328</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_χρῆμα_1049">; Cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 754">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 754</ref> (Hecuba having said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκετεύω</quote>,) <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΓ. τί χρῆμα μαστεύουσα</quote>; So oft. in questions, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 228"> <title>Ai.</title> 228</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1231."> <title>Ph.</title> 1231.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγκοινον_1049">, a sneer at the generality of the seer's exordium. What aphorism is this to be? But the seer's thought has a terribly definite point, as Creon is soon to feel (1066). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτημάτων_1050">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 684">cp. 684</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσῳπερ_1050"> with superl., as <ref n="Soph. OC 743"> <title>O. C.</title> 743</ref> n. By </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_φρονεῖν_1050"> Creon hints that the seer's cleverness has outrun his prudence (1046). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόσου_1052">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 732">cp. 732</ref>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλήρης_1052">, 1017. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μήν_1054">, ‘and verily,’ meaning here, ‘and <emph>yet</emph>,’—the adversative force arising from the contrast between Creon's profession and his practice. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 221">Cp. 221</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγεις_1054">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς τὸν μάντιν</quote>.—For the metre, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 44">cp. 44</ref>, 502. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένος_1055">: 1035. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 520">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 520</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μαντικὸν πᾶν σπέρμα φιλότιμον κακόν</quote> </cit>. <title>Helen.</title> 755 (of <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντική</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">βίου γὰρ ἄλλως δέλεαρ ηὑρέθη τόδε, | κοὐδεὶς ἐπλούτησ’ ἐμπύροισιν ἀργὸς ὤν</quote>,—i.e., the seer's client is never enriched (though the seer himself is). </gloss>

data.quote: τὸ μαντικὸν πᾶν σπέρμα φιλότιμον κακόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τυράννων_1056">. The text is sound. Instead of saying, ‘the race of tyrants’ (i.e., all the tyrants who exist), he says, with more rhetorical force, ‘the race <emph>bred</emph> of tyrants,’ i.e., the tyrants whose progenitors have also been tyrants. Thus </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1056"> expresses that the love of ‘base gain’ is hereditary. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τύραννος</quote> in the bad sense, see <ref n="Soph. OT 873"> <title>O. T.</title> 873</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχροκέρδειαν_1056">: not in the literal sense in which Creon imputed it to his servants (313), but in this, that Creon secures an unworthy personal triumph by trampling on religion and silencing just remonstrance (505 ff.). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1349"> <title>Ai.</title> 1349</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ χαῖρ’, Ἀτρείδη, κέρδεσιν τοῖς μὴ καλοῖς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ χαῖρ’, Ἀτρείδη, κέρδεσιν τοῖς μὴ
									καλοῖς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_οἶσθα_1057"> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγων ταγοὺς ὄντας ἃ ἂν λέγῃς</quote>; knowest thou that whatever it pleases thee to say is said of men who are rulers? <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω τινά τι</quote>=to say something <emph>of</emph> him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_λέγῃς_1057"> is a scornful euphemism, implying that he indulges in random abuse. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταγούς_1057">: only here in Soph. : oft. in Aesch. (in <ref n="Aesch. PB 96">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 96</ref>Zeus is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ νέος ταγὸς μακάρων</quote>); once in Eur. , <title>I. A.</title> 269 (Adrastus). Here the word is not specially=<quote xml:lang="grc">στρατηγός</quote> (8), but simply =<quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλεύς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἐμοῦ_1058">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1221"> <title>O. T.</title> 1221</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέπνευσα ...ἐκ σέθεν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_σώσας_1058">, merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">σέσωκας</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 22">cp. 22</ref>). The rare position of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις</quote> might suggest the prose sense (‘thou hast saved, and keepest’); but that position occurs where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω</quote> is merely the auxiliary (794; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 22"> <title>Ai.</title> 22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει περάνας</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀκίνητα_φρενῶν_1060">, = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ διὰ φρενῶν ἀκίνητα</foreign>, those secrets in my soul which ought to be let alone. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1526"> <title>O. C.</title> 1526</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ δ’ ἐξάγιστα μηδὲ κινεῖται λόγῳ</quote> </cit>, n. For the place of the adv. <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ φρενῶν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 659">cp. 659</ref> n.: for <quote xml:lang="grc">διά</quote>, 639 n.: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 593">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 593</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαθεῖαν ἄλοκα διὰ φρενὸς καρπούμενος</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἃ δ’ ἐξάγιστα μηδὲ κινεῖται λόγῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κίνει_1061">: a word used esp. of sacrilege: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.134">Her. 6.134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κινήσοντά τι τῶν ἀκινήτων</quote> </cit> (in a temple): <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.98">Thuc. 4.98</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕδωρ...κινῆσαι</quote> </cit> (to profane, by secular use, water reserved for sacrifices).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_δέ_1061">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">κίνει.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_κέρδεσιν_1061">, i.e., with a view to receiving money from the Thebans for persuading me to bury Polyneices. So Oed. (<ref n="Soph. OT 388"> <title>O. T.</title> 388</ref>) calls the seer, <quote xml:lang="grc">δόλιον ἀγύρτην, ὅστις ἐν τοῖς κέρδεσιν | μόνον δέδορκε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτω_ἤδη_1062">: ‘indeed, as matters stand (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δοκῶ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">λέξειν</quote>), I think that I <emph>shall</emph> speak thus—i.e., not for gain—so far as thou art concerned.’ The seer, with grave irony, gives a new turn to Creon's phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐπὶ κέρδεσιν</quote>, and says that the admonition is superfluous. The message which he has to utter is fraught with no <quote xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</quote>—for Creon. For the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</quote> in this general sense, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1326">cp. 1326</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μέρος_1062"> here=<foreign xml:lang="lat">quantum ad te attinet:</foreign> a sense quite as correct for it as the more usual <foreign xml:lang="lat">quantum in te est</foreign> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1509"> <title>O. T.</title> 1509</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1366"> <title>O. C.</title> 1366</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1215"> <title>Tr.</title> 1215</ref>). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_1062"> emphasising <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">λέξειν</quote>), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 726">cp. 726</ref>. Creon's reply (1063) refers to the covert threat: ‘say what thou wilt, thou shalt not shake my purpose.’— The choice lies between this view and that of the Scholiast, who makes the verse interrogative:—<quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω νομίζεις, ὅτι ἐπὶ κέρδεσι λέγω</quote>; i.e., ‘what, do I seem <emph>now</emph>— on thy part—to be speaking for money?’ The points in favour of the Scholiast's interpretation are:—(<emph>a</emph>) The combination <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ...καί</quote> (before the verb) suits an indignant question: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 770">cp. 770</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1124"> <title>Tr.</title> 1124</ref>. (<emph>b</emph>) The tone of rising anger—which began at 1060—fitly preludes the outburst at 1064: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 343"> <title>O. T.</title> 343</ref>—350. But on the other hand:—(<emph>a</emph>) The indignation comes late, seeing that Creon has already used the same taunt four times (1036, 1047, 1055, 1059); not, indeed, in so directly personal a form, yet still openly enough. (<emph>b</emph>) Though the seer is angered (1085), it is dramatically better to conceive him as speaking here with a stern calmness. (<emph>c</emph>) It would be correct to say (e.g.) <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφασμαι λέγων, τὸ σὸν μέρος</quote> (‘I have been represented as speaking..., so far as you could create such a belief’): but hardly, <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ τὸ σὸν μέρος</quote>, as merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ σοί</quote>.— On the whole, then, the first view is best. —Others, which may be rejected, are:— (1) ‘I think that I <emph>shall</emph> speak for your <emph>good.</emph>’ But, if we are thus to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ κέρδεσιν</quote>, and not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐπὶ κέρδεσιν</quote>, the verse must be interrogative. (2) ‘So far as you are concerned, I do not expect to speak for my own profit’; i.e., I shall receive no thanks from you. (3) ‘Do you really think that I shall find any satisfaction in speaking?’—i.e., it will be only pain for you, without advantage for me. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσθι_ἐμπ_1063">, rest assured that thou art not to trade (1037) on my resolve; i.e., to make profit out of it (from the Thebans) by persuading me to surrender it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1063"> (which might have been absent) adds emphasis by marking the point of view at which he is to place himself. In such phrases it is more often added to a partic. in the accus., the object of the imperat. verb: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 253"> <title>Ph.</title> 253</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότ’ ἴσθι μ’ ὧν ἀνιστορεῖς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 848"> <title>O. T.</title> 848</ref> n. But cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.91">Her. 1.91</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάσθω Κροῖσος ὡς ὕστερον...ἁλοὺς τῆς πεπρωμένης.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπιστάσθω Κροῖσος ὡς ὕστερον...ἁλοὺς τῆς
									πεπρωμένης.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρένα_1063">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 993">cp. 993</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τοι_1064">: 473 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μη_τελῶν_1064">, that thou art not to accomplish, i.e., live through: <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> is due to the imperat. <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτισθι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 78"> <title>O. C.</title> 78</ref> n.). The easy correction, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥλιον_τελεῖν_1064">, has been received by some recent edd. (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτισθι_1064"> then has the constr. with inf., as 473 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσθι ...πίπτειν</quote>). It may be right. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελῶν_1064">, if not a usual phrase, is a natural one; and it is more impressive here to say, ‘<emph>thou shalt not live through</emph> many days,’ than, ‘the sun shall not fulfil many days.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρόχους_1064"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">δρόμους</foreign>, ‘courses.’ The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροχους_1064">=‘runners,’ i.e., <quote xml:lang="grc">κύκλους</quote>, wheels. The authority for this Attic distinction goes back at least to the Augustan age: see Chandler § 332 n. 1 (2nd ed.), who cites Ammonius p. 137 <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχοὶ ὀξυτόνως καὶ τρόχοι βαρυτόνως διαφέρουσι παρὰ τοῖς Ἀττικοῖς. φησὶ Τρύφων</quote> (in the Augustan age) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δευτέρᾳ περὶ Ἀττικῆς προσῳδίας. τοὺς μὲν γὰρ περιφερεῖς τροχοὺς ὁμοίως ἡμῖν προφέρονται ὀξυτονοῦντες· τρόχους δὲ βαρυτόνως λέγουσι τοὺς δρόμους</quote>. This passage helps to explain why our MSS. all give <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχούς</quote> here. When Ammonius wrote (towards the end of the 4th cent. A.D. ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τρόχος</quote>, ‘course,’ was known only as an Atticism, while <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχός</quote>, ‘wheel,’ was a common word.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁμιλλητῆρας_1064">, racing, rapid: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 456">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 456</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γέροντι δεῦρ’ ἁμιλλᾶται ποδί</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.4.44">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.4.44</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥρμησαν ἁμιλλᾶσθαι ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον</quote> </cit>.—The Schol. explains, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἀλλήλους διαδεχομένους</quote>, ‘successive’; perh. taking the word to mean, ‘competitors,’ i.e., ‘vying in swiftness.’ But that does not warrant his version. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_οἷσι_1066"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐντὸς ὧν</foreign>, i.e., before they have elapsed: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 619"> <title>O. C.</title> 619</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἕνα_1066">, a strong fig. phrase, one whose life is nourished by thine own heart's blood,—the son begotten of thee. If the ref. were to the mother, <quote xml:lang="grc">σπλάγχνα</quote> could mean ‘womb’: cp. <ref>Kaibel <title>Epigr.</title> 691</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζωὴ δὲ πλείων μητρὸς ἐν σπλάγχνοις ἐμή</quote> (of a babe who died just after birth). So brothers and sisters are <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμόσπλαγχνοι</quote> (511). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέκυν_νεκρῶν_1067">: 596 n. The <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκροί</quote> are Polyneices and Antigone.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιδοὺς_ἔσει_1067">, fut. perf.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 816"> <title>O. C.</title> 816</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθ_ὧν_1068"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τούτων ὅτι</quote>, ‘because’: so <title>Plut.</title> 434. The phrase more often means ‘wherefore’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 1295"> <title>O. C.</title> 1295</ref>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 264"> <title>O. T.</title> 264</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_ἄνω_1068"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τινά</quote>), thou hast thrust to the grave (one) of the living. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote> after the partitive gen., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1322"> <title>El.</title> 1322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύω | τῶν ἔνδοθεν χωροῦντος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1069"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχήν_1069">, takes the latter with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἄνω_1069">, ‘a life belonging to the upper world.’ We could then read either (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτίμως...κατοικίσας</quote>, or (<emph>b</emph>) with Bergk, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτίμως τ’...κατοικίσας</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῴκισας</quote>. But I prefer the MS. reading, because (<emph>a</emph>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἄνω_1069_2"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἄνω τινά</quote> has a certain tone of solemnity and mystery which befits the utterance: (<emph>b</emph>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ψυχήν_1069"> is somewhat weak: (<emph>c</emph>) the words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχήν_κατῴκισας_1069">, both by rhythm and by diction, naturally form one clause, —paraphrasing and interpreting the darker utterance in v. 1068.—Schütz takes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθ_ὧν_1069"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τούτων οὕς</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἄνω</quote> as by attraction for <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἄνω</quote>: i.e., ‘on account of those persons whom, being alive, thou hast entombed.’ Kern, too, so takes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθ’ ὧν</quote>, but makes <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἄνω</quote> partitive (‘on account of those among the living whom’); and so, I think, it must be on any view. But the parallelism of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις μέν...ἔχεις δέ</quote> plainly requires that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθ’ ὧν</quote> should apply in the same sense to both clauses. Schütz, however, has to supply it with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις δέ</quote> in the changed sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τούτων</quote> (neut.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι</quote>.—For <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἄνω = οἱ ἐν φάει</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 890">cp. 890</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1348"> <title>Ph.</title> 1348</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ στυγνὸς αἰών, τί μ’ ἔτι δῆτ’ ἔχεις ἄνω | βλέποντα, κοὐκ ἀφῆκας εἰς Ἅιδου μολεῖν</quote>;—Some take </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἄνω_1069_3"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἄνω θεῶν</quote>: ‘one belonging to the gods above.’ This is too forced.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτίμως_1069">, ruthlessly: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 428"> <title>O. C.</title> 428</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1181"> <title>El.</title> 1181</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_δέ_1070"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατέχεις δέ</foreign>. Since in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις μέν...ἔχεις δέ</quote> the rhetorical effect depends simply on the repetition (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαναφορά</quote>), the change of sense is immaterial. —</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_θεῶν_1070">, possess. gen. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέκυν_1070">, a corpse belonging to them. For <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτωθεν = κάτω</quote>, 521 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄμοιρον_1070">, without its due <quote xml:lang="grc">μοῖρα</quote> of burial rites: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 13"> <title>Ai.</title> 13</ref>-7 <quote xml:lang="grc">νεκρὸν ταφῆς | ἄμοιρον</quote>. Others take <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκτέριστον_1070"> (1207), without offerings at the grave, <quote xml:lang="grc">κτερίσματα</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 1410"> <title>O. C.</title> 1410</ref>): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 204">cp. 204</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνόσιον_1070">, ‘unhallowed,’ sums up the state of the dead who has received no rites: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 545">cp. 545</ref> n. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. Ham 1.5">Shaksp. <title>Haml.</title> 1. 5. 77</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">‘Unhousel'd, disappointed, unanel'd’</quote> </cit> [without sacrament—unprepared for death—without extreme unction]. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_1072">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν νεκρῶν</quote>, suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυν</quote>. Others make it neut., ‘such acts as these.’ It cannot refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ κάτωθεν θεοί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιάζονται_1072">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἄνω θεοί</quote>: because it was an offence against the pure <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐράνιοι θεοί</quote> to keep a <quote xml:lang="grc">μίασμα</quote> in their presence. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1425"> <title>O. T.</title> 1425</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν γοῦν πάντα βόσκουσαν φλόγα | αἰδεῖσθ’ ἄνακτος Ἡλίου</quote> </cit>, and see n. there on 1427. The subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">βιάζονται</quote> might, indeed, be <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ κάτωθεν θεοί</quote>, for Greek idiom is often bold in such transitions: but the verb suits a positive better than a negative wrong. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν γοῦν πάντα βόσκουσαν φλόγα | αἰδεῖσθ’
									ἄνακτος Ἡλίου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_1074">, neut., causal gen.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 931">cp. 931</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωβητῆρες_1074">, though the subject is fem.: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 850"> <title>El.</title> 850</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴστωρ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 111">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χερὶ πράκτορι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 664"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 664</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχη...σωτήρ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 1040"> <title>Suppl.</title> 1040</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλκτορι Πειθοῖ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑστεροφθόροι_1074">, destroying after (though not, here, <emph>long</emph> after) the crime. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 58">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 58</ref> (Zeus) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑστερόποινον | πέμπει παραβᾶσιν Ἐρινύν</quote>. <title>Anthol.</title> 12. 229 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑστερόπουν ἁζόμενοι Νέμεσιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λοχῶσιν_1075">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 490"> <title>El.</title> 490</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ δεινοῖς κρυπτομένα λόχοις χαλκόπους Ἐρινύς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἁ δεινοῖς κρυπτομένα λόχοις χαλκόπους
									Ἐρινύς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἅιδου_θεῶν_1075">, possess. gen.; the Erinyes are their ministers, avenging their wrongs: so oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρός, μητρός, Ἐρινύες</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. El. 112"> <title>El.</title> 112</ref> the Erinyes are <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμναὶ...θεῶν παῖδες.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ληφθῆναι_1075">, inf. of result: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 64">cp. 64</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούειν</quote>. The omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> is somewhat bold, since the subject of the inf. is not that of <quote xml:lang="grc">λοχῶσιν</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 385"> <title>O. C.</title> 385</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ θεοὺς | ὤραν τιν’ ἕξειν ὥστε σωθῆναί ποτε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐμοῦ θεοὺς | ὤραν τιν’ ἕξειν ὥστε σωθῆναί
									ποτε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηργ_1077">, prop., overlaid with silver (<ref n="Hdt. 1.98">Her. 1.98</ref>); hence, fig., bribed. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 11">Pind. <title>P.</title> 11. 41</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μισθοῖο συνέθευ παρέχειν | φωνὰν ὑπάργυρον</quote> (a word prop. said of a gilded surface, with silver below). —</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_τριβή_1077"> =a time for which thou wilt not have long to wait. Some, less naturally, make these words a parenthesis with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσται</quote> understood, and supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> as subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανεῖ_1077">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 156">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 156</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θιάσους εὐδαίμονας | ἀνδρῶν γυναικῶν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεις_1080"> are the cities which had furnished contingents to the Argive expedition against Thebes. These cities are stirred with passionate hatred against Creon by the tidings that burial has been refused to their fallen warriors. There is no <emph>direct</emph> allusion to the war of the Epigoni,—the expedition which the sons of the fallen chiefs led against Thebes, and in which they destroyed it. Bergk's <quote xml:lang="grc">συνταράξονται</quote> might suggest such an allusion; but the pres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνταράσσονται_1080"> is right. The reference is to the feelings which <emph>now</emph> agitate the cities. Those feelings are one day to produce the new war. Here the prophet notes them only as signs of a still distant storm. Having foretold a domestic sorrow for the father, he now foreshadows a public danger for the king.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσων_1081"> (fem.) <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαράγματα</quote>, mangled bodies <emph>belonging</emph> to them, as being the corpses of their citizens. The possessive gen. in this sense is quite justifiable, since <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαράγματα = σώματα ἐσπαραγμένα</quote>, just as <quote xml:lang="grc">πτώματα = σώματα πεπτωκότα</quote>. (It would be possible, but harsh, to make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσων_1081_2"> masc., as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ τοσούτων</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title> 263</ref> n.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθήγνισαν_1081">=‘hallowed’ them, in the sense of, ‘gave burial rites to them’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 40">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ πυρὶ καθήγνισται δέμας</quote> </cit> (has had the funeral rite of fire): <title>Suppl.</title> 1211 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ αὐτῶν σώμαθ’ ἡγνίσθη πυρί</quote>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθήγισαν_1081"> reaches the same meaning (‘buried’) by a different channel. <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαγίζω</quote> was properly ‘to devote’ or ‘dedicate’: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.86">Her. 1.86</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκροθίνια ...καταγιεῖν θεῶν ὅτεῳ δή</quote> </cit>. Then, fig., to devote to the gods below by the funeral fire; <title>Anton.</title> 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ...σῶμα τοῦ Καίσαρος ἐν ἀγορᾷ καθαγίσαι</quote> (‘solemnly burn’). Either </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθήγνισαν_1081_2"> or </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθήγισαν_1081_2">, then, is admissible. But (apart from L's support) <quote xml:lang="grc">καθήγνισαν</quote> seems preferable on two grounds: (<emph>a</emph>) its primary sense lends force to the grim irony: (<emph>b</emph>) the funereal sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαγίζω</quote> has only post-classical evidence.—Hesychius (<quote xml:lang="grc">καθαγίσω</quote>) says that Soph. used <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαγίζω</quote>, not in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">καθιερόω</quote>, but in that of <quote xml:lang="grc">μιαίνω</quote>:—a statement perh. founded on a misunderstanding</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστιοῦχον_πόλιν_1083">, the city containing the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστίαι</quote> of those on whose flesh the bird has fed. The sing. is used, although several <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεις</quote> are concerned, since the case of one city is the case of all. For the adj., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 510">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 510</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκουσιν ἐκφυγόντες, οὐ πολλοί τινες, | ἐφ’ ἑστιοῦχον γαῖαν</quote> </cit>, ‘the land of their homes.’ <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 283">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 283</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστιοῦχον αὐλάν</quote> </cit>, the abode that contains his hearth. Here, the word serves to suggest a pollution of hearth and altar (1016). Pollution, in a ceremonial sense, could be brought by the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀσμή</quote>, even without an actual transport of carrion. And it is only the birds that are said to carry the taint.—See Appendix on 1080 ff. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἥκουσιν ἐκφυγόντες, οὐ πολλοί τινες, | ἐφ’
									ἑστιοῦχον γαῖαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σου_1084">, ‘at thee,’ with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφῆκα_1084">: 1033 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμῷ_1084">, modal dat.: 620 n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καρδίας_τοξεύματα_1084">, heart-arrows, i.e., arrows for thy heart. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 235">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 235</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδίας δηκτήρια</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 1360"> <title>Med.</title> 1360</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς σῆς γάρ, ὡς χρῆν, καρδίας ἀνθηψάμην</quote>.—Not, arrows <emph>from</emph> my (angry) heart, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμματος... τόξευμα</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. Supp. 1004">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 1004</ref>).—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τῶν</emph> = ὧν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 747"> <title>O. C.</title> 747</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_παῖ_1087">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 444"> <title>O. T.</title> 444</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπειμι τοίνυν· καὶ σύ, παῖ, κόμιζέ με</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἄπειμι τοίνυν· καὶ σύ, παῖ, κόμιζέ με

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφειν_1089">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 660">cp. 660</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡσυχωτέραν_1089">, the MS. reading, has been prudently retained by most of the recent edd. In <ref n="Plat. Charm. 160">Plat. <title>Charm.</title> 160</ref>A the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἡσυχώτατος</quote>, though two lines before they give <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἡσυχαίτατα</quote>. A grammarian in Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> 98. 19 quotes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσυχώτερον</quote>. In <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 223">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 223</ref> the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσυχαιτέραν</quote>, and in <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phileb. 24C">Plat. <title>Phileb.</title> 24C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσυχαιτέρου</quote> </cit>. It is true that our MSS. have no great weight on such a point, and that, if the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ω_1089"> form had been the current one in later Greek, it would have been likely to oust an older form in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_1089">. But we see that sometimes, at least, the MSS. could preserve the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_1089_2"> and the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ω_1089_2"> forms side by side. It seems safer, then, to suppose that the normal </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ω_1089_3"> form and the irregular </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_1089_3"> form were both in Attic use, than to assume that the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_1089_4"> form alone was tolerated. The dictum of Thomas Magister, (quoted by Dindorf,) p. 426 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσυχαίτερον· οὐχ ἡσυχώτερον</quote>, is indecisive without more evidence than we possess. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἀμείνω_1090"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφειν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ νῦν φέρει</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 18.419"> <title>Il.</title> 18.419</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇς ἐν μὲν νόος ἐστὶ μετὰ φρεσίν</quote> </cit>, there is understanding in their breasts: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 22.475">22. 475</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς φρένα θυμὸς ἀγέρθη</quote> </cit>, the soul returned to her breast. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">φρήν</quote> being thus associated with the physical seat of thought and feeling, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ νοῦς τῶν φρενῶν</quote> was a possible phrase. So trag. adesp. fr. 240 (when divine anger visits a man) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαφαιρεῖται φρενῶν | τὸν νοῦν τὸν ἐσθλόν</quote>. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 176">Cp. 176</ref> n. ad fin.)</gloss>

data.quote: τῇς ἐν μὲν νόος ἐστὶ μετὰ φρεσίν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_1090">: <ref target="cl705">705 n</ref>.—If we took </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_φρενῶν_1090"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμείνω_1090">, then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_1090"> must be changed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_1090">, with Brunck. In so compact a clause, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote> could not be an irregular substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote>. Nor could <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ νῦν φέρει</quote> be an epexegesis: ‘better than his (present) mind,—(that is, better) than he now bears it.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ὅτου_1092">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 12">cp. 12</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λευκὴν_μελαίνης_1092">. The words could mean either: (1) ‘since this hair which clothes my head, once dark, has been white’: or (2) ‘since this hair,—once dark, now white,—has clothed my head,’ i.e., from infancy. The first is the sense intended here. There is a certain looseness of expression, since the thought is, ‘though I am old, I can recall no such case’; whereas the period actually described might be a comparatively short one. So we can say, ‘he has grown grey in the service of his country,’ meaning, ‘he has served it all his life.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιβάλλομαι_1092">: cp. Rhianus (the elegiac poet of Crete, <date when="-0225">c. 225 B.C.</date>) <title>Anthol. P.</title> 12. 93 <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρετε, καλοὶ παῖδες, ἐς ἀκμαίην δὲ μόλοιτε | ἥβην, καὶ λευκὴν ἀμφιέσαισθε κόμην</quote>. For the 1st pers. sing. following <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάμεσθα</quote>, see <ref target="cl734">734 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_1094">. We might have had the <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> of oratio obliqua with <quote xml:lang="grc">λακεῖν, = ὅτι οὐκ ἔλακε</quote>. But here we have <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, as after <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστεύω</quote> and like verbs. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1455"> <title>O. T.</title> 1455</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα, μήτε μ’ ἂν νόσον | μήτ’ ἄλλο πέρσαι μηδέν</quote> </cit> (n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 656"> <title>O. C.</title> 656</ref> n., 797 n. In such cases <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> seems to add a certain emphasis to the statement of fact (like saying, ‘I <emph>protest</emph> that I know no instance’).</gloss>

data.quote:
οἶδα, μήτε μ’ ἂν νόσον | μήτ’ ἄλλο πέρσαι
									μηδέν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λακεῖν_1094">, infin. (instead of the more usual partic.) after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάμεσθα</quote>: 293 n. This verb is esp. used of prophecy: cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 822"> <title>Tr.</title> 822</ref> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔπος τὸ θεοπρόπον</quote> is subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλακεν</quote>): <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1426">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1426</ref> (of Clytaemnestra) <quote xml:lang="grc">περίφρονα δ’ ἔλακες</quote>. The ref. is esp. to the seer's denunciation of Oedipus, and his command regarding Megareus (1303 n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγνωκα_1095">, I have noted it=I know it well; more emphatic than <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 553"> <title>O. C.</title> 553</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_δέ_1096">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τε...τε</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 285"> <title>Tr.</title> 285</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα γὰρ πόσις τε σὸς ἐφεῖτ’, ἐγὼ δὲ πιστὸς ὢν κείνῳ τελῶ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 333"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 333</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς σύ θ’ οἷ θέλεις | σπεύδῃς, ἐγὼ δὲ τἄνδον ἐξαρκῆ τιθῶ</quote> </cit>. See also <ref n="Soph. OC 367"> <title>O. C.</title> 367</ref> n., 442 n.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1312"> <title>Ph.</title> 1312</ref> f. Here, </gloss>

data.quote:
ταῦτα γὰρ πόσις τε σὸς ἐφεῖτ’, ἐγὼ δὲ πιστὸς
									ὢν κείνῳ τελῶ

ὡς σύ θ’ οἷ θέλεις | σπεύδῃς, ἐγὼ δὲ τἄνδον
									ἐξαρκῆ τιθῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_1096"> is accentless in L, and the first hand has written </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τ_1096"> above; but, if the genuine reading had been </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_τε_1096">, the change to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_δέ_1096_2"> was not likely to occur; and the antithesis makes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_1096_2"> very natural. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 206">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 206</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρεῖα μὲν κὴρ τὸ μὴ πιθέσθαι, | βαρεῖα δ’, εἰ τέκνον δατξω</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
βαρεῖα μὲν κὴρ τὸ μὴ πιθέσθαι, | βαρεῖα δ’, εἰ
									τέκνον δατξω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτῃ_πατάξαι_1097"> (170 n.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμὸν_1097">, to smite my proud spirit with a curse. </gloss>
