<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντιστάντα_1097"> implies that he is stationary: the image is not, then, like that in 854 (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς Δίκας βάθρον | προσέπεσες</quote>). Rather the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote> is to be conceived as sweeping down on him, like the torrent which destroys trees that resist it (712).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πάρα_1097"> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστιν</quote>), it is open to me, as the dreadful alternative; lit., as a thing in the region of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δεινόν</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δεινῷ</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 384"> <title>El.</title> 384</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν γὰρ ἐν καλῷ φρονεῖν</quote> </cit> ('tis opportune). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 971">Eur. <title>Her.</title> 971</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκοῦν ἔτ’ ἐστὶν ἐν καλῷ δοῦναι δίκην</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Eur. IA 969"> <title>I.A.</title> 969</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν εὐμαρεῖ τε</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν</quote>: <title>Helen.</title> 1277 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν εὐσεβεῖ γοῦν νόμιμα μὴ κλέπτειν νεκρῶν</quote> ('tis a matter of piety). Here, the only peculiarity arises from the fusion of two propositions, viz. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστιν</quote>, and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δεινῷ ἐστιν</quote>. The phrase would have been clearer if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote> had been added to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δεινῷ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 471">cp. 471</ref> n. It may be noticed that elsewhere also Soph. uses <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">παρόν</quote> of an <emph>evil</emph> lot: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 432"> <title>Ai.</title> 432</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν γὰρ πάρεστι καὶ δὶς αἰάζειν ἐμοί</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 959"> <title>El.</title> 959</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ πάρεστι μὲν στένειν... | πάρεστι δ’ ἀλγεῖν</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 283"> <title>Ph.</title> 283</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὕρισκον οὐδὲν πλὴν ἀνιᾶσθαι παρόν</quote>. This is a point in favour of the traditional </gloss>

data.quote: οὐκοῦν ἔτ’ ἐστὶν ἐν καλῷ δοῦναι δίκην
νῦν γὰρ πάρεστι καὶ δὶς αἰάζειν ἐμοί

ᾗ πάρεστι μὲν στένειν... | πάρεστι δ’
									ἀλγεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρα_1097">.—Seyffert's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἓν_πέρα_1097"> would be excellent, were it not for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕν</quote>, which cannot be justified by the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς</quote> with superlatives (<ref n="Soph. OC 563"> <title>O. C.</title> 563</ref> n.). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 45.73">[Dem. ] or. 45 § 73</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν, ὦ γῆ καὶ θεοί, καὶ πέρα δεινοῦ</quote> </cit>. Wecklein conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινῷ</quote>: ‘by resisting, it is possible that, <emph>in addition to</emph> the difficulty (of resistance), I may incur calamity.’ But, apart from the risk of calamity, there was nothing in resistance that he could call <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν</quote>. There is no likelihood in conjectures which displace <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμόν</quote>, such as Nauck's (see cr. n.). </gloss>

data.quote: δεινόν, ὦ γῆ καὶ θεοί, καὶ πέρα δεινοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_1098">, and the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρέον_1098"> of later MSS., is not an easy one to decide. If <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> is an error, then it must be explained by the scribe's eye having wandered to v. 1094. But it has not been noticed (I think) that the argument from v. 1094 is two-edged. There, the scribe of L wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>, which was afterwards corrected to <quote xml:lang="grc">λακεῖν</quote>, either by his own hand or by another. It might be held, then, that he wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>, by an error of the eye, in 1094, because his archetype had <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> in 1098. The epexegetic construction of the inf. (= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε λαβεῖν αὐτήν</quote>, see examples on 489 f.) may have been a stumblingblock, leading transcribers to think it a redundant gloss; when <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέον</quote> would have been the obvious resource. Everything considered, I prefer to retain <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατώρυχος_1100">: 774 n.<emph>—<quote xml:lang="grc">κτίσον</quote> </emph> is here more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίησον</quote>, as it implies observance of solemn rites: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1201">cp. 1201</ref> ff.: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 483">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 483</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω γὰρ ἄν σοι δαῖτες ἔννομοι βροτῶν | κτιζοίατ</quote> </cit>'. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὕτω γὰρ ἄν σοι δαῖτες ἔννομοι βροτῶν |
									κτιζοίατ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖ_1102">: ‘and does it seem good (to you) that I should yield?’ The dat. can be understood, as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 526"> <title>Ph.</title> 526</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’, εἰ δοκεῖ, πλέωμεν</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 645"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 645</ref>. This correction is confirmed by <ref n="Aesch. Seven 650">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 650</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ αὐτὸς ἤδη γνῶθι, τίνα πέμπειν δοκεῖ</quote>, where L has <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ</quote> with an accent erased over the <quote xml:lang="grc">ο</quote>,—showing that the use of the impers. verb without a dat., seeming strange, had suggested <quote xml:lang="grc">δόκει</quote> (imperat.). There, some of the later MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖς</quote>. Here, L shares the error of the rest, and has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖς_1102">—generated, doubtless, from <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ</quote> by the same misapprehension as in <emph>Aesch. l. c.</emph> The decisive objection to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖς_1102_2"> here is that it could mean only, ‘art thou minded to yield?’ (<cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 16">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν δ’ ἀείδειν ἢ μινύρεσθαι δοκῶ</quote> </cit>); not, ‘dost thou think it right that I should yield?’</gloss>

data.quote: ὅταν δ’ ἀείδειν ἢ μινύρεσθαι δοκῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεικαθεῖν_1102">: for the form, see on <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συντέμνουσι_κ_1103">, cut them (i.e., their careers) short, ‘cut them off.’ The compressed phrase, though not strictly correct, is natural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποδώκεις_1103">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.505"> <title>Il.</title> 9.505</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ Ἄτη σθεναρή τε καὶ ἀρτίπος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 837"> <title>Ai.</title> 837</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐρινῦς τανύποδας</quote> </cit> (who are <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖαι</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 843"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 843</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 418"> <title>O. T.</title> 418</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόπους ἀρά.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ἡ δ’ Ἄτη σθεναρή τε καὶ ἀρτίπος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλάβαι_1103">, ‘harms,’ ‘mischiefs,’ with ref. to the primary sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάπτω</quote>, to disable, or stop: <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.39"> <title>Il.</title> 6.39</ref> (horses) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄζῳ ἐνί βλαφθέντε μυρικίνῳ</quote>, ‘caught in’ a tamarisk branch: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 120">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 120</ref> (a hare) <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαβέντα λοισθίων δρόμων</quote>, ‘stopped’ from running further. The <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάβαι θεῶν</quote> cannot, however, be properly regarded as personified beings; and therefore we should not write <quote xml:lang="grc">Βλάβαι</quote>. In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 491">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 491</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ κρατήσει δίκα τε καὶ βλάβα | τοῦδε μητροκτόνου</quote> </cit>, where some write <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκα—Βλάβα</quote>, the sense is, ‘if the cause and the wrong (=the wrongful cause) of Orestes shall prevail.’</gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ κρατήσει δίκα τε καὶ βλάβα | τοῦδε
									μητροκτόνου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακόφρονας_1103">: for the <quote xml:lang="grc">ο—</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">φρ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 336">cp. 336</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόλις_μέν_1105"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξίστ</quote>.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξίστ. δέ</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1421">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1421</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόλις μέν, ἐξέτεινε δ’ εἰς ἧπαρ ξίφος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1363">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1363</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀγὼ μόλις μέν, ἀλλ’ ὅμως ἠνεσχόμην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: μόλις μέν, ἐξέτεινε δ’ εἰς ἧπαρ ξίφος
κἀγὼ μόλις μέν, ἀλλ’ ὅμως ἠνεσχόμην.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξίσταμαι_καρδίας_1105">, resign my cherished resolve: <title>Phaedr.</title> 249 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξιστάμενος...τῶν ἀνθρωπίνων σπουδασμάτων, καὶ πρὸς τῷ θείῳ γιγνόμενος</quote>. This use of <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδία</quote> was suggested by the similar use of <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμός</quote>, with which Homer associates it as the seat of desire or passion (<cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.784"> <title>Il.</title> 13.784</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ἄρχ’, ὅππη σε κραδίη θυμός τε κελεύει</quote> </cit>): thus <quote xml:lang="grc">πληροῦσα θυμόν</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1328">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1328</ref>)= <quote xml:lang="grc">πληροῦσα ἐπιθυμίαν.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν δ’ ἄρχ’, ὅππη σε κραδίη θυμός τε
									κελεύει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρᾶν_1105">, acc. of inner object, defining the concession: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1252"> <title>Ph.</title> 1252</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδέ τοι σῇ χειρὶ πείθομαι τὸ δρᾶν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσμαχητέον_1105">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 492"> <title>Tr.</title> 492</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖσι δυσμαχοῦντες</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_τρέπε_1107"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοις ἐπίτρεπε</foreign>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 434">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 434</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ κἀπ’ ἐμοὶ τρέποιτ’ ἂν αἰτίας τέλος</quote> </cit>; (‘would ye commit the decision of the charge to me’?) This is ‘tmesis’ in the proper sense,—where the prep. determines the special sense of the verb: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 8.108"> <title>Il.</title> 8.108</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕς ποτ’ ἀπ’ Αἰνείαν ἑλόμην = ἀφειλόμην</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: ἦ κἀπ’ ἐμοὶ τρέποιτ’ ἂν αἰτίας τέλος

οὕς ποτ’ ἀπ’ Αἰνείαν ἑλόμην =
									ἀφειλόμην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔχω_1108">, i.e., forthwith: 1235: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 819"> <title>Ph.</title> 819</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέξαι θανάσιμόν μ’ ὅπως ἔχω.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχοιμ_ἄν_1108">, optat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> expressing a fixed resolve: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 343"> <title>O. T.</title> 343</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν πέρα φράσαιμι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 45"> <title>O. C.</title> 45</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ...ἂν ἐξέλθοιμ’ ἔτι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴτ_ἴτ_1108">. The rhythm given by the tribrach suits this agitated utterance. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶ_1108"> would be no improvement (see cr. n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵ_ἀπόντες_1109">, one and all. This was doubtless a familiar phrase: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 40">cp. 40</ref> n. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 305"> <title>El.</title> 305</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς οὔσας τέ μοι | καὶ τὰς ἀπούσας ἐλπίδας διέφθορεν</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Pl. Trin. 2.2">Plaut. <title>Trin.</title> 360</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">comedit quod fuit quod non fuit.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰς οὔσας τέ μοι | καὶ τὰς ἀπούσας ἐλπίδας
									διέφθορεν
comedit quod fuit quod non fuit.

lemma: οἵ τ’ ὄντες οἵ τ’ ἀπόντες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξίνας_1109">. In <ref n="Xen. Anab. 1.5.12">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 1.5.12</ref> the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξίνη</quote> is used by one who is <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύλα σχίζων</quote>. Here it has usually been supposed that the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξῖναι</quote> were to cut wood for the burning of the corpse. But no regular <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρά</quote> was made; the remains of the corpse were burned with <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοσπάδες θαλλοί</quote>, branches freshly <emph>plucked</emph> from the trees in the plain (1201). On the other hand, some implement was needed to raise the <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβος ὀρθόκρανος</quote> of earth (1203). It seems, then, as if Soph. referred to some kind of axe which could serve like the <quote xml:lang="grc">γενῄς</quote> of v. 249 (n.). No tool was used to break open Antigone's tomb; the stones were dragged away (1216). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπόψιον_1110">, pass., looked-upon, beheld, from here: hence= <quote xml:lang="grc">φανερόν</quote> (schol.), ‘in view.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1600"> <title>O. C.</title> 1600</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς προσόψιον | πάγον</quote> </cit>, the hill which was in view. —As Creon speaks, he points with his hand in a direction to the left of the spectators. The region meant is the furthest and highest part of the Theban plain (1197), where the body of Polyneices still lay. In the <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι</quote> adjacent to it was the rocky tomb of Antigone (774 n.).—Hermann assumed the loss of some vv. after 1110, in which Creon described the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπόψιος τόπος</quote>,—explaining that he would first bury Polyneices, and then free Antigone. But what need was there for this, when he was himself to accompany his servants? Besides, his men, like all the other Thebans, might be supposed to know the place meant; and the Chorus had already said what was to be done there. Equally baseless is Bergk's theory that vv. 1111 —1114 are an interpolation, designed to fill a gap in the original text. See the notes on them. Dindorf agrees with Bergk only so far as to suspect vv. 1111, 1112. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_δ_1111">. The sense is not: ‘do <emph>you</emph> go and bury Polyneices, while <title>I</title> release Antigone.’ Creon takes part in both acts (1196 ff.). But at this moment his foremost thought is of saving Antigone. If she dies, his son must die (1066). Therefore, while he glances at the burial-rites by telling his men to bring axes, he describes his own part by his <emph>most urgent</emph> task,—the release.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστράφη_1111">, prop., ‘turned round’; as a person faces about, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 187">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 187</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πολλὰ θάλαμον ἐξιοῦσ’ ἐπεστράφη.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδ_1111">, this way, in this direction (<ref n="Soph. OC 1547"> <title>O. C.</title> 1547</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτός_ἔδησα_1112"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ αὐτὸς ἔδησα, οὕτω καὶ αὐτὸς παρὼν ἐκλύσομαι</foreign>. The co-ordination (parataxis) of clauses by <quote xml:lang="grc">τε...καί</quote>, as elsewhere by <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν... δέ</quote>, is peculiarly Greek. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 419"> <title>O. T.</title> 419</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέποντα νῦν μὲν ὄρθ’, ἔπειτα δὲ σκότον</quote> </cit> (=dark then, <emph>though</emph> now thou hast sight): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 853"> <title>O. C.</title> 853</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε νῦν καλὰ | δρᾷς, οὔτε πρόσθεν εἰργάσω</quote> </cit> (=thou art not doing well now, <emph>as</emph> neither didst thou formerly): <ref n="Soph. OC 1202"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1202</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ καλὸν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν μὲν εὖ | πάσχειν, παθόντα δ’ οὐκ ἐπίστασθαι τίνειν</quote> (<emph>while</emph> receiving benefits, to be incapable of requiting them). Here, the rhetorical effect of the idiom is to place the two acts in bolder contrast. The middle <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλύομαι</quote> and the active <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλύω</quote> (<ref n="Aesch. PB 326">Aesch. <title>PV</title> 326</ref>) are equivalent in poetry. They do not differ as <quote xml:lang="grc">λύω</quote> (said of the captor) from <quote xml:lang="grc">λύομαι</quote> (of the ransomer).—Nauck and others take the words figuratively; ‘As I have made the tangle, I will unravel it’ (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 40">cp. 40</ref> n.). This is surely wrong. See on v. 1111. </gloss>

data.quote:
βλέποντα νῦν μὲν ὄρθ’, ἔπειτα δὲ
									σκότον

οὔτε νῦν καλὰ | δρᾷς, οὔτε πρόσθεν
									εἰργάσω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδοικα_ᾖ_1113">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 747"> <title>O. T.</title> 747</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινῶς ἀθυμῶ μὴ βλέπων ὁ μάντις ᾖ</quote> </cit>. In both these places, ‘I fear lest...’ means ‘I shrewdly suspect that...,’ and <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα μή</quote> might therefore have been followed by pres. indic., which expresses a fear that something is now going on (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 278">cp. 278</ref> n. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 493">Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 493</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα...μὴ πληγῶν δέει</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote: δεινῶς ἀθυμῶ μὴ βλέπων ὁ μάντις ᾖ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_νόμους_1113">, the laws established by the gods,—the <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν νόμιμα</quote> of 454 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῴζοντα_τελεῖν_1113"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">σῴζειν ἕως ἂν τελευτῄσης</foreign>: but the turn of phrase chosen unconsciously foreshadows Antigone's fate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυώνυμε_1115">, i.e., worshipped by various special titles in different places. The reference of the epithet to local rituals is well brought out by <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 15">Theocr. 15. 109</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" xml:lang="eng">(aphrodite)</note> πολυώνυμε καὶ πολύναε</quote> </cit>. Most of the greater deities are called <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυώνυμοι</quote> by the poets; but the word is peculiarly suitable to Dionysus, owing to the manner in which his cult was interwoven with other cults; thus in relation to Demeter he was <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴακχος</quote>; to the Muses, <quote xml:lang="grc">Μελπόμενος</quote>; to Hades, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζαγρεύς</quote>. Dionysus was distinctively <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυειδὴς καὶ πολύμορφος</quote> ( <ref n="Plut. Mor. 389">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 389</ref> c). Upwards of sixty titles given to him can be enumerated (see Preller, <title>Griech. Mythol.</title>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Καδμείας_1115">. We should not write <quote xml:lang="grc">Καδμεΐας</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">διλόφοιο</quote> in 1126, with Dindorf. Nor is it necessary to place <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφας</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγαλμα</quote>, with Nauck. See Metrical Analysis.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγαλμα_1115">, glory: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 207">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 207</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνον...δόμων ἄγαλμα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νύμφας_1115">, bride, young wife. Semele, daughter of Cadmus and Harmonia, was beloved by Zeus, and was ensnared by Hera into praying him that he would come to her in the same guise as to Hera. He came to her. therefore, armed with his thunderbolts, and amid lightning, which destroyed her. She was great with child, and Zeus saved her son, Dionysus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρυβρεμέτα_1115"> (for the <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 336">cp. 336</ref> n.) alludes to this story. <ref n="Ov. Met. 3. 298">Ov. <title>Met.</title> 3. 298</ref>(Jupiter, bound by his own oath, grants Semele's prayer): <quote xml:lang="lat">ergo maestissimus altum | Aethera conscendit, nutuque sequentia traxit | Nubila; quis nimbos immixtaque fulgura ventis | Addidit, et tonitrus, et inevitabile fulmen.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰταλίαν_1119">, may be supported by these considerations. (1) Southern Italy, the seat of so many Greek colonies, was preeminently associated with the cultivation of the vine; and Sophocles has himself used the name which expresses that fact: <title>Triptolemus</title> fr. 538 <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰνωτρία τε πᾶσα καὶ Τυρσηνικὸς | κόλπος Αιγυστική τε γῆ σε δέξεται</quote>. (2) The opening words of the ode, <quote xml:lang="grc">Καδμείας νύμφας ἄγαλμα</quote>, claim Thebes as the birth-place of Dionysus. Though Italy, then, is mentioned before Eleusis, Parnassus and Euboea, that precedence has not the effect of representing Italy as the head-quarters of the Dionysiac worship. Rather the mention of Italy just after Thebes serves to exalt the Theban god by marking the wide range of his power. And this reference to a distant country well suits the immediately following <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίνοις</quote>, expressing that Eleusis receives votaries from every part of the Greek world. (3) Athenian colonists founded Thurii, on the site of Sybaris, in 444—3 B.C.,—only two or three years before the probable date of this play. Thus, just at this time, the Athenian mind had been turned towards Southern Italy, and the allusion would strike a chord of sympathy in the audience. It may be worth remembering that the poet himself would naturally have felt a more than common interest in the new home of his friend Herodotus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰκαρίαν_1119">. This was the name of a deme in the N. E. of Attica, picturesquely situated in an upland valley bounded on the N. by the mountainchain (‘Aphorismo’) which shuts in the plain of Marathon, and on the s. by Pentelicus. The site—at a place called ‘Dionyso’—is proved by local inscriptions, found by members of the American School in 1888. The story was that, when Dionysus first entered Attica, he was received at Icaria by Icarius, whom he taught to make wine. Icaria was associated with the earliest celebrations of the rural Dionysia (thus the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσκωλιασμός</quote>, or dancing on greased wine-skins, was said to have been introduced by Icarius himself), and with the infancy of Attic drama in both kinds,—as it was also the birth-place of Thespis , and, at a later time, of the comic poet Magnes. Inscriptions and other remains show that, in the 5th century B.C., it was the seat of an active Dionysiac worship, with dramatic performances. These discoveries remind us that Sophocles might well have called Icaria <quote xml:lang="grc">κλυτάν</quote>. Prof. C. Merriam A. further points out that, in literature, the legend of Icaria is often associated with that of Eleusis (<title>American School at Athens: Seventh Annual Report</title>, 1887—88, p. 96). To Statius, <ref n="Stat. Theb. 12.619"> <title>Theb.</title> 12. 619</ref>(<title>Icarii Celeique domus</title>), may be added <ref n="Apollod. 3.14.7">Apollod. 3. 14. 7</ref>, Lucian <title>De Salt.</title> 39 f., Nonnus 27. 283 ff., etc. But these facts remain: (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰταλίαν_1119_2"> is also suitable, and is in all the MSS.: (2) it widens the range ascribed to the god's power: (3) a corruption of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰκαρίαν</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰταλίαν</quote> is not one to which the letters would readily lend themselves, and would have been the less likely to occur because Icaria was familiarly associated with Dionysus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκοίνοις_1120">, welcoming guests from every quarter to the Eleusinian Mysteries: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἶς πάντες συνάγονται διὰ τὰς πανηγύρεις</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 138"> <title>El.</title> 138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἀΐδα παγκοίνου λίμνας</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Pind. O. 6">Pind. <title>O.</title> 6. 63</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόλποις_1120">, recesses, i.e., the sheltered Thriasian plain, enclosed by hills,—Aegaleos on the E. , Cithaeron on the N., and the Kerāta range on N. W. and W. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 373">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 373</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοὺς εὐανθεῖς κόλπους λειμώνων</quote> </cit> (where, though the scene is in Hades, the allusion is to the Initiated visiting Eleusis). So <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 9">Pind. <title>O.</title> 9. 87</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Νεμέας... κατὰ κόλπον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 14"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 14. 23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόλποις παρ’ εὐδόξου Πίσας</quote> </cit>. This is better than to refer <quote xml:lang="grc">κόλποις</quote> to the Bay of Eleusis, whose shores are the <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπάδες ἀκταί</quote> of <ref n="Soph. OC 1049"> <title>O. C.</title> 1049</ref> (where see nn.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἐλευσινίας_1120">: on the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ι_1120">, see n. on this v. in Analysis. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph> μετρ.Δηοῦς</emph> = Δήμητρος</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="HH 2.47"> <title>Hom. h. Dem.</title> 47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότνια Δηώ</quote> </cit>. In this connection the proper name of the god was <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴακχος</quote> (1152), a young deity who was represented as the son of Cora (or of Demeter); cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 682"> <title>O. C.</title> 682</ref> n. Indeed, Arrian expressly distinguishes the Eleusinian Iacchus from the Theban Dionysus, <cit> <bibl n="Arr. An. 2.16.3"> <title>An.</title> 2. 16 § 3:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηναῖοι Διόνυσον τὸν Διὸς καὶ Κόρης σέβουσιν...καὶ ὁ Ἴακχος ὁ μυστικὸς</quote> </cit> (the chant of the initiated) <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ τῷ Διονύσῳ, οὐχὶ τῷ Θηβαίῳ, ἐπᾴδεται</quote>. But, as Welcker remarks (<title>Götterl.</title> 2, p. 543), Dionysus was the general name, often substituted for the special title. </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἀθηναῖοι Διόνυσον τὸν Διὸς καὶ Κόρης
									σέβουσιν...καὶ ὁ Ἴακχος ὁ μυστικὸς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Βακχεῦ_1122">. The omission of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_1122"> before this word, and the addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_1122"> before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρόπολιν_1122">, are conjectural (cr. n.). But they are certainly right; for the antistrophic words (1133) answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">Βακχεῦ—Θήβαν</quote>, are <quote xml:lang="grc">χλωρά τ’ ἀκτὰ | πολυστάφυλος πέμπει</quote>, which are unquestionably sound.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρόπολιν_1122_2">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 707"> <title>O. C.</title> 707</ref> n. Thebes is the ‘mother-city’ of the Bacchants, as being the city of Semele and the native place of Dionysus. It was the place at which the Dionysiac cult, coming from Asia Minor by way of Thrace, first established itself in Greece Proper. From Thebes the cult was propagated to Delphi, and associated with the worship of Apollo. See <ref n="Eur. Ba. 306">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 306</ref>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 210"> <title>O. T.</title> 210</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 510"> <title>Tr.</title> 510</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑγρὸν_ῥέεθρον_1123">, not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑγρῶν_ῥεέθρων_1123">, which was merely a conjecture of Triclinius. And the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote> with the genit. is not only unexampled (see n. on 966), but here, at least, wholly unintelligible. Metre requires, however, that a long syllable (answering to the first syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">Θηβαΐας</quote> in 1135) should precede <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγρίου</quote>. I obtain this by adding <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote> </emph> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥεῖθρον_1123">. The second syll. of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑγρόν_1123">, as the last of a verse, is common. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρά_1123"> with acc. is correct in ref. to a river, the notion being that his abode extends along its banks: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 184"> <title>El.</title> 184</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ παρὰ τὸν Ἀχέροντα θεὸς ἀνάσσων</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 4.3.1">Xen. <title>An.</title> 4. 3§ 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ πεδίου τοῦ παρὰ τὸν Κεντρίτην ποταμόν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 4.3.6"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστρατοπεδεύσαντο παρὰ τὸν ποταμόν</quote> </cit>. For the position of <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote> </emph>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 258"> <title>O. T.</title> 258</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. OC 33"> <title>O. C.</title> 33</ref> n. The sing. </gloss>

data.quote: ὁ παρὰ τὸν Ἀχέροντα θεὸς ἀνάσσων

τοῦ πεδίου τοῦ παρὰ τὸν Κεντρίτην
									ποταμόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥεῖθρον_1123_2"> is not less suitable than the plur.: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 497">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 497</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 790."> <title>P. V.</title> 790.</ref>For the epithet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑγρόν_1123_2">, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.458"> <title>Od.</title> 4.458</ref> (Proteus) <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνετο δ’ ὑγρὸν ὕδωρ</quote>, ‘running water.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰσμηνοῦ_1123">: see n. on 103 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_δρ_1125">, ‘over the seed of the dragon,’ i.e., on the ground where Cadmus sowed the dragon's teeth, from which the ancestors of the Cadmeans sprang. Hence the Thebans are called <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαρτοὶ ἄνδρες</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 1534 n.). The place where Cadmus sowed the teeth was shown on the s. side of Thebes, near the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἤλεκτραι πύλαι</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 9.10.1">Paus. 9.10.1</ref>).—Not, ‘(ruling) over the dragon's seed,’ as if <quote xml:lang="grc">σπορᾷ</quote> meant the Thebans. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διλόφου_πέτρας_1126">: i.e., two <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτραι</quote>, each with a <quote xml:lang="grc">λόφος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 146">cp. 146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικρατεῖς λόγχας</quote>, n.): two peaks, one of which stands on each side of a great recess in the steep cliffs above Delphi,—the cliffs called <quote xml:lang="grc">Φαιδριάδες</quote>, ‘gleaming,’ from their splendour in the morning sunshine (cp. <ref n="Eur. Ion 86">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 86</ref> ff.). These cliffs are about 2000 ft. above sealevel. The easternmost of the two peaks was called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑάμπεια</quote>: the westernmost, perh. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ναυπλία</quote>, but this is doubtful. Neither of them is the summit of Parnassus. That summit, called <quote xml:lang="grc">Αυκώρεια</quote>, rises high above them (about 8000 ft. above the sea). Misunderstanding <quote xml:lang="grc">δικόρυφος</quote>, the Roman poets gave a wrong impression by their ‘biceps Parnassus,’ which Lucan brings out when he says (<ref n="Luc. 5.72">5. 72</ref>) ‘Parnassus gemino petit aethera colle.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπὲρ_πέτρας_1126"> Soph. means the high ground above these two lower péaks, but below the summit of Parnassus. This high ground is what Eur. calls the <quote xml:lang="grc">δικόρυφος πλάξ</quote> (<ref n="Eur. Ba. 307">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 307</ref>). It consists of uplands stretching about 16 miles westward from the summit, and affording pasturage, interspersed with firs, and with pieces of arable land: wheat, oats, and barley are now grown there. These uplands were the scene of a Dionysiac <quote xml:lang="grc">τριετηρίς</quote>, a torch-festival, held every second year, at the end of winter, by women from the surrounding districts; even Attic women went to it (<ref n="Paus. 10.4.3">Paus. 10.4.3</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Luc. 5.73">Lucan 5. 73</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">Mons Phoebo Bromioque sacer, cui numine misto | Delphica Thebanae referunt trieteria Bacchae:</quote> and <ref n="Macr. 1.18.3">Macrobius <title>Sat.</title> 1. 18. 3</ref>. Here, however, the poet alludes, not to the human festival, but to supernatural revels.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιγνύς_1126"> is a smoky flame, such as a resinous pine-torch gives; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέροψ_1126"> finely expresses the lurid and fitful glare flashing through the smoke.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄπωπε_1126">, gnomic perf., ‘hath (oft) seen thee’: i.e., when the Nymphs brandish their torches, Dionysus is in the midst of them. It was the popular belief that dancing fires could be seen by night on Parnassus, when the god was holding his revels. <ref n="Eur. Ion 716">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 716</ref> (Parnassus) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵνα Βάκχιος ἀμφιπύρους ἀνέχων πεύκας | λαιψηρὰ πηδᾷ νυκτιπόλοις ἅμα σὺν Βάκχαις</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Ion 1125"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1125</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Ba. 306"> <title>Bacch.</title> 306</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 226"> <title>Phoen.</title> 226</ref>: <ref n="Eur. IT 1243"> <title>I. T.</title> 1243.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κωρύκιαι_Νύμφαι_1126">: Nymphs who haunt the <quote xml:lang="grc">Κωρύκιον ἄντρον</quote> and its neighbourhood. The name is from <quote xml:lang="grc">κώρυκος</quote>, ‘a wallet’ (and so, a hollow thing), and was given also to a cave on the Cilician coast. The Parnassian cave is near the top of a hill on the high table-land which lies at the base of the central cone,— about 7 miles N. E. of Delphi, and as many N.W. of Aráchova. E. Itis a large stalactite cavern, consisting of an outer chamber of some 200 ft. in length, and an inner one of about 100 ft.; the greatest breadth is about 200 ft., and the greatest height, <ref n="Eur. IT 40.">E. It 40.</ref>In 480 B.C., when the Persians were coming, many of the Delphians took refuge in it (<ref n="Hdt. 8.36">Her. 8.36</ref>). An old place of sacrifice can still be seen in it; and an inscription found there shows that it was sacred <quote xml:lang="grc">Πανὶ καὶ νύμφαις</quote> (<title>C. I. G.</title> 1728). <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 22">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβω δὲ νύμφας, ἔνθα Κωρυκὶς πέτρα | κοίλη, φίλορνις, δαιμόνων ἀναστροφή</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

data.quote:
σέβω δὲ νύμφας, ἔνθα Κωρυκὶς πέτρα | κοίλη,
									φίλορνις, δαιμόνων ἀναστροφή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχουσι_νύμφαι_1126"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφαι στείχουσι</quote>, satisfies the metre, and is far more probable than the change of <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχουσι</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">στίχουσι</quote>,—a form which, though noticed by Hesychius, is not known to have been used by any Attic writer of the classical age. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κασταλίας_νᾶμα_1130">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄπωπέ σε</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">Κασταλία</quote> is a stream which flows from a fissure in the high cliffs above Delphi. It issues near the easternmost of the two peaks (1126 ff., n.),—that which was called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑάμπεια</quote>: and bounds in cataracts, down a precipitous channel, to Delphi, where its water was used for all sacred purposes. Below Delphi it joins the Pleistus (<ref n="Aesch. Eum. 27">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 27</ref>). It is now called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅγιος Ἰωάννης</quote>. It is fitly mentioned here, since it rises on the edge of the highlands which form the scene of the revels. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_Νυσαίων_1131">. And from Nysa in Euboea thou comest to visit Thebes, with thy followers who cry <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐοῖ</quote>. The Euboean Nysa was imagined near Aegae (famous for its temple of Poseidon), on the W. coast of the island, opposite Anthedon. Cp. Stephanus Byz. and Hesych. <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">Νῦσα</quote>. That word prob. denoted a moist and fertile place: Welcker would refer it to a lost <quote xml:lang="grc">νύω</quote> from rt. <quote xml:lang="grc">νυ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">νέω</quote>): <title>Götterl.</title> 1. 439. ‘Dionysos’ was ‘the Zeus of Nysa’ (Preller <title>Myth.</title> 1. 549). Legend placed a Nysa in Thrace (<ref n="Hom. Il. 6.133"> <title>Il.</title> 6.133</ref>), Macedonia, Thessaly, Boeotia, Naxos, Caria, Lydia, Cilicia, Arabia, Aethiopia, Libya, India, and even at Parnassus. In a fragment of the <title>Thyestes</title> Sophocles beautifully describes a wondrous vine of Euboea, which puts forth leaves and bears fruit in the same day: fr. 235 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι γάρ τις ἐναλία | Εὐβοιὶς αἷα· τῇδε βάκχειος βότρυς | ἐπ’ ἦμαρ ἕρπει, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κισσήρεις_ὄχθαι_1132">, ivy-clad hills. Usually <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχθη</quote> =a river-bank, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχθος</quote> =a hill: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 726"> <title>Ph.</title> 726</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Σπερχειοῦ...παρ’ ὄχθας</quote>, and just afterwards (729) <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἴτας ὑπὲρ ὄχθων</quote>. But the distinction is not always observed; nor need we suspect our MSS.,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυστάφυλος_1133">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.537"> <title>Il.</title> 2.537</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Χαλκίδα τ’ Εἰρέτριάν τε πολυστάφυλόν θ’ Ἱστίαιαν</quote> </cit>. As Histiaea, afterwards Oreus, was on the N. coast, we may suppose that the Homeric epithet—here borrowed by Soph. —would have been at least equally applicable to other parts of the island.</gloss>

data.quote:
Χαλκίδα τ’ Εἰρέτριάν τε πολυστάφυλόν θ’
									Ἱστίαιαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέμπει_1133">, agreeing with nearest subject: 830 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμβρότων_ἐπέων_1134"> answer metrically to <quote xml:lang="grc">ναιετῶν παρ’ ὑγρόν</quote> (1123). There is no metrical reason, then, for altering the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμβρότων</quote>, with Turnebus, to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβρότων</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 158"> <title>O. T.</title> 158</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμβροτε Φάμα</quote> </cit>. There is no certain instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄβροτος</quote> in Tragedy. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 299</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμβροσίων ἐπέων</quote>, ‘divine strains.’ Here the epithet suggests the mystic power of the invocation.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐαζόντων_1134">: see on <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔιον</quote>, 964: ‘while divine chants resound with the cry <emph>evoe</emph>’; i.e., while the bacchants escort thee on thy way to Thebes with chants of praise. The conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑπετᾶν</quote> is neat, but needless. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_πασᾶν_1137">. There is no reason, metrical or other, for suspecting the MS. reading here. <ref target="cl1146">See on 1146</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάν_1137">, <foreign xml:lang="grc">Θήβαν</foreign>, implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">Θηβαΐας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 668">cp. 668</ref>, 1072.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1137"> (‘chosen out of’) <ref n="Soph. Ant. 164">cp. 164</ref>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερτάταν_1137">, proleptic; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 573">Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 573</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μείζω πόλιν αὔξει</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 565C">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 565C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον τρέφειν τε καὶ αὔξειν μέγαν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: τοῦτον τρέφειν τε καὶ αὔξειν μέγαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεραυνίᾳ_1139">, destroyed by the lightning of Zeus: <ref target="cl1115">see on 1115</ref> f. Works of art frequently associate Dionysus with his mother. Thus a fragmentary vase-painting shows him introducing her to Olympus (Welcker, <title>Alte Denkm.</title> III. pl. 13). On coins she is sometimes enthroned beside him. See Baumeister, <title>Denkm.</title> p. 443. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεται_νόσου_1140">, is captive to a violent (=a most grievous) plague. The <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote> is the divine anger which Thebes has incurred (1015). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ νόσου</quote> seems to be like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ εἰρήνης</quote> (‘in time of peace’<ref n="Hom. Il. 2.797"> <title>Il.</title> 2.797</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ σχολῆς</quote> ( Aeschin. or. 3 § 191): i.e., the prep. expresses the continuing presence of the <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote>, and the whole phrase strictly means, ‘the city is in distress, under the prevalence of a malady.’ (We could scarcely compare <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ξύλου</quote>, and suppose a metaphor from a rack or cross; cp. n. on 308 f.) Musgrave's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπό_1140"> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote>) is tame. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεται_1140"> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 780B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 780B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ πολλῆς ἀπορίας ἐχομένοις</quote> </cit>. There is only a verbal likeness to <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.11">Her. 6.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ξυροῦ γὰρ ἀκμῆς ἔχεται</quote> </cit> (are poised) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν τὰ πρήγματα</quote>.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιαιας_1140"> cp. n. on 1310 <quote xml:lang="grc">δείλαι^ος</quote>. The text is sound, without Boeckh's conjectural insertion of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμὰ_1140"> before </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλις_1140">: <ref target="cl1150">see on 1150</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάνδαμος_1140">: cp. on 7. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολεῖν_1143">: infin. for imperat.: cp. n. on 150 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθαρσίῳ_1143">: Dionysus was often invested with the attributes of the Purifier and Healer, as <quote xml:lang="grc">καθάρσιος, ἀλεξίκακος, ἀκέσιος</quote>, etc. Cp. Athen. 22 E, and 36—37. This was one aspect of the Delphian cult which associated him with Apollo. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλιτύν_1143"> ¯˘, as <ref n="Soph. Trach. 271"> <title>Tr.</title> 271</ref>: but ¯¯, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 5.470"> <title>Od.</title> 5.470</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς κλιτὺν ἀναβάς</quote> </cit>. Here, the last syll. of the verse being common, <quote xml:lang="grc">υ^ν</quote> stands for <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—ν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πορθμόν_1143">, the Euripus, between Euboea and Boeotia. At Chalcis (Egripo) it is only 40 yards across.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στονόεντα_1143"> refers to the noise of wind and water in the strait, with its constantly changing currents. Strabo 9. 403 <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ δὲ τῆς παλιρροίας τοῦ Εὐρίπου τοσοῦτον μονον εἰπεῖν ἱκανόν, ὅτι ἑπτάκις μεταβάλλειν φασὶ καθ’ ἡμέραν ἑκάστην καὶ νύκτα</quote>. Livy (28. 6) explains this by the squalls from the hills. Cp. Lucan's description of the Euripus, 5. 234, <title>Arctatus rapido fervet qua gurgite pontus.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πνειόντων_1146">, Brunck's simple correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεόντων</quote>, heals the metre. The MS. reading in 1137 f. is above all reasonable suspicion; and these verses now agree with them. It is a sin against all critical method to make violent changes in 1137 f.—as Dindorf (followed by Wecklein) does—in order to keep the short syllable of <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεόντων</quote> here. Hermann's argument against </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πνειόντων_1146_2">, which has deterred editors from admitting it, was strangely weal. He said that the first syllable of the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">πνείω</quote> never occurs with ictus (i.e., in arsis); and that, if the tragic poets had used that form, they would at least not have put an ictus on the <quote xml:lang="grc">πνει</quote>. But Homer repeatedly has <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοιῇ</quote> with ictus on the 1st syll. (as first word of the verse): and as <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοιή</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοή</quote>, so is <quote xml:lang="grc">πνείω</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέω</quote>. It is plain, therefore, that the Homeric absence of ictus from the <quote xml:lang="grc">πνει</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">πνείω</quote> was purely an accident of convenience in composition, —the phrases being <quote xml:lang="grc">μένεα πνείοντες, ζεφυρίη πνείουσα, ἡδὺ μάλα πνείουσαν, πνείει τε καὶ ἕρπει</quote>, etc. We need not dwell, then, on the fact which makes a second fallacy in the argument,—viz. that the ictus on <quote xml:lang="grc">πνει</quote> here is only equal to that which falls on <quote xml:lang="grc">οντ</quote> (see Anal.). Tragic lyrics teem with epic forms and phrases. <quote xml:lang="grc">ζάω</quote> was at least as familiar a word as <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέω</quote>. Yet twice in lyrics Soph. has ventured to use the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώω</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 157"> <title>El.</title> 157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵα Χρυσόθεμις ζώει</quote> </cit>: <title>O.C.</title> 1213 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώειν</quote>. Is it, then, reasonable to suppose that the poet, requiring ¯¯¯ instead of ˘¯¯, would have hesitated to use the familiar epic form <quote xml:lang="grc">πνειόντων</quote>? Nor is this all. In <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 621">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 621</ref> the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέονθ’ ἁ κυνόφρων ὕπνῳ</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">πνέονθ’ ἁ = σύμμετρον</quote> in the strophe (610), and the 1st syllable is (pace Hermanni) necessarily long, being that of a spondee (or trochee): Heath's correction, <quote xml:lang="grc">πνείονθ’</quote>, is therefore certain.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_νυχίων_1146"> (G. Wolff). The objection is that the contracted <quote xml:lang="grc">πύρπνους</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πύρπνουν</quote> do not justify <quote xml:lang="grc">πύρπνων</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρπνόων</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 478">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 478</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύρων πυρπνόων ἐπιστάτην</quote> </cit>. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὢ</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ</quote>) </gloss>
lemma: ἰὼ πύρπνων ἄστρων χοραγὲ καὶ νυχίων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῦρ_νυχίων_1146"> (Campbell). Here <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεόντων</quote> is a spondee. But such a synizesis seems very improbable. Remark, too, that L's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοραγὲ_ἄστρων_1146"> does not warrant us in supposing that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστρων</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_1146"> before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυχίων_1146"> is also varranted by instances in which <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> has been thrust into L. Here, the <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> would decidedly enfeeble the passage.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοράγ_ἄστρων_1146">. The sympathetic joy of the elemental powers—stars, moon, and sea—was especially associated with those night-festivals in which Dionysus bore his mystic character, as the young <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴακχος</quote> of the Eleusinian ritual, the companion of Demeter and Cora (n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 682"> <title>O. C.</title> 682</ref> ff.). See <ref n="Eur. Ion 1078">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1078</ref> ff., where the reference is to the Dionysus of the Great Mysteries at Eleusis: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε καὶ Διὸς ἀστερωπὸς | ἀνεχόρευσεν αἰθήρ, | χορεύει δὲ Σελάνα | καὶ πεντήκοντα κόραι | Νηρέος</quote>. Hence this crowning strain, which begins by greeting him as <quote xml:lang="grc">χοραγὸς ἄστρων</quote>, fitly closes with his Eleusinian name.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυχίων_φθεγμάτων_1146">, the songs, or wild cries, of his worshippers. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 485">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 485</ref> (Pentheus) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἱερὰ νύκτωρ ἣ μεθ’ ἡμέραν τελεῖς;— ΔΙ. νύκτωρ τὰ πολλά· σεμνότητ’ ἔχει σκότος</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Mor. 291">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 291</ref>A mentions, as Boeotian festivals of Dionysus, the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγριώνια</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Νυκτέλια,—ὧν τὰ πολλὰ διὰ σκότους δρᾶται</quote>. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 340">Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 340</ref> (the Chorus of the Initiated) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγειρε φλογέας λαμπάδας ἐν χερσὶ τινάσσων, | Ἴακχ’, ὦ Ἴακχε, | νυκτέρου τελετῆς φωσφόρος ἀστήρ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖ_Διὸς_1149"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν ὡς</foreign> (1140), but is sound, since the second syllable may be either long or short (see Anal.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦναξ_1150"> is a certain correction of L's <quote xml:lang="grc">ναξίαις</quote>. The latter, i.e. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ναξίαις</quote>, may be rejected for two reasons. (1) vv. 1140 f. (<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν—ἐπὶ νόσου</quote>) are clearly sound, and the weak addition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμά</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote> is a pure guess, based on the supposed genuineness of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ναξίαις</quote> here. (2) Naxos was, indeed, peculiarly associated with Dionysus, through Ariadne's story, and in other ways ( <ref n="Diod. 5. 50">Diod. 5. 50</ref> ff., <title>N. H.</title> 4. 12. 22): but, here, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ναξίαις</quote>, as the epithet of his followers, would be inappropriate, since he is to visit Thebes either from Parnassus or from Euboea (1143 f.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θυίαισιν_1152"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">θύω</quote>, to sacrifice), female votaries of Dionysus,—here, his attendant Nymphs (<ref n="Soph. OC 679"> <title>O. C.</title> 679</ref> n.),—not human worshippers. The pediment of the temple at Delphi represented Dionysus with the Thyiads, and a setting sun (Stephani, <title>Compt. rend.</title>, 1860, vol. 3 pp. 77 ff.). Similar names were <quote xml:lang="grc">Βάκχαι, Αῆναι, Μαινάδες</quote> (this properly a general epithet); and, in Macedonia, <quote xml:lang="grc">Κλώδωνες, Μιμαλλόνες</quote> ( <ref n="Plut. Alex. 2">Plut. <title>Alex.</title> 2</ref>). <ref n="Plut. Mor. 389">Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 389</ref>C quotes some words of a thyiad song, <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔιον ὀρσιγύναικα μαινομέναις Διόνυσον ἀνθέοντα τιμαῖς</quote>. In Elis a Dionysiac festival was called <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ Θυῖα</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 6.26.1">Paus. 6.26.1</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Catul. 64">Catull. 64. 255 ff.</ref>: <ref n="Verg. A. 4.301">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 4. 301 ff.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χορεύουσι_1152"> with acc. of the god, as <ref n="Pind. I. 1">Pind. <title>I.</title> 1. 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Φοῖβον χορεύων</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">κόπτομαι, τίλλομαι, τύπτομαι</quote> with acc. of person mourned. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταμίαν_1154">, dispenser (of their fortunes): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 379E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 379E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ‘ταμίασ’ ἡμῖν Ζεὺς ‘ἀγαθῶν τε κακῶν τε τέτυκται.’</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς ‘ταμίασ’ ἡμῖν Ζεὺς ‘ἀγαθῶν τε κακῶν τε
									τέτυκται.’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἴακχον_1154">: see on <quote xml:lang="grc">χοράγ’ ἄστρων</quote> (1146). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμων_1155"> goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κάδμου_1155"> also: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 417"> <title>O. T.</title> 417</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρός τε καὶ τοῦ σοῦ πατρός</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1399"> <title>O. C.</title> 1399</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κελεύθου τῆς τ’ ἐμῆς δυσπραξίας</quote> </cit>. Cadmus founded Thebes; at a later time, Amphion (Niobe's husband) and his brother Zethus built a wall round it (Apoll. Rhod. 1. 740 ff.). The Thebans are </gloss>

data.quote: μητρός τε καὶ τοῦ σοῦ πατρός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάροικοι_1155"> (neighbours) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμων_1155_2">, as dwelling around the <quote xml:lang="grc">Καδμεία</quote>, the Theban acropolis which was the seat of Cadmus (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 20"> <title>O. T.</title> 20</ref> n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμων_1155"> goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κάδμου_1155"> also: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 417"> <title>O. T.</title> 417</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρός τε καὶ τοῦ σοῦ πατρός</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1399"> <title>O. C.</title> 1399</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κελεύθου τῆς τ’ ἐμῆς δυσπραξίας</quote> </cit>. Cadmus founded Thebes; at a later time, Amphion (Niobe's husband) and his brother Zethus built a wall round it (Apoll. Rhod. 1. 740 ff.). The Thebans are </gloss>

data.quote: μητρός τε καὶ τοῦ σοῦ πατρός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάροικοι_1155"> (neighbours) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμων_1155_2">, as dwelling around the <quote xml:lang="grc">Καδμεία</quote>, the Theban acropolis which was the seat of Cadmus (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 20"> <title>O. T.</title> 20</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔστι_1156"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτος ἀνθρώπου βίος</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον οὔτ’ αἰνέσαιμι ἄν ποτε οὔτε μεμψαίμην στάντα</quote>: there is no kind of human life that I would ever praise, or complain of, as fixed. The partic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στάντα_1156"> has a causal force, giving the ground for the praise or blame. Prosperity may seem secure, or misery irremediable; but no condition can be regarded as really stable (<quote xml:lang="grc">στάσιμον</quote>). Soph. has given us a perfect comment on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στάντα_1156_2"> (which Nauck calls ‘undoubtedly’ corrupt) in fr. 786, and it is strange that it should have escaped notice:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὑμὸς ἀεὶ πότμος ἐν πυκνῷ θεοῦ | τρόχῳ κυκλεῖται, καὶ μεταλλάσσει φύσιν· | ὥσπερ σελήνης δ’ ὄψις εὐφρόνας δύο | στῆναι δύναιτ’ ἂν οὔποτ’ ἐν μορφῇ μιᾷ</quote>,—cannot <emph>remain fixed</emph> in one phase.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίον_1156"> is the antecedent drawn into the clause and case of the relative: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 56"> <title>O. C.</title> 56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν δ’ ἐπιστείβεις τόπον | ...καλεῖται</quote> </cit> etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 907"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 907</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ οὕσπερ αὐτὸς τοὺς νόμους εἰσῆλθ’ ἔχων</quote> </cit>.—The only other tenable view would be: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">βίος τοιοῦτος στὰς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον αἰνέσαιμι ἄν</quote>: there is no life so situated that I could praise it. On this view, <quote xml:lang="grc">στάντα</quote> would cohere closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποῖον</quote>, having been attracted into the acc. like <quote xml:lang="grc">βίον</quote> itself. This is not impossible; but, if this were the construction, I should wish to read </gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν δ’ οὕσπερ αὐτὸς τοὺς νόμους εἰσῆλθ’
									ἔχων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁποίᾳ_1156">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 950"> <title>Ai.</title> 950</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν τάδ’ ἔστη τῇδε μὴ θεῶν μέτα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐκ ἂν τάδ’ ἔστη τῇδε μὴ θεῶν μέτα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάντις_καθεστώτων_1160">, a prophet <emph>about</emph> them,—i.e. one who can say how long they will last. The conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφεστώτων_1160"> (‘imminent’), which Nauck receives, is decidedly wrong for two reasons. (1) Though we find <quote xml:lang="grc">Κῆρες ἐφεστᾶσιν</quote>, etc., the perf. <emph>part.</emph> was regularly used as it is in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1072"> <title>Ai.</title> 1072</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐφεστώτων</quote> </cit> (masc.) <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύειν</quote> ‘to obey the <emph>rulers</emph>’, and here a Greek would rather have supposed the sense to be, ‘no one in authority is a prophet.’ (2) The point is that things may seem established, and yet be unstable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐμοί_1161">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 396"> <title>Ai.</title> 396</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρεβος ω<*> φαεννότατον ὡς ἐμοί</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 20"> <title>O. C.</title> 20</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σώσας_ἐχθρῶν_1162">: the gen. as after <quote xml:lang="grc">λύω, ἐλευθερόω</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 919"> <title>Ph.</title> 919</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶσαι κακοῦ</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 779">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 779</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σωθῆναι κακῶν</quote> </cit>.— The regular constr. would have been <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν ζηλωτός, σώσας <emph>μὲν</emph> χθόνα, λαβὼν <emph>δὲ</emph> μοναρχίαν</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_1162">, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1162"> has been substituted, as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1012"> <title>Tr.</title> 1012</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν ἐν πόντῳ κατά τε δρία πάντα καθαίρων</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1056"> <title>Ph.</title> 1056</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι μὲν | Τεῦκρος,... | ἐγώ θ’</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1136"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1136</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶν μὲν αἰσχρὰς ἀπάτας, | στυγνόν τε φῶτ’</quote> </cit>. Then in the second clause, </gloss>

data.quote:
πολλὰ μὲν ἐν πόντῳ κατά τε δρία πάντα
									καθαίρων

ὁρῶν μὲν αἰσχρὰς ἀπάτας, | στυγνόν τε
									φῶτ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβών_τε_1162">, <emph>a</emph> new finite verb, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ηὔθυνε_1162">, has been inserted, with the result that <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβών</quote> now begins a new sentence. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 815">Cp. 815</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνησεν</quote>, n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντελῆ_1162">, complete; <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 698A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 698A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ παντελὴς...ἐλευθερία</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 737">cp. 737</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ηὔθυνε_1162_2">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 178">cp. 178</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 104"> <title>O. T.</title> 104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπευθύνειν πόλιν</quote> </cit>. The temporal augment for verbs beginning with <quote xml:lang="grc">ευ</quote> is attested by Attic inscrr. of c. 403—321 B.C. (Meisterhans, p. 78).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐγενεῖ_1162">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐγενῶν</quote>: 793 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφεῖται_πάντα_1165">, all has been given up,—has slipped from his grasp, and been lost. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.49">Her. 8.49</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γὰρ Ἀττικὴ ἀπεῖτο ἤδη</quote> </cit> (to the Persians). The perf. pass. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφίημι</quote> is always pass. in sense, usu. meaning either ‘set free’ (as <ref n="Isoc. 17.11">Isocr. or. 17 § 11</ref>), or, ‘left free’ (<ref n="Plat. Criti. 117C">Plat. <title>Critias</title> 117C</ref>, of open ground), or ‘permitted’ (<ref n="Thuc. 5.91">Thuc. 5.91</ref>). The only apparent instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφεῖμαι</quote> as a perf. <emph>midd.</emph> is <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 23.157">Dem. or. 23 § 157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μὲν τιμωρεῖσθαι τὸν Χαρίδημον ἀφεῖσθαι, ἀποστεῖλαι δ’ ὑπόσπονδον</quote> </cit>. But there, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστεῖλαι</quote> suggests, we must surely read the 2nd aor. midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφέσθαι</quote>, which was frequent in this sense (<ref n="Plat. Gorg. 458C">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 458C</ref>, etc.). If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφεῖται</quote> were midd. here, we should require <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.60">Thuc. 2.60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κοινοῦ τῆς σωτηρίας ἀφίεσθε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῦ μὲν τιμωρεῖσθαι τὸν Χαρίδημον ἀφεῖσθαι,
									ἀποστεῖλαι δ’ ὑπόσπονδον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προδῶσιν_1166">. <foreign xml:lang="grc">προδιδόναι ἡδονάς</foreign> could not mean merely, ‘to <emph>resign</emph>’ one's joys. It necessarily implies a fault on the loser's part; and it is precisely because Creon had committed such a fault that I believe </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προδῶσιν_1166_2"> to be sound. The man accused of taking a bribe to break the law was described at 322 as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀργύρῳ... τὴν ψυχὴν προδούς</quote>. Our word, ‘to forfeit,’ i.e. ‘to lose by one's own fault,’ seems fairly to represent the shade of meaning which distinguishes <quote xml:lang="grc">προδιδόναι ἡδονάς</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολλύναι ἡδονάς</quote>. Creon's joys—the life of his son, and the good opinion of his subjects—have been sacrificed by him to the indulgence of stubborn self-will. Athenaeus, who twice quotes this passage (cr. n.), shows that c. 200 A. D. it was read as above: he is our oldest and best source for it. L's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρὸς_1166"> must be considered in connection with the fact that L (like our other MSS.) omits v. 1167. L has a point at </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προδῶσιν_1166_3">, and its reading was (I suspect) understood thus: ‘when (men) forfeit their pleasures, I do not count that the part of a man’ (i.e. of one who can be really said to live). Hence I do not think that L's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς</quote> really confirms Seyffert's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>καὶ γὰρ ἡδοναὶ</emph> | ὅταν προδῶσιν ἀνδρός</quote>, ‘when a man's pleasures <emph>fail.</emph>’ For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">προδιδόναι</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.187">Her. 7.187</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν μοι θωῦμα παρίσταται προδοῦναι τὰ ῥέεθρα τῶν ποταμῶν</quote> </cit> (that they failed=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιλιπεῖν</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.52">id. 8. 52</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ φράγματος προδεδωκότος</quote> </cit>, the barricade having failed (them). Xenophanes fr. 1. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος δ’ οἶνος ἑτοῖμος, ὃς οὔποτέ φησι προδώσειν</quote>. So with acc., <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 52.13">[Dem. ] or. 52 § 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὀφθαλμὸν αὐτὸν προδιδόντα</quote> </cit> (his eye-sight failing him). Yet here the phrase would seem a strange one. And if </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδέν μοι θωῦμα παρίσταται προδοῦναι τὰ ῥέεθρα
									τῶν ποταμῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνδρες_1166"> was older than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρός_1166">, as we have reason to believe that it was, that fact would confirm the genuineness of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_ἡδονάς_1166">.—See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_τίθημι_1166"> with inf., as oft. with the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">τίθεμαι</quote>; <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 93C">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 93C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν οὖν τιθεμένων ψυχὴν ἁρμονίαν εἶναι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1270"> <title>El.</title> 1270</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαιμόνιον αὐτὸ τίθημ’ ἐγώ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: τῶν οὖν τιθεμένων ψυχὴν ἁρμονίαν εἶναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτον_1167"> after the plur. as, conversely, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτοι</quote> (709 n.), and <quote xml:lang="grc">νέκυν</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote> (1072).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμψ_νεκρόν_1167">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1018"> <title>Ph.</title> 1018</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφιλον, ἔρημον, ἄπολιν, ἐν ζῶσιν νεκρόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλούτει_ζῆ_1168">, the hypothetical imperat.: Antiphon fr. 130 (<emph>ap.</emph> <title>Flor.</title> 68. 37) <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε δὴ καὶ παῖδες γενέσθωσαν· φροντίδων ἤδη πάντα πλέα</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 20.14">Dem. or. 20 § 14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γὰρ εἰ πάνυ χρηστός ἐσθ’, ὡς ἐμοῦ γ’ ἕνεκα ἔστω, βελτίων ἐστὶ τῆς πόλεως τὸ ἦθος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδὲ γὰρ εἰ πάνυ χρηστός ἐσθ’, ὡς ἐμοῦ γ’
									ἕνεκα ἔστω, βελτίων ἐστὶ τῆς πόλεως τὸ ἦθος.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_βούλει_1168">: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 432A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 432A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἰσχυροτάτους καὶ τοὺς μέσους, εἰ μὲν βούλει, φρονήσει, εἰ δὲ βούλει, ἰσχύϊ</quote> </cit>. For the form </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς ἰσχυροτάτους καὶ τοὺς μέσους, εἰ μὲν
									βούλει, φρονήσει, εἰ δὲ βούλει, ἰσχύϊ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῆ_1168">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 699">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 699</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἕρπε καὶ ζῆ καὶ δόμους οἴκει πατρός</quote> </cit>. But <title>Anthol. P.</title> 11. 57 <quote xml:lang="grc">πῖνε, γέρον, καὶ ζῆθι</quote> (by Agathias, c. <date when="-0550">550 A.D.</date>): and so <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 10. 43 (author uncertain). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἕρπε καὶ ζῆ καὶ δόμους οἴκει
									πατρός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχῆμα_1168">, outward show, dignity, pomp: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 685C">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 685C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τῆς ἀρχῆς σχῆμα...οὐ σμικρόν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_χαίρειν_1170">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 555"> <title>Ai.</title> 555</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως τὸ χαίρειν καὶ τὸ λυπεῖσθαι μάθῃς</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 301">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρειν, τὸ χαίρειν μὴ μαθόνθ’ ὅπου φρενῶν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 423"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 423</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου τὸ χαίρειν μηδαμοῦ νομίζεται</quote> </cit>. For the thought, cp. Simonides fr. 71 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς γὰρ ἁδονᾶς ἄτερ θνατῶν βίος ποθεινὸς ἢ ποία τυραννίς; | τᾶς δ’ ἄτερ οὐδὲ θεῶν ζαλωτὸς αἰών</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονή</quote> is as general as <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ χαίρειν</quote> here. More often, however, the sentiment refers to sensuous <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδοναί</quote>: cp. Mimnermus fr. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθναίην ὅτε μοι μηκέτι ταῦτα μέλοι</quote>: Antiphanes fr. incert. 51 (it is foolish to disparage <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρως</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γὰρ ἀφέλοι τις τοῦ βίου τὰς ἡδονάς, | καταλείπετ’ οὐδὲν ἕτερον ἢ τεθνηκέναι.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote: ἕως τὸ χαίρειν καὶ τὸ λυπεῖσθαι μάθῃς

ἔρρειν, τὸ χαίρειν μὴ μαθόνθ’ ὅπου
									φρενῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καπνοῦ_σκιᾶς_1170">: gen. of price (nom., <quote xml:lang="grc">καπνοῦ σκιά</quote>). <ref n="Soph. Phil. 946"> <title>Ph.</title> 946</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ οἶδ’ ἐναίρων νεκρὸν ἢ καπνοῦ σκιάν, | εἴδωλον ἄλλως</quote>. Aesch. fr. 390 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ βρότειον σπέρμ’ ἐφ’ ἡμέραν φρονεῖ, | καὶ πιστὸν οὐδὲν μᾶλλον ἢ καπνοῦ σκιά</quote>. So Soph. fr. 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνθρωπός ἐστι πνεῦμα καὶ σκιὰ μόνον</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 8">Pind. <title>P.</title> 8. 95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκιᾶς ὄναρ | ἄνθρωπος</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 839">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 839</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴδωλον σκιᾶς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πριαίμην_ἀνδρί_1171">. After a verb of buying or receiving, the dat. of interest denotes the person who has the thing taken off his hands: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 812">Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 812</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόσου πρίωμαί σοι τὰ χοιρίδια</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.87"> <title>Il.</title> 15.87</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θέμιστι δὲ καλλιπαρῃῳ | δέκτο δέπας.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἡδ_1171">, <emph>compared with</emph> it: fr. 327. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄστι πρὸς τὰ χρήματα | θνητοῖσι τἄλλα δεύτερ’</quote>: Eur. fr. 96 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδὲν ηὑγένεια πρὸς τὰ χρήματα</quote>. Suppose that one could buy either (1) wealth and power without joy, or (2) joy without wealth and power; in comparison with (2), (1) would be worth nothing.—Not, ‘<emph>in exchange for</emph> pleasure,’ like <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 69A">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 69A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδονὰς πρὸς ἡδονὰς... καταλλάττεσθαι</quote> </cit>: for the price is expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">καπνοῦ σκιᾶς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βασιλέων_1172"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τυράννων</foreign>, the royal house. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 316"> <title>Tr.</title> 316</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τῶν τυράννων</quote> </cit>; is she of the royal stock?—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἴτιοι_θανεῖν_1172">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θανεῖν</quote>: Antiphon or. 5 § 23 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ αἴτιος ἦν πεμφθῆναι ἄγγελον</quote>.—As vv. 1186 ff. show, Eurydicè is supposed to be in the act of opening the palace-door, to come out, when she overhears evil tidings. If she is supposed to have fainted (1188) immediately on hearing the general announcement in v. 1173, then her request in v. 1190 is the more natural. Possibly the spectators were allowed to catch a glimpse of her through the partly opened doors; though the Chorus announce her only at 1180. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φονεύει_1174"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁ φονεύς ἐστιν</foreign>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 437"> <title>O. T.</title> 437</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς δέ μ’ ἐκφύει βροτῶν</quote> </cit> (is my sire), where see n.—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_κείμενος_1174">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 590">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 590</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ κειμένῳ πω τόνδε κομπάζεις λόγον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόχειρ_1175"> could mean either ‘by a kinsman's hand,’ or ‘by his own hand.’ See n. on 56 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοκτονοῦντε</quote>. Hence such compounds sometimes receive a further definition, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 841"> <title>Ai.</title> 841</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοσφαγεῖς | πρὸς τῶν φιλίστων ἐκγόνων</quote> </cit>. But in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 336">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 336</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτουργίαι</quote> </cit>, without any such addition,=‘murders of kinsfolk.’ G. Wolff ought not, however, to have compared <ref n="Xen. Hell. 6.4.35">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 6.4.35</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποθνῄσκει αὐτόχειρίᾳ μὲν ὑπὸ τῶν τῆς γυναικὸς ἀδελφῶν, βουλῇ δὲ ὑπ’ αὐτῆς ἐκείνης</quote>, i.e., ‘by the deed of their hands’ (cp. above, v. 306), ‘though at her instigation.’ Attic prose does not use <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόχειρ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοχειρία</quote> in the pregnant poetical sense (a slayer, or a slaying, of kinsfolk), but merely in the general sense, ‘doing with one's own hands,’ etc. See <ref n="Plat. Laws 872B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 872B</ref>: <ref n="Plat. Laws 865b"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 865 B</ref>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνου_1177">, causal gen.: 931 (<quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote>) n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθὸν_ἤνυσας_1177">, hast fulfilled it, so that it comes right (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1136">cp. 1136</ref>, n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερτάταν</quote>): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 506"> <title>O. T.</title> 506</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἴδοιμ’ ὀρθὸν ἔπος</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 853"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 853</ref>. <ref n="Soph. OC 454"> <title>O. C.</title> 454</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μαντεῖα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμοὶ Φοῖβος ἤνυσέν ποτε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1179"> with the gen. absol. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1063">cp. 1063</ref> n.) marks the point of view which is to be taken: ‘in the certainty that matters stand thus.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρεστι_ἄλλα_1179">, ‘ye may consider of the rest’: i.e., such are the facts; it only remains to deal with them as may seem best. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεύειν_1179">, to form plans, decide on a course of action, <ref n="Soph. OT 619"> <title>O. T.</title> 619</ref>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἄλλα_1179">, adverbial acc. of respect, ‘as to what remains,’ instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ τῶν ἄλλων</quote> (<cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 551"> <title>Ai.</title> 551</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἄλλ’ ὅμοιος</quote> </cit>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_ἐχόντων_1179">, neut. gen. abs., without a subject: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 981"> <title>Ai.</title> 981</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὧδ’ ἐχόντων... | πάρα στενάζειν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1344"> <title>El.</title> 1344</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελουμένων εἴποιμ’ ἄν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 170">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τάδ’, ὡς οὕτως ἐχόντων τῶνδε, σύμβουλοι λόγου | τοῦδέ μοι γένεσθε, Πέρσαι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.116">Thuc. 1.116</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσαγγελθέντων ὅτι...νῆες...πλέουσιν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
πρὸς τάδ’, ὡς οὕτως ἐχόντων τῶνδε, σύμβουλοι
									λόγου | τοῦδέ μοι γένεσθε, Πέρσαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μήν_1180">: 526 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐρυδίκην_1180">: anapaest in 5th place: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11">cp. 11</ref> n. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡσίοδος Ἡνιόχην αὐτὴν καλεῖ· ἵκετο δ’ εἰς Κρείοντα καὶ Ἡνιόχην [τανύπεπλον</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> Heracles: <title>Scut.</title> 83].—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὁμοῦ</emph> = ἐγγύς</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Peace 513">Aristoph. <title>Pax</title> 513</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴν ὁμοῦ ’στιν ἤδη</quote> </cit>. Soph. has it also as a prep.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 767"> <title>Ai.</title> 767</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς...ὁμοῦ = σὺν θ</quote> </cit>.: in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1218"> <title>Ph.</title> 1218</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεὼς ὁμοῦ = πέλας νεώς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύουσα_παιδός_1182">: gen. of connection, ‘about’ him: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 307"> <title>O. C.</title> 307</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύων σοῦ</quote> </cit> (n.), hearing of thee. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ἀστοί_1183">, said to the Chorus and the Messenger, as representing the Thebans generally. In <ref n="Eur. IT 1422">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1422</ref> Thoas says <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πάντες ἀστοί</quote>, not to the Chorus (of Greek women), but to the Tauri as represented by his attendants. So, too, in <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Eccl. 834">Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 834</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πάντες ἀστοί</quote> </cit> is said to an imaginary body of Athenian citizens, represented by two men on the stage. In <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 638"> <title>Lys.</title> 638</ref> the Chorus say <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πάντες ἀστοί</quote> to the audience. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Παλλάδος_εὐγμάτων_1184"> answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">Παλλάδα προσαγορεύω εὔγματα</quote>: i.e., the first gen. is objective, while the second represents an ‘inner’ accus. A combination of genitives was easily tolerated by Greek idiom: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 795">cp. 795</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βλεφάρων ἵμερος...νύμφας</quote> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 8.3.19">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8.3.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεόμενοι Κύρου ἄλλος ἄλλης πράξεως. <emph>προσήγορος</emph> </quote> </cit>, active, as <ref n="Soph. OT 1338"> <title>O. T.</title> 1338</ref>: it is passive <ref n="Soph. OT 1437"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1437</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1353."> <title>Ph.</title> 1353.</ref>—The shrine to which Eurydicè was going may be imagined as one of the two <quote xml:lang="grc">Παλλάδος ναοί</quote> at Thebes mentioned in <ref n="Soph. OT 20"> <title>O. T.</title> 20</ref> (n.).
