She was anxious to do her part in seeking to propitiate the angry gods (1019).—So Iocasta comes forth from the house to offer prayers at the altar of Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκειος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 919"> <title>O. T.</title> 919</ref>), and Clytaemnestra at that of Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">προστατήριος</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 637"> <title>El.</title> 637</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυγχάνω_βάλλει_1186">: just as she was loosing the bolt, she heard</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_καί_1186">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 1. 8">Xen. <title>An.</title> 1. 8 § 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη τε ἦν ἀμφὶ ἀγορὰν πλήθουσαν καὶ πλησίον ἦν ὁ σταθμός</quote> </cit>: so <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4.2.12"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 4. 2. 12</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 6. 2">Xen. An. 4. 6. 2</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 1. 4. 28.">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1. 4. 28.</ref> So with <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> alone, <ref n="Soph. OT 718"> <title>O. T.</title> 718</ref> n.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἤδη τε ἦν ἀμφὶ ἀγορὰν πλήθουσαν καὶ πλησίον ἦν
									ὁ σταθμός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλῇθρα_ἀνασπαστοῦ_1186">, loosing the bolts of the door, so that it should be opened (proleptic, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 475">cp. 475</ref>, 881). For the fem. of the verbal in -<quote xml:lang="grc">τός</quote>, see <ref n="Soph. OT 384"> <title>O. T.</title> 384</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλῇθρα_1186">, ‘bolts,’ are bars of wood drawn across the doors inside, and held by staples or sockets (<quote xml:lang="grc">πυθμένες</quote> <ref n="Soph. OT 1261"> <title>O. T.</title> 1261</ref>) in the door-posts (<quote xml:lang="grc">σταθμοί</quote>). Such bars were usu. called <quote xml:lang="grc">μοχλοί</quote>, but even in prose we find the more general word <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῇθρα</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 7.1.17">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 7.1.17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διακόπτοντες ταῖς ἀξίναις τὰ κλεῖθρα ἀναπεταννύουσι τὰς πύλας</quote> </cit>. There, as here, the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῇθρα</quote>, referring to only one gate, indicates that more than one bolt was used; so, too, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 310">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 310</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄν μὴ καλούντων τοὺς μοχλοὺς χαλῶσιν αἱ γυναῖκες, | ἐμπιμπράναι χρὴ τὰς θύρας</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 878">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 878</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύλας | μοχλοῖς χαλᾶτε</quote> </cit>, open the door by (withdrawing) the bars. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1314">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1314</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλᾶτε κλῇδας</quote> </cit>. <title>Pelop.</title> 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδοῦναι καὶ χαλάσαι τὰς θύρας</quote>.</gloss>

data.quote:
διακόπτοντες ταῖς ἀξίναις τὰ κλεῖθρα
									ἀναπεταννύουσι τὰς πύλας

κἄν μὴ καλούντων τοὺς μοχλοὺς χαλῶσιν αἱ
									γυναῖκες, | ἐμπιμπράναι χρὴ τὰς θύρας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνασπαστοῦ_1186">. These doors opened inwards. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισπᾶν θύραν</quote> meant to shut the door after one, in going out. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 6.4.36">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 6.4.36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δ’ εἰσῆλθον, ἐπισπάσασα τὴν θύραν εἴχετο τοῦ ῥόπτρου</quote> </cit> (Thebè's object was to shut her brothers into the room, till they had killed Alexander of Pherae): ‘when they had entered, she, [having gone out and] shut the door, held the knocker,’—the <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥόπτρον</quote> being a metal ring on the outside of the door, which also served as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισπαστήρ</quote> or handle (cp. <ref n="Hdt. 6.91">Her. 6.91</ref>). <title>Dion</title> 57 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ μὲν ἔξω τὰς θύρας ἐπισπασάμενοι κατεῖχον</quote>. (In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1244"> <title>O. T.</title> 1244</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύλας...ἐπιρράξασα</quote> </cit> is said of Iocasta, within the room, shutting the doors; but they, too, opened inwards, see <ref n="Soph. OT 1261"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1261 ff.</ref>) Hence <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνασπᾶν θύραν</quote>, ‘to draw it back,’ is the opposite of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισπᾶν</quote>, and means ‘to open it’ (from within). That phrase was not actually current, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνοιγνύναι</quote> being the common word; but the poetical <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνασπαστός</quote> here implies it. Polyb. 5. 39. 4 is not rightly compared: there, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀνασπάσοντες...τὰς πυλίδας</quote>=‘intending to wrench the gates open,’ from outside: cp. id. 2. 5. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ...γεφύρας ἀνασπάσαντες τὰς σανίδας</quote>, ‘having torn up the floor of the bridge.’</gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς δ’ εἰσῆλθον, ἐπισπάσασα τὴν θύραν εἴχετο
									τοῦ ῥόπτρου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθόγγος_1186">, the Messenger's words. She had fainted before hearing more, perhaps, than vv. 1172 f., where see n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκείου_1186">, domestic (affecting her family): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1249">cp. 1249</ref>. —Distinguish <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκ. πάθη</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 260"> <title>Ai.</title> 260</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκ. ἄτας</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 215"> <title>El.</title> 215</ref>) as=‘caused by oneself.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῑ_ὤτων_1188">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 737"> <title>El.</title> 737</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξὺν δῑ ὤτων κέλαδον ἐνσείσας θοαῖς | πώλοις</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1386"> <title>O. T.</title> 1386</ref> f.</gloss>

data.quote:
ὀξὺν δῑ ὤτων κέλαδον ἐνσείσας θοαῖς |
									πώλοις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_δμωαῖσι_1188">, with <quote xml:lang="grc">κλίνομαι</quote>: a use of the dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> which has epic precedent, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 20.420"> <title>Il.</title> 20.420</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λιαζόμενον προτὶ γαίῃ</quote> </cit>, sinking to earth: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 21.507"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 21. 507</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δὲ προτὶ οἷ | εἷλε πατὴρ Κρονίδης</quote> </cit>, took her to his arms: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1302"> <title>O. T.</title> 1302</ref> n.</gloss>

data.quote: τὴν δὲ προτὶ οἷ | εἷλε πατὴρ Κρονίδης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποπλήσσομαι_1188">, as=<quote xml:lang="grc">λιποψυχῶ</quote>, only here: Hippocr. uses it of apoplexy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_παρήσω_1192">: I <emph>both</emph> will speak as one who was present, <emph>and</emph> will omit nothing: i.e., <emph>as</emph> my knowledge is full, <emph>so</emph> shall the story be told without reserve. For the parataxis, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1112">cp. 1112</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 680"> <title>El.</title> 680</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπεμπόμην πρὸς ταῦτα καὶ τὸ πᾶν φράσω</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 626"> <title>Tr.</title> 626</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσταμαί τε καὶ φράσω σεσωσμένα</quote> </cit>. For </gloss>

data.quote: κἀπεμπόμην πρὸς ταῦτα καὶ τὸ πᾶν φράσω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὠν_1192"> as partic. of the imperfect, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 166">cp. 166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σέβοντας</quote> (n.). Verses 1192—1195 form the dramatic apology for a trait which is manifestly open to criticism,— viz., the fulness of harrowing details communicated by the Messenger to this unhappy mother, who has only just recovered from a swoon. (See esp. 1231 —1239.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_1194"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τούτοις ὧν</foreign>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 1.2.6">Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1.2.6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλέγεσθαι παρ’ ὧν λάβοιεν τὸν μισθόν</quote> </cit>, to converse with any who might pay them. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 35">Cp. 35</ref> n. </gloss>

data.quote: διαλέγεσθαι παρ’ ὧν λάβοιεν τὸν μισθόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_1194_2"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψεῦσται_1194"> (=fabricators), as one could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα ἐψεύσαντο</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανούμεθ_1194"> after the sing. verb, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 734">cp. 734</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθόν_1194">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθή</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 542"> <title>O. T.</title> 542</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. OC 592"> <title>O. C.</title> 592</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποδαγός_1196">, Doric (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 715">cp. 715</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναός</quote>, n.), ‘guide,’ as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1715">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1715</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ μοι ποδαγὸς ἀθλία γενοῦ</quote> </cit> (Oed. to Antigone). <ref n="Plat. Laws 899A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 899A</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδηγεῖν</quote> as= ‘to guide.’ —The word is usu. taken here as merely <foreign xml:lang="lat">pedissequus</foreign>, ‘attendant.’ But the sense of ‘guiding’ is essential to it. Creon had indicated the region (1110), but he need not be supposed to know the spot where the body lay, or even the exact situation of Antigone's tomb. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑσπόμην_1196">, ‘attended,’ is compatible with guiding. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεδίον_ἄκρον_1197">=the furthest part of the plain. Near this part were the <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι</quote> on which the watchers sat (411), and Antigone's tomb was in one of those <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγοι</quote> (774 n.). Thebes had hills to the N. of it, and stood on a low spur which they throw out southward (117 ff., n.). The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκρον πεδίον</quote>, then, is the plain's northern edge, where it touches the lower slopes of the hills. The ‘furthest’ was thus also the highest part.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νηλεές_1197">, passive: <ref n="Soph. OT 180"> <title>O. T.</title> 180</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυνοσπάρακτον_1198">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 206">cp. 206</ref>, 1017. Antigone had paid the rites while the corpse was still intact (257), and in this sense is said to have saved it from birds and dogs (697: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 467">cp. 467</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_μέν_1199">, as opposed to Antigone: but instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς δὲ νυμφεῖον</quote>, etc., we have a change of construction (1204 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖθις κ.τ.λ.</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνοδίαν_θεόν_1199">, Hecatè, who was conceived as a wandering goddess, haunting the places where roads met, and where offerings were left for her. (<quote xml:lang="grc">Τριοδῖτις</quote>, <title>Trivia:</title> <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 2">Theocr. 2. 36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ θεὸς ἐν τριόδοισι</quote> </cit>.) Sophocles in his <quote xml:lang="grc">*(πιζοτόμοι</quote> gave an incantation by Medea, invoking Helios and Hecatè (fr. 490, schol. Apoll. Rhod. 3. 1214): <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥλιε δέσποτα καὶ πῦρ ἱερόν, | τῆς εἰνοδίας Ἑκάτης ἔγχος, | τὸ δῑ Οὐλύμπου πωλοῦσα φέρει</quote> (which she bears when she moves through the sky, as Selenè), | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γῆσναίουσ’ ίερὰς τριόδους, | στεφανωσαμένη δρυΐ καὶ πλεκταῖς | ὠμῶν σπείραισι δρακόντων</quote>. The last two lines refer to a custom of representing her as crowned with serpents, and with chaplets of oakleaves. Creon invokes her along with Pluto (Hades, <ref n="Soph. OT 30"> <title>O. T.</title> 30</ref> n.), because on earth she represented the <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιοι</quote>. As <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνοδία</quote>, she was more especially associated with Hermes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνόδιος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχοπομπός</quote>: hence she was sometimes called <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεόν_1199">, fem., as 834: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1548"> <title>O. C.</title> 1548</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ... νερτέρα θεός</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1556"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1556</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἀφανῆ θεόν</quote> </cit> (Persephone). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 683"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 683</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐμενεῖς_1199">, proleptic: <ref target="cl881">881 n.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασχεθεῖν_1199">: cp. <ref target="cl1102">on 1102.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λούσαντες_λουτρόν_1201"> (cognate acc.): cp. <ref target="cl900">on 901.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_1201">, ‘with,’ of the instrument: 764 (n.), 962, 1003.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαλλοῖς_1201">, from the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕλη πεδιάς</quote> close by (420).—Boeckh thought that <emph>olive</emph>-boughs were meant, citing a <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμος</quote> <emph>ap.</emph> <ref n="Dem. 43.71">[Dem. ] or. 43 § 71</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν μὴ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλαίᾳ</quote>)...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ἀποθανόντα δέῃ χρήσασθαι</quote>. But that, surely, does not refer to a <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρά</quote>. The olive, like the laurel, was used for other purposes connected with the dead,—viz., in crowning the corpse for the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόθεσις</quote> (cp. schol. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1626">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1626</ref>), and in decking the <quote xml:lang="grc">κλίνη</quote> on which the corpse was laid (<ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 1030">Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 1030</ref>).—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ δὴ = ὅ τι δήποτε</quote>, implying that much of the body had been destroyed. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.160">Her. 1.160</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ μισθῷ ὅσῳ δή· οὐ γὰρ ἔχω τοῦτό γε εἰπεῖν ἀτρεκέως.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπὶ μισθῷ ὅσῳ δή· οὐ γὰρ ἔχω τοῦτό γε εἰπεῖν
									ἀτρεκέως.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέλειπτο_1201">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 457">cp. 457</ref> '<quote xml:lang="grc">φάνη</quote> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκατῄθομεν_1201">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote> here is perh. not merely ‘completely,’ but implies the collecting of dismembered pieces: as <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκατακαίω</quote> regularly=to burn something ‘along with’ something else (<ref n="Xen. Anab. 3.2.27">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.2.27</ref>). Like </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσεβαίνομεν_1201"> (1205), this is the imperf. of consecutive action (‘proceeded to burn,’ = ‘next burned’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύμβον_1203">. If the Homeric usage was followed, when the flesh had been burned the bones would be washed with wine or oil, wrapped in fat, and placed in an urn (<quote xml:lang="grc">λάρναξ</quote>). The urn having been deposited in a grave (<quote xml:lang="grc">κάπετος</quote>), the <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβος</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆμα</quote>) would be raised over it. Cp. <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, ch. 11. § 33.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρθόκρανον_1203">, lit., with head erect, so=‘high’: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψικάρηνος, ὑψίλοφος</quote> (of hills). From <quote xml:lang="grc">κρα—ν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κρανίον</quote>) we have also <quote xml:lang="grc">βούκρα—νος, ταυρόκρα—νος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκείας_1203">, ‘native,’—a thing pleasing to the dead: so in <ref n="Soph. OC 406"> <title>O. C.</title> 406</ref> Oed. asks, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καὶ κατασκιῶσι Θηβαίᾳ κόνει</quote>; The father's prophecy for his sons was fulfilled: of their father-land they obtained <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπόσαν καὶ φθιμένοισιν κατέχειν</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 731">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 731</ref>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 789"> <title>O. C.</title> 789</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_1204"> answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μέν</quote> (1199), as in 167 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ αὖθις</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο μέν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιθόστρωτον_1204">, ‘with floor of stone,’ here suggests, ‘affording no couch but one of stone,’ in contrast with a real <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφεῖον</quote>, which contains a <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχος εὔστρωτον... | χλαίνῃσιν μαλακῇς ἐστρωμένον</quote> (<title>Hom. hymn. Ven.</title> 157 f.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόρης_νυμφεῖονἍιδου_1204">, the maiden's death-bower: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 795">cp. 795</ref> n., 929. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κωκυμάτων_1206">, the word used by Teiresias (1079): here, as usu., for the dead: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 28">cp. 28</ref>, 204, 1302: so <quote xml:lang="grc">κωκυτός</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 851"> <title>Ai.</title> 851</ref> etc.—Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 683"> <title>El.</title> 683</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθίων κηρυγμάτων</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκτέριστον_παστάδα_1207">, near (i.e. from the quarter of) the bridalchamber where no funeral-rites had been paid; i.e., where Antigone had been made the bride of Death, without even such honours as befitted such nuptials. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτέριστος</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1071">cp. 1071</ref> n. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">παστάς</quote> seems to be here used simply as a poetical equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλαμος</quote>. There is probably no reference to pillars of rock (natural or artificial) in the <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφος</quote>. On the uses of <quote xml:lang="grc">παστάς</quote>, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθλίας_βοῆς_1209">, indistinct sounds, consisting in an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθλία βοή</quote>: i.e., as he drew nearer, the sounds resolved themselves into the mournful cry of a human voice. The genit. is thus a ‘defining’ one. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 923"> <title>O. C.</title> 923</ref> n. <quote xml:lang="grc">φωτῶν ἀθλίων ἱκτήρια</quote> (suppliant objects consisting in unhappy persons). Below, in 1265, the form of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι ἐμῶν ἄνολβα βουλευμάτων</quote> is analogous, but the gen. is there partitive (see n.). Here, <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῆς</quote> could, indeed, be possessive (‘sounds belonging to, i.e., forming part of, a cry’). But the perspective of the description is better kept by the other view of the genitive, which supposes that a sound, ambiguous at a distance, defines itself as we approach.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιβαίνει_1209">, with dat. instead of the normal acc.; this dat. denotes the person interested, i.e., here affected through the senses: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 372"> <title>O. C.</title> 372</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσῆλθε τοῖν τρὶς ἀθλίοιν ἔρις</quote> </cit> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 298"> <title>Tr.</title> 298</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ γὰρ οἶκτος... εἰσέβη</quote> </cit>. For the image, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1477"> <title>O. C.</title> 1477</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίσταται | διαπρύσιος ὄτοβος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 6.122"> <title>Od.</title> 6.122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς τέ με κουράων ἀμφήλυθε θῆλυς ἀϋτή</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote: ὥς τέ με κουράων ἀμφήλυθε θῆλυς ἀϋτή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μᾶλλον_ἆσσον_1210">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 673">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 673</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἐνδικώτερος</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 222">Eur. <title>El.</title> 222</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἐχθίους</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 781A">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 781A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαθραιότερον μᾶλλον καὶ ἐπικλοπώτερον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπος_1210"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">θρῆνον</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσθρήνητον_1210">: see n. on 7.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τάλας_1210">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 744"> <title>O. T.</title> 744</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_παρελθουσῶν_1213">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 100">cp. 100</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλιστον...τῶν προτέρων</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαίνει_1213">, ‘greets my ear.’ As <quote xml:lang="grc">σαίνω</quote> was properly said of a dog wagging its tail or fawning so it could be said of a sight or a sound which <emph>appeals for recognition</emph> by vividly striking our senses. Like <emph>arridere</emph>, the word usually implied a sensation of pleasure (<ref n="Soph. OC 319"> <title>O. C.</title> 319</ref> n.). But it could also denote, as here, a recognition attended by pain. So in <title>Hippol.</title> 862 f., where Theseus recognises the seal on the tablets left by his dead wife, he says <quote xml:lang="grc">τύποι...προσσαίνουσί με</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠκεῖς_1215">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 823">cp. 823</ref>, 847: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 927"> <title>Tr.</title> 927</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δρομαία βᾶς</quote> </cit>'. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁρμὸν_λιθοσπ_1216">, an opening in the stones heaped up at the entrance, made by dragging some of them away. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 848">Cp. 848</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕργμα τυμβόχωστον</quote>. Haemon, in his frenzy of despair, had broken into the tomb by wrenching away part of this rude wall-work. The gap remained as he had made it. He had reached the spot only a short time before Creon (cp. on 1223).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁρμόν_1216">. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμός</quote> means, (1) a fastening: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1315">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1315</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλύεθ’ ἁρμούς</quote> </cit>, undo the fastenings of the doors: (2) the chink between two things which are jointed together: so in <ref n="Plut. Alex. 3">Plut. <title>Alex.</title> 3</ref> a furtive listener is described as <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ τῆς θύρας ἁρμῷ προσβαλὼν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ὄψιν</quote>), ‘having put his eye to the chink in the door.’ So here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμός</quote> is an aperture, just wide enough to admit of a man going through (cp. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύντες_1216">).— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμός</quote> (with its derivatives <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρμοῖ, ἁρμόζω, ἁρμονία</quote>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρπεδόνη</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρπεδών</quote>), ‘rope,’ are connected with the causative form of the root <emph>ar</emph>, <emph>ar-pajā-mi</emph>, ‘to fasten’: see Curt. <title>Etym.</title> § 488. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόμιον_1217">. Having passed through the gap, they will find themselves in a narrow passage. They are to go along this passage to the very mouth (<quote xml:lang="grc">στόμιον</quote>) of the sepulchral chamber into which it opens.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῖσι_1218">: dat. of agent with <emph>pres.</emph> pass.: see n. on 503 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλέπτομαι_1218">, am deluded: 681 n.—Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 316"> <title>O. C.</title> 316</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ γνώμη πλανᾷ;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελευσμάτων_1219"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">κελεύσμασιν</quote>, is (I think) certainly right. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 397">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 397</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαισαν ἅλμην βρύχιον ἐκ κελεύσματος</quote> </cit> (and similarly <ref n="Eur. IT 1405">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1405</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.50">Her. 6.50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεγε δὲ ταῦτα ἐξ ἐπιστολῆς τῆς Δημαρήτου</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἔλεγε δὲ ταῦτα ἐξ ἐπιστολῆς τῆς
									Δημαρήτου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελεύσμασιν_1219">, we have only two tolerable resources. (1) To join <quote xml:lang="grc">κελεύσμασιν ἐξ ἀθύμου δεσπότου</quote>, ‘orders given by him.’ But, though <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἐξ ἀθ. δεσπ. κελεύσμασιν</quote> could be defended by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἐξ ἐμοῦ δυσβουλίαν</quote> (95), the phrase without the article is very strange. In phrases which might appear similar, it will be found that a verb has influenced the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> with gen.: thus <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 137"> <title>Ai.</title> 137</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ δ’ ὅταν... | λόγος ἐκ Δαναῶν κακόθρους ἐπιβῇ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1366">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1366</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ γὰρ τεκμηρίοισιν ἐξ οἰμωγμάτων | μαντευσόμεσθα τἀνδρὸς ὡς ὀλωλότος</quote> </cit>; here, however, it is impossible to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀθύμου δεσπότου</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠθροῦμεν</quote>, and to make <quote xml:lang="grc">κελεύσμασιν</quote> a mere epexegesis (‘looked, at our master's instigation, i.e., by his command’).</gloss>

data.quote:
σὲ δ’ ὅταν... | λόγος ἐκ Δαναῶν κακόθρους
									ἐπιβῇ

ἦ γὰρ τεκμηρίοισιν ἐξ οἰμωγμάτων |
									μαντευσόμεσθα τἀνδρὸς ὡς ὀλωλότος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἀθύμου_1219">. Only one correction is probable, viz. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξυθύμου_1219">. The decisive objection to this is the sense. It could mean only, ‘swift to wrath,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξύχολος</quote> (955). It could not mean merely, ‘agitated’ (by alarm). But Creon is no longer proud or fierce; he has been humbled: his late words (1211 ff.) expressed only grief and fear.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελεύμασιν_1219">, holding this to be the older Attic form (cr. n.). The fact appears to be that both <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλευμα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλευσμα</quote> are well attested in our MSS. of some authors: and there is no evidence from inscriptions. As regards the verb, Lobeck (on <ref n="Soph. Aj. 704"> <title>Ai.</title> 704</ref>) remarks that, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκελεύσθην</quote> is far commoner than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκελεύθην, κεκέλευμαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκέλευσμαι</quote> are both well attested for the best age. But Veitch's statement on this point is more accurate. While <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκελεύθην</quote> is extremely rare in classical Greek, <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκέλευμαι</quote> is nearly (if not quite) unknown to it. It would be very rash, then, to affirm that Soph. must have used the nonsigmatic form of the noun. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐχένος_1221">: the gen. of the part, as with verbs of seizing, etc.: <title>H.A.</title> 9. 50. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν κρεμάσωσι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ὗς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὀπισθίων ποδῶν</quote>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">κρεμῶμεν</quote> with gen., <title>Plut.</title> 312: <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.289"> <title>Il.</title> 17.289</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδὸς ἕλκε</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 3.439"> <title>Od.</title> 3.439</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῦν δ’ ἀγέτην κεράων</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μιτώδει_1222">, thread-like, i.e., formed by a thread-wrought fabric (the <quote xml:lang="grc">σινδών</quote>), and not, as usual, by a cord. <quote xml:lang="grc">μίτρος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ</quote>), the thread of the warp: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 817">Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 817</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕφηνα καὶ τόδ’ εἶδος εὐμίτοις πλοκαῖς</quote> </cit> (I wrought this scene, too, with threads deftly woven).—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>σινδόνος</emph>. σινδών</quote> (prob. from <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰνδ</quote>-, <title>Sind</title>) was a general term for a smooth, fine texture, as <quote xml:lang="grc">βύσσος</quote> was the specific word for a kind of fine linen: <ref n="Hdt. 2.86">Her. 2.86</ref> describes mummies as swathed <quote xml:lang="grc">σινδόνος βυσσίνης τελαμῶσι</quote> (where see Stein). <ref n="Thuc. 2.49">Thuc. 2.49</ref>. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν πάνυ λεπτῶν ἱματίων καὶ σινδόνων</quote>. Laert. <ref n="D. L. 6.90">Diog. 6. 90</ref> tells of an Athenian (c. 300 B.C.) being reprimanded by the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστυνόμοι</quote> for luxuriousness, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι σινδόνα ἠμφίεστο</quote>.—Antigone used her veil (<quote xml:lang="grc">κάλυμμα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 532">Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 532</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote: ὕφηνα καὶ τόδ’ εἶδος εὐμίτοις πλοκαῖς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφὶ_περιπετῆ_1223">), embracing her (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσκείμενον_1223">), where she hangs lifeless. But verses 1236—1240 require us to suppose that Antigone's body is then stretched on the ground. We are left to understand that Haemon, while uttering his lament (1224 f.), has lifted the corpse, so as to extricate it from the noose, and has laid it down. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1266"> <title>O. T.</title> 1266</ref> (where Oed. finds Iocasta hanging), <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλᾷ κρεμαστὴν ἀρτάνην.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσσῃ_1223">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1236">cp. 1236</ref>: fr. 235. 5 (iambics). Eur. has this form only in lyr.; Aesch. nowhere.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιπετῆ_1223">, act.; but pass. in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 907"> <title>Ai.</title> 907</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγχος πε, πετές</quote> </cit> (‘on which he fell’), unless I am right in suspecting that there we should read, <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’ ἔγχος περιπετοῦς κατηγορεῖ</quote>, ‘shows that he fell upon it.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1620"> <title>O. C.</title> 1620</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοισιν ἀμφικείμενοι</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνῆς_κάτω_1224">, his bride who is dead. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 831">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 831</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ μὲν εὐνάς</quote> </cit> (husbands), <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ δὲ παῖδας, | αἱ δὲ ματέρας γεραιάς</quote>. It would be awkward to understand, ‘the ruin of his marriage, (which is to be only) in the world below.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_ἔργα_1224">: he does not know that Creon is listening.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέχος_1224">, marriage, as in 573. This word, too, could mean ‘bride’ (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 211"> <title>Ai.</title> 211</ref>): it is v. 1224 that decides our version. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφε_1226">, Haemon: 44 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγνόν_1226">, bitter,—the notion of ‘sad,’ ‘gloomy,’ coming from that of ‘hateful’: cp. Moschus 3. 68 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ στυγνοὶ</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">tristes</foreign>) <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ σῶμα τεὸν κλαίουσιν Ἔρωτες</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷον_ἔργον_1228">: i.e., Haemon's forcible entrance into Antigone's tomb.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνα_ἔσχες_1228">; lit., ‘what thoughts hast thou conceived?’—the aor. meaning, as usu., not ‘had,’ but ‘came to have.’ So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1013"> <title>El.</title> 1013 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦν σχὲς...εἰκαθεῖν</quote> </cit>, ‘<emph>form</emph> the purpose to yield’: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1465"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1465</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦν ἔσχον, ὤστε συμφέρειν τοῖς κρείσσοσιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
νοῦν ἔσχον, ὤστε συμφέρειν τοῖς
									κρείσσοσιν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_συμφορᾶς_1228">, by what manner of calamity: i.e., ‘what cruel god hath deprived thee of thy reason?’ <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 314"> <title>Ai.</title> 314</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνήρετ’ ἐν τῷ πράγματος κυροῖ ποτε</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 174"> <title>Ph.</title> 174</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ παντί τῳ χρείας</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 1195">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 1195</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ δὲ κεῖσαι συμφορᾶς;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: κἀνήρετ’ ἐν τῷ πράγματος κυροῖ ποτε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διεφθάρης_1228">, mentally: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.128"> <title>Il.</title> 15.128</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαινόμενε, φρένας ἠλέ, διέφθορας</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 1192">Eur. <title>Helen</title> 1192</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διέφθαρσαι φρένας</quote> </cit>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱκέσιος_1230">, adverbial: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1215">cp. 1215</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠκεῖς</quote>, n. He extends his right hand in supplication.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄσσοισι_1230">: Aesch. admits <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄσσοις</quote>, and Eur. both <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄσσοις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄσσων</quote>, in iambics no less than in lyrics.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παπτήνας_1230">: with an acc. this verb usu.=‘to look around <emph>for</emph>,’ as <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.200"> <title>Il.</title> 4.200</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτύσας_προσώπῳ_1232">. Haemon is momentarily insane with despair and rage: the very words <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτῷ χολωθείς</quote>, 1235, indicate the transport of frenzy which these verses were meant to depict. Nothing could do more violence to the language, or more injury to the dramatic effect, than the Scholiast's theory that <quote xml:lang="grc">πτύσας προσώπῳ</quote> has a merely figurative sense, ‘with an expression of loathing on his face.’ When the figurative sense of a word (like <quote xml:lang="grc">πτύσας</quote>) is to be marked by a qualifying addition (like <quote xml:lang="grc">προσώπῳ</quote>), that addition must not be such as equally to suggest the literal sense. Thus a socialist riot might be called ‘a fire not of Hephaestus’ (<ref n="Eur. Orest. 621">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 621</ref>); but it would not be equally happy to describe it as ‘a fire kindled by the tables of the rich.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">πτύσας προσώπῳ</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιπτύσας προσώπῳ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πατρός</quote>), is merely an instance of the boldness with which poetry could use a simple dative to express the object to (or against) which an action is directed. Such a dat. is often equivalent to (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> with dat., (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί, πρός</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote>, with acc., —in various relations, and with various shades of meaning. Thus we have such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς γελῶν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Aj. 1042"> <title>Ai.</title> 1042</ref>)=<quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖς ἐπεγγελῶν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 67"> <title>Ph.</title> 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπην...Ἀργείοις βαλεῖς = ἐμβαλεῖς</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 322">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς κερτομοῦσι γοργὸν ὡς ἀναβλέπει</quote> </cit>, how she looks up sternly <emph>at</emph> her revilers: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 7.101"> <title>Il.</title> 7.101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷδε δ’ ἐγὼν αὐτὸς θωρήξομαι</quote> </cit>, against him: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.635"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 23. 635</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς μοι ἀνέστη</quote> </cit>, against me: and below <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1236">1236</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρεισε πλευραῖς = ἐπήρεισε</quote> </cit>. Prose would have <quote xml:lang="grc">πτύσας εἰς</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσωπον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλοῦς_ξίφους_1233">, his cross-hilted sword. <quote xml:lang="grc">κνώδοντες</quote> are the two projecting cross-pieces at the point where the hilt joins the blade. The hilt (<quote xml:lang="grc">κώπη</quote>) of the Greek sword had no guard, nor had it always the cross-pieces; but these, when used, served partly to protect the hand. The <quote xml:lang="grc">κνώδοντες</quote>, or cross-hilt, can be seen on some of the swords given by Guhl and Koner, p. 244, fig. 277 (<emph>a</emph>, <emph>d</emph>). The cross-hilt was sometimes simply a straight cross-bar; sometimes the side next the hand was rounded. Cp. <ref n="Sil. Ital. 1.515">Silius Italicus <title>Pun.</title> 1. 515</ref> <quote xml:lang="lat">pressumque ira simul exigit ensem, | Qua capuli <emph>statuere</emph> morae.</quote> —<quote xml:lang="grc">κνώδων</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κνάω, ὀδούς</quote>) meant properly any tooth-like prong or spike: see <title>Cyneg.</title> 10. 3, where boar-spears (<quote xml:lang="grc">προβόλια</quote>) have <quote xml:lang="grc">κνώδοντας ἀποκεχαλκευμένους στιφρούς</quote>, stout <emph>teeth</emph> forged of bronze, projecting from the shaft a little below the head (<quote xml:lang="grc">λόγχη</quote>). In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1025"> <title>Ai.</title> 1025</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’ αἰόλου κνώδοντος</quote>, ‘this gleaming spike,’ is the end of the sword-blade projecting through the body of Ajax. So in <ref>Kaibel <title>Epigr.</title> 549. 11</ref> (an epitaph of the 1st cent. A.D. ) <quote xml:lang="grc">φασγάνου κνώδοντι</quote>=‘with the point (not, ‘edge’) of the sword’: the ref. is to thrusting, not cutting.—The Scholiast wrongly explains </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλοῦς_κνώδοντας_1233"> by <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλᾶς ἀκμάς</quote>, ‘double edge.’ This interpretation was obviously suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">διπλοῦς</quote> (since a sword is often called <quote xml:lang="grc">δίστομον</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμφηκες</quote>), while the true sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">κνώδων</quote> was not accurately remembered: thus the Schol. vaguely calls it <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ὀξὺ τοῦ ξίφους</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ὁρμ_1233">, tmesis: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 427">cp. 427</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυγαῖσιν_1233">, dat. of manner (620 n.). The poet. plur. of <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγή</quote>, when it does not mean ‘remedies’ (364), usu. means ‘exile’ (<ref n="Eur. El. 233">Eur. <title>El.</title> 233</ref>). The gen. might be absol., but is more simply taken with <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤμπλακ’</quote> </emph>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥσπερ_εἶχ_1235">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1108">cp. 1108</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπενταθείς_1235">, lit., ‘stretched,’ or ‘strained,’ against the sword: i.e., pressing his right side against the point of the sword, which at the same time he drove home with his right hand.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλευραῖς_1235">, used as though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρεισε</quote> were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπήρεισε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 10">Pind. <title>P.</title> 10. 51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγκυραν ἔρεισον χθονί</quote>. For the verb cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 844">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 844</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ξίφος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόδος,...ἵν’ ἀνταίαν | ἐρείσω πλαγάν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσσον_1235">, predicative, denoting the point up to which he drove it in: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 899"> <title>Ai.</title> 899</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται κρυφαίῳ φασγάνῳ περιπτυχής.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγχος_1235"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξίφος</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 95"> <title>Ai.</title> 95</ref>, 658, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_ἀγκῶν_1235">, since <quote xml:lang="grc">π. προσπτύσσεται = παρθένον λαμβάνει</quote>: cp. the beautiful lines in <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1439">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1439</ref> (the dying Eteocles): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤκουσε μητρός, κἀπιθεὶς ὑγρὰν χέρα | φωνὴν μὲν οὐκ ἀφῆκεν, ὀμμάτων δ’ ἄπο | προσεῖπε δακρύοις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυσιῶν_1238">, breathing hard: <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥοήν</quote> is governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβάλλει</quote> only. But in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1389">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1389</ref> the compound governs the acc.: <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκφυσιῶν ὀξεῖαν αἵματος σφαγὴν | βάλλει μ’ ἐρεμνῇ ψακάδι φοινίας δρόσου.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥοὴν_1238"> is plainly right: the bad variant, <quote xml:lang="grc">πνοήν</quote>, was perh. suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">φυσιῶν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέλη_1241">, rites: <ref n="Soph. OC 1050"> <title>O. C.</title> 1050</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_Ἅιδου_1241">. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰν</quote> occurs nowhere else in tragic iambics, it might fairly be defended, in a <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥῆσις</quote> of epic colour, as a reminiscence of the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰν Ἀΐδαο</quote>. But I decidedly prefer Heath's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_γ_1241">' (‘in that world, though not in this’), because it adds point and pathos to what would otherwise be a somewhat tame statement of fact. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 750">Cp. 750</ref>. For another (probable) loss of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1241"> in this play, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 648">cp. 648</ref>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_γε_1241">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1380"> <title>O. T.</title> 1380</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν γε ταῖς Θήβαις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 153"> <title>O. C.</title> 153</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν γ’ ἐμοί</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 685"> <title>Ph.</title> 685</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσος ἔν γ’ ἴσοις</quote>: Eur. fr. 349 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔν γ’ ἐμοὶ κρίνοιτ’ ἂν οὐ καλῶς φρονεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείξας_ἀβουλίαν_1242">: for the constr., cp. n. on 883 f. For <quote xml:lang="grc">δεικνύναι</quote> said of a warning example, see <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1382"> <title>El.</title> 1382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δεῖξον ἀνθρώποισι τἀπιτίμια | τῆς δυσσεβείας οἷα δωροῦνται θεοί</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref> append. on 622 ff. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ δεῖξον ἀνθρώποισι τἀπιτίμια | τῆς
									δυσσεβείας οἷα δωροῦνται θεοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσκειται_1243">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 94">cp. 94</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἰκάς_1244">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>: what wouldst thou conjecture this to be (or, to mean)? The optat. ending used here was the usual one in Attic: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 843 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσθλὸν_κακόν_1244">: cp. on 40.— A silent exit is similarly a prelude to disaster in the case of Deianeira (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 813"> <title>Tr.</title> 813</ref>). Iocasta, too, quits the scene, not, indeed, without a word, yet with a reticence which is called <quote xml:lang="grc">σιωπή</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 1075). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεθάμβηκ_1246">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1140"> <title>O. C.</title> 1140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαυμάσας ἔχω</quote> </cit> (n.): and so oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθαύμακα.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βόσκομαι_1246">: cp. fr. 863 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπὶς γὰρ ἡ βόσκουσα τοὺς πολλοὺς βροτῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόους_ἀξιώσειν_1247">=to think them <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιοι</quote>, i.e., meet. This use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιόω</quote> is freq. in regard to persons, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1114"> <title>Ai.</title> 1114</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἠξίου τοὺς μηδένας</quote> </cit> (esteem them). On the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιος</quote>, as applied to actions, oft.=‘proper,’ ‘becoming’: as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 616">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 616</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν ἄρ’ ἄξιόν γε πᾶσίν ἐστιν ἐπολολύξαι</quote> </cit>. But, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιοῖ τινα</quote> could mean, ‘he thinks a person estimable,’ poetry, at least, could surely say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιοῖ τι</quote>, ‘he thinks a thing proper.’ The text, then, seems sound.</gloss>

data.quote:
νῦν ἄρ’ ἄξιόν γε πᾶσίν ἐστιν
									ἐπολολύξαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπὸ_στέγης_1247">: for the gen., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 692">cp. 692</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προθήσειν_1249"> governs <quote xml:lang="grc">πένθος</quote>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στένειν_1249"> is epexeg. (for them to mourn): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 216">cp. 216</ref> n. She will ‘set the grief before</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμης_ἄπειρος_1250">. The reading has been unjustly suspected. <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote>, ‘judgment,’ or ‘discretion,’ is here regarded as an influence moulding the character from without. The phrase means, then, ‘not uninformed by discretion,’—not unversed in its teachings. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 519B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 519B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἀπαιδεύτους καὶ ἀληθείας ἀπείρους</quote> </cit>, ‘uninformed by truth.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_οὖν_1251">: 688 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσεῖναι_1251">: so oft. of attendant circumstances (or of characteristic attributes): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 250"> <title>Tr.</title> 250</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ λόγου δ’ οὐ χρὴ φθόνον, | γύναι, προσεῖναι</quote> </cit>. —<ref n="Soph. Ant. 720">Cp. 720</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῦ λόγου δ’ οὐ χρὴ φθόνον, | γύναι,
									προσεῖναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσόμεσθα_καλύπτει_1253">, ‘we shall know (about our fear) lest (<quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>) she is concealing,’ i.e., whether we are right in fearing that she conceals something. As Goodwin says (<title>Moods and Tenses</title>, § 46, N. 5 <emph>a</emph>), this passage is one of the most favourable to the view that <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> has an <emph>interrogative</emph> force, and yet here also <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καλύπτει</quote> plainly expresses a fear. The pres. <emph>indic.</emph> is used, because the fear is strictly present; there is no thought that the thing feared can possibly be prevented. Before assuming that <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> could have the force of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ οὐ</quote> (‘whether not’), we should require an example in which the clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, after a verb like <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα</quote>, expressed something which is not <emph>feared</emph> (but hoped; or else regarded with neither fear nor hope). As if here we had, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσόμεσθα μὴ ζήσειν ἔτι μέλλει</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 278">Cp. 278</ref> n. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> in <emph>direct</emph> question (<ref n="Soph. OC 1502"> <title>O. C.</title> 1502</ref>) is, of course, elliptical: e.g., <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὕτως ἔχει</quote>; comes from (<quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὕτως ἔχει.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_1253"> (‘indeed’) goes with the whole phrase </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατάσχετον_καλύπτει_1253">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 770">cp. 770</ref>n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατάσχετον_1253">, a poet. word, here=‘repressed’ (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1011"> <title>El.</title> 1011</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάσχες ὀργήν</quote> </cit>): usu., ‘possessed’ (by a god, or by passion), like <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτοχος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμους_παραστείχοντες_1255">, advancing into the house: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1137">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1137</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρῆλθε νυμφικοὺς δόμους</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μήν_1257">: 526.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφήκει_1257">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 34"> <title>Ai.</title> 34</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρὸν δ’ ἐφήκεις</quote> </cit>.—The Messenger now goes into the palace. The same actor returns at 1277 as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξάγγελος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μνῆμ_1258">', as the epithet </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίσημον_1258"> shows, means that the son's corpse is a memorial of the father's unwisdom.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_χειρός_1258">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 916">cp. 916</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_εἰπεῖν_1259"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1556"> <title>O. C.</title> 1556</ref>), because it is a heavy charge against the King, that he has caused his son's death.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτην_1259">, in apposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">μνῆμα</quote>: the corpse is an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote>, because the death was caused by Creon's infatuation. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλοτρίαν_1259"> here answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκεῖος</quote> as=‘caused by oneself’ (cp. on 1187).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁμαρτών_1259"> is causal: he is bringing a corpse, not through the fault of others, but <emph>because</emph> he himself has erred. For the partic. in the nom., opposed to a clause of different form, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 23.156">Dem. or. 23 § 156</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶδεν, εἴτε δή τινος εἰπόντος εἴτ’ αὐτὸς συνείς</quote> </cit>. See also <ref n="Soph. Ant. 381">381 f.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
εἶδεν, εἴτε δή τινος εἰπόντος εἴτ’ αὐτὸς
									συνείς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενῶν_δυσφρόνων_1261">: 502 n. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 874">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 874</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ ἰὼ δύσφρονες</quote>, ‘misguided ones.’ More often, <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσφρων</quote> =‘gloomy,’ or ‘malignant.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στερεά_1261">, with ref. to his own <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθάδεια</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1028">cp. 1028</ref>. So <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Stat. 309B">Plat. <title>Polit.</title> 309B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ στερεὸν ἦθος</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 925"> <title>Ai.</title> 925</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμελλες χρόνῳ | στερεόφρων ἄρ’ ὦδ’ ἐξανύσειν κακὰν | μοῖραν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔμελλες χρόνῳ | στερεόφρων ἄρ’ ὦδ’ ἐξανύσειν
									κακὰν | μοῖραν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_βλέποντες_1263">. Like Antigone (937), Creon now calls the Theban Elders to witness. Cp. n. on 162—210.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτανόντας_1263"> refers to Creon himself (for the plur., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 10">cp. 10</ref>), as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντας_1263"> to Haemon: for the <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 336"> <title>Ph.</title> 336</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κτανών τε χὡ θανών.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμφυλίους_1263"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">συγγενεῖς</foreign>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1406"> <title>O. T.</title> 1406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμ’ ἐμφύλιον</quote> </cit> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1385"> <title>O. C.</title> 1385</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς ἐμφυλίου</quote> </cit>, ‘the land of thy race.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμῶν_βουλευμάτων_1265">, the unhappy (counsels) among my counsels (partitive gen.); i.e., the unhappiness involved in my counsels. <ref target="cl1209">See on 1209</ref>. This poetical periphrasis has the effect of making the idea expressed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνολβα_1265"> stand out with a quasi-substantival force, and so is slightly stronger than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι ἐμὰ ἄνολβα βουλεύματα</quote>. It would be possible, but it is neither requisite nor fitting, to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμαρτήματα</quote> (1261) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνολβα</quote>, placing only a comma at <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφυλίους</quote>.— For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνολβος</quote>, of folly, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1026">cp. 1026</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέος_μόρῳ_1266">, ‘young, and by an untimely death,’ is a pleonasm, but a natural one. The Schol. explains </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέῳ_1266"> by <quote xml:lang="grc">καινοπρεπεῖ</quote> (‘a death of a strange kind’). This sense is possible (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 712">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 712</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπροσδοκήτους τούσδε καὶ νέους λόγους</quote> </cit>), but is far less fitting here. <quote xml:lang="grc">νέῳ ξὺν μόρῳ</quote>, suggesting the thought that his years had been few, recalls Andromache's lament,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἆνερ, ἀπ’ αἰῶνος νέος ὤλεο</quote> (<ref n="Hom. Il. 24.725"> <title>Il.</title> 24.725</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπελύθης_1268">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1314">cp. 1314</ref>, where the midd. aor. has the same sense. In later Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολύεσθαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόλυσις</quote> came to be used of any ‘departure’: thus in Polyb. 3. 69 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἀποχώρησιν...ἐποιοῦντο</quote> is presently varied to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποιοῦντο τὴν ἀπόλυσιν</quote>. Here, however, the word has a distinctly poetical colour, and suggests the release of <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχή</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote>,—though without the feeling expressed by the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολύεις τὸν δοῦλόν σου...ἐν εἰρήνῃ</quote> (St Luke ii. 29). A fragment of Plutarch (Wyttenbach, p. 135) attests a familiar use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολύεσθαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόλυσις</quote> with reference to death. Eustathius quotes this v., and v. 1314, in support of a like statement (p. 548, 52). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμαῖς_σαῖσι_1269">. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> here= <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ οὐ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 492">cp. 492</ref>. The negative form would be <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐμαῖς ἀλλὰ σαῖς</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 1470"> <title>El.</title> 1470</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχω_μαθὼν_1272"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">μεμάθηκα</foreign> (21 n.), though here with a slightly stronger emphasis than that of an ordinary perf.: ‘I have fully learned.’—No change is required in 1273. The soundness of the metre is confirmed by the antistrophic verse (1296), which is free from suspicion. Construe: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δὲ θεὸς ἐν τῷ ἐμῷ κάρᾳ ἔπαισέ με, μέγα βάρος ἔχων</quote>. Three points claim notice. (1) The place of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_1272">. This was possible, because <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα βάρος</quote>, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote>, could have stood as an adverbial cognate acc.: hence <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote> is rather a superfluity than a word for which the ear was waiting. Greek poetry (esp. lyric) often has bold arrangements of words: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 944">cp. 944</ref>, 960 (n.). (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγα_ἔχων_1272"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">σφόδρα βαρὺς ὤν</foreign>. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 300">Cp. 300</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.249"> <title>Od.</title> 24.249</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆρας | λυγρὸν ἔχεις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.368"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1. 368</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβριν ἔχοντες</quote> </cit>. (3) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κάρᾳ_1272"> might have been followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνήλατο</quote>, or the like; but, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπαισε_1272"> being used, the enclitic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_1272_2"> was required to make it clear. The charge of redundancy would be just only if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῷ</quote> were followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote>.—For the image, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1345">cp. 1345</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1175">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1175</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων ὑπερβαρὴς ἐμπίτνων</quote> </cit>: and see <ref n="Soph. OT 263"> <title>O. T.</title> 263</ref>n. Triclinius understood the blow on the head to mean a disordering of the intellect (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τοῦ, ἐξέστησε τὰς ἐμὰς φρένας</quote>). But it is simply a poetical picture of the fell swoop with which the god descended on his victim,—taking possession of him, and driving him astray. Perhaps <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμβρόντητος</quote> helped to suggest the other view. For the form of the dat. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρᾳ_1272">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 564"> <title>O. C.</title> 564</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἔσεισεν_1272">, tmesis (420).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγρίαις_ὁδοῖς_1272">: cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title>P.</title> 2. 85</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἄλλοτε πατέων ὁδοῖς σκολιαῖς</quote>, in paths of guile. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λακπάτητον_1275">, proleptic (475). The form <quote xml:lang="grc">λαξπάτητον</quote>, which Eustathius treats as the normal one (adding, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τινες ..διὰ τοῦ κ γράφουσιν</quote>), is defended by Ellendt. He thinks that the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κ_1275"> form came from correctors who supposed that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξπ_1275"> was an impossible combination for Attic Greek. We find, indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑξπηχυστί</quote> Soph. fr. 938, and the ‘Attic’ forms <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕξπουν, ἕξκλινον, ἑξμέδμινον</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OT 1137"> <title>O. T.</title> 1137</ref> n.). But, though <quote xml:lang="grc">λαξπάτητον</quote> may well have been admissible, it is evident that the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κ_1275_2"> form would be recommended by ease of pronunciation. The compound occurs only here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντρέπων_1275">, as though it were an altar, a statue, or a fair building. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 383">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 383</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 539"> <title>Eum.</title> 539</ref>(quoted on 853 ff.).—For the apocopè of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά</quote> in comp., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1070"> <title>O. C.</title> 1070</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμβασις</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 528"> <title>Tr.</title> 528</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμμένει</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 839"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 839</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμμιγα</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 416"> <title>Ai.</title> 416</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμπνοάς</quote> </cit> (all lyr.). In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 396"> <title>Tr.</title> 396</ref> (dial.) Herm. conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀννεώσασθαι</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νεώσασθαι</quote>. It is unknown whether <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγχαζε</quote> (fr. 883) occurred in lyr. or in dial. Cp. <title>Introd. to Homer</title>, Appendix, p. 197. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_ὦ_1276">. The hiatus is excused by the pause.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνοι_δύσπονοι_1276">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 502">cp. 502</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_κεκτημένος_1278">. Creon is actually touching (or helping to support) his son's corpse (1258 <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χειρὸς ἔχων</quote>, 1297 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω μὲν ἐν χείρεσσιν</quote>). And meanwhile his wife lies dead within the house. The Messenger therefore says that Creon has come as one who both <emph>has in hand</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote>), and <emph>has in store</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κεκτημένος</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχων_1278"> is explained by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν πρὸ χειρῶν..φέρων</quote>, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκτημένος_1278"> by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἐν δόμοις</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Theaet. 197b">Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 197 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοίνυν μοι ταὐτὸν φαίνεται τῷ κεκτῆσθαι τὸ ἔχειν. οἶον εἰ ἱμάτιον πριάμενός τις καὶ ἐγκρατὴς ὢν μὴ φοροῖ, ἔχειν μὲν οὐκ ἂν αὐτὸν αὐτό, κεκτῆσθαι δέ γε φαῖμεν</quote> </cit>. So <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 198d"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 198 D</ref>; the chase after knowledge has a view either to (<emph>a</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κεκτῆσθαι</quote>, possession, or (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔχειν</quote>, holding, ready for use, that which is already possessed,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν ἐκέκτητο μὲν πάλαι, πρόχειρον δ’ οὐκ εἶχε τῇ διανοίᾳ</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 382b"> <title>Rep.</title> 382 B</ref> (men do not like) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ ψυχῇ περὶ τὰ ὄντα ψεύδεσθαί τε καὶ ἐψεῦσθαι καὶ ἀμαθῆ εἶναι καὶ ἐνταῦθα ἔχειν τε καὶ κεκτῆσθαι τὸ ψεῦδος</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ψεύδεσθαι</quote> answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν τὸ ψεῦδος</quote>,—to be deceived at a given time on a given matter; and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐψεῦσθαι</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκτῆσθαι τὸ ψεῦδος</quote>, —the settled incapacity for apprehending realities. In <ref n="Plat. Crat. 393a"> <title>Crat.</title> 393 A</ref> he says that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναξ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκτωρ</quote> mean the same thing; <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗ γὰρ ἄν τις ἄναξ ᾖ, .. δῆλον .. ὅτι κρατεῖ τε αὐτοῦ καὶ κέκτηται καὶ ἔχει αὐτό</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκτωρ</quote> has suggested both verbs).— The point of the phrase here is missed when it is taken as merely, ‘possessing sorrows in the fullest sense of possession.’ </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ τοίνυν μοι ταὐτὸν φαίνεται τῷ κεκτῆσθαι τὸ
									ἔχειν. οἶον εἰ ἱμάτιον πριάμενός τις καὶ ἐγκρατὴς ὢν μὴ φοροῖ,
									ἔχειν μὲν οὐκ ἂν αὐτὸν αὐτό, κεκτῆσθαι δέ γε φαῖμεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸ_χειρῶν_1279">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 1207">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1207</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴν πρὸ χειρῶν αἵδε σοι σκυλευμάτων | Φρυγίων φέρουσι κόσμον</quote> </cit> (they are carrying robes, <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1220"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1220</ref>). <title>I. A.</title> 36 <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτον τε γράφεις | τήνδ’ ἣν πρὸ χερῶν ἔτι βαστάζεις</quote>. Thus the phrase means merely, ‘visible in the hands,’ without implying that the hands are outstretched.</gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μὴν πρὸ χειρῶν αἵδε σοι σκυλευμάτων |
									Φρυγίων φέρουσι κόσμον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_1279">, with adverbial force, ‘yonder’: so 155, 386, 526, 626, 805, 868, 1257.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δόμοις_1279"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The regular constr. would have been, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔοικας ἥκειν ὡς ἔχων τε καὶ κεκτημένος,—τὰ μὲν πρὸ χειρῶν τάδε φέρων, τὰ δ’ ἐν δόμοις τάχ’ ὀψόμενος</quote>. The present form has arisen thus. (1) Since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_φέρων_1279"> interprets <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote>, the poet wished it to come immediately after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων τε καὶ κεκτημένος</quote>. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔοικας_ἥκειν_1279">, although thus postponed, ought still to have been followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ ὀψόμενος</quote>. But the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔοικας</quote> in the long sentence now prompted the change of <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ ὀψόμενος</quote> into </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ὄψεσθαι_1279">. The sentence, as it stands, would have seemed less boldly irregular to the Greek ear than it does to us, because Greek idiom so readily permitted the change of a second participial clause into a clause with a finite verb. (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 256">Cp. 256</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῆν</quote>: 816 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνησεν</quote>.) Thus there would be nothing unusual in the following:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκεις, τὰ μὲν φέρων, τὰ δὲ ἔοικας τάχα ὄψεσθαι</quote>. Here, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκεις</quote>, we have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔοικας ἥκειν</quote>, and the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔοικας</quote> has led to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκειν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψεσθαι</quote> being linked by <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δόμοις_1279_2"> is governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψεσθαι</quote> only, the words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκειν_καὶ_1279"> form a parenthesis, being equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκων</quote>. This is a rare constr., and alleged examples should be scrutinised before acceptance (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 537">cp. 537</ref> n.); but there are some undoubted instances. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 7.3.7">Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 7.3.7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς τοὺς περὶ Ἀρχίαν</quote> </cit>...(<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ ψῆφον ἀνεμείνατε, ἀλλὰ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁπότε πρῶτον ἐδυνάσθητε ἐτιμωρήσασθε</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 6.68.3">Thuc. 6.68 § 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἧς</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κρατεῖν δεῖ ἢ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ῥᾳδίως ἀποχωρεῖν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 934E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 934E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδασκέτω</quote> </cit> (<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μανθανέτω</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν .. ἀμφισβητοῦντα</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 6.33">[Lys. ] <title>In Andoc.</title> § 33</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιτιμᾷ (καὶ ἀποδοκιμάζεἰ τῶν ἀρχόντων τισί</quote> </cit>. <title>Anlhol. P.</title> 7. 664 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρχἱλοχον</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τάχα_1279"> as=‘full soon,’ and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψεσθαι</quote> as depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκειν</quote>: ‘thou seemest to have come <emph>in order to</emph> see full soon,’ etc. This final inf. is tenable (<ref n="Soph. OT 198"> <title>O. T.</title> 198</ref> n.). But I know no example of <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τάχα</quote> as=‘full soon,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μάλα, καὶ λίαν</quote>, etc. And, even if it were possible, it would here be weak.—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστιν_1281">. In order to form a judgment on this difficult verse, a careful scrutiny of Sophoclean usage is required. (1) The reading closest to the MSS. would be, <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν <emph>αὖ</emph>; κάκιον <emph>ἦ</emph> κακῶν ἔτι</quote>; This involves merely a change of punctuation, and of accent (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote>). But it suggests these difficulties. (<emph>a</emph>) The interrogative </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_1281"> occurs about 50 times in Soph. : and in every instance it is the first word of the interrogative clause. Only a vocative sometimes precedes it, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1102"> <title>O. C.</title> 1102</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τέκνον, ἦ πάρεστον</quote> </cit>; so <ref n="Soph. OC 863"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 863</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 369."> <title>Ph.</title> 369.</ref>Eur. , indeed, does not always observe this rule: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 967"> <title>El.</title> 967</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτα δρῶμεν; μητέρ’ ἦ φονεύσομεν</quote> </cit>; In <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1013">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1013</ref> I should point thus, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ δῆτα; πέπλων ἐντὸς ἦ κρύψασ’ ἔχεις</quote>; (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ</quote> Valckenaer for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ</quote>). But, if we read <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιον ἦ κακῶν ἔτι</quote> here, it would be a solitary departure from the practice of Soph. , as seen in fifty other examples. (<emph>b</emph>) The formula <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστι</quote> (cp. on v. 20) occurs 21 times in Soph. (including <ref n="Soph. Phil. 733">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 733</ref>, where the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ἔστι</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’</quote>) as a question complete in itself. But there is not one instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν αὖ</quote>; which is, indeed, illsuited to the rhythm of the tragic senarius.</gloss>

data.quote: τί δῆτα δρῶμεν; μητέρ’ ἦ φονεύσομεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_1281"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤ_1281">, we could read, <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν; <emph>ἢ</emph> </quote> [or better, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_1281_2">] <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιον <emph>αὖ</emph> κακῶν ἔτι</quote>; But: (<emph>a</emph>) if this had been the original order, it is most improbable that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖ</quote> would have changed places. The sense would have been perfectly clear, whereas with <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖ...ἤ</quote> (the order in the MSS.) it is obscure. (<emph>b</emph>) The prominent place of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὖ</quote> in the MSS. is confirmed by many like instances: e.g. 1172: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1500"> <title>O. C.</title> 1500</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς αὖ παρ’ ὑμῶν κοινὸς ἠχεῖται χτύπος</quote> </cit>; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1089"> <title>Ph.</title> 1089</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίπτ’ αὖ μοι τὸ κατ’ ἆμαρ | ἔσται;</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1263"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1263</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς αὖ παρ’ ἄντροις θόρυβος ἵσταται βοῆς;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: τίς αὖ παρ’ ὑμῶν κοινὸς ἠχεῖται χτύπος

τίς αὖ παρ’ ἄντροις θόρυβος ἵσταται
									βοῆς;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1281">, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 28"> <title>Tr.</title> 28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί τιν’ ἐκ φόβου φόβον τρέφω</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 19.290"> <title>Il.</title> 19.290</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤς μοι δέχεται κακὸν ἐκ κακοῦ αἰεί</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 371">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 371</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐκ γἀρ ἄλγους ἄλγος αὖ σέ δέρκομαι | ...ἔχουσαν</quote> </cit>. On the grounds stated above, I prefer this</gloss>

data.quote: ἀεί τιν’ ἐκ φόβου φόβον τρέφω
ὤς μοι δέχεται κακὸν ἐκ κακοῦ αἰεί

ἀλλ’ ἐκ γἀρ ἄλγους ἄλγος αὖ σέ δέρκομαι |
									...ἔχουσαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάκιον_1281"> means merely that the sum of his misery will be greater: not that he can conceive a calamity sorer than his son's death. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1364"> <title>O. T.</title> 1364</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τι πρεσβύτερον ἔτι κακοῦ κακόν, | τοῦτ’ ἔλαχ’ Οἰδίπους</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παμμήτωρ_1282">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ κατὰ πάντα μήτηρ</quote>: true mother; whose grief for her son would not suffer her to survive him; and whose act shows the same passionate temperament as his. Contrast <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ ἀμήτωρ</quote> (<ref n="Soph. El. 1154"> <title>El.</title> 1154</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc">παμμήτωρ</quote> usu. =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πάντων μήτηρ</quote> (n. on 338). Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">παμβασιλεία</quote> as=‘monarchy in the fullest sense’ (n. on 737).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεοτόμ_1282">.: adj. compounded with a word cognate in sense to the subst.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 7">cp. 7</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρτι_1282"> (‘a moment ago’) gives precision to the less definite <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοτόμοισι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1130"> <title>Tr.</title> 1130</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκεν ἀρτίως νεοσφαγής</quote> </cit> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 898"> <title>Ai.</title> 898</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 792E">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 792E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀρτίως νεογενῆ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσκάθαρτος_λιμήν_1284"> (nom. for voc., 1211). The ‘haven’ or ‘receptacle’ of Hades,—that nether world in which he receives the dead (810, 893)—is ‘hard to be appeased,’ in the sense that Hades is ever demanding fresh victims. The life of Haemon has already been exacted by Hades as a penalty for the offence of Creon against the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέρτεροι</quote>. But even this atonement (<quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρμός</quote>) has not proved enough. <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσκάθαρτος</quote> is used here as if one could say <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαίρω</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱλάσκομαι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">θεόν</quote>: but that constr. does not occur. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 466"> <title>O. C.</title> 466</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρμὸν...δαιμόνων</quote> </cit> (n.), such an atonement as belongs (is due) to them. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 364E">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 364E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσεις τε καὶ καθαρμοὶ ἀδικημάτων</quote> </cit>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιμήν_1284"> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1000">cp. 1000</ref>: <title>Anth. P.</title> 7. 452 <quote xml:lang="grc">μνήμονες Εὐβούλοιο σαόφρονος, ὦ παριόντες, | πίνωμεν· κοινὸς πᾶσι λιμὴν Ἀτδης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακάγγελτα_1286"> is equiv. to two distinct epithets, <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγελλόμενα</quote>, so that the whole phrase=‘tidings of dire woes.’ <ref n="Soph. Ant. 146">Cp. 146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικρατεῖς λόγχας</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπέμψας_1286">, said to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξάγγελος</quote>, as the <emph>herald</emph> of the tidings. This use of <quote xml:lang="grc">προπέμπω</quote> comes from its sense of ‘escorting’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 1667"> <title>O. C.</title> 1667</ref>): we should not compare <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1155"> <title>El.</title> 1155</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φήμας λάθρᾳ προὔπεμπες ὡς φανούμενος | τιμωρὸς αὐτός</quote> </cit> (‘didst send forth,’ from thy secret place of exile); nor, again, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1205"> <title>Ph.</title> 1205</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βελέων τι προπέμψατε</quote> (‘produce,’ ‘furnish’): but rather <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1265"> <title>Ph.</title> 1265</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μῶν τί μοι vέα | πάρεστε πρὸς κακοῖσι πέμποντες κακά;</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
φήμας λάθρᾳ προὔπεμπες ὡς φανούμενος | τιμωρὸς
									αὐτός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεξειργάσω_1288">: <ref target="cl1030">see on 1030</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_παῖ_1289">, said to the Messenger. It has been objected that, at such a time, Creon could not use those words except with reference to Haemon (as in 1266, 1340). From a modern literary point of view, the objection is just. But we should remember how very familiar <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παῖ</quote> actually was as a mode of address, whether by elders to juniors, or by masters to slaves. Here it is used, not as to a slave, but merely as to a younger man; there is in it a certain pathetic appeal for sympathy. (Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παῖ, ὦ τέκνον</quote>, as said by the Messenger to Oed. in <ref n="Soph. OT 1008"> <title>O. T.</title> 1008</ref>, 1030.) Enger's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὢ τίν’</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνα_ὀλέθρῳ_1289">, ‘what new death,’—the bloody death of a woman,—dost thou describe as heaped on destruction (i.e., superadded to Haemon's death), for my sorrow (<quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote>)?' (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 595">Cp. 595</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πήματα φθιτῶν ἐπὶ πήμασι πίπτοντ’</quote>.) <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>γυναικεῖον</emph> = γυναικός</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 8">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόστῳ τῷ βασιλείῳ</quote> </cit>.—The notion expressed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφικεῖσθαι ἐπ’ ὀλέθρῳ</quote> seems to be, strictly, that of death entwined with death, like corpse embracing corpse (1240). The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφικεῖσθαι</quote> prop.=‘to be set around’ (as a wall round a city). Perhaps the bold phrase here was partly prompted by the fact that persons embracing each other could be described (<ref n="Soph. OC 1620"> <title>O. C.</title> 1620</ref> n.) as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἀλλήλοισιν ἀμφικείμενοι</quote>. I prefer this view.</gloss>
lemma:
τίνα νέον σφάγιον γυναικεῖον μόρον λέγεις
									ἀμφικεῖσθαί μοι ἐπ’ ὀλέθρῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶν_πάρεστιν_1294">. The corpse of Eurydicè, and probably also the altar at which she fell (1301), are now shown to the spectators by means of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκύκλημα</quote>. The precise mechanism of this contrivance is unknown; but the texts leave no doubt as to its general nature. It was a small stage, with space enough for</gloss>
