<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χείρεσσιν_1297"> (976), though in an iambic verse, is excused by the lyric character of the whole <quote xml:lang="grc">κομμός</quote>. Eur. once admits it in dial., <ref n="Eur. Alc. 756">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 756</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτῆρα δ’ ἐν χείρεσσι κίσσινον λαβών</quote>, where Monk needlessly proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτήριον δ’ ἐν χερσί</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔναντα_1298">: an epic form, sometimes admitted in Attic poetry. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1478">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1478</ref> (lyr.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔναντα δ’ ἦλθεν | Πυλάδης</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 342">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 342</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ καὶ πεποιθὼς ἀξιοῖς ἐμοῦ λέγειν ἔναντα</quote> </cit>; Triclinius gave here the Hellenistic form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔναντι</quote> (St Luke i. 8), which seems to be confined to the LXX., Apocrypha, and N.T.; see n. by Moulton in his ed. of Winer's Grammar, p. 591 (8th Engl. ed.). </gloss>

data.quote:
τῷ καὶ πεποιθὼς ἀξιοῖς ἐμοῦ λέγειν
									ἔναντα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδ_1301">: he indicates the dead body of Eurydicè, now made visible by the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκύκλημα</quote>.—Arndt's first emendation is given in the text. His later substitution of <quote xml:lang="grc">πτώσιμος</quote> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμία_1301"> was not an instance of second thoughts being wiser. The altar meant is that of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Ἑρκεῖος</quote> in the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote> of the house (487). The objection made to <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμία</quote> here is to the effect that one could say <quote xml:lang="grc">βώμιος ἐφέζεται</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵσταται</quote>, but not <quote xml:lang="grc">βώμιος ποιεῖ τι</quote>: i.e. that the verb must refer directly to the assuming of the position denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">βώμιος</quote>. It is quite true that this is usually the case; <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 93">Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 93</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμίαν ἐφημένην</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 32"> <title>O. T.</title> 32</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑζόμεσθ’ ἐφέστιοι</quote> </cit>: and cp. above, 785 f. But here </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμία_1301_2"> is not merely an adverbial word, to be taken closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει</quote>. It is rather an instance of an adj. used with the force of a participle, and virtually equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμία στᾶσα</quote>: i.e. it means, ‘having taken her place at the altar,’ she slew herself. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 83"> <title>O. C.</title> 83</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐμοῦ μόνης πέλας</quote> </cit> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔσης</quote>): and see above on 471. Further: even if it were necessary to bind <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμία</quote> closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει</quote>, it would be bold to say that poetry could not permit this slight modification in the ordinary use of the word, when we remember how free was the adverbial use of adjectives in poetry (e.g., <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 217"> <title>Ai.</title> 217</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νύκτερος Αἴας ἀπελωβήθη</quote> </cit>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξίφει_1301">. A sacrificial knife, which lay on the altar. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 74">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 74</ref> (Death speaks) <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχω δ’ ἐπ’ αὐτήν, ὡς κατάρξωμαι ξίφει</quote>. For the prep. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 828"> <title>Ai.</title> 828</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπτῶτα τῷδε περὶ νεορράντῳ ξίφει</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδ_πέρι_1301">. In favour of Arndt's we may observe:—(<emph>a</emph>) the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">πέριξ</quote> (a word not used by Soph. , and nowhere common) was not likely to have originated from <quote xml:lang="grc">πέρι</quote> alone: whereas it could easily arise from <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ ξίφει</quote>, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ίφει</quote> had been blotted or lost. (<emph>b</emph>) The MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δὲ</quote>) is just the kind of feeble make-shift which is sometimes found in the MSS., where a verse had come down in a mutilated state: see, e.g., on <ref n="Soph. OT 943"> <title>O. T.</title> 943</ref> f., 1264 f.—For other conjectures, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύει_βλέφαρα_1302">, allows her eyes to close in darkness. <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει</quote>=relaxes: the eyelids are deprived of power to remain open. The phrase has been suggested by the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">λῦσε δὲ γυῖα, λέλυντο δὲ γυῖα</quote>, etc., and seems quite intelligible; though, doubtless, it would have been more natural to say <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῄει</quote>, as Soph. has done in fr. 640, <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέφαρα κέκλῃται</quote>. In <ref n="Eur. Rh. 8">[Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 8</ref> we have <quote xml:lang="grc">λῦσον βλεφάρων γοργωπὸν ἔδραν</quote>, of <emph>opening</emph> the eyes; but that has no bearing on the different use here. Wolff brings what at first sight is a perfect parallel: <title>Anthol. P.</title> 3. 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνθ’ ὧν ὄμματ’ ἔλυσε τὰ Γοργόνος ἐνθάδε Περσεύς</quote>. But unfortunately neither he nor Bellermann has observed the meaning. It is not, ‘<emph>caused</emph> the Gorgon 's eyes <emph>to close</emph>,’ but ‘<emph>uncovered</emph> the Gorgon 's head.’ The epigram refers to Perseus bringing Medusa's head to Seriphos, and therewith petrifying Polydectes, who had married Danaë, and sent her son on his perilous mission.—The objection to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μύει_1302"> is that elsewhere the verb has these usages:—(1) intrans.,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμματα μύει</quote>, the eyes close, or <quote xml:lang="grc">μύομεν</quote>, we shut our eyes. (2) trans., as <title>Anth. P.</title> 9. 558 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνος ἔμυσε κόρας</quote> (with the post-classical <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—</quote>), ‘caused to close.’ That is, there is no classical example of such a phrase as <quote xml:lang="grc">μύει ὀφθαλμούς</quote>, she shuts her eyes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μεγαρέως_1303">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 474">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 474</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Μεγαρεύς, Κρέοντος σπέρμα, τοῦ σπαρτῶν γένους</quote> </cit>, where he is one of the Theban warriors who guard the gates: his patriotic death is foreshadowed <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 477"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 477</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανὼν τροφεῖα πληρώσει χθονί</quote> </cit>. The story is thus told by Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 930">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 930</ref>—1018), who calls him Menoeceus. While the Argives are pressing Thebes, Creon and Eteocles send for Teiresias. The seer says that Ares is wroth, because Cadmus of old slew the god's offspring, a dragon (or serpent?) which had its lair outside the walls. One of the Cadmean race, sprung from the dragon's teeth, must die to appease him. Now, Creon and his two sons are the only pure-bred <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαρτοί</quote> left. And Haemon is married. The seer therefore suggests that Menoeceus should die. Menoeceus pretends that he means to fly to Delphi. Creon leaves the scene, in order to provide him with money for the journey. Menoeceus then rushes to the top of a tower on the walls, where he cuts his throat, and falls into the dragon's former den (<quote xml:lang="grc">σηκὸν ἐς μελαμβαθῆ | δράκοντος</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1010"> <title>Ph.</title> 1010</ref>, see n. above on 411). Statius, who also calls him Menoeceus, tells the story in <ref n="Stat. Theb. 10.589"> <title>Theb.</title> 10. 589</ref>—782, and, like Eur. , makes the son practise a pious fraud in order to hinder his father from preventing the sacrifice.</gloss>

data.quote:
Μεγαρεύς, Κρέοντος σπέρμα, τοῦ σπαρτῶν
									γένους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλεινὸν_λάχος_1303">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1013">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1013</ref>, where he says, <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχω δὲ θανάτου δῶρον οὐκ αἰσχρὸν πόλει | δώσων, νόσου δὲ τήνδ’ ἀπαλλάξω χθόνα</quote>. Statius <ref n="Stat. Theb. 10.670"> <title>Th.</title> 10. 670</ref> where Virtus says to Menoeceus, <quote xml:lang="lat">rape nobile fatum.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάχος_1303"> is freq. in poetry, and is used by Xenophon. The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέχος_1303"> would be forced as an allusion to the dragon's den (<quote xml:lang="grc">θαλάμαι</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 931">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 931</ref>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">σηκός</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1010"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1010</ref>) into which the corpse fell. And it could not here be a general word for ‘grave.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὰς_πράξεις_1304"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπραξίας</quote>. A solitary instance of the plur. in this sense; as, conversely, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 879"> <title>Tr.</title> 879</ref> is the only instance of the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξις</quote> as=‘mode of doing,’ instead of ‘fortune’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 560"> <title>O. C.</title> 560</ref> n.). In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 1305">Eur. <title>El.</title> 1305</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιναὶ πράξεις, κοινοὶ δὲ πότμοι</quote> </cit>, the sense is, ‘actions.’ But the peculiarity here does not warrant a suspicion (see cr. n.). It is equally exceptional, the other way, when <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσειν καλῶς</quote> means ‘to act well’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 1764"> <title>O. C.</title> 1764</ref> n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφυμνής_1304"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπαρασαμένη</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 658">cp. 658</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέπταν_1307">, aor. referring to a moment just past; we should use the pres.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1466"> <title>O. C.</title> 1466</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπτηξα θυμόν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 693"> <title>Ai.</title> 693</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφριξ’ ἔρωτι περιχαρὴς δ’ ἀνεπτάμην</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 337"> <title>O. T.</title> 337</ref> n. The act. aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπτην</quote> is once used in lyrics by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 555">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 555</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέπτα</quote>), and once by Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 440">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 440</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέπτα</quote>). Eur. Itis a poetical form, but occurs in late prose (Arrian, Lucian, etc.).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόβῳ_1307">, with fear of the curses invoked by Eurydicè.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνταίαν_1307">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγήν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. OC 544"> <title>O. C.</title> 544</ref> n.), a blow which strikes one full on the breast: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 195"> <title>El.</title> 195</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παγχάλκων ἀνταία | γενύων ὡρμάθη πλαγά</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 844">Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 844</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἀνταίαν | ἐρείσω πλαγάν</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">διανταία</quote> =a thrust which passes through the body: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 639">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 639</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξίφος | διανταίαν ὀξυπευκὲς οὐτᾷ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείλαιος_1310">, but in 1311 <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλαίᾳ</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">αι—</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 442"> <title>O. C.</title> 442</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τοῦ πατρὸς τῷ πατρί</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 883"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐχ ὕβρις τάδ’; ὕβρις</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 296"> <title>Ph.</title> 296</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐν πέτροισι πέτρον</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 827"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 827</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπν’...ὕπνε</quote> </cit> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—</quote> in the first place, but <quote xml:lang="grc">υ^</quote> in the second): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 148"> <title>El.</title> 148</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ Ἴτυ^ν, αἰὲν</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αι_1310"> before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ο_1310">: 827 <quote xml:lang="grc">πετραία</quote>: 1131 <quote xml:lang="grc">Νυσαίων</quote>: 1140 <quote xml:lang="grc">βιαίας</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 20.379"> <title>Od.</title> 20.379</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπαιον</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Tyrt. 10. 20">Tyrt. 10. 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γεραιούς</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 385">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 385</ref> (lyr.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκταίου</quote> (Dind. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκτίου</quote>): <ref n="Eur. El. 497">Eur. <title>El.</title> 497</ref> (dial.) <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιόν</quote>. For the repetition <ref n="Soph. Ant. 379">cp. 379</ref>, 977.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκέκραμαι_1310">, ‘blended with’ anguish, i.e. steeped in it: (Whitelaw: ‘Fulfilled with sorrow, and made one with grief.’) Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 895"> <title>Ai.</title> 895</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκτῳ τῷδε συγκεκραμένην</quote> </cit>, ‘her soul is steeped in the anguish of that wail’: <title>Plut.</title> 853 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω πολυφόρῳ συγκέκραμαι δαίμονι</quote>, where the words just before, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀπόλωλα δείλαι^ος</quote> (850), might suggest that the parody glanced at our passage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔχων_1312">, as being responsible for,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς αἴτιος ὤν</quote>. So <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 579">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 579</ref> Apollo, defending the accused Orestes, says, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτίαν δ’ ἔχω | τῆς τοῦδε μητρὸς τοῦ φόνου</quote>, I am responsible for (not, ‘am accused of’) the deed. In this sense of the phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω = παρέχω</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.41">Thuc. 2.41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγανάκτησιν ἔχει</quote> </cit>, gives cause of resentment; id. 2. 61 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει αἴσθησιν</quote>, makes itself felt. But in prose <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτίαν ἔχω</quote> usu.=‘to bear the <emph>blame</emph>’ for a thing, i.e. to <emph>be held</emph> responsible for it: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.70">Her. 5.70</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶχον αἰτίην τοῦ φόνου τούτου</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Apol. 38c">Plat. <title>Apol.</title> 38 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα ἕξετε καὶ αἰτίαν...ὡς Σωκράτη ἀπεκτόνατε.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄνομα ἕξετε καὶ αἰτίαν...ὡς Σωκράτη
									ἀπεκτόνατε.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_μόρων_1312">, that of Haemon: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκείνων_1312">, that of Megareus (1303 f.). For the plur., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 205"> <title>El.</title> 205</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐμὸς ἴδε πατὴρ | θανάτους αἰκεῖς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: τοὺς ἐμὸς ἴδε πατὴρ | θανάτους αἰκεῖς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεσκήπτου_1312">, wast denounced. In Attic law <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισκήπτομαί τινι</quote> (midd.) meant, to take proceedings against a witness for perjury (<quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδομαρτυριῶν</quote>): Isae. or. 5 § 9 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἐπεξελθεῖν οἶς ἐπεσκήψατο τῶν μαρρύρων</quote>. The rare pass. occurs in <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 937B">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 937B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν δούλη ἐπισκηφθῇ τὰ ψευδῆ μαρτυρῆσαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐὰν δούλη ἐπισκηφθῇ τὰ ψευδῆ
									μαρτυρῆσαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπελύσατ_1314">, quitted life: <ref target="cl1268">see on 1268</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπελύθης</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_1314">, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 772">cp. 772</ref> n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_φοναῖς_1314">: 696 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφ_ἦπαρ_1315">, expressing movement, ‘home to’ it: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 930"> <title>Tr.</title> 930</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶμεν αὐτὴν ἀμφιπλῆγι φασγάνῳ | πλευρὰν ὑφ’ ἧπαρ καὶ φρένας πεπληγμένην</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1063">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1063</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παίσας πρὸς ἧπαρ φασγάνῳ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὁρῶμεν αὐτὴν ἀμφιπλῆγι φασγάνῳ | πλευρὰν ὑφ’
									ἧπαρ καὶ φρένας πεπληγμένην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξυκώκυτον_1315">, by the household (<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1079">cp. 1079</ref>): she herself heard the news in silence (1256). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_βροτῶν_1317"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁρμόσει_ποτέ_1317">, the guilt can never fit (=be fixed upon) another man, <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἐμᾶς αἰτίας</quote>,</emph> (being transferred) from my responsibility, —i.e., so as to leave me blameless. For the intrans. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁρμόσει_1317">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 902"> <title>O. T.</title> 902</ref> (n.), <ref n="Soph. El. 1293"> <title>El.</title> 1293</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1317"> here is not for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote>, but is used as if we had, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔποτε ἐξ ὑπαιτίου ἀναίτιος φανοῦμαι</quote> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 284"> <title>Tr.</title> 284</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὀλβίων ἄζηλον εὑροῦσαι βίον</quote> </cit>). Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἐμᾶς αἰτίας</quote> is really a compressed way of saying, ‘by change from a state of things in which the <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτία</quote> (blame) was mine.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλεος_1319">: for the nom., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1211">cp. 1211</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάμ_ἔτυμον_1319">, i.e., this is the simple truth: I was virtually, though not actually, his slayer. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅ_τάχιστ_1322">. This (Erfurdt's) emendation seems the simplest and best cure for the metre (see cr. n.). It is worth noticing that Soph. has this phrase in a closely similar passage, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1340"> <title>O. T.</title> 1340</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγετ’ ἐκτόπιον ὅ τι τάχιστά με</quote> </cit>. He has <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον τάχος</quote> thrice, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τάχος</quote> eight times, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι τάχος</quote> nowhere else. </gloss>

data.quote: ἀπάγετ’ ἐκτόπιον ὅ τι τάχιστά με

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_μηδένα_1325">, one who exists no more than a nonentity. In <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδένα, μή</quote> has its generic force: one who is such as to be a mere cipher. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1114"> <title>Ai.</title> 1114</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἠξίου τοὺς μηδένας</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1019"> <title>O. T.</title> 1019</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πῶς ὁ φύσας ἐξ ἴσου τῷ μηδενί</quote> </cit>; (dat. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μηδείς</quote>,—he who is <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδείς</quote> in respect to consanguinity). Here <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μηδέν</quote> would have been equally fitting: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1231"> <title>Ai.</title> 1231</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτ’ οὐδὲν ὢν τοῦ μηδὲν</quote> </cit> (the dead) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντέστης ὔπερ</quote>.—Postgate suggests (<title>Trans. Cambridge Phil. Soc.</title>, 1886, p. 58) that this use of the oblique cases of <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδείς</quote> in sing., and of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδείς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδείς</quote> in plur., may have come from an attraction of the neuter by the masc. article: e.g., <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μηδένας</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μηδέν</quote>. We do not find <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μηδείς</quote>. When it became declinable, the phrase could dispense with the article; e.g., <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μηδέν</quote> could be simply <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδένα</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: καὶ πῶς ὁ φύσας ἐξ ἴσου τῷ μηδενί

lemma: τὸν οὐκ ὄντα μᾶλλον ἢ μηδένα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέρδη_1326">: the plur. more often refers to money (1061); but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 767"> <title>El.</title> 767</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ δεινὰ μέν, κέρδη δέ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_κράτιστά_1326"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote>) </gloss>
lemma: τὰ γὰρ ἐν ποσὶ κακὰ κράτιστά

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βράχιστα_1326"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>): instead of, <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτιστόν ἐστι τὰ...κακὰ βράχιστα εἶναι</quote>. For the personal constr., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1368"> <title>O. T.</title> 1368</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρείσσων γὰρ ἦσθα μηκέτ’ ὢν ἢ ζῶν τυφλός</quote> </cit>, and n. <ref n="Soph. OT 1061"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1061</ref>. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote>, cp. the oracle <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κίνει Καμάριναν· ἀκίνητος γὰρ ἀμείνων</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσα</quote>), <emph>ap.</emph> Stephanus Byz. <emph>s. v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">Καμἁρινα.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
κρείσσων γὰρ ἦσθα μηκέτ’ ὢν ἢ ζῶν
									τυφλός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀν_ποσίν_1326">, before our feet, claiming immediate attention. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 739">Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 739</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμεῖς δέ, τοὐν ποσὶν γὰρ οἰστέον κακόν, | στείχωμεν, ὡς ἂν ἐν πυρᾷ θῶμεν νεκρόν</quote> </cit>. So <ref n="Pind. P. 8">Pind. <title>P.</title> 8. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ἐν ποσί μοι τράχον</quote>, my present theme. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡμεῖς δέ, τοὐν ποσὶν γὰρ οἰστέον κακόν, |
									στείχωμεν, ὡς ἂν ἐν πυρᾷ θῶμεν νεκρόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόρων_ἔχων_1329">. I have adopted <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote>, a conjecture of Pallis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῶν</quote>, on the following grounds. (1) The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρων ἐμῶν</quote> could mean nothing but, ‘of all fates possible for me.’ This, however, is most strange. In 1313 <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρων</quote> meant ‘violent deaths’: so <ref n="Aesch. Seven 420">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 420</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱματῃφόρους μόρους</quote>. Hence it has been proposed to render <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρων ἐμῶν</quote> here, (<emph>a</emph>) ‘the deaths caused by me’: as Hermann, ‘veniat caedium per me factarum suprema, exoptatissime mihi ultimum diem adducens.’ (<emph>b</emph>) Figuratively, ‘the many deaths that I have died’; <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1288">cp. 1288</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλωλότ’ ἄνδρ’ ἐπεξειργάσω</quote>. But neither version is tolerable. (2) Triclinius proposed to make <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῶν</quote> fem., and to take it with <quote xml:lang="grc">τερμίαν</quote>: when it would at least be necessary to write <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμᾶν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμερᾶν</quote>). But, either with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῶν</quote> or with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμᾶν</quote>, the relation of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κάλλιστ’ ...ἄγων</quote> to the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρων</quote> is exceedingly awkward. ‘That one among fates which <emph>best</emph> brings my last day,’ cannot be explained as an equivalent for, ‘that best of fates which brings it’; i.e., for <quote xml:lang="grc">μόρων ὁ κάλλιστος, ὁ...ἄγων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπατος_1332">, an emphatic repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κάλλιστ’ ἔχων</quote>,—‘supreme of fates,’— far best. It has been usual to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατος</quote> here as ‘last.’ But neither <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατος</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρτατος</quote> ever bears that sense in classical Greek. Pindar often uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατος</quote> as ‘best,’ but never as ‘last’: <ref n="Pind. O. 1"> <title>O.</title> 1. 100</ref>, <ref n="Pind. P. 6"> <title>P.</title> 6. 42</ref> and <ref n="Pind. P. 10">10. 9</ref>, <ref n="Pind. N. 10"> <title>N.</title> 10. 32</ref>. In post-classical poetry <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατος</quote> sometimes means ‘last,’ but that use was imitated from the Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">supremus</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">summus.</foreign> Thus in an epitaph on an Italian, a certain Aelius, Apollonides writes (<title>Anthol. P.</title> 7. 233), <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦσον ὅτ’ εἰς ὑπάτην ὠλίσθανε, τέρμα τ’ ἄφυκτον | εἶδεν</quote>. Whether the Apollonides of the Anthology was or was not he of Nicaea, who dedicated to Tiberius a commentary on Timon's <quote xml:lang="grc">Σιλλοί</quote> (<ref n="D. L. 9.109">Diog. Laert. 9. 109</ref>), at least he belonged to that age. This is proved by his words in <title>Anthol. P.</title> 9. 287, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠελίου νῆσον ὅτ’ εἶχε Νέρων</quote>, alluding to the residence of Tiberius at Rhodes (c. 6 B.C.— 2 A.D.). The epigram was written after Tiberius had been adopted by Augustus in 4 A.D., as he is called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζῆνα τὸν ἐσσόμενον</quote>, and perhaps after he had come to the throne (14 A.D.). It would be interesting to know whether <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατος</quote> as =‘last’ can be carried back beyond the Roman, or later Alexandrian, age; I can find no trace of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλοντα_1334">, belonging to the future. To Creon's wish for death the Chorus replies, in effect, ‘Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_προκειμένων_1334">: the duties which lie immediately before us; meaning here especially the obsequies of the dead.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_1334"> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 500"> <title>O. C.</title> 500</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐν τάχει τι πράσσετον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_1334"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶν μελλόντων.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτοισι_μέλειν_1334">, i.e., <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς θεοῖς</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1036"> <title>Ph.</title> 1036</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖσιν εἰ δίκης μέλει, | ἔξοιδα δ’ ὡς μέλει γε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
θεοῖσιν εἰ δίκης μέλει, | ἔξοιδα δ’ ὡς μέλει
									γε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶ_μέν_1336">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> cp. n. on 11. It merely gives a slight emphasis to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρῶ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκατηυξάμην_1336">: <quote xml:lang="grc">κατά</quote> expresses that the prayer is solemn; <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote>, that it sums up his desires. (For this force of <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1202">cp. 1202</ref>.) Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 585"> <title>O. C.</title> 585</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθα γάρ μοι κεῖνα συγκομίζεται</quote> </cit> (‘by that boon I reap all the rest’).—Nauck thinks that L's reading, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_συγκατηυξάμην_1336">, is sound, and that in the corresponding verse, 1314, we should perh. read, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίῳ δὲ <emph>κἀλύετ’</emph> </quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπελύσατ’</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν φοναῖς τρόπῳ</quote>; He refers to the scholium on 1314: <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι τρόπῳ, φησίν, ἐλύετο, καὶ ἐφέρετο εἰς φονάς; ἀντὶ τοῦ, ποίῳ τρόπῳ εἰς φόνον ἔπεσεν</quote>; Now, this does not point, I think, to the Scholiast's having <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλύετο</quote> in his text, though it suggests that he had <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς φονάς</quote>. He used the simple verb in his paraphrase in order to bring out the literal sense (as he took it) of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπελύσατο</quote>. This is shown by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφέρετο</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπεσεν</quote>: he understood, ‘she was set free (as a runner in a race is dismissed from the starting-post), and rushed (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφέρετο</quote>) to bloodshed.’—Further, the origin of L's reading is manifest. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶ_μὲν_1336"> had become </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶμεν_1336"> (as it actually is in at least one later MS.). Then the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρῶμεν</quote> seemed too harsh with the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκατηυξάμην</quote> immediately following (though, in fact, it would have been quite defensible, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 734">cp. 734</ref> n.), and was changed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρῶ_1336"> (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεν_1336"> disappearing). Semitelos would read with L here, and yet leave 1314 unaltered. He refers to <ref n="Soph. Aj. 905"> <title>Ai.</title> 905</ref> where L has <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος ποτ’ ἆρ’ ἔπραξε χειρὶ δύσμορος</quote> corresponding with 951 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγαν ὑπερβριθὲς ἄχθος ἤνυσαν</quote>. But there <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπραξε</quote> is surely corrupt: Hermann gives <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρξε</quote>, and Wecklein <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαθε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσεύχου_1337">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">θεούς</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.48">Her. 1.48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τὸ ἐκ Δελφῶν ἤκουσε, αὐτίκα προσεύχετό τε καὶ προσεδέξατο</quote> </cit>.—Campbell thinks that ‘the rationalism of the day appears in this advice of the Chorus.’ But such an interpretation ill accords with the tone of the Chorus, which presently insists on the duty of piety towards the gods (1348). Nor does it seem in harmony with the pervading spirit of the poet's work. Rather Creon is exhorted to recognise, with pious resignation, the fixity of the divine decrees. Cp. the closing words of the <title>Oed. Col.</title>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀποπαύετε μηδ’ ἐπὶ πλείω | θρῆνον ἐγείρετε· | πάντως γὰρ ἔχει τάδε κῦρος</quote>. Brunck compared <ref n="Verg. A. 6. 376"> <title>Aen.</title> 6. 376</ref>(Aeneas to Palinurus in the shades) <title>Desine fata deum flecti sperare precando.</title> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς τὸ ἐκ Δελφῶν ἤκουσε, αὐτίκα προσεύχετό τε
									καὶ προσεδέξατο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγοιτ_ἄν_1339">, an entreaty: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 725"> <title>O. C.</title> 725</ref>. The opt. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> had a different tone in 444.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάταιον_1339"> here expresses rash folly: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 891"> <title>O. T.</title> 891</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ματᾴζων</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 565"> <title>Tr.</title> 565</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ματαίαις χερσί</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέκανον_1340"> is the best, as it is the simplest, emendation of <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέκτανον</quote> (see cr. n.). Though the pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατακαίνω</quote> is not classical, the aor. is frequent; Xen. uses it ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 1. 2">Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 1. 2</ref>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_τάνδ_1340"> is a certain correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ τ’ αὐτάν</quote>. Here the latter would be like saying, ‘and actually <emph>thee</emph>,’—as if the slaying of Haemon had been comparatively venial. It cannot be naturally explained as meaning, ‘the mother with the son.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_γάρ_1342">. The reading of this verse cannot be certainly determined. The traditional text (see cr. n.) exceeds the metre. My own view is as follows:</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπᾳ_1342"> should be struck out. It evidently came in from the margin, having been a gloss on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾷ_1342">, meant to show that <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾷ κλιθῶ</quote> is not a direct question, but depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔχω</quote>. Retaining <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπᾳ</quote>, we should have to suppose a double question: ‘nor do I know in what direction, (or) to which thing, I am to look.’ This is not only very awkward, but very weak. The hiatus after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω</quote>, though not unexampled, is at least another point against <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπᾳ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_γάρ_1342"> in a line by itself: but, considering the caprices of lyric division in that MS. (as in the rest), we cannot urge that fact as a hint of interpolation. If <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπᾳ</quote> was a spurious addition to 1342, then <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα γάρ</quote> might easily have been carried over. Again, the words <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα γάρ</quote> are not indispensable; yet the effect of <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχρια τἀν χεροῖν</quote>, without them, would be rather oddly abrupt. Therefore we are by no means warranted (I think) in ejecting <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα γάρ</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλιθῶ_1342">, for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_θῶ_1342">, is certain. On this last point there is now a general agreement.—The resulting dochmiac differs from that in 1320 only by the ‘irrational’ long (the <quote xml:lang="grc">ω</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδω</quote>) for short (the first of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτυμον</quote>): and this is admissible. See Metrical Analysis.—Other views are noticed in the Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_πότερον_1342">, i.e., to the corpse of Haemon at his side, or to that of Eurydicè in front of him (1297 ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδω_1342">, deliberative subjunct. in the indirect question, depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω</quote>: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 72"> <title>O. T.</title> 72</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾷ_κλιθῶ_1342">, in what direction I am to lean, i.e., where I am to find any support: my son and my wife have fallen: all my fortunes lie in ruin. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾷ_1342_2"> here answers to the dat. after <quote xml:lang="grc">κλίνομαι</quote> when it means ‘to lean against’ a thing, as <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.307"> <title>Od.</title> 6.307</ref> (she sits) <quote xml:lang="grc">κίονι κεκλιμένη</quote>.—Not merely, ‘whither I am to betake myself,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ τράπωμαι</quote>; This is shown by <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχρια</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέχρια_χεροῖν_1344">. <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀν</quote> seems right (see cr. n.): the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ἐν</quote> would come from <foreign xml:lang="grc">ΤΑΕΝ</foreign>. Creon is still touching the corpse of Haemon. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐν χεροῖν</quote> would mean, figuratively, ‘the matters with which I am engaged’ (so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν τι ἐν χερσί,</quote> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.35">Her. 1.35</bibl> </cit>). Here, the words take a dramatic force from their literal sense. ‘All is amiss with that which I handle.’ Creon has, indeed, mismanaged the work which his hands found to do; and the proof of it is the corpse which he is touching. <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχριος</quote> =‘slanting,’ ‘oblique.’ As <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθός</quote> means either ‘straight’ or ‘upright,’ so <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχριος</quote> can mean either ‘moving sideways’ (<ref n="Soph. OC 195"> <title>O.C.</title> 195</ref>), or, ‘not upright,’ ‘slanting.’ Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάγιος</quote>, the ordinary prose equiv. of <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχριος</quote>, which has the second sense in Philemon <title xml:lang="grc">Ἀγύρτης</title> 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">σχήματα | πλάγῑ ἐστὶ τἄλλα, τοῦτο δ’ ὀρθὸν θηρίον</quote>, man alone is <emph>erect</emph>, while other creatures (i.e. quadrupeds) are bent earthward (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Sal. Cat. 1">Sallust, <title>Cat.</title> 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">pecora quae natura <emph>prona...</emph> finxit</quote> </cit>). So, here, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέχρια_1344"> means primarily ‘awry’:—<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πράγματα οὐκ ὀρθῶς ἔχει</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. R2 2.4">Shaksp. <title>Rich. II.</title> 2. 4. 24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">And crossly to thy good all fortune goes.</quote> </cit> But it is further tinged with the sense of ‘prone,’ applicable to the corpse. The Scholiast here has usu. been understood as explaining <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχρια</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάγια καὶ πεπτωκότα</quote>. But he meant only <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάγια</quote> to explain <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχρια</quote>, while <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπτωκότα</quote> referred to <quote xml:lang="grc">πότμος... εἰσήλατο</quote>: this is clear (I think) from his whole phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάγια καὶ πεπτωκότα, τὰ μὲν ἐν χερσί, τὰ δὲ ἐπὶ τῇ κεφαλῇ</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
And crossly to thy good all fortune
									goes.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μοι_1344"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, while on the other hand: for the adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’</quote>, see <ref n="Soph. OT 666"> <title>O. T.</title> 666</ref> n. These words refer to the deaths of Eurydicè and Antigone, as <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐν χεροῖν</quote> referred to the death of Haemon. It is quite possible to read <emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’</quote> </emph>, as =‘thus’; but then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_χεροῖν_1344"> would denote <emph>all</emph> his woes, and so we should lose the dramatic blending of a literal with a figurative sense.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσήλατο_1344">: cp. <ref target="cl1272">on 1272 f.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐδαιμονίας_πρῶτον_1347">, the most important element in it. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 389D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 389D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφροσύνης δέ, ὡς πλήθει, οὐ τὰ τοιάδε μέγιστα, ἀρχόντων μὲν ὑπηκόους εἶναι, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
σωφροσύνης δέ, ὡς πλήθει, οὐ τὰ τοιάδε
									μέγιστα, ἀρχόντων μὲν ὑπηκόους εἶναι, κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_θεούς_1347">: <ref target="cl889">cp. 889 n.</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 706"> <title>O. T.</title> 706</ref> (n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γ’ εἰς ἑαυτόν</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1441"> <title>Ph.</title> 1441</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβεῖν τὰ πρὸς θεούς</quote> </cit>.—For the sentiment, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1050">cp. 1050 f.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεγάλοι_λόγοι_1349">: <ref target="cl127">cp. 127 n.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ὑπεραύχων_1349">, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 944">cp. 944 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δανάας...δέμας.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πληγὰς_ἀποτείσαντες_1349">, as the price: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.65">Her. 2.65</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποτίνει ζημίην</quote> </cit> (a fine). So <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.56"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 5. 56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς ἀνθρώπων ἀδικῶν τίσιν οὐκ ἀποτείσει</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδεὶς ἀνθρώπων ἀδικῶν τίσιν οὐκ
									ἀποτείσει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γήρᾳ_1352">, without a prep.: so <ref n="Eur. Hec. 203">Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 203</ref>, etc.: but this is poetical, prose preferring <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν γήρᾳ, ἐν τῷ γήρᾳ</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ γήρως.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φρονεῖν_1352">, so soon after <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1347">1347</ref>: cp. <ref target="cl76">on 76</ref>, <ref target="cl625">625</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτὸς ἄτας</quote>), <ref target="cl956">956</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κερτομίοις</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδίδαξαν_1352">, gnomic aor. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 709">(709).</ref> </gloss>
